topics.title: Introduction to Internet of Things: Definition and Characteristics of IoT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Internet of Things: Definition and Characteristics of IoT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of IoT

The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a global, distributed network infrastructure in which physical and virtual "things" — equipped with sensors, actuators, microcontrollers, communication modules, and software — are seamlessly interconnected through information and communication technologies (ICT) to enable **data acquisition, intelligent processing, decision-making, and service delivery** with minimal human intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**  
> The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is an ecosystem of uniquely identifiable *Things* (objects, devices, sensors, actuators) connected to the Internet, communicating through standard protocols, and exchanging **sensor data → analytics → actuation** cycles to deliver context-aware services.

### Formal Structural Equation (Conceptual Model)

$$
\text{IoT} \;=\; f(\text{Things}, \text{Internet}, \text{Data}, \text{Process}, \text{People})
$$

Where each variable carries a specific role in the architecture.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Think of IoT like a "Smart Human Body":**
- **Senses (Eyes, Ears, Skin)** = Sensors collecting data from the environment (temperature, motion, pressure).
- **Nervous System (Nerves & Spinal Cord)** = Communication networks (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, 5G, LoRa) carrying the data.
- **Brain (Cerebrum)** = Cloud / Edge processors that analyze data and make decisions.
- **Muscles (Hands, Legs)** = Actuators that perform physical actions (switching on a fan, releasing insulin, locking a door).
- **Memory & Learning** = Databases, AI/ML models that remember past patterns and improve decisions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In IoT, just as the human body reacts *without consciously thinking about every breath*, IoT systems can *sense, decide, and act* autonomously — this is the heart of the **"smart"** in smart devices.

## 1.3 Origin & Historical Context

The term **"Internet of Things"** was coined by **Kevin Ashton** in **1999** during a presentation at **Procter & Gamble**, where he described a system where the Internet was connected to the physical world via sensors (specifically, RFID tags).

> [!NOTE]
> **Timeline Snapshot for KTU:**
> - **1999** – Term coined by Kevin Ashton
> - **2005–2008** – IoT formally adopted by ITU (International Telecommunication Union)
> - **2009–Present** – Explosive growth with 4G/5G, cloud computing, edge AI
> - **2030 (Projected)** – An estimated **29 billion** connected IoT devices globally

## 1.4 Why IoT Matters — The Engineering Motivation

| Driver | Impact |
|---|---|
| Miniaturization of hardware | Sensors are now sub-centimeter and sub-gram |
| Ubiquitous connectivity | 5G/6G enables real-time data exchange |
| Cheap storage & cloud | Petabytes of sensor data can be archived cheaply |
| AI/ML maturity | Enables pattern recognition in sensor streams |
| Demand for automation | Healthcare, agriculture, and industry 4.0 need it |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Foundational Characteristics of IoT (KTU Board-Standard)

IoT is **not** just "things connected to the Internet." The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to enumerate and explain the following six **defining characteristics**:

### Characteristic 1: **Connectivity**
- Devices connect to **IoT infrastructure** (Wi-Fi, ZigBee, BLE, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, 5G).
- Connectivity must be **reliable, secure, and energy-efficient**.
- **Engineering meaning:** A device that is *not connected* is just an *embedded system*, not an IoT device.

### Characteristic 2: **Intelligence & Identity**
- Each "thing" has a **unique identifier** (e.g., IPv6 address, RFID tag, MAC ID, EPC code).
- The identifier enables **addressability and authentication** in the network.
- **Identity equation:**

$$
\text{Unique ID} \;\mapsto\; \text{Device}_{\text{IPv6, EPC, MAC}}
$$

### Characteristic 3: **Dynamic & Self-Adapting Nature**
- IoT devices adapt to **changing context** (location, user behavior, environment).
- They reconfigure their **state, behavior, and network** dynamically.
- **Example:** A smart thermostat adjusts its setpoint based on whether the room is occupied.

### Characteristic 4: **Heterogeneity**
- IoT networks are built from **diverse hardware platforms** (ARM Cortex, AVR, ESP32, Raspberry Pi).
- They run on **multiple OS** (Contiki, RIOT, FreeRTOS, Linux).
- They support **multiple protocols** (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, AMQP).
- **Engineering challenge:** Interoperability and protocol translation (gateways).

### Characteristic 5: **Sensing**
- IoT devices **detect physical phenomena** using sensors:
  - Temperature (LM35, DHT22)
  - Motion (PIR, MPU6050)
  - Biometric (ECG, SpO2, EMG)
  - Chemical (pH, gas sensors)
- **Equation of sensing:**

$$
S(t) = f(\text{physical stimulus at time } t)
$$

### Characteristic 6: **Safety, Security & Privacy**
- Data integrity, confidentiality, and authentication are **non-negotiable**.
- **Engineering demand:** Encryption (AES-256, TLS 1.3), secure boot, OTA updates.
- **Privacy equation (conceptual):**

$$
\text{Privacy} = g(\text{Data Anonymization}, \text{Access Control}, \text{Encryption})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Always quote the **"Sense → Analyze → Actuate → Communicate"** cycle when describing IoT. Examiners reward structured answers.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No `pipe` characters are used inside the table cells** (using `\vert` / `\mid` for absolute-value expressions to preserve markdown).

| Concept | Mathematical / Logical Form | Units / Domain | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| IoT definition | $\text{IoT} = \{ \text{Sensors}, \text{Network}, \text{Cloud}, \text{Analytics}, \text{Actuators} \}$ | Set of subsystems | Architecture design |
| Sensing function | $S(t) = f(\text{stimulus}, t)$ | Volts, °C, Pa, etc. | Sensor calibration |
| Identity mapping | $\text{ID} : \text{Device} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}^{128}$ | 128-bit (IPv6) | Unique addressing |
| Data transmission rate | $R = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bits/second (Shannon) | Bandwidth planning |
| Latency (5G URLLC) | $L_{\text{target}} \le 1 \text{ ms}$ | milliseconds | Real-time control |
| Energy per bit (BLE) | $E_b = V \cdot I \cdot t \big/ N_{\text{bits}}$ | Joules/bit | Battery life design |
| Battery lifetime | $T_{\text{life}} = C_{\text{battery}} \big/ I_{\text{avg}}$ | hours / days / years | LPWAN design |
| Privacy factor | $P = \alpha A + \beta E + \gamma C$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Security scoring |
| IoT device count (growth) | $N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{kt}$ | billions | Network capacity planning |

> [!NOTE]
> The Shannon-Hartley theorem $R = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ is universally applicable to IoT link-budget calculations — examiners may give a numerical problem.

## 2.3 The IoT Reference Model (Three-Layer & Five-Layer Architectures)

### A. Three-Layer Architecture (Simplest KTU-expected model)

| Layer | Function | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Perception Layer** | Physical sensing & actuation | Sensors, RFID, GPS, motors |
| **Network Layer** | Data transmission & routing | Wi-Fi, 5G, LoRa, gateways |
| **Application Layer** | User-facing services & analytics | Smart home dashboards, hospital apps |

### B. Five-Layer Architecture (Advanced)

| Layer | Function |
|---|---|
| Perception (Sensors) | Data acquisition |
| Transport (Network) | Reliable data movement |
| Processing (Edge/Fog) | Local data analytics |
| Application | Service delivery to users |
| Business | ROI, regulation, policy modeling |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | IoT Application |
|---|---|
| **Healthcare (Biomedical)** | Remote ECG monitoring, insulin pumps, smart prosthetics |
| **Agriculture** | Soil moisture sensing, drone-based crop health analysis |
| **Smart Cities** | Traffic management, air-quality monitoring, smart lighting |
| **Industry 4.0** | Predictive maintenance, robotic process control |
| **Wearables** | Fitness bands, fall-detection devices for elderly |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Architectural Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This topic is conceptual; hence the "derivation" below is a **step-by-step logical decomposition** of the IoT operational cycle — explicitly enumerated for KTU 14-mark answers.

## 3.1 The IoT Operational Cycle (Sense → Transmit → Analyze → Decide → Actuate)

Let us denote the entire IoT pipeline as the function $\Phi$ acting on a physical stimulus. The following step-by-step breakdown mirrors the **IEEE/ITU IoT reference model**.

### Step 1: **Sensing (Perception Layer)**

The sensor transduces a physical quantity into an electrical signal.

$$
S_{\text{raw}}(t) = K_s \cdot x(t) + \epsilon(t)
$$

- $x(t)$ = physical quantity (e.g., temperature in °C, pressure in Pa).
- $K_s$ = sensor sensitivity (V/unit).
- $\epsilon(t)$ = measurement noise (additive, zero-mean).
- **Output:** an analog voltage $S_{\text{raw}}(t)$.

### Step 2: **Signal Conditioning & Analog-to-Digital Conversion**

The raw signal is amplified, filtered, and digitized.

$$
S_{\text{digital}}[n] = \left\lfloor \frac{S_{\text{raw}}(nT_s) - V_{\min}}{V_{\max} - V_{\min}} \cdot (2^N - 1) \right\rfloor
$$

- $T_s$ = sampling period (seconds).
- $N$ = ADC resolution (bits).
- $V_{\min}, V_{\max}$ = ADC input range.
- **Engineering output:** a quantized integer sample $S_{\text{digital}}[n]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why quantize?** Digital microcontrollers and cloud servers only understand discrete numbers. Resolution $N$ directly controls accuracy.

### Step 3: **Local Processing (Edge Layer)**

The microcontroller preprocesses data — filtering, compression, feature extraction.

$$
\mathbf{F} = \mathcal{H}(S_{\text{digital}}[0{:}W])
$$

- $\mathcal{H}$ = feature extraction operator (FFT, moving average, thresholding).
- $W$ = window length (number of samples).
- $\mathbf{F}$ = feature vector.

### Step 4: **Communication (Network Layer)**

The features are packetized and transmitted over a chosen protocol.

$$
T_{\text{tx}} = \frac{L_{\text{packet}}}{R_{\text{link}}}
$$

- $L_{\text{packet}}$ = packet size in bits.
- $R_{\text{link}}$ = link data rate (bps).
- **Engineering meaning:** Lower $T_{\text{tx}}$ → more real-time, but higher energy cost.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU students often confuse *throughput* and *latency*. $R_{\text{link}}$ is the **theoretical** rate; actual throughput is lower due to overhead, collisions, and retransmissions.

### Step 5: **Cloud / Fog Analytics (Processing Layer)**

Data is aggregated, stored, and analyzed using AI/ML models.

$$
\hat{y} = \text{ML-Model}(\mathbf{F}_1, \mathbf{F}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{F}_M)
$$

- $\hat{y}$ = predicted outcome (e.g., "Patient tachycardia detected").
- $M$ = number of feature vectors in a batch.

### Step 6: **Decision & Actuation (Application Layer)**

A control command is sent back to the actuator.

$$
A(t) = \begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } \hat{y} = \text{critical} \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

- $A(t) = 1$ → actuator ON (e.g., alarm rings, insulin pump delivers dose).
- $A(t) = 0$ → actuator OFF (or standby).

### Step 7: **Feedback Loop**

The output of the actuator modifies the physical environment, which is sensed again — closing the loop.

$$
x(t + \Delta t) \rightarrow S_{\text{raw}}(t + \Delta t) \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow A(t + \Delta t)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the closed-loop control nature of IoT.** A single-pass data-logging system is *not* true IoT — feedback is essential.

## 3.2 The Six Characteristics — Analytical Matrix

| # | Characteristic | Definition | Engineering Example | KTU Board-Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Connectivity** | Devices link to network and to each other | Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, 5G | "Always-connected" |
| 2 | **Intelligence & Identity** | Unique ID + on-device smart decision | IPv6 + ARM Cortex-M | "Addressable" |
| 3 | **Dynamic / Self-Adaptive** | Adapts to context, re-configures itself | Smart AC adjusts to occupancy | "Context-aware" |
| 4 | **Heterogeneity** | Multiple hardware, software, protocols coexist | ESP32 + Raspberry Pi + Cloud | "Interoperable" |
| 5 | **Sensing** | Detects physical changes in environment | ECG sensor → 12-lead ECG | "Sensor-enabled" |
| 6 | **Safety / Security / Privacy** | Confidentiality, integrity, authentication | AES-256, TLS, OAuth2 | "Secure-by-design" |

## 3.3 IoT vs. Traditional Embedded System (High-Yield Comparison)

| Parameter | Embedded System | IoT System |
|---|---|---|
| Connectivity | Often isolated | Internet-connected, mandatory |
| Intelligence | Limited, local | Cloud + edge + on-device |
| Identity | Optional | **Required** (unique ID) |
| Data destination | Local display/actuator | Cloud / Fog / Edge servers |
| User interaction | Direct, local | Remote, via mobile/web apps |
| Update mechanism | Manual firmware | OTA (Over-The-Air) updates |
| Scale | Single device | Massive (millions) |
| Security | Often minimal | **Mandatory (TLS, encryption)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Hack:** If the question asks "Is a calculator an IoT device?" the answer is **NO** — it is an embedded system but lacks **Internet connectivity, unique ID, and cloud communication**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IoT Reference Architecture (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Stimulus] --> B[Sensor Node]
    B --> C[Microcontroller Edge Processor]
    C --> D{Connectivity Layer}
    D -->|Wi-Fi / 5G / LoRa| E[Gateway / Router]
    E --> F[Cloud Server]
    F --> G[Big Data Analytics]
    G --> H[AI / ML Decision Engine]
    H --> I[Application Dashboard]
    I --> J[Control Command]
    J --> K[Actuator]
    K -->|Modifies environment| A

    subgraph perceptionLayer[Perception Layer]
        A
        B
    end

    subgraph networkLayer[Network Layer]
        D
        E
    end

    subgraph processingLayer[Processing Layer]
        F
        G
        H
    end

    subgraph applicationLayer[Application Layer]
        I
        J
        K
    end
```

## 4.2 IoT Functional Block Diagram (Sense → Transmit → Analyze → Actuate)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensor 1 Temperature] --> MUX[Multiplexer]
    S2[Sensor 2 ECG] --> MUX
    S3[Sensor 3 Motion] --> MUX
    MUX --> ADC[ADC]
    ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller]
    MCU --> COMM[Communication Module]
    COMM --> NET((Internet))
    NET --> CLOUD[Cloud Server]
    CLOUD --> DB[(Database)]
    CLOUD --> ML[ML Engine]
    ML --> DEC[Decision Logic]
    DEC --> NET
    NET --> ACT[Actuator]
    ACT --> ENV[Physical Environment]
    ENV --> S1
    ENV --> S2
    ENV --> S3
```

## 4.3 The Six Characteristics — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((IoT Core Characteristics))
    Connectivity
      Wi-Fi
      5G
      LoRa
      Bluetooth
    Intelligence and Identity
      IPv6 addressing
      Unique EPC
      On-device processing
    Dynamic Adaptation
      Context aware
      Self reconfiguring
    Heterogeneity
      Multiple OS
      Multiple protocols
      Cross platform
    Sensing
      Temperature
      Biometric
      Motion
      Chemical
    Security and Privacy
      AES encryption
      TLS protocol
      Authentication
```

## 4.4 Healthcare / Biomedical IoT Use Case — Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Patient Body] -->|ECG Signal| ECG[ECG Sensor]
    P -->|SpO2| SPO2[Pulse Oximeter]
    P -->|Body Temp| TEMP[Temp Sensor]
    ECG --> MCU[Edge MCU ESP32]
    SPO2 --> MCU
    TEMP --> MCU
    MCU -->|BLE / Wi-Fi| GW[Smartphone Gateway]
    GW --> CLOUD[Hospital Cloud Server]
    CLOUD --> DOC[Doctor Dashboard]
    CLOUD --> AI[AI Diagnostic Engine]
    AI -->|Alert| DOC
    DOC -->|Prescription| ACT[Smart Drug Delivery Actuator]
    ACT --> P
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above **biomedical** block diagram is critical for the course OEEBT831 — examiners may ask you to *map* any biomedical monitoring system to the IoT architecture.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
**Define the Internet of Things. List any FOUR defining characteristics of IoT.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a network of uniquely identifiable physical objects ("things") embedded with sensors, software, and communication technologies that enable them to **collect, exchange, and act upon data** over the Internet with minimal human intervention.

**Four defining characteristics:**

1. **Connectivity** — Devices are connected to the Internet and to each other via heterogeneous networks.
2. **Sensing** — IoT devices sense physical parameters (temperature, ECG, motion) from the environment.
3. **Intelligence & Identity** — Each device has a unique ID (IPv6) and processes data intelligently.
4. **Dynamic & Self-Adaptive** — Devices adapt their behavior based on changing context (e.g., smart AC adjusting to occupancy).

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Differentiate between a traditional embedded system and an IoT system. Mention any THREE points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Embedded System | IoT System |
|---|---|---|
| **Connectivity** | Usually standalone or local | Always connected to the Internet |
| **Data Destination** | Local display / actuator | Cloud, fog, or edge servers |
| **Updates** | Manual firmware flashing | **OTA (Over-The-Air) updates** |
| **Identity** | Optional unique ID | **Mandatory unique ID** (IPv6 / MAC) |
| **Scale** | Single or few devices | **Massive** (millions) |
| **Security** | Often minimal | **Mandatory** (TLS, AES encryption) |

---

## 📕 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU's End Semester Exam (ESE) allows **internal choice** within a module. The following are two alternative 14-mark questions — you can answer **either one** in your exam.

---

### **📌 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Module 1 Pattern]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **six fundamental characteristics of IoT** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Describe the **three-layer and five-layer IoT reference architectures** with neat diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

The six fundamental characteristics of IoT are:

1. **Connectivity** — IoT devices are connected to the Internet via various technologies such as Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee, LoRa, 5G, and NB-IoT. Connectivity is the *first* and most important characteristic. Without connectivity, a device is just an embedded system, not an IoT device. *Example:* A smart bulb connected via Wi-Fi to a smartphone app.

2. **Intelligence & Identity** — Each IoT device has a **unique identifier** (IPv6 address, EPC code for RFID, or MAC address). This identity allows it to be addressed, authenticated, and tracked. The device also has on-board intelligence (microcontroller + firmware) for local decision-making. *Example:* A wearable fitness band with a unique Bluetooth MAC ID.

3. **Dynamic and Self-Adaptive Nature** — IoT devices **adapt their behavior dynamically** based on changing context (location, user behavior, environment). *Example:* A smart thermostat that lowers the setpoint when no one is home, detected via motion sensors.

4. **Heterogeneity** — IoT systems are built from **diverse hardware, software, and communication protocols**. Devices from different vendors (ARM, AVR, ESP, Raspberry Pi) must interoperate. *Example:* A smart home where a ZigBee bulb talks to a Wi-Fi camera via a hub.

5. **Sensing** — IoT devices **sense physical phenomena** from the environment using sensors. Sensing is the *data-acquisition* foundation. *Example (Biomedical):* An ECG sensor monitoring a patient's heartbeat in real time.

6. **Safety, Security, and Privacy** — IoT systems handle **sensitive personal and operational data**. Security is non-negotiable. Encryption (AES-256), authentication (OAuth2), and secure transport (TLS 1.3) are mandatory. *Example:* Encrypted transmission of patient ECG data from a wearable to a hospital cloud.

> Closing sentence: These six characteristics together distinguish an IoT system from a simple embedded system, and every KTU answer on IoT *must* enumerate them.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Three-Layer Architecture:**

| Layer | Function |
|---|---|
| **Perception Layer** | Physical sensing via sensors / RFID / actuators |
| **Network Layer** | Data transmission using Wi-Fi / 5G / LoRa |
| **Application Layer** | User-facing services and analytics |

*Example:* A smart agriculture system — soil moisture sensor (perception) → LoRa gateway (network) → farmer's mobile app (application).

**Five-Layer Architecture:**

| Layer | Function |
|---|---|
| **Perception** | Data acquisition from sensors |
| **Transport (Network)** | Reliable data routing and transmission |
| **Processing (Middleware)** | Data storage, analytics, edge/fog processing |
| **Application** | Service delivery to end-users |
| **Business** | Policy, regulation, ROI modeling |

*Example (Biomedical):* ECG sensor (perception) → 5G link (transport) → hospital cloud analytics (processing) → doctor dashboard (application) → hospital billing & compliance (business).

The **five-layer model adds Processing and Business layers** to handle modern big-data and enterprise needs.

---

### **📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Module 1 Pattern]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **IoT operational cycle** (Sense → Transmit → Analyze → Decide → Actuate) using a **biomedical monitoring example** (e.g., remote ECG monitoring). **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the **key challenges and real-world applications** of IoT, with at least **three examples from the biomedical domain**. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

Consider a **remote ECG monitoring system** for a cardiac patient.

**Step 1: Sensing (Perception Layer)**
ECG electrodes on the patient's chest detect cardiac electrical activity, producing a low-voltage analog signal:

$$
S_{\text{raw}}(t) = K_s \cdot \text{ECG}(t) + \epsilon(t)
$$

**Step 2: Signal Conditioning & ADC**
The analog ECG is amplified, filtered (0.5 Hz – 40 Hz bandpass), and digitized by an ADC:

$$
\text{ECG}_{\text{digital}}[n] = \text{ADC}(S_{\text{raw}}(nT_s))
$$

**Step 3: Edge Processing (Microcontroller)**
A microcontroller (e.g., ESP32) detects QRS complexes, computes heart rate, and compresses data.

**Step 4: Communication (Network Layer)**
The processed ECG is transmitted via BLE to a smartphone, then via 4G/5G/Wi-Fi to a hospital cloud server using MQTT protocol.

**Step 5: Cloud Analytics (Processing Layer)**
Cloud servers run AI/ML models to detect arrhythmias (e.g., atrial fibrillation, tachycardia).

**Step 6: Decision & Actuation (Application Layer)**
If critical, an alert is sent to the doctor and the patient. Optionally, a smart drug-delivery actuator (e.g., nitroglycerin pump) is activated.

**Step 7: Feedback**
The patient's heart responds to medication, and the loop continues.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[ECG Electrodes] → [Amplifier + Filter] → [ADC] → [ESP32 MCU]
        → [BLE / Wi-Fi] → [Smartphone Gateway] → [Hospital Cloud]
        → [ML Diagnostic Engine] → [Doctor Dashboard]
        → [Alerts / Drug Delivery Actuator] → [Patient]
```

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Key Challenges of IoT:**
1. **Security & Privacy** — Vulnerable to cyber-attacks; biomedical data leakage.
2. **Interoperability** — Different vendors use different protocols.
3. **Power Management** — Battery-operated devices need ultra-low-power designs.
4. **Scalability** — Managing millions of devices.
5. **Data Overload** — Massive sensor data requires AI/edge processing.

**Real-World Biomedical IoT Applications:**

1. **Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM):** Wearable ECG patches (e.g., Zio Patch) continuously record cardiac activity and transmit it to a cardiologist.
2. **Smart Insulin Pumps:** IoT-enabled pumps read CGM (continuous glucose monitoring) data and auto-deliver insulin doses (closed-loop "artificial pancreas").
3. **Hospital Asset Tracking:** RFID-tagged wheelchairs, ventilators, and infusion pumps are tracked in real time.
4. **Smart Prosthetics:** IoT-enabled prosthetic limbs with EMG sensors adapt grip strength based on object type.
5. **Elderly Fall Detection:** Wearable accelerometers detect falls and auto-alert caregivers.

> Closing sentence: IoT is **revolutionizing healthcare** by enabling continuous, real-time, remote, and intelligent biomedical monitoring.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in IoT Definition Answers:**
> 1. **Writing only "Internet + Things"** — Examiners deduct 1 mark. Always mention *sensors, connectivity, and data exchange*.
> 2. **Confusing IoT with M2M (Machine-to-Machine)** — IoT includes *cloud analytics, AI, and end-user apps*, while M2M is point-to-point.
> 3. **Forgetting "unique identity"** — A device without a unique ID is not IoT-grade.
> 4. **Missing feedback loop** — Many students describe only sense → transmit. A true IoT system must have a **closed feedback loop** (sense → actuate → sense again).
> 5. **Ignoring biomedical context** — In OEEBT831, every IoT answer should ideally reference a **biomedical application** to score full marks.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **IoT = "Things + Internet + Sensors + Data + Smart Services"** — never just "Internet of objects."
- 🔹 Coined by **Kevin Ashton in 1999** at Procter & Gamble.
- 🔹 **Six Characteristics:** Connectivity, Intelligence & Identity, Dynamic/Self-Adaptive, Heterogeneity, Sensing, Security/Privacy.
- 🔹 **Three-Layer Architecture:** Perception, Network, Application.
- 🔹 **Five-Layer Architecture:** Perception, Transport, Processing, Application, Business.
- 🔹 IoT cycle is **closed-loop:** Sense → Transmit → Analyze → Decide → Actuate → (feedback) → Sense.
- 🔹 Each device needs a **unique ID** (IPv6 / EPC / MAC).
- 🔹 For **OEEBT831**, always tie examples to **biomedical applications** (ECG, SpO2, insulin pump).
- 🔹 Shannon's formula $R = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ is your go-to for bandwidth / data-rate questions.
- 🔹 **Security** (AES-256, TLS 1.3, OAuth2) is **non-negotiable** in any IoT discussion.
- 🔹 An IoT device without **Internet connectivity and a unique ID** is *not* IoT — it's just an embedded system.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Design of IoT: IoT Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Physical Design of IoT: IoT Protocols # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **IoT Protocols** are the standardized set of communication rules, message formats, and procedures that govern the exchange of data between heterogeneous physical devices (sensors, actuators, gateways, cloud servers) across the different layers of the IoT protocol stack. They ensure interoperability, reliability, and security in resource-constrained IoT environments. In the context of the **Physical Design of IoT**, protocols define *how* the "Things" (smart objects) talk to each other across the four generic functional blocks — **Device, Network, Cloud, and Application Layer**. Since IoT devices are often constrained by **power (battery)**, **memory (KB to few MB of RAM)**, and **bandwidth (kbps range)**, traditional internet protocols (like heavy TCP/HTTP) are often unsuitable, prompting the rise of lightweight IoT-specific protocols. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Universal Postal System":** > Think of IoT protocols as the **languages and postal rules** used between countries. > - **A temperature sensor in a hospital ICU** (India) and a **monitoring dashboard in the cloud** (USA) need a common **language** to communicate → this is the **protocol** (e.g., MQTT). > - The **postal truck** that carries the letter is the **physical/link layer** (e.g., WiFi, Bluetooth, ZigBee). > - The **address on the envelope** is the **network layer** (e.g., IPv6, 6LoWPAN). > - The **type of letter** (registered post, express, regular) is the **transport layer** (TCP reliable vs. UDP fast). > - The **letter format / content** is the **application layer** (e.g., CoAP, HTTP, DDS). > > Just like a postal system needs all these elements working together, IoT needs a *stack* of protocols — each layer playing a specific role. ## 1.3 The IoT Protocol Stack — Top Level View | Layer | Generic Function | Standard IoT Protocols | |---|---|---| | **Application Layer** | Data format, semantics, business logic | **CoAP, MQTT, AMQP, XMPP, DDS, HTTP, WebSocket** | | **Transport Layer** | End-to-end delivery, flow control | **TCP, UDP, DTLS** | | **Network / Internet Layer** | Addressing & routing | **IPv4, IPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL** | | **Link / Physical Layer** | Bit transmission over medium | **IEEE 802.15.4, WiFi (802.11), Bluetooth/BLE, ZigBee, LoRaWAN, NFC, RFID, LTE-M, NB-IoT** | > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Bodies:** **IETF** (Internet Engineering Task Force) defines 6LoWPAN, CoAP, RPL. **IEEE** defines 802.15.4 (the radio basis of ZigBee/6LoWPAN). **OASIS** standardizes MQTT. **Eclipse Foundation** maintains many IoT specs. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics in IoT Protocol Design - **Latency:** Time delay from sensing to decision (often **< 10 ms** for medical, **< 1 s** for environmental). - **Throughput:** Data rate supported — from **250 kbps** (802.15.4) to **hundreds of Mbps** (WiFi). - **Power Consumption:** Critical metric — ZigBee/BLE consume **< 30 mA**, NB-IoT can sleep at **< 5 µA**. - **Range:** Short (**< 10 m** NFC) → Medium (**10–100 m** ZigBee/BLE) → Long (**2–10 km** LoRaWAN). - **Duty Cycle:** Ratio of active time to total time — **< 1 %** in many IoT devices to preserve battery. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of IoT Protocols (Layer-by-Layer) ### 2.1.1 Link / Physical Layer Protocols These define the **electromagnetic (radio/optical) interface** — modulation, encoding, channel access. **(a) IEEE 802.15.4 — The Radio Foundation** - Operates in **2.4 GHz, 915 MHz, 868 MHz** ISM bands. - Maximum data rate: **250 kbps** (2.4 GHz). - Range: **10–100 m** line-of-sight. - Basis for **ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, Thread, WirelessHART**. - Highly suitable for **low-power, low-data-rate** sensor networks (WBAN — Wireless Body Area Networks). **(b) WiFi (IEEE 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax)** - High data rate: **54 Mbps → 9.6 Gbps** (WiFi 6/6E). - Short range: **< 100 m**. High power: **~250 mA** active. - Used for **IoT gateways, high-throughput biomedical imaging devices**. **(c) Bluetooth & BLE (IEEE 802.15.1)** - Classic Bluetooth: **1–3 Mbps**, range **< 10 m**. - **BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy) v5.0+:** 2 Mbps, range up to **400 m** (coded PHY), power **< 15 mA peak**. - Core for **wearable biomedical devices** (ECG patches, pulse oximeters, smart inhalers). **(d) ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4 based)** - Mesh networking, supports **65,000 nodes** per coordinator. - Used in **smart home healthcare**, hospital asset tracking. **(e) LoRaWAN (Long Range WAN)** - Uses **CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum)** modulation. - Range: **2–10 km** (rural), data rate **0.3 – 50 kbps**. - Excellent for **remote patient monitoring**, environmental sensing. **(f) RFID & NFC** - **RFID:** Range up to **10 m** (passive UHF), used for asset/patient tagging. - **NFC:** Range **< 10 cm**, used for secure device pairing, smart pills, glucose meter ID. ### 2.1.2 Network / Internet Layer Protocols **(a) IPv6 over Low-Power WPAN (6LoWPAN)** - Allows **IEEE 802.15.4** radios to carry **IPv6** packets. - Performs **header compression** (40-byte IPv6 header → few bytes). - Essential for connecting **medical sensor motes** to the IP internet. **(b) RPL (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks)** - Distance-vector routing for LLNs (RFC 6550). - Builds a **DODAG (Destination Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph)** rooted at the sink. - Used in **IoT health monitoring mesh networks**. ### 2.1.3 Transport Layer Protocols | Protocol | Nature | Header Size | Reliability | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **TCP** | Connection-oriented | 20 bytes | Reliable, ordered | Firmware updates, EHR sync | | **UDP** | Connectionless | 8 bytes | Unreliable, fast | Real-time vitals streaming, CoAP | | **DTLS** | Datagram TLS | ~13 bytes overhead | Secure UDP | Secure medical telemetry | ### 2.1.4 Application Layer Protocols (KTU HIGH-YIELD ⭐) **(a) MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** - **Publish/Subscribe** model over **TCP** (port 1883; TLS on 8883). - Designed by IBM (1999), standardized at **OASIS**. - Tiny header: **2 bytes fixed + variable**. - Three components: **Publisher, Broker, Subscriber**. - QoS levels: **0 (at most once), 1 (at least once), 2 (exactly once)**. - Ideal for **remote patient monitoring, push notifications from biomedical devices**. **(b) CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol) — RFC 7252** - **Request/Response** model (RESTful like HTTP) over **UDP**. - Header: **4 bytes** (binary). - Methods: **GET, POST, PUT, DELETE**. - Supports **observe** pattern (server-push to clients). - **DTLS** for security. - Ideal for **resource-constrained medical sensors, low-power actuation**. **(c) AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol)** - Heavy, enterprise-grade. Used in **hospital middleware** for reliable, transactional messaging. - Components: **Exchange, Queue, Binding**. **(d) XMPP (Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol)** - XML-based, used for **real-time chat / presence** (e.g., doctor-to-doctor coordination). **(e) DDS (Data Distribution Service)** - **Real-time, peer-to-peer**, no broker. Used in **surgical robotics, ICU device integration**. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet | Protocol | Layer | Model | Transport | Header Size | QoS | Best For (Biomedical Context) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **MQTT** | Application | Pub/Sub | TCP | 2 bytes | 0/1/2 | Remote patient vitals, cloud telemetry | | **CoAP** | Application | Req/Resp | UDP | 4 bytes | Confirmable/Non | WBSN motes, in-hospital actuation | | **AMQP** | Application | Pub/Sub | TCP | 8 bytes | At-least/exactly | Hospital EHR middleware | | **DDS** | Application | Pub/Sub | RTPS/UDP | Variable | 22 levels | Real-time OR imaging, surgical bots | | **HTTP** | Application | Req/Resp | TCP | ~20 bytes | None | Cloud dashboards, firmware OTA | | **XMPP** | Application | Req/Resp | TCP | XML verbose | None | Telemedicine chat, presence | | Link Protocol | Frequency | Range | Data Rate | Power | WBAN Use | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **802.15.4** | 2.4 GHz | ~30 m | 250 kbps | Low | ✅ ZigBee/6LoWPAN biosensors | | **BLE 5** | 2.4 GHz | ~75 m (coded) | 2 Mbps | Very low | ✅ Wearables (smartwatch ECG) | | **WiFi 6** | 2.4/5/6 GHz | ~50 m | >1 Gbps | High | Medical imaging transfer | | **LoRaWAN** | 868/915 MHz | 2–10 km | 0.3–50 kbps | Ultra-low | Rural tele-monitoring | | **NFC** | 13.56 MHz | <10 cm | 106–424 kbps | Passive | Smart pill ID, device pairing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the **transport layer** (TCP/UDP) the application protocol uses. Examiners award 1 mark for this alone. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hospital Asset Tracking:** RFID + WiFi gateway. - **ICU Monitoring:** BLE → Gateway → MQTT → Cloud (AWS IoT). - **Smart Pill Compliance:** NFC-tagged pill + smartphone reader + CoAP/HTTPS cloud API. - **Ambulance Telemetry:** LTE-M / 5G + MQTT (reliable cellular push). # Section 3: Analytical Matrix & Quantitative Foundations ## 3.1 Quantitative Foundations for Protocol Selection Although IoT protocol selection is largely architectural, several **derived metrics** govern performance. Below are the foundational equations used in KTU-level analytical questions. ### 3.1.1 Effective Throughput (Application Level) The **effective data rate** an application actually achieves after all protocol overheads: $$ R_{eff} = \frac{L_{payload} \cdot f_{pkt}}{L_{payload} + H_{overhead}} \quad \text{[bps]} $$ Where: - $L_{payload}$ = useful payload size (bytes) - $H_{overhead}$ = total headers from all layers (bytes) - $f_{pkt}$ = packet transmission frequency (packets/second) > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example:** A CoAP packet over 6LoWPAN sends 30 bytes of ECG payload. > CoAP header (4) + UDP header (8) + 6LoWPAN compressed header (8) + 802.15.4 MAC (25) + PHY (6) = 51 bytes overhead. Sent at $f_{pkt}$ = 10 Hz. > > $R_{eff} = \dfrac{30 \times 10 \times 8}{30 + 51} = \dfrac{2400}{81} \approx 29.6 \text{ bps application throughput...}$ > > Per-packet **overhead ratio** = $\dfrac{H}{L+H} = \dfrac{51}{81} \approx 63\%$. This is why small payloads suffer badly with high-overhead protocols. ### 3.1.2 Energy Per Bit ($E_b$) for a Transmitted Packet $$ E_{tx}(L) = \big( V \cdot I_{tx} \big) \cdot \frac{L}{R_{b}} \quad \text{[Joules]} $$ Where $V$ is supply voltage, $I_{tx}$ is TX current, $L$ is total bits, $R_b$ is bit rate. > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** Doubling header size nearly doubles TX energy for the same payload. **MQTT (2-byte header)** vs **HTTP (100+ byte header)** over TCP at 1 kbps can mean **>40× less TX energy** for the same data — vital for implanted sensors. ### 3.1.3 Battery Lifetime Estimate $$ T_{life} = \frac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}} \quad \text{[hours]} $$ Where average current depends on duty cycle: $$ I_{avg} = I_{sleep} \cdot (1-D) + I_{tx} \cdot D $$ With $D$ = duty cycle = $t_{active} / t_{period}$. ### 3.1.4 6LoWPAN Header Compression Gain $$ G_{comp} = 1 - \frac{H_{6LoWPAN}}{H_{IPv6}} = 1 - \frac{8}{40} = 0.8 \;(80\% \text{ reduction}) $$ ### 3.1.5 LoRaWAN Link Budget & Range (Friis Path Loss basis) $$ L_{path} \;=\; 20\log_{10}(d) \;+\; 20\log_{10}(f) \;-\; 147.55 \quad \text{[dB]} $$ Where $d$ is in **meters**, $f$ in **Hz**. Maximum range occurs when: $$ L_{path} \;\le\; P_{tx} + G_{tx} + G_{rx} - S_{rx} - M_{fade} $$ > $S_{rx}$ = receiver sensitivity (e.g., **−137 dBm** for LoRa SF12/BW125). $M_{fade}$ = fade margin (typically **15–30 dB**). ## 3.2 KTU Analytical Question Type — Model Walkthrough **Problem:** A wearable ECG sensor uses BLE 5 (PHY = 2 Mbps) and sends a CoAP packet with 40 bytes ECG data. Estimate effective throughput per packet and TX energy, given $I_{tx}$ = 15 mA, $V$ = 3 V. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 1 — Total packet size.** > $\text{CoAP} = 4\text{ B}$, $\text{UDP} = 8\text{ B}$, $\text{BLE L2CAP} = 4\text{ B}$, $\text{BLE ATT} = 3\text{ B}$, $\text{BLE LL header} = 9\text{ B}$. Total overhead = $4+8+4+3+9 = 28$ bytes. > Total bits = $(40 + 28) \times 8 = 544$ bits. > **Step 2 — Time on air.** > $\text{TOA} = \dfrac{544 \text{ bits}}{2 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps}} = 272\ \mu s$. > **Step 3 — TX energy per packet.** > $\begin{aligned} > E_{tx} &= V \cdot I_{tx} \cdot TOA \\ > &= 3\text{ V} \times 15 \times 10^{-3}\text{ A} \times 272 \times 10^{-6}\text{ s} \\ > &= 12.24 \times 10^{-6}\text{ J} = 12.24\ \mu J > \end{aligned}$ > **Step 4 — Effective throughput.** > $\begin{aligned} > R_{eff} &= \frac{L_{payload}}{L_{total}} \cdot R_{b} = \frac{40}{68} \times 2 \text{ Mbps} \\ > &\approx 1.18 \text{ Mbps raw effective.} > \end{aligned}$ > **Step 5 — Battery lifetime (1 sample/sec, 240 mAh @ 3 V).** > $I_{avg} \approx 15\text{ mA} \times 2.72 \times 10^{-4} = 4.08\ \mu A$. > $\begin{aligned} > T_{life} &= \frac{240 \text{ mAh}}{4.08 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mA}} = 58{,}823 \text{ h} \approx 6.7 \text{ years.} > \end{aligned}$ > **Inference:** BLE + CoAP is *ideal* for low-rate medical telemetry. Replace with HTTP+TCP and energy increases by **~20×**. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 IoT Protocol Stack (Layered Reference Model) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[APPLICATION LAYER
CoAP, MQTT, AMQP, XMPP, DDS, HTTP] --> B[TRANSPORT LAYER
TCP, UDP, DTLS] B --> C[NETWORK LAYER
IPv4, IPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL] C --> D[LINK LAYER
IEEE 802.15.4, BLE, WiFi, LoRaWAN, NFC] D --> E[PHYSICAL LAYER
RF 2.4 GHz, Sub-GHz, Optical] ``` ## 4.2 MQTT Publish/Subscribe Architecture (Block Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Publishers [Sensors and Devices] P1[ECG Sensor] P2[SpO2 Sensor] P3[Glucose Meter] end subgraph Broker [MQTT Broker] BR[Topic Manager
patient 001 vitals] end subgraph Subscribers [Consumers] S1[Doctor Mobile App] S2[Hospital Dashboard] S3[Cloud Analytics AWS] end P1 -- publish to topic vitals/ecg --> BR P2 -- publish to topic vitals/spo2 --> BR P3 -- publish to topic vitals/glucose --> BR BR -- subscribe pushed --> S1 BR -- subscribe pushed --> S2 BR -- subscribe pushed --> S3 ``` ## 4.3 CoAP Request/Response Model ```mermaid flowchart TB S[CoAP Client
e.g. Smartphone App] -->|CON GET temperature| DEV[CoAP Server
Wireless Thermometer] DEV -->|ACK payload 37.2 C| S S -->|CON POST alert onThreshold| DEV DEV -->|ACK 2.01 Created| S ``` ## 4.4 Biomedical IoT End-to-End Protocol Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Body [On Body] S1[ECG Electrode] S2[PPG Pulse Ox] end Body -->|BLE 5| GATE[BLE to WiFi Gateway] GATE -->|MQTT over TLS| CLOUD[(Cloud Broker)] CLOUD -->|HTTPS| DOC[Clinician Dashboard] CLOUD -->|DDS| ICU[ICU Real-time Monitor] DOC -->|AMQP| EHR[(Hospital EHR System)] ``` ## 4.5 Protocol Selection Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Need real-time video?} -->|Yes| WIFI[Use WiFi or 5G] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Battery powered for years?} Q2 -->|Yes| LP[Use BLE 5 or LoRaWAN] Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need reliable cloud push?} Q3 -->|Yes| MQ[Use MQTT over TCP] Q3 -->|No| Q4{Need RESTful semantics?} Q4 -->|Yes| COAP[Use CoAP over UDP] Q4 -->|No| DDS[Use DDS for peer to peer] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **List any three application layer IoT protocols and state one key feature of each.** > **Model Answer:** > 1. **MQTT** — Lightweight **Publish/Subscribe** protocol over **TCP** with very low overhead (2-byte fixed header), used in **remote patient monitoring**. > 2. **CoAP** — RESTful **Request/Response** protocol over **UDP** designed for constrained nodes, with 4-byte binary header, supports **observe** pattern. > 3. **DDS** — Real-time, **broker-less**, peer-to-peer pub/sub with **22 QoS levels**, used in **surgical robotics and ICU device integration**. > *(Any three valid protocols with one distinguishing feature — 1 mark each.)* ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Retest] **Compare 6LoWPAN and RPL. State the layer they operate at.** > **Model Answer:** > - **6LoWPAN (RFC 4944, 6282)** operates at the **Network/Adaptation layer** between 802.15.4 MAC and IPv6. It enables IPv6 packets to be carried over low-power 802.15.4 radios by **compressing the 40-byte IPv6 header** to a few bytes and performing fragmentation/reassembly. > - **RPL (RFC 6550)** operates at the **Network/Routing layer** for **Low-Power and Lossy Networks (LLNs)**. It builds a **DODAG** rooted at a sink using objective functions (e.g., OF0, MRHOF) for energy-efficient routing. > - **Key difference:** 6LoWPAN = *adaptation* (how IPv6 fits on a small radio); RPL = *routing* (how packets travel across many motes). ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > **ESE Module 1 — Answer any ONE full question (a + b).** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of the MQTT protocol with a neat block diagram. Discuss its three QoS levels. **(b) [7 Marks)** With a block schematic, describe the layered protocol stack of a Wireless Body Area Network (WBAN) for patient monitoring, identifying the role of **IEEE 802.15.4, 6LoWPAN, RPL, UDP, and CoAP** in the stack. #### **Model Solution — (a)** **MQTT Architecture (5 marks):** - MQTT follows a **Publish/Subscribe** pattern decoupled by a central **Broker**. - **Three components:** - **Publisher** — IoT sensor (e.g., ECG patch) publishes messages with a **topic** (e.g., `hospital/ward3/patient/ecg`). - **Broker** — Server (e.g., **Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX, AWS IoT Core**) that receives, filters, and forwards messages. Default port **1883** (or **8883** for TLS). - **Subscriber** — Application/dashboard that subscribes to topics of interest. - **Message anatomy:** Fixed header (2 bytes) + Variable header + Payload. Connect, Publish, Subscribe, Ping, Disconnect are key control packets. - Broker supports **wildcards**: `+` (single level), `#` (multi level). **Three QoS Levels (2 marks):** | Level | Name | Mechanism | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **QoS 0** | At most once | Fire and forget | Frequent non-critical vitals (temperature) | | **QoS 1** | At least once | PUBACK handshake | Drug dosage alarms | | **QoS 2** | Exactly once | 4-step handshake (PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP) | Critical: insulin pump commands | #### **Model Solution — (b)** **WBAN Protocol Stack — Block Schematic (5 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart TB L5[APPLICATION: CoAP REST API for vitals] L4[TRANSPORT: UDP with DTLS for security] L3[NETWORK: 6LoWPAN adapts IPv6 over 802.15.4, RPL provides mesh routing] L2[LINK: IEEE 802.15.4 MAC CSMA CA] L1[PHYSICAL: 2.4 GHz OQPSK radio 250 kbps] ``` **Role of each layer (2 marks):** - **IEEE 802.15.4** — Low-power radio PHY/MAC, ideal for on-body sensors. - **6LoWPAN** — Compresses IPv6 headers (40 → ~8 bytes) so 802.15.4 frames can carry IP packets. - **RPL** — Maintains a **DODAG** rooted at the body-worn gateway for multi-hop routing through motes. - **UDP** — Lightweight, connectionless transport suited to real-time vitals. - **CoAP** — Application-level REST semantics (`GET /temperature`) over UDP, with **observe** for server push, and **DTLS** security. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **MQTT and CoAP** in terms of architecture, transport, overhead, QoS, and biomedical use cases. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **link layer protocols** — IEEE 802.15.4, BLE, and LoRaWAN — highlighting their **frequency, range, data rate, and power** with one biomedical application each. #### **Model Solution — (a)** — Comparative Table (7 marks) | Parameter | **MQTT** | **CoAP** | |---|---|---| | **Architecture** | Publish/Subscribe (Broker-centric) | Request/Response (RESTful, server–client) | | **Transport** | TCP (reliable, ordered) | UDP (fast, unreliable) | | **Security** | TLS over TCP | DTLS over UDP | | **Header Size** | 2 bytes (fixed) | 4 bytes (binary) | | **Default Port** | 1883 (8883 TLS) | 5683 (5684 DTLS) | | **QoS / Reliability** | 3 levels: 0, 1, 2 | Confirmable (CON) or Non-confirmable (NON) | | **Topology** | Many-to-many via broker | 1-to-1 direct | | **Power Profile** | Higher (TCP keep-alive) | Lower (UDP, low duty cycle) | | **Biomedical Use** | Cloud-pushed vitals, alerts to phones | In-body sensor actuation, in-hospital REST control | #### **Model Solution — (b)** — Link Layer Comparison (7 marks) | Parameter | **IEEE 802.15.4** | **BLE 5.0** | **LoRaWAN** | |---|---|---|---| | **Frequency** | 2.4 / 868 / 915 MHz | 2.4 GHz | 868 / 915 MHz (sub-GHz) | | **Modulation** | O-QPSK / BPSK | GFSK | Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) | | **Range** | 10–100 m | 30–400 m (coded PHY) | 2–10 km (outdoor) | | **Data Rate** | 250 kbps | 2 Mbps | 0.3–50 kbps | | **TX Power** | ~30 mA | ~15 mA | ~100 mA (short bursts) | | **Sleep Current** | ~1 µA | ~1 µA | ~1 µA | | **Biomedical App** | ZigBee WBAN, in-hospital mesh | Wearable ECG/PPG patches | Rural elderly fall detection, ambulance telemetry | > [!WARNING] > **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — DO NOT LOSE MARKS:** > 1. **Mistake:** Writing only protocol *names* without specifying **layer and transport** → loses 2–3 marks. Always say *"MQTT — application layer — over TCP."* > 2. **Mistake:** Confusing **MQTT-SN** (UDP version for sensor networks) with **MQTT** (TCP). State which one. > 3. **Mistake:** Saying *HTTP* is an IoT-specific protocol. It is **not** designed for IoT — be ready to justify why **CoAP** is preferred for constrained nodes. > 4. **Mistake:** Skipping **QoS levels** in MQTT — examiners specifically test this. Memorize **0, 1, 2** and the 4-step handshake for QoS 2. > 5. **Mistake:** Drawing **CoAP** diagram with a **broker** — CoAP has **no broker**, unlike MQTT. Confusing this costs 2 marks. > 6. **Mistake:** Forgetting the **port numbers** (1883 / 8883 for MQTT, 5683 / 5684 for CoAP) — 1 mark is reserved for these in KTU papers. > 7. **Mistake:** Calling **6LoWPAN a routing protocol** — it is an **adaptation layer**, not a routing protocol. RPL is the routing protocol. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember 📌 - **IoT Protocol Stack has 4–5 layers:** Application, Transport, Network, Link, Physical. - **MQTT** = Pub/Sub, **TCP**, port **1883/8883**, header **2 bytes**, **3 QoS levels** (0,1,2), designed by **IBM**, standardized at **OASIS**. - **CoAP** = REST/Req-Resp, **UDP**, port **5683/5684**, header **4 bytes**, RFC 7252, supports **observe pattern**, secured by **DTLS**. - **AMQP** = Heavy, transactional, hospital middleware; **DDS** = real-time, broker-less, surgical robotics; **XMPP** = chat/presence for telemedicine. - **6LoWPAN** = Adaptation layer that compresses **IPv6 header (40 → ~8 bytes)** over **IEEE 802.15.4**. - **RPL** = Routing for **LLNs (Low-Power Lossy Networks)**, builds **DODAG** rooted at sink, RFC 6550. - **802.15.4** = 2.4 GHz, **250 kbps**, basis of ZigBee/6LoWPAN, low power. - **BLE 5.0** = 2.4 GHz, 2 Mbps, range up to **400 m**, low power → ideal for **wearable biomedical sensors**. - **WiFi** = high throughput, high power → for medical imaging / IoT gateways. - **LoRaWAN** = sub-GHz, **2–10 km**, ultra-low power → **rural/remote patient monitoring**. - **NFC** = < 10 cm, passive → **smart pills, device pairing, patient ID**. - **RFID** = up to 10 m, passive/active → **asset and patient tracking in hospitals**. - **TCP** = reliable, heavy; **UDP** = fast, lightweight; **DTLS** = UDP + security. - **Energy per bit is dominated by header overhead** — small payloads benefit hugely from MQTT/CoAP vs HTTP. - Always remember to **state the layer and transport** when naming any protocol in KTU answers.

topics.title: Logical Design of IoT: IoT Communication Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Logical Design of IoT: IoT Communication Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is the Logical Design of IoT?

The **Logical Design of IoT** refers to an abstract representation of the entities and processes involved in an IoT system, independent of specific hardware, vendors, or implementation technologies. It defines *how* the system functions, communicates, and exchanges data using standardized protocols, models, and APIs. Unlike physical design (which deals with sensors, microcontrollers, and circuits), logical design focuses on the **software-level data flow, communication patterns, and services** that allow heterogeneous IoT devices to interoperate seamlessly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Logical design is the foundation upon which all IoT architectures are built. It encompasses **IoT Communication Models, IoT Communication APIs, and Web Service architectures (REST, SOAP)**.

### 1.2 What are IoT Communication Models?

An **IoT Communication Model** is a standardized, abstract pattern that governs **how two or more IoT entities (devices, services, applications) exchange data** over the network. It defines the **direction, timing, and style** of communication without dictating the underlying transport protocol.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> *IoT Communication Models are logical templates that describe the roles and interaction patterns between IoT system components — namely Sensors/Actuators (Things), Gateways, Cloud Services, and Applications — for the purpose of reliable and structured data exchange.*

### 1.3 The Four Primary IoT Communication Models

| # | Model | One-Line Definition |
|---|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Request–Response Model** | A consumer sends a request and waits synchronously for a response. |
| 2 | **Publish–Subscribe Model** | Publishers push messages to a broker; subscribers receive only those messages of interest. |
| 3 | **Push–Pull Model** | Producers push data into a queue; consumers pull data at their own pace. |
| 4 | **Exclusive Pair Model** | A dedicated, stateful, bidirectional connection is established between two endpoints. |

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> [!TIP]
> **Think of an IoT Communication Model as a "postal rulebook" between two offices.**

- **Request–Response** → You walk to the post office, hand over a form, and wait at the counter for the clerk to give you the parcel. (Synchronous)
- **Publish–Subscribe** → You *subscribe* to a newspaper; the *publisher* delivers only your preferred sections to your door automatically. (Asynchronous, filtered)
- **Push–Pull** → Trucks *push* boxes into a warehouse loading dock; workers *pull* them out whenever they are free. (Decoupled)
- **Exclusive Pair** → A dedicated, always-open *private telephone hotline* between the manager and a single trusted engineer. (Stateful, bidirectional)

### 1.5 Why are Communication Models Critical in IoT?

1. **Interoperability** — A smart ECG sensor manufactured in India must talk to a hospital cloud in the US. Communication models make this possible.
2. **Scalability** — A hospital may deploy **10,000+ biosensors**; only a well-chosen model prevents data collisions.
3. **Resource Efficiency** — Battery-powered medical wearables cannot run full TCP servers; the model dictates whether the device is a *client*, *publisher*, or *peer*.
4. **Real-time vs. Batch Processing** — A ventilator alarm needs push-based delivery; periodic vitals uploads use request–response.
5. **Decoupling** — Separating the *thing* from the *application* allows independent upgrades without redesigning the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomedical Context (KTU OEEBT831):** In a remote patient monitoring system, the wearable ECG patch is a *publisher* (Publish–Subscribe), while the doctor's dashboard pulling historical reports is a *consumer* (Request–Response). A ventilator's critical alarm uses *Exclusive Pair* for guaranteed delivery.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Analysis of the Four IoT Communication Models

#### 🔹 Model 1: Request–Response Communication Model

**How it works:**
1. The **Client** sends a *request* message to the **Server**.
2. The server processes the request.
3. The server returns a *response* message.
4. The client receives the response and the connection is logically closed (in HTTP/1.1 non-keep-alive) or reused (in keep-alive).

**Why use it?**
- Best suited for **on-demand data retrieval** (e.g., doctor's dashboard querying the latest patient report).
- Stateless by default → easy to scale horizontally.
- Strongly tied to **RESTful APIs** using HTTP methods: `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`.

**How it works internally:**
- The client must know the server's **endpoint URL** in advance.
- The server may queue or reject requests when overloaded.
- The client *blocks* (waits) until the response arrives or a timeout occurs.

**Pros:**
- Simple to understand and debug.
- Caching-friendly (HTTP `GET` responses can be cached).
- Wide tooling support (cURL, Postman, browser DevTools).

**Cons:**
- Not ideal for high-frequency, real-time sensor streams.
- Synchronous blocking wastes client resources during long waits.

**Real-world biomedical example:** A cardiologist's mobile app fetching the last 24-hour ECG record of a patient from the hospital cloud.

#### 🔹 Model 2: Publish–Subscribe Communication Model

**How it works:**
1. A central **Broker** (e.g., MQTT broker, Kafka) mediates all communication.
2. **Publishers** send messages to a named *topic* on the broker.
3. **Subscribers** register interest in specific *topics* with the broker.
4. The broker **filters** and **forwards** relevant messages to interested subscribers only.

**Why use it?**
- Decouples publishers from subscribers — neither needs to know the other.
- Highly scalable: millions of devices can share one broker.
- Supports **topic-based filtering** (e.g., `hospital/ward5/bed12/ecg`) and **content-based filtering**.

**How it works internally:**
- Three roles: **Publisher**, **Subscriber**, **Broker**.
- The broker maintains a *topic subscription table*:
  
  | Topic Pattern | Subscribed Client |
  |---------------|-------------------|
  | `hospital/+/ecg` | Dashboard A, App B |
  | `hospital/ward5/#` | Nurse Station 5 |

- QoS levels (in MQTT): 0 (at most once), 1 (at least once), 2 (exactly once).

**Pros:**
- Asynchronous, non-blocking.
- Efficient multicast-like behavior over unreliable networks (e.g., LPWAN, cellular for remote wearables).
- Built-in fan-out to multiple consumers.

**Cons:**
- Single point of failure (broker must be highly available).
- More complex to debug and secure (topic ACLs required).

**Real-world biomedical example:** A continuous glucose monitor (CGM) publishing glucose readings every 5 minutes to a broker; the patient's phone app, doctor's dashboard, and emergency alert system all subscribe to relevant topics.

#### 🔹 Model 3: Push–Pull Communication Model

**How it works:**
1. **Producers (Pushers)** write data into a **Message Queue** (e.g., RabbitMQ, Kafka, Redis Streams).
2. **Consumers (Pullers)** retrieve data from the queue at their own processing pace.
3. The queue acts as a **buffer** between fast producers and slow consumers.
4. Once a consumer pulls a message, it is typically **acknowledged** and removed (or marked as committed).

**Why use it?**
- **Decouples speed** between sensor data arrival and processing.
- Provides **durability** — data survives consumer crashes.
- Supports **load leveling** and **backpressure** (consumers pull only when ready).

**How it works internally:**
- Producer API: `queue.push(message)`
- Consumer API: `queue.pull(timeout=N)` or `queue.consume(callback)`
- Queues may follow **FIFO** (First-In-First-Out) or **priority** ordering.

**Pros:**
- Resilient to consumer downtime (messages persist).
- Excellent for batch processing pipelines (e.g., ML model training on biosignals).
- Natural backpressure handling.

**Cons:**
- Higher latency than pub–sub (consumer must poll).
- Not suitable for instant notifications without an additional push layer.

**Real-world biomedical example:** A wearable EEG headband pushing raw EEG samples to a Kafka queue; a cloud ML pipeline pulls the data nightly to train seizure-detection models.

#### 🔹 Model 4: Exclusive Pair (Full-Duplex) Communication Model

**How it works:**
1. A **persistent, stateful, bidirectional** connection is established between exactly **two endpoints**.
2. Both parties can send messages at any time in **either direction** (full-duplex).
3. The connection is kept **always-on** using heartbeats/keep-alives.
4. Either party can terminate the connection explicitly.

**Why use it?**
- Required for **real-time, low-latency, bi-directional** control loops.
- Essential for **actuator control** (e.g., remotely adjusting an insulin pump's flow rate).
- Maintains **session state** (e.g., authentication, negotiated parameters).

**How it works internally:**
- Underlying transport: **WebSockets** (over TCP), **Bluetooth BR/EDR**, **Serial**.
- Each side has a unique session ID.
- Messages are framed and may be text (JSON) or binary.

**Pros:**
- Lowest latency among all four models.
- Stateful — server can remember device context.
- Enables server-pushed commands to actuators.

**Cons:**
- Resource-intensive (one TCP socket per device on the server).
- Hard to scale to millions (requires connection pooling, sharding).
- Connection loss handling is non-trivial (reconnect, re-authenticate, resume).

**Real-world biomedical example:** A remote robotic surgery console maintains a WebSocket with the robotic arm controller; the surgeon receives real-time haptic feedback and sends micro-movements continuously.

---

### 2.2 Comparative Analysis Matrix (Cheat Sheet for KTU Board)

| Parameter | Request–Response | Publish–Subscribe | Push–Pull | Exclusive Pair |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Architecture** | Client–Server | Broker-mediated | Queue-mediated | Peer-to-Peer |
| **Coupling** | Tight (URL known) | Loose (topic-based) | Loose (queue-based) | Tight (stateful) |
| **Synchrony** | Synchronous | Asynchronous | Asynchronous | Asynchronous / Synchronous |
| **Direction** | Bi-directional (request→response) | One-way push + filtered fan-out | One-way push, decoupled pull | Full-duplex (bi-directional) |
| **Number of endpoints** | 2 (1:1) | N (N:M via broker) | N (M producers, K consumers) | 2 (1:1) |
| **State** | Stateless | Stateless (per message) | Stateful (durable queue) | Stateful (persistent session) |
| **Latency** | Medium | Low | Medium–High | **Lowest** |
| **Scalability** | High (load-balanced) | **Very High** | High | Limited |
| **Best For** | On-demand queries | Event broadcasting | Reliable data pipelines | Real-time control |
| **Common Protocol** | HTTP/REST, CoAP | **MQTT, AMQP, Kafka** | Kafka, RabbitMQ, Redis | **WebSockets, Bluetooth** |
| **Biomedical Example** | Fetch patient report | Stream vitals to dashboard | Batch upload of EEG for ML | Remote robotic surgery control |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Equation Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although IoT communication models are largely conceptual, several **quantitative performance metrics** are frequently asked in KTU exams. Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute value to avoid table breakage.

| Metric | Formula | Units | Description |
|--------|---------|-------|-------------|
| **Throughput (T)** | $T = \frac{N_{msg}}{t_{total}}$ | messages/second | Messages successfully delivered per unit time. |
| **End-to-End Latency (L)** | $L = t_{receive} - t_{send}$ | milliseconds (ms) | Time from message creation to delivery. |
| **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** | $RTT = t_{req \to res}$ | ms | Specific to Request–Response model. |
| **Packet Loss Probability (P_loss)** | $P_{loss} = 1 - \frac{N_{delivered}}{N_{sent}}$ | dimensionless | Reliability metric. |
| **Message Delivery Ratio (MDR)** | $MDR = \frac{N_{delivered}}{N_{sent}}$ | dimensionless | $\in [0, 1]$; higher is better. |
| **Broker Fan-out Factor (F)** | $F = N_{subscribers\_per\_topic}$ | count | Multiplier effect in pub–sub. |
| **Queue Depth (Q)** | $Q(t) = \lambda t - \mu t$ | messages | Where $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $\mu$ = service rate. |
| **Little's Law** | $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | messages | Avg. items in queue = arrival rate × avg. wait time. |
| **MQTT QoS Effective Reliability** | $R_{QoS=2} \approx 1$ | dimensionless | Exactly-once delivery. |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** For a hospital deploying **10,000 biosensors** publishing every second to an MQTT broker, the broker must sustain $T \geq 10{,}000$ msg/s. Using **Little's Law**, if average processing time per message is $W = 5\,\text{ms}$, then the broker must hold $L = 10{,}000 \times 0.005 = 50$ concurrent messages on average.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Hospitals:** Combine all four models — REST for clinician apps, MQTT for wearables, Kafka for EHR pipelines, WebSockets for ICU dashboards.
- **Smart Cities:** Streetlight controllers use **CoAP** (Request–Response) for status queries; air-quality sensors use **MQTT** (Pub–Sub) for alerts.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Factory robots use **OPC UA over WebSockets** (Exclusive Pair) for deterministic control loops.
- **Connected Vehicles:** Telematics use **MQTT-SN** (Pub–Sub) over cellular; infotainment uses **HTTP/2** (Request–Response).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section derives **quantitative relationships** and **decision frameworks** relevant to IoT communication model selection — a high-yield area for KTU 14-mark questions.

### 3.1 Derivation: Latency Comparison Between Models

We derive an **analytical expression for end-to-end latency** of each model, decomposed into network and processing components.

**Step 1 — Define Components:**

Let:
- $t_{prop}$ = propagation delay (signal travel time over the link)
- $t_{trans}$ = transmission delay (size of message / bandwidth)
- $t_{proc}$ = processing delay (broker/server compute time)
- $t_{queue}$ = queuing delay (waiting in buffer)
- $t_{ack}$ = acknowledgement delay

**Step 2 — Request–Response Latency:**

$$L_{RR} = 2 \cdot (t_{prop} + t_{trans}) + 2 \cdot t_{proc} + t_{ack}$$

*Reasoning:* The request travels one way, gets processed, the response travels back, and an acknowledgement closes the loop. Hence **two round trips** are involved in HTTP/1.1 with keep-alive.

**Step 3 — Publish–Subscribe Latency:**

$$L_{PS} = t_{prop} + t_{trans} + t_{broker} + t_{prop} + t_{trans}$$

$$\therefore L_{PS} = 2 \cdot (t_{prop} + t_{trans}) + t_{broker}$$

*Reasoning:* One publish, one broker filter-and-forward, one delivery. **No acknowledgement** in QoS 0; QoS 1/2 add an additional RTT.

**Step 4 — Push–Pull Latency:**

$$L_{PP} = t_{prop} + t_{trans} + t_{queue} + t_{consume}$$

*Reasoning:* Producer pushes, message sits in queue, consumer pulls when ready. **Queuing delay dominates**.

**Step 5 — Exclusive Pair Latency:**

$$L_{EP} = t_{prop} + t_{trans} + t_{proc}$$

*Reasoning:* Persistent connection → no handshake overhead per message. **Lowest latency** because the TCP+TLS handshake is amortized over the entire session lifetime.

**Step 6 — Comparative Ordering (assuming equal network):**

$$L_{EP} < L_{PS} \leq L_{RR} < L_{PP}$$

This ordering justifies the use of **Exclusive Pair** for real-time control and **Push–Pull** for batch analytics.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Scalability Limit of Exclusive Pair Model

A server can handle only a finite number of concurrent WebSocket connections. Let us derive this limit.

**Step 1 — Assume a single server machine with:**
- Total file descriptors available: $FD_{max} = 65{,}535$ (Linux default)
- Each WebSocket consumes 1 file descriptor per client
- Plus overhead for database connections, OS resources: utilization factor $u = 0.7$

**Step 2 — Maximum concurrent clients:**

$$N_{max} = FD_{max} \cdot u$$

$$N_{max} = 65{,}535 \times 0.7 = 45{,}874 \text{ clients}$$

**Step 3 — Required servers for $N$ total clients:**

$$S_{req} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{N_{max}} \right\rceil$$

$$\therefore S_{req} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{45{,}874} \right\rceil$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example:**

If a hospital wants to monitor **$N = 500{,}000$** ICU beds in real time via WebSockets:

$$S_{req} = \left\lceil \frac{500{,}000}{45{,}874} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 10.9 \right\rceil = 11 \text{ servers}$$

*Conclusion:* This is why real-time control at scale often switches to **Pub–Sub with WebSockets as the transport between broker and dashboard**, but **MQTT for the device-to-broker leg**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Model (Decision Tree)

The following decision rules are derived from the comparative analysis in §2.2.

| If the application requires… | Then choose… | Protocol Stack |
|------------------------------|--------------|----------------|
| On-demand data fetch (REST semantics) | **Request–Response** | HTTP/REST, CoAP |
| 1-to-many fan-out, decoupled producers/consumers | **Publish–Subscribe** | MQTT, AMQP, Kafka |
| Durable buffering + batch processing | **Push–Pull** | Kafka, RabbitMQ, Redis Streams |
| Real-time bi-directional control, <50 ms latency | **Exclusive Pair** | WebSockets, Bluetooth, OPC UA |
| Battery-powered device, low bandwidth, intermittent connectivity | **Publish–Subscribe** (MQTT-SN) | MQTT-SN over LoRa/NB-IoT |
| Critical alarm (loss unacceptable) | **Publish–Subscribe (QoS 2)** or **Exclusive Pair** | MQTT QoS 2, WebSockets + ACK |
| Periodic small reports (e.g., hourly vitals summary) | **Request–Response** | HTTP POST / CoAP PUT |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: How an MQTT Pub–Sub Cycle Works

This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark derivation-style question**. We walk through it explicitly.

**Step 1 — Device Boot-up**
The wearable ECG patch boots, connects to Wi-Fi, and opens a TCP socket to the MQTT broker at `mqtt.hospital.com:1883`.

**Step 2 — CONNECT Handshake**
The device sends a `CONNECT` packet containing `clientId = "ecg_patch_bed12"`, `cleanSession=true`, `keepAlive=60`.

The broker replies with `CONNACK` (Connection Accepted).

**Step 3 — SUBSCRIBE**
The nurse's dashboard app sends `SUBSCRIBE` packet with topic filter `hospital/ward5/bed12/ecg` and QoS=1.

The broker stores the subscription in its topic tree:
$$
\text{Topic Tree} = \left\{ \text{"hospital/ward5/bed12/ecg"} \mapsto [\text{dashboard\_app}] \right\}
$$

**Step 4 — PUBLISH (Repeated Loop)**
Every 1 second, the ECG patch sends:
```
PUBLISH topic="hospital/ward5/bed12/ecg" QoS=1 payload="{"hr":78,"qrs":...}"
```

**Step 5 — Broker Forwards**
The broker matches the topic, looks up subscribers, and forwards the payload to `dashboard_app` with a `PUBACK` round-trip for QoS 1 acknowledgement.

**Step 6 — Last Will and Testament (LWT)**
At `CONNECT`, the device registered a LWT message: *"patch disconnected unexpectedly"* on topic `hospital/ward5/bed12/status`. If the broker detects a missed keep-alive, it auto-publishes the LWT to notify nurses.

**Step 7 — DISCONNECT**
On normal shutdown, the device sends `DISCONNECT` → broker cleans up session.

This 7-step cycle is **the most commonly drawn exam diagram** in KTU Module 1.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the four basic IoT communication models with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four basic IoT communication models are:

1. **Request–Response Model:** A client sends a request to a server and waits for a response. Example: A doctor's mobile app fetching patient records from a hospital REST API using `GET /patient/12`.

2. **Publish–Subscribe Model:** Publishers send messages to a broker on named topics; subscribers receive only those topics they registered for. Example: An ECG wearable patch publishing heart-rate data via MQTT to a broker, which fans it out to the dashboard and alert system.

3. **Push–Pull Model:** Producers push messages into a queue; consumers pull them out at their own pace. Example: A wearable EEG headband pushing raw EEG samples to a Kafka queue, which a cloud ML pipeline pulls nightly.

4. **Exclusive Pair Model:** A persistent, stateful, full-duplex connection exists between exactly two endpoints. Example: A remote robotic surgery console maintaining a WebSocket with the robotic arm controller for real-time haptic feedback.

*(Each model: 0.5 mark definition + 0.25 mark example = 0.75 × 4 = 3 marks)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between Publish–Subscribe and Push–Pull communication models.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Publish–Subscribe | Push–Pull |
|--------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Mediator** | Topic-based broker (e.g., MQTT) | Message queue (e.g., Kafka) |
| **Filtering** | Broker filters by topic/content | No filtering; consumer gets all queued messages |
| **Consumer Count** | Multiple subscribers per topic | Multiple consumers share a queue (competing) |
| **Message Retention** | Not retained by default (except retained flag) | Retained until consumer acknowledges |
| **Latency** | Low (push-based) | Higher (consumer must poll) |
| **Biomedical Use** | Real-time vitals to dashboard | Batch EEG data to ML pipeline |

*(1 mark for each valid difference × 3 differences = 3 marks)*
