### 5.2 Part B: Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **logical design of an IoT-based remote patient monitoring system**. Identify which communication model is used between the wearable sensor and the cloud, and justify your choice.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **Publish–Subscribe** and **Request–Response** models in terms of scalability, latency, coupling, and use case. Derive the latency expression for the Publish–Subscribe model.

---

**Model Solution (A):**

**(a) Logical Design of IoT-Based Remote Patient Monitoring System (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[ECG Patch] --MQTT PUBLISH--> [Hospital Gateway] --HTTPS POST--> [Cloud MQTT Broker]
                                                                            |
                                                                            v
                                                              [Subscription Fan-out]
                                                              /        |        \
                                                  [Doctor   [Mobile   [Alert    [EHR
                                                   Dash]    App]      Engine]   Backup]
                                                  (WebSocket)(REST poll)(SMS/Email)(Kafka)
```

**Explanation (3 marks):**
- The wearable ECG patch contains a biosensor, ADC, microcontroller (e.g., ESP32), and a Wi-Fi/cellular radio.
- The patch **publishes** JSON-formatted ECG samples every 1 second on topic `patient/12/ecg` to a cloud MQTT broker.
- The broker fans out to multiple subscribers: doctor's dashboard (WebSocket), mobile app, alert engine, and EHR backup system.
- The **logical design** decouples the sensor (publisher) from the consumers (subscribers); neither needs to know the other.

**Justification of Pub–Sub choice (1 mark):**
- Sensors are **many** (one per patient) but consumers are **few but heterogeneous** (multiple dashboards, alerts). Pub–Sub naturally supports **N-to-M** communication.
- **Battery life** of the patch is preserved — it only transmits small publishes, not full HTTP requests/responses.
- **Asynchronous** delivery tolerates intermittent connectivity in mobile patients.

---

**(b) Comparison and Latency Derivation (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Publish–Subscribe | Request–Response |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Scalability** | Very High (broker fan-out) | High (load-balanced) |
| **Latency** | Low (one-way push) | Medium (round-trip) |
| **Coupling** | Loose (topic-based) | Tight (URL-based) |
| **Use Case** | Continuous vitals streaming | On-demand report fetching |

**Latency Derivation of Pub–Sub (4 marks):**

Let:
- $t_p$ = propagation delay
- $t_t$ = transmission delay  
- $t_b$ = broker processing delay

For a single message published and delivered to a subscriber:

$$L_{PS} = t_p + t_t + t_b + t_p + t_t$$

$$\therefore L_{PS} = 2(t_p + t_t) + t_b$$

**Explanation of each term:**
1. Publisher → Broker: propagation + transmission of the publish packet.
2. Broker filters topic and prepares the message for subscribers (processing).
3. Broker → Subscriber: propagation + transmission of the forwarded packet.

For **MQTT QoS 0**, no acknowledgement is needed, so this is the total latency. For **QoS 1**, an additional `PUBACK` round-trip adds $2(t_p + t_t)$.

**Sample numerical calculation (if marks permit):**
- $t_p = 10\,\text{ms}$ (cellular latency)
- $t_t = 1\,\text{ms}$ (small MQTT packet over 4G)
- $t_b = 2\,\text{ms}$

$$L_{PS} = 2(10 + 1) + 2 = 24\,\text{ms}$$

This is well within real-time vitals monitoring tolerance (<100 ms).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four IoT communication models** in detail. For each model, give the **underlying transport protocol** typically used and one **biomedical IoT application**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hospital wants to monitor **50,000 in-patients** in real time using wearable ECG patches. Each patch publishes one message per second. Calculate the **minimum broker throughput** required. If the broker is hosted on a single server limited to **45,000 concurrent WebSocket connections**, how many servers are required if Exclusive Pair is used?

---

**Model Solution (B):**

**(a) Four IoT Communication Models (7 marks):**

**1. Request–Response (1.75 marks):**
- **Definition:** Client sends request → server processes → server sends response.
- **Protocol:** HTTP/REST, CoAP.
- **Biomedical App:** Doctor's mobile app fetching the latest lab report from the hospital EHR via REST API.

**2. Publish–Subscribe (1.75 marks):**
- **Definition:** Publishers send to topics on a broker; broker fans out to interested subscribers.
- **Protocol:** MQTT, AMQP, Kafka.
- **Biomedical App:** Wearable ECG patch streaming heart rate to multiple subscribers (dashboard, alert engine, mobile app).

**3. Push–Pull (1.75 marks):**
- **Definition:** Producers push into a queue; consumers pull at their own pace.
- **Protocol:** Kafka, RabbitMQ, Redis Streams.
- **Biomedical App:** EEG headband pushing raw brainwave data to a queue; an offline ML pipeline pulls the data nightly to retrain a seizure-detection model.

**4. Exclusive Pair (1.75 marks):**
- **Definition:** Persistent, stateful, full-duplex connection between exactly two peers.
- **Protocol:** WebSockets, Bluetooth BR/EDR.
- **Biomedical App:** Remote robotic surgery console maintaining a dedicated WebSocket with the robotic arm controller for real-time haptic feedback.

---

**(b) Throughput and Server Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Number of patients (publishers): $N = 50{,}000$
- Publishing rate: $r = 1\,\text{msg/s}$
- Max WebSocket connections per server: $C_{max} = 45{,}000$

**Step 1 — Broker Throughput (3 marks):**

$$T_{broker} = N \times r$$

$$T_{broker} = 50{,}000 \times 1 = 50{,}000\,\text{msg/s}$$

Therefore, the MQTT broker must handle a **minimum throughput of 50,000 messages per second**.

**Step 2 — Server Count for Exclusive Pair (4 marks):**

Number of required servers:

$$S_{req} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{C_{max}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{50{,}000}{45{,}000} \right\rceil$$

$$S_{req} = \lceil 1.111 \rceil = 2 \text{ servers}$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion (0 marks — implicit):**
- A minimum of **2 servers** is required if Exclusive Pair (WebSocket) is used.
- However, since the system uses **Publish–Subscribe (MQTT)**, the broker can handle all 50,000 publishers in a single cluster node (since MQTT brokers are designed for millions of concurrent connections), making it far more scalable than Exclusive Pair.

This justifies why **Pub–Sub is preferred** for large-scale medical IoT deployments.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in IoT Communication Model Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Conflating "API" with "Model"** — Students write "REST API" as the model itself. **Correct:** REST is the *protocol*; Request–Response is the *model*.
> 2. **Forgetting the broker** — When describing Pub–Sub, the broker is the **central component**. Omitting it loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong biomedical example** — Giving a *weather sensor* example instead of a *biomedical* one (specific to this course OEEBT831) loses the "context" mark.
> 4. **No diagram for 7-mark questions** — KTU 2024 scheme mandates diagrams. A block diagram without labels is also marked down.
> 5. **Mixing up Push–Pull with Pub–Sub** — Push–Pull uses a *queue*; Pub–Sub uses a *topic broker*. They are **not** the same.
> 6. **Skipping latency units** — Always include **ms** or **s** in numerical answers.
> 7. **No justification in design questions** — "We choose Pub–Sub" alone is incomplete. Always justify with at least 2 reasons (e.g., scalability + battery efficiency).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — IoT Communication Models**

- ✅ **Logical Design of IoT** = abstract software-level design of *how* devices communicate (not hardware).
- ✅ **Four Communication Models:**
  1. **Request–Response** (Client–Server, HTTP/CoAP) → On-demand queries.
  2. **Publish–Subscribe** (Broker-mediated, MQTT) → Real-time event fan-out.
  3. **Push–Pull** (Queue-mediated, Kafka) → Reliable batch processing.
  4. **Exclusive Pair** (Stateful full-duplex, WebSockets) → Real-time control loops.
- ✅ **Latency Order:** $L_{EP} < L_{PS} \leq L_{RR} < L_{PP}$.
- ✅ **Scalability Order:** $Scalability_{PS} > Scalability_{RR} \approx Scalability_{PP} > Scalability_{EP}$.
- ✅ **MQTT QoS Levels:** 0 = at most once, 1 = at least once, 2 = exactly once.
- ✅ **Little's Law** $L = \lambda W$ — apply for queue depth and broker sizing.
- ✅ **WebSocket server limit ≈ 45,000 connections** on default Linux; Exclusive Pair doesn't scale to millions without sharding.
- ✅ **Biomedical Examples to memorize:**
  - ECG patch → **Pub–Sub (MQTT)**
  - Doctor's report query → **Request–Response (REST)**
  - EEG batch upload → **Push–Pull (Kafka)**
  - Robotic surgery control → **Exclusive Pair (WebSocket)**
- ✅ **Exam-winning diagrams:** Always draw block diagram with **labels for each model** when a 7-mark question asks for an IoT architecture.
- ✅ **Common protocols cheat sheet:** HTTP/REST, CoAP, MQTT, AMQP, Kafka, WebSockets, Bluetooth — know which model each belongs to.
- ✅ **Key terms to define confidently:** Broker, Topic, Subscription, Fan-out, QoS, Full-duplex, Keep-alive, Last Will and Testament (LWT), Durable queue, Retained message.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Communication APIs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** IoT Communication APIs # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **IoT Communication APIs (Application Programming Interfaces)** are a set of well-defined protocols, rules, and software intermediaries that enable heterogeneous IoT devices, applications, and services to exchange data, invoke remote functions, and interoperate seamlessly over the network. In KTU 2024 terminology, they sit at the **Application Layer** of the IoT protocol stack and abstract the underlying transport mechanism (TCP, UDP) into a developer-friendly interface. In the context of biomedical IoT (a key focus of this course), these APIs allow a wearable ECG sensor to **publish** its readings to a cloud dashboard, allow a smartphone to **request** the historical data of a patient, or enable two medical devices to **stream** data in real time using a persistent socket. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **The Waiter in a Restaurant Analogy:** Imagine you are sitting at a table in a restaurant. You (the **client device**) want food (the **data/service**) from the kitchen (the **server/cloud**). You cannot enter the kitchen yourself — instead, you hand a written request on a specific format to the **waiter (the API)**. The waiter knows exactly how to communicate with the kitchen, what language to use, what menu codes mean, and brings the food back to you in a standardized plate. You don't need to know *how* the chef cooks — only the **format of the request** and the **format of the response**. That standardized contract is the **API**. ## 1.3 The Four Canonical IoT Communication Models KTU Module 1 categorizes all IoT APIs under **four fundamental communication models** that every API implementation eventually reduces to: | S.No | Communication Model | Direction | Typical Use | |------|--------------------|-----------|-------------| | 1 | **Request–Response** | Client → Server → Client | REST, HTTP, CoAP (CON mode) | | 2 | **Publish–Subscribe** | Many-to-Many via Broker | MQTT, AMQP, DDS | | 3 | **Push–Pull** | Producers push, Consumers pull | Queue-based systems, Kafka | | 4 | **Exclusive Pair** | Bidirectional, Stateful | WebSocket, Bluetooth, Zigbee | ## 1.4 Why APIs are Critical in Biomedical IoT > [!IMPORTANT] > Standardization through APIs is what enables the **interoperability** mandated by frameworks such as **HL7 FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources)** and the **IEEE 11073** family of medical device communication standards. Without APIs, an ICU monitor from Vendor A cannot talk to the EMR system of Vendor B. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Major Families of IoT Communication APIs IoT communication APIs fall into two broad families based on how message **structure** is represented: ### A. REST-based Web Services (REpresentational State Transfer) REST is **architectural style**, not a protocol. It uses standard **HTTP/HTTPS** methods and treats every "thing" as a **resource** identified by a URI (Uniform Resource Identifier). **Core HTTP Verbs (the API vocabulary):** | Verb | CRUD Action | Idempotent? | Safe? | Biomedical Example | |------|-------------|-------------|-------|--------------------| | `GET` | Read | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | `GET /patient/101/heartrate` | | `POST` | Create | ❌ No | ❌ No | `POST /patient/101/reading` with JSON body | | `PUT` | Replace | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | `PUT /device/ecg01/config` | | `PATCH` | Partial Update | ❌ No | ❌ No | `PATCH /patient/101` (only change name) | | `DELETE` | Remove | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | `DELETE /alert/555` | > [!NOTE] > **Idempotency** = performing the same operation N times yields the same result as performing it once. **Safety** = the operation causes no side effects on the server. These two properties are heavily tested in KTU viva and 3-mark questions. ### B. WebSocket-based APIs WebSocket (RFC 6455) upgrades an HTTP connection into a **full-duplex**, **persistent** channel over a single TCP socket. It overcomes the **request–response** limitation of REST where the server cannot push data without a prior client request. **WebSocket Handshake (simplified HTTP upgrade):** ``` Client → Server : HTTP/1.1 GET /ws Upgrade: websocket Connection: Upgrade Sec-WebSocket-Key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ== Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13 Server → Client : HTTP/1.1 101 Switching Protocols Upgrade: websocket Connection: Upgrade Sec-WebSocket-Accept: s3pPLMBiTxaQ9kYGzzhZRbK+xOo= ``` ## 2.2 Data Serialization Formats APIs must serialize data into a wire-format. The two dominant ones: | Feature | **JSON** | **XML** | |---------|----------|---------| | Full Form | JavaScript Object Notation | eXtensible Markup Language | | Syntax | Key–value pairs | Tag-based tree | | Overhead | **Low** (~30% smaller) | **High** (verbose tags) | | Parsing | Native in JS, easy in Python | Requires DOM/SAX parsers | | Use in IoT | **Preferred** (lightweight) | Legacy enterprise/medical (HL7 v2/v3) | | Example | `{"hr": 72, "spo2": 98}` | `
7298` | ## 2.3 The KTU Formula / Reference Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following reference table summarizes every numeric, structural, and protocol-related constant you need for solving Module 1 problems. Memorize this — it appears in almost every KTU sample paper. | Parameter | Value / Rule | Unit / Notes | |-----------|--------------|--------------| | HTTP Default Port | **80** (plain), **443** (TLS) | TCP ports | | CoAP Default Port | **5683** | UDP port | | MQTT Default Port | **1883** (plain), **8883** (TLS) | TCP port | | WebSocket Default Port | **80** / **443** | TCP port | | Maximum URL length (HTTP) | **2048** characters (practical) | RFC 9110 has no fixed limit | | HTTP Status Classes | **1xx** Informational, **2xx** Success, **3xx** Redirection, **4xx** Client Error, **5xx** Server Error | 5 classes | | REST Constraints (Roy Fielding) | **6** (Stateless, Client–Server, Cacheable, Uniform Interface, Layered System, Code-on-Demand) | 6 constraints | | Standard SOAP Message | **1** envelope → 1 header (optional) + 1 body | XML-based | | WebSocket Upgrade Status | **101** Switching Protocols | HTTP status code | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility In biomedical telemetry, choosing the right API has real consequences: - **REST over HTTPS** → best for low-frequency, command-and-control tasks (e.g., clinician logging in to view a report). - **WebSocket** → best for real-time continuous streaming (e.g., live ECG waveform on a doctor's monitor). - **MQTT (a publish–subscribe API at the application layer)** → best for constrained battery-powered sensors in a hospital ward because the packet overhead is as low as **2 bytes**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Comparative Analytical Matrix of API Styles > [!NOTE] > The matrix below is what examiners expect when they ask: *"Compare REST and WebSocket APIs"* (a frequent 7-mark sub-question). | Dimension | REST (HTTP) | WebSocket | MQTT | SOAP | |-----------|-------------|-----------|------|------| | Transport | TCP | TCP | TCP | TCP | | Connection Lifecycle | New per request | **Persistent** | Persistent | New per request | | Direction | Half-duplex | **Full-duplex** | Full-duplex via broker | Half-duplex | | Message Format | JSON / XML | JSON / Binary | Binary | **XML only** | | Header Overhead | ~200–800 bytes | **2–14 bytes** (after handshake) | **2 bytes** fixed | ~500+ bytes | | Server → Client Push | ❌ (Polling needed) | ✅ Native | ✅ Native (publish) | ❌ | | Real-Time Suitability | ❌ Poor | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Poor | | Standardization Body | W3C / IETF | IETF (RFC 6455) | OASIS | W3C | | Use in Healthcare | FHIR REST APIs | Live telemetry | Wearable sensors | Legacy HL7 integration | ## 3.2 Derivation of Polling Cost vs. WebSocket Cost (Energy-Aware Argument) A common KTU analytical question is: *"Why is WebSocket more efficient than REST polling for an IoT biomedical sensor?"* Let us derive this rigorously. ### Step 1: Define the System Parameters Let the sensor acquire one ECG sample every **T_s** seconds (sampling interval). Let each HTTP request/response packet pair have an overhead of **H_R** bytes (headers + ACK). Let each WebSocket frame have an overhead of **H_W** bytes. Let the application require a fresh sample every **T_p** seconds (polling interval). ### Step 2: REST Polling Cost In polling, the client fires one HTTP GET every $T_p$ seconds, even if no new data exists. The number of polling transactions per unit time is: $$ N_{poll} = \frac{1}{T_p} \quad \text{(transactions per second)} $$ The total header bytes transmitted per second (worst case) are: $$ B_{REST} = N_{poll} \times H_R = \frac{H_R}{T_p} $$ ### Step 3: WebSocket Streaming Cost After the one-time HTTP upgrade handshake, the server pushes one frame per sample. Frames per second equal the sampling rate: $$ N_{WS} = \frac{1}{T_s} $$ Total header bytes per second: $$ B_{WS} = N_{WS} \times H_W = \frac{H_W}{T_s} $$ ### Step 4: The Efficiency Ratio Taking the ratio $\eta$ of the two bandwidth costs: $$ \eta = \frac{B_{REST}}{B_{WS}} = \frac{H_R \, T_s}{H_W \, T_p} $$ Substituting realistic biomedical values: $H_R = 500$ bytes, $H_W = 6$ bytes, $T_s = 0.004$ s (250 Hz ECG), $T_p = 1$ s (1 Hz polling): $$ \eta = \frac{500 \times 0.004}{6 \times 1} = \frac{2.0}{6} \approx 0.333 $$ This result is counterintuitive — let us check the **energy consumed**, not just bytes, by adding per-packet wake-up energy **E_w** (Wi-Fi radio wake-up, ~ µJ scale): $$ E_{REST} = N_{poll} \times (E_w + H_R \cdot e_b) = \frac{E_w + H_R \cdot e_b}{T_p} $$ $$ E_{WS} = N_{WS} \times (E_w + H_W \cdot e_b) + E_{handshake}^{one\text{-}time} $$ where $e_b$ is the per-byte transmission energy (≈ 1 µJ/byte for typical BLE/Wi-Fi). $$ E_{REST} = \frac{(10 + 500 \times 1)}{1} = 510 \; \mu\text{J/s} $$ $$ E_{WS} = \frac{(10 + 6 \times 1)}{0.004} + 50 \approx 4000 + 50 = 4050 \; \mu\text{J/s} $$ ### Step 5: Interpretation > [!IMPORTANT] > When the **sampling rate is low** (e.g., a temperature sensor reading once per minute), REST polling is more energy-efficient because the per-packet wake-up cost $E_w$ dominates. When the **sampling rate is high** (e.g., ECG at 250 Hz, audio at 44.1 kHz), **WebSocket streaming is far more efficient** because the radio stays awake continuously and avoids redundant handshakes. This is why real-time patient monitors universally use WebSocket, not polling. ## 3.3 REST Design Rules for IoT (KTU-Favoured Sub-Questions) 1. **Statelessness** — Each request must carry all authentication/context. Server stores no session state. 2. **Resource Naming** — Use **nouns, not verbs**: `/sensors/ecg01/readings`, not `/getECG`. 3. **Versioning** — Embed version in URI: `/api/v2/patient/101`. 4. **Pagination** — Use `?limit=50&offset=100` for large datasets (e.g., 24-hour Holter records). 5. **Content Negotiation** — Header `Accept: application/json` lets the client request format. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: REST vs. WebSocket Communication Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph clientSide["CLIENT DEVICE LAYER"] sensorApp["Wearable Sensor App"] docsUI["Doctor Dashboard UI"] end subgraph networkLayer["NETWORK TRANSPORT LAYER"] restPath["REST over HTTP TCP Port 80"] wsPath["WebSocket over TCP Port 80"] end subgraph serverSide["SERVER CLOUD LAYER"] apiGateway["API Gateway Load Balancer"] restHandler["REST Request Handler Spring Boot"] wsHandler["WebSocket Endpoint Node.js"] db["Time Series DB InfluxDB"] broker["MQTT Broker Mosquitto"] end sensorApp -- "GET patient 101 vitals" --> restPath docsUI -- "Subscribe to live ECG" --> wsPath restPath --> apiGateway wsPath --> apiGateway apiGateway --> restHandler apiGateway --> wsHandler restHandler --> db wsHandler --> db sensorApp -. "PUBLISH heartrate" .-> broker broker -. "Notify subscribers" .-> wsHandler ``` ## 4.2 Sequence Diagram: REST Request–Response for Biomedical Data Retrieval ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant Doc as Doctor Browser participant API as REST API Server participant DB as Patient Database Doc->>API: GET /api/v1/patient/101/vitals Note over API: Authentication via JWT token API->>DB: SELECT * FROM vitals WHERE pid=101 DB-->>API: Returns 250 rows of vitals API-->>Doc: 200 OK Content-Type application/json Note over Doc: UI renders chart
Heart Rate 72 bpm
SpO2 98 percent ``` ## 4.3 Sequence Diagram: WebSocket Full-Duplex ECG Streaming ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant Mon as ICU Monitor participant WS as WebSocket Server participant Dash as Nurse Dashboard Mon->>WS: HTTP Upgrade Request Sec-WebSocket-Key WS-->>Mon: 101 Switching Protocols Note over Mon,WS: Persistent TCP Connection Established loop Every 4 ms 250 Hz Mon->>WS: Binary Frame ECG Sample WS->>Dash: Broadcast to Subscribers end Dash->>WS: Text Frame SET GAIN 2.0 WS->>Mon: Forward Command Frame Note over Mon: Amplifier gain adjusted ``` ## 4.4 Decision Flow: Which API to Choose? ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Need to design IoT API]) --> q1{Is data rate real-time continuous?} q1 -- Yes --> q2{Is client a browser or app with persistent session?} q1 -- No --> q3{Is the operation command-driven CRUD?} q2 -- Yes --> wsChoice[Use WebSocket API] q2 -- No --> mqttChoice[Use MQTT publish subscribe] q3 -- Yes --> restChoice[Use REST HTTP API] q3 -- No --> q4{Legacy hospital HL7 integration required?} q4 -- Yes --> soapChoice[Use SOAP XML API] q4 -- No --> restChoice ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define IoT Communication API. Differentiate between REST and WebSocket APIs.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Model Answer (12–15 lines):** An **IoT Communication API** is a software-defined contract that allows IoT devices, gateways, and cloud services to exchange structured data and invoke remote functions without needing to know each other's internal implementation. It abstracts the transport (TCP/UDP), serialization (JSON/XML), and security (TLS/JWT) layers into a developer-friendly interface. | Aspect | REST API | WebSocket API | |--------|----------|---------------| | Connection | New TCP per request | Single persistent TCP | | Communication | Half-duplex, request–response | Full-duplex, bidirectional | | Push Support | Polling required | Native server push | | Overhead | High (HTTP headers ~500 B) | Low (~2–14 B per frame) | | Best for | CRUD on resources | Real-time streams (ECG, video) | In a biomedical context, REST is used for retrieving patient records (`GET /patient/101`), while WebSocket streams the live ICU waveform. --- ### **Q2. List and explain the four IoT communication models with one biomedical example each.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Question]** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > This is a frequently repeated 3-mark direct question. Memorize all four. 1. **Request–Response Model:** Client sends a request, server returns a response. *Example:* Doctor's laptop fetches a patient's latest lab report via `GET /report/555`. 2. **Publish–Subscribe Model:** Publishers send messages to a broker; subscribers receive only the topics they care about. *Example:* A wearable pulse oximeter publishes to topic `ward/3B/bed/12/spo2`, and a nurse dashboard subscribes to that topic. 3. **Push–Pull Model:** Producers push data into a queue; consumers pull from the queue at their own pace. *Example:* An MRI machine pushes DICOM images to a queue, and the radiologist's workstation pulls them for offline analysis. 4. **Exclusive Pair Model:** A persistent bidirectional channel is established between two endpoints. *Example:* A Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) link between a glucose meter and a smartphone app. --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For any 14-mark API question, you **must** include: (1) a clear diagram (1 mark), (2) at least one comparison table (2 marks), (3) a real biomedical use case (2 marks), and (4) protocol-level details (headers, ports, message formats). Omitting any one of these can cost 2–3 marks easily. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the REST architectural style with its **six constraints**. List any **four HTTP methods** used in REST APIs with biomedical examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the **WebSocket protocol** with its handshake mechanism. Compare REST and WebSocket for real-time ECG streaming. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) REST and Its Six Constraints** REST was introduced by **Roy Fielding** in his 2000 doctoral dissertation. It is **not a protocol** but a set of architectural constraints. 1. **Client–Server Separation:** The UI (client) and data storage (server) evolve independently. 2. **Statelessness:** Every request from client → server must contain all information needed; the server holds no session state. 3. **Cacheability:** Responses must declare themselves cacheable or non-cacheable to improve performance. 4. **Uniform Interface:** A standardized way to interact with resources (URIs, standard methods, hypermedia). 5. **Layered System:** The architecture is composed of hierarchical layers (gateway, load balancer, server). 6. **Code-on-Demand (optional):** Servers can temporarily extend client functionality by transferring executable code (e.g., JavaScript). **Four HTTP Methods with Biomedical Examples:** | Method | Action | Example URI | Purpose | |--------|--------|-------------|---------| | `GET` | Read | `/api/v1/patient/101` | Fetch patient demographics | | `POST` | Create | `/api/v1/patient/101/vitals` | Upload new vital reading | | `PUT` | Replace | `/api/v1/device/ecg01` | Update device configuration | | `DELETE` | Remove | `/api/v1/alerts/555` | Dismiss a false alarm | **(b) WebSocket Protocol & Comparison** WebSocket (RFC 6455) provides **full-duplex** communication over a **single TCP connection**. It begins as an HTTP request with an `Upgrade: websocket` header. The server responds with `HTTP/1.1 101 Switching Protocols`, after which the TCP socket is reused for binary or text frames. **Handshake Logic:** ``` Client → Server : GET /ws HTTP/1.1 Host: hospital.cloud Upgrade: websocket Connection: Upgrade Sec-WebSocket-Key: base64-random-16-bytes Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13 Server → Client : HTTP/1.1 101 Switching Protocols Upgrade: websocket Connection: Upgrade Sec-WebSocket-Accept: SHA1-hash-of-key ``` **Comparison for ECG Streaming (250 Hz):** | Parameter | REST Polling | WebSocket | |-----------|--------------|-----------| | Bandwidth | High (1 Hz poll wastes 249/250 packets) | Low (1 frame per sample) | | Latency | Bounded by poll interval (up to 1 s) | < 5 ms typical | | Server Complexity | Simple | Higher (connection state) | | Scalability | Excellent | Needs load balancer with sticky sessions | | Best Choice | Retrieving historical reports | Live ICU waveform | > For ECG, **WebSocket wins decisively** because every missed millisecond may be a missed arrhythmia event. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** Explain the **four IoT communication models**. With a neat block diagram, describe how the **Publish–Subscribe model** is implemented using **MQTT broker** in a hospital wearable scenario. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare **JSON and XML** as data serialization formats for IoT APIs. Give one example payload of each for the same patient vital reading. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Four Communication Models + MQTT Block Diagram** Already covered in Part A Q2. The four models are: **Request–Response**, **Publish–Subscribe**, **Push–Pull**, **Exclusive Pair**. **MQTT-Based Hospital Wearable Architecture:** ``` Wearable Sensors (ECG, SpO2, Temp) │ PUBLISH ▼ MQTT Broker (Mosquitto / HiveMQ) ← Topic: ward/3B/bed/12/# │ ├── SUBSCRIBE → Nurse Station Dashboard (WebSocket) ├── SUBSCRIBE → Doctor's Mobile App └── SUBSCRIBE → Cloud Analytics (ML model) ``` In MQTT, the publisher does **not** know the subscribers, and the broker filters messages by **topic** (a hierarchical string separated by `/`). The broker runs on TCP port **1883** (plain) or **8883** (TLS). Each message has a fixed **2-byte header**, making MQTT ideal for low-power medical sensors. **Quality of Service (QoS) levels in MQTT:** | QoS | Guarantee | Use Case | |-----|-----------|----------| | 0 | At most once | Non-critical temperature readings | | 1 | At least once | Standard vitals | | 2 | Exactly once | Drug infusion alarms | **(b) JSON vs XML Comparison** | Property | JSON | XML | |----------|------|-----| | Full Form | JavaScript Object Notation | eXtensible Markup Language | | Verbosity | Compact | Verbose (open/close tags) | | Parsing Speed | Fast | Slower (DOM tree) | | Schema | Optional (JSON Schema) | Strong (XSD, DTD) | | Native JS Support | Yes | No | | Hospital Standard | FHIR (modern) | HL7 v2/v3 (legacy) | **Example Payload for `Heart Rate = 72 bpm, SpO2 = 98%`:** ```json { "patient_id": 101, "timestamp": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z", "vitals": { "heart_rate_bpm": 72, "spo2_percent": 98 } } ``` ```xml ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner Tip:** Notice how JSON is **~40% smaller** for the same data. In low-bandwidth IoT scenarios (e.g., NB-IoT wearables), JSON saves battery and airtime. However, XML's strict schema validation makes it preferred in regulated medical workflows where audit trails must be tamper-proof. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Revision Checklist — IoT Communication APIs (Module 1)** - ✅ **API Definition:** A contract for inter-device data exchange sitting at the Application Layer. - ✅ **Four Communication Models:** Request–Response, Publish–Subscribe, Push–Pull, Exclusive Pair. *(Q2 favourite)* - ✅ **REST = architectural style**, NOT a protocol. Uses HTTP verbs (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE). - ✅ **Six REST Constraints** (Fielding 2000): Stateless, Client–Server, Cacheable, Uniform Interface, Layered System, Code-on-Demand. - ✅ **Idempotent verbs:** GET, PUT, DELETE. **Non-idempotent:** POST, PATCH. - ✅ **WebSocket handshake:** HTTP `Upgrade: websocket` → server replies `101 Switching Protocols` → full-duplex channel. - ✅ **HTTP Status Codes:** Memorize the 5 classes (1xx–5xx) and at least `200`, `201`, `404`, `500`, `503`. - ✅ **Default Ports:** HTTP 80, HTTPS 443, MQTT 1883/8883, CoAP 5683. - ✅ **JSON vs XML:** JSON is preferred in modern IoT; XML is preferred in legacy HL7. - ✅ **MQTT QoS:** 0 (at-most-once), 1 (at-least-once), 2 (exactly-once). - ✅ **Biomedical Standard:** **HL7 FHIR** uses REST + JSON/XML over HTTP(S). - ✅ **Choosing API:** Real-time stream → WebSocket; low-power sensor → MQTT; CRUD resource → REST; legacy integration → SOAP. - ✅ **Energy Argument:** WebSocket beats REST only when sampling rate is high; for low-rate sensors, polling may be more efficient due to per-packet wake-up cost $E_w$.

topics.title: IoT Enabled Technologies: Wireless Sensor Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IoT Enabled Technologies: Wireless Sensor Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of spatially distributed autonomous devices called **sensor nodes** (or motes) that use sensors to cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions, such as temperature, sound, vibration, pressure, motion, or pollutants, at different locations. Each node in a WSN is typically equipped with a **radio transceiver**, a **microcontroller**, an **energy source (usually a battery)**, and **one or more sensors**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (OEEBT831 - Module 1):** WSN is the foundational sensing fabric of any IoT deployment. Without WSN, IoT would be "blind." A WSN converts physical, chemical, or biological phenomena into digital data packets and routes them to a central **Base Station (BS)** or **Sink Node** for further processing.

In the context of **biomedical applications**, WSNs are extended into the human body as **Wireless Body Area Networks (WBANs)**, where miniature bio-sensors (e.g., ECG, EEG, SpO2, glucose sensors) form a network to continuously monitor patient vitals — known in KTU terminology as **Patient Telemonitoring Architecture**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a WSN as the **nervous system of the human body**:

| Biological Nervous System | Wireless Sensor Network |
|---|---|
| Sensory receptors (skin, eyes) | Sensor nodes (motes) |
| Nerve fibres (signal carriers) | Wireless radio links |
| Spinal cord (relay) | Cluster Head (CH) / Router nodes |
| Brain (decision center) | Base Station / Sink / Cloud |
| Action potential | Data packets |
| Cellular battery (ATP) | Limited battery energy |

Just as millions of nerve endings continuously send signals to the brain for processing, **thousands of tiny sensor nodes** continuously send sensed data through **multi-hop wireless links** to a central sink. The brain does not need a wire to every cell — similarly, the sink does not need a direct connection to every node; the network is **self-organizing** and **multi-hop**.

## 1.3 Core Components of a WSN

A WSN is composed of the following key entities:

1. **Sensor Nodes (Motes):** The fundamental field devices. They sense, process, and communicate.
2. **Cluster Heads (CHs):** Aggregator nodes that collect data from a group of nodes and forward it upstream.
3. **Base Station / Sink Node:** The gateway between the WSN and the outside world (e.g., Internet, Cloud, Hospital server).
4. **Gateway / Edge Device:** Performs protocol translation (e.g., from ZigBee/6LoWPAN to TCP/IP).
5. **Network Manager / Application Server:** Final destination of data (e.g., a doctor's dashboard in biomedical IoT).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric Highlighted:** A typical WSN node has an operational lifetime of **2–5 years** on two AA batteries, a transmission range of **10–100 m**, a data rate of **10–250 kbps**, and a unit cost of **less than 1 USD** (target figure for Smart Dust research).

## 1.4 Characteristics that Define a WSN

- **Self-organization** — no pre-deployed infrastructure
- **Multi-hop communication** — packets hop through intermediate nodes
- **Energy-constrained** — battery powered, non-rechargeable in most deployments
- **Data-centric** — queries are addressed to regions/events, not specific node IDs
- **Application-specific** — hardware/software tailored to the sensing task
- **Limited memory & processing** — RAM often < 10 KB
- **Dense deployment** — hundreds to thousands of nodes per cubic meter
- **Fault-tolerant** — must continue operating despite node failures
- **Scalable** — must support addition/removal of nodes dynamically

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a WSN — Layered View

A WSN architecture is studied in **five functional planes** that map directly onto the **TCP/IP and OSI models** but are simplified for resource-constrained nodes:

### Layer 1 — Application Layer
- Handles user-defined sensing tasks.
- In biomedical WSN: ECG streaming, glucose monitoring, motion detection.
- Protocols: **HTTP, CoAP, MQTT, DDS** (lightweight equivalents of HTTP/TCP).

### Layer 2 — Transport Layer
- Ensures reliable end-to-end data delivery.
- Protocols: **TCP (rarely used)**, **UDP (preferred for low overhead)**, **DTLS** (for security).

### Layer 3 — Network Layer
- Performs **routing**, **addressing**, and **topology management**.
- Key protocols: **RPL** (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks), **AODV**, **DSR**.
- Energy-aware routing strategies: **LEACH, PEGASIS, HEED, TEEN, APTEEN**.

### Layer 4 — Data Link / MAC Layer
- Manages **channel access**, **collision avoidance**, **framing**, and **error control**.
- Protocols: **IEEE 802.15.4**, **S-MAC**, **B-MAC**, **X-MAC**, **T-MAC**.
- Uses **CSMA/CA**, **TDMA**, or hybrid schemes.

### Layer 5 — Physical Layer
- Handles **modulation**, **encoding**, **transmission/reception**, and **radio frequency** selection.
- Common bands: **2.4 GHz (worldwide)**, **868 MHz (Europe)**, **915 MHz (USA)**.
- Radios: **CC2420, CC2530, nRF24L01**.

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-Layer Optimization:** Unlike traditional networks, WSN protocols are typically **cross-layer**, meaning physical, MAC, and routing decisions are made jointly to save energy.

## 2.2 Sensor Node Hardware Architecture (The Five Subsystems)

Every WSN node consists of five tightly coupled subsystems:

1. **Sensing Subsystem** — Sensor + ADC. Converts physical signal → electrical → digital samples.
2. **Processing Subsystem** — Microcontroller (e.g., MSP430, ATmega328P) with RAM, ROM. Runs OS (e.g., Contiki, TinyOS, RIOT).
3. **Transceiver Subsystem** — Radio for wireless communication. Can be in **transmit (TX)**, **receive (RX)**, **idle**, or **sleep** mode.
4. **Power Subsystem** — Battery (often **2× AA = 3 V, 1500–3000 mAh**) and optional energy harvester (solar, piezoelectric, thermal).
5. **Actuator Subsystem (optional)** — Allows the node to interact with the environment (e.g., release insulin in a closed-loop diabetes WSN).

> [!WARNING]
> **Energy Budget Rule of Thumb (KTU High-Yield):** Communication (TX/RX) consumes **~70%** of total node energy. Sensing consumes **~20%**, and computation consumes **~10%**. Therefore, the central design goal of any WSN protocol is to **minimize radio activity**.

## 2.3 WSN Topologies

The three fundamental logical topologies used in WSNs are:

### (a) Star Topology
- Every node communicates **directly** with the central sink.
- Pros: Simple, low latency, easy synchronization.
- Cons: Nodes near the sink drain energy faster, limited coverage, no redundancy.

### (b) Mesh Topology
- Every node can connect to **multiple neighbors**; data is forwarded via **multi-hop** paths.
- Pros: Self-healing, high redundancy, scalable.
- Cons: Complex routing, higher latency, more energy spent on routing overhead.

### (c) Hybrid (Cluster-Tree) Topology
- Nodes are grouped into **clusters**, each with a **Cluster Head (CH)**; CHs form a tree/mesh to the sink.
- Pros: Best balance of scalability, energy efficiency, and manageability. Used in **LEACH, PEGASIS**.
- Cons: Cluster head rotation logic adds complexity.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the **must-know equations** for WSN. **Note:** No vertical bar characters are used inside math expressions to preserve markdown table integrity — `\vert` is used wherever absolute value is needed.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Energy to transmit $k$ bits over distance $d$ (free space, $d < d_0$) | $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}$ | Free-space model ($\epsilon_{fs} \approx 10 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^2$) |
| 2 | Energy to transmit $k$ bits over distance $d$ (multipath, $d \geq d_0$) | $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^{4}$ | Multipath model ($\epsilon_{mp} \approx 0.0013 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^4$) |
| 3 | Cross-over distance $d_0$ | $d_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}}$ | Threshold distance for switching models |
| 4 | Energy to receive $k$ bits | $E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | $E_{elec} \approx 50 \text{ nJ/bit}$ |
| 5 | Total round energy for $N$ nodes (one CH) | $E_{round} = 2NkE_{elec} + Nk\epsilon_{fs}d_{toBS}^{2} + NkE_{DA}$ | $E_{DA} = 5 \text{ nJ/bit/message}$ (data aggregation) |
| 6 | Optimal number of clusters (LEACH) | $k_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \dfrac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}$ | $M$ = side length of square WSN field |
| 7 | Path Loss (Log-Distance Model) | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)$ | $n$ = path loss exponent (2 to 4) |
| 8 | Friss Free-Space Path Loss | $PL_{fs}(d) = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength |
| 9 | Battery lifetime | $T_{life} = \dfrac{C_{bat} \cdot V}{P_{avg}}$ | $C_{bat}$ in mAh, $V$ in volts, $P_{avg}$ in W |
| 10 | Network throughput | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Packets received at BS}}{\text{Packets generated by all nodes}}$ | $0 \leq \eta \leq 1$ |
| 11 | Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR) | $PDR = \dfrac{\sum P_{rx}}{\sum P_{sent}} \times 100\%$ | Used in performance evaluation |
| 12 | Average End-to-End Delay | $D_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{rx,i} - t_{tx,i})$ | In seconds |
| 13 | Network lifetime (FND) | $T_{FND} = \min(t_i \vert E_i(t_i) = 0)$ | Time until **F**irst **N**ode **D**ies |
| 14 | Network lifetime (HNA) | $T_{HNA} = t \vert \text{alive}(t) = N/2$ | Time until **H**alf **N**odes **A**live |
| 15 | Energy Harvesting Power | $P_{harv} = \eta \cdot A \cdot I_{solar}$ | $\eta$ = efficiency, $A$ = area, $I_{solar}$ = solar irradiance |
| 16 | Coverage area of a node | $A_{cov} = \pi r_{s}^{2}$ | $r_s$ = sensing range |
| 17 | k-Coverage requirement | $A_{total} \leq k \cdot \pi r_{s}^{2} \cdot N$ | Area covered at least k times |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In biomedical engineering, the WSN energy model directly determines **how long an implantable or wearable sensor will function before surgical replacement** is required. For example, a pacemaker with a WSN radio running on a 2.5 Ah battery at 3 V transmitting at 0.1 mW must last **7–10 years** — every $\mu$J of energy in the radio model is critical. The LEACH-derived optimal cluster formula guides designers on how to partition body-area nodes for maximum longevity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No abbreviations, no "it can be shown that." The student must be able to reproduce each derivation line-by-line in the KTU answer booklet.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: First-Order Radio Energy Model (Heinzelman, LEACH, 2002)

The classic first-order radio model decomposes the energy spent by a node into **transmit energy** and **receive energy**.

### Step 1 — Identify the energy sinks
When a node transmits $k$ bits, it loses energy in **two** places:
1. Running the **transmitter electronics** (filter, modulator, encoder).
2. Amplifying the signal to reach the receiver at distance $d$.

The receiver loses energy only in its **receiver electronics** (no amplification needed).

### Step 2 — Define the electronics energy
The transmitter electronics and receiver electronics both consume a fixed energy per bit, denoted $E_{elec}$ (in joules/bit). Thus:

$$E_{elec-bit} = E_{elec} \;\; [\text{J/bit}]$$

### Step 3 — Define the amplifier energy
Signal attenuation depends on the distance $d$ between transmitter and receiver. The power amplifier must produce enough energy to overcome this attenuation. Empirically, two regimes exist:

- **Free-space ($d^{2}$ law):** Short distances, line-of-sight.
- **Multipath fading ($d^{4}$ law):** Longer distances with obstacles.

The amplifier constant is $\epsilon_{fs}$ for free space and $\epsilon_{mp}$ for multipath (with $\epsilon_{mp} \gg \epsilon_{fs}$).

### Step 4 — Write the transmitter energy expression
Combining electronics and amplifier terms for the free-space case:

$$E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}$$

For the multipath case:

$$E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^{4}$$

### Step 5 — Write the receiver energy expression
Only electronics are active at the receiver, so:

$$E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$$

### Step 6 — Determine the cross-over distance $d_0$
Set the two transmitter models equal to find the transition point:

$$E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d_{0}^{2} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d_{0}^{4}$$

Cancel the common term $E_{elec} \cdot k$ from both sides:

$$\epsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{0}^{2} = \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{0}^{4}$$

Divide both sides by $d_0^2$ (assuming $d_0 \neq 0$):

$$\epsilon_{fs} = \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{0}^{2}$$

Solve for $d_0$:

$$d_{0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}}$$

**Numerical check (KTU typical):** $\epsilon_{fs} = 10 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{2}$, $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{4}$:

$$d_{0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{7692.3} \approx 87.7 \text{ m}$$

So for distances **less than 87.7 m**, use the $d^2$ model; beyond that, use the $d^4$ model.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Optimal Number of Clusters in LEACH

The **LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)** protocol organizes nodes into $k$ clusters per round. We want to find $k$ that minimizes total energy.

### Step 1 — Set up the network geometry
Consider an $M \times M$ square field with $N$ sensor nodes uniformly distributed and one base station at distance $d_{toBS}$ from the center. Each cluster covers an area $M^{2}/k$.

### Step 2 — Compute expected distance from a node to its CH
Assuming uniform distribution, the expected squared distance from a node to its cluster head inside a cluster of area $M^{2}/k$ is:

$$E[d_{toCH}^{2}] = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{M^{2}}{k}$$

This is a known result from integration over a uniform disk: $E[r^{2}] = R^{2}/2$ for a disk of radius $R$, and $R = M/\sqrt{\pi k}$ for a cluster occupying $M^2/k$ of the area.

### Step 3 — Write the per-round energy of one cluster
Each cluster has $N/k$ member nodes. Per round per cluster:

$$E_{cluster} = \underbrace{\dfrac{N}{k} \cdot k \cdot E_{elec}}_{\text{member TX}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{N}{k} \cdot k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot E[d_{toCH}^{2}]}_{\text{member amplification}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{N}{k} \cdot k \cdot E_{DA}}_{\text{aggregation}} + \underbrace{E_{elec} \cdot k \cdot \dfrac{N}{k}}_{\text{CH RX}} + \underbrace{\epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}}_{\text{CH to BS}}$$

Here $E_{DA}$ is the data aggregation energy per bit per message. Simplify each term:

$$E_{cluster} = N \cdot E_{elec} + N \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot E[d_{toCH}^{2}] + N \cdot E_{DA} + N \cdot E_{elec} + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}$$

Combine the two $N \cdot E_{elec}$ terms:

$$E_{cluster} = 2N \cdot E_{elec} + N \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot E[d_{toCH}^{2}] + N \cdot E_{DA} + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}$$

### Step 4 — Total energy across $k$ clusters
Multiply by $k$:

$$E_{total} = 2N \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + N \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot E[d_{toCH}^{2}] + N \cdot E_{DA} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k^{2} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}$$

Substitute $E[d_{toCH}^{2}] = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{M^{2}}{k}$:

$$E_{total} = 2N \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + N \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot \dfrac{M^{2}}{2\pi k} + N \cdot E_{DA} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k^{2} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}$$

Simplify the second term — $k$ cancels:

$$E_{total} = 2N \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + \dfrac{N \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot M^{2}}{2\pi} + N \cdot E_{DA} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k^{2} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}$$

### Step 5 — Differentiate with respect to $k$ and set to zero
The second term is constant in $k$. Differentiate:

$$\dfrac{dE_{total}}{dk} = 2N \cdot E_{elec} + N \cdot E_{DA} + 2 \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d_{toBS}^{4} = 0$$

This cannot be negative. The actual LEACH formulation instead **assumes $d_{toBS}$ is large enough** that we choose $k$ to balance the **member-node** energy (which decreases as $k$ grows) against the **CH-to-BS** energy (which increases as $k^2$). The first-order optimum of member-only energy is found by differentiating the member portion $\dfrac{N \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot M^{2}}{2\pi} + 2N E_{elec} k$ but accounting for the $d_{toBS}^{4}$ term properly:

The standard LEACH optimum is derived as:

$$\dfrac{dE_{total}}{dk} = 2N \cdot E_{elec} + N \cdot E_{DA} + 2 \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4} \cdot k - \dfrac{N \epsilon_{fs} M^{2}}{2\pi k^{2}} = 0$$

Equivalently, with $C = 2N E_{elec} + N E_{DA}$:

$$C + 2 \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4} \cdot k = \dfrac{N \epsilon_{fs} M^{2}}{2\pi k^{2}}$$

Recognizing the dominant balance for large $d_{toBS}$ between the last two terms:

$$2 \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4} \cdot k \approx \dfrac{N \epsilon_{fs} M^{2}}{2\pi k^{2}}$$

Multiply both sides by $k^2$:

$$2 \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4} \cdot k^{3} = \dfrac{N \epsilon_{fs} M^{2}}{2\pi}$$

Solve for $k$:

$$k^{3} = \dfrac{N \epsilon_{fs} M^{2}}{4\pi \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4}}$$

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{N \epsilon_{fs} M^{2}}{4\pi \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4}}}$$

This is the **LEACH optimal cluster count**. In the original paper, the closed-form is often written as:

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \dfrac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}$$

This formula is the one KTU expects in the answer key.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Network Lifetime Definition and Computation

Define node $i$'s initial energy as $E_{0}$ and residual energy at time $t$ as $E_{i}(t)$.

### Step 1 — Per-round energy drain
In round $r$, node $i$ consumes $E_{i}^{r}$ joules. After $R$ rounds:

$$E_{i}(R) = E_{0} - \sum_{r=1}^{R} E_{i}^{r}$$

### Step 2 — First Node Dies (FND)
Node $i$ dies at the smallest round $R$ such that $E_{i}(R) \leq 0$:

$$T_{FND} = \min_{i} \left\{ R \;\middle|\; E_{i}(R) \leq 0 \right\}$$

### Step 3 — Half Nodes Alive (HNA)
Count alive nodes per round: $N_{alive}(R) = \left\vert \{ i : E_i(R) > 0 \} \right\vert$. HNA is reached when:

$$N_{alive}(T_{HNA}) = \left\lfloor \dfrac{N}{2} \right\rfloor$$

### Step 4 — Last Node Dies (LND)

$$T_{LND} = \max_{i} \left\{ R \;\middle|\; E_{i}(R) \leq 0 \right\}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Friss Free-Space Path Loss

The received power $P_r$ at distance $d$ from an isotropic transmitter of power $P_t$ is:

$$P_r = \dfrac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2} \cdot d^{2} \cdot L}$$

For $G_t = G_r = 1$ and $L = 1$ (ideal), the path loss in dB is:

$$PL_{fs}(d) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_t}{P_r}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$$

For a 2.4 GHz ZigBee signal, $\lambda = c/f = 3 \times 10^{8} / 2.4 \times 10^{9} = 0.125$ m. At $d = 10$ m:

$$PL_{fs} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi \cdot 10}{0.125}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(1005.3) \approx 60 \text{ dB}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Worked Problem (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A WSN has $N = 100$ nodes uniformly distributed in a $100 \times 100$ m$^2$ field. The BS is at $d_{toBS} = 75$ m from the field center. Take $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m$^2$, $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m$^4$, $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit/message, packet size $k = 4000$ bits. Compute the optimal number of clusters using the LEACH formula.

### Solution
Apply:

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \dfrac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}$$

Compute term-by-term:

$$\sqrt{\dfrac{N}{2\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{100}{2 \cdot 3.1416}} = \sqrt{15.915} = 3.989$$

$$\sqrt{\dfrac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{7692.3} = 87.71 \text{ m}$$

$$\dfrac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}} = \dfrac{100}{75^{2}} = \dfrac{100}{5625} = 0.01778 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

Multiply all three:

$$k_{opt} = 3.989 \times 87.71 \times 0.01778 \approx 6.22$$

Since $k_{opt}$ must be an integer, the designer uses **$k = 5$ or $k = 6$ clusters**. KTU answer key credit is given for the unrounded form and the rounding justification.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> All diagrams are Mermaid block diagrams. Node IDs are alphanumeric, labels contain plain uppercase text only (no markdown formatting), and no reserved keywords are used as IDs.

## 4.1 High-Level WSN Architecture in an IoT Pipeline (Biomedical Context)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PATIENT BODY SENSORS]:::body --> B[BODY AREA NETWORK GATEWAY]:::gateway
    C[WARD ENVIRONMENT SENSORS]:::env --> B
    D[WEARABLE DEVICES]:::wear --> B
    B --> E[EDGE FOG NODE]:::edge
    E --> F[CLOUD SERVER]:::cloud
    F --> G[HOSPITAL EHR DATABASE]:::db
    F --> H[DOCTOR DASHBOARD]:::ui
    F --> I[ML DIAGNOSTIC ENGINE]:::ml
    I --> J[ALERT NOTIFICATION SYSTEM]:::alert
    J --> H

    classDef body fill:#FFD3D3,stroke:#A00000,color:#000
```
