Three-layer is a simplified subset (Perception, Network, Application). - **MQTT**: TCP, broker-based, Pub/Sub, 2-byte fixed header, **3 QoS levels**, ideal for telemetry. - **CoAP**: UDP, RESTful, 4-byte binary header, **Observe pattern**, DTLS security, ideal for constrained nodes. - **HTTP/HTTPS**: TCP, heavy, web-native, NOT preferred for constrained IoT. - **AMQP**: TCP, broker with **Exchanges/Queues/Bindings**, enterprise-grade with transactions. - **DDS**: UDP, broker-less, **23 QoS policies**, real-time, used in robotics and defense. - **6LoWPAN**: Adaptation layer; compresses IPv6 (1280 bytes) to fit IEEE 802.15.4 (127 bytes). - **RPL**: Routing protocol for LLNs using **DODAG** rooted at border router. - **LoRaWAN**: Sub-GHz, 2–15 km, ADR (Adaptive Data Rate), ALOHA access, deep indoor. - **NB-IoT**: Licensed cellular, 3GPP Cat-NB1/NB2, 200 kHz bandwidth, indoor penetration. - **ZigBee vs BLE vs Wi-Fi**: ZigBee (mesh, low data), BLE (point-to-point, wearables), Wi-Fi (high bandwidth, video). - **Overhead Rule of Thumb**: MQTT ≈ 35%, HTTP ≈ 88%, CoAP ≈ 25%, DDS ≈ 10% (header-to-payload). - **Shannon Capacity**: $C = B \log_2(1 + SNR)$ — upper bound; real systems (LoRa) trade capacity for sensitivity. - **Biomedical Relevance**: ECG over MQTT QoS 2, glucose over CoAP/BLE, RFID for drug tracking, DDS for surgical robots. - **Security Standard**: TLS 1.2+ for TCP-based, DTLS 1.2+ for UDP-based; HIPAA compliance mandatory for medical IoT. - **Energy Metric**: Energy/bit $E_b = P_{tx} \cdot t_{tx} / N_{bits}$ — lower is better; favors short headers and sleep modes. - **Mnemonic**: "**M**y **C**ute **A**nimal **D**igs **H**eaps" → **M**QTT, **C**oAP, **A**MQP, **D**DS, **H**TTP.

topics.title: Protocol Standardization for IoT Efforts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Protocol Standardization for IoT Efforts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Protocol Standardization for IoT** refers to the coordinated, consensus-driven engineering effort to establish **uniform communication rules, data formats, semantic interpretations, and interoperability frameworks** that allow heterogeneous IoT devices, networks, and platforms to exchange information reliably, securely, and unambiguously across the global Internet ecosystem.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this spans the activities undertaken by recognized Standards Development Organizations (SDOs), industry alliances, and open-source communities to define:

- **Application Layer Protocols** (e.g., CoAP, MQTT, HTTP/REST, AMQP, DDS)
- **Network/Transport Layer Adaptations** (e.g., 6LoWPAN, RPL, UDP/TCP variants for constrained devices)
- **Semantic Interoperability Frameworks** (e.g., W3C SSN/SOSA, oneM2M base ontology)
- **Data Encoding Standards** (e.g., JSON, CBOR, SenML, Protocol Buffers)
- **Security & Identity Standards** (e.g., X.509, OAuth 2.0, DTLS, OSCORE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Protocol standardization is positioned as a foundational requirement to prevent *vendor lock-in*, *protocol fragmentation*, and *vertical silo architectures* in IoT deployments.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a vast international airport where **hundreds of airlines** operate flights from **thousands of cities** to a common terminal hub. If every airline invented its own runway length, fuel nozzle diameter, baggage tag format, and language for boarding announcements, the airport would collapse into chaos within hours.

**IoT is exactly this airport.** Billions of sensors, actuators, gateways, and cloud platforms manufactured by different vendors must "land" on a common communication infrastructure — the Internet.

> Protocol standardization is the equivalent of:
> - **ICAO** setting universal runway standards (Physical/MAC layer)
> - **IATA** defining baggage handling codes (Application layer)
> - **ISO** standardizing air traffic control language (Semantic layer)
> - **WMO** synchronizing weather reporting (Data encoding)

Without these agreements, an **Omron blood pressure sensor** could never securely send a reading to an **AWS cloud dashboard** readable by a **doctor's iOS app running on a hospital's Wi-Fi** — and then trigger a Philips defibrillator alarm.

## 1.3 Why Standardization is Non-Negotiable in IoT

| Engineering Challenge | Standardization Solution |
|---|---|
| Device heterogeneity (8-bit MCUs to 64-bit gateways) | RFC 7228 Device Classes (Class 0, 1, 2, 3) |
| Constrained bandwidth / battery | CoAP over UDP, 6LoWPAN header compression |
| Insecure firmware in millions of nodes | Lightweight DTLS / OSCORE / SUIT standards |
| Vendor lock-in | Open API specs (oneM2M, OCF, Matter) |
| Data silos in healthcare / industrial IoT | Semantic ontologies (SSN/SOSA, SAREF) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** The **Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)** alone has published over **9,000+ RFCs**, of which hundreds directly govern IoT behavior. Memorize **6LoWPAN (RFC 4944 / RFC 6282)**, **CoAP (RFC 7252)**, and **RPL (RFC 6550)** as the "Holy Trinity" of constrained IoT networking.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Conceptual Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of IoT Protocol Standardization

IoT standardization efforts are organized into three logical pillars. KTU frequently asks students to classify protocols under these pillars.

### Pillar 1 — Connectivity & Networking Standards
Govern *how* bits travel from device to cloud.

- **IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force):** CoAP, 6LoWPAN, RPL, CBOR
- **IEEE:** IEEE 802.15.4 (LR-WPAN PHY/MAC), IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi)
- **ITU-T:** Y.2060 IoT reference model, Y.2070 framework
- **3GPP:** LTE-M, NB-IoT, 5G mMTC for cellular IoT

### Pillar 2 — Application & Service Standards
Govern *what* the bits mean and how services compose.

- **OASIS:** MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)
- **IETF:** HTTP/2, HTTP/3, WebTransport
- **OASIS / ISO:** AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol)
- **OMG:** DDS (Data Distribution Service) for real-time industrial IoT
- **W3C:** WoT (Web of Things) Thing Description, SSN/SOSA Ontology

### Pillar 3 — Cross-Domain & Vertical Standards
Govern *how* industries integrate IoT (healthcare, smart grid, automotive).

- **oneM2M:** Global horizontal service platform standard
- **OCF (Open Connectivity Foundation):** Now unified into **Matter / Thread**
- **CSA (Connectivity Standards Alliance):** Matter smart-home protocol
- **HL7 FHIR:** Healthcare data exchange
- **ETSI:** Smart M2M, MEC, NGSIoT
- **ISO/IEC:** JTC 1/SC 41 — IoT and digital twins
- **Industrial Internet Consortium (IIC):** Industrial Internet Reference Architecture (IIRA)
- **RAMI 4.0 (Reference Architecture Model Industrie 4.0):** German Industry 4.0 reference

## 2.2 Layered Mapping of Major Standardized IoT Protocols

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This mapping table is **examination-critical** for KTU 14-mark questions. Memorize the layer-to-protocol association.

| OSI / IoT Layer | Standardized Protocol | Standard Body | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Application (Messaging) | **MQTT v5.0** | OASIS | Telemetry, low-bandwidth pub/sub |
| Application (REST) | **HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2** | IETF / W3C | Web dashboards, cloud APIs |
| Application (Constrained REST) | **CoAP (RFC 7252)** | IETF | 8-bit sensor networks |
| Application (Industrial Pub/Sub) | **DDS (OMG)** | OMG | Robotics, autonomous vehicles |
| Application (Queuing) | **AMQP 1.0** | OASIS / ISO 19464 | Reliable enterprise messaging |
| Application (Semantic) | **SSN/SOSA, SAREF** | W3C / ETSI | Sensor data meaning |
| Transport | **UDP, TCP, DTLS, TLS 1.3, QUIC** | IETF | Secure transport |
| Network (Routing) | **RPL (RFC 6550)** | IETF | Low-power lossy networks (LLNs) |
| Adaptation (Compression) | **6LoWPAN (RFC 4944, 6282)** | IETF | IPv6 over IEEE 802.15.4 |
| MAC / PHY | **IEEE 802.15.4, BLE 5.x, LoRaWAN** | IEEE / Bluetooth SIG | Short-range radio |

## 2.3 The IETF Constrained Node Classification (RFC 7228)

A foundational KTU conceptual question concerns device classes:

| Class | RAM | Flash | Example Device | Power Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Class 0 | <10 KB | <100 KB | Dust motes, passive sensors | Energy harvesting |
| Class 1 | ~10 KB | ~100 KB | Thread sensor, BLE beacon | Battery (months) |
| Class 2 | ~50 KB | ~250 KB | ESP32-based node | Battery (years) |
| Class 3 | ~250 KB+ | MBs | Raspberry Pi gateway | Mains powered |

**Implication:** CoAP is designed for Class 1–2, while HTTP/CoAP proxies bridge to Class 3+ clouds.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet (Conceptual Metrics)

Since this is a protocols topic, the "formulas" are standardized numerics, payload sizes, and latency metrics that examiners love to test.

| Metric / Constant | Value / Definition | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| IEEE 802.15.4 Max PHY Frame | **127 bytes** | Header compression justification |
| IPv6 Address Length | **128 bits** | Why 6LoWPAN compression is needed |
| 6LoWPAN Compression Ratio | 40+ bytes compressed to 6–8 bytes | **HC encoding efficiency** |
| CoAP Default Port | **UDP 5683**, DTLS 5684 | Frequently asked in 3-mark Qs |
| MQTT Default Port | **TCP 1883**, TLS 8883 | Frequently asked in 3-mark Qs |
| AMQP Default Port | **TCP 5672**, TLS 5671 | Enterprise IoT |
| QoS Levels in MQTT | 0 (at most once), 1 (at least once), 2 (exactly once) | 3-mark conceptual |
| QoS in CoAP | NON (Non-confirmable), CON (Confirmable) | 3-mark conceptual |
| RPL Trickle Timer | Adaptive DIO suppression (RFC 6550) | Energy efficiency |
| 5G mMTC Target Density | **1,000,000 devices / km²** | 3GPP massive IoT |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Standardized IoT protocols are deployed in:
- **Healthcare:** MQTT + HL7 FHIR (remote patient monitoring, RPM)
- **Industry 4.0:** OPC UA over TSN for deterministic machine control
- **Smart Energy:** DLMS/COSEM (IEC 62056) for smart meters
- **Automotive:** DDS for V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything)
- **Smart Cities:** oneM2M service platform in Seoul, Busan deployments
- **Agriculture:** LoRaWAN + CBOR (Concise Binary Object Representation) for soil sensors

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Frameworks & Standardization Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation of the 6LoWPAN Header Compression Logic

**Problem Context:** An IEEE 802.15.4 frame can carry a maximum of **127 bytes** total. After MAC overhead (~25 bytes), only **~81 bytes** remain. A full IPv6 header alone is **40 bytes**, and UDP adds another **8 bytes**, leaving essentially **no payload room** for application data without compression.

**Step-by-Step Derivation of available payload:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Frame Capacity}_{\text{802.15.4}} &= 127 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{MAC Overhead} &= 25 \text{ bytes (header + FCS)} \\
\text{Link-Layer Security (AES-CCM-128)} &= 21 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Net Available} &= 127 - 25 - 21 = 81 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Uncompressed IPv6 Header} &= 40 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Uncompressed UDP Header} &= 8 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Uncompressed Application Data} &= 81 - 40 - 8 = 33 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logical Deduction:** Without 6LoWPAN's **IPHC (IP Header Compression, RFC 6282)**, a sensor could only send 33 bytes of data per frame. IPHC eliminates redundant IPv6 fields (version, traffic class, flow label derived from link-layer context), achieving:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Compressed IPv6 Header (best case)} &= 2 \text{ bytes (dispatch + compressed fields)} \\
\text{Compressed UDP Header (NHC)} &= 4 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{New Net Application Data} &= 81 - 2 - 4 = 75 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** 6LoWPAN increases effective payload from **33 → 75 bytes** — a **~127% efficiency gain** that makes IPv6 connectivity feasible for Class 1/2 devices.

## 3.2 Comparative Derivation: MQTT vs. CoAP Selection Logic

A classic KTU 14-mark question asks for a justified protocol selection. The derivation below formalizes the decision tree.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Decision}_{\text{MQTT vs CoAP}} &= f(\text{Topology}, \text{Power}, \text{Message Size}, \text{QoS})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Analyze the topology:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If} \quad \text{Devices} &= 1{:}\text{Many (Cloud-centric)} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{MQTT preferred (hub-and-spoke broker model)} \\
\text{If} \quad \text{Devices} &= M{:}M (peer-to-peer mesh) \\
&\Rightarrow \text{CoAP preferred (stateless, RESTful over UDP)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate power and network characteristics:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{TCP Handshake Cost} &= 3 \text{ packets (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK)} \\
\text{+ TLS 1.2 Handshake} &= 2 \text{ round-trips (4–6 messages)} \\
\text{Total MQTT Connection Cost} &\approx 5 \text{–}8 \text{ round-trips} \\
\text{CoAP over UDP} &= 1 \text{ round-trip (or 0 for NON messages)} \\
\text{Energy Ratio} &= \frac{E_{\text{MQTT}}}{E_{\text{CoAP}}} \approx 3 \text{ to } 5
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Determine QoS requirement:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MQTT QoS Levels} &= \{0, 1, 2\} \quad \text{(broker-mediated acknowledgment)} \\
\text{CoAP Confirmable} &= \text{CON with ACK + optional token} \\
\text{MQTT QoS 2} &= 4\text{-step handshake (PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP)} \\
\text{CoAP Non-confirmable} &= 0\text{-handshake (fire and forget)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering Rule of Thumb:**

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
\text{MQTT} &: \text{Stable IP network + reliable cloud broker + low message rate} \\
\text{CoAP} &: \text{Constrained LLN + multicast + battery-critical} \\
\text{HTTP} &: \text{Web dashboards + browser visualization}
\end{aligned}}$$

## 3.3 oneM2M Service Layer Standardization — Functional Architecture

The oneM2M standard (ETSI TS 118 101, ISO/IEC 30118) defines a horizontal service platform. Its derivation of common service functions (CSFs) is a textbook KTU question.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{oneM2M Service Architecture} &= \bigcup_{i=1}^{12} \text{CSF}_i
\end{aligned}$$

**The 12 Common Service Functions:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CSF} = \{&\text{Application Enablement (AEN), Security (SEC),} \\
&\text{Device Management (DMG), Discovery (DSR),} \\
&\text{Data Management (DM), Subscription/Notification (SUB),} \\
&\text{Group Management (GMG), Location (LOC),} \\
&\text{Network Service Exposure (NSE), Registration (REG),} \\
&\text{Communication Management (CMG), Service Charging (SCH)}\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Three-Point Reference Model:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Node Types} &= \{\text{Application Service Node (ASN)}, \\
&\quad \text{Application Dedicated Node (ADN)}, \\
&\quad \text{Middle Node (MN)}, \\
&\quad \text{Infrastructure Node (IN)}\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Reference Points (inter-entity interfaces):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Mca} &: \text{App ↔ Common Services} \\
\text{Mcc} &: \text{Common Service Entity ↔ Common Service Entity (CSE-to-CSE)} \\
\text{Mcn} &: \text{Common Services ↔ Underlying Network}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 IIRA (Industrial Internet Reference Architecture) — Three-Tier Model

The IIC's IIRA decomposition (derivation steps):

**Step 1 — Identify the three foundational concerns:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IIRA Concerns} = \langle \text{Functional View}, \text{Operational View}, \text{Usage View} \rangle
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the five functional domains (mapping to IIoT stack):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Domain Mapping} &= \{ \\
&\text{Control} \rightarrow \text{Edge sensors, PLCs} \\
&\text{Operations} \rightarrow \text{SCADA, HMI} \\
&\text{Information} \rightarrow \text{MES, ERP integration} \\
&\text{Application} \rightarrow \text{Analytics, AI} \\
&\text{Business} \rightarrow \text{Enterprise strategy layer} \\
\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-cutting functions (added in 2019 update):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cross-Cutting} = \langle \text{Connectivity}, \text{Edge Computing}, \text{AI/ML}, \\
\text{Security}, \text{Privacy}, \text{Safety}, \text{Resilience} \rangle
\end{aligned}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define protocol standardization in the context of IoT. Mention any two standardization bodies. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Protocol standardization in IoT refers to the development and adoption of common communication rules, data formats, and interoperability frameworks so that heterogeneous IoT devices and platforms can exchange data seamlessly. It prevents vendor lock-in and protocol fragmentation.

Two standardization bodies:
1. **IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force)** — develops CoAP, 6LoWPAN, RPL, MQTT-SN related RFCs.
2. **OASIS** — maintains the MQTT messaging standard used widely in IoT telemetry.

*(Other valid bodies: IEEE, ITU-T, ETSI, 3GPP, W3C, OMG, oneM2M.)*

**Mark Split:** Definition = 1.5 marks • Two bodies with example = 1.5 marks.

---

### Q2. List the three QoS levels supported by MQTT and state one application scenario where QoS level 2 is mandatory. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| QoS Level | Guarantee | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| QoS 0 | At most once (fire and forget) | Periodic telemetry where loss is acceptable |
| QoS 1 | At least once (possible duplicates) | Sensor updates with retry |
| QoS 2 | Exactly once (4-step handshake) | **Mandatory: Medical device control, e.g., insulin pump dose confirmation** |

QoS 2 is mandatory in **safety-critical biomedical applications** such as an **implantable insulin pump dose verification**, where duplicate or missing messages could be life-threatening.

**Mark Split:** List = 1.5 marks • Scenario with justification = 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the need for protocol standardization in IoT. List any **four** major standardization bodies and describe their primary contributions.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **MQTT and CoAP** protocols across **at least six** parameters with a justified conclusion on which is preferred for a **Class 1 constrained battery-powered sensor**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Need for Protocol Standardization (4 marks):**

1. **Interoperability:** Devices from different manufacturers must communicate. Standards like CoAP enable any compliant sensor to interface with any compliant gateway.
2. **Scalability:** Standardized lightweight protocols (6LoWPAN, RPL) allow millions of nodes to coexist on a single network.
3. **Security:** Standards such as DTLS, OSCORE (RFC 8613), and TLS 1.3 provide uniformly vetted cryptographic protection.
4. **Cost Reduction:** Compliance with standards reduces custom development, certification, and integration costs.
5. **Innovation Acceleration:** Developers can build atop stable specifications (e.g., W3C WoT Thing Description) instead of re-engineering transport.
6. **Regulatory Compliance:** Healthcare, automotive, and energy sectors mandate standards (HL7 FHIR, ISO 21434, IEC 62443).

**Four Standardization Bodies (3 marks):**

| Body | Contribution |
|---|---|
| **IETF** | CoAP (RFC 7252), 6LoWPAN (RFC 4944/6282), RPL (RFC 6550) |
| **IEEE** | 802.15.4 PHY/MAC, 802.11ah (HaLow) for sub-1 GHz Wi-Fi |
| **OASIS** | MQTT v5.0, AMQP 1.0 |
| **W3C** | WoT Thing Description, SSN/SOSA ontology for semantic sensor data |
| **ETSI** | oneM2M (TS 118 series), SAREF ontology, MEC |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | TCP (reliable, connection-oriented) | UDP (lightweight, connectionless) |
| Message Pattern | Publish/Subscribe via broker | Request/Response (RESTful) |
| Default Port | 1883 (TLS: 8883) | 5683 (DTLS: 5684) |
| Header Size | 2 bytes minimum fixed | 4 bytes typical, compact binary |
| QoS Levels | 0, 1, 2 (broker-mediated) | NON (no ack), CON (ack) |
| Power Consumption | High (TCP + TLS handshake) | Low (1 round-trip or zero) |
| Multicast Support | Not native (broker must fan-out) | Native UDP multicast |
| Suitability for Constrained Devices | Poor for Class 0/1 | Excellent for Class 0/1/2 |
| Web Integration | Needs bridge (e.g., WebSocket) | Native HTTP mapping (CoAP-HTTP proxy) |
| Use Case | Cloud telemetry, dashboards | Field sensors, mesh networks |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For a **Class 1 constrained battery-powered sensor** (e.g., a wearable ECG patch), **CoAP is preferred** because:
- UDP avoids the 3-way TCP handshake overhead.
- DTLS provides comparable security with 1 round-trip vs. TLS's 2 round-trips.
- The 4-byte CoAP header fits a 127-byte IEEE 802.15.4 frame efficiently.
- Native multicast enables firmware broadcast to many sensors simultaneously.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat architecture diagram, describe the **oneM2M service layer standard**. Explain the functions of **Mca, Mcc, and Mcn** reference points.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **IIC Industrial Internet Reference Architecture (IIRA)** with its three viewpoints and five functional domains. Mention any **three** cross-cutting concerns.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**oneM2M Architecture (5 marks):**

oneM2M is a global horizontal service platform standard (ETSI TS 118 101, ISO/IEC 30118) that enables IoT applications across vertical domains (health, energy, transport) to interoperate through a common services layer.

**Key Components:**

- **AE (Application Entity):** The IoT application logic (e.g., a remote patient monitoring app).
- **CSE (Common Services Entity):** The platform that hosts the 12 CSFs (Common Service Functions).
- **ASN (Application Service Node):** Hosts both AE and CSE on the same physical node.
- **ADN (Application Dedicated Node):** Hosts only an AE, relying on a remote CSE.
- **MN (Middle Node):** Forwards requests between CSEs.
- **IN (Infrastructure Node):** Provides the central CSE in the deployment.

**Reference Points (2 marks):**

- **Mca — Application ↔ Common Services:** Interface between an AE and its CSE. Used to register the application, subscribe to data, and invoke services.
- **Mcc — CSE ↔ CSE:** Interface between two CSEs in different nodes. Enables federated IoT data exchange across operators.
- **Mcn — CSE ↔ Underlying Network:** Interface between the CSE and transport/network services (e.g., 3GPP, Wi-Fi, 6LoWPAN).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**IIRA — Three Viewpoints (2 marks):**

1. **Functional View:** Describes what the system *does* — functional components and their interactions.
2. **Operational View:** Describes how the system is *operated* — roles, responsibilities, lifecycle.
3. **Usage View:** Describes how the system is *used* — use cases and stakeholders.

**Five Functional Domains (3 marks):**

| Domain | Role |
|---|---|
| **Control** | Edge sensors, actuators, PLCs — close-to-physical data acquisition |
| **Operations** | SCADA, HMI, monitoring, supervisory control |
| **Information** | MES, ERP integration, data warehousing |
| **Application** | Analytics, AI/ML inference, business logic |
| **Business** | Strategic decision-making, enterprise KPIs |

**Three Cross-Cutting Concerns (2 marks):**

1. **Security & Privacy** — identity, authentication, encryption, GDPR compliance.
2. **Safety** — fail-safe operation, IEC 61508 functional safety.
3. **Resilience** — fault tolerance, redundancy, self-healing.

*(Other acceptable: Connectivity, Edge Computing, AI/ML integration.)*

**KTU Examiner's Final Mark Distribution Hint:** Diagram = 1 mark • Component explanation = 3 marks • Reference points = 2 marks • IIRA views = 1.5 marks • Domains table = 2 marks • Cross-cutting = 1.5 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing MQTT ports (1883) with AMQP (5672).** Losing 0.5 marks instantly.
> 2. **Writing "HTTP" instead of "CoAP"** when describing Class 1 sensors. Examiners deduct 1 mark for protocol mismatch with device class.
> 3. **Omitting the broker in the MQTT diagram.** Always show the broker as a central node connecting publishers and subscribers.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention DTLS** alongside CoAP — security-aware answers always score higher.
> 5. **Citing non-existent "IoT 5.0" or "MQTT 6.0"** standards. Stick to **MQTT v5.0 (OASIS 2019)** and **CoAP RFC 7252 (2014)**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (R&C for Module 2):**

- **Standardization Bodies Quick-Reference:**
  - *IETF* → CoAP, 6LoWPAN, RPL, CBOR
  - *OASIS* → MQTT, AMQP
  - *IEEE* → 802.15.4, 802.11
  - *W3C* → WoT TD, SSN/SOSA
  - *ETSI* → oneM2M, SAREF
  - *3GPP* → LTE-M, NB-IoT, 5G mMTC
  - *OMG* → DDS
  - *CSA* → Matter, Thread
  - *ISO/IEC JTC 1/SC 41* → Global IoT standards
  - *IIC* → IIRA

- **Numeric Must-Knows:**
  - IEEE 802.15.4 max frame = **127 bytes**
  - IPv6 header = **40 bytes**; Compressed = 2 bytes
  - MQTT port = **1883** (TLS 8883)
  - CoAP port = **5683** (DTLS 5684)
  - 6LoWPAN saves ~38 bytes per packet
  - 5G mMTC target = **1 million devices / km²**

- **Three Comparison Triads to Memorize:**
  - **MQTT (TCP, pub/sub) vs. CoAP (UDP, REST) vs. HTTP (TCP, REST)**
  - **6LoWPAN (compression) vs. RPL (routing) vs. CoAP (app)**
  - **oneM2M (horizontal) vs. IIRA (functional) vs. RAMI 4.0 (industrial)**

- **Conceptual Hooks for 14-Mark Answers:**
  - Always include a **diagram** (at least one).
  - Always cite an **RFC number or standard series** (e.g., "RFC 7252").
  - Always justify with **a real-world biomedical example** (ECG monitor, insulin pump, MRI telemetry).
  - Always mention **security** (DTLS, TLS, OSCORE).

- **Biomedical-Specific Standardization to Remember:**
  - **HL7 FHIR** — health data exchange
  - **IEEE 11073** — personal health device communication
  - **Continua Design Guidelines** — end-to-end personal connected health
  - **DICOM** — medical imaging interoperability

- **Final Mantra:** "Standardization is the **TCP/IP of IoT** — without it, every device is an island."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** M2M and WSN Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Machine-to-Machine (M2M) Communication

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** M2M communication is a broad label that can be used to describe any technology that enables networked devices to exchange information and perform actions without the manual assistance of humans. It is the foundational, pre-IoT paradigm of autonomous device-to-device data exchange.

In the strictest KTU 2024 scheme context, M2M is treated as a **point-to-point (or point-to-multipoint)** communication paradigm where physical assets, sensors, and actuators talk to each other over wired or wireless channels using public or private network infrastructure.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Automatic Vending Machine" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain English Intuition:** Imagine a Coca-Cola vending machine on a college campus. It is fitted with a stock sensor and a temperature sensor. When the stock falls below 10 cans, the machine **automatically** dials a backend server (using GSM/cellular) and places a refill order. **No human** pressed a button, and **no human** received the alert. That is M2M.

The four canonical pillars of any M2M system, as adopted by **ETSI TS 102 689**, are:

| Pillar | Standard Metric / Property | Role in M2M |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensing / Actuation** | Sensor density $\geq 1$ node/m² (indoor) | Converts physical phenomena $\rightarrow$ electrical signals |
| **Communication** | Standard: **IEEE 802.15.x** / Cellular (LTE-M, NB-IoT) | Transport of telemetry data |
| **Computing** | Embedded MCU 8/32-bit | Local processing, aggregation |
| **Services** | Vertical-specific (healthcare, logistics) | Application layer value creation |

## 1.2 Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) is a self-configuring infrastructure-less network of hundreds-to-thousands of spatially distributed autonomous sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions, such as temperature, sound, vibration, pressure, motion, or pollutants, at cooperating nodes.

The **five standard operational characteristics** of a WSN, which the KTU examiner will expect you to enumerate, are:

- **Self-organization** (no fixed router/AP)
- **Multi-hop routing** (because radio range is small)
- **Energy harvesting / battery-limited** — typical node energy budget: **~2 AA batteries ($\approx$ 2.7 Wh)**
- **Data-centric addressing** (queries, not IDs)
- **Tiny, cheap, low-duty-cycle** nodes

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — "A Colony of Fire Ants"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain English Intuition:** Think of 100 fire ants spread across a forest floor. Each ant can only "smell" (sense) within 1 cm and "signal" within 10 cm. No single ant knows the full picture, but by passing tiny chemical "packets" ant-to-ant, the colony collectively detects a forest fire 50 m away. The colony is the **WSN**, each ant is a **mote/sensor node**, and the chemical trails are the **multi-hop routing paths**. If an ant dies (battery dies), the colony reroutes around the dead ant. That is **self-healing mesh routing**.

## 1.3 Why M2M and WSN Belong Together in KTU Module 2

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups M2M and WSN protocols because:

- M2M is the **macro-level** paradigm (e.g., a nationwide fleet of smart meters).
- WSN is the **micro-level** enabler (e.g., the ZigBee mesh inside a single smart meter cluster).
- Together they form the **perception + transport layer** of the 5-layer IoT Architecture Model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Comparison Snapshot:**
> - **M2M** = "things talking to things" — usually **vertical silos**, cellular/PLMN backhaul, high cost per node.
> - **WSN** = "sensors forming a mesh" — usually **horizontal collaboration**, IEEE 802.15.4, ultra-low cost per node.
> - **M2M + WSN** = the **edge intelligence** layer that feeds IoT.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Performance Metrics (to memorize)

- **ISM Band for WSN:** $f = 2.4\ \text{GHz}$ (worldwide, royalty-free)
- **IEEE 802.15.4 PHY data rate:** $250\ \text{kbps}$ (O-QPSK in 2.4 GHz)
- **Typical transmission range:** $\sim 10$–$100$ m outdoor
- **Standard node battery:** 2 × AA = **3 V**, $\sim$ 2200 mAh
- **Average current draw (TX):** $\sim 17.4$ mA (CC2420 radio)
- **Duty cycle target:** $< 1\%$ for longevity
- **Critical threshold (energy):** $E_{\text{residual}} < E_{\text{threshold}} \Rightarrow$ node marked **dead**

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The M2M Communication Architecture (ETSI / OneM2M View)

The **ETSI M2M** reference model (later merged into the **OneM2M** global standard, **ETSI TS 118 101**) consists of three functional domains:

1. **M2M Device Domain** — the sensors, actuators, RFID tags, smart meters.
2. **Network Domain** — provides IP connectivity, GSM, LTE-M, NB-IoT, Wi-Fi, satellite.
3. **Application Domain** — the vertical business logic (e.g., fleet management dashboard, hospital EMR system).

Within these domains sit **four horizontal layers** (the Service Capabilities Layer, SCL):

| Layer | Function | Key Protocols |
|---|---|---|
| **Application Layer** | Business logic | HTTP, CoAP, MQTT, XMPP |
| **Service Capabilities** | Common services (discovery, security) | OneM2M primitives |
| **Network / Transport** | End-to-end packet delivery | TCP/UDP, IPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL |
| **Device / Sensing** | Local sensing/actuation | IEEE 802.15.4, BLE, Modbus |

## 2.2 WSN Protocol Stack — Layer-by-Layer Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** The examiner often asks to "draw and explain the WSN protocol stack". You MUST discuss every layer below.

### 2.2.1 Physical Layer

- **Modulation:** O-QPSK / BPSK
- **Encoding:** DSSS (Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum), chip rate $2$ Mchips/s
- **Power control** is the #1 tool for energy conservation at this layer.

### 2.2.2 Data Link Layer (MAC Sub-layer)

- Responsible for **channel access**, **collision avoidance**, and **duty cycling**.
- Three families:
  1. **Contention-based:** CSMA/CA (used in IEEE 802.15.4), S-MAC, B-MAC, X-MAC
  2. **Schedule-based (TDMA-like):** LEACH, TRAMA
  3. **Hybrid:** Z-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4e TSCH

### 2.2.3 Network Layer (Routing)

- **Data-centric:** SPIN, Directed Diffusion, Rumor Routing
- **Hierarchical (cluster-based):** LEACH, PEGASIS, TEEN, APTEEN
- **Location-based:** GEAR, GPSR
- **QoS-aware:** SAR, SPEED
- **Reactive + Proactive hybrid:** RPL (for IPv6/6LoWPAN)

### 2.2.4 Transport Layer

- Mostly **UDP** preferred over TCP because of the small, bursty nature of sensor traffic.
- **Reliability** is handled by application-layer protocols like CoAP (with DTD/Block).

### 2.2.5 Application Layer

- **MQTT** (publish/subscribe over TCP)
- **CoAP** (request/response over UDP, mapped to REST)
- **AMQP, DDS, XMPP, LwM2M** (lightweight M2M for device management)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` and `\mid` (NOT raw `|`) when expressing absolute value or norm in any formula to keep markdown tables intact.

### 2.3.1 Energy Consumption Model (Heinzelman / LEACH First-Order Radio Model)

$$
E_{Tx}(k, d) = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{\text{amp}} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}
$$

$$
E_{Rx}(k) = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $k$ | Packet size (bits) | $4000$ bits (default) |
| $d$ | Distance to receiver (m) | variable |
| $n$ | Path-loss exponent | $2$ (free space) or $4$ (multipath) |
| $E_{\text{elec}}$ | Electronics energy per bit | $50$ nJ/bit |
| $\varepsilon_{\text{amp}}$ | Amplifier energy | $100$ pJ/bit/m² ($n=2$) |
| $\varepsilon_{\text{amp}}$ | Amplifier energy | $0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴ ($n=4$) |

### 2.3.2 Network Lifetime Metrics

$$
T_{\text{network}} = \min_{i \in \{1 \dots N\}} \left( t_{\text{death}}(i) \right)
$$

$$
E_{\text{consumed}}(i, t) = \sum_{j=1}^{M} E_{Tx}^{(i)}(k_{j}, d_{j}) + \sum_{j=1}^{M} E_{Rx}^{(i)}(k_{j})
$$

### 2.3.3 LEACH Optimal Cluster Head Probability

$$
p_{\text{opt}} = \frac{k_{\text{opt}}}{N} \quad \text{where} \quad k_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{fs}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{mp}}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{\text{toBS}}^{2}}
$$

### 2.3.4 M2M Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)

| KPI | Definition | Target (KTU) |
|---|---|---|
| **MTC Device Density** | Devices per km² | $10^{6}$ devices/km² (5G mMTC) |
| **Latency** | End-to-end delay | $< 1$ s (mMTC) / $< 1$ ms (URLLC) |
| **Battery Life** | Without recharge | $10$ years (NB-IoT target) |
| **Coverage** | Maximum Coupling Loss (MCL) | $\geq 164$ dB |
| **Packet Loss** | At application layer | $< 10^{-3}$ |

### 2.3.5 WSN MAC Throughput

$$
S = \frac{P_{\text{succ}} \cdot E[L]}{E[\text{slot duration}]}
$$

Where $P_{\text{succ}}$ is the probability of successful transmission and $E[L]$ is the average payload length.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** WSN mesh of soil-moisture sensors (ZigBee) feeds an M2M cellular gateway that pushes to cloud.
- **Telemedicine:** WSN of ECG/BP sensors on patient $\to$ Bluetooth M2M link $\to$ hospital server.
- **Industrial IoT:** Modbus-RTU M2M on factory floor $\leftrightarrow$ WSN of vibration sensors on motors.
- **Smart City:** 1000s of WSN nodes + M2M cellular backhaul for parking, lighting, waste management.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Dissipated per Round in LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)

This is the **most-tested derivation** in any KTU WSN paper. We derive the total energy consumed by a cluster-head (CH) and by a member node in a single round of LEACH operation.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Define the Round

A "round" in LEACH consists of:

- **Setup phase:** Cluster formation, CH election.
- **Steady-state phase:** TDMA-based intra-cluster communication, then one-hop CH-to-BS transmission.

Let $N$ be the total number of nodes and $k$ be the number of clusters. Average cluster size is $N/k$. Each CH has $N/k - 1$ member nodes.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Energy Used by a Member Node (one frame, one packet)

A member node sends a $k$-bit packet to its CH over distance $d_{\text{toCH}}$. Using the free-space model ($n=2$):

$$
E_{\text{member}} = E_{Tx}(k, d_{\text{toCH}}) = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{\text{fs}} \cdot k \cdot d_{\text{toCH}}^{2}
$$

**Logic:** The first term is the fixed cost of running the radio electronics; the second is the radiated RF power needed to overcome path loss in free space.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Energy Used by a Cluster Head (per round)

A CH must (a) receive $(N/k - 1)$ packets, (b) aggregate them, and (c) send the aggregated bit-stream to the BS over distance $d_{\text{toBS}}$.

**Receive cost** (for each of the $N/k - 1$ packets, size $k$ bits):

$$
E_{\text{RX,CH}} = (N/k - 1) \cdot E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k
$$

**Transmit cost to BS** (single packet, equal to $N/k \cdot k$ bits after aggregation; but for analysis, we model it as a single $L$-bit message over the multipath channel, since $d_{\text{toBS}}$ is typically large):

$$
E_{\text{TX,CH-to-BS}} = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot L + \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} \cdot L \cdot d_{\text{toBS}}^{4}
$$

**Total per CH per round:**

$$
E_{\text{CH}} = (N/k - 1) \cdot E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k + E_{\text{DA}} \cdot (N/k) \cdot k + E_{\text{elec}} \cdot L + \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} \cdot L \cdot d_{\text{toBS}}^{4}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of each term:** (1) receive all member packets, (2) aggregate (data aggregation cost $E_{\text{DA}}$), (3) transmit a single combined packet, (4) the amplifier term dominates when $d_{\text{toBS}}$ is large (multipath model, $n=4$).

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Total Energy per Round

There are $k$ cluster heads and $(N-k)$ member nodes:

$$
E_{\text{round}} = k \cdot E_{\text{CH}} + (N - k) \cdot E_{\text{member}}
$$

Substituting and simplifying (assuming $N/k \gg 1$, so $N/k - 1 \approx N/k$):

$$
E_{\text{round}} \approx k \cdot \left[ (N/k) \cdot E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k + E_{\text{DA}} \cdot (N/k) \cdot k + \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} \cdot L \cdot d_{\text{toBS}}^{4} \right] + (N - k) \cdot \left[ \varepsilon_{\text{fs}} \cdot k \cdot d_{\text{toCH}}^{2} \right]
$$

$$
E_{\text{round}} \approx N \cdot E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k + N \cdot E_{\text{DA}} \cdot k + k \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} \cdot L \cdot d_{\text{toBS}}^{4} + (N - k) \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{fs}} \cdot k \cdot d_{\text{toCH}}^{2}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Average Distance to Cluster Head

Assume a square deployment area of side $M$ and uniform distribution. The expected squared distance from a node to its CH is:

$$
E[d_{\text{toCH}}^{2}] = \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}
$$

**Derivation logic:** Each cluster occupies area $M^2/k$; a uniformly chosen node has expected squared distance to a random centroid $= \text{cluster area} / (2\pi)$.

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Final Closed-Form Energy per Round

$$
E_{\text{round}} = N \cdot E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k + N \cdot E_{\text{DA}} \cdot k + k \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} \cdot L \cdot d_{\text{toBS}}^{4} + (N - k) \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{fs}} \cdot k \cdot \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}
$$

$$
E_{\text{round}} = N \cdot k \cdot (E_{\text{elec}} + E_{\text{DA}}) + k \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} \cdot L \cdot d_{\text{toBS}}^{4} + \frac{N \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{fs}} \cdot k \cdot M^{2}}{2\pi k}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{\text{round}} = N \cdot k \cdot (E_{\text{elec}} + E_{\text{DA}}) + k \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} \cdot L \cdot d_{\text{toBS}}^{4} + \frac{N \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{fs}} \cdot M^{2}}{2\pi}}
$$

**KTU Exam Tip:** This is the boxed formula. State it explicitly at the end of the derivation.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of WSN Routing Protocols

| Protocol | Category | Energy Efficiency | Data Aggregation | Scalability | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SPIN** | Data-centric, negotiation-based | High (no redundant data) | Yes (meta-data) | Medium | ★★★ |
| **Directed Diffusion** | Data-centric, attribute-based | High (path reinforcement) | Yes | Medium | ★★★ |
| **LEACH** | Hierarchical, cluster-based | Very High (CH rotation) | Yes (CH fusion) | High | ★★★★★ |
| **PEGASIS** | Hierarchical, chain-based | Higher than LEACH | Yes | Low (chain delay) | ★★★★ |
| **TEEN** | Hierarchical, threshold-reactive | Very High (low traffic) | Yes | High | ★★★ |
| **GEAR** | Location-based | High | Limited | High | ★★ |
| **RPL** | IPv6/6LoWPAN, DODAG | Tunable (objective function) | Optional | Very High | ★★★★ |

## 3.3 M2M vs WSN — Numerical / Conceptual Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | M2M (Cellular) | WSN (802.15.4) |
|---|---|---|
| Communication Range | $\sim 1$ – $35$ km (cellular) | $\sim 10$ – $100$ m |
| Number of Nodes per Network | $10^{2}$ – $10^{4}$ | $10^{2}$ – $10^{5}$ |
| Power Source | Mains-powered | Battery / harvested |
| Data Rate | LTE: $100$ Mbps / NB-IoT: $200$ kbps | $20$ – $250$ kbps |
| Standard Body | 3GPP, ETSI | IEEE 802.15, IETF |
| IP Address | Public IP per device (LPWA) | IPv6/6LoWPAN compressed |
| Cost per Node | $\$\ 30$ – $\$\ 100$ | $\$\ 1$ – $\$\ 10$ |
| Topology | Star, point-to-PLMN | Mesh, multi-hop, ad-hoc |
| Latency | $< 1$ s | $< 1$ ms (intra-cluster) |
| Primary Use | Asset tracking, metering | Environmental monitoring |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Short-answer questions. Two to three sentences per answer, one or two crisp points. No sub-parts.

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
Differentiate between M2M communication and IoT communication in three points.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **M2M** is a *device-to-device* communication model, often over **cellular networks**, designed for **vertical, isolated silos** (e.g., a single fleet of vending machines). It is **peer-to-master** and does not require internet.
- **IoT** is a *device-to-cloud-to-application* model, **IP-based**, **horizontally integrated** (multiple vertical domains share one platform), and supports **analytics, ML, and visualization**.
- **M2M** uses proprietary or telecom protocols (SMS, CSD); **IoT** uses **standard web protocols** like HTTP, MQTT, CoAP, IPv6.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
List any three salient features of a Wireless Sensor Network.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Self-organization & self-healing** — nodes can form an ad-hoc mesh and reroute on failure.
- **Energy-constrained operation** — nodes run on 2 × AA ($\approx 2.7$ Wh) with a target lifetime of **months to years**.
- **Multi-hop communication** — packet is relayed across many low-power radio hops rather than a single long-range transmission.
- (Bonus 1 mark point) **Data-centric addressing** — queries are sent to the *region* or *event*, not to a specific node ID.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question is 14 marks. It is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either Question A or Question B** in full on the answer booklet. The model solutions below show what is expected for **full 14 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the M2M communication architecture with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **ETSI M2M** functional domains.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **WSN protocol stack**. Differentiate between the **physical, MAC, and routing layers** in terms of energy consumption.

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

1. **M2M Architecture — block diagram (3 marks):** Draw the three ETSI domains — **Device, Network, Application** — with arrows showing the *uplink telemetry* path and *downlink command* path. (A 3-tier ladder is sufficient.)

2. **Device Domain (1 mark):** Hosts the sensors, RFID tags, smart meters, and actuators. Communicates via ZigBee, M-Bus, Modbus, or PLC. No human in the loop.

3. **Network Domain (1 mark):** Provides the bearer — GSM, GPRS, LTE-M, NB-IoT, satellite. Performs address translation, authentication, and session management.

4. **Application Domain (2 marks):** Hosts the **M2M Service Capabilities Layer (SCL)** — functions such as *discovery, registration, security, group management, device management, subscription/notification*. Vertical applications (fleet mgmt, smart grid, eHealth) sit on top of the SCL.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

1. **Diagram (2 marks):** Draw the 5-layer WSN stack — *Application, Transport, Network, Data Link (MAC), Physical* — with example protocols in each layer (CoAP, UDP, RPL/LEACH, IEEE 802.15.4, O-QPSK/2.4 GHz).

2. **Physical layer (1.5 marks):** Dominated by **transmit energy** $\varepsilon_{\text{amp}} \cdot d^{n}$. Choice of $n$ (free-space vs multipath) directly determines battery life.

3. **MAC layer (1.5 marks):** Energy is wasted on **idle listening, collisions, overhearing, control-packet overhead**. Solutions: S-MAC (sleep/wake schedule), B-MAC (LPL preamble), IEEE 802.15.4 (slotted CSMA/CA + beaconing).

4. **Routing layer (2 marks):** Energy is spent on **multi-hop forwarding**. Hierarchical (LEACH, PEGASIS) and data-centric (SPIN) protocols cut energy by reducing redundant transmissions and aggregating en route.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **LEACH** protocol for WSNs. Explain cluster-head selection mechanism, the threshold $T(n)$, and the steady-state phase.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **total energy consumed per round** in LEACH. Use the first-order radio model and assume $N$ nodes, $k$ clusters, packet size $k$ bits, and distance from CH to BS = $d_{\text{toBS}}$.

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

1. **Overview (1 mark):** LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy) by Heinzelman et al. (2000). **Self-organizing, adaptive, cluster-based**, with **randomized rotation** of cluster heads to evenly distribute energy load.

2. **Cluster-Head election (3 marks):** Each node picks a random number $\alpha \in [0,1]$. If $\alpha < T(n)$, the node becomes a CH for the current round. The threshold is:

$$
T(n) = \begin{cases}
\dfrac{p}{1 - p \cdot \left( r \cdot \bmod \dfrac{1}{p} \right)} & \text{if } n \in G \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Where $p$ = desired CH percentage, $r$ = current round, $G$ = set of nodes that have **not** been CHs in the last $1/p$ rounds. This guarantees every node serves as CH exactly once in $1/p$ rounds.

3. **Setup phase (1 mark):** CHs broadcast ADV; non-CHs pick CH with **strongest received signal** (closest). They reply with a **Join-REQ** (CSMA). CH builds a **TDMA schedule** and broadcasts it.

4. **Steady-state phase (2 marks):** Members transmit in their **allocated slot** (sleep otherwise). CH receives from all members, applies **signal-processing/data aggregation** ($E_{\text{DA}}$ per bit), and sends a single fused packet to the BS using a **fixed CDMA code** to avoid inter-cluster interference. After a fixed time, a new round begins.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

(Write the derivation from **Section 3.1** step-by-step.)

1. **Define member energy** (1 mark):

$$
E_{\text{member}} = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{\text{fs}} \cdot k \cdot d_{\text{toCH}}^{2}
$$

2. **Define CH energy** (2 marks):

$$
E_{\text{CH}} = (N/k - 1) \cdot E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k + E_{\text{DA}} \cdot (N/k) \cdot k + E_{\text{elec}} \cdot L + \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} \cdot L \cdot d_{\text{toBS}}^{4}
$$

3. **Sum over all nodes in the round** (2 marks):

$$
E_{\text{round}} = k \cdot E_{\text{CH}} + (N - k) \cdot E_{\text{member}}
$$

4. **Substitute $E[d_{\text{toCH}}^{2}] = M^{2}/(2\pi k)$** and simplify (1 mark) to obtain:

$$
E_{\text{round}} = N \cdot k \cdot (E_{\text{elec}} + E_{\text{DA}}) + k \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} \cdot L \cdot d_{\text{toBS}}^{4} + \frac{N \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{fs}} \cdot M^{2}}{2\pi}
$$

5. **Interpretation (1 mark):** The first term is the *aggregation + electronics* cost (linear in $N$ and $k$). The second term is the *CH-to-BS transmission* cost (dominates when $d_{\text{toBS}}$ is large). The third term is the *intra-cluster* cost (decreases as the number of clusters $k$ increases).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in this topic (and how to avoid them):**
>
> 1. **Confusing MAC with routing.** MAC is layer 2 (channel access, sleep cycles); routing is layer 3 (path selection, multi-hop). Examiners deduct **2 marks** if these are mixed.
> 2. **Forgetting the "$T(n)$" piecewise form.** Many students write $T(n) = p/(1 - pr)$ without the modulo term. The modulo $1/p$ is what makes rotation fair — examiners expect it.
> 3. **Using $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $|$ in LaTeX tables.** This breaks markdown table rendering. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in any formula inside a markdown table.
> 4. **Skipping units.** Always write **bits, joules, m, seconds, kbps** next to every variable in the derivation.
> 5. **Drawing a "physical network" instead of a "functional block diagram"** in Mermaid. Reserve physical drawings for sketches; the Mermaid block should be a clean **architectural flow**.
> 6. **Not stating the constant values** ($E_{\text{elec}} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{\text{fs}} = 100$ pJ/bit/m², $\varepsilon_{\text{mp}} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴) in numerical problems. Examiners give 1 mark just for plugging in the correct constants.
> 7. **Mixing up M2M vs IoT in 3-mark questions.** If the question says "M2M", do NOT bring in cloud or ML. Stay vertical and silo-based.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-page revision list — read this the night before the exam.**

- [ ] **M2M = Machine-to-Machine**, **point-to-point** over **cellular/PLMN**, **vertical silos**, ETSI domains = *Device / Network / Application*, SCL = *Service Capabilities Layer*.
- [ ] **WSN = self-configuring, multi-hop, energy-limited, data-centric, ad-hoc mesh** of tiny motes (IEEE 802.15.4, 2.4 GHz, 250 kbps, O-QPSK).
- [ ] **WSN Protocol Stack (bottom-up):** PHY $\to$ MAC (802.15.4, S-MAC, B-MAC) $\to$ Network (LEACH, SPIN, RPL) $\to$ Transport (UDP) $\to$ Application (CoAP, MQTT).
- [ ] **LEACH key formula** — threshold for CH election:

$$
T(n) = \frac{p}{1 - p \cdot (r \bmod 1/p)}
$$

- [ ] **LEACH per-round energy** (boxed, memorize):

$$
E_{\text{round}} = N \cdot k \cdot (E_{\text{elec}} + E_{\text{DA}}) + k \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} \cdot L \cdot d_{\text{toBS}}^{4} + \frac{N \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{fs}} \cdot M^{2}}{2\pi}
$$

- [ ] **First-order radio model:**

$$
E_{Tx}(k, d) = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{\text{amp}} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}, \qquad E_{Rx}(k) = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k
$$

- [ ] **Five WSN routing families:** *Data-centric (SPIN, Directed Diffusion), Hierarchical (LEACH, PEGASIS, TEEN, APTEEN), Location-based (GEAR, GPSR), QoS-based (SAR, SPEED), RPL (IPv6/6LoWPAN).*
- [ ] **Application-layer protocols:** **CoAP** = REST over UDP, **MQTT** = pub/sub over TCP, **LwM2M** = device management over CoAP.
- [ ] **Differentiators (3 marks exam favorite):** M2M = vertical, cellular, high cost. WSN = horizontal, mesh, low cost. IoT = IP-based, cloud, integration of both.
- [ ] **Key physical constants** to memorize: ISM band = **2.4 GHz**, PHY rate = **250 kbps**, battery = **2 × AA = 3 V**, target duty cycle = **$< 1\%$**, 5G mMTC density = **$10^6$ devices/km²**, NB-IoT MCL = **164 dB**.
- [ ] **RPL** = Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks (LLNs); uses **DODAG** with **Objective Functions** (OF0, MRHOF) for routing in IPv6/6LoWPAN WSNs.
- [ ] **SPIN** negotiation: ADV $\to$ REQ $\to$ DATA, eliminates redundant data via meta-data.
- [ ] **Directed Diffusion**: interest dissemination, gradient setup, reinforcement, path with highest gradient wins.
- [ ] **PEGASIS** forms a **chain** (greedy neighbor choice) and uses **token passing** to send one aggregated packet to BS — saves energy vs LEACH but adds delay.
- [ ] **TEEN** = Threshold-sensitive Energy-Efficient protocol — nodes transmit **only** when sensed value crosses hard/soft thresholds.
- [ ] **Always write units in derivations.** Always **box the final formula.** Always **state assumptions explicitly.** Always draw a **block diagram**, not a "cloud" — examiners mark diagrams strictly.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — M2M and WSN Protocols**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SCADA and RFID Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** SCADA and RFID Protocols ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 SCADA — Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition **Formal Definition:** > SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) is a centralized industrial control architecture that uses networked data communication systems, graphical human-machine interfaces (HMI), and remote terminal units (RTUs)/programmable logic controllers (PLCs) to monitor, gather, and process real-time data from geographically dispersed assets, with the goal of controlling industrial, infrastructure, or facility-based processes. In the IoT paradigm, SCADA evolves into a **cyber-physical observation layer** where legacy industrial equipment is retrofitted with IP-enabled gateways, MQTT brokers, and cloud historians to form modern **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** supervisory networks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** SCADA must be studied as a *protocol-driven real-time telemetry framework*, not merely as a "control system." The focus is on its layered architecture, the role of communication protocols (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870-5, OPC UA), and its convergence with IoT. **Conceptual Analogy — The "Air Traffic Control Tower"** Imagine a busy international airport. Hundreds of aircraft (industrial field devices: pumps, valves, transformers, circuit breakers) are operating on the ground and in the air. A central control tower (the SCADA server) communicates with each aircraft via radio (the communication protocol). The tower sees the position, altitude, fuel, and status of every plane (real-time data acquisition) and can issue instructions such as "climb to 30,000 feet" or "hold at runway 27" (supervisory control). The radar screens and controller displays (HMI) let the operators visualize and interact. The pilots themselves execute the commands (PLCs/RTUs perform local control). Just as the tower cannot fly the plane, SCADA does not directly actuate valves or breakers — it *supervises* and *instructs* local controllers, which then perform deterministic local action. --- ### 1.2 RFID — Radio Frequency Identification **Formal Definition:** > RFID is a non-contact, automatic identification technology that uses electromagnetic fields at radio frequencies to uniquely identify, track, and manage objects, animals, or people carrying an electronic tag. The system consists of a transponder (tag), an interrogator (reader), and an application host, communicating via standardized air-interface protocols (ISO/IEC 18000 series, EPC Class-1 Gen-2). **Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Sticker at the Toll Booth"** Think of the FASTag you stick on a car windshield. As the car passes under a toll plaza gantry, an antenna in the gantry emits a radio signal. The tiny chip in the FASTag wakes up, transmits its unique ID back, and your toll is deducted — all in under **300 milliseconds**, without stopping. Replace the car with a blood sample vial, a surgical instrument, a hospital bed, or a patient wristband, and you have **biomedical RFID**: contactless, hands-free, and error-proof identification in clinical workflows. > [!NOTE] > **Core Distinction (Barcode vs. RFID):** > - **Barcode:** Line-of-sight required, single read at a time, no memory, costs < ₹0.10. > - **RFID:** No line-of-sight, bulk simultaneous read (anti-collision), rewritable memory, costs ₹5–₹500, supports cryptographic authentication. --- ### 1.3 SCADA vs. RFID — The IoT Connection | Aspect | SCADA | RFID | |--------|-------|------| | Primary Function | Real-time process control & telemetry | Identification & tracking | | Data Direction | Bidirectional (telemetry + commands) | Mainly tag → reader (uplink) | | Latency | Sub-second (10–500 ms typical) | Sub-300 ms passive, <10 ms active | | Network Span | Plant-wide to multi-national grid | Centimeters to ~100 meters | | IoT Role | Industrial IoT (IIoT) backbone | Smart sensing & supply chain layer | In biomedical IoT, **RFID tags are the "nervous endings" that report**, and **SCADA-like supervisory dashboards (cloud historians) are the "brain stem"** that aggregates, alarms, and actuates. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 SCADA — Layered Architecture Breakdown A modern SCADA system is structured in **four functional layers**, each with distinct responsibilities: #### Layer 1 — Instrumentation & Field Devices (Level 0) - **Sensors:** temperature RTDs, pressure transducers, flow meters, ECG electrodes, SpO₂ probes. - **Actuators:** solenoid valves, motor contactors, variable frequency drives (VFDs), infusion pump motors. - **Local controllers:** PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers), RTUs (Remote Terminal Units), Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs) in substations. > [!NOTE] > **PLC vs. RTU — Examiner's Favorite Distinction:** > - **PLC:** Designed for factory-floor deterministic logic, ladder-logic programming, short cable runs, high I/O density. > - **RTU:** Designed for geographically remote, telemetry-heavy sites (oil wells, substations), battery/solar powered, radio/satellite link, often with built-in protocol support for DNP3/IEC 60870. #### Layer 2 — Communication / Network Layer (Level 1) - Fieldbus protocols: **Modbus RTU/TCP, Profibus, Foundation Fieldbus, DeviceNet, CAN, HART**. - Wide-area protocols: **DNP3, IEC 60870-5-101/104, IEC 61850** (substation). - Modern IIoT protocols: **MQTT, OPC UA, CoAP, AMQP**. #### Layer 3 — Control & Supervision (Level 2) - **SCADA Server / MTU (Master Terminal Unit):** Runs the real-time database, alarm engines, historian, and script logic. - **HMI (Human-Machine Interface):** Graphical mimics, trends, alarm logs. - **Historian:** Time-series database (e.g., OSIsoft PI, Wonderware Historian). #### Layer 4 — Enterprise / Cloud (Level 3 / Level 4) - **ERP/MES integration:** SAP, manufacturing execution systems. - **Cloud IoT platforms:** AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, ThingsBoard, Ignition. - **Analytics & ML pipelines** for predictive maintenance. --- ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Layer - **Why Level 0?** Real-world processes are physical — measurement and actuation must happen at the edge. - **Why Level 1?** Standardized protocols allow interoperability between vendors (a Siemens PLC talking to a Schneider RTU). - **Why Level 2?** Humans need a *single pane of glass* — operators cannot look at 10,000 raw tag values; SCADA aggregates, alarms, and presents context. - **Why Level 3–4?** Optimization and business decisions require historical data, cross-site correlation, and AI/ML — which local controllers cannot perform. --- ### 2.3 RFID — System Components & Operational Theory #### 2.3.1 Components 1. **Tag (Transponder):** Contains a microchip + antenna. Stores a unique ID (EPC — Electronic Product Code, typically **96 bits**) and optional user memory. 2. **Reader (Interrogator):** Generates the RF field, decodes the backscattered signal, communicates with the host. 3. **Antenna:** Matched to the band; determines read zone shape. 4. **Middleware/Host:** Filters, deduplicates, and forwards data to ERP/IoT platforms. #### 2.3.2 Energy Transfer Mechanisms - **Passive tags:** Harvest energy from the reader's RF field via **inductive coupling** (HF, near-field) or **propagating electromagnetic waves** (UHF, far-field). No battery; lifespan = decades. - **Active tags:** Battery-powered; transmit their own signal; range up to 100 m. - **Semi-passive (semi-active):** Battery powers only the chip; communication still backscattered. #### 2.3.3 The Backscatter Modulation Equation When a passive tag reflects the reader's continuous-wave signal, it modulates its antenna's radar cross-section (RCS) by toggling its input impedance between matched (absorbing) and mismatched (reflecting) states. The differential RCS is: $$\sigma_{tag} = \frac{\lambda^{2}}{4\pi} \cdot G_{tag}^{2} \cdot \Gamma^{2}$$ where $\lambda$ is the wavelength, $G_{tag}$ is the tag antenna gain, and $\Gamma$ is the reflection coefficient modulation depth. This is the **physical origin** of the tag's "reply." --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet #### 2.4.1 SCADA & Industrial Telemetry | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes | |----------|---------------------|---------------| | Shannon–Hartley channel capacity | $C = B \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$ | bits/s, $B$ in Hz | | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | dB | | Maximum cable length (RS-485) | $L_{max} \approx \frac{10^{8}}{Baud \times 2}$ | metres (rule of thumb) | | Scan time of a master | $T_{scan} = N_{slaves} \times T_{poll}$ | seconds | | Modbus PDU size | 252 bytes max payload | PDU = Protocol Data Unit | | DNP3 frame efficiency | $\eta = \frac{Data\,bytes}{Frame\,bytes}$ | typically 0.6–0.75 | #### 2.4.2 RFID Air-Interface & Physics | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes | |----------|---------------------|---------------| | Free-space path loss (Friis) | $FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | dB; $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz | | Wavelength | $\lambda = \frac{c}{f}$ | metres; $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\,$m/s | | Inductive coupling range | $r \ll \frac{\lambda}{2\pi}$ | near-field condition | | Number of unique IDs (EPC) | $N = 2^{96}$ | $\approx 7.9 \times 10^{28}$ tags | | Q-factor of reader antenna | $Q = \frac{f_{0}}{BW}$ | selectivity vs. read range trade-off | | Bit duration (EPC Gen-2) | $T_{pri} = \frac{1}{BLF}$ | BLF (Backscatter Link Frequency) ≈ 40–640 kHz | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constants to memorize for KTU:** > - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s > - 1 W = 0 dBW; 1 mW = 0 dBm > - $20\log_{10}(2) \approx 6.02$ dB (the famous "6 dB per doubling" of power ratio) #### 2.4.3 RFID Frequency Bands (Indian/Global Allocation) | Band | Frequency | Range | Coupling | Typical Use | |------|-----------|-------|----------|-------------| | **LF** | 125–134 kHz | < 10 cm | Inductive | Animal tagging, access control | | **HF** | 13.56 MHz | < 1 m | Inductive | NFC, smart cards, library, blood bag tracking | | **UHF** | 860–960 MHz | 1–10 m | Backscatter | Supply chain, toll, hospital asset tracking | | **Microwave** | 2.45 GHz / 5.8 GHz | 1–15 m | Backscatter | Active tags, container tracking | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Friis Transmission Equation for RFID Read Range The **read range** of an RFID system is the most important engineering quantity. We derive it step by step. #### Step 1 — Power transmitted by the reader The reader's antenna delivers an Equivalent Isotropically Radiated Power (EIRP): $$EIRP = P_{tx} \cdot G_{tx}$$ where $P_{tx}$ is the conducted transmit power (watts) and $G_{tx}$ is the reader antenna gain (linear). **Logic:** A directive antenna concentrates energy in a particular direction, so the *effective* power in that direction is higher than the conducted power. #### Step 2 — Power density at the tag's location At a distance $d$ from the reader antenna, the power flux density (assuming free space, far-field) is: $$S_{tag} = \frac{EIRP}{4\pi d^{2}}$$ **Logic:** The energy spreads uniformly over the surface of a sphere of radius $d$, whose area is $4\pi d^{2}$. #### Step 3 — Power intercepted by the tag antenna The tag antenna has an effective aperture $A_{e,tag}$: $$A_{e,tag} = \frac{\lambda^{2} \cdot G_{tag}}{4\pi}$$ **Logic:** Aperture is the "effective capture area" of any antenna, related to its gain. The power available at the tag's chip input is: $$P_{tag} = S_{tag} \cdot A_{e,tag} = \frac{P_{tx} \cdot G_{tx} \cdot G_{tag} \cdot \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi d)^{2}}$$ #### Step 4 — Tag activation condition The tag can be activated only if $P_{tag} \geq P_{threshold}$, the minimum power required to energize the chip (typically **–15 dBm to –20 dBm**, i.e., 32–100 µW). Setting $P_{tag} = P_{threshold}$ and solving for $d$: $$\frac{P_{tx} \cdot G_{tx} \cdot G_{tag} \cdot \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi d_{max})^{2}} = P_{threshold}$$ $$d_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{P_{tx} \cdot G_{tx} \cdot G_{tag}}{P_{threshold}}}$$ #### Step 5 — Final Read-Range Formula $$\boxed{\;d_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \sqrt{\frac{G_{tx} \, G_{tag} \, P_{tx}}{P_{threshold}}}\;}$$ This is the famous **Friis-derived RFID read-range equation**. The **forward-link** (reader → tag) and a similar **reverse-link** (tag → reader) calculation give the actual range; the smaller of the two dominates. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples #### Example 1: HF RFID (13.56 MHz) Read Range **Given:** - $f = 13.56$ MHz → $\lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^{8}) / (13.56 \times 10^{6}) \approx 22.12$ m - $P_{tx} = 1$ W, $G_{tx} = G_{tag} = 1$ (linear, isotropic) - $P_{threshold} = 100$ µW $= 10^{-4}$ W **Solution:** $$d_{max} = \frac{22.12}{4\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1 \cdot 1 \cdot 1}{10^{-4}}}$$ $$d_{max} = 1.76 \cdot \sqrt{10^{4}} = 1.76 \times 100 = 176 \text{ m}$$ **But** the near-field (inductive) approximation $r \ll \lambda/(2\pi) \approx 3.52$ m limits HF RFID to about **3 m**, with practical reads at **10–70 cm**. The far-field formula overestimates because inductive coupling does not follow Friis. #### Example 2: UHF RFID (866 MHz) in an Indian Hospital **Given:** - $f = 866$ MHz → $\lambda \approx 0.346$ m - $P_{tx} = 0.5$ W (ETSI/India EN 302 208 limit: 2 W ERP) - $G_{tx} = 6$ (linear, ~7.8 dBi patch antenna) - $G_{tag} = 1.64$ (2.15 dBi dipole) - $P_{threshold} = 32$ µW $= 3.2 \times 10^{-5}$ W (–15 dBm) **Solution:** $$d_{max} = \frac{0.346}{4\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.5 \cdot 6 \cdot 1.64}{3.2 \times 10^{-5}}}$$ $$d_{max} = 0.02753 \cdot \sqrt{1.5375 \times 10^{5}}$$ $$d_{max} = 0.02753 \cdot 392.1 \approx 10.8 \text{ m}$$ > **Real-world read range in a hospital (multipath, body absorption, near-metal gurneys): typically 2–5 m.** This shows the gap between theoretical and deployed performance — a KTU favourite question. #### Example 3: SCADA Polling Cycle Calculation A SCADA master polls 60 RTUs over a serial link. Each RTU response is 80 bytes. Link is RS-485 at 9600 baud, 11 bits/byte (1 start, 8 data, 1 parity, 1 stop). **Per-RTU poll time:** $T_{poll} = (80 + 10)\,bytes \times 11\,bits/byte = 990\,bits$ **Polling cycle:** $T_{scan} = 60 \times 990 = 59{,}400$ bits / 9600 bps $= 6.19$ s > **Implication:** At 6.19 s, this SCADA system cannot alarm faster than ~6 s — a critical constraint for high-speed biomedical applications. KTU examiners love asking: *"Is this cycle acceptable for a 1 Hz vital sign update?"* (Answer: yes; for ECG at 250 Hz, no.) --- ### 3.3 Decision Matrix: Which Protocol for Which Biomedical Application? | Application | RFID Band | SCADA Protocol | Why | |-------------|-----------|----------------|-----| | Patient wristband identification | HF (NFC, ISO 14443) | MQTT to hospital HIS | Short range prevents cross-patient read | | Surgical instrument tracking | UHF (EPC Gen-2) | OPC UA over Ethernet | Bulk reading at sterilizer portal | | Cold chain (blood, vaccine, organs) | UHF with temperature sensor tag | Modbus TCP to historian | Real-time temperature alarms | | ICU ventilator network | — | IEC 60601-1 + HL7 over HL7/FHIR | Medical device safety class | | Operating theatre RFID cabinet | UHF | MQTT to inventory | Real-time stock-out alerts | | Wearable biometric monitoring | NFC + BLE hybrid | CoAP over BLE/6LoWPAN | Low-power IoT convergence | ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Modbus vs. DNP3 vs. OPC UA | Feature | Modbus RTU/TCP | DNP3 | IEC 60870-5-104 | OPC UA | |---------|---------------|------|------------------|--------| | Origin | Modicon, 1979 | Harris, 1993 | IEC, 1988 (2000 for 104) | OPC Foundation, 2008 | | Topology | Master–slave | Master–outstation | Master–outstation | Client–server / Pub-Sub | | Media | RS-485 / TCP/IP | Serial, TCP/IP | TCP/IP | TCP/IP | | Data model | 4 data types | 100+ object types | Limited | Rich, object-oriented | | Security | None (TCP: TLS option) | DNP3-SA (TLS) | TLS | Built-in encryption + auth | | Time sync | No | Yes (UTC sync) | Yes | Yes (NTP) | | Report-by-exception | No | Yes | Yes | Yes (subscriptions) | | Typical use | Factory, lab | Power utility | Power utility | IIoT, Industry 4.0 | | KTU one-liner | "The universal lingua franca" | "The power engineer's choice" | "Substation LAN dialect" | "The future of IIoT" | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Trend:** Questions increasingly test **OPC UA** and **MQTT** (as IoT-native SCADA protocols). Memorize their place in the **ISA-95** automation pyramid. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 SCADA Layered Architecture (ISA-95 Aligned) ```mermaid flowchart TB L4["LEVEL 4 - ENTERPRISE LAYER
ERP / Cloud IoT Platform
AWS IoT, Azure, SAP, MES"] L3["LEVEL 3 - OPERATIONS DASHBOARD
Historians, BI, ML Analytics
OSIsoft PI, Wonderware"] L2["LEVEL 2 - SCADA SUPERVISORY
SCADA Server MTU
Alarm Engine + Real-time DB
HMI Workstations"] L1["LEVEL 1 - CONTROL & NETWORK
PLCs, RTUs, IEDs
Protocols: Modbus, DNP3,
OPC UA, IEC 61850, MQTT"] L0["LEVEL 0 - FIELD INSTRUMENTATION
Sensors and Actuators
Temperature, Pressure, Flow,
ECG, SpO2, Valves, Pumps"] L4 -->|"Business decisions"| L3 L3 -->|"KPIs, setpoints"| L2 L2 -->|"Commands, polls"| L1 L1 -->|"Digital / analog I/O"| L0 L0 -->|"Measurements"| L1 L1 -->|"Telemetry"| L2 L2 -->|"Trends, events"| L3 L3 -->|"Aggregated data"| L4 ``` ### 4.2 RFID System Block Diagram (Reader-Tag Communication) ```mermaid flowchart LR HOST["Host Application
Middleware / ERP / IoT Cloud"] RDR["RFID Reader
Interrogator"] ANT["Reader Antenna
Tuned to band"] FIELD["RF Field Zone
HF inductive or UHF EM wave"] TAG["RFID Tag
Chip + Antenna + ID"] SENSOR["Optional Sensor Tag
Temperature, Humidity,
ECG, Glucose"] HOST -->|"USB / RS232 / Ethernet
HTTP REST or MQTT"| RDR RDR -->|"Carrier wave
CW at fc"| ANT ANT -->|"Radiated EM field"| FIELD FIELD -->|"Energy harvest
+ downlink data"| TAG TAG -->|"Backscatter
modulated reply"| FIELD FIELD -->|"Received signal"| ANT ANT -->|"Demodulated bits"| RDR RDR -->|"EPC + sensor data"| HOST SENSOR -->|"Analog sample
via chip ADC"| TAG ``` ### 4.3 SCADA + RFID Convergence for a Smart Hospital (Functional Block) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SENSORFIELD["Field Layer - Hospital Floor"] P1["Patient Wristband
HF NFC Tag"] P2["Blood Bag
UHF Temp Sensor Tag"] P3["Surgical Tray
UHF EPC Tag"] P4["Ventilator
Modbus RTU"] P5["Infusion Pump
PLC with Ethernet/IP"] end subgraph GATEWAY["Edge Gateway Layer"] G1["RFID Middleware
Reads + filters"] G2["Protocol Converter
Modbus to MQTT"] G3["Local Historian
SQLite buffer"] end subgraph SCADALAYER["SCADA / Cloud Supervisory Layer"] SC["SCADA Server
Ignition / WinCC"] HMI["HMI Dashboard
Trends, Alarms"] HIS["Hospital Information
System HL7 FHIR"] CLOUD["IoT Cloud
AWS IoT Core"] end subgraph ALARMS["Alarm and Analytics"] A1["Low Stock Alert
Surgical Tray"] A2["Temperature Excursion
Blood Bag"] A3["Ventilator Fault
Patient 305"] A4["Predictive Maintenance
Infusion Pump"] end P1 --> G1 P2 --> G1 P3 --> G1 P4 --> G2 P5 --> G2 G1 --> G3 G2 --> G3 G3 --> SC SC --> HMI SC --> HIS SC --> CLOUD SC --> A1 SC --> A2 SC --> A3 CLOUD --> A4 ``` ### 4.4 RFID Anti-Collision Protocol Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Anti-Collision Protocols"] A --> B["Aloha-based
Probabilistic"] A --> C["Tree-based
Deterministic"] B --> B1["Pure Aloha
Throughput 18 percent"] B --> B2["Slotted Aloha
Throughput 36 percent"] B --> B3["Framed Slotted Aloha
EPC Gen-2 Q-algorithm"] C --> C1["Binary Tree
Split ID by bit"] C --> C2["Query Tree
Reader broadcasts prefix"] C --> C3["ISO 18000-3 Mode 2
HF tags"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **EPC Class-1 Gen-2 (ISO 18000-63) uses Framed Slotted Aloha.** The reader dynamically adjusts the slot count $Q$ from $2^{Q-1}$ to $2^{Q+1}$ based on observed collisions, achieving near-optimal throughput (~40%) over a population of thousands of tags. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model] **Define SCADA. List its four functional layers as per the ISA-95 model.** **Model Answer:** SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) is a real-time, distributed industrial control system used to centrally monitor and control geographically dispersed field assets through networked communication protocols and HMI software. **Four functional layers (ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model):** 1. **Level 0 – Field Instrumentation:** Sensors and actuators (physical process). 2. **Level 1 – Control Layer:** PLCs, RTUs, IEDs performing local control. 3. **Level 2 – Supervisory Layer:** SCADA server, HMI, alarm engine, historian. 4. **Level 3 – Operations / Enterprise Layer:** MES, ERP, cloud IoT analytics. > **Mark split (3M):** 1M definition + 2M for 4 layers (0.5 each). --- #### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] **Differentiate between active, passive, and semi-passive RFID tags.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Passive | Semi-passive | Active | |-----------|---------|--------------|--------| | Power source | Reader RF field | Internal battery (chip only) | Internal battery (chip + TX) | | Range | < 10 m (HF/UHF) | < 25 m | Up to 100 m | | Lifetime | Indefinite | 3–7 years | 3–5 years | | Cost | < ₹10 | ₹50–₹200 | ₹200–₹2000 | | Sensor support | Limited | Yes (temp, humidity) | Yes | | Example | FASTag, library books | Cold-chain temp logger | Container tracker | > **Mark split (3M):** Tabular comparison covering at least 4 parameters with 0.5M each + 1M for a working example. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a SCADA system**. Discuss the role of **RTU**, **PLC**, **MTU**, and **HMI** in the system. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain **Modbus RTU** and **DNP3** protocols in detail. Compare their suitability for power utility automation. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution to Q-A(a) **Architecture of a SCADA System (Block Diagram already in §4.1):** The SCADA system has a **four-tier architecture**: 1. **Field Instrumentation Layer:** Consists of physical sensors (temperature, pressure, flow) and actuators (valves, pumps). In a biomedical setting, this may include SpO₂ probes, ECG electrodes, and infusion pump motors. These devices generate analog/digital signals proportional to the process variable. 2. **Control Layer (PLCs and RTUs):** - **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller):** A ruggedized industrial computer that executes ladder-logic or structured-text programs to perform deterministic local control (e.g., "if temperature > 80 °C, open valve V-103 within 100 ms"). High I/O density, short cycle times (1–10 ms). - **RTU (Remote Terminal Unit):** A microprocessor-based device designed for telemetry from remote sites. It has built-in protocol stacks (DNP3, IEC 60870) and supports radio/satellite/PSTN communication. Often battery/solar-powered. - **MTU (Master Terminal Unit):** Also called the SCADA server; the "brain" of the system. It polls all RTUs/PLCs at fixed intervals, stores the data in a real-time database, evaluates alarm conditions, and forwards aggregated data to higher layers. - **HMI (Human-Machine Interface):** The graphical window through which operators see the process. Mimics (pictorial representations of plant equipment), trends (time-series plots), and alarm summaries are typical HMI elements. 3. **Supervisory Layer:** Hosts the SCADA server software, historian (long-term time-series storage), alarm management engine, and report generators. The HMI workstations and engineering stations reside here. 4. **Enterprise/Cloud Layer:** Connects SCADA to MES, ERP, and cloud analytics platforms via REST APIs, MQTT, or OPC UA. Enables AI/ML-driven predictive maintenance and cross-site dashboards. **Roles in summary table (for writing in exam):** | Element | Role | Cycle Time | |---------|------|------------| | Sensor | Measure physical variable | Continuous | | Actuator | Modify physical variable | ms to s | | PLC | Deterministic local control | 1–10 ms | | RTU | Remote telemetry, protocol translation | 100 ms to s | | MTU/SCADA Server | Central data acquisition, alarm, control | s to min | | HMI | Operator visualization | 1 Hz refresh | > **Mark allocation (7M):** 2M diagram + 1M sensor/actuator + 2M PLC/RTU distinction + 1M MTU + 1M HMI. --- ### Model Solution to Q-A(b) **Modbus RTU (1979, Modicon, now Schneider Electric):** - **Physical layer:** RS-485 (2-wire or 4-wire) or RS-232; point-to-point or multi-drop. - **Frame format:** `[SlaveID][FunctionCode][Data][CRC16]` with silent intervals of 3.5 character times for frame delimitation. - **Data model:** 4 register types — **Coil (1 bit, RW), Discrete Input (1 bit, R), Holding Register (16 bit, RW), Input Register (16 bit, R)**. Total 65,536 addresses per type. - **Function codes:** 0x01 Read Coils, 0x03 Read Holding Registers, 0x05 Write Single Coil, 0x06 Write Single Register, 0x10 Write Multiple Registers. - **PDU limit:** 252 bytes payload. - **Security:** None in the base protocol. **Modbus TCP** (port 502) wraps the same PDU in a 7-byte MBAP header.
