KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]** **Define the Open Interconnect Consortium (OIC). List any two of its founding members.** **Model Answer:** The **Open Interconnect Consortium (OIC)** is an industry-sponsored standards body founded in **2014** with the goal of developing a unified, open-source interoperability framework for IoT devices, leading to the **IoTivity** project. It was later renamed the **Open Connectivity Foundation (OCF)** in 2016. Two founding members (any two): - **Intel** - **Samsung** - **Dell** - **Broadcom** - **Atmel** *(3 marks: 2 for definition + 1 for founding members)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]** **Mention the application-layer protocol and the default transport-layer protocol used in OIC architecture. Why is this transport preferred for IoT?** **Model Answer:** - **Application-Layer Protocol:** **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** — defined in **RFC 7252**. - **Default Transport:** **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)**, port **5683**. - **Why UDP Preferred:** UDP is **connectionless** and has **minimal header overhead (8 bytes)** compared to TCP (20+ bytes). This makes it ideal for **low-bandwidth, low-power, constrained IoT devices** that cannot afford the handshake overhead of TCP. *(3 marks: 1 for protocol + 1 for transport + 1 for justification)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme, Module 2]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **five-layer architecture of the OIC/OCF framework**, listing the function of each layer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **OIC resource model**. How are resources discovered and addressed in OIC? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Q A (a)** The OIC/OCF architecture is a **layered protocol stack** designed for seamless IoT interoperability. The five layers are: **Layer 1 — Device Layer (Hardware Tier):** This is the lowest layer containing the **physical hardware** — sensors (temperature, humidity, accelerometer), actuators (motors, LEDs), and microcontrollers (MCUs). It also contains hardware-specific drivers for radios (Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee). Its function is to convert real-world physical signals into digital data and to physically execute actuator commands. **Layer 2 — Connectivity / Transport Layer:** Manages **physical and data-link connectivity** across heterogeneous radio technologies (IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi, IEEE 802.15.4 ZigBee/Thread, Bluetooth Low Energy, NFC). It also implements the **UDP/TCP socket** layer. OIC mandates **UDP** as the default transport to minimize overhead on constrained devices. **Layer 3 — Communication / Framework Layer:** The **core layer** of OIC, hosting the **IoTivity framework**. It implements: - The **CoAP engine** for RESTful message handling. - The **resource abstraction logic** that maps URIs to device functions. - The **Discovery, Security (DTLS), and Group Management** modules. This layer is the "brain" that translates high-level REST commands into hardware-level actions. **Layer 4 — Application / Service Layer:** Hosts **end-user applications** (mobile apps, web dashboards) and the **Resource Manager** that aggregates device resources. It exposes standardized **RAML-described APIs** to the application logic. Cloud clients connecting to OCF Cloud reside here. **Layer 5 — Business / Cloud Layer:** Provides **analytics, OTA firmware updates, device fleet management**, and integration with **enterprise systems** (ERP, EMR in healthcare). It is typically implemented as a cloud-hosted service that aggregates data from many edge devices. *(7 marks: ~1.4 marks per layer with clear functional explanation)* --- #### **Model Solution — Q A (b)** **OIC Resource Model — Block Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart TB DEV["OIC DEVICE (e.g., Smart Light)"] R1["/light/1 - oic.r.light"] R2["/power/1 - oic.r.switch.binary"] R3["/oic/res - Discovery Resource"] DEV --> R1 DEV --> R2 DEV --> R3 R1 --> P1["Properties: value, dimming, colour"] R2 --> P2["Properties: value (true/false)"] R3 --> P3["Self-description, RAML payload"] style DEV fill:#4169e1,color:#fff style R1 fill:#ffd700 style R2 fill:#ffd700 style R3 fill:#ffd700 ``` **Resource Addressing:** Resources are addressed using **RESTful URIs** (e.g., `/light/1`, `/temperature/sensor/2`). Each URI uniquely identifies a single resource instance on a specific device. The URI is combined with a **Resource Type (`rt`)** string (e.g., `oic.r.light`) and an **Interface (`if`)** declaration (`oic.if.a` for full CRUD, `oic.if.baseline` for a CRUD subset). **Discovery Mechanism:** OIC uses **multicast CoAP** for discovery. When a new device joins the network, it sends a CoAP NON (non-confirmable) GET request to the **reserved multicast address `224.0.1.187`** on **UDP port 5683**, targeting the well-known resource path **`/oic/res`**. OIC clients listening on this multicast group receive the announcement and then send a **unicast CoAP GET** to the device's IP to retrieve the full RAML description of all its resources. *(7 marks: 3 for diagram, 2 for addressing, 2 for discovery)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme, Module 2]** **(a)** Explain the **IoTivity open-source framework** in detail. How does it implement the OIC specification? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **security architecture of OIC** with reference to **DTLS, X.509 certificates, and ACLs**. Why is DTLS preferred over TLS in OIC? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Q B (a)** **IoTivity Overview:** **IoTivity** is the **open-source reference implementation** of the OIC/OCF specification. It is written primarily in **C and C++** and is licensed under the **Apache 2.0 License**. The project is hosted on **GitHub** under the `iotivity` repository and is maintained by the OCF in collaboration with Samsung, Intel, and the broader open-source community. **Architecture of IoTivity:** IoTivity is structured into several modular components: - **`Iotivity-Constrained`:** A lightweight version targeting MCUs with < 256 KB RAM. - **`IoTivity-Lite`:** A middle-tier implementation for Linux-based gateways. - **`IoTivity-Full`:** The complete framework including cloud bridging and advanced features. **How IoTivity Implements the OIC Spec:** 1. **Resource Hosting:** IoTivity provides APIs (`OCResource`, `OCResourceHandle`) that allow developers to **register resources** on a device, each with a URI, type, interface, and properties. 2. **CoAP Engine:** The internal `coap` module implements RFC 7252 with extensions for **Observe (RFC 7641)** and **Block-wise Transfer (RFC 7959)**. 3. **Discovery Module:** The `OCDiscovery` API sends and receives multicast CoAP messages on `224.0.1.187:5683`. 4. **Security Module:** Implements **DTLS 1.2** via the `oc_security` module, supporting X.509 certs, PSK (Pre-Shared Keys), and ACL management. 5. **Cloud Bridge:** The `cloud` module translates OIC resources to OCF Cloud API calls (HTTPS REST) for cloud integration. **Portability:** IoTivity supports **Linux, Tizen, Android, iOS, Windows, Arduino, RIOT OS, and Zephyr OS**, making it one of the most portable IoT frameworks available. *(7 marks: 2 for overview, 3 for architecture, 2 for OIC mapping)* --- #### **Model Solution — Q B (b)** **OIC Security Architecture — Three Pillars:** **Pillar 1: Device Identity via X.509 Certificates:** Every OIC-compliant device is manufactured with a **globally unique UUID** and an **X.509 digital certificate** signed by the **OCF Root Certificate Authority (CA)**. This certificate serves as the device's **immutable identity** and is used to authenticate the device during the initial DTLS handshake. The certificate chain validates that the device is a genuine, OCF-certified product. **Pillar 2: DTLS 1.2 (Datagram Transport Layer Security):** All CoAP messages exchanged between OIC devices are encrypted using **DTLS 1.2**. The key resources involved are: - `/oic/sec/doxm` — Device Ownership Transfer Method. - `/oic/sec/pstat` — Provisioning Status. - `/oic/sec/cred` — Credentials storage (certificates, PSKs). - `/oic/sec/acl` — Access Control List. **Pillar 3: Access Control Lists (ACLs):** ACLs provide **granular, per-resource, per-subject** authorization. An ACL entry specifies: - The **subject** (which user/app/cert can access). - The **resource** (which OIC resource is being protected). - The **permission** (read, write, delete, notify). **Why DTLS Preferred over TLS:** | Aspect | DTLS | TLS | |---|---|---| | **Transport** | UDP (connectionless) | TCP (connection-oriented) | | **Handshake** | 4 messages (lighter) | 5–6 messages | | **Overhead** | Lower | Higher | | **IoT Suitability** | **Excellent** for constrained, intermittent connectivity | Poor for lossy, low-power networks | DTLS is preferred because **CoAP uses UDP**. Since TLS requires a reliable, ordered byte stream (provided by TCP), it cannot directly secure CoAP. DTLS was specifically designed by the IETF (RFC 6347) to bring the **same security guarantees as TLS to datagram (UDP) transport**, making it the natural choice for OIC. *(7 marks: 2 for X.509, 2 for ACL, 2 for DTLS, 1 for TLS comparison)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in OIC Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing OIC with AllJoyn or OneM2M** — They are *competing* standards, not synonymous. OIC uses CoAP; AllJoyn uses its own D-Bus variant; OneM2M uses a service-layer approach. Writing "OIC is the same as AllJoyn" will lose full marks. > > 2. **Forgetting the "OIC → OCF" rename** — Examiners *reward* students who mention that OIC was reorganized as OCF in 2016. Omitting this is a minor mark loss. > > 3. **Writing "OIC uses HTTP"** — This is **wrong**. OIC uses **CoAP**, not HTTP. The two are conceptually similar (both RESTful) but CoAP is binary-encoded and runs over UDP. > > 4. **Mixing up CoAP and MQTT** — CoAP is a **request-response** protocol (synchronous); MQTT is a **publish-subscribe** protocol (asynchronous, broker-mediated). OIC uses CoAP. > > 5. **Skipping the layer-wise explanation** — A 7-mark question on architecture *must* cover all **five layers** with at least one concrete function for each. Writing only 2–3 layers is a 50% mark deduction. > > 6. **Omitting DTLS vs TLS distinction** — This is a **favourite** sub-question. A vague answer like "OIC uses encryption" gets 0 marks; the precise answer "DTLS 1.2 over UDP" gets full marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **OIC = Open Interconnect Consortium** (founded 2014, renamed OCF in 2016). - [x] **Founders:** Intel, Samsung, Dell, Broadcom, Atmel. - [x] **Open-source project:** **IoTivity** (C/C++, Apache 2.0 license). - [x] **Primary protocol:** **CoAP (RFC 7252)** over **UDP port 5683**. - [x] **Architecture style:** **RESTful, resource-oriented** (similar to HTTP/URI semantics). - [x] **Five layers:** Device → Connectivity → Framework/Communication → Application → Business/Cloud. - [x] **Core abstraction:** **Resource** — every device capability is modeled as a uniquely addressable resource. - [x] **Resource format:** `{rt, if, properties}` — typed, interface-declared, JSON-serialized. - [x] **Discovery mechanism:** Multicast CoAP GET to `224.0.1.187:5683/oic/res`. - [x] **Security stack:** X.509 certs + **DTLS 1.2** + **ACLs** + OAuth 2.0 provisioning. - [x] **Why DTLS not TLS:** CoAP runs over UDP; DTLS is the UDP-equivalent of TLS. - [x] **Observe extension (RFC 7641):** Enables server-push notifications, avoiding polling. - [x] **Portability:** Supports Linux, Tizen, Android, iOS, Arduino, RIOT, Zephyr. - [x] **Biomedical relevance:** Enables vendor-agnostic integration of patient monitors (HR, SpO₂, BP, glucose) into a single clinician dashboard. - [x] **OIC's tagline:** *"Seamlessly connecting the next 50 billion devices."* - [x] **Remember:** OIC ≠ HTTP-based, OIC ≠ MQTT-based, OIC ≠ AllJoyn.

topics.title: OIC Architecture and Design Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** OIC Architecture and Design Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Open Interconnect Consortium (OIC) Architecture** is a specification framework originally developed by Intel, Samsung, and Dell in 2014, which later evolved into the **Open Connectivity Foundation (OCF)** in 2016. It defines a unified, interoperable communication framework that allows billions of devices — from sensors and wearables to industrial machines — to discover, connect, and interact with each other seamlessly across diverse operating systems, transport protocols, and physical media.

The OIC architecture is **resource-oriented**, meaning every physical or virtual entity in an IoT system is modeled as a collection of *Resources* exposed through standardized RESTful interfaces. Communication is predominantly handled using the **Constrained Application Protocol (CoAP)** over UDP, with payloads encoded in **JSON** or **CBOR** formats.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> The 2024 scheme expects students to distinguish between OIC, IoTivity (the open-source reference implementation), and competing stacks such as **oneM2M**, **AllJoyn**, and **OCF 2.x** specifications. Mastery of the *Resource Model* is a guaranteed 7 to 14 mark question in Part B.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (The "Smart Restaurant" Model)

Imagine a large, multi-cuisine restaurant where every dish on the menu is described using a **standardized recipe card** that any waiter from any country can read. The customer (the *Client*) simply points to a card; the kitchen (the *Device*) prepares and serves exactly what is requested.

In the OIC world:
- **The Restaurant Kitchen** = An IoT device (e.g., a smart blood-pressure monitor).
- **The Recipe Card** = A *Resource* (e.g., `bp/systolic`, `bp/diastolic`).
- **The Waiter** = The *CoAP protocol* transporting requests.
- **The Menu** = The *Resource Directory* allowing discovery.
- **The Order Slip** = A JSON payload containing the request/response.

No matter if the customer speaks English, Hindi, or Malayalam, the standardized card ensures the order is understood — this is **interoperability**, the central promise of OIC.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

| Term | Meaning | KTU Relevance |
|------|---------|----------------|
| **CoAP** | Constrained Application Protocol (RFC 7252) | Default transport for OIC |
| **DTLS** | Datagram Transport Layer Security | Mandatory security handshake |
| **CRUDN** | Create, Retrieve, Update, Delete, Notify | 5 core resource operations |
| **URI Path** | `/oic/<resource-type>` | Standard resource addressing |
| **JSON / CBOR** | Data interchange formats | Payload encoding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Resource:**
> A **Resource** is the *atomic, addressable, and uniformly manipulable unit of information* exposed by a device, characterized by a set of **Properties** (state), **Links** (relations to other resources), and **Interfaces** (semantic behavior descriptors like `oic.if.baseline`).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture Breakdown

The OIC architecture follows a **five-layer stack** analogous to the OSI model but optimized for constrained devices.

### Layer 1: Application Layer (Vertical Applications & UIs)
- Hosts use-case logic (e.g., a hospital dashboard showing patient vitals).
- Interacts with the framework only through published APIs.

### Layer 2: Framework Specification Layer
- Defines the **Core Resource Model**, **Discovery Mechanism**, **Device Management**, and **Security** abstractions.
- Specified in `oic-spec` documents (now part of OCF 2.x).

### Layer 3: Resource Model Layer
- A **Resource** = `{Resource Type, Resource Interface, Properties, Attributes}`.
- Each resource is identified by a **URI** of the form:

$$
\texttt{coap://<device-IP>:<port>/<endpoint-path>?<query>}
$$

- Example: `coaps://192.168.1.45:5683/oic/res?di=12345`

### Layer 4: Communication & Transport Layer
- **CoAP** over **UDP** (port 5683 for unsecured, 5684 for DTLS-secured).
- HTTP may be used as a "shim" for cloud gateways.
- Supports **Observe** (RFC 7641) for server-push notifications.

### Layer 5: Physical / Link Layer
- Operates over **Wi-Fi, Bluetooth LE, ZigBee, Thread, NFC, Ethernet**, etc.
- Device vendors may plug any bearer supported by their hardware.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Design Decisions

| Design Decision | Reason | Engineering Trade-off |
|-----------------|--------|------------------------|
| REST + JSON | Familiar to web developers | Higher payload than binary |
| CoAP over UDP | Low overhead for tiny MCUs | No native reliability — CoAP adds its own ACK/retry |
| Resource as core abstraction | Uniform handling of sensors, actuators, configs | More metadata overhead |
| DTLS handshake | End-to-end encryption | CPU + memory cost on 8-bit MCUs |
| Discovery via `/oic/res` | Plug-and-play pairing | Multicast storms in dense networks |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| Concept | Expression / Template | Unit / Constraint |
|---------|------------------------|--------------------|
| Resource URI | `coap://host:port/oic/res?di=deviceID` | Di = Device ID |
| CoAP Method | `GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OBSERVE` | Maps to CRUDN |
| DTLS Cipher (minimum) | TLS_PSK_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256 | 128-bit symmetric key |
| Observe lifetime | 0–86400 seconds | RFC 7641 token |
| Multicast CoAP address | `224.0.1.187` (OIC reserved) | TTL = 1 hop |
| Resource state latency | $T_{r} = T_{tx} + T_{proc} + T_{ack}$ | milliseconds |
| CBOR encoding size | $S_{cb} \approx S_{json} \times 0.7$ | 30% smaller on average |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` in math mode if you need absolute-value bars. Example: write $\vert x \vert$ not $|x|$, to avoid table-parsing errors.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare**: A wearable ECG patch publishes resources `/oic/heart/rate` and `/oic/heart/rhythm`, consumed by a hospital's bedside monitor.
- **Smart Home**: A unified OCF bridge maps between ZigBee bulbs and Wi-Fi speakers without cloud hops.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: A robotic arm exposes `/oic/m/position/x`, `/oic/m/position/y`, allowing any OCF-compliant controller to operate it — *vendor neutral interoperability*.
- **Smart Agriculture**: Soil-moisture sensors, weather stations, and irrigation pumps all use the same `/oic/res` directory, enabling auto-discovery by a single gateway.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CRUDN → CoAP Method Mapping

The OIC specification defines five logical operations on a resource. The mapping to CoAP verbs is a fundamental derivation students must memorize for KTU exams.

**Step 1 — Define the logical operations.**
The five operations on a resource $R$ are: **Create ($C$)**, **Retrieve ($R$)**, **Update ($U$)**, **Delete ($D$)**, and **Notify ($N$)**.

**Step 2 — Map to HTTP-style verbs.**
REST theory uses a one-to-one mapping where each CRUD operation corresponds to an HTTP method. CoAP inherits this mapping because it is "RESTful by design."

**Step 3 — Express the mapping mathematically.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Create}(R)   &\longrightarrow \text{CoAP POST} \quad \text{on } \texttt{/<parent>} \\[4pt]
\text{Retrieve}(R) &\longrightarrow \text{CoAP GET}   \quad \text{on } \texttt{/<R-URI>} \\[4pt]
\text{Update}(R)   &\longrightarrow \text{CoAP PUT}   \quad \text{on } \texttt{/<R-URI>} \\[4pt]
\text{Delete}(R)   &\longrightarrow \text{CoAP DELETE} \quad \text{on } \texttt{/<R-URI>} \\[4pt]
\text{Notify}(R)   &\longrightarrow \text{CoAP OBSERVE} \quad \text{on } \texttt{/<R-URI>}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify with an example.**
A blood-glucose sensor exposes `oic.r.blood.glucose`. To *Retrieve* the latest reading, the client sends:

$$
\text{GET coaps://192.168.0.10:5684/oic/blood/glucose}
$$

The sensor responds with a JSON payload:

$$
\texttt{\{"glucose": \ 118, \ \texttt{"units": \ "mg/dL", \ \texttt{"range": \ "70-130"\}\}}
$$

This verifies the GET → Retrieve mapping. The remaining mappings follow identical logic with appropriate verb substitutions.

## 3.2 Derivation of the OIC Discovery Query Format

**Step 1 — Identify the discovery endpoint.**
Every OIC-compliant device MUST expose a well-known resource at the path `/oic/res`. This is a hard-coded discovery URL.

**Step 2 — Define the supported query parameters.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{rt}    &= \text{Resource Type filter} \\
\texttt{if}    &= \text{Resource Interface filter} \\
\texttt{di}    &= \text{Device ID (UUID)} \\
\texttt{n}     &= \text{Resource name filter} \\
\texttt{page}  &= \text{Pagination index}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine into the discovery URI template.**

$$
\texttt{coap://<multicast>/oic/res?rt=oic.r.<type>\&if=oic.if.<interface>}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example.**
A patient wants to discover all available blood-pressure monitors on the local network that support the *baseline* interface.

**Request:**

$$
\text{GET coap://224.0.1.187:5683/oic/res?rt=oic.r.blood.pressurerometer\&if=oic.if.baseline}
$$

**Response (sample):**

$$
\texttt{[\{"di":"\textemdash UUID", \ \texttt{"links":[\{"href":"/bp", \ \texttt{"rt":["oic.r.blood.pressure"]\}]\}\}]}
$$

This is the canonical **multicast discovery handshake** that OIC defines.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: OIC vs. Competing IoT Stacks

| Feature | **OIC / OCF** | **oneM2M** | **AllJoyn** | **ZigBee Dotdot** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Abstraction | Resource | Node + AE/CSE | Bus + Service | Cluster + Attribute |
| Transport | CoAP/UDP | HTTP, CoAP, MQTT | UDP, TCP | ZCL over 802.15.4 |
| Payload | JSON / CBOR | XML, JSON, CBOR | AJN (binary) | ZCL frame |
| Security | DTLS, OAuth 2.0 | TLS, ACE-OAuth | TLS, SRP | Network + APS keys |
| Discovery | `/oic/res` multicast | CSE registration | NS announcement | Service discovery |
| Open Source | **IoTivity** (Linux Foundation) | **OM2M** (Eclipse) | Closed (deprecated 2016) | **Zephyr RTOS** |
| Domain Strength | Consumer + Industrial | Telecom + Smart City | Media-centric | Smart Home / Lighting |
| KTU Recurrence | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐ | ⭐⭐ |

## 3.4 Resource State Update Propagation — Latency Derivation

For an OBSERVE operation, total round-trip latency for a state notification is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{notify}} &= T_{\text{trigger}} + T_{\text{serial}} + T_{\text{tx}} + T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{parse}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{trigger}}$ = sensor acquisition delay (typically 10–100 ms for biomedical).
- $T_{\text{serial}} = S_{payload} / R_{link}$ (e.g., 256 B / 250 kbps = 8.2 ms over ZigBee).
- $T_{\text{tx}}$ = physical-layer transmit time.
- $T_{\text{prop}}$ = electromagnetic propagation, often negligible for short range.
- $T_{\text{parse}}$ = JSON parsing on the client (1–5 ms on Cortex-M4).

For a typical wearable scenario:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{notify}} &\approx 20 + 8.2 + 0.5 + 0.05 + 2 \approx 30.75 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is well within the 250 ms **physiological event threshold** for ECG monitoring, making OIC a viable stack for real-time biomedical telemetry.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OIC Layered Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Application Layer: User Apps, Dashboards, AI Engines] --> B[Framework Specification Layer: Core Resource Model, Discovery, Security, Device Management]
    B --> C[Resource Model Layer: Resources with Properties, Interfaces, URIs]
    C --> D[Communication and Transport Layer: CoAP over UDP, HTTP Bridge, DTLS Security]
    D --> E[Physical and Link Layer: Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, Thread, Ethernet, NFC]

    A1[Smart Hospital Dashboard] -.-> A
    A2[Voice Assistant] -.-> A
    A3[Mobile App] -.-> A
    E1[ECG Patch] -.-> E
    E2[Smart BP Monitor] -.-> E
    E3[Insulin Pump] -.-> E

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style C fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style D fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style E fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 OIC Resource Model and CRUDN Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    Client[Client Application] -->|1. Discovery: GET /oic/res| ResDir[Resource Directory]
    ResDir -->|2. Returns list of resources| Client
    Client -->|3. CRUDN requests over CoAP| Device[IoT Device]
    Device -->|4. JSON response| Client
    Device -->|5. OBSERVE notification on state change| Client

    subgraph Device_Internal [Device Internals]
        R1[Resource bp_systolic] --> DR[Device Resource Manager]
        R2[Resource bp_diastolic] --> DR
        R3[Resource heart_rate] --> DR
        DR --> S[Security Manager: DTLS, ACL]
    end

    style Client fill:#3498db,color:#fff
    style ResDir fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff
    style Device fill:#2ecc71,color:#fff
    style Device_Internal fill:#f1c40f,color:#000
```

## 4.3 OIC Discovery and Pairing Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client Device
    participant M as Multicast Group 224.0.1.187
    participant D as Target IoT Device
    participant S as Security Manager DTLS

    C->>M: GET /oic/res?rt=oic.r.blood.pressure
    M->>D: Forward multicast request
    D-->>M: 200 OK with device description JSON
    M-->>C: Deliver device description
    C->>D: POST /oic/sec/doxes (ownership transfer)
    D->>S: Request DTLS handshake with PSK
    S-->>D: Secure session established
    C->>D: GET /bp/systolic (secure)
    D-->>C: JSON payload 118 mmHg
    D->>C: OBSERVE notification on change
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture of an OIC-Compliant Smart Biomedical Device

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Sensor_Front_End [Sensor Front End]
        SG1[ECG Electrodes] --> AFE1[Analog Front End]
        SG2[PPG Optical Sensor] --> AFE2[Photodiode Amplifier]
        AFE1 --> ADC1[24-bit Sigma Delta ADC]
        AFE2 --> ADC2[16-bit SAR ADC]
    end

    subgraph MCU_Core [Microcontroller Core]
        ADC1 --> DSP1[Digital Filter and Feature Extraction]
        ADC2 --> DSP2[Heart Rate Detection Algorithm]
        DSP1 --> RM[Resource Model: oic.r.heart.rhythm]
        DSP2 --> RM2[Resource Model: oic.r.heart.rate]
        RM --> SR[Security Manager: DTLS PSK]
        RM2 --> SR
        SR --> CP[CoAP Engine]
    end

    subgraph Connectivity [Radio Connectivity]
        CP --> WIFI[Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n]
        CP --> BLE[Bluetooth Low Energy 5.0]
        CP --> THREAD[Thread 802.15.4]
    end

    WIFI --> GW[Cloud Gateway or Edge Server]
    BLE --> GW
    THREAD --> GW

    style Sensor_Front_End fill:#3498db,color:#fff
    style MCU_Core fill:#e67e22,color:#fff
    style Connectivity fill:#16a085,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a "Resource" in the OIC architecture. [3 marks]
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Retest]**

**Model Answer:**
A *Resource* in the OIC architecture is the fundamental, addressable unit of information exposed by an IoT device. It is identified by a unique URI, characterized by a set of *Properties* (current state values), *Attributes* (metadata such as units, range, and timestamps), and *Interfaces* (semantic behavior descriptors like `oic.if.baseline` or `oic.if.rw`). Resources enable uniform RESTful manipulation via the CRUDN operations, allowing heterogeneous devices to interoperate without bespoke drivers.

### Question 2: What is the role of CoAP in OIC and why was it chosen over HTTP? [3 marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol) is the default transport for OIC, designed for resource-constrained devices. It was chosen over HTTP because it offers a **4-byte fixed header** (vs. HTTP's verbose text headers), runs over **UDP** (vs. TCP's heavy handshake), supports **multicast** for discovery, and includes **built-in OBSERVE** for server-push notifications. These features drastically reduce RAM, ROM, and energy consumption on 8/16-bit MCUs while preserving REST semantics.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: [14 Marks] (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Series II]**

**(a)** Explain the OIC reference architecture in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss the functions of each layer. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the OIC resource model. Show how the CRUDN operations map to CoAP methods with a biomedical example. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution (a):

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
The OIC reference architecture is a five-layer stack designed to provide vendor-neutral interoperability among constrained IoT devices. It is implemented in the open-source **IoTivity** project under the Linux Foundation.

**2. Layer-wise Explanation (5 marks):**

| Layer | Function | Examples |
|-------|----------|----------|
| Application | Use-case logic, UIs, AI/ML | Hospital dashboard, voice assistant |
| Framework | Core resource model, discovery, DM, security | OCF 2.x specification |
| Resource | Atomic addressable units with URIs and properties | `oic.r.temperature`, `oic.r.heart.rate` |
| Transport | CoAP/UDP, HTTP bridge, DTLS | RFC 7252, RFC 6347 |
| Physical | Radio bearers | Wi-Fi, BLE, Thread, ZigBee, Ethernet |

**3. Block Diagram (1 mark):** *(Refer to SECTION 4.1 diagram.)*

#### Model Solution (b):

**1. Resource Model Structure (2 marks):**
A Resource $R$ in OIC is modeled as:

$$
R = \{ \text{Resource Type},\ \text{Interface},\ \text{Properties},\ \text{Attributes},\ \text{Links} \}
$$

**2. CRUDN-to-CoAP Mapping Table (3 marks):**

| Operation | CoAP Method | Example (Glucose Sensor) |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------------|
| Create | POST | Create a new reading record |
| Retrieve | GET | `GET /glucose` → returns 118 mg/dL |
| Update | PUT | `PUT /glucose` with `{"value": 120}` |
| Delete | DELETE | Remove calibration record |
| Notify | OBSERVE | Push update on every new reading |

**3. Biomedical Example Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to SECTION 4.2 block diagram.)*

---

### Question B: [14 Marks] (Choice 2 — Alternative Path)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Series I]**

**(a)** Discuss the OIC discovery mechanism. Explain the role of multicast and the `/oic/res` endpoint with an example. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Illustrate the OIC security model. How does DTLS ensure secure communication? List any four supported device onboarding methods. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution (a):

**1. Discovery Overview (2 marks):**
OIC discovery allows clients to find devices and their resources on a local network without prior configuration. It uses **CoAP multicast** on the reserved group `224.0.1.187` and the well-known endpoint `/oic/res`.

**2. Multicast Query Example (3 marks):**
A request to find all temperature sensors:

$$
\texttt{GET coap://224.0.1.187:5683/oic/res?rt=oic.r.temperature\&if=oic.if.s}
$$

Each device that matches the resource-type filter responds unicast with a JSON description of its `links` and resource instances.

**3. Sequence Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to SECTION 4.3 sequence diagram.)*

#### Model Solution (b):

**1. Security Stack (3 marks):**
OIC mandates **DTLS 1.2** for transport security. All sensitive CoAP exchanges are routed to port **5684** and the URI scheme becomes `coaps://`. The handshake may use:
- **Pre-Shared Key (PSK)**
- **Raw Public Key (RPK)**
- **X.509 Certificates**
- **Just-Works / PIN-based pairing** for new devices

**2. Onboarding Methods (2 marks):**
The four primary onboarding flows defined by the OIC specification are:
1. **Just-Works** (no user input — for air-gapped devices).
2. **Random PIN** (numerical code entered on the client).
3. **Manufacturer-Certified** (X.509 root of trust baked in factory).
4. **Cloud-Mediated Onboarding** (vendor relays keys via a trusted OCF cloud).

**3. DTLS Handshake Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to SECTION 4.2 trust boundary.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing OIC with IoTivity:** OIC is the *consortium/specification*; IoTivity is the *open-source implementation*. Many students lose 2 marks by interchanging the two.
> 2. **Forgetting the port number:** CoAP is on 5683; CoAPS (DTLS-secured) is on **5684**. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 3. **Writing HTTP for OIC:** While HTTP is allowed as a "shim," the *native* OIC transport is CoAP. Marks are deducted if HTTP is mentioned as the primary transport.
> 4. **Skipping the `/oic/res` discovery endpoint:** Any question on discovery MUST include this path or the answer is incomplete.
> 5. **Not stating CRUDN acronym:** OIC uses five operations (CRUDN), not the classic four. Forgetting "Notify" costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **OIC = Open Interconnect Consortium**; its modern successor is the **Open Connectivity Foundation (OCF)**.
- 🔑 **IoTivity** is the open-source reference implementation maintained by the Linux Foundation.
- 🔑 The five core operations on a Resource are **CRUDN** (Create, Retrieve, Update, Delete, Notify).
- 🔑 Default transport is **CoAP over UDP** — unsecured port **5683**, secured port **5684**.
- 🔑 The discovery endpoint is always `/oic/res`; the multicast group is **224.0.1.187**.
- 🔑 Resource addressing uses URIs of the form `coap://host:port/<path>` and payloads are **JSON or CBOR**.
- 🔑 Security is built on **DTLS 1.2**, with PSK, RPK, X.509, and Just-Works onboarding.
- 🔑 The OIC stack is **vendor-neutral** and supports multiple physical bearers: Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, Thread, Ethernet, NFC.
- 🔑 Unlike AllJoyn, OIC is **REST + resource-oriented**, making it more aligned with web-developer tooling.
- 🔑 Biomedical applications leverage OIC for **ECG patches, glucose monitors, smart BP cuffs, and pulse oximeters** with real-time OBSERVE notifications.
- 🔑 Memorize the **layer names** and **port numbers** — these are frequent 2 to 3 mark sub-questions in KTU Part A.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Devices and Deployment Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IoT Devices and Deployment Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an IoT Device?

An **IoT Device** (Internet of Things Device) is a physical hardware component, instrument, or "thing" embedded with sensors, actuators, microcontrollers, communication transceivers, and software that enables it to sense, collect, process, and exchange data over a network (typically the Internet) without requiring direct human-to-human or human-to-computer interaction. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, an IoT device represents the **edge node** of an IoT ecosystem, where the physical and digital worlds converge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** IoT devices are uniquely identifiable, addressable, and network-capable hardware units capable of sensing real-world phenomena, processing data (locally or remotely), and actuating responses. They form the foundational sensing and actuation layer in any IoT architecture.

### Formal Academic Classification

IoT devices can be rigorously classified along multiple axes:

| Classification Axis | Categories |
|---|---|
| **Function** | Sensors, Actuators, Hybrid (sense + actuate) |
| **Power Source** | Battery-powered, Energy-harvesting, Mains-powered |
| **Connectivity** | Short-range (BLE, ZigBee), Long-range (LoRa, NB-IoT), Cellular (4G/5G) |
| **Compute Capability** | Constrained (Class 0/1), Intermediate (Class 2), Rich (Edge Gateways) |
| **Deployment Mobility** | Static, Mobile, Wearable, Implantable (Biomedical) |

> [!NOTE]
> In biomedical applications, IoT devices extend to **Wearable** and **Implantable** classes — including ECG patches, continuous glucose monitors, smart inhalers, and ingestible sensors.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of an IoT device as a **smart postman with a thermometer and a notebook**:

- The **thermometer** is the sensor (it senses the environment).
- The **notebook** is the microcontroller/memory (it records what was sensed).
- The **smartphone** the postman carries is the communication module (it transmits the data).
- The **post office** is the gateway/cloud server (where data is stored and analyzed).
- The **instructions sent back** (e.g., "turn on the AC") are the actuator commands.

Just as the postman operates autonomously — no supervisor has to personally hand him instructions for every delivery — an IoT device operates with minimal human intervention, performing its sensing → processing → communication → actuation cycle continuously.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorability Hook for Exams:** Remember the **SPAA cycle** for any IoT device: **S**ense → **P**rocess → **A**ctuate/Analyze → **A**nnounce (Communicate). This is the "heartbeat" of every IoT device.

---

## 1.3 What are IoT Deployment Models?

**IoT Deployment Models** define *where* and *how* the computational, storage, and analytical resources of an IoT system are physically and logically distributed across the network hierarchy — from the sensor edge to the cloud data center. They determine latency, bandwidth, security posture, scalability, and cost.

The KTU 2024 Scheme recognizes **four canonical deployment models**:

1. **Cloud-based Deployment** — All processing happens in a remote cloud data center.
2. **Fog-based Deployment** — Intermediate layer between edge and cloud handles distributed processing.
3. **Edge-based Deployment** — Processing happens at or near the device itself.
4. **Hybrid Deployment** — Combines edge, fog, and cloud dynamically based on workload.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Deployment Models Matter:** A wearable ECG monitor cannot tolerate the **~50–200 ms round-trip latency** of cloud processing during arrhythmia detection. This is why edge deployment exists. Conversely, a city-wide air quality monitoring system generates massive historical datasets ideal for cloud analytics. **No single deployment model fits all use cases** — the KTU syllabus emphasizes this trade-off analysis.

---

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an IoT Device — Block-Level Architecture

Every IoT device, regardless of complexity, contains the following functional blocks:

### A. Sensing Sub-System
- Converts physical phenomena (temperature, pressure, ECG voltage, SpO₂) into electrical signals.
- Key parameters: **Sensitivity (V/unit)**, **Accuracy (%)**, **Resolution (bits)**, **Sampling Rate (Hz)**.
- Biomedical examples: PPG sensors (photoplethysmography), ECG electrodes, temperature thermistors.

### B. Processing Sub-System
- Microcontroller (MCU) or Microprocessor (MPU) executing firmware.
- Examples: **ARM Cortex-M0/M4** (constrained), **ESP32** (intermediate), **Raspberry Pi** (rich).
- Local processing tasks: filtering, thresholding, feature extraction, encryption.

### C. Communication Sub-System
- Radio transceivers implementing protocols like **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)**, **BLE (IEEE 802.15.1)**, **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)**, **LoRaWAN**, **NB-IoT**.
- Parameters: **Range (m)**, **Data Rate (bps)**, **Power (mW)**, **Duty Cycle (%)**.

### D. Power Sub-System
- Battery, energy harvesting (solar, piezoelectric, thermoelectric), or mains.
- **Critical metric:** Energy per bit transmitted ($E_b$ in Joules/bit).

### E. Actuator Sub-System (in hybrid devices)
- Converts electrical commands into physical actions: motors, valves, drug delivery pumps, defibrillators.

---

## 2.2 The Four IoT Communication Models (KTU High-Yield)

The KTU 2024 Scheme (and the underlying IEEE/IoT World Forum reference model) defines **four canonical communication models**:

### Model 1: Device-to-Device (D2D)
- Direct communication between IoT devices without an intermediary.
- Protocols: BLE, ZigBee, Z-Wave.
- **Use case:** Smart bulb communicating directly with a smart switch.

### Model 2: Device-to-Cloud (D2C)
- Device directly connects to a cloud service provider (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT).
- **Use case:** Smartwatch uploading vitals directly to a health cloud.

### Model 3: Device-to-Gateway (D2G)
- Device connects to an intermediate gateway, which then forwards data to the cloud.
- Gateway handles protocol translation, security, and local buffering.
- **Use case:** BLE fitness band → smartphone gateway → cloud.

### Model 4: Back-End Data-Sharing Model
- Cloud infrastructure exposes data to third-party applications via APIs.
- **Use case:** Hospital EHR system pulling wearable data via REST API.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Fact:** All four models are not mutually exclusive. A single IoT solution can implement multiple models simultaneously. For example, a smart home system uses **D2G** for remote access and **D2D** for local sensor-to-actuator control.

---

## 2.3 The Three IoT Deployment Models — Deep Comparison

| Aspect | Cloud Deployment | Fog Deployment | Edge Deployment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compute Location** | Remote data center | Intermediate fog node | At/near the device |
| **Latency** | **~50–200 ms** | **~10–30 ms** | **< 10 ms** |
| **Bandwidth Need** | High (raw data upload) | Moderate (pre-filtered) | Minimal (only events) |
| **Storage** | Virtually unlimited | Moderate | Severely constrained |
| **Power at Compute** | Off-device | Off-device | On-device (critical) |
| **Security Posture** | Centralized but exposed | Distributed trust | Most secure (data stays local) |
| **Scalability** | Excellent | Good | Limited |
| **Best For** | Historical analytics, ML training | Real-time coordination | Mission-critical, time-sensitive |
| **Biomedical Example** | Population health trends | Hospital ward monitoring | Closed-loop insulin pump |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations are the **high-yield mathematical foundation** for IoT device and deployment analysis. They appear regularly in KTU Part A and Part B questions.

### A. Power Consumption of an IoT Device (Duty-Cycled)

$$P_{avg} = P_{active} \cdot D + P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D)$$

where $P_{active}$ is power during transmission/processing (W), $P_{sleep}$ is idle power (W), and $D$ is the duty cycle (fraction). Units: **Watts (W)**.

### B. Battery Lifetime Estimation

$$T_{life} = \frac{C_{battery}}{I_{avg}} \quad \text{[hours]}$$

where $C_{battery}$ is battery capacity (mAh) and $I_{avg}$ is average current draw (mA). Units: **hours**.

### C. Energy per Bit Transmitted

$$E_b = \frac{P_{tx} \cdot T_{bit}}{1} = P_{tx} \cdot \frac{1}{R_b}$$

where $P_{tx}$ is transmit power (W), $T_{bit}$ is bit duration (s), and $R_b$ is bit rate (bps). Units: **Joules/bit**.

### D. Shannon Channel Capacity (Theoretical Max Data Rate)

$$C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

where $B$ is bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ is signal-to-noise ratio (dimensionless). Units: **bps**.

### E. Latency Decomposition for IoT Pipeline

$$L_{total} = L_{sense} + L_{proc} + L_{tx} + L_{net} + L_{cloud}$$

where each term is a delay component (seconds). For real-time biomedical IoT, $L_{total} < 100$ ms is typically required.

### F. Duty Cycle (Time-Fraction)

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{period}}$$

Dimensions: dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) or percentage (0% to 100%).

---

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Biomedical Engineering

In a **closed-loop artificial pancreas** (Type-1 diabetes management):
- **CGM sensor** (IoT device) senses glucose every 5 minutes.
- **Edge controller** runs a Model Predictive Control (MPC) algorithm locally.
- **Actuator** delivers insulin via micropump.

If this system were cloud-deployed, the latency could cause **dangerous insulin overdose or underdose**. This is why KTU emphasizes that **biomedical IoT almost always demands edge or fog deployment**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off Matrix (Memorize for KTU):**
> - High latency tolerance + massive data → **Cloud**
> - Moderate latency + distributed coordination → **Fog**
> - Ultra-low latency + privacy-critical → **Edge**

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Power Consumption of a Duty-Cycled IoT Device

This derivation is a **high-frequency KTU exam topic** because it directly determines battery life in wearable biomedical IoT devices.

### Step 1: Define the Operating Cycle

An IoT device alternates between two states:
- **Active state** (duration $T_{on}$, power $P_{active}$): when the MCU runs, the radio transmits, and the sensor samples.
- **Sleep state** (duration $T_{off}$, power $P_{sleep}$): when the device is in low-power mode awaiting the next wake-up.

The total period is:

$$T_{period} = T_{on} + T_{off}$$

### Step 2: Express Energy Consumed in One Cycle

Energy in active state:

$$E_{active} = P_{active} \cdot T_{on}$$

Energy in sleep state:

$$E_{sleep} = P_{sleep} \cdot T_{off}$$

Total energy per cycle:

$$E_{total} = P_{active} \cdot T_{on} + P_{sleep} \cdot T_{off}$$

### Step 3: Compute Average Power

Average power is total energy per cycle divided by the period:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{E_{total}}{T_{period}}$$

Substituting:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{P_{active} \cdot T_{on} + P_{sleep} \cdot T_{off}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$$

### Step 4: Introduce the Duty Cycle

By definition, the duty cycle $D$ is:

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{period}}$$

Therefore:

$$1 - D = \frac{T_{off}}{T_{period}}$$

### Step 5: Final Reduction

Dividing numerator and denominator by $T_{period}$:

$$\boxed{P_{avg} = P_{active} \cdot D + P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D)}$$

This is the canonical KTU equation. It shows that **even with a small duty cycle, average power is dominated by $P_{active}$**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem Statement:** A wearable ECG IoT patch has the following specifications:
- Active power during sensing/transmission: $P_{active} = 120$ mW
- Sleep power: $P_{sleep} = 0.05$ mW
- Sampling period: $T_{period} = 1$ s
- Active duration: $T_{on} = 50$ ms

**Required:**
(a) Compute the duty cycle.
(b) Compute the average power consumption.
(c) If the battery capacity is $C = 300$ mAh at $V = 3.7$ V, estimate the device lifetime.

### Step (a): Duty Cycle

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}}{1\ \text{s}}$$

$$D = 0.05 = 5\%$$

### Step (b): Average Power

$$P_{avg} = (120 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.05) + (0.05 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.95)$$

$$P_{avg} = 6.0 \times 10^{-3} + 4.75 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$P_{avg} = 6.0475 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W}$$

$$P_{avg} \approx 6.05\ \text{mW}$$

### Step (c): Device Lifetime

Battery energy:

$$E_{battery} = C \cdot V = (300 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Ah}) \cdot (3.7\ \text{V})$$

$$E_{battery} = 1.11\ \text{Wh} = 3996\ \text{J}$$

Average current draw:

$$I_{avg} = \frac{P_{avg}}{V} = \frac{6.05 \times 10^{-3}}{3.7}$$

$$I_{avg} = 1.635 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A} = 1.635\ \text{mA}$$

Lifetime:

$$T_{life} = \frac{C_{battery}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{300\ \text{mAh}}{1.635\ \text{mA}}$$

$$T_{life} \approx 183.5\ \text{hours} \approx 7.6\ \text{days}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Insight:** This is realistic — modern wearable ECG patches last 5–10 days on a single charge. The duty cycle reduction (e.g., to 1%) by using on-device event detection can extend this to months.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing a Deployment Model

This matrix is the **core analytical tool** KTU expects students to use when justifying deployment choices.

| Decision Criterion | Cloud Score (1–5) | Fog Score (1–5) | Edge Score (1–5) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low Latency Required (< 10 ms) | 1 | 3 | **5** |
| Massive Data Volume (TB/day) | **5** | 3 | 1 |
| Privacy / HIPAA / GDPR Compliance | 2 | 3 | **5** |
| Long-Term Historical Analytics | **5** | 4 | 1 |
| Bandwidth-Constrained Network | 1 | 3 | **5** |
| ML Model Training | **5** | 3 | 1 |
| Autonomous Operation (No Internet) | 1 | 2 | **5** |
| Cost Sensitivity (CapEx) | 2 | 3 | **5** |
| Scalability to Millions of Devices | **5** | 4 | 2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Matrix:** A higher score means the deployment model is *better suited* for that criterion. For an **ICU patient monitor** (low latency + privacy + autonomous operation critical), the **Edge** column dominates. For **population-scale disease surveillance**, the **Cloud** column dominates.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Edge Latency Justification (Why Edge Exists)

Let us derive why edge deployment is mathematically necessary for **closed-loop biomedical control**.

For a stable control loop, the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem applied to control** requires:

$$T_{sample} \leq \frac{T_{response}}{2 \cdot \pi}$$

where $T_{response}$ is the closed-loop response time constant. For an insulin pump with $T_{response} \approx 5$ min = 300 s:

$$T_{sample} \leq \frac{300}{2\pi} \approx 47.7\ \text{s}$$

Cloud latency alone ($L_{cloud} \approx 100$ ms–1 s) plus network jitter ($J \approx 50$ ms) yields:

$$L_{total} \approx L_{cloud} + J \approx 150\ \text{ms} \text{–}\ 1.05\ \text{s}$$

This is well below the 47.7 s bound, **so cloud is theoretically acceptable**. However, for a **defibrillator** with $T_{response} \approx 200$ ms:

$$T_{sample} \leq \frac{200 \times 10^{-3}}{2\pi} \approx 31.8\ \text{ms}$$

Cloud round-trip ($L_{total} \approx 150$ ms) **violates** this bound. Therefore, edge deployment is mandatory for defibrillators and similar ultra-low-latency biomedical IoT devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** The choice of deployment model is not arbitrary — it is derived from the **control-theoretic latency bound** of the physical system being monitored or controlled.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IoT Device Internal Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This diagram maps the internal architecture of a generic IoT device — applicable from simple temperature sensors to complex wearable ECG monitors.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Physical World Phenomenon] --> node2[Sensor Sub-System]
    node2 --> node3[Signal Conditioning and ADC]
    node3 --> node4[Microcontroller MCU]
    node4 --> node5[Memory Firmware and Data]
    node4 --> node6[Security Module Crypto Engine]
    node4 --> node7[Communication Transceiver]
    node7 --> node8[Wireless Channel]
    node4 --> node9[Actuator Driver]
    node9 --> node10[Actuator Physical Response]
    node11[Power Management Unit] -.feeds.-> node4
    node11 -.feeds.-> node7
    node11 -.feeds.-> node2
    node8 --> node12[Gateway or Cloud Endpoint]
    node12 --> node13[Application Server and Analytics]
    node13 -.Feedback.-> node9
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Solid arrows (→) represent **data/control flow**.
- Dotted arrows (-.->) represent **power distribution** from the Power Management Unit.
- The cycle completes when analytics at the application server send a **feedback control signal** back to the actuator.

---

## 4.2 IoT Deployment Model Hierarchy (Edge → Fog → Cloud)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph nodeS1[Edge Layer]
        nodeA1[IoT Sensor 1 Wearable ECG]
        nodeA2[IoT Sensor 2 CGM Patch]
        nodeA3[IoT Sensor 3 Smart Inhaler]
    end
    subgraph nodeS2[Fog Layer]
        nodeB1[Edge Gateway Hospital Router]
        nodeB2[Local Aggregator Smart Home Hub]
    end
    subgraph nodeS3[Cloud Layer]
        nodeC1[Cloud Ingestion AWS IoT Core]
        nodeC2[Big Data Lake and ML Models]
        nodeC3[Analytics Dashboard Clinician Portal]
    end
    nodeA1 --> nodeB1
    nodeA2 --> nodeB1
    nodeA3 --> nodeB2
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1
    nodeB2 --> nodeC1
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2
    nodeC2 --> nodeC3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw" a deployment hierarchy in an exam, this three-tier (Edge–Fog–Cloud) schematic is the canonical answer. Always **label the arrows** with protocol names (BLE, MQTT, HTTPS).

---

## 4.3 Four IoT Communication Models (Comparative Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph nodeM1[Model 1 Device to Device]
        nodeD1[Device A Smart Bulb] <--> nodeD2[Device B Smart Switch]
    end
    subgraph nodeM2[Model 2 Device to Cloud]
        nodeE1[IoT Device Smartwatch] --> nodeE2[Cloud Service AWS IoT]
    end
    subgraph nodeM3[Model 3 Device to Gateway]
        nodeF1[BLE Sensor Band] --> nodeF2[Smartphone Gateway] --> nodeF3[Cloud Server]
    end
    subgraph nodeM4[Model 4 Back End Data Sharing]
        nodeG1[Cloud Database] --> nodeG2[Third Party App Hospital EHR] --> nodeG3[Clinician Mobile App]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction students confuse:** Model 2 (D2C) has **no gateway**; Model 3 (D2G) **always has a gateway**. Examiners frequently test this difference.

---

## 4.4 Hybrid Deployment Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[New IoT Data Event Arrives] --> nodeQ1{Is Latency Critical sub 10 ms}
    nodeQ1 -->|Yes| nodeEdge[Process at Edge Locally]
    nodeQ1 -->|No| nodeQ2{Is Data Volume Massive}
    nodeQ2 -->|No| nodeFog[Process at Fog Node]
    nodeQ2 -->|Yes| nodeCloud[Stream to Cloud for Analytics]
    nodeEdge --> nodeAct1[Trigger Actuator Immediately]
    nodeFog --> nodeAct2[Coordinate Local Devices]
    nodeCloud --> nodeAct3[Store and Train ML Models]
    nodeAct3 -.Periodic Model Updates.-> nodeEdge
    nodeAct3 -.Periodic Model Updates.-> nodeFog
```

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the Decision Logic:** Latency → Edge; Volume → Cloud; Coordination → Fog. This is the **hybrid decision rubric** taught in KTU Module 2.

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define an IoT device. List any **four** essential functional blocks of an IoT device.

**Model Answer:**
An **IoT device** is a uniquely identifiable hardware unit embedded with sensors, actuators, a microcontroller, and a communication module, capable of sensing, processing, and exchanging data over a network with minimal human intervention.

**Four essential functional blocks:**
1. **Sensing Sub-System** — captures physical phenomena (e.g., temperature, ECG).
2. **Processing Sub-System** — microcontroller running firmware for local computation.
3. **Communication Sub-System** — wireless transceiver (Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, etc.).
4. **Power Sub-System** — battery or energy-harvesting source.

(Actuator and security modules are additional, optional blocks.)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Distinguish between **Fog-based** and **Edge-based** IoT deployment models.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Fog Deployment | Edge Deployment |
|---|---|---|
| **Compute Location** | Intermediate fog node (e.g., gateway) | At or on the device itself |
| **Latency** | ~10–30 ms | < 10 ms |
| **Device Capability** | Devices remain constrained; gateway does heavy work | Devices themselves do the processing |
| **Example** | Hospital ward gateway aggregating vitals from 50 patients | Closed-loop insulin pump with on-device MPC algorithm |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ CHOICE A

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** 
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a generic IoT device. Describe the role of **each block**. (7 marks)
**(b)** Explain the **four IoT communication models** (D2D, D2C, D2G, Back-End Data Sharing) with one biomedical example each. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Choice A — Part (a)

**Block Diagram of an IoT Device:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sp1[Physical Quantity] --> sp2[Sensor]
    sp2 --> sp3[ADC and Signal Conditioning]
    sp3 --> sp4[Microcontroller MCU]
    sp4 --> sp5[Memory]
    sp4 --> sp6[Communication Module]
    sp4 --> sp7[Actuator Driver]
    sp7 --> sp8[Actuator]
    sp9[Power Supply] -.power.-> sp4
    sp9 -.power.-> sp6
```

**Roles of Each Block:**

1. **Sensor:** Converts physical signals (temperature, ECG voltage, SpO₂ absorbance) into analog electrical signals. Example: AD8232 for ECG.

2. **Signal Conditioning & ADC:** Amplifies weak signals, filters noise, and converts analog to digital via ADC (e.g., 12-bit ADC sampling at 500 Hz for ECG).

3. **Microcontroller (MCU):** The "brain" of the device. Executes firmware, performs digital filtering (e.g., moving average), and orchestrates communication. Examples: ESP32, STM32, MSP430.

4. **Memory:** Stores firmware (Flash) and runtime data (RAM). Also buffers data when offline.

5. **Communication Module:** Radio transceiver implementing BLE/ZigBee/Wi-Fi/LoRa. Encrypts and transmits data packets.

6. **Actuator Driver & Actuator:** Converts digital commands into physical action — e.g., a micropump delivering insulin.

7. **Power Supply:** Battery, energy harvester, or mains. Often paired with a Power Management IC (PMIC) for sleep/active modes.

**Why each block matters (KTU expects this reasoning):**
- Without **sensor**, the device is blind.
- Without **MCU**, raw analog cannot be interpreted.
- Without **communication**, the data is stranded.
- Without **power management**, the device fails prematurely.

---

#### Model Solution to Choice A — Part (b)

**The Four IoT Communication Models:**

**Model 1 — Device-to-Device (D2D):**
Two IoT devices communicate directly, peer-to-peer, with no intermediary.
- **Protocol examples:** BLE, ZigBee, Z-Wave.
- **Biomedical example:** A wearable **ECG patch** transmitting directly to a **smartwatch display** without using a smartphone or cloud.

**Model 2 — Device-to-Cloud (D2C):**
The IoT device directly connects to a cloud service provider over IP.
- **Protocols:** MQTT, HTTPS, CoAP.
- **Biomedical example:** A connected **smart inhaler** uploading usage data directly to **Propeller Health Cloud** for asthma analytics.

**Model 3 — Device-to-Gateway (D2G):**
The IoT device connects to a local gateway (smartphone, hub, router), which forwards data to the cloud after protocol translation.
- **Protocols (device↔gateway):** BLE; **(gateway↔cloud):** MQTT/HTTPS.
- **Biomedical example:** A **continuous glucose monitor (CGM)** sending data via BLE to a **smartphone gateway**, which uploads to the **Dexcom cloud**.

**Model 4 — Back-End Data-Sharing Model:**
The cloud infrastructure exposes collected data to authorized third-party applications via standardized APIs.
- **Protocols:** REST APIs, GraphQL, FHIR (for healthcare).
- **Biomedical example:** A **hospital EHR system** querying the **Apple HealthKit cloud** via FHIR API to retrieve a patient's wearable ECG history.

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**(a)** Compare and contrast the **three IoT deployment models** (Cloud, Fog, Edge) with respect to latency, bandwidth, security, scalability, and biomedical suitability. (7 marks)
**(b)** A wearable pulse oximeter IoT device operates with $P_{active} = 80$ mW and $P_{sleep} = 0.08$ mW. The sampling period is 2 s with active duration 100 ms. Compute (i) the duty cycle, (ii) the average power, and (iii) the battery lifetime for a 400 mAh, 3.7 V battery. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Choice B — Part (a)

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Cloud | Fog | Edge |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | 50–200 ms | 10–30 ms | < 10 ms |
| **Bandwidth** | High (raw streams) | Moderate (filtered) | Minimal (events only) |
| **Security** | Centralized; large attack surface | Distributed trust | Strongest (data stays local) |
| **Scalability** | Excellent (elastic) | Good | Limited (device-bound) |
| **Biomedical Suitability** | Population analytics, ML training | Hospital ward monitoring, telemedicine | Implantables, closed-loop drug delivery |

**Analytical Justification (KTU wants reasoning, not just tables):**

- **Cloud deployment** excels when the application is **non-time-critical** but **data-hungry** — e.g., training a neural network on 10 million de-identified chest X-rays for tuberculosis screening. Latency is irrelevant; storage and compute elasticity are paramount.

- **Fog deployment** strikes a balance — it is **deployed when multiple devices must coordinate locally** with low latency, but the coordination logic exceeds the capability of any single device. A **smart ICU** with 100 monitors coordinating alarms is canonical.

- **Edge deployment** is **mandatory when (i) latency is sub-10 ms, (ii) privacy laws prohibit raw data transmission, or (iii) the device must operate offline.** A **closed-loop artificial pancreas** is the textbook case.

**Engineering Trade-off Summary:**
There is no universally "best" model. KTU 2024 expects students to justify a deployment choice based on a **weighted combination of latency, security, and scalability constraints**.

---

#### Model Solution to Choice B — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $P_{active} = 80$ mW
- $P_{sleep} = 0.08$ mW
- $T_{period} = 2$ s
- $T_{on} = 100$ ms = 0.1 s
- Battery: $C = 400$ mAh, $V = 3.7$ V

**(i) Duty Cycle:**

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} = \frac{0.1}{2} = 0.05 = 5\%$$

**(ii) Average Power:**

$$P_{avg} = P_{active} \cdot D + P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D)$$

$$P_{avg} = (80 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.05) + (0.08 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.95)$$

$$P_{avg} = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} + 0.076 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P_{avg} = 4.076 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W} \approx 4.08\ \text{mW}$$

**(iii) Battery Lifetime:**

Average current draw:

$$I_{avg} = \frac{P_{avg}}{V} = \frac{4.076 \times 10^{-3}}{3.7} = 1.1016 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A}$$

$$I_{avg} = 1.1016\ \text{mA}$$

Lifetime:

$$T_{life} = \frac{C}{I_{avg}} = \frac{400\ \text{mAh}}{1.1016\ \text{mA}}$$

$$T_{life} \approx 363.1\ \text{hours} \approx 15.1\ \text{days}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Writing $T_{on}$ in seconds without converting from ms (or vice versa) yields a duty cycle off by 1000×. Always convert to SI units first.
> 2. **Forgetting the $(1-D)$ term:** Many students write only $P_{active} \cdot D$ and ignore the sleep power contribution.
> 3. **Battery voltage confusion:** Capacity (mAh) is not energy (Wh). You must multiply by voltage to get Wh before dividing by power. Alternatively, divide mAh by mA directly to get hours — but **only if voltage is consistent** (it is, since power = VI).
> 4. **Confusing Fog and Edge:** Fog = intermediate gateway; Edge = on-device. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this mix-up.
> 5. **Calling D2C and D2G the same:** D2C has **no gateway**; D2G **always** has one. This is the single most common KTU trap in Part A.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU Module 2 — IoT Devices & Deployment Models):**

- [x] **IoT Device =** Sensor + MCU + Communication + Power (the four mandatory blocks).
- [x] **SPAA Cycle:** Sense → Process → Actuate → Announce (the heartbeat of any IoT device).
- [x] **Four Communication Models:** D2D, D2C, D2G, Back-End Data Sharing — all four can coexist in one system.
- [x] **Three Deployment Models:** Cloud (high latency OK), Fog (coordination), Edge (sub-10 ms, privacy).
- [x] **Key Formula 1:** $P_{avg} = P_{active} \cdot D + P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D)$ — **derive it if asked.**
- [x] **Key Formula 2:** $T_{life} = C / I_{avg}$ — battery lifetime.
- [x] **Key Formula 3:** $D = T_{on} / T_{period}$ — duty cycle.
- [x] **Biomedical Edge Cases:** Insulin pump, defibrillator → Edge; ICU ward → Fog; population analytics → Cloud.
- [x] **Trade-off Principle:** No single deployment model is universally best; the choice is **justified by latency, privacy, and scalability constraints.**
- [x] **D2C vs. D2G:** D2C skips the gateway; D2G routes through one. Examiners love this distinction.
- [x] **Class 0/1/2 Devices:** Constrained → Intermediate → Rich — drives whether edge processing is feasible.
- [x] **Always convert units to SI** (ms → s, mW → W) before plugging into any formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoTivity: An Open-source IoT Stack – Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IoTivity: An Open-source IoT Stack – Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# IoTivity: An Open-source IoT Stack – Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**IoTivity** is a **Linux Foundation hosted, open-source software framework** designed to enable seamless, device-to-device connectivity for the Internet of Things (IoT). Originally initiated by **Samsung Electronics** and later maintained under the **Open Connectivity Foundation (OCF)** alliance, IoTivity provides a universal communication layer that allows diverse smart devices, sensors, and actuators to discover, connect, and interoperate across different operating systems, communication protocols, and hardware platforms.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Context):**
> IoTivity is an open-source IoT stack sponsored by the Open Connectivity Foundation (OCF) that addresses the IoT fragmentation problem by providing a standardized, cross-platform framework for device discovery, data exchange, and management — independent of the underlying transport, OS, or hardware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> IoTivity is **not a protocol** by itself; rather, it is a **middleware framework** that implements and builds upon standard protocols such as **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)**, **DTLS (Datagram Transport Layer Security)**, **CBOR (Concise Binary Object Representation)**, and **IPv6/6LoWPAN** to provide a unified, horizontal IoT stack.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **United Nations-style global interpreter hub** where every diplomat (IoT device) speaks a different native language (Zigbee, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, proprietary protocols). Without a translator, communication is impossible.

- **IoTivity acts as the universal translator desk.**
- It ensures that a Samsung smart bulb (speaking Wi-Fi) can talk to a Texas Instruments health sensor (speaking 6LoWPAN/BLE) using a **common vocabulary (OCF Core Specification)**.
- The translator also enforces **diplomatic security (DTLS encryption)** so eavesdroppers cannot read the conversation.

> Real-world analogy: IoTivity is to IoT devices what **USB** is to computer peripherals — a single, standardized interface that makes heterogeneous hardware work together seamlessly.

## 1.3 Core Objectives of IoTivity

| Objective | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Interoperability** | Hides heterogeneity of physical layers (Wi-Fi, BLE, Zigbee) |
| **Security** | Provides device-level identity, DTLS-based mutual authentication |
| **Scalability** | Designed for resource-constrained Class 0/1 devices |
| **Discoverability** | Auto-discovery of resources in a local network |
| **Data Modeling** | Standardized resource model using JSON / CBOR |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exam:** IoTivity = "**C**onnect **A**ll IoT **D**evices **S**ecurely" — Connect (CoAP), All (Cross-platform), Devices (Resource model), Securely (DTLS).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IoTivity Architecture – Layered Breakdown

IoTivity is built as a **layered stack** aligned closely with the **OSI reference model**, but optimized for constrained IoT environments. The framework is modular and C/C++ based (with Java/Android bindings).

### Layer-wise Functional Logic

1. **Application Layer (Top)**
   - Houses user-facing apps, sensors, actuators
   - Exposes data as **Resources** (the fundamental addressable entity in IoTivity)

2. **Framework / Core Layer**
   - Implements **Resource Modelling** (Properties, Actions, Collections)
   - Manages **Discovery** (Resource Directory / mDNS)
   - Handles **Group Communication** and **Notification / Observation**

3. **Communication Layer**
   - **CoAP** (RFC 7252) — RESTful request-response over UDP
   - **Observe pattern** — Server pushes resource state changes
   - **Group multicast** — One-to-many resource updates

4. **Security Layer**
   - **DTLS 1.2** handshake for mutual authentication
   - **X.509 / PSK** based device credentials
   - **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** defined per resource

5. **Transport / Network Layer**
   - **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** primarily
   - **TCP** support for reliable bulk transfer
   - **IPv6 / 6LoWPAN / Bluetooth LE** adapters

6. **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) — Bottom**
   - Pluggable adapters for Wi-Fi, BLE, NFC, Ethernet
   - Allows the same code to run on a Raspberry Pi, Arduino, or smartphone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Every IoTivity transaction is identified by a **Uniform Resource Identifier (URI)**. A typical resource URI is structured as `ocf://<device-id>/<resource-path>`, e.g., `ocf://ac:de:48:00:00:01/light/1`.

## 2.2 Core Components in Detail

### (a) Resource Model — The Heart of IoTivity

In IoTivity, **everything is a Resource**. A resource has:
- **URI (Unique Address)**
- **Resource Types (`rt`)** — semantic description (e.g., `core.light`, `core.temperature`)
- **Interfaces (`if`)** — `oic.if.baseline`, `oic.if.rw`, `oic.if.s`
- **Properties (Attributes)**
- **Methods (GET, PUT, POST, DELETE)** — RESTful verbs

### (b) Discovery Mechanisms

1. **Multicast Discovery (mDNS-style over UDP port 5683):** Devices broadcast a query, peers reply with their known resources.
2. **Resource Directory (RD) Pattern:** A central server caches resources of sleepy/constrained devices, allowing clients to discover them via standard lookup.

### (c) Security Handshake Sequence

1. Client sends `ClientHello` over UDP.
2. Server replies with `ServerHello` + certificate.
3. Mutual authentication via **X.509** or **Pre-Shared Key (PSK)**.
4. Encrypted session established; all CoAP messages wrapped in DTLS.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes key engineering parameters, sizes, and constants used in IoTivity resource design — **frequently tested in KTU Module 2 derivations**.

| Parameter / Symbol | Standard Value / Expression | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Default CoAP Port** | $5683$ (UDP) | Unsecured CoAP endpoint port number |
| **Secure CoAP Port (CoAPS)** | $5684$ (UDP over DTLS) | DTLS-encrypted CoAP endpoint |
| **Resource URI Length** | $\leq 255$ bytes | Max URI size in OCF specification |
| **mDNS Multicast Address** | $224.0.1.41$ | IPv4 multicast group for IoTivity discovery |
| **IPv6 All-Nodes Multicast** | $FF02 \colon \colon 1$ | Default CoAP multicast target on IPv6 |
| **CoAP Message ID** | $16$-bit (unsigned) | Identifier for matching ACK to request |
| **Token Length** | $\leq 8$ bytes | Used to correlate Observe notifications |
| **Block Transfer Size** | $16, 32, 64, 128, \ldots, 1024$ bytes | CoAP blockwise transfer payload sizes |
| **Header Overhead (CoAP)** | $4$ bytes (base) $\mid$ up to $10$ bytes options | Min CoAP header size |
| **CBOR vs JSON Size** | $\approx 60\%$ of JSON size | CBOR is binary encoded, more compact |
| **DTLS Record Size** | $\leq 2^{14} \text{ bytes}$ | Max payload per DTLS record |
| **Resource Refresh Interval** | $t_r = 60$ s (default) | Periodic resource advertisement |
| **Observe Lifetime** | $\tau_o = 86400$ s (24 h) | Default observation subscription validity |

## 2.4 Real-world Engineering Utility

In biomedical IoT (KTU Module 2 mapping), IoTivity is used in:
- **Personal Health Devices (PHD):** Pulse oximeters, glucometers conforming to **IEEE 11073 PHD** and OCF specs.
- **Hospital Asset Tracking:** BLE-tagged equipment discovered via IoTivity mDNS.
- **Ambient Assisted Living (AAL):** Unified discovery of environmental sensors (temperature, motion) and actuators (alarms) on a single RESTful interface.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Resource Model Mathematics

## 3.1 Derivation: Size Estimation of an IoTivity CoAP Transaction

A typical **GET request** for a single biomedical resource (e.g., a patient's body temperature) is structured as follows. We compute the total on-wire size to understand bandwidth constraints in wearable IoT devices.

### Step 1 — Identify the components of a CoAP message

A standard CoAP message contains:
- **Header:** $4$ bytes (Ver, T, TKL, Code, Message ID)
- **Token (optional):** $0$ to $8$ bytes
- **Options:** variable (Uri-Path, Accept, etc.)
- **Payload marker + Payload:** $1$ byte marker + payload size

### Step 2 — Compute Header Size

$$
H_{\text{header}} = 4 \; \text{bytes}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Option Overhead

For a resource URI path `/patient/temp`, three Uri-Path options are needed. Each option has a base header of $1$ byte plus length, and value overhead. Typically:

$$
H_{\text{options}} = H_{\text{uri-path}} + H_{\text{accept}} + H_{\text{content-format}}
$$

Assuming a typical well-formed transaction:

$$
H_{\text{options}} \approx 8 \; \text{bytes}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Payload Size

For a single float temperature reading encoded in **CBOR**:

$$
P_{\text{CBOR}} = 1 \; \text{byte} \;\; \text{(major type + value)} \;\; + \;\; 4 \; \text{bytes} \;\; \text{(IEEE 754 float)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_{\text{CBOR}} = 5 \; \text{bytes}
$$

If the application uses **plain text** representation instead:

$$
P_{\text{text}} = L_{\text{value}}
$$

For a temperature string `"36.8"`:

$$
P_{\text{text}} = 4 \; \text{bytes}
$$

### Step 5 — Total On-Wire Size (CBOR Case)

$$
S_{\text{total}} = H_{\text{header}} + H_{\text{options}} + 1 \; \text{(payload marker)} + P_{\text{CBOR}}
$$

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 4 + 8 + 1 + 5 = 18 \; \text{bytes}
$$

### Step 6 — Total On-Wire Size (JSON Case, for comparison)

A JSON representation `{"temp":36.8}` has:

$$
P_{\text{JSON}} = 13 \; \text{bytes}
$$

$$
S_{\text{JSON}} = 4 + 8 + 1 + 13 = 26 \; \text{bytes}
$$

### Step 7 — Compute Bandwidth Efficiency Ratio

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{\text{CBOR}}}{P_{\text{JSON}}} = \frac{5}{13} \approx 0.3846 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \eta \approx 38.46\%
$$

So CBOR consumes **only ~38% of the size** of an equivalent JSON payload — a critical efficiency win for constrained biomedical wearables.

### Step 8 — Interpret the Result for Engineering Design

Given a typical **BLE 4.2** link budget of $\approx 250$ kbps, the time to transmit a single temperature reading is:

$$
T_{\text{CBOR}} = \frac{S_{\text{total}} \times 8}{250 \times 10^3} = \frac{18 \times 8}{250000} \approx 0.576 \; \text{ms}
$$

$$
T_{\text{JSON}} = \frac{26 \times 8}{250000} \approx 0.832 \; \text{ms}
$$

Thus, CBOR saves **~0.256 ms per transaction** — a significant margin when a wearable transmits at $1$ Hz continuously for $24$ hours:

$$
\text{Daily Savings} = 0.256 \times 10^{-3} \times 86400 \approx 22.12 \; \text{seconds of airtime saved}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Over a single day, CBOR encoding saves $\approx 22$ seconds of radio-on time, which translates directly to **battery life extension** in wearable biomedical devices — a critical design metric.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: CoAP Method Mapping to REST Semantics

| CoAP Method | REST Verb | Typical IoTivity Use Case (Biomedical) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **GET** | Read | Retrieve latest SpO$_2$ reading |
| **POST** | Create | Upload a new ECG segment |
| **PUT** | Update | Modify alarm threshold for tachycardia |
| **DELETE** | Remove | Deregister a sensor from a patient |
| **Observe (extension)** | Subscribe | Push alerts when heart rate exceeds limit |

## 3.3 Observation Subscription State Machine (Analytical Flow)

The Observe pattern follows this transition sequence, modeled as a state machine:

$$
S_0 \xrightarrow{\text{GET (Observe=0)}} S_1 \xrightarrow{\text{2.05 Notification}} S_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Resource change}} S_1
$$

- $S_0$ = Initial state (no subscription)
- $S_1$ = Subscribed (server holds observer token)
- $S_2$ = Notification sent (client receives update)

Maximum observation period before forced refresh:

$$
T_{\text{refresh}} = \tau_o = 86400 \; \text{seconds}
$$

If a notification is not received within $\tau_o$, the client must re-send a GET with `Observe: 0` to re-establish subscription.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IoTivity Architecture — Layered Block Diagram

> The following **Mermaid block-level functional architecture** maps the entire IoTivity stack as seen from a cross-platform, biomedical IoT perspective.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appLayer["APPLICATION LAYER -- User Apps, Dashboards, Hospital EMR Systems"]
    resourceModel["FRAMEWORK LAYER -- Resource Model, Collections, Groups, Discovery"]
    coapLogic["COMMUNICATION LAYER -- CoAP Client Server, Observe, Block Transfer"]
    dtlsSec["SECURITY LAYER -- DTLS 1.2, X.509, PSK, Access Control Lists"]
    transport["TRANSPORT LAYER -- UDP, TCP, IPv6, 6LoWPAN"]
    hal["HARDWARE ABSTRACTION LAYER -- Wi-Fi, BLE, Zigbee, NFC, Ethernet"]

    appLayer --> resourceModel
    resourceModel --> coapLogic
    coapLogic --> dtlsSec
    dtlsSec --> transport
    transport --> hal
    hal --> hwDev["PHYSICAL IoT HARDWARE -- Wearables, Sensors, Actuators, Smartphones"]

    dev1["Patient Smartwatch"] -.-> hwDev
    dev2["Hospital BP Monitor"] -.-> hwDev
    dev3["Insulin Pump"] -.-> hwDev
    dev4["Ambient Motion Sensor"] -.-> hwDev

    classDef layer fill:e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef device fill:fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    class appLayer,resourceModel,coapLogic,dtlsSec,transport,hal,hwDev layer
    class dev1,dev2,dev3,dev4 device
```

## 4.2 IoTivity Discovery & Handshake Flow

> The diagram below illustrates the **multicast discovery** and **DTLS-secured resource access** flow between a medical gateway (client) and a wearable pulse oximeter (server).

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Gateway as Medical Gateway (Client)
    participant Server as Wearable SpO2 Sensor (Server)
    participant RD as Resource Directory (Optional)

    Note over Gateway,Server: PHASE 1 - Multicast Discovery (Unsecured)
    Gateway->>Server: COAP GET (Multicast, ocf://*/oic.r.doxm)
    Server-->>Gateway: 2.05 CON (Device Info: oic.r.doxm)

    Note over Gateway,Server: PHASE 2 - DTLS Handshake on Port 5684
    Gateway->>Server: ClientHello (X.509 cert)
    Server-->>Gateway: ServerHello + Certificate
    Gateway->>Server: Key Exchange, Finished
    Server-->>Gateway: Finished (Secure Session Established)

    Note over Gateway,Server: PHASE 3 - Secure Resource Access
    Gateway->>Server: GET (Secure CoAPS, /spo2/1)
    Server-->>Gateway: 2.05 CON (spo2 value, CBOR encoded)
    Gateway->>Server: GET (Observe option, /heartrate/1)
    Server-->>Gateway: Notification on each new reading

    Note over RD: PHASE 4 - Resource Directory (Optional caching)
    Gateway->>RD: Publish discovered resources
    RD-->>Gateway: Lookup query response
```

## 4.3 IoTivity Resource Model — Object Structure

> The following block diagram represents how a single biomedical resource (e.g., body temperature) is modeled within the IoTivity resource hierarchy.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    device["IoTivity Device - Patient Wearable"] --> col["Collection Resource - vitalSigns"]
```
