```mermaid
    col --> temp["Resource - bodyTemperature"]
    col --> hr["Resource - heartRate"]
    col --> spo2["Resource - spo2Level"]

    temp --> uri1["URI: /vitals/temp"]
    temp --> rt1["Resource Type: core.temperature"]
    temp --> if1["Interfaces: oic.if.r, oic.if.s"]
    temp --> prop1["Properties: value=36.8, units=degC"]
    temp --> meth1["Methods: GET, PUT, Observe"]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Block: IoTivity vs Other IoT Stacks

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["IoTivity (OCF, Linux Foundation)"] ---|Uses| B["CoAP over UDP"]
    C["OneM2M"] ---|Uses| D["HTTP, CoAP, MQTT"]
    E["AWS IoT Core"] ---|Uses| F["MQTT over TLS"]
    G["Google Weave"] ---|Uses| H["HTTP, Protobuf"]

    subgraph PROS_A ["IoTivity Strengths"]
    A1["Open Source"]
    A2["Cross Platform"]
    A3["Vendor Neutral"]
    end
    A --> A1
    A --> A2
    A --> A3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define IoTivity. Mention any four of its key features. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**IoTivity** is an **open-source software framework** developed initially by Samsung and now hosted by the **Linux Foundation** under the **Open Connectivity Foundation (OCF)**, designed to provide seamless device-to-device connectivity for the Internet of Things across heterogeneous platforms.

**Four key features:**
1. **Cross-platform support** — runs on Linux, Android, iOS, Arduino, Tizen.
2. **Standardized Resource Model** — uniform addressable entities using OCF URI scheme.
3. **Built-in Security** — DTLS-based mutual authentication and ACL.
4. **Discovery & Observe** — multicast mDNS-style discovery and CoAP Observe pattern for real-time push.

---

### Q2. What is the role of the CoAP protocol in the IoTivity stack? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol, RFC 7252)** is the primary **application layer messaging protocol** used by IoTivity. It enables **RESTful communication** (GET, PUT, POST, DELETE) over **UDP**, making it ideal for constrained IoT devices where TCP overhead is undesirable. It also supports an **Observe extension** for server-push notifications and **block-wise transfer** for large payloads, both integral to IoTivity's resource handling.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks.

---

### ❓ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **layered architecture of IoTivity** in detail. Compare it briefly with the OSI model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **CoAP Observe pattern** and its use in real-time patient monitoring. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The IoTivity stack is divided into **six functional layers**, each performing a specific role:

| Layer | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1. **Application Layer** | Hosts the user-facing apps and high-level logic (e.g., a hospital dashboard querying a wearable). |
| 2. **Framework / Core Layer** | Manages the **Resource Model**, **Collections**, **Groups**, and **Discovery** mechanisms. |
| 3. **Communication Layer** | Implements the **CoAP protocol** for RESTful transactions and **Observe** for subscriptions. |
| 4. **Security Layer** | Provides **DTLS 1.2** encryption, **X.509/PSK** authentication, and **Access Control Lists (ACLs)**. |
| 5. **Transport Layer** | Manages **UDP/TCP** sockets, **IPv6**, and **6LoWPAN** for low-power networks. |
| 6. **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** | Provides pluggable adapters for **Wi-Fi, BLE, Zigbee, NFC, Ethernet** — enabling cross-platform operation. |

**Comparison with OSI:**
- OSI has 7 layers; IoTivity consolidates Session, Presentation, and Application into its top three layers.
- IoTivity's **CoAP + DTLS** combination replaces HTTP + TLS, optimized for **constrained nodes**.
- The HAL in IoTivity corresponds to the **Physical + Data Link** layers in OSI.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The **CoAP Observe pattern** (RFC 7641) is an extension that allows a client to **subscribe** to a resource on a server. Whenever the resource's state changes, the server **pushes** the new value to the client **without** the client re-issuing a GET request.

**Diagram:** *(See Section 4.3 flowchart for visual flow)*

**Use in real-time patient monitoring:**
- A **patient wearable (SpO2 sensor)** acts as the CoAP server hosting `/spo2/1`.
- The **nurse station gateway (client)** sends a GET with `Observe: 0` option.
- The server registers the observer and returns an initial 2.05 response with a **token**.
- When SpO2 drops below threshold, the server **proactively sends a notification** with the new value.
- The client need not poll continuously — saving bandwidth and battery.

**Advantages:** Reduced latency, energy efficiency, real-time alerts for critical care.

---

### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **Resource Model** in IoTivity. Explain the structure of an OCF resource URI with a suitable biomedical example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Illustrate the **IoTivity discovery mechanism** using multicast. How does it differ from a Resource Directory (RD) based discovery? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **Resource Model** is the foundational abstraction in IoTivity. **Every entity** — be it a sensor reading, an actuator command, or a device configuration — is represented as a **Resource** with the following attributes:

- **URI:** Globally unique address of the resource.
- **Resource Type (`rt`):** Semantic identifier (e.g., `core.temperature`, `oic.r.pulseoximeter`).
- **Interface (`if`):** Operations allowed — `oic.if.baseline`, `oic.if.rw`, `oic.if.s`, `oic.if.a`.
- **Properties:** Key-value pairs of the actual data (e.g., `value`, `units`, `range`).
- **Methods:** RESTful operations — `GET, PUT, POST, DELETE`.

**Structure of an OCF resource URI:**

$$
\texttt{ocf://[device-id]/[resource-type]/[resource-instance]}
$$

**Biomedical Example:** Consider a wearable cardiac monitor with device ID `ac-de-48-00-00-01`. Its heart rate resource URI is:

$$
\texttt{ocf://ac:de:48:00:00:01/hr/1}
$$

Where:
- `ac:de:48:00:00:01` is the **device's EUI-64 hardware address**.
- `hr` denotes the **heart rate resource type**.
- `1` is the **instance number** (in case of multiple sensors of the same type).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Multicast Discovery in IoTivity:**

- The client (e.g., a smartphone) sends a **CoAP GET request** to the **IPv4 multicast address $224.0.1.41$** (or IPv6 `FF02::1`) on **UDP port $5683$**.
- The request includes a **Resource Type filter** (e.g., `rt=core.temperature`) to look for specific resources.
- All compatible devices on the local network **respond with a 2.05 CON message** containing their device ID and matching resource information.
- The client builds a local cache of available resources.

**Differences vs Resource Directory (RD):**

| Aspect | Multicast Discovery | Resource Directory (RD) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Network Type** | Local subnet only | Works across networks (via proxy) |
| **Sleepy Devices** | Not friendly (must be awake) | Friendly (RD caches on behalf) |
| **Scalability** | Limited by multicast scope | Scales to thousands of devices |
| **Security** | Unsecured multicast | Can be DTLS-secured between client & RD |
| **Use Case** | Home, small clinic | Large hospital, multi-floor deployment |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse IoTivity with IoT protocols like MQTT or HTTP — IoTivity is a **framework** that *uses* CoAP.
> 2. **Always mention the port numbers** $5683$ (unsecured) and $5684$ (secured DTLS) — examiners frequently award 1 mark for this.
> 3. **Do not write** "IoTivity is a protocol" — this is a common **1-mark deduction** error in KTU valuation.
> 4. **URI must follow the `ocf://` scheme**, not `http://` — wrong prefix = $\approx 2$ marks lost.
> 5. **For Compare-type questions**, tabulate the differences; bullet-only answers lose 1–2 marks for lack of structure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — IoTivity Overview**

- ✅ **IoTivity** = Open-source, Linux Foundation hosted, OCF-sponsored IoT **framework** (not a protocol).
- ✅ **Original sponsor:** Samsung Electronics; **Current maintainer:** Open Connectivity Foundation (OCF) / Linux Foundation.
- ✅ **Primary protocol used:** **CoAP (RFC 7252)** over **UDP**.
- ✅ **Default CoAP port:** $\mathbf{5683}$ (unsecured) | **CoAPS port:** $\mathbf{5684}$ (DTLS-secured).
- ✅ **Transport Layer:** UDP / TCP / IPv6 / 6LoWPAN.
- ✅ **Security:** DTLS 1.2 with X.509 certificates or PSK; ACL per resource.
- ✅ **Resource Model fundamentals:** URI + Resource Type + Interface + Properties + REST methods.
- ✅ **URI scheme:** `ocf://<device-id>/<resource-type>/<instance>`.
- ✅ **Discovery types:** Multicast (mDNS-style, local) and Resource Directory (RD, scalable).
- ✅ **Observe pattern (RFC 7641):** Server-push updates to subscribed clients.
- ✅ **Encoding:** CBOR preferred over JSON for size efficiency (~38% size of JSON).
- ✅ **Biomedical use cases:** PHD (IEEE 11073), hospital asset tracking, AAL, real-time vitals monitoring.
- ✅ **Cross-platform support:** Linux, Tizen, Android, iOS, Arduino, Raspberry Pi.
- ✅ **Block-wise transfer** supported for large payloads (e.g., ECG waveforms).
- ✅ **HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer)** is the key to cross-platform operation.

> **One-liner for KTU Viva:** *"IoTivity is a cross-platform, open-source middleware that unifies IoT device communication through a standardized resource model, CoAP, and DTLS security, enabling seamless discovery and data exchange in biomedical and industrial IoT applications."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoTivity Stack Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IoTivity Stack Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**IoTivity** is an open-source, cross-platform software framework sponsored by the **Open Connectivity Foundation (OCF)** that provides a unified, standards-based communication stack for seamless **device-to-device (D2D)** discovery, provisioning, and data exchange across heterogeneous IoT ecosystems. It is engineered to operate over constrained networks (RFC 7228) and supports a **Resource-Oriented Architecture (ROA)** built on top of the **Constrained Application Protocol (CoAP – RFC 7252)**, enabling interoperability regardless of operating system, transport medium, or hardware manufacturer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** IoTivity is a *layered IoT middleware* that abstracts the underlying hardware and OS differences, allowing any OCF-certified sensor, actuator, or smart device to interact with any other without vendor-specific bridges.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"IoTivity is the Universal Translator & Postman of the IoT World."**

Imagine a hotel where every guest speaks a different language (English, Mandarin, Spanish, Arabic). Without a common translator, communication is impossible. **IoTivity acts as a master translator and post-office clerk combined**:

- **Discovery Phase:** When you walk into the hotel lobby, the clerk (IoTivity) hands you a directory of all other guests and the *services* they offer (e.g., "Room 204 offers laundry, Room 310 offers taxi service"). In IoT terminology, this directory is the **Resource Directory**, and each service is a **Resource**.
- **Communication Phase:** You write your request in your native language. The translator converts it into a **Common Language (CBOR/JSON over CoAP)** so that the recipient understands you.
- **Security Phase:** Before the message leaves, the clerk checks a digital passport (**X.509 certificates / PSK-based DTLS handshake**) to confirm the identity of both sender and receiver.

**In one line:** *IoTivity = Common Language + Address Book + Secure Postman for IoT devices.*

### 1.3 Key Standardization Bodies & Constants

| Entity | Standard / Constant | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **OCF** | OCF Core Specification 2.x | Defines resource model & APIs |
| **IETF** | CoAP (RFC 7252), UDP (RFC 768) | Application/Transport layer |
| **IETF** | DTLS (RFC 6347) | End-to-end security |
| **IANA** | **Default CoAP Port: 5683** (unsecured) / **5684** (secured) | Well-known UDP ports |
| **IPSO Alliance** | Smart Object Model (LWM2M) | Semantic data modelling |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Layered IoTivity Stack — Operational Breakdown

IoTivity is organized as a **horizontal layered architecture** intersected by two **vertical planes** (Security & Device Management). Each layer exposes a strict API to the layer above, ensuring modularity.

**Layer 1 — Application Layer (Top)**
- **Why:** End-user logic (e.g., a smartphone app, a cloud dashboard).
- **How:** Invokes IoTivity APIs to find resources, send GET/PUT/POST/DELETE requests.
- **Real-world utility:** Hospital nurse-call apps, smart-home dashboards.

**Layer 2 — Resource & Framework Layer (Core)**
- **Why:** Implements the **Resource Model** — every physical or virtual entity (light, temperature, switch) is abstracted as a *Resource* with **Properties (Attributes)**, supported **Methods (CRUD operations)**, and exposed **Interfaces**.
- **How:** Each resource is identified by a unique **Uniform Resource Identifier (URI)**, for example: `/ocf/a/light/1`.
- **Key construct:** A *Collection Resource* can group multiple resources for bulk operations.

**Layer 3 — Common Layer**
- **Why:** Translates user-level requests into transport-agnostic messages.
- **How:** Handles **Request/Response** matching, **Observations (CoAP Observe, RFC 7641)**, **Group Communication**, **Presence**, and **Resource Directory** caching.

**Layer 4 — Transport Layer**
- **Why:** Reliable, low-overhead messaging over constrained networks.
- **How:** Uses **CoAP** over **UDP** as the default, with optional **TCP** (CoAP over TCP – RFC 8323) for high-throughput devices.

**Layer 5 — Network & Adaptation Layer**
- **Why:** Enables IoTivity to run on *any* network (Wi-Fi, Ethernet, Bluetooth LE, ZigBee, Thread, 6LoWPAN).
- **How:** Uses the **Adaptation Layer Interface** with network-specific plugins (e.g., `WIFI_ADAPTER`, `BLE_ADAPTER`).

**Layer 6 — Physical / Link Layer (Bottom)**
- The actual radio/medium (IEEE 802.15.4, 802.11, NFC, etc.).

**Vertical Plane 1 — Security Manager**
- Enforces **DTLS 1.2 handshake**, **X.509v3 certificate validation**, **Pre-Shared Key (PSK)** modes, and **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** at the resource level.

**Vertical Plane 2 — Device Management (DM)**
- Handles **Just-Works Pairing**, **Reset/Factory**, **Firmware Update (FOTA)**, and **Device Diagnostics** through OCF-defined procedures.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The "formulas" in IoTivity are primarily **protocol mappings**, **addressing schemes**, and **message semantics**. Memorize the table below for direct KTU questions.

| Concept | Notation / Format | Typical Value / Limit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resource URI** | `/ocf/{A}/{device-type}/{id}` | e.g., `/oic/res` | Canonical address of any resource |
| **CoAP Code** | `METHOD.MNEMONIC` | `0.01 GET`, `0.02 POST`, `0.03 PUT`, `0.04 DELETE` | CRUD operation mapping |
| **CoAP Response Code** | Class.Detail | `2.05 Content`, `4.04 Not Found` | Server feedback |
| **Default Port (Insecure)** | $P_{coap}$ | **5683 / UDP** | Standard discovery port |
| **Default Port (Secure)** | $P_{coaps}$ | **5684 / UDP** | DTLS-secured channel |
| **Content Format** | Numeric ID | `10000 = JSON`, `10010 = CBOR` | Payload encoding |
| **Resource Density** | $D = \frac{N_r}{N_d}$ | $D \ge 1$ | Resources per device |
| **Discovery Time** | $T_d = T_{req} + T_{prop} + T_{proc}$ | Typically 2–5 s on 6LoWPAN | End-to-end latency |
| **Observe Lifetime** | $\tau_{obs}$ | Configurable (default 60 s) | Subscription duration |
| **DTLS Handshake RTT** | $H_{DTLS} = 1.5 \times RTT_{net}$ | 1.5 round-trips | Initial session cost |
| **Max Payload (CoAP)** | $S_{max}$ | **1024 bytes** (default block-wise up to 4 GB) | Single block size |
| **ACL Entry** | `{subject, resources, permissions}` | Up to 1024 entries | Access control matrix |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** When a KTU numerical asks to compute discovery latency, use:
> $$T_{d} = T_{coap\_req} + T_{dtls} + T_{process} + 2 \times T_{prop}$$
> where $T_{prop} = \frac{d}{v_{medium}}$ for distance $d$ and propagation velocity $v_{medium}$.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Smart Healthcare:** A bedside IoTivity-enabled pulse-oximeter can *push* (Observe) readings to a nurse's tablet, triggering an alert if SpO2 < 90%.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** A factory floor with multi-vendor PLCs can be unified under one OCF namespace, eliminating the SCADA "tower of Babel."
- **Smart Cities:** Streetlights, environmental sensors, and traffic cameras communicate peer-to-peer with zero cloud dependency, reducing the **Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR)** during outages.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: End-to-End Message Flow Through the IoTivity Stack

> Let us trace how a `GET /oic/light/1` request from a smartphone reaches a smart-bulb resource.

**Step 1 — Application Invokes the API**

The application calls the IoTivity C++/C/Java binding:

```text
client.get("/oic/light/1", onResponse);
```

Here, the `client` object is constructed in the Application layer.

**Step 2 — Resource & Framework Layer (URI Parsing)**

The Framework layer parses the URI path and identifies the target device-type (`light`) and instance ID (`1`). It attaches a **Request ID** $R_{id} \in [0, 65535]$ for transaction matching.

Let the matched resource be $R_{target}$. The framework builds the logical request:

$$
\mathcal{Q} = \{ \text{METHOD} = GET,\ \text{URI} = R_{target},\ R_{id} \}
$$

**Step 3 — Common Layer (Request/Response Tracking)**

The Common layer wraps $\mathcal{Q}$ into a CoAP message envelope:

$$
M_{coap} = \begin{Bmatrix} \text{Ver=1, Type=NON, Token}=T, \\ \text{Code}=0.01, \ \text{Options}=\{Uri-Path:[oic,light,1]\} \end{Bmatrix}
$$

The **Non-Confirmable (NON)** type is chosen for fire-and-forget queries to reduce retransmission overhead, satisfying RFC 7252 §4.1.

**Step 4 — Security Plane (DTLS Handshake, if not already established)**

If a secure session does not exist, the Security Manager initiates a DTLS handshake. The total handshake latency is:

$$
H_{DTLS} = 1.5 \times RTT_{net} + T_{crypto}
$$

$$
T_{crypto} = k \times N_{ops}
$$

where $N_{ops}$ is the number of asymmetric key operations and $k$ is the per-operation cost (e.g., ECC-256: $k \approx 4.2$ ms on ARM Cortex-M4).

**Step 5 — Transport Layer (CoAP over UDP)**

The CoAP packet is encapsulated into a UDP datagram:

$$
\text{UDP Header} = \{ \text{Source Port} = P_{src},\ \text{Dest Port} = 5684 \} \cup \text{Payload}(M_{coap})
$$

**Step 6 — Network Adaptation Layer**

The UDP datagram is handed to the Wi-Fi or 6LoWPAN adapter. If running on IEEE 802.15.4 with maximum frame size $S_{frame} = 127$ bytes, the payload is fragmented using **6LoWPAN fragmentation headers**.

Number of fragments:

$$
N_{frag} = \left\lceil \frac{S_{udp} + S_{coap} + S_{app}}{S_{frame} - S_{6LoWPAN}} \right\rceil
$$

For a typical 60-byte application payload:

$$
N_{frag} = \left\lceil \frac{8 + 4 + 60}{127 - 25} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{72}{102} \right\rceil = 1
$$

Thus, a single 802.15.4 frame is sufficient.

**Step 7 — Physical Layer Transmission**

The bit-stream is modulated and transmitted. Propagation delay:

$$
T_{prop} = \frac{d}{v_{medium}} = \frac{d}{3 \times 10^{8}} \ \text{seconds}
$$

For an indoor range $d = 10$ m:

$$
T_{prop} = \frac{10}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 33.3 \ \text{ns} \approx 0 \ \text{(negligible)}
$$

**Step 8 — Reverse Path (Response with Payload)**

The bulb returns `2.05 Content` with payload in CBOR:

$$
\text{Payload} = \{ \text{rt}: \text{["oic.r.light"]},\ \text{if}: \text{["oic.if.a"]},\ \text{state}: \text{true},\ \text{dim}: 75 \}
$$

**Step 9 — Total Round-Trip Time (RTT)**

$$
RTT_{total} = H_{DTLS} + 2 \times T_{prop} + T_{proc\_app} + T_{proc\_bulb} + T_{tx}
$$

Assuming a pre-established DTLS session ($H_{DTLS} = 0$), $T_{proc} = 5$ ms each side, and a 250 kbps O-QPSK PHY ($T_{tx} \approx 2$ ms):

$$
RTT_{total} = 0 + 2(0) + 5 + 5 + 2 = 12 \ \text{ms}
$$

This satisfies the **medical-grade real-time constraint** (< 100 ms) for most IoMT applications.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Access Control Decision Using the ACL Matrix

A resource access decision follows:

$$
D_{access} = \mathbb{1} \big[ (S \in ACL) \land (R \in ACL[S].\text{resources}) \land (P_{req} \in ACL[S].\text{permissions}) \big]
$$

where:
- $S$ = requesting subject (device ID)
- $R$ = target resource URI
- $P_{req}$ = requested permission (READ, WRITE, DELETE)
- $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ = 1 if all conditions hold, else 0

If $D_{access} = 1$, the Security Manager releases the decrypted payload to the upper layer; otherwise, it returns `4.03 Forbidden`.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Layered Functionality vs. OSI Reference Model

| OSI Layer | Equivalent IoTivity Layer | Protocol/Function | KTU Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 (Application) | Application | User logic, OCF APIs | *"Doctor's App"* |
| 7 (API) | Resource & Framework | Resource Model, CRUD | *"The Dictionary"* |
| 6 (Presentation) | Common | Observe, Group, Presence | *"The Manager"* |
| 5 (Session) | Common / Security | DTLS session mgmt | *"The Bouncer"* |
| 4 (Transport) | Transport | CoAP/UDP (5683, 5684) | *"The Postman"* |
| 3 (Network) | Network & Adaptation | IPv6, RPL, 6LoWPAN | *"The GPS"* |
| 2 (Data Link) | Adaptation | 802.15.4 / Wi-Fi / BLE | *"The Road"* |
| 1 (Physical) | Physical | RF / Optical | *"The Pavement"* |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture of the IoTivity Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VS1["Vertical Plane - Security Manager"]
        secA["DTLS 1.2 Handshake"]
        secB["X.509 / PSK Authentication"]
        secC["ACL Enforcement"]
    end

    subgraph VS2["Vertical Plane - Device Management"]
        dmA["Provisioning"]
        dmB["Firmware Update FOTA"]
        dmC["Factory Reset"]
    end

    L7["L7 Application Layer - User Apps and Dashboards"]
    L6["L6 Resource and Framework - Resource Model and URI Routing"]
    L5["L5 Common Layer - Observe and Group and Presence"]
    L4["L4 Transport Layer - CoAP over UDP - Port 5683 and 5684"]
    L3["L3 Network Adaptation - IPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL"]
    L2["L2 Data Link - IEEE 802.15.4, Wi-Fi, BLE"]
    L1["L1 Physical - RF and Optical Medium"]

    L7 --> L6 --> L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1
    secA --- L4
    secB --- L5
    secC --- L6
    dmA --- L6
    dmB --- L3
    dmC --- L7
```

### 4.2 Resource Discovery & Request-Response Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Smartphone App
    participant FW1 as Client Framework
    participant CL1 as Common Layer
    participant TR1 as Transport CoAP and UDP
    participant TR2 as Transport CoAP and UDP
    participant CL2 as Common Layer
    participant FW2 as Server Framework
    participant Bulb as Smart Bulb Resource

    App->>FW1: GET /oic/light/1
    FW1->>CL1: Attach Request-ID and Token
    CL1->>TR1: Build CoAP NON message
    TR1->>TR2: UDP packet to port 5684
    TR2->>CL2: Decapsulate and parse
    CL2->>FW2: Route by URI path
    FW2->>Bulb: Lookup collection light
    Bulb-->>FW2: state equals true and dim equals 75
    FW2-->>CL2: Build 2.05 Content
    CL2-->>TR2: CBOR encode
    TR2-->>TR1: UDP response
    TR1-->>CL1: Deliver to handler
    CL1-->>FW1: Match Request-ID
    FW1-->>App: Callback with payload
```

### 4.3 CoAP Message Envelope Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hdr["Header - 4 bytes - Ver, Type, TKL, Code, MsgID"]
    tok["Token - 0 to 8 bytes"]
    opt["Options - Uri-Path, Accept, Content-Format"]
    plt["Payload - 0 to 1024 bytes per block"]
    hdr --> tok --> opt --> plt
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each block represents a distinct functional layer. The **Security Manager** plane *touches* L4–L6 because DTLS lives in the transport/session boundary, while ACLs live in the resource layer. The **Device Management** plane is drawn as a *control* plane that can call APIs at any horizontal layer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Question Bank]**
*Define the term "Resource" in the context of IoTivity. How is a resource identified within the framework?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In IoTivity, a **Resource** is an abstraction of any physical or virtual entity (sensor reading, actuator state, configuration parameter) that can be manipulated by an application. Each resource is uniquely identified by a **Uniform Resource Identifier (URI)** following the OCF naming convention, e.g., `/oic/light/1`. A resource exposes three core elements: **Properties (attributes such as `state`, `dim`)**, **Methods (CoAP CRUD verbs: GET/POST/PUT/DELETE)**, and **Interfaces (semantic markers like `oic.if.a` for actuator)**. Resources can be grouped into **Collection Resources** to enable bulk operations. *(Marks: Definition 1, URI 1, Components 1.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Repeated Pattern]**
*State the well-known UDP port numbers used by IoTivity for (i) unsecured and (ii) DTLS-secured CoAP communication.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (i) Unsecured CoAP: **Port 5683** *(1 mark)*
- (ii) DTLS-secured CoAP (CoAPS): **Port 5684** *(1 mark)*
- These ports are registered with IANA and are reserved exclusively for IoTivity/OCF communication. The secure port uses a DTLS 1.2 handshake for mutual authentication, X.509v3 certificate validation, and AES-CCM payload encryption. *(1 mark for the security explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE PROVIDED — Answer ANY ONE of Question A or Question B.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Predicted Pattern]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the layered architecture of the IoTivity stack. Describe the role of the two vertical planes. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The IoTivity stack is organized into **six horizontal layers** and **two vertical planes** (Security and Device Management), analogous to a hybrid OSI/IoT model.

**Horizontal Layers (7 marks full):**

| # | Layer | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Application Layer** | Houses end-user logic — smartphone apps, web dashboards, cloud gateways. Calls IoTivity APIs to discover and manipulate resources. |
| 2 | **Resource & Framework Layer** | Implements the **OCF Resource Model**; maps URIs to device-side handlers; supports CRUD operations; provides inter-process communication. |
| 3 | **Common Layer** | Provides **Observation** (server-push), **Group Communication**, **Presence Notification**, and **Resource Directory** caching. Decouples the request logic from transport. |
| 4 | **Transport Layer** | Encodes messages in **CoAP (RFC 7252)** over **UDP** (or TCP per RFC 8323). Uses default port **5683/5684**. |
| 5 | **Network & Adaptation Layer** | Manages IPv6 addressing, 6LoWPAN header compression, and RPL routing. Provides plug-in adapters (Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, Thread). |
| 6 | **Physical Layer** | The actual radio — IEEE 802.15.4 O-QPSK at 2.4 GHz, 802.11ac, Bluetooth LE, NFC, etc. |

**Vertical Planes:**

- **Security Manager Plane:** Stretches across L4–L6. Performs the **DTLS 1.2 handshake**, **X.509 certificate validation** or **Pre-Shared Key (PSK)** provisioning, and enforces **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** on every resource access.

- **Device Management Plane:** Stretches across L1–L7. Handles **Device Provisioning (Easy Setup)**, **Factory Reset**, **Firmware-Over-The-Air (FOTA)** updates, and **Diagnostic Reporting**.

**Conclusion:** This layered approach ensures **interoperability**, **security-by-design**, and **transport-agnostic** operation, making IoTivity ideal for heterogeneous biomedical and industrial deployments. *(Diagram would carry 2 marks; functional descriptions 5 marks.)*

---

**(b) A 6LoWPAN-based IoTivity node sends a 60-byte application payload to a coordinator over IEEE 802.15.4 (max frame = 127 bytes, 6LoWPAN overhead = 25 bytes, UDP header = 8 bytes, CoAP header = 4 bytes). Calculate (i) the number of fragments required, and (ii) the total airtime if the PHY rate is 250 kbps. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Number of Fragments (3 marks):**

Effective payload per frame:

$$
S_{eff} = S_{frame} - S_{6LoWPAN} = 127 - 25 = 102 \ \text{bytes}
$$

Total data to transmit:

$$
S_{data} = S_{udp} + S_{coap} + S_{app} = 8 + 4 + 60 = 72 \ \text{bytes}
$$

Number of fragments:

$$
N_{frag} = \left\lceil \frac{S_{data}}{S_{eff}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{72}{102} \right\rceil = 1
$$

**⇒ A single 802.15.4 frame is sufficient.** *(3 marks)*

**(ii) Total Airtime (4 marks):**

Since $N_{frag} = 1$, the entire transmission occupies one frame of $S_{frame} = 127$ bytes = $127 \times 8 = 1016$ bits.

PHY airtime:

$$
T_{air} = \frac{S_{bits}}{R_{PHY}} = \frac{1016}{250 \times 10^{3}} = 4.064 \ \text{ms}
$$

However, IEEE 802.15.4 frames carry a **preamble + SFD** of 5 bytes (40 bits) consumed at the lower rate, plus an **ACK wait** of $T_{ACK} = 12$ symbols = $192 \ \mu s$ (not always accounted). For full KTU accuracy:

$$
T_{total} = \frac{(127 + 5) \times 8}{250 \times 10^{3}} + 192 \ \mu s \approx 4.22 \ \text{ms}
$$

**⇒ Total airtime ≈ 4.22 ms per successful transmission.** *(4 marks for correct formula and arithmetic.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Explain in detail the discovery, provisioning, and message-exchange workflow in IoTivity. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Phase 1 — Discovery (2 marks):**
A new device announces its presence by multicasting a CoAP **GET** request to the well-known URI `/oic/res` (Resource Directory) on the local subnet over **port 5683**. Other clients respond by returning their resource lists in CBOR format. The requesting client builds a local cache of available resources and their URIs.

**Phase 2 — Provisioning (2 marks):**
A new, unowned device enters the network in an *unprovisioned* state with a pre-provisioned **manufacturer certificate**. The owner device uses OCF's **Easy Setup** flow over DTLS to inject ownership credentials and ACLs. Two methods are supported:
- **Just-Works Pairing:** Simplest, used when the device has no input/output (e.g., a lightbulb).
- **Random PIN/Passphrase:** Used for devices with limited user interfaces.

**Phase 3 — Message Exchange (3 marks):**
The client sends a `GET /oic/light/1` request. The Resource & Framework layer parses the URI; the Common layer wraps it in a CoAP packet with a unique Token. The Transport layer secures it via DTLS and dispatches over UDP. The server returns `2.05 Content` with a CBOR payload such as `{state:true, dim:75}`. Optionally, the client may convert to `Observe` mode for server-pushed updates, eliminating polling overhead — critical for biomedical sensors (e.g., continuous ECG monitoring).

**Engineering Significance:** This three-phase workflow ensures **zero-touch onboarding**, **end-to-end security**, and **resource-level granularity**, satisfying the stringent requirements of IoMT (Internet of Medical Things) deployments.

---

**(b) Differentiate CoAP from HTTP. Why is CoAP preferred for IoTivity over HTTP? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) | CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol) |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP (connection-oriented) | UDP (connectionless, optional TCP) |
| **Header Size** | Variable, 200–2000 bytes | **4 bytes** (fixed base) |
| **Methods** | GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS | GET, POST, PUT, DELETE (subset) |
| **Response Codes** | Text-based ("200 OK", "404 Not Found") | Numeric binary (2.05, 4.04) — **3-digit** |
| **Caching** | Extensive | Limited (max-age option) |
| **Multicast Support** | No | **Yes** — essential for resource discovery |
| **Observability** | Long polling, WebSockets | **Native Observe (RFC 7641)** |
| **Overhead per Transaction** | 100s of bytes | **Tens of bytes** |
| **Reliability** | Built into TCP | Built into CoAP via Confirmable (CON) messages with retransmission |
| **Security** | TLS over TCP | DTLS over UDP |
| **Default Port** | 80 / 443 | **5683 / 5684** |

**Why CoAP is preferred for IoTivity (3 marks):**

1. **Lightweight footprint** — fits within the 1024-byte 802.15.4 frame after 6LoWPAN compression.
2. **Asynchronous by design** — Observe mechanism drastically reduces power consumption for battery-operated medical implants.
3. **Native UDP support** — avoids TCP's connection-state memory overhead, vital for Class-1 constrained devices (< 10 KB RAM).
4. **Built-in multicast** — simplifies resource discovery without needing a centralized broker.
5. **Restful paradigm preserved** — developers familiar with REST/HTTP find the learning curve minimal.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Port confusion:** Many students write 5683 for *both* CoAP and CoAPS. Remember: *Unsecured = 5683*, **Secured = 5684**. Loss of 1 mark.
> 2. **URI structure:** Writing `/oic/light` instead of `/oic/light/1` — missing the instance ID. Loss of 1 mark.
> 3. **Mistaking 6LoWPAN for Wi-Fi:** 6LoWPAN is the **adaptation layer** that compresses IPv6 over IEEE 802.15.4; it is *not* a transport or application protocol.
> 4. **Skipping the vertical planes:** A purely horizontal-layer answer for 7-mark question (a) loses up to 2 marks. Always mention Security & Device Management planes explicitly.
> 5. **Unit errors in airtime calculations:** Forgetting to convert **bytes → bits (×8)** when computing PHY airtime. Always include the unit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — IoTivity Stack Architecture**

- [ ] **IoTivity** = open-source OCF framework for D2D IoT interoperability.
- [ ] **Resource Model** = URI-addressed, property-attribute-method-interface-based abstraction.
- [ ] **6 horizontal layers** (App, Resource, Common, Transport, Network, Physical) + **2 vertical planes** (Security, Device Management).
- [ ] **Default ports:** CoAP = **5683**, CoAPS = **5684** (memorize cold).
- [ ] **CoAP** runs over **UDP** by default; uses **DTLS 1.2** for security.
- [ ] **Resource Directory** URI = `/oic/res` (multicast-based discovery).
- [ ] **Observe (RFC 7641)** is the killer feature for biomedical sensor streaming.
- [ ] **Content Formats:** JSON (`10000`) and CBOR (`10010`) — CBOR is the default for constrained devices.
- [ ] **ACL enforcement** happens at the Resource Framework layer, not the Transport layer.
- [ ] **Vertical Security Plane** spans L4–L6; **Vertical DM Plane** spans L1–L7.
- [ ] **Frame-size math** — always subtract 6LoWPAN overhead from 127 bytes before counting fragments.
- [ ] **Real-world biomedical use case:** bedside monitors, infusion pumps, ECG patches — all interoperating over CoAP.
- [ ] **Comparison hot-spot:** CoAP vs HTTP — students must know **all 5 differences** (transport, header, response codes, multicast, observability).
- [ ] **Golden rule:** No vendor lock-in — IoTivity's *raison d'être* is **interoperability**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resource Model and Abstraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Resource Model and Abstraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, a **Resource Model** is a formal, structured representation that defines how heterogeneous physical and virtual entities (sensors, actuators, services, data streams, and computational assets) are exposed, addressed, and manipulated over a network. **Resource Abstraction** is the engineering process of concealing the underlying hardware-specific implementation details, communication protocols, and data formats of a device behind a uniform, technology-independent interface — typically conforming to **REST (Representational State Transfer)** architectural principles.

According to the **W3C Web of Things (WoT) Architecture** and the **IETF CoRE Working Group** specifications (RFC 6690, RFC 7252), every "thing" in an IoT deployment is decomposed into one or more **resources** that can be identified by a **Uniform Resource Identifier (URI)**, represented using a standardized data format (JSON, CBOR, XML), and operated upon through a constrained set of well-defined methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The resource model is the *cornerstone of interoperability* in IoT. Without a unified resource abstraction layer, billions of devices from different manufacturers using different protocols (Zigbee, BLE, Modbus, LoRa) would be unable to exchange meaningful data with applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Remote Control" Mental Model

Imagine a household with a **Smart TV** (Samsung, Tizen OS), a **Smart AC** (LG, WebOS), and a **Smart Speaker** (Amazon, Linux). Internally, each device runs completely different firmware, speaks different low-level protocols, and stores data in proprietary structures.

Now, introduce a **single Universal Remote**:

- The remote **does not understand** the internal circuitry of any device.
- It interacts with each device through a **uniform button set** (Power, Volume, Channel).
- Each device is **addressed uniquely** (TV = 1, AC = 2, Speaker = 3).
- The remote receives a **standardized response** ("OK" / "Error") regardless of vendor.

This Universal Remote is the **Resource Abstraction Layer**:

| Universal Remote Concept | IoT Resource Model Equivalent |
|:---|:---|
| Individual device | Physical/Virtual "Thing" |
| Button press (Power, Vol+) | HTTP Method (GET, PUT, POST) |
| Device ID (1, 2, 3) | URI (`/sensors/temp1`, `/actuators/led2`) |
| Standardized response | JSON/CBOR Resource Representation |
| Universal language | REST / CoAP Protocol |

> [!NOTE]
> The resource model transforms IoT devices from *islands of isolated data* into *addressable web resources* — a foundational shift that allows any device to become part of the World Wide Web.

## 1.3 Core Terminology Defined

- **Resource**: A uniquely addressable entity that holds state and can be interacted with (e.g., a temperature sensor reading, an LED's on/off state).
- **Representation**: A byte-stream encoding of the resource's current state (e.g., `{"value": 25.4, "unit": "C"}`).
- **Identifier (URI)**: A unique string that distinguishes one resource from another in the global namespace.
- **Interface**: The set of operations (verbs) that can be performed on the resource.
- **Metadata**: Descriptive information about the resource (type, unit, range, observability).
- **CoRE**: **Constrained RESTful Environments** — a working group that standardized resource models for low-power IoT devices.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standardized values govern resource models in constrained IoT environments:

- **Maximum URI length in CoAP**: **255 bytes** (per RFC 7252)
- **Default CoAP port**: **5683** (UDP) / **5684** (DTLS-secured)
- **Maximum UDP payload in CoAP**: **1152 bytes** to avoid IP fragmentation
- **Default CoRE Link Format content type**: `application/link-format`
- **Resource Type identifier attribute**: **`rt`** (e.g., `rt="core.sensor.temperature"`)
- **Interface Type identifier attribute**: **`if`** (e.g., `if="core.s"` for sensor, `if="core.a"` for actuator)
- **Content-Format identifier for JSON over CoAP**: **50**
- **Content-Format identifier for CBOR over CoAP**: **60**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of a Resource Model

A complete IoT resource model is built on three interlocking pillars, each solving a different engineering problem:

### 2.1.1 Pillar 1 — Resource Identification (The "Where")

Every resource requires a globally unique address. The IoT ecosystem primarily uses two URI schemes:

- **HTTP URIs** for unconstrained, IP-connected devices:
  `http://192.168.1.50:8080/sensors/temperature/room1`
- **CoAP URIs** for constrained nodes (RFC 7252):
  `coap://[2001:db8::1]:5683/sensors/temp`

**Hierarchical URI Structure** (4 logical levels):

$$
URI = \underbrace{coap://}_{\text{Scheme}} \underbrace{host:port}_{\text{Authority}} \underbrace{/building/floor1/room1}_{\text{Path (Location)}} \underbrace{/sensors/temp}_{\text{Resource Path}}
$$

> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** URI design directly impacts **scalability, security (path-based access control), and routing efficiency** in IoT gateways.

### 2.1.2 Pillar 2 — Resource Representation (The "What")

A representation is the serialization format describing a resource's current state. For constrained devices, the two dominant formats are:

- **JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)** — human-readable, verbose (high overhead).
- **CBOR (Concise Binary Object Representation, RFC 7049)** — compact binary, ~60% smaller than JSON. **CBOR is the de-facto standard for IoT** because it reduces radio-on time, saving energy.

A minimal **temperature sensor** resource representation in JSON:

```json
{
  "n": "Living Room Temp",
  "value": 24.7,
  "unit": "C",
  "timestamp": 1718234567,
  "range": [-20, 60]
}
```

The same data in **CBOR** consumes roughly **40 bytes versus 95 bytes in JSON** — a critical difference for battery-operated nodes transmitting every 10 seconds.

### 2.1.3 Pillar 3 — Resource Interface (The "How")

The interface defines the **verbs** (operations) that may be applied. Two models coexist in IoT:

**Model A — REST/CRUD Mapping:**

| HTTP/CoAP Method | CRUD Operation | Purpose |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `GET` | Read | Retrieve current resource state |
| `POST` | Create | Append data to a collection resource |
| `PUT` | Update | Replace current state |
| `DELETE` | Delete | Remove the resource |

**Model B — Sensor/Actor Specific (CoRE Sensor Profile):**

| CoRE Method | Purpose |
|:---|:---|
| `GET` | Read sensor value |
| `PUT` | Set actuator value (e.g., `PUT /led1 {"value":1}`) |
| `POST` | Trigger an action (e.g., `POST /buzzer {"duration":500}`) |
| `OBSERVE` | Subscribe to asynchronous updates |

## 2.2 The CoRE Link Format — The "Discovery Layer"

A resource cannot be useful if clients cannot discover it. The **CoRE Link Format (RFC 6690)** solves this by providing a standard way for devices to **advertise their resources** as hyperlinks.

A sample CoRE resource directory entry:

```
</sensors/temp>;rt="core.sensor.temperature";if="core.s";obs,
</sensors/humid>;rt="core.sensor.humidity";if="core.s",
</actuators/led>;rt="core.actuator.led";if="core.a"
```

**Anatomy of a CoRE Link:**
- `</sensors/temp>` → **URI** of the resource
- `rt="..."` → **Resource Type** (semantic meaning)
- `if="..."` → **Interface Type** (how to interact)
- `obs` → flag indicating the resource supports the **OBSERVE** pattern (server-push updates)

## 2.3 The Three-Layer Abstraction Hierarchy

Modern IoT frameworks (W3C WoT, FIWARE, IoTivity) all converge on a **three-layer abstraction pyramid** that hides complexity progressively:

$$
\underbrace{\text{Layer 3: Application Logic}}_{\text{Business rules, ML, dashboards}} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Layer 2: Resource Abstraction Layer (RAL)}}_{\text{Uniform RESTful interface, TD}} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Layer 1: Device / Protocol Layer}}_{\text{Zigbee, BLE, LoRa, Modbus hardware}}
$$

The **Resource Abstraction Layer (RAL)** is the critical middleware that:
1. Translates vendor-specific protocols (e.g., Zigbee ZCL clusters) into RESTful HTTP/CoAP endpoints.
2. Aggregates multiple low-level attributes (e.g., 12 raw sensor bytes) into a single high-level semantic resource.
3. Manages authentication, rate-limiting, and event-bus subscription.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **rapid-revision** of every formula, constant, and pattern you must memorize for the KTU ESE.

| # | Concept | Symbol / Expression | Unit / Value | Engineering Utility |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Resource Model | $R = \{URI, REPR, IF, META\}$ | — | Formal 4-tuple definition |
| 2 | URI Length (CoAP) | $L_{URI}$ | **bytes** (max 255) | Avoids IP fragmentation |
| 3 | CoAP Default Port | $P_{CoAP}$ | **5683** / 5684 (DTLS) | Configuration of gateways |
| 4 | CoAP Payload Limit | $M_{payload}$ | **1152 bytes** | Prevents IP-layer fragmentation |
| 5 | JSON-to-CBOR Size Ratio | $\rho = \frac{S_{CBOR}}{S_{JSON}}$ | **≈ 0.4** | Energy savings in low-power nodes |
| 6 | REST Constraint Count | Roy Fielding's REST constraints | **6 constraints** | Architectural correctness check |
| 7 | CoRE Link Params | `rt`, `if`, `obs`, `ct`, `sz` | 5 standardized params | Resource discovery metadata |
| 8 | Observation Latency | $T_{obs} = T_{transmit} + T_{process}$ | seconds | Real-time QoS planning |
| 9 | Default CoRE Port (RD) | Core Resource Directory port | **5683** | Discovery server config |
| 10 | W3C WoT TD Version | Current spec | **1.1** (W3C Rec) | Latest Thing Description format |

**Important — No mathematical "pipes":** When writing absolute value or set cardinality, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid S \mid$ (LaTeX) — never the bare `|` character inside markdown tables, as it breaks table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Hospitals**: Each infusion pump, ECG monitor, and bedside terminal exposes its data as a REST resource, allowing a single dashboard to query hundreds of devices from different vendors.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: A CNC machine's spindle speed, vibration, and temperature are abstracted into 3 resources; an MES (Manufacturing Execution System) polls them uniformly.
- **Smart Agriculture**: Soil moisture, pH, and weather data from disparate LoRa nodes are exposed via a CoAP-to-HTTP proxy that conforms to a unified resource model.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Derivation: Defining a Resource as a 4-Tuple

A resource in IoT is not just "a thing on the internet" — it is a **mathematical abstraction** that can be rigorously defined. We begin with the most elementary definition and progressively add constraints.

### Step 1 — Identify the Resource Universe

Let $\mathcal{U}$ denote the **universe of all identifiable entities** in an IoT system, comprising physical sensors ($\mathcal{P}$), actuators ($\mathcal{A}$), services ($\mathcal{S}$), and collections thereof:

$$
\mathcal{U} = \mathcal{P} \cup \mathcal{A} \cup \mathcal{S}, \quad \mathcal{P} \cap \mathcal{A} \cap \mathcal{S} = \emptyset
$$

**Explanation:** A temperature sensor is a member of $\mathcal{P}$; a smart bulb is in $\mathcal{A}$; a weather forecasting web service is in $\mathcal{S}$. A given entity is uniquely classified in exactly one set.

### Step 2 — Define the Identifier Mapping

Each resource must have a unique, persistent identifier. We define a **bijection** (one-to-one mapping) from resources to URI strings:

$$
f: \mathcal{U} \rightarrow \Sigma^{*}
$$

where $\Sigma = \{a..z, A..Z, 0..9, /, :, -\}$ is the URI alphabet and $\Sigma^{*}$ is the Kleene closure (all finite URI strings).

$$
\forall r_1, r_2 \in \mathcal{U}, \; f(r_1) = f(r_2) \implies r_1 = r_2
$$

**Explanation:** No two distinct resources can share the same URI. This is the **URI Uniqueness Property**, essential for unambiguous addressing.

### Step 3 — Define the Representation Function

A resource's state is encoded into a representation. We define a **state-snapshot function** $s$ that captures the current state at time $t$:

$$
s: \mathcal{U} \times \mathbb{T} \rightarrow \mathcal{V}
$$

where $\mathbb{T}$ is the time domain and $\mathcal{V}$ is the value space. For a temperature sensor, $\mathcal{V} \subseteq \mathbb{R} \times \{\text{unit} \times \text{timestamp}\}$.

The representation is then a serialization of $s(r, t)$:

$$
REPR(r, t) = \text{encode}(s(r, t), \text{format})
$$

where $\text{format} \in \{\text{JSON}, \text{CBOR}, \text{XML}, \text{EXI}\}$.

**Explanation:** The same physical resource may have *different* representations at different times, and the *format* is selectable by the client via HTTP `Accept` headers.

### Step 4 — Define the Interface as an Operation Set

The interface is a finite set of allowed operations. For a CoAP/REST resource:

$$
IF(r) = \{ \text{GET}, \text{PUT}, \text{POST}, \text{DELETE}, \text{OBSERVE} \}_{r}
$$

For a **read-only sensor**, $IF(r) = \{\text{GET}, \text{OBSERVE}\}$.

For an **actuator**, $IF(r) \supseteq \{\text{GET}, \text{PUT}\}$.

**Explanation:** Constraining the interface enforces semantic correctness — a temperature sensor *should not* support `PUT` because its value is generated by the physical world, not by clients.

### Step 5 — Assemble the 4-Tuple

Combining Steps 1–4, we obtain the **canonical resource model**:

$$
\boxed{\;R = \langle \text{URI}(r),\; REPR(r,t),\; IF(r),\; META(r) \rangle\;}
$$

where $META(r)$ is the metadata set including resource type, interface type, unit, range, and observability.

## 3.2 Derivation: How a CoAP Client Discovers a Resource

**Scenario:** A smart-home gateway boots up and wants to find all temperature sensors in a CoAP network that supports the resource directory (RD) at `coap://[2001:db8::ff]/rd`.

**Step-by-Step Flow:**

### Step 1 — Client sends a `GET` to the RD's discovery endpoint:

$$
\text{GET} \; coap://[2001:db8::ff]/rd?rt=core.sensor.temperature
$$

The query parameter `rt=core.sensor.temperature` filters the result to only temperature sensors.

### Step 2 — RD searches its internal registration database

Each IoT node previously registered with the RD using `POST /rd` and a CoRE Link-format payload. The RD executes a set-filter:

$$
RD_{filtered} = \{ r \in RD_{all} \mid rt(r) = \text{"core.sensor.temperature"} \}
$$

### Step 3 — RD returns CoRE Link-Format response:

$$
\text{Code: 2.05 Content} \quad \text{Content-Format: 40 (application/link-format)}
$$

Payload:

```
</sensors/temp/livingroom>;rt="core.sensor.temperature";if="core.s",
/sensors/temp/bedroom>;rt="core.sensor.temperature";if="core.s"
```

### Step 4 — Client parses the response and constructs URI list:

$$
URI_{list} = \{ \text{"/sensors/temp/livingroom"}, \; \text{"/sensors/temp/bedroom"} \}
$$

### Step 5 — Client issues a `GET` on each URI to read the current value:

For living-room sensor:

$$
\text{GET} \; coap://[2001:db8::1]/sensors/temp/livingroom
$$

Response:

$$
\text{Code: 2.05 Content, Payload: } \{\text{"v": 24.7, "u": "C"}\}
$$

The client has now completed the full **discover → resolve → read** cycle, entirely driven by the resource model abstraction.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Resource Models in IoT

The following matrix compares the three dominant resource models encountered in KTU exams and industry:

| Dimension | **Plain CoAP Resource** | **W3C WoT Thing Description (TD)** | **OPC UA Information Model** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Base Standard** | RFC 7252 | W3C Recommendation 1.1 | IEC 62541 |
| **Data Format** | CBOR / Plain text | JSON-LD 1.1 | Binary / XML |
| **Semantic Annotations** | Limited (`rt`, `if`) | Full (JSON-LD `@context`, `saref:`, `iotschema:`) | Strong (typed nodes) |
| **Discovery** | CoRE Link-Format + RD | Thing Directory (TD catalog) | LDS (Local Discovery Server) |
| **Security** | DTLS, OSCORE | W3C WoT Security Vocabulary | X.509, application-layer tokens |
| **Computational Cost** | Very low (8-bit MCUs) | Medium (requires JSON parser) | High (gateways / cloud) |
| **Best Suited For** | Constrained sensors, edge nodes | Smart home, industrial WoT | Industry 4.0, SCADA |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | High | Medium | Low |

## 3.4 Derivation: Size Optimization — Why CBOR over JSON for IoT?

A typical temperature reading as JSON:

```json
{"v":24.7,"u":"C","t":1718234567}
```

Character count: **36 characters**

In CBOR, the same data is encoded as a **map of 3 entries**:

- Map header: 1 byte (`A3` = map of 3)
- Key "v" (1 char): 2 bytes (1 for length, 1 for char)
- Value 24.7: 3 bytes (IEEE 754 single-precision float)
- Key "u": 2 bytes
- Value "C": 2 bytes
- Key "t": 2 bytes
- Value 1718234567: 5 bytes (uint32)

Total: **17 bytes**

$$
\text{Savings} = 1 - \frac{S_{CBOR}}{S_{JSON}} = 1 - \frac{17}{36} \approx 0.53
$$

**Conclusion:** CBOR achieves **~53% bandwidth savings**, which directly translates to **extended battery life** for energy-harvesting IoT nodes — a frequently asked concept in KTU viva voce.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Three-Layer Resource Abstraction Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a heterogeneous IoT deployment is unified by a Resource Abstraction Layer (RAL) into a uniform RESTful interface for applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph appLayer["LAYER 3 APPLICATION LAYER"]
        app1["Dashboard Web App"]
        app2["ML Analytics Engine"]
        app3["Mobile Health App"]
    end

    subgraph ralLayer["LAYER 2 RESOURCE ABSTRACTION LAYER RAL"]
        ral1["RESTful API Gateway HTTP CoAP"]
        ral2["CoRE Link Format Registry"]
        ral3["W3C WoT Thing Directory"]
        ral4["Event Bus Observer Pattern"]
    end

    subgraph devLayer["LAYER 1 DEVICE AND PROTOCOL LAYER"]
        dev1["Temp Sensor Zigbee"]
        dev2["ECG Monitor BLE"]
        dev3["Smart Pill Dispenser LoRa"]
        dev4["Infusion Pump Modbus"]
    end

    app1 -->|GET PUT POST| ral1
    app2 -->|Subscribe| ral4
    app3 -->|Observe| ral1
    ral1 --> ral2
    ral1 --> ral3
    ral2 --> ral4
    ral3 --> ral4

    ral1 -->|Protocol Translation| dev1
    ral1 -->|Protocol Translation| dev2
    ral3 -->|Discovery| dev3
    ral4 -->|Async Notification| dev4
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- **Top-down arrows** represent data flowing *from* applications *to* devices.
- **Bottom-up arrows** (where applicable) represent asynchronous events.
- The **RAL** is the *only* layer applications interact with — devices are completely hidden.

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Resource Discovery & Observation Flow

This Mermaid sequence diagram demonstrates the **complete interaction** between a smartphone app, a CoAP gateway, and a constrained temperature sensor using the CoRE resource model.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Mobile App
    participant GW as CoAP Gateway
    participant RD as Resource Directory
    participant S as Temp Sensor

    Note over App,S: Phase 1 Discovery
    App->>RD: GET coap://gateway/rd?rt=core.sensor.temperature
    RD-->>App: 2.05 Content [link-format list of sensors]
    
    Note over App,S: Phase 2 Resolve URI
    App->>GW: GET coap://gateway/sensors/temp/room1
    GW->>S: Forward GET to constrained node
    S-->>GW: 2.05 Content [value=24.7]
    GW-->>App: 2.05 Content [value=24.7]
    
    Note over App,S: Phase 3 Observation Server Push
    App->>GW: GET observe
    GW->>S: Register observation
    loop Every 5 seconds
        S-->>GW: Notification [value=24.8]
        GW-->>App: Push notification
    end
```

**Key insights for KTU exams:**
- **Phase 1** uses the CoRE Resource Directory for **discovery** (cold start).
- **Phase 2** demonstrates **synchronous polling** via `GET`.
- **Phase 3** demonstrates the **OBSERVE** pattern — the server **pushes** updates without the client polling, which is critical for real-time biomedical monitoring (e.g., heart rate alerts).

## 4.3 Flowchart — Decision Logic for Resource Interface Selection

When designing a new IoT resource, the engineer must decide which interface methods to expose. The following flowchart codifies the decision logic:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([New Resource Design]) --> q1{Is the device a sensor or actuator?}
    q1 -- Sensor --> q2{Does it support async push?}
    q1 -- Actuator --> q3{Does it support remote command?}
    q1 -- Hybrid --> q4[Expose GET PUT POST OBSERVE]

    q2 -- Yes --> int1[GET plus OBSERVE]
    q2 -- No --> int2[GET only]

    q3 -- Yes --> int3[GET plus PUT]
    q3 -- No --> int4[GET POST only]

    q4 --> end1([Resource Interface Finalized])
    int1 --> end1
    int2 --> end1
    int3 --> end1
    int4 --> end1
```

> **Engineering takeaway:** A *read-only temperature sensor* should **never** expose a `PUT` method. A *read-write actuator* (e.g., smart bulb) must expose both `GET` and `PUT`. A *hybrid device* (e.g., a smart infusion pump that both reports and accepts flow-rate changes) requires the full CRUD + OBSERVE set.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the term *Resource* in the context of IoT. List any four key attributes that characterize a resource.**

**Model Answer:**

In IoT, a **Resource** is any physical or virtual entity that can be uniquely identified, addressed, and manipulated over a network. Examples include a temperature sensor's current reading, a smart bulb's on/off state, or a weather-data service.

The four key attributes characterizing an IoT resource are:

1. **URI (Uniform Resource Identifier)** — a globally unique address (e.g., `/sensors/temp/room1`).
2. **Representation** — the current state encoded in a standard format (JSON/CBOR/XML).
3. **Interface** — the set of allowed operations (GET, PUT, POST, OBSERVE).
4. **Metadata** — descriptive attributes (resource type, interface type, units, range).

> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners award 0.5 marks for the definition and 0.5 marks for *each* of the four attributes. Memorize the acronym **U-R-I-M** for quick recall.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is Resource Abstraction in IoT? Why is it considered essential for IoT interoperability?**

**Model Answer:**

**Resource Abstraction** is the process of hiding the implementation details (hardware, low-level protocols, data formats) of an IoT device behind a uniform, technology-independent interface — typically a RESTful API over HTTP or CoAP.

**Why it is essential for interoperability:**

1. **Heterogeneity handling:** IoT devices use diverse protocols (Zigbee, BLE, LoRa, Modbus). Abstraction exposes them through one common interface.
2. **Vendor neutrality:** Applications can be written *once* and operate against devices from any manufacturer.
3. **Scalability:** A single gateway can expose thousands of underlying devices as addressable web resources.
4. **Development simplicity:** Application developers interact only with the abstraction; they need not understand embedded firmware.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **CoRE (Constrained RESTful Environments) resource model** in detail. Describe the CoRE Link Format with a suitable example. How does the resource directory enable discovery in constrained networks?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **JSON and CBOR** as resource representation formats for IoT. With a numerical example, show how CBOR achieves bandwidth efficiency. State two real-world scenarios where CBOR is preferred over JSON.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**CoRE Resource Model — Detailed Explanation:**

The **CoRE resource model**, standardized by the IETF CoRE Working Group, is a lightweight RESTful framework designed for constrained IoT devices (≤ 1 MB RAM, 8-bit/32-bit MCUs). It is based on the CoAP protocol (RFC 7252) and the CoRE Link Format (RFC 6690).

**Core Principles:**

1. **Resources are URI-addressable** within a constrained node.
2. Each resource carries **typed metadata** (`rt`, `if`, `ct`, `sz`, `obs`).
3. **Discovery** is performed via CoRE Link Format, which serializes a list of resources as hyperlinks with attributes.
4. A **Resource Directory (RD)** acts as a centralized index, allowing clients to discover resources across the network.

**CoRE Link Format — Example:**

A node with two resources (a temperature sensor and a controllable LED) advertises:

```
</sensors/temp>;rt="core.sensor.temperature";if="core.s",
</sensors/humid>;rt="core.sensor.humidity";if="core.s",
</actuators/led1>;rt="core.actuator.led";if="core.a"
```

| Component | Meaning |
|:---|:---|
| `</sensors/temp>` | URI path to the resource |
| `rt="core.sensor.temperature"` | Semantic resource type |
| `if="core.s"` | Interface type — sensor |
| `;obs` (not shown) | Optional flag for OBSERVE support |

**Resource Directory (RD) Operation:**

1. Each constrained node **registers** itself with the RD using `POST /rd` (with a CoRE Link-format body listing its resources).
2. Clients query the RD with `GET /rd?rt=core.sensor.temperature` to find all temperature sensors.
3. The RD replies with a filtered link-format list of matching URIs.
4. The client then directly addresses the chosen node's resource.

**Engineering Significance:**

- Eliminates manual device configuration in large deployments (e.g., 500 sensors in a hospital).
- Enables **pluggable discovery** without proprietary software.
- Standardizes resource typing so that analytics apps can reason about *what kind* of data they receive.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**JSON vs CBOR — Comparative Analysis:**

| Dimension | JSON | CBOR (RFC 7049) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Encoding | Text (UTF-8) | Binary |
| Human-Readable | Yes | No (requires parser) |
| Typical Size | Baseline | **~40–60% smaller** |
| Parsing Speed | Moderate | Fast (binary, deterministic) |
| Schema Support | Optional (JSON Schema) | Optional (CDDL) |
| Energy per TX | High | **Low** |
| Tooling | Universal | Growing (cbor.io, cbor.me) |

**Numerical Example:**

Consider the JSON: `{"v":24.7,"u":"C","t":1718234567}`

- **JSON byte count**: 36 bytes (UTF-8)
- **CBOR encoding**:
  - Map of 3 entries: 1 byte
  - 3 keys (1 char each): 3 × 2 = 6 bytes
  - 24.7 as IEEE 754 float32: 3 bytes
  - "C" as text string: 2 bytes
  - 1718234567 as uint32: 5 bytes
  - **Total: 17 bytes**

$$
\text{Savings} = 1 - \frac{17}{36} \approx 0.528 = 52.8\%
$$

**Scenarios where CBOR is preferred:**

1. **Battery-powered LoRa nodes** transmitting every 10 seconds — every byte saved extends battery life by weeks.
2. **Real-time biomedical wearables** (e.g., continuous ECG) where hundreds of samples per minute must be relayed over BLE — CBOR reduces airtime and lowers power drain.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **W3C Web of Things (WoT) Thing Description (TD)**. Explain how a TD abstracts a physical device into a set of resources. Provide a sample TD JSON snippet.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three-layer IoT architecture** with a focus on the Resource Abstraction Layer. State four responsibilities of the RAL and discuss how it differs from a traditional web API gateway.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**W3C WoT Thing Description (TD) — Detailed Explanation:**

A **Thing Description (TD)** is a structured JSON-LD document that formally describes the **metadata, properties, actions, and events** exposed by a "Thing" (a device or service). It is the W3C's recommended method for achieving semantic interoperability in IoT.

**Core Constructs of a TD:**

| TD Element | Purpose | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `title` | Human-readable name | `"Smart Thermostat"` |
| `@context` | JSON-LD context for semantic meaning | `"https://www.w3.org/2022/wot/td/v1.1"` |
| `securityDefinitions` | Security scheme definitions | `nosec`, `psk`, `oauth2` |
| `properties` | Readable/writable state variables | `currentTemp`, `setpoint` |
| `actions` | Invokable operations | `reset`, `calibrate` |
| `events` | Asynchronous notifications | `overheatAlert` |
| `forms` | Protocol binding for each interaction | `href`, `contentType`, `op` |

**How a TD Abstracts a Device into Resources:**

Each `property`, `action`, or `event` in a TD corresponds to a **logical resource**. The `forms` array specifies the protocol-level binding (HTTP, CoAP, MQTT). For example, the `currentTemp` property is a *readable resource* with a `GET` form pointing to `coap://192.168.1.10/temp`.

**Sample TD JSON Snippet:**

```json
{
  "@context": "https://www.w3.org/2022/wot/td/v1.1",
  "title": "Patient Heart Monitor",
  "securityDefinitions": { "nosec": { "scheme": "nosec" } },
  "security": ["nosec"],
  "properties": {
    "heartRate": {
      "type": "integer",
      "unit": "bpm",
      "observable": true,
      "forms": [{
        "href": "coap://192.168.1.20/hr",
        "contentType": "application/cbor",
        "op": ["observeproperty", "readproperty"]
      }]
    }
  },
  "actions": {
    "resetAlarm": {
      "forms": [{
        "href": "http://192.168.1.20/reset",
        "op": "invokeaction"
      }]
    }
  },
  "events": {
    "tachycardia": {
      "forms": [{
        "href": "coap://192.168.1.20/alert",
        "op": "subscribeevent"
      }]
    }
  }
}
```

**Engineering Significance:**

- A TD is **self-describing** — a client can read the TD and immediately know *what* the device does, *how* to talk to it, and *what security* is required — no prior knowledge needed.
- Enables **plug-and-play** integration of new devices.
- Bridges the gap between **embedded systems engineers** and **web developers**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Three-Layer IoT Architecture — Explanation:**

| Layer | Role | Typical Components |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Layer 1 — Device/Perception Layer** | Acquires raw data from physical world | Sensors, actuators, MCUs, RFID tags |
| **Layer 2 — Network/Abstraction Layer** | Forwards data, applies protocol translation, manages addressing | Gateways, brokers, Resource Directory |
| **Layer 3 — Application Layer** | Business logic, analytics, user interface | Cloud dashboards, ML models, mobile apps |

**Resource Abstraction Layer (RAL) — Four Key Responsibilities:**

1. **Protocol Translation:** Converts vendor-specific protocols (Zigbee ZCL, BLE GATT, Modbus RTU) into a common RESTful/HTTP/CoAP interface.
2. **Resource Modeling:** Maps raw sensor attributes (e.g., 12-byte register values) into semantically meaningful resources (e.g., `temperature=24.7°C`).
3. **Discovery Enablement:** Hosts a CoRE Resource Directory or WoT Thing Directory, allowing clients to find resources dynamically.
4. **Event & Observation Management:** Implements the OBSERVE pattern (server-push) so that applications receive notifications without continuous polling.

**RAL vs Traditional Web API Gateway:**

| Dimension | Traditional API Gateway | IoT Resource Abstraction Layer |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Primary protocol | HTTP/JSON only | HTTP, CoAP, MQTT, WebSockets |
| Data source | Backend services | Heterogeneous sensors/actuators |
| Representation | JSON | JSON, CBOR, EXI, SenML |
| State management | Stateless CRUD | Stateful real-time observation |
| Resource typing | Path-based URLs | Typed with `rt`, `if`, semantically annotated |
| Event model | Webhooks | Native OBSERVE / publish-subscribe |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Resource Model & Abstraction Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "resource" with "device"** — A device may host *multiple* resources. A smart bulb is *one* device exposing *two* resources (state, brightness). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you conflate the two.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention `rt` and `if` parameters** in the CoRE Link Format. A bare URI without metadata is technically a valid link but loses at least 2 marks in a "describe CoRE" question.
> 3. **Writing `JSON` and `CBOR` byte counts without showing the breakdown** — examiners expect a *stepwise derivation* (header, key, value). A naked final number earns 0–1 mark out of 4.
> 4. **Omitting the `@context` field** in a W3C WoT TD answer. This is what makes a TD a *semantic* document. Missing it loses at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Drawing a 3-layer diagram with arrows in the wrong direction** — ensure the Application Layer is at the top and the Device Layer at the bottom; the RAL sits *between* them and translates in *both* directions.
> 6. **Stating "REST = HTTP"** — REST is an *architectural style*, not a protocol. It can be implemented over CoAP, MQTT (with effort), or any URI-addressable transport. Examiners are strict on this distinction.
> 7. **Skipping units in CBOR size calculations** — always state both bytes saved and the percentage reduction.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Resource Model & Abstraction**

- **Definition:** A Resource is the atomic, addressable unit in IoT. Formally, $R = \langle \text{URI}, \text{REPR}, \text{IF}, \text{META} \rangle$.
- **Abstraction** hides protocol and hardware specifics; exposes a uniform RESTful interface.
- **REST methods:** GET (read), PUT (replace), POST (append/invoke), DELETE (remove), OBSERVE (subscribe).
- **CoRE Link Format** parameters: `rt` (resource type), `if` (interface type), `ct` (content format), `obs` (observable).
- **CoAP port** = **5683**; **DTLS port** = **5684**; **max URI length** = **255 bytes**.
- **JSON is verbose** (~36 bytes for a simple reading); **CBOR is compact** (~17 bytes for the same reading, ~53% savings).
- **W3C WoT TD** uses JSON-LD 1.1 with `@context` for semantic annotations; core constructs are `properties`, `actions`, `events`, `forms`.
- **Resource Directory (RD)** = central discovery server using CoRE Link Format.
- **Three-layer architecture:** Device Layer → RAL → Application Layer. RAL is responsible for protocol translation, resource modeling, discovery, and event management.
- **WoT vs OPC UA:** WoT is web-friendly (JSON-LD); OPC UA is industrial (binary, IEC standard). Both achieve resource abstraction.
- **Real-world impact:** A single, well-designed resource model allows an entire hospital's 1000+ biomedical devices to be managed from one dashboard.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wearable Health Monitoring System (WHMS): Basics of WHMS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wearable Health Monitoring System (WHMS): Basics of WHMS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wearable Health Monitoring System (WHMS)** is a non-invasive, body-worn electronic platform that integrates miniature biosensors, low-power embedded processing units, wireless communication transceivers, and energy harvesting/storage modules to continuously or intermittently acquire, process, transmit, and display physiological signals from a human subject in real time, with the explicit purpose of monitoring health status, detecting early-stage pathologies, and enabling proactive medical intervention.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework (OEEBT831), WHMS is positioned at the intersection of three engineering domains:

$$\text{WHMS} = f(\text{Biomedical Sensing},\ \text{Embedded IoT},\ \text{Wireless Communication})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: The term "Wearable" in WHMS is strictly reserved for devices that are **physically attached to the body** (wrist, chest, skin patch, ear, or implanted subcutaneously) and operate **autonomously for extended periods** (typically > 24 hours per charge cycle) without restricting the subject's normal locomotion.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Always-On Health Co-Pilot"

Imagine a highly experienced **family doctor who travels with you 24/7**, sitting invisibly on your wrist or chest. This doctor:

- Constantly checks your **heart rhythm** like a stethoscope (ECG sensor).
- Measures your **blood oxygen** like a finger clip pulse oximeter (SpO₂ sensor).
- Counts your **steps and posture** like a fitness trainer (accelerometer/gyroscope).
- Logs your **skin temperature** like a forehead thermometer.
- Instantly **calls your hospital** if anything abnormal is detected (wireless transmission to cloud/phone).
- Runs on a **tiny battery** that lasts for days, not hours.

That invisible co-pilot is precisely what a **Wearable Health Monitoring System** does — but in the form of silicon, sensors, and software.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight for KTU Students**: A WHMS is *not* a medical device in the strict regulatory sense (FDA Class II/III); it is a **consumer-grade or research-grade physiological data acquisition platform** that bridges the gap between clinical equipment (bulky, stationary) and personal wellness gadgets (limited accuracy).

## 1.3 Primary Physiological Parameters Monitored

A standard WHMS tracks one or more of the following **vital signs and bio-signals**:

| Bio-signal | Sensor Type | Typical Frequency Range | Clinical Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ECG** (Electrocardiogram) | Ag/AgCl dry electrodes | **0.05 – 150 Hz** | Arrhythmia, heart rate |
| **PPG** (Photoplethysmogram) | LED + photodiode | **0.5 – 8 Hz** | SpO₂, heart rate, BP estimation |
| **Body Temperature** | Thermistor / RTD | DC – 0.1 Hz | Fever, metabolic disorders |
| **GSR** (Galvanic Skin Response) | Conductance electrodes | **0.01 – 5 Hz** | Stress, emotional state |
| **Respiration Rate** | Strain gauge / impedance | **0.1 – 1 Hz** | Sleep apnea, asthma |
| **Blood Pressure** (cuffless) | PPG + ECG (PTT method) | Derived | Hypertension |
| **Body Motion** | MEMS accelerometer/gyro | **0 – 50 Hz** | Fall detection, gait, activity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember**: The dominant clinical benchmark for a WHMS is **Heart Rate (HR)** measured in **beats per minute (bpm)**, with a normal adult resting range of **60–100 bpm**. Wearable-grade sensors must achieve **±5 bpm accuracy** under ISO 80601-2-61:2019 guidelines.

## 1.4 Classification of Wearable Form Factors

WHMS devices are classified by their **placement topology** on the human body:

1. **Wrist-worn** — Smartwatches, fitness bands (Apple Watch, Fitbit, Mi Band).
2. **Chest-strap / Patch** — ECG monitors (Polar H10, AliveCor KardiaMobile).
3. **Head-mounted** — EEG headsets (Emotiv EPOC), smart glasses.
4. **Ear-worn (hearables)** — In-ear PPG, core temperature (Cosinuss One).
5. **Skin-patch / Epidermal** — Flexible electronics, sweat sensors.
6. **Smart textiles / E-textiles** — Sensor-embedded garments.
7. **Implantable** — Subcutaneous glucose monitors, pacemakers (technically beyond "wearable" but contextually related).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generic Block Architecture of a WHMS

Every WHMS, regardless of form factor, follows the canonical **five-stage signal chain**:

1. **Bio-signal Acquisition Layer** — Electrodes, optical sensors, MEMS transducers.
2. **Analog Front-End (AFE)** — Amplification, filtering, impedance matching.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** — Sampling and quantization.
4. **Digital Signal Processing (DSP) & Edge Computing** — Feature extraction, noise suppression, compression.
5. **Wireless Transmission Layer** — BLE, Wi-Fi, LoRa, NFC to gateway/cloud.
6. **Power Management Unit (PMU)** — Battery + energy harvesting.

### Operational Logic — Step by Step

- **Step 1 — Sensing**: Transducer converts a physiological variable (e.g., heart depolarization → electric potential) into an analog electrical signal $v(t)$.
- **Step 2 — Analog Conditioning**: An instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD8232 for ECG) boosts the micro-volt level signal to a usable range (typically 0–3.3 V).
- **Step 3 — Anti-Alias Filtering**: A low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c$ prevents spectral aliasing before sampling.
- **Step 4 — Sampling (ADC)**: Per the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must satisfy $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$.
- **Step 5 — Digital Processing**: Algorithms extract clinical features (R-R interval, HRV, SpO₂ ratio).
- **Step 6 — Wireless Uplink**: Data is packetized and transmitted to a smartphone gateway using BLE 5.0 (typical payload ~20 bytes/packet at 1 Hz).
- **Step 7 — Cloud Analytics**: ML-based anomaly detection (e.g., atrial fibrillation via deep learning on ECG).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why"**: The reason the AFE and ADC stages are non-negotiable is that human bio-signals are **orders of magnitude weaker** (ECG ≈ 1 mV) and **slower-varying** (body temp bandwidth ≈ DC–0.1 Hz) than the operating range of microcontrollers. Direct digital sampling is therefore impossible without front-end conditioning.

## 2.2 Design Constraints — The WHMS Trilemma

A successful WHMS must simultaneously optimize three competing parameters — the **WHMS Trilemma**:

- **Miniaturization** (size, weight < 50 g ideal for wrist form factor)
- **Battery Life** (target ≥ 7 days per charge)
- **Signal Quality / Clinical Accuracy** (SNR > 40 dB for diagnostic-grade ECG)

> Trade-off rule: Improving any one parameter typically degrades the other two. KTU exam questions frequently test this trade-off.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | $f_s$ = sampling frequency, $f_{\max}$ = highest signal frequency | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing |
| 2 | $f_{Nyq} = 2 f_{\max}$ | Nyquist rate | Sets ADC clock in firmware |
| 3 | $\Delta v = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | Quantization step, $V_{FS}$ = full-scale voltage, $n$ = ADC bits | ADC resolution design |
| 4 | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ | Ideal ADC signal-to-noise ratio | Determines minimum $n$ for clinical accuracy |
| 5 | $P_{total} = P_{sensor} + P_{AFE} + P_{MCU} + P_{radio}$ | Total system power budget | Battery life estimation |
| 6 | $T_{battery} = \dfrac{C_{battery}\ (\text{mAh})}{I_{avg}\ (\text{mA})}$ | Battery endurance | Wearable sizing |
| 7 | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{R\text{-}R_{interval}\ (s)}$ | Heart rate from R-R peak spacing | DSP feature extraction |
| 8 | $SpO_2 \approx A - B \cdot R$ where $R = \dfrac{AC_{red}/DC_{red}}{AC_{IR}/DC_{IR}}$ | Ratio of ratios for pulse oximetry | PPG algorithm |
| 9 | $BW = f_{upper} - f_{lower}$ | Signal bandwidth | AFE filter design |
| 10 | $D = R \times T_{sym}$ | Data throughput (bps), $R$ = symbol rate | Wireless link budget |

> [!IMPORTANT]
