> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note**: The vertical bar `\|` is intentionally avoided in formulas. In the SpO₂ formula above, the AC/DC ratios use a *forward slash* `\` notation. In actual KTU answer sheets, you may write the absolute value as `\vert x \vert` to prevent markdown rendering issues.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital telemetry**: WHMS enables early patient discharge with continuous remote monitoring (RPM — Remote Patient Monitoring), reducing ICU bed occupancy by up to **30%** in pilot studies.
- **Sports physiology**: Elite athletes use chest-strap WHMS to monitor HRV and lactate threshold in real time.
- **Elderly care**: Fall-detection wearables trigger automatic SOS alerts to caregivers.
- **Pandemic response**: WHMS enabled remote COVID-19 patient monitoring (SpO₂ trending) when hospital beds were saturated.
- **Insurance & wellness**: Insurers (e.g., John Hancock Vitality) subsidize WHMS use to lower actuarial risk.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Minimum Sampling Frequency for ECG Signal

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-Mark Pattern)**: The ECG bio-signal has a useful bandwidth of **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz**. Design a sampling system for a wearable ECG monitor. Compute: (a) the minimum sampling frequency, (b) the ADC bit-depth required for a clinical-grade SNR, and (c) the resulting data rate in bytes per second.

### Given Data
- Highest signal frequency: $f_{\max} = 150\ \text{Hz}$
- Target clinical SNR: $\text{SNR}_{dB} \geq 40\ \text{dB}$
- Resolution per sample: 2 bytes (16-bit) for storage

### Solution — Step by Step

**Part (a): Minimum Sampling Frequency**

We invoke the Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem:

$$
f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{\max}
$$

$$
f_s \geq 2 \times 150
$$

$$
\boxed{f_s \geq 300\ \text{Hz}}
$$

In practice, a **safety factor of 2.5× to 5×** is applied to account for anti-aliasing filter roll-off non-idealities. Hence commercial wearable ECG monitors typically sample at:

$$
f_s^{practical} = 300 \times 5 = 1500\ \text{Hz}\ \text{(a common value)}
$$

**Part (b): Required ADC Bit-Depth**

Using the ideal ADC SNR relation:

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76
$$

We solve for $n$ given $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 40$:

$$
40 = 6.02\,n + 1.76
$$

$$
6.02\,n = 40 - 1.76 = 38.24
$$

$$
n = \frac{38.24}{6.02}
$$

$$
\boxed{n \approx 6.35\ \text{bits}}
$$

Rounding **up** to the next integer (since ADC bits are discrete) yields $n = 7$ bits minimum, but a **16-bit ADC** is universally used in commercial WHMS to provide ~20 dB of safety margin and to capture subtle ECG morphology (P-wave, T-wave).

**Part (c): Data Rate Computation**

$$
D = f_s \times n_{bytes/sample}
$$

$$
D = 1500\ \text{samples/s} \times 2\ \bytes/sample}
$$

$$
\boxed{D = 3000\ \text{bytes/s} = 24\ \text{kbps}}
$$

> **Engineering Verdict**: This data rate is well within the capacity of BLE 5.0 (2 Mbps PHY) but exceeds BLE 4.0 advertising-mode bandwidth (~100 kbps), explaining why ECG streaming requires a *connected* BLE link, not advertising packets.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Battery Life Estimation for a Multi-Sensor WHMS

**Problem Statement**: A wrist-worn WHMS uses an accelerometer (avg current = 0.5 mA), a PPG sensor (avg = 1.2 mA), an MCU in active mode (avg = 3.0 mA, duty cycle 5%), and a BLE radio (avg = 5.0 mA, duty cycle 2%). Battery capacity = 200 mAh. Compute the average current and the battery endurance in days.

### Step 1 — Compute Time-Averaged Currents

$$
I_{MCU}^{avg} = 3.0\ \text{mA} \times 0.05 = 0.15\ \text{mA}
$$

$$
I_{radio}^{avg} = 5.0\ \text{mA} \times 0.02 = 0.10\ \text{mA}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Average Current

$$
I_{avg} = I_{accel} + I_{PPG} + I_{MCU}^{avg} + I_{radio}^{avg}
$$

$$
I_{avg} = 0.5 + 1.2 + 0.15 + 0.10
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{avg} = 1.95\ \text{mA}}
$$

### Step 3 — Battery Endurance

$$
T_{battery} = \frac{C_{battery}}{I_{avg}}
$$

$$
T_{battery} = \frac{200\ \text{mAh}}{1.95\ \text{mA}} \approx 102.56\ \text{hours}
$$

Converting to days:

$$
T_{battery} = \frac{102.56}{24}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{battery} \approx 4.27\ \text{days}}
$$

> **Engineering Insight**: Notice that the **PPG sensor dominates the power budget** (1.2 mA out of 1.95 mA ≈ 61.5%). This is why modern smartwatches employ **duty-cycled optical sampling** (e.g., ON for 5 ms every 1 s) to extend battery life to 7–14 days.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Sensor Power vs. Accuracy

| Sensor | Current Draw | Information Density | Clinical Acceptable Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accelerometer (3-axis) | 0.1 – 0.5 mA | Low (step count) | ±2% step count |
| PPG (HR + SpO₂) | 0.5 – 1.5 mA | Medium | ±5 bpm HR, ±2% SpO₂ |
| ECG (1-lead) | 2.0 – 4.0 mA | High | ±1 bpm HR, R-peak detection |
| Continuous Skin Temp | 0.05 mA | Low | ±0.1 °C |
| Multi-lead ECG (12-lead) | > 8 mA (impractical) | Very High | Diagnostic grade |

## 3.4 Derivation III — SpO₂ Ratio-of-Ratios Calculation

The pulse oximeter estimates arterial oxygen saturation using two wavelengths of light (red ≈ 660 nm, infrared ≈ 940 nm):

$$
R = \frac{AC_{red} / DC_{red}}{AC_{IR} / DC_{IR}}
$$

A typical empirical calibration (linear approximation):

$$
SpO_2\ (\%) = A - B \cdot R
$$

where empirical constants are commonly $A \approx 110$, $B \approx 25$ for finger-tip PPG sensors.

**Worked Example**: If $AC_{red}/DC_{red} = 0.025$ and $AC_{IR}/DC_{IR} = 0.040$:

$$
R = \frac{0.025}{0.040} = 0.625
$$

$$
SpO_2 = 110 - 25 \times 0.625 = 110 - 15.625
$$

$$
\boxed{SpO_2 \approx 94.4\%}
$$

This falls within the **normal clinical range (95–100%)** but is borderline — a clinical alert would be raised below 92%.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level WHMS System Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the canonical **five-layer architecture** of a wearable health monitoring system, mapping the flow of bio-signals from acquisition to cloud analytics.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Body Surface] --> A2[Bio Sensor 1: ECG Electrode]
    A1 --> A3[Bio Sensor 2: PPG LED + Photodiode]
    A1 --> A4[Bio Sensor 3: MEMS Accelerometer]
    A1 --> A5[Bio Sensor 4: Temperature Thermistor]
    A2 --> B1[Analog Front End: Instrumentation Amplifier]
    A3 --> B2[Analog Front End: Transimpedance Amplifier]
    A4 --> B3[Signal Conditioning: Charge Amplifier]
    A5 --> B4[Signal Conditioning: Wheatstone Bridge]
    B1 --> C1[Anti Alias Low Pass Filter]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Analog to Digital Converter ADC 16 bit]
    D1 --> E1[Microcontroller MCU: ARM Cortex M4]
    E1 --> F1[Digital Signal Processing: HR R Peak SpO2]
    F1 --> G1[BLE 5.0 Radio Module]
    G1 --> H1[Smartphone Gateway]
    H1 --> I1[Cloud Server: AWS IoT Core]
    I1 --> J1[Machine Learning Anomaly Detection]
    J1 --> K1[Mobile App Dashboard and Alerts]
    E1 --> L1[Power Management Unit: Li Ion 200 mAh]
    L1 --> B1
    L1 --> B2
    L1 --> B3
    L1 --> B4
    L1 --> D1
    L1 --> E1
    L1 --> G1
    L1 --> M1[Energy Harvester Optional: Solar or Thermoelectric]
    M1 --> L1
```

## 4.2 Signal Processing Chain for ECG

The following diagram details the *digital* signal processing pipeline that runs on the MCU after the ADC. Note that the node IDs are alphanumeric and contain no markdown formatting to ensure Mermaid compatibility.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Raw ECG Samples 16 bit 1500 Hz] --> node2[Digital Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 40 Hz]
    node2 --> node3[50 Hz Notch Filter Powerline Rejection]
    node3 --> node4[Baseline Wander Removal High Pass]
    node4 --> node5[Pan Tompkins R Peak Detection]
    node5 --> node6[RR Interval Computation]
    node6 --> node7[Heart Rate Calculation BPM]
    node6 --> node8[HRV Time Domain SDNN RMSSD]
    node6 --> node9[HRV Frequency Domain LF HF Ratio]
    node7 --> node10[Output Buffer]
    node8 --> node10
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11[BLE Packetization and Transmission]
```

## 4.3 Power State Machine of a Duty-Cycled WHMS

To extend battery life, a wearable MCU is **not** continuously active. The following state diagram shows the typical power-mode transitions over a one-second duty cycle.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SleepMode
    SleepMode --> ActiveMode: Timer Wakeup every 1 s
    ActiveMode: Active Mode MCU + Sensors ON
    ActiveMode: Duration 50 ms
    ActiveMode --> TransmitMode: BLE Buffer Ready
    TransmitMode: Transmit Mode Radio ON
    TransmitMode: Duration 5 ms
    TransmitMode --> SleepMode: Radio Off
    SleepMode: Sleep Mode Current 50 uA
    SleepMode: Duration 945 ms
    SleepMode --> ActiveMode
    note right of ActiveMode: Current Draw ~15 mA peak
    note right of SleepMode: Current Draw ~0.05 mA idle
```

## 4.4 Wearable Form Factor Decision Tree

The following flowchart helps an engineer select the appropriate form factor for a target bio-signal.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[New WHMS Design] --> q1{Signal to Monitor?}
    q1 -->|ECG Arrhythmia| r1[Chest Strap or Patch]
    q1 -->|SpO2 HR Daily| r2[Wrist Watch or Ear Bud]
    q1 -->|Body Temp Continuous| r3[Axillary Patch or Earpiece]
    q1 -->|Gait Fall Detection| r4[Waist Belt or Smart Shoe]
    q1 -->|EEG Brain Activity| r5[Headset or Behind Ear]
    r1 --> s1{Continuous vs Spot?}
    r2 --> s1
    r3 --> s1
    r4 --> s1
    r5 --> s1
    s1 -->|Continuous 24x7| t1[BLE Connected Mode + Rechargeable]
    s1 -->|Spot On Demand| t2[BLE Advertising + Coin Cell]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** 
> **Define a Wearable Health Monitoring System (WHMS) and list any four physiological parameters it commonly monitors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Wearable Health Monitoring System is a body-worn electronic platform that integrates biosensors, low-power embedded processing, and wireless communication to continuously acquire and transmit physiological signals for health assessment. *(1.5 marks)*

Four commonly monitored parameters:
1. Heart Rate (ECG/PPG)
2. Blood Oxygen Saturation — SpO₂
3. Body Temperature
4. Galvanic Skin Response (GSR) / Skin Conductance

*(1.5 marks — 0.375 each)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sessional Exam Pattern]** 
> **What is the Nyquist Sampling Theorem? Why is it critical in the design of a wearable ECG monitor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Nyquist Sampling Theorem states that to accurately reconstruct a band-limited analog signal, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must be at least twice the highest frequency component $f_{\max}$ present in the signal: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$. *(1.5 marks)*

In a wearable ECG monitor, the ECG signal contains frequency components up to ~150 Hz, so the ADC must sample at $f_s \geq 300$ Hz. Failure to meet this criterion causes **aliasing**, in which high-frequency content is folded back into the low-frequency band, producing clinically misleading waveforms (e.g., false ST-segment elevation). *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> ### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the generic architecture of a Wearable Health Monitoring System. List the functions of each block. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An ECG signal is to be sampled by a wearable monitor. The signal bandwidth is 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz. Determine:
   (i) The minimum sampling frequency.
   (ii) The ADC resolution in bits required for a clinical SNR of 40 dB.
   (iii) The data rate in kbps if each sample is stored as 2 bytes. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks)

A WHMS consists of **five functional layers**:

1. **Bio-Signal Acquisition Layer** *(1 mark)*: Comprises electrodes (ECG), optical sensors (PPG), or MEMS transducers (motion). Converts physiological events into analog electrical signals.

2. **Analog Front-End (AFE)** *(1 mark)*: Instrumentation amplifiers (e.g., AD8232) boost micro-volt-level signals; bandpass filters remove DC offset and high-frequency noise.

3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** *(1 mark)*: Samples the conditioned analog signal at $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ and quantizes each sample to $n$-bit digital code.

4. **Digital Signal Processing & Wireless Transmitter** *(2 marks)*: The MCU runs feature-extraction algorithms (e.g., Pan-Tompkins for ECG R-peak detection) and packetizes data for BLE 5.0 transmission to a smartphone gateway.

5. **Power Management Unit (PMU)** *(1 mark)*: Li-ion or coin-cell battery with optional energy harvesting (solar, thermoelectric) regulates supply rails to all sub-systems.

6. **Cloud + Application Layer** *(1 mark)*: Smartphone app and cloud server perform ML-based anomaly detection and provide alerts.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 marks)

**(i) Minimum Sampling Frequency** *(2 marks)*:

By Nyquist theorem:
$$
f_s \geq 2 \times f_{\max} = 2 \times 150 = 300\ \text{Hz}
$$

**(ii) ADC Bit Depth** *(3 marks)*:

Using $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$:

$$
n = \frac{40 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{38.24}{6.02} \approx 6.35\ \text{bits}
$$

Rounding up: $n = 7$ bits minimum. In practice, **16-bit ADC** is used. *(1 mark for justifying the practical choice)*

**(iii) Data Rate** *(2 marks)*:

$$
D = f_s \times \text{bytes/sample} = 1500\ \text{samples/s} \times 2\ \bytes
$$

(Note: We use the practical $f_s = 1500$ Hz for a 5× safety factor.)

$$
D = 3000\ \text{B/s} = 3000 \times 8\ \text{bps} = 24\ \text{kbps}
$$

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style — Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Compare and contrast wrist-worn, chest-strap, and ear-worn (hearable) form factors of WHMS devices in terms of sensor placement, signal accuracy, battery life, and user compliance. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A wrist-worn WHMS has the following average current consumptions:
   - Accelerometer: 0.4 mA (always ON)
   - PPG sensor: 1.5 mA (always ON)
   - MCU: 4.0 mA (duty cycle 4%)
   - BLE radio: 6.0 mA (duty cycle 1.5%)
   
   The device is powered by a 180 mAh Li-Po battery. Compute: (i) the time-averaged current, (ii) the battery endurance in hours, and (iii) comment on how duty cycling the PPG sensor to 20% duty cycle would affect endurance. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Comparative Form Factor Analysis (7 marks)

| Parameter | Wrist-Worn | Chest-Strap | Ear-Worn (Hearable) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Typical Sensors** | PPG, Accel, Temp, ECG (some) | ECG (1-lead), Accel | PPG (in-ear), Core Temp, Accel |
| **Signal Accuracy (ECG)** | Poor (motion artifacts) | Excellent (close to heart) | Poor to Moderate |
| **Signal Accuracy (SpO₂)** | Moderate (peripheral perfusion) | N/A | Good (rich vascularization) |
| **Battery Life** | 5–14 days | 1–3 days | 3–7 days |
| **User Compliance** | Very High (fashionable) | Moderate (chest discomfort) | Moderate to High (invisible) |
| **Form Factor** | Watch, band | Adhesive patch or strap | Earbud, clip |
| **Best Use Case** | Daily fitness, step count, sleep | Clinical ECG, arrhythmia | SpO₂, core temperature |

*(1 mark per row × 5 rows = 5 marks; 2 marks for summary conclusion justifying one form factor over another for a specific use case.)*

#### Part (b) — Power Budget Computation (7 marks)

**(i) Time-Averaged Currents** *(2 marks)*:

$$
I_{MCU}^{avg} = 4.0 \times 0.04 = 0.16\ \text{mA}
$$

$$
I_{radio}^{avg} = 6.0 \times 0.015 = 0.09\ \text{mA}
$$

**Total Average Current** *(2 marks)*:

$$
I_{avg} = 0.4 + 1.5 + 0.16 + 0.09 = 2.15\ \text{mA}
$$

**(ii) Battery Endurance** *(2 marks)*:

$$
T_{battery} = \frac{180\ \text{mAh}}{2.15\ \text{mA}} \approx 83.72\ \text{hours} \approx 3.49\ \text{days}
$$

**(iii) Effect of Duty Cycling PPG to 20%** *(1 mark)*:

New $I_{PPG}^{avg} = 1.5 \times 0.20 = 0.30\ \text{mA}$

New $I_{avg} = 0.4 + 0.30 + 0.16 + 0.09 = 0.95\ \text{mA}$

$$
T_{battery}^{new} = \frac{180}{0.95} \approx 189.5\ \text{hours} \approx 7.9\ \text{days}
$$

**Endurance improvement**: $(189.5 - 83.72) / 83.72 \approx 126\%$ — battery life more than doubles, validating the duty-cycling strategy used in commercial wearables.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "sampling rate" with "Nyquist rate"**: The Nyquist rate is $2 f_{\max}$, the actual sampling rate is typically 2.5–5× higher. Using 300 Hz *as* the sampling rate in numerical problems may cost 0.5–1 mark.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to round up the ADC bit depth**: Always round **up** to the next integer; never round down. Writing $n = 6$ instead of $n = 7$ is a common 0.5-mark loss.
> 
> 3. **Units mismatch in data rate**: Mixing bps and B/s without the factor of 8. Always state units explicitly.
> 
> 4. **Power budget — using peak current instead of time-averaged current**: For battery life calculation, you MUST multiply by the duty cycle. This is the most common error, costing up to 2 marks in 14-mark questions.
> 
> 5. **Block diagram in part (a)**: Marks are reserved for a *labeled* diagram with arrows. A diagram without labels gets only 50% credit. Always include input → output direction and key component names.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the "Why"**: KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes *understanding* over memorization. Writing $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ without explaining aliasing loses you the application mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as your final 5-minute revision before the exam.**

✅ **WHMS = Biosensors + AFE + ADC + DSP/MCU + Wireless + Power + Cloud** (seven-layer canonical model).

✅ **Heart rate normal range**: **60–100 bpm**; SpO₂ normal: **95–100%**; body temp: **36.1–37.2 °C**.

✅ **Nyquist Theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$. Always add 2.5–5× safety factor for real anti-aliasing filters.

✅ **ADC SNR formula**: $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$. This is **high-yield** — appears in 90% of KTU numericals.

✅ **Power budget trilemma**: Miniaturization ↔ Battery life ↔ Signal quality. You cannot maximize all three simultaneously.

✅ **Always use time-averaged current** for battery life: $I_{avg} = \sum I_i \times D_i$, where $D_i$ is the duty cycle.

✅ **PPG dominates power budget** in wrist-worn devices (~60% of total). This is why duty cycling is universally used.

✅ **SpO₂ formula**: $R = (AC_{red}/DC_{red}) / (AC_{IR}/DC_{IR})$; calibrate as $SpO_2 \approx 110 - 25 R$.

✅ **Form factor trade-offs**: Wrist = high compliance, low ECG accuracy; Chest = high ECG accuracy, low compliance; Ear = good SpO₂, moderate compliance.

✅ **Wireless protocols in WHMS**: BLE 5.0 (most common), Wi-Fi (high bandwidth), LoRa (long range, low data), NFC (zero-power, on-demand).

✅ **Clinical accuracy standard**: ISO 80601-2-61:2019 for SpO₂; IEC 60601-2-25 for ECG.

✅ **Energy harvesting options**: Photovoltaic (outdoor), thermoelectric (body heat ~5 μW/cm²), piezoelectric (motion), RF (ambient).

✅ **Typical KTU 14-mark question pattern**: (a) Block diagram + architecture explanation (7 marks) + (b) Numerical on sampling/ADC/power budget (7 marks). Practice at least 5 such questions before the exam.

✅ **Three Cs of WHMS design**: **C**ontinuous monitoring, **C**omfortable wear, **C**linically valid data.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Wearable Health Monitoring System (WHMS): Basics of WHMS**
> **Module 3 | OEEBT831 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for Wearable Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Need for Wearable Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Wearable System** is a class of compact, body-mounted electronic device that integrates **micro-sensors**, **microprocessors**, **wireless communication modules**, and **energy harvesting/power management units** to continuously monitor physiological, biochemical, or motion-related parameters of a human or animal in real-time, while ensuring user comfort, mobility, and non-intrusive operation.

The **"Need for Wearable Systems"** arises from the convergence of four global megatrends recognized by the KTU 2024 OBE framework:

1. The exponential rise in **chronic lifestyle diseases** (diabetes, CVDs, hypertension).
2. The demographic shift toward an **aging population** requiring continuous geriatric care.
3. The demand for **personalized, data-driven healthcare** enabled by IoT.
4. The requirement for **unobtrusive, real-time, and remote health monitoring** to reduce hospitalization costs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim recall line):**
> *"Wearable systems are miniaturized, body-worn computational and sensing devices that enable continuous, real-time, and non-invasive monitoring of an individual's physiological signals, activity, and environment, thereby transforming reactive healthcare into proactive, preventive, and personalized care."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are wearing a **smart wristwatch that is also a tiny doctor, a fitness trainer, and a 24/7 emergency call button — all stitched into one band on your wrist**.

- **Without a wearable** → You only know you are sick when symptoms become severe enough to visit a hospital. It is like waiting for a car to completely break down before checking the engine.
- **With a wearable** → Your heartbeat, oxygen level, sleep quality, steps, and even stress levels are being quietly recorded every second. If something abnormal is detected (say, an irregular heartbeat at 3 AM), the watch alerts you and your doctor immediately — *before* you even feel pain. It is like having a **dashboard warning light** that flashes the moment engine oil pressure drops.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** The fundamental "need" for wearables comes from one central idea — **shifting the healthcare model from "cure after sickness" to "predict and prevent before sickness."**

## 1.3 Key Drivers Justifying the Need (Board-Favorite Points)

| S.No | Driver | Why It Creates the Need |
|------|--------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Rising chronic diseases** | Continuous monitoring outside hospital is required |
| 2 | **Aging population** | Geriatric patients need unobtrusive round-the-clock surveillance |
| 3 | **Healthcare cost reduction** | Preventive care is cheaper than ICU admission |
| 4 | **IoT proliferation** | Cloud + 5G makes remote data transfer feasible |
| 5 | **Personalized medicine** | Individualized baselines require longitudinal data |
| 6 | **Sports & fitness boom** | Quantified-self movement demands real-time metrics |
| 7 | **Pandemic preparedness** | Remote vital monitoring (e.g., SpO₂ during COVID-19) |
| 8 | **Emergency detection** | Fall detection, arrhythmia alerts save lives |

## 1.4 Salient Features of an Ideal Wearable System (Definition-Grade List)

A wearable system is deemed *necessary and acceptable* only when it satisfies the following characteristics (frequently asked as **3-mark definitions** in KTU):

- **Unobtrusive / Non-invasive** — no penetration of skin.
- **Lightweight and compact** — typically **< 50 g** for wrist-worn devices.
- **Low power consumption** — battery life of at least **24–72 hours**.
- **Wireless connectivity** — Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, ZigBee, or NB-IoT.
- **Real-time processing** — latency under **~200 ms** for alerts.
- **Data security** — AES-128 or higher encryption.
- **Biocompatibility** — hypoallergenic, ISO 10993 compliant.
- **High accuracy & reliability** — clinically validated sensor calibration.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick — "U-L-L-W-R-D-B-H":** Unobtrusive, Lightweight, Low-power, Wireless, Real-time, Data-secure, Biocompatible, High-accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational "Why" — Breaking Down the Need Step-by-Step

The necessity of wearable systems can be analyzed across **three layers of justification** (the most common KTU 14-mark answer structure):

### Layer 1: Clinical Need
- Hospital-based monitoring captures only **snapshot data** (a 30-second ECG strip during a clinic visit).
- Many pathologies (nocturnal arrhythmia, sleep apnea, episodic hypertension) are **intermittent** and missed during a single visit.
- Wearables provide **longitudinal, continuous data**, dramatically increasing diagnostic yield.
- Early detection reduces **mortality** (e.g., atrial fibrillation stroke risk reduction by **~68%** when detected early).

### Layer 2: Technological Enablement
- Advances in **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)** sensors have shrunk sensor size to **mm³** scale.
- **BLE 5.0 / 5G** allows < 10 mW transmission at Mbps speeds.
- **Edge AI** (TinyML) enables on-device inference without cloud round-trip.
- **Energy harvesting** (thermoelectric, piezoelectric, photovoltaic) extends battery life indefinitely for low-duty-cycle sensors.

### Layer 3: Socio-Economic Need
- WHO estimates that **~71% of global deaths** are from chronic diseases.
- Hospital ICU bed-day cost in India ≈ **₹25,000–₹50,000**; a wearable costs a fraction over its lifetime.
- Rural India faces a **doctor-to-patient ratio of 1:1456** (vs WHO ideal of 1:1000) — wearables can act as a force multiplier.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Although the topic is largely conceptual, the following quantitative metrics are **mandatory for 14-mark numerical/analytical KTU questions**:

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Battery life (hours)** | $T_{bat} = \dfrac{C_{bat} \cdot V_{nom}}{P_{sensor} + P_{proc} + P_{TX}}$ | 24–168 h | Power budgeting for wearables |
| **Sampling theorem constraint** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist) | ECG: $f_s \geq 500$ Hz | Determines ADC sampling rate |
| **Wireless range (Friis equation)** | $P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | BLE: 10–100 m | Antenna-link budget design |
| **Specific Absorption Rate (SAR)** | $\text{SAR} = \dfrac{\sigma \vert E \vert^{2}}{\rho}$ | $\leq 1.6$ W/kg (FCC) | Safety of RF-emitting wearables |
| **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | ECG: $\geq 40$ dB | Sensor signal quality check |
| **Heart rate from PPG** | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{T_{peak-to-peak}}$ | 60–100 bpm | Vital extraction algorithm |
| **Data storage (per day)** | $D = f_s \cdot N_b \cdot 86400$ bits | ECG 500 Hz × 12 bit ≈ 6.5 MB/day | Memory sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember the FCC safety line for SAR:** Wearable devices operating near the body **must** satisfy $\text{SAR} \leq 1.6$ W/kg averaged over 1 g of tissue. KTU examiners love this as a 2-mark safety-compliance sub-question.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Wearable Example | Parameter Tracked | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cardiology** | Apple Watch, AliveCor KardiaMobile | ECG, HR, HRV | AFib detection, stroke prevention |
| **Diabetology** | Dexcom G7, Abbott FreeStyle Libre 3 | Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM) | Insulin-dosing automation |
| **Pulmonology** | Oxitone, Masimo MightySat | SpO₂, respiratory rate | COPD/COVID-19 monitoring |
| **Neurology** | Empatica EpiMonitor | EEG, EDA, temperature | Epilepsy seizure alerts |
| **Sports Science** | Garmin Fenix, Whoop band | VO₂ max, HRV, sleep | Performance optimization |
| **Industrial Safety** | Guardhat, ProGlove | Gas exposure, posture, fatigue | Worker injury prevention |
| **Geriatrics** | Apple Watch fall detection | Accelerometer + gyroscope | Auto-SOS after fall |
| **Defense** | BAE Systems Broadsword | ECG, hydration, ballistics | Soldier readiness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how almost every wearable uses the same tri-axis **accelerometer + gyroscope + biosensor** triplet. The *universal* sensor stack is the technical reason why miniaturized wearables have become economically viable — economy of scale at the MEMS level.

## 2.4 Characteristics Differentiating Wearables from Other Medical Devices

| Property | Wearable System | Conventional Medical Device |
|---|---|---|
| Form factor | Body-worn, miniature | Bench-top, trolley-mounted |
| Power source | Battery / harvested | Mains AC |
| Operation | Continuous (24/7) | Episodic, in-clinic |
| Patient mobility | Fully mobile | Restricted to bed/ward |
| Data volume | High-frequency streams | Snapshot measurements |
| Comfort | Designed for daily wear | Not designed for comfort |
| Connectivity | Wireless (IoT-native) | Often wired/local |
| Use case | Preventive + lifestyle | Diagnostic + therapeutic |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why Continuous Monitoring Outperforms Snapshot Monitoring

**Statement to prove:** A wearable's continuous data stream provides a higher probability of detecting an *intermittent pathological event* than periodic in-clinic measurement.

### Step 1 — Define the problem mathematically

Let the probability that a pathological event occurs within a single hour be $p$ (a small number, e.g., $p = 0.01$ for nocturnal arrhythmia).

The probability of **at least one event** occurring in $n$ independent hours is given by the complement rule:

$$P(\text{event observed}) = 1 - (1 - p)^{n}$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate for snapshot (clinic visit) case

A clinic visit typically captures only **$n_{clinic} = 1$ hour of data**.

$$P_{clinic} = 1 - (1 - p)^{1} = p = 0.01 \;\; (\text{i.e., } 1\%)$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate for wearable continuous case

A wearable worn 24 hours/day for 7 days captures $n_{wear} = 168$ hours.

$$P_{wear} = 1 - (1 - p)^{168}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify using binomial approximation

Since $p$ is small, we use the approximation $(1 - p)^{n} \approx e^{-np}$:

$$P_{wear} \approx 1 - e^{-168 \times 0.01} = 1 - e^{-1.68}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical evaluation

$$P_{wear} \approx 1 - 0.1864 = 0.8136 \;\; (\text{i.e., } 81.36\%)$$

### Step 6 — Conclusion

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{P_{wear}}{P_{clinic}} = \dfrac{0.8136}{0.01} \approx 81\times\;\text{higher detection probability}\;}$$

> **Interpretation:** This single derivation is the **quantitative heart** of the "Need for Wearables" argument. KTU examiners award full 7 marks when a student writes this exact binomial logic.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Budgeting for a Wearable (Battery Life)

A wearable with the following subsystem power draw is given:

- Sensor: $P_{sensor} = 5$ mW
- Microcontroller: $P_{proc} = 15$ mW
- BLE transmitter: $P_{TX} = 20$ mW (active only 5% of the time → average ≈ 1 mW)

### Step 1 — Compute total average power

$$P_{total} = P_{sensor} + P_{proc} + (P_{TX} \times \text{duty cycle})$$

$$P_{total} = 5 \text{ mW} + 15 \text{ mW} + (20 \text{ mW} \times 0.05)$$

$$P_{total} = 5 + 15 + 1 = 21 \text{ mW}$$

### Step 2 — Apply battery-life formula

A typical smartwatch Li-ion battery has $C_{bat} = 300$ mAh at $V_{nom} = 3.7$ V.

Energy stored:

$$E_{bat} = C_{bat} \times V_{nom} = 300 \text{ mAh} \times 3.7 \text{ V} = 1110 \text{ mWh}$$

### Step 3 — Compute battery life

$$T_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{total}} = \dfrac{1110 \text{ mWh}}{21 \text{ mW}}$$

$$\boxed{\;T_{bat} \approx 52.86 \text{ hours} \approx 2.2 \text{ days}\;}$$

### Step 4 — Engineering conclusion

This confirms a real-world wearable target of **~2 days** battery life, validating the need for **ultra-low-power MCUs** (e.g., Nordic nRF52840, Ambiq Apollo4) and **energy harvesting** extensions.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping "Need" to "Engineering Solution"

| Identified Need | Quantitative Justification | Engineering Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous monitoring | $81\times$ higher detection (Derivation 1) | Always-on low-power sensors |
| Mobility | Patient walks during monitoring | Wireless BLE/5G link |
| Non-invasiveness | Skin-safe, no surgery | Surface bioelectric electrodes, optical PPG |
| Real-time alerts | Latency < 200 ms | Edge AI inference on MCU |
| Long battery life | Need 24+ h (Derivation 2) | Energy harvesting + duty cycling |
| Data security | PHI protection (HIPAA/GDPR) | AES-128 + TLS 1.3 |
| Comfort | Wear-time compliance > 80% | Flexible substrates, polymer electrodes |
| Multi-parameter | ECG, SpO₂, temp, motion | Sensor fusion with IMU |
| Affordability | Mass-market < ₹5,000 | MEMS economies of scale |
| Interoperability | Multi-vendor integration | IEEE 11073 / FHIR standards |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Linking *need → metric → solution* in a 3-column matrix is the **gold-standard 14-mark answer structure**. A student who draws this matrix visibly scores higher than one who writes paragraphs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Wearable System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BIO SIGNAL SOURCE\nHeart, Muscle, Skin, Blood] --> B[BIOSENSOR LAYER\nPPG, ECG, EMG, Temp]
    B --> C[ANALOG FRONT END\nAmplifier, Filter, ADC]
    C --> D[PROCESSING UNIT\nMCU with TinyML]
    D --> E[POWER MANAGEMENT\nBattery + Harvester]
    D --> F[COMMUNICATION MODULE\nBLE / Wi-Fi / NB-IoT]
    F --> G[CLOUD EDGE LAYER\nGateway, Smartphone, Cloud]
    G --> H[APPLICATION LAYER\nDoctor Dashboard, Alerts, ML Analytics]
    H --> I[FEEDBACK LOOP\nUser Notifications, Therapy Adjustment]
    I --> A
    E -.-> B
    E -.-> C
    E -.-> D
    E -.-> F
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of "Why We Need Wearables" — A Tree View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[NEED FOR WEARABLE SYSTEMS] --> c1[Clinical Need]
    root --> c2[Technological Enablement]
    root --> c3[Socio Economic Need]

    c1 --> c1a[Continuous vs Snapshot Monitoring]
    c1 --> c1b[Early Disease Detection]
    c1 --> c1c[Chronic Disease Management]
    c1 --> c1d[Geriatric Fall Detection]

    c2 --> c2a[MEMS Sensor Miniaturization]
    c2 --> c2b[Low Power BLE 5G]
    c2 --> c2c[Edge AI and TinyML]
    c2 --> c2d[Energy Harvesting]

    c3 --> c3a[Aging Population Globally]
    c3 --> c3b[Reducing Hospitalization Cost]
    c3 --> c4c[Doctor to Patient Ratio Gap]
    c3 --> c4d[Pandemic and Remote Care]
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — When is a Wearable Truly *Needed*?

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1{Is continuous\nmonitoring\nrequired?} -- YES --> Q2{Is patient\nmobile or\nremote?}
    Q1 -- NO --> X1[Conventional device is enough]
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3{Non invasive\nand 24 hour\noperation feasible?}
    Q2 -- NO --> X2[In-clinic or bedside monitor]
    Q3 -- YES --> Y1[WEARABLE IS NEEDED]
    Q3 -- NO --> X3[Implantable is required]
```

## 4.4 Data Flow of a Typical Wearable Health Monitoring Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Patient Body] --> P2[Sensor on Skin]
    P2 --> P3[Analog Signal]
    P3 --> P4[ADC Conversion]
    P4 --> P5[Digital Signal Processing]
    P5 --> P6{Local Anomaly?}
    P6 -- YES --> P7[On Device Alert]
    P6 -- NO --> P8[BLE Transmission]
    P8 --> P9[Smartphone App]
    P9 --> P10[Cloud Server]
    P10 --> P11[Doctor Dashboard]
    P11 --> P12[Clinical Decision Support]
    P12 --> P13[Treatment Adjustment]
    P13 --> P1
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram-viva takeaway:** The examiner can ask *"Why is the data flow not uni-directional?"* — the answer is the **closed-loop feedback** for therapy adjustment (e.g., automated insulin pump, closed-loop cardiac pacemaker). Always draw the return arrow.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: Define a Wearable System. List any four characteristics that justify the need for such a system in modern healthcare.  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Wearable System** is a body-mounted, compact electronic device that integrates sensors, processing, communication, and power subsystems to provide **continuous, real-time, and non-invasive monitoring** of physiological or environmental parameters.

**Four justifying characteristics:**

1. **Unobtrusive and lightweight** — ensures patient compliance and long wear time.
2. **Low power consumption** — enables 24–72 hours of operation on a single charge.
3. **Wireless connectivity (BLE/Wi-Fi/5G)** — supports real-time remote monitoring.
4. **Biocompatible and safe** — hypoallergenic materials complying with ISO 10993 and FCC SAR limits ($\leq 1.6$ W/kg).

### Question 2: Differentiate between wearable and implantable medical devices. Give one example of each.  `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Wearable Device | Implantable Device |
|---|---|---|
| Placement | External, on body surface | Internal, inside body tissue |
| Invasiveness | Non-invasive | Invasive (surgical) |
| Examples | Smartwatch ECG (Apple Watch) | Pacemaker, Cochlear implant |
| Power | Rechargeable battery / harvested | Inductive / battery (long-life) |
| Risk | Minimal skin irritation | Surgical/infection risk |
| Data access | Easy retrieval via BLE | Requires telemetry wand |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) **Explain in detail the various factors that justify the need for wearable systems in modern healthcare. Discuss the role of IoT in enabling this transformation.** — 7 marks  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The need for wearable systems is justified by the convergence of **clinical, technological, and socio-economic** factors.

**1. Clinical factors (3 marks):**

- Conventional hospital-based monitoring captures only **snapshot data**, missing intermittent pathologies.
- Wearables offer **continuous longitudinal data** (e.g., 168 h/week vs 1 h/clinic visit), increasing detection probability by **~81×** as shown in the binomial derivation.
- Applications: continuous ECG for arrhythmia, CGM for diabetes, SpO₂ for COPD, fall detection in elderly.

**2. Technological factors (2 marks):**

- **MEMS miniaturization** has shrunk sensors to mm³ scale.
- **Low-power wireless** (BLE 5.0, NB-IoT) and **5G** enable real-time cloud upload.
- **Edge AI / TinyML** allows on-device inference with sub-milliwatt power.

**3. Socio-economic factors (1 mark):**

- Rising chronic-disease burden, aging population, doctor–patient gap, and high ICU costs.

**4. Role of IoT (1 mark):**

IoT acts as the **connective tissue** — wearables → gateway (smartphone) → cloud → analytics → clinician dashboard, forming a closed-loop proactive healthcare model.

#### (b) **With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a generic wearable health-monitoring system. Explain the function of each block.** — 7 marks  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Refer to the block diagram drawn in Section 4.1. The functional blocks are:

1. **Biosensor Layer** — Captures physiological signals (PPG, ECG, EMG, temperature).
2. **Analog Front End (AFE)** — Amplifies weak biopotential signals (gain ~1000), filters 50/60 Hz noise, performs 12–16 bit ADC.
3. **Processing Unit (MCU)** — Runs digital signal processing, feature extraction, and on-device ML inference (e.g., HRV, QRS detection).
4. **Communication Module** — Transmits data via BLE/Wi-Fi/NB-IoT to a gateway.
5. **Power Management** — Li-ion battery, buck-boost regulator, optional energy harvester (solar/thermoelectric).
6. **Cloud + Application Layer** — Storage, longitudinal analytics, doctor dashboard, alert generation.
7. **Feedback Loop** — User notifications, automated therapy (e.g., insulin pump, neurostimulator).

**Conclusion:** This modular architecture enables **plug-and-play sensor upgrades**, making the wearable future-proof.

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

#### (a) **Discuss the major challenges in designing wearable systems for biomedical applications. Suggest suitable engineering solutions for each.** — 7 marks  `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Challenge | Engineering Solution |
|---|---|
| **Limited battery life** | Energy harvesting + ultra-low-power MCUs (sub-threshold CMOS) |
| **Motion artifacts** | Adaptive filtering, sensor fusion with IMU |
| **Data security / privacy** | AES-128 encryption, TLS 1.3, on-device anonymization |
| **Skin irritation / biocompatibility** | Hypoallergenic silicone, ISO 10993 testing |
| **Accuracy & calibration drift** | Periodic auto-calibration, machine-learned baselines |
| **Wireless interference** | Adaptive frequency hopping, BLE channel selection |
| **Data overload** | Edge AI feature extraction before cloud upload |
| **Form factor vs functionality** | Flexible PCBs, system-on-chip (SoC) integration |

#### (b) **Compare and contrast wearable systems with conventional medical monitoring systems. Justify with examples why the shift towards wearables is inevitable in the next decade.** — 7 marks  `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The comparison matrix from Section 2.4 forms the core. **Key points:**

- Wearables provide **24/7 continuous data**; conventional devices provide episodic data.
- Wearables are **mobile, non-invasive, IoT-native**; conventional devices are **stationary, often invasive or operator-dependent**.
- **Example justification:**
  - **Apple Watch ECG** detected AFib in asymptomatic patients, preventing strokes — a feat impossible with a single 12-lead ECG taken in-clinic.
  - **Dexcom G7 CGM** revolutionized diabetes management by replacing finger-prick snapshots with 288 glucose readings/day.
  - **Oxitone wrist SpO₂** allowed home-based COVID-19 monitoring, reducing hospital burden during the pandemic.
- The shift is inevitable because of **5G rollout, AI maturity, miniaturization, and rising consumer health awareness**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. ❌ Writing "wearable = smartwatch" only — this loses marks. **Mention at least 4 form factors** (wrist, patch, textile, implant-adjacent, hearable).
> 2. ❌ Skipping the **quantitative justification** (Section 3.1 derivation) — examiners explicitly award 2–3 marks for the 81× detection probability calculation.
> 3. ❌ Forgetting the **closed-loop feedback** arrow in the block diagram — KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes Outcome-Based Education (CO3: Design), so closed-loop design is graded.
> 4. ❌ Confusing **wearable** with **portable** — portable devices (e.g., Holter monitor) are not worn continuously; clarity of definition matters.
> 5. ❌ Omitting the **SAR / FCC safety compliance** line ($\leq 1.6$ W/kg) in any biomedical question — this is a guaranteed 2-mark safety sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Core need:** Shift from *reactive cure* → *proactive prevention* through continuous, real-time, non-invasive monitoring.
- ✅ **Four pillars of need:** Clinical (early detection), Technological (MEMS + BLE + AI), Socio-economic (cost + aging), Pandemic-readiness.
- ✅ **Three layers of justification** structure is the *gold-standard* 14-mark answer skeleton.
- ✅ **Binomial detection probability derivation** (Section 3.1) is the single most important quantitative result — memorize the $81\times$ factor.
- ✅ **Battery-life formula:** $T_{bat} = \dfrac{C_{bat} V_{nom}}{P_{sensor} + P_{proc} + P_{TX} \cdot \text{DC}}$.
- ✅ **SAR safety limit:** $\text{SAR} \leq 1.6$ W/kg (FCC) and $\leq 2.0$ W/kg (ICNIRP, 10 g avg).
- ✅ **Nyquist sampling:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ → ECG: ≥ 500 Hz, EMG: ≥ 1000 Hz, EEG: ≥ 250 Hz.
- ✅ **Power draw typical:** Sensor 5 mW, MCU 15 mW, BLE TX 20 mW (5% duty cycle) → ~21 mW total average.
- ✅ **Key form factors:** Wrist-watch, chest-patch, headband, hearable, smart-textile, skin-tattoo, contact-lens.
- ✅ **IoT architecture flow:** Sensor → AFE → MCU → BLE → Gateway → Cloud → Dashboard → Feedback.
- ✅ **Closed-loop feedback** is mandatory in every block diagram drawn (autonomous therapy = wearable + actuator).
- ✅ **Standards to mention:** ISO 10993 (biocompatibility), IEEE 11073 (interoperability), HL7 FHIR (data exchange), GDPR/HIPAA (privacy).
- ✅ **Mnemonic for features:** "U-L-L-W-R-D-B-H" = Unobtrusive, Lightweight, Low-power, Wireless, Real-time, Data-secure, Biocompatible, High-accuracy.
- ✅ **Examiner's trick question:** *"Is a Holter monitor a wearable?"* — Strictly NO in the modern sense; it is *portable ambulatory* but not *continuous unobtrusive*. Differentiate clearly.
- ✅ **The 5G + TinyML + Energy Harvesting trio** is the future-facing trio KTU loves to test as a 3-mark "future scope" sub-question.

> **End of Module 3 – Topic 1 Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of WHMS: Home Healthcare
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications of WHMS: Home Healthcare

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wireless Health Monitoring System (WHMS)** dedicated to **Home Healthcare** is a distributed, patient-centric Internet of Things (IoT) architecture that enables the **continuous, non-intrusive, and real-time acquisition, transmission, and analysis of physiological and contextual bio-signals** from an individual residing in a domestic (non-clinical) environment, transmitting the data over short-range or wide-area wireless networks to caregivers, clinicians, or cloud-based decision support systems.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEEBT831 – IoT & Biomedical Applications)**, Home Healthcare under WHMS is defined as the *deployment of body-area, ambient, and environmental biomedical sensors integrated through an IoT gateway, allowing chronic disease management, elderly fall detection, post-operative recovery tracking, and remote consultation outside the walls of a hospital.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Home Healthcare is positioned as the *primary civilian application* of WHMS. It demonstrates how the convergence of **WBAN (Wireless Body Area Network)**, **edge computing**, and **cloud analytics** produces a closed-loop monitoring environment in the patient's own home.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a traditional hospital ICU room: it is full of monitors, cables, and a nurse station where every beep is observed. Now, **imagine unplugging those monitors from the bed and embedding them into a wristwatch, a chest patch, a toilet seat, and a bedroom ceiling**, while the nurse station moves to a smartphone and a cloud dashboard. That is **Home Healthcare via WHMS**.

**The "Smart Home + Smart Body" Analogy:**
- **Smart Body layer** → Wearable sensors (smartwatch, ECG patch, smart inhaler).
- **Smart Home layer** → Ambient sensors (motion detector on the floor, pressure mat on the bed, humidity sensor in the bathroom).
- **Smart Pipe** → The IoT gateway (router/hub) that ferries data.
- **Smart Brain** → Cloud analytics + doctor's tablet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Reminders (Must Memorize for KTU):**
> - **Sampling Rate (Fs):** ECG $\geq 250$ Hz, SpO₂ $\geq 100$ Hz, Body Temperature $0.1$–$1$ Hz, Accelerometer $50$–$100$ Hz.
> - **Standard Wireless Bands used:** **2.4 GHz** (Wi-Fi/Bluetooth), **868/915 MHz** (ZigBee/LoRa), **402–405 MHz** (Medical Implant Communication Service – MICS).

### 1.3 Why Home Healthcare? — Driving Forces

1. **Aging Population** — Globally, the proportion of people aged **60+** is rising; chronic care is shifting from hospital to home.
2. **Cost Reduction** — A home-day of monitoring costs a small fraction of an ICU bed-day.
3. **Pandemic Acceleration** — COVID-19 established *remote patient monitoring (RPM)* as the new standard of care.
4. **IoT Maturity** — Cheap, low-power radios (BLE 5.x, LoRaWAN) and cloud platforms (AWS IoT, Azure Health) make deployment viable.
5. **Patient Comfort & Compliance** — Continuous monitoring at home improves quality of life and adherence to therapy.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Layer Architecture of Home Healthcare WHMS

A KTU-favoured way to answer this question is to present the system as **four logical tiers**:

**Layer 1 – Perception / Sensing Layer**
- *Wearable biomedical sensors:* ECG electrodes, PPG (photoplethysmography) for SpO₂, thermometer, glucometer, continuous blood pressure cuff, accelerometer/gyroscope (for fall detection), EMG patch.
- *Ambient sensors:* PIR motion sensor, acoustic sensor (cough/snore), gas sensor (CO₂ for sleep apnea), smart floor mat, smart toilet (for urine analysis), RFID-tagged pillbox.
- *Actuators:* Smart insulin pump, vibrating pill dispenser, smart bed (adjustable tilt), emergency beacon.

**Layer 2 – Network / Communication Layer**
- Short-range: **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE), ZigBee, NFC** — for body-area links.
- Mid-range: **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), Ethernet** — for home gateway.
- Long-range: **LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, 4G/5G** — for connecting rural homes to hospitals.

**Layer 3 – Processing / Edge–Fog Layer**
- **Edge device** (smartphone, Raspberry Pi, dedicated hub) performs local filtering, noise removal, feature extraction, and **anomaly detection**.
- **Fog node** aggregates data from multiple sensors and runs ML inference locally to reduce cloud dependency.

**Layer 4 – Application / Cloud Layer**
- **Cloud Server** (AWS HealthLake, Azure FHIR) stores Electronic Health Records (EHR).
- **Decision Support System (DSS)** uses ML/DL models (LSTM for ECG arrhythmia, CNN for cough classification).
- **End-user Apps** for patient, family, and clinician — with alert push notifications.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Layer:** Each layer solves a specific problem — sensing (acquisition), network (transport), edge (latency + privacy), cloud (scalability + analytics). Removing any layer breaks the closed-loop design expected by KTU examiners.

### 2.2 Key Functional Modules of a Home Healthcare WHMS

| Module | Function | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Acquisition** | Sample bio-signals at proper Fs | Higher Fs = better fidelity but more power & bandwidth |
| **Signal Conditioning** | Amplify, filter, denoise | Analog vs Digital filtering |
| **Local Storage** | Buffer when connectivity is lost | SRAM size vs power |
| **Encryption (AES-128/256)** | HIPAA/GDPR compliance | Throughput vs security |
| **Alert Engine** | Threshold or ML-based | False positives vs missed events |
| **Gateway Sync** | Forward packets to cloud | Latency vs battery |
| **User Interface** | Visualization for patient/clinician | Usability vs information density |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **frequently tested** in KTU University Exams for this module. Memorize the symbols, units, and the boundary conditions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampling Frequency (Nyquist) | $F_s \geq 2 \cdot F_{max}$ | $F_{max}$ = highest frequency component | Hz | ECG: $\geq 500$ Hz clinical, $250$ Hz WHMS |
| 2 | Data Rate (bit/s) | $R_b = F_s \cdot N \cdot b$ | $N$ = channels, $b$ = bits/sample | bps | ECG 3-lead @ 250 Hz, 12-bit: $9$ kbps |
| 3 | Battery Life | $T_{life} = \dfrac{C_{mAh} \cdot V_{bat}}{P_{avg}}$ | $C$ = capacity, $P$ = avg power draw | hours | Wearable: 7–14 days |
| 4 | Power Consumption | $P = V \cdot I$ | Voltage × Current | W | BLE: $\sim 10$ mW peak, $\sim 1$ μW avg |
| 5 | Free-Space Path Loss (Friis) | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right)$ | $d$ = distance (m), $f$ = Hz | dB | 2.4 GHz @ 10 m $\approx 60$ dB |
| 6 | Received Power (Friis) | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | $G_t, G_r$ = antenna gains | W | — |
| 7 | SNR | $SNR_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB | ECG: $\geq 20$ dB for clean morphology |
| 8 | Shannon Capacity | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $B$ = bandwidth (Hz) | bps | — |
| 9 | Compression Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | — | unitless | Lossless ECG: 2–3× ; Lossy: 10–20× |
| 10 | Body-Area Path Loss (CM3) | $PL(d) = a \cdot \log_{10}(d) + b + N$ | $a, b$ = env. constants, $N$ = Gaussian noise | dB | $a \approx 18$–$34$ in-body |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In every row above, absolute-value bars and conditional signs have been rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chronic Disease Management:** Continuous glucose monitoring + insulin pump = **closed-loop artificial pancreas** (e.g., Medtronic 780G).
- **Elderly Fall Detection:** Accelerometer + barometer worn on waist → BLE to smartphone → caregiver SMS.
- **Cardiac Rehab:** Post-MI patients wear ECG patch at home; cardiologist reviews weekly.
- **Sleep Apnea:** Smart bed sensor + SpO₂ ring → CPAP auto-titration.
- **Mental Health:** Voice/speech analysis in elderly living alone for early dementia detection.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Data Rate Required for a Home WHMS Node

**Problem Setup:** A patient at home wears a 3-lead ECG sensor (sampled at 250 Hz, 12-bit ADC) and a tri-axial accelerometer (sampled at 50 Hz, 16-bit ADC). Both stream to a BLE gateway. Calculate the total uplink data rate.

**Step 1 — Identify the per-channel bit rate.**
For any sampled signal, the bit rate is the product of sampling frequency, number of channels, and bit depth.

$$
R_{b,\text{channel}} = F_s \cdot N_{ch} \cdot b
$$

**Step 2 — Compute ECG contribution.**
ECG has 3 leads, $F_s = 250$ Hz, $b = 12$ bits.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{b,\text{ECG}} &= 250 \cdot 3 \cdot 12 \\
&= 750 \cdot 12 \\
&= 9\,000 \text{ bits/s} = 9 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute accelerometer contribution.**
Accelerometer has 3 axes, $F_s = 50$ Hz, $b = 16$ bits.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{b,\text{ACC}} &= 50 \cdot 3 \cdot 16 \\
&= 150 \cdot 16 \\
&= 2\,400 \text{ bits/s} = 2.4 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum and add protocol overhead.**
Assume a BLE 5.0 link-layer overhead of roughly **30 %** (acknowledgements, headers, encryption padding).

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{b,\text{total,raw}} &= 9.0 + 2.4 = 11.4 \text{ kbps} \\[4pt]
R_{b,\text{total,eff}} &= 11.4 \times 1.30 = 14.82 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to KB/min for cloud storage estimation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bytes per minute} &= \dfrac{14\,820 \text{ bits/s} \cdot 60 \text{ s}}{8 \text{ bits/byte}} \\
&= \dfrac{889\,200}{8} \\
&= 111\,150 \text{ bytes/min} \approx 108.5 \text{ KB/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Daily storage estimate (24 hours of monitoring).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Storage} &= 108.5 \text{ KB/min} \cdot 60 \cdot 24 \\
&= 108.5 \cdot 1\,440 \\
&\approx 156\,240 \text{ KB/day} \approx 152.6 \text{ MB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion for the KTU answer:** A single home WHMS patient can generate **≈ 150 MB/day** of raw data — hence the *necessity* of edge compression, downsampling, and event-driven transmission (only sending abnormal episodes).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery Life of a Wearable Sensor Node

**Problem Setup:** A wearable SpO₂ patch is powered by a **3.7 V, 250 mAh Li-Po battery**. The node's average current draw is **$1.5$ mA** in active mode and **$5$ μA** in sleep mode. Duty cycle: active $5$ % of the time, sleep $95$ %. Estimate battery life in days.

**Step 1 — Compute the effective average current.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{avg} &= D \cdot I_{active} + (1-D) \cdot I_{sleep} \\
&= 0.05 \cdot 1.5\text{ mA} + 0.95 \cdot 0.005\text{ mA} \\
&= 0.075 + 0.00475 \\
&= 0.07975 \text{ mA} \approx 79.75 \text{ μA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the battery-life equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{life} &= \dfrac{C_{mAh}}{I_{avg,mA}} \\
&= \dfrac{250}{0.07975} \\
&\approx 3\,134.8 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to days.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{life,\text{days}} &= \dfrac{3\,134.8}{24} \approx 130.6 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A well-designed duty-cycled wearable can last **≈ 4 months** on a small Li-Po cell, satisfying the *recharge-cycle comfort* expected in home deployments.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Friis Free-Space Link Budget for Home Gateway

**Problem Setup:** A home gateway transmits at **$P_t = 10$ dBm** with unity-gain antennas ($G_t = G_r = 0$ dBi) to a wearable sensor inside the house at **$f = 2.45$ GHz**, $d = 8$ m away. Compute received power and verify link margin against a receiver sensitivity of **$-90$ dBm**.

**Step 1 — Wavelength.**

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2.45 \times 10^{9}} \approx 0.1224 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — FSPL.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FSPL &= 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right) \\
&= 20\log_{10}(8) + 20\log_{10}(2.45 \times 10^{9}) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^{8}}\right) \\
&= 18.06 + 187.78 + (-147.56) \\
&\approx 58.28 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Received power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{r,\text{dBm}} &= P_{t,\text{dBm}} + G_t + G_r - FSPL \\
&= 10 + 0 + 0 - 58.28 \\
&\approx -48.28 \text{ dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Link margin.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Margin} &= P_r - \text{Sensitivity} \\
&= -48.28 - (-90) \\
&= 41.72 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A massive **41.7 dB link margin** exists. The extra margin compensates for **wall attenuation, body shadowing, and multipath fading** typical in a real home — confirming BLE 2.4 GHz is a robust choice for in-home WHMS.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Communication Technologies for Home WHMS

| Parameter | BLE 5.0 | ZigBee | Wi-Fi (802.11n) | LoRaWAN |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency Band | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz / 868 MHz | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 868 / 915 MHz |
| Range (indoor) | 10–30 m | 10–30 m | 30–50 m | 1–3 km |
| Data Rate | 2 Mbps | 250 kbps | 150 Mbps | 0.3–50 kbps |
| Power | Very Low | Low | High | Very Low |
| Best For | Wearable | Meshed home sensors | Cloud gateway uplink | Rural/elderly home |
| Topology | Star / Mesh | Mesh | Star | Star-of-stars |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Architecture of a Home Healthcare WHMS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[ECG Patch Sensor] --> H1[BLE Body Area Network]
    A2[SpO2 Ring Sensor] --> H1
    A3[BP Cuff Sensor] --> H1
    A4[Glucose Monitor] --> H1
    A5[Accelerometer Fall Detector] --> H1

    H1 --> G1[IoT Home Gateway Smartphone or Hub]

    B1[Ambient PIR Motion Sensor] --> G1
    B2[Smart Floor Mat] --> G1
    B3[Air Quality and Gas Sensor] --> G1
    B4[Smart Pillbox RFID] --> G1

    G1 --> C1{Edge Analytics Anomaly Detection}
    C1 -->|Normal| C2[Local Cache and Log]
    C1 -->|Abnormal| C3[Trigger Alert Engine]

    C3 --> D1[Push Notification to Caregiver]
    C3 --> D2[SMS to Doctor]
    C3 --> D3[Voice Call Emergency Services]

    C2 --> E1[Cloud FHIR Server EHR Storage]
    E1 --> E2[ML Decision Support LSTM and CNN Models]
    E2 --> E3[Clinician Dashboard Web and Mobile]
    E2 --> E4[Patient Mobile App Reports and Trends]
    E3 --> E2
    E4 --> E2
```

### 4.2 Data Flow Sequence — ECG Anomaly Event

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as ECG Patch Sensor
    participant G as Home Gateway
    participant E as Edge Analytics
    participant C as Cloud Server
    participant D as Doctor Dashboard
    participant N as Caregiver Notification

    S->>G: Stream ECG at 250 Hz, 12 bit
    G->>E: Forward 1 second window
    E->>E: Bandpass filter, R peak detect
    E->>E: Compute HRV and detect arrhythmia
    alt Normal Beat
        E->>C: Store compressed 1 min summary
    else Abnormal Beat Detected
        E->>C: Upload full 10 second episode
        E->>N: Push alert with severity level
        C->>D: Flag patient record for review
    end
    D->>C: Doctor reviews and updates care plan
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Layer View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Layer 1 Perception and Sensing Layer] --> L2[Layer 2 Network and Communication Layer]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Edge and Fog Processing Layer]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Cloud and Application Layer]
    L4 --> U1[Patient]
    L4 --> U2[Family Caregiver]
    L4 --> U3[Hospital Clinician]
    L4 --> U4[Emergency Services]
```

> **Reading Tip for KTU:** When asked to "explain the architecture of a Home Healthcare WHMS," always **labour all four layers** and explicitly state **which hardware falls into which layer** — examiners award marks for layered thinking, not just listing devices.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define Wireless Health Monitoring System (WHMS) for Home Healthcare. List any four wearable sensors used.*

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**
A Wireless Health Monitoring System (WHMS) for Home Healthcare is an IoT-enabled framework that continuously acquires, transmits, and analyses a patient's physiological and environmental parameters in a domestic setting, enabling remote clinical supervision. The four primary wearable sensors are:
1. **ECG patch** for cardiac activity.
2. **PPG / SpO₂ ring** for oxygen saturation and pulse rate.
3. **Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM)** for diabetes management.
4. **Tri-axial accelerometer** for fall detection and gait analysis.

These sensors form a Wireless Body Area Network (WBAN) that forwards data to a home gateway for edge and cloud analytics.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between edge computing and cloud computing in the context of a Home Healthcare WHMS. State two advantages of edge processing.*

**Model Answer (≈ 100 words):**

| Aspect | Edge Computing | Cloud Computing |
|---|---|---|
| Location | At the home gateway / wearable | Remote data centre |
| Latency | **< 10 ms** | 50–500 ms |
| Privacy | Data stays local | Data leaves the home |
| Compute Power | Limited (MCU class) | Virtually unlimited |
| Internet | Not mandatory | Mandatory |

**Two advantages of edge processing:**
1. **Reduced latency** → enables real-time alert for cardiac arrest.
2. **Bandwidth saving** → only abnormal episodes are uploaded, not the entire raw stream.
3. **Privacy preservation** → raw bio-signal never leaves the home.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Module 3 Part B questions carry an *internal choice*. You must answer either **Question A** *or* **Question B**. Each carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the four-layer architecture of a Home Healthcare WHMS. List the function of each layer.

**Model Solution:**

A Home Healthcare WHMS is structured into **four cooperating layers**:

**Layer 1 – Perception / Sensing Layer:**
This is the data acquisition tier containing biomedical sensors (ECG, SpO₂, BP, temperature, accelerometer) and ambient sensors (motion, gas, smart mat). The function is to convert physical/physiological phenomena into electrical signals, perform analogue conditioning, and digitize via an ADC.

**Layer 2 – Network / Communication Layer:**
This layer handles the *transport* of digitized bio-signals. Body-area links use BLE/ZigBee; in-home backhaul uses Wi-Fi; remote uplink uses 4G/5G/LoRaWAN. Protocols like MQTT and CoAP ensure lightweight, reliable delivery.

**Layer 3 – Edge / Fog Processing Layer:**
A local gateway (smartphone or hub) performs *denoising, feature extraction, and anomaly detection* using embedded ML. This enables real-time decision-making without depending on the cloud.

**Layer 4 – Cloud / Application Layer:**
Stores longitudinal EHR data, runs heavy deep-learning inference (LSTM, CNN), and provides clinician/patient dashboards. Generates trend reports, predicts exacerbations, and triggers emergency response.

> *Block diagram is shown in Section 4.1 of these notes — present the same 4-block Mermaid-style block diagram in your answer script.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** An ECG sensor samples a 3-lead signal at 250 Hz with 12-bit resolution. The patient wears it 24 hours a day. Calculate (i) raw data rate in kbps, (ii) total data generated per day in MB, and (iii) how much storage is saved per day if the data is compressed with a ratio of 10:1.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Raw data rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_b &= F_s \cdot N \cdot b \\
&= 250 \cdot 3 \cdot 12 \\
&= 9\,000 \text{ bps} = 9 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Daily data volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bits/day} &= 9\,000 \cdot 60 \cdot 60 \cdot 24 \\
&= 9\,000 \cdot 86\,400 \\
&= 777\,600\,000 \text{ bits/day} \\[4pt]
\text{MB/day} &= \dfrac{777\,600\,000}{8 \times 1\,024 \times 1\,024} \\
&= \dfrac{777\,600\,000}{8\,388\,608} \\
&\approx 92.7 \text{ MB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Compressed size and saving:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compressed} &= \dfrac{92.7}{10} = 9.27 \text{ MB/day} \\[4pt]
\text{Saving} &= 92.7 - 9.27 = 83.43 \text{ MB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Raw rate = **9 kbps**; Daily raw = **92.7 MB**; After 10:1 compression = **9.27 MB**; Saving = **83.43 MB/day**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of the Wireless Body Area Network (WBAN) in Home Healthcare. Compare WBAN communication technologies (BLE, ZigBee, LoRa) using any four parameters.

**Model Solution:**

**Role of WBAN:**
A Wireless Body Area Network (WBAN) is a *network of low-power, miniaturized biomedical sensors placed on, around, or inside the human body* that cooperate to monitor vital signs. In a Home Healthcare setting, WBAN serves as the **first-mile data acquisition fabric** — capturing ECG, EMG, SpO₂, body temperature, motion, and bio-impedance continuously. It provides:
- *Mobility* — patient is not tethered to bedside monitors.
- *Continuous longitudinal data* — superior to episodic clinical measurements.
- *Closed-loop actuation* — insulin pump, neurostimulator.

**Comparison Table (4 parameters):**

| Parameter | BLE 5.0 | ZigBee | LoRaWAN |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz / 868 MHz | 868 / 915 MHz |
| Data Rate | 2 Mbps | 250 kbps | 0.3–50 kbps |
| Power Consumption | Very Low | Low | Ultra Low |
| Indoor Range | 10–30 m | 10–30 m | 1–3 km |
| Topology | Star / Mesh | Mesh | Star of stars |

**Inference for Home Healthcare:** BLE is preferred for wearable streaming; ZigBee for ambient meshed sensors; LoRa for rural homes with poor cellular coverage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wearable SpO₂ sensor node is powered by a **3.7 V, 200 mAh** battery. Active mode draws **3 mA**, sleep mode draws **8 μA**, and the duty cycle is **8 %** active. Calculate (i) average current, (ii) total battery life in hours, and (iii) the percentage improvement in life if the duty cycle is reduced to 4 %.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Average current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{avg} &= D \cdot I_{active} + (1-D) \cdot I_{sleep} \\
&= 0.08 \cdot 3\text{ mA} + 0.92 \cdot 0.008\text{ mA} \\
&= 0.2400 + 0.00736 \\
&= 0.24736 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Battery life (8 % duty):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= \dfrac{200 \text{ mAh}}{0.24736 \text{ mA}} \\
&\approx 808.5 \text{ hours} \approx 33.7 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Battery life at 4 % duty cycle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{avg,2} &= 0.04 \cdot 3 + 0.96 \cdot 0.008 \\
&= 0.1200 + 0.00768 \\
&= 0.12768 \text{ mA} \\[4pt]
T_2 &= \dfrac{200}{0.12768} \approx 1\,566.4 \text{ hours} \approx 65.3 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Percentage improvement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gain \%} &= \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{1\,566.4 - 808.5}{808.5} \times 100 \\
&\approx 93.7 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $I_{avg} = 0.247$ mA, life at 8 % duty ≈ **33.7 days**, life at 4 % duty ≈ **65.3 days**, improvement ≈ **93.7 %**.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Triggers in Home Healthcare / WHMS Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "IoMT" with "IoT":** IoMT (Internet of Medical Things) is a *medical-grade subset* — examiners want you to state that it is regulated (FDA, EU MDR) and HIPAA-compliant.
> 2. **Forgetting Units:** Writing "$F_s = 250$" without "Hz" loses one mark. Always include units in the final answer line.
> 3. **Mixing BLE and Bluetooth Classic:** BLE 5.0 is *not* the same as Bluetooth Classic — they have different power profiles. Examiners deduct for the mix-up.
> 4. **Skipping Privacy/Security Layer:** A full-mark answer on home WHMS *must* mention data encryption, GDPR/HIPAA, and consent management.
> 5. **Not labelling Mermaid/diagram arrows:** Unlabelled arrows in architecture diagrams fetch only partial marks — always write "BLE", "Wi-Fi", "MQTT" etc. on each link.
> 6. **Battery-life formula mistake:** Forgetting to convert μA → mA before dividing is a *fatal* error — examiners will mark the entire numerical wrong.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **WHMS for Home Healthcare** = IoT-based remote patient monitoring in domestic settings.
- ✅ **Four layers:** Sensing → Network → Edge/Fog → Cloud/Application.
- ✅ **Key wearable sensors:** ECG, SpO₂, BP, Glucose, Accelerometer, Temperature.
- ✅ **Ambient sensors:** PIR motion, gas, smart mat, smart pillbox.
- ✅ **Communication choices:** BLE 5.0 (2.4 GHz), ZigBee, Wi-Fi, LoRaWAN.
- ✅ **Nyquist rule:** $F_s \geq 2 F_{max}$ — ECG $\geq 250$ Hz.
- ✅ **Data rate formula:** $R_b = F_s \cdot N \cdot b$ → always in *bits per second*.
- ✅ **Battery life formula:** $T = C/I_{avg}$ — average current must be in *mA*.
- ✅ **Friis path loss** governs 2.4 GHz home link budgets.
- ✅ **Edge processing** is preferred for latency-critical alerts (falls, arrhythmia).
- ✅ **Cloud layer** handles long-term storage, ML inference, and clinician dashboards.
- ✅ **Standard daily data volume** of a single home WHMS patient ≈ **90–150 MB/day** raw.
- ✅ **Compression ratios** of **10:1** (lossy ECG) are typical and save **~83 MB/day**.
- ✅ **Duty cycling** from 8 % → 4 % can **double** the battery life (~94 % gain).
- ✅ **Compliance:** HIPAA (US), GDPR (EU), DISHA (India) — mention in any home-care answer.
- ✅ **Actuators close the loop:** insulin pump, smart bed, vibrating pill dispenser.
- ✅ **Draw a labelled block diagram** for any 7-mark architecture question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sports/Fitness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Sports/Fitness # 🏅 MODULE 3: IoT IN SPORTS & FITNESS — Core Technical Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** > *IoT in Sports/Fitness* refers to the integrated ecosystem of **wearable sensors**, **smart connected equipment**, **biometric monitoring systems**, and **cloud-based analytics platforms** that collectively capture, transmit, and analyze physiological, biomechanical, and environmental data of athletes and fitness enthusiasts to optimize performance, prevent injury, and personalize training regimes. ## 1.1 What is IoT in Sports/Fitness? At its core, **IoT in Sports/Fitness** is the convergence of: 1. **Wearable Technology** — devices worn on the body (smartwatches, fitness bands, smart clothing, heart-rate straps). 2. **Ambient Sensors** — devices embedded in equipment, gyms, playing fields, or stadiums. 3. **Wireless Communication** — Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE), Wi-Fi, NFC, ANT+, LoRa. 4. **Cloud/Edge Analytics** — AI/ML pipelines that interpret the raw data. 5. **Feedback Loops** — real-time visual, haptic, or auditory cues back to the user. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric Definitions (Highlighted for Exam):** > - **HR (Heart Rate):** Beats per minute (**bpm**). Resting: **60–100 bpm**. Athletic: **40–60 bpm**. > - **VO₂ Max:** Maximal oxygen uptake in **mL/kg/min**. Elite athletes: **>70**. Average: **35–40**. > - **HRV (Heart Rate Variability):** Variation in time between heartbeats, measured in **ms**. > - **Cadence:** Steps per minute (**spm**) for running; revolutions per minute (**rpm**) for cycling. > - **GPS Accuracy:** Standard民用 GPS ≈ **2.5 m CEP**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Smart Coach on Your Wrist" > [!TIP] > **Conceptual Analogy:** > Imagine you hire a personal coach who follows you **24/7** — measuring your heartbeat while you sleep, counting your steps during a walk, detecting your posture during a deadlift, and warning you when your form risks injury. Now, instead of a human, imagine this coach is a **tiny electronic guardian** stitched into your shirt, embedded in your shoes, and listening through your skin. That guardian is an **IoT wearable**. It "talks" to your phone, which "talks" to the cloud, where massive data centers compare your performance against millions of other athletes and whisper back, *"Slow down — your HRV is dropping, you need recovery."* ## 1.3 Why IoT Matters in Sports (The Five Pillars) | Pillar | Description | Real-World Example | |---|---|---| | **Performance Optimization** | Quantifies training load and recovery | Strava, Garmin Connect | | **Injury Prevention** | Detects biomechanical anomalies in real-time | Catapult Vector (NFL) | | **Personalized Training** | Adapts workouts to individual biometrics | Whoop, Apple Fitness+ | | **Fan Engagement** | Real-time stats broadcast to spectators | Hawk-Eye in Tennis | | **Medical Monitoring** | Continuous ECG for cardiac athletes | KardiaMobile | ## 1.4 Key IoT Components in a Sports System 1. **Sensor Layer:** Accelerometer, Gyroscope, Magnetometer (IMU), PPG, ECG, EMG, Temperature, GPS, Galvanic Skin Response (GSR). 2. **Edge/Processing Layer:** Microcontroller (e.g., **nRF52840**, **STM32**), on-device AI (TinyML). 3. **Communication Layer:** BLE 5.0, Wi-Fi 6, ANT+, NFC. 4. **Cloud Layer:** AWS IoT, Azure Digital Twins, Google Fit. 5. **Application Layer:** Mobile app dashboards, AR/VR coaching, coach portals. # ⚙️ DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Architectural Layers of an IoT Sports System > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes a **5-layer IoT reference architecture**. For Sports/Fitness, this maps as follows: ### Layer 1: **Perception / Sensor Layer** This is the *eyes and ears* of the system. It captures raw physical signals. - **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU):** Combines a 3-axis accelerometer, 3-axis gyroscope, and 3-axis magnetometer. Used for **activity recognition** (walking, running, cycling, swimming). - **Photoplethysmography (PPG) Sensor:** Measures blood volume changes using green/infrared LEDs on the skin. **Derived metric:** Heart Rate, SpO₂. - **Electrocardiography (ECG) Sensor:** Measures electrical activity of the heart via skin electrodes. **Gold standard** for HR and HRV. - **GPS Module:** Provides location, speed, distance, altitude using satellite trilateration. - **Strain Gauge / Pressure Sensor:** Embedded in shoes or racket grips to measure force. - **Thermistor:** Skin/body temperature. ### Layer 2: **Network / Communication Layer** - **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE 5.0):** Range ≈ **200 m**, data rate ≈ **2 Mbps**, ultra-low power. **Default for wearables.** - **ANT+:** Proprietary protocol by Garmin. Used for **multi-device sensor networks** (e.g., HR strap + power meter + foot pod all talking to a single watch). - **Wi-Fi (802.11ax/Wi-Fi 6):** Used for high-bandwidth applications like live video in smart gyms. - **LoRaWAN:** Long-range (**2–15 km**), low data rate. Used in large outdoor sports complexes or golf courses. - **NFC:** Tap-to-pair, payment at stadiums. ### Layer 3: **Processing / Edge Layer** - Microcontrollers run **pre-processing algorithms** to reduce noise and extract features. - **Edge AI / TinyML:** On-device inference (e.g., detecting a fall, recognizing a tennis serve). - Common boards: **ESP32**, **nRF52840**, **Raspberry Pi Pico W**. ### Layer 4: **Cloud / Storage Layer** - **Time-series databases** (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) store sensor streams. - **Data lake** for raw historical data. - **AI/ML pipelines** for predictive analytics (e.g., injury risk prediction). ### Layer 5: **Application Layer** - Mobile apps (iOS/Android), web dashboards, AR/VR interfaces, smart-watches, electronic scoreboards. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Ready) > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU NOTE:** In KTU board exams, you may be asked to derive or apply the following formulas. Memorize the **form**, **variables**, and **units**. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Heart Rate from PPG** | $HR = \frac{60}{T_{peak}}$ | $T_{peak}$ = peak-to-peak interval (seconds); HR in **bpm** | Wearable pulse oximeters | | 2 | **HRV (RMSSD)** | $RMSSD = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1}(RR_i - RR_{i+1})^2}$ | $RR_i$ = R-R intervals in **ms**; $N$ = number of beats | Recovery & stress monitoring | | 3 | **Distance (GPS Haversine)** | $d = 2R \cdot \arcsin\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1\cos\phi_2\sin^2\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)}\right)$ | $R$ = Earth radius (**6371 km**); $\phi, \lambda$ = lat/long in radians | Running/cycling trackers | | 4 | **Speed** | $v = \frac{\Delta d}{\Delta t}$ | $v$ in **m/s** or **km/h** | Speed display in smartwatches | | 5 | **Calorie Burn (Keytel)** | $Cal = (0.6309 \cdot HR + 0.1988 \cdot W + 0.2017 \cdot A - 55.0969 + 0.1863 \cdot t) \cdot \frac{60}{t}$ | $HR$ in bpm; $W$ = weight (kg); $A$ = age (years); $t$ = time (min) | Fitness band calorie counters | | 6 | **VO₂ Max (Uth–Sørensen)** | $VO_2 = \frac{HR_{max}}{HR_{rest}} \cdot 15$ | $HR_{max}$ = max heart rate; $HR_{rest}$ = resting HR | Cardio fitness scoring | | 7 | **Max Heart Age Formula** | $HR_{max} = 220 - A$ | $A$ = age in years | Target zone calculation | | 8 | **Karvonen Target HR** | $THR = (HR_{max} - HR_{rest}) \cdot k + HR_{rest}$ | $k$ = intensity factor (**0.5–0.85**) | Personalized training zones | | 9 | **Pace** | $Pace = \frac{t}{d}$ | $t$ in minutes; $d$ in km → **min/km** | Running apps | | 10 | **Step Length** | $SL = \frac{h \cdot K}{N}$ | $h$ = height; $K$ = gender factor (0.415 male, 0.413 female); $N$ = steps | Pedometer calibration | | 11 | **Ground Reaction Force** | $F = m \cdot a$ | $m$ = body mass (kg); $a$ = acceleration (m/s²) | Force-sensitive shoe insoles | | 12 | **Cadence (running)** | $Cad = \frac{2 \cdot N}{T}$ | $N$ = number of steps; $T$ = time (min) | Running efficiency analysis | | 13 | **Body Fat % (BMI proxy)** | $BMI = \frac{W}{H^2}$ | $W$ = weight (kg);
