```mermaid
    G --> H["Back to idle"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **actuation delay (typically 5–15 ms)** and **release delay (5–10 ms)** are non-negligible in time-critical medical applications such as defibrillator discharge timing or syringe pump actuation. For sub-millisecond switching, a **Solid State Relay (SSR)** is used instead.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*  
**Define a relay. Why is it considered an essential interface in IoT-based biomedical systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **relay** is an electromechanical switch that uses an electromagnet to mechanically operate one or more sets of contacts, enabling a low-power control signal to switch a high-power load while providing **galvanic isolation** between the two circuits.

In IoT-based biomedical systems, the relay is essential because:
1. **Voltage/Current Mismatch:** Microcontrollers operate at **3.3 V / 5 V DC** with limited sourcing current (~20 mA), but medical loads (heaters, pumps, lamps) require **230 V AC** and several amperes — a relay bridges this gap.
2. **Patient Safety:** The galvanic isolation prevents dangerous mains transients from reaching patient-connected sensors and the controller.
3. **Bidirectional AC Switching:** Relays can switch both AC and DC loads, unlike many solid-state devices that are polarity-sensitive.
*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for justification)*

---

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*  
**What is the function of a flyback diode across a relay coil? What happens if it is omitted?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **flyback diode** (typically **1N4007**) is connected in **reverse-bias** across the relay coil. When the driving transistor switches OFF, the inductor's collapsing magnetic field induces a large reverse voltage spike given by $V_{spike} = -L \cdot di/dt$, which can reach several hundred to several thousand volts. The flyback diode provides a freewheeling path for the coil current, clamping the voltage to approximately **−0.7 V** and allowing the current to decay safely through the coil resistance.

If omitted, the high-voltage spike **destroys the driving transistor** (or MOSFET) and may reset or damage the microcontroller, potentially causing a hazardous failure in a medical device.  
*(1 mark for function + 1 mark for consequence + 1 mark for numerical/circuit context)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

*[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024, Module 3]*  

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the interfacing of a **5 V SPDT relay** with an **ESP32 microcontroller** to control a **230 V AC lamp**. Include the driver transistor, base resistor, and flyback diode in your diagram. Mention the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A relay has coil resistance $R = 100\ \Omega$ and inductance $L = 80$ mH. It is energized by a 5 V DC source through a transistor switch. Calculate:  
  (i) The steady-state coil current.  
  (ii) The time constant of the coil.  
  (iii) The time required for the current to reach **90%** of its final value.  
  (iv) The peak back-EMF if the transistor is switched off in **2 µs**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Circuit Interfacing Diagram and Explanation (7 marks):**

The complete interfacing circuit consists of three sections:

**1. Control Section (ESP32 side):**
- The **ESP32 GPIO pin** (e.g., GPIO26) outputs **3.3 V HIGH** when it needs to turn ON the lamp.
- A current-limiting **base resistor $R_B$ = 1 kΩ** is connected between the GPIO and the base of the transistor to limit base current to a safe value: $I_B = (3.3 - 0.7) / 1000 \approx 2.6$ mA.

**2. Driver Section:**
- An **NPN transistor BC547** is used in common-emitter configuration.
- When the GPIO is HIGH, the transistor enters **saturation**, allowing collector current to flow through the relay coil.
- A **flyback diode 1N4007** is connected across the coil in **reverse-bias** (cathode to VCC, anode to collector) to suppress back-EMF.

**3. Load Section (230 V AC side):**
- The **common (COM) terminal** of the SPDT relay is connected to the **AC Live wire (230 V, 50 Hz)**.
- The **Normally Open (NO) terminal** is connected to one terminal of the **lamp**.
- The other terminal of the lamp returns to the **AC Neutral**.
- When the coil energizes, the armature moves, COM connects to NO, and the lamp receives full mains voltage.

**Role of each component:**

| Component | Role |
|-----------|------|
| ESP32 GPIO | Generates control signal (3.3 V) |
| $R_B$ (1 kΩ) | Limits base current, protects GPIO |
| BC547 | Current amplifier; switches coil ON/OFF |
| 1N4007 | Suppresses back-EMF spike |
| Relay coil | Generates magnetic field for actuation |
| SPDT contacts | Switches the AC mains to the load |

*(Mark distribution: 3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for explanation, 1 mark for component table, 1 mark for safety note.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $R_{coil} = 100\ \Omega$, $L = 80$ mH $= 80 \times 10^{-3}$ H, $V = 5$ V DC, $\Delta t = 2\ \mu$s.

**(i) Steady-state coil current:**

$$I_\infty = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{5}{100} = 0.05\ \text{A} = 50\ \text{mA}$$

**(ii) Time constant of the coil:**

$$\tau = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{80 \times 10^{-3}}{100} = 0.8 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 0.8\ \text{ms}$$

**(iii) Time to reach 90% of final value:**

The current equation is:

$$i(t) = I_\infty \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

Setting $i(t) = 0.9 \cdot I_\infty$:

$$0.9 = 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \implies e^{-t/\tau} = 0.1$$

$$t = -\tau \cdot \ln(0.1) = \tau \cdot \ln(10) = 0.8 \times 2.3026 = 1.842\ \text{ms}$$

**(iv) Peak back-EMF when switched off in 2 µs:**

At the moment of switching off, the coil current is at $I_\infty = 50$ mA, and this current decays to zero in $\Delta t = 2\ \mu$s.

$$V_{spike} = L \cdot \frac{di}{dt} = L \cdot \frac{I_\infty}{\Delta t}$$

$$V_{spike} = 80 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{0.05}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 80 \times 10^{-3} \times 25{,}000$$

$$\boxed{V_{spike} = 2000\ \text{V} = 2\ \text{kV}}$$

This **2 kV spike** confirms why a flyback diode is absolutely essential — a typical BC547 has a $V_{CEO}$ of only **45 V** and would be instantly destroyed.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for each sub-part with correct formula, 1 mark each for accurate substitution, 1 mark for final answer with units, 1 mark for engineering conclusion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

*(a)* Explain the **construction and working of an electromagnetic relay** with a labeled diagram. Differentiate between **NO (Normally Open)** and **NC (Normally Closed)** contacts. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Compare **electromechanical relays (EMR)** and **solid-state relays (SSR)** in terms of construction, switching speed, lifespan, isolation, and suitability for biomedical applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution Outline for Question B**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

A relay consists of:
- **Core:** Soft iron core to concentrate magnetic flux.
- **Coil:** Insulated copper wire wound around the core (turns N).
- **Armature:** Movable iron lever attracted by the energized coil.
- **Spring:** Returns armature to default position when coil is de-energized.
- **Contacts:** Common (COM), Normally Open (NO), Normally Closed (NC).
- **Yoke:** Completes the magnetic circuit.

**Working:** When current $I$ flows through the coil, MMF $= N \cdot I$ produces flux $\Phi$ in the core. The flux creates an attractive force $F = \mu_0 N^2 I^2 A / (2g^2)$ on the armature. When $F$ exceeds the spring tension, the armature pivots, breaking the NC contact and making the NO contact. On de-energization, the spring restores the original state.

**NO vs NC:**
- **NO contact** is open in the default (unpowered) state; closes when the coil is energized.
- **NC contact** is closed in the default state; opens when the coil is energized.

*(3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for working, 1 mark for force equation, 1 mark for NO/NC distinction.)*

---

**(b) EMR vs SSR Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Electromechanical Relay (EMR) | Solid-State Relay (SSR) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Switching element | Physical moving contacts | TRIAC, SCR, or MOSFET |
| Switching speed | 5–15 ms (slow) | < 1 ms (very fast) |
| Mechanical lifespan | ~10⁵–10⁷ operations | > 10⁹ operations |
| Contact wear / arcing | Yes (eventual failure) | No (no contacts) |
| Isolation method | Magnetic + physical gap | Optical (optocoupler) |
| Isolation voltage | 1.5–5 kV | 2.5–4 kV typical |
| Audible click | Yes | No (silent) |
| AC zero-crossing | Naturally commutated | Built-in zero-cross detector |
| On-state resistance | ~50 mΩ (low) | Higher (~1 Ω, heat dissipation) |
| Leakage current in OFF | Zero | ~1–10 mA (cannot switch low-power circuits) |
| Biomedical suitability | Heaters, lamps, pumps | Defibrillator timing, infusion pump, MRI-safe variants |

**Conclusion:** For **high-power AC switching** (sterilizer, lamp, heater), EMR is cost-effective and reliable. For **fast, silent, high-cycle medical switching** (e.g., ventilator valves, infusion pump motor control), SSR is preferred.

*(1 mark per row in table + 1 mark for engineering conclusion.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (and lose 2–3 marks per question):**
> 1. **Forgetting the flyback diode** in the circuit diagram — instant 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing NO and NC terminals** — a NO contact is OPEN when coil is OFF, NOT the other way around.
> 3. **Using DC contact rating for AC load** (or vice versa) — contact ratings are voltage- and current-type specific. A "10 A / 30 V DC" contact is NOT safe for "10 A / 250 V AC" because DC arcs are harder to extinguish.
> 4. **Not showing AC mains isolation** — in any biomedical or IoT exam, the examiner expects the candidate to mention that the relay provides **galvanic isolation**, which is critical for patient safety.
> 5. **Calculating back-EMF without the negative sign** — remember $V_L = -L \cdot di/dt$, and the magnitude is what destroys the transistor.
> 6. **Wrong base resistor value** — a 10 kΩ base resistor will not provide enough base current to saturate the BC547 for a 70 mA coil; the typical recommended value is **1 kΩ** for a 5 V supply.
> 7. **Drawing the relay coil in parallel with the load (instead of in series with VCC)** — the coil must complete its loop with VCC, not with the AC mains.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for the KTU ESE:**

- **Relay definition:** Electromagnetic switch providing **galvanic isolation** between control and load.
- **Standard operating voltages of relay coil:** **3 V, 5 V, 6 V, 12 V, 24 V DC** (most common: **5 V** for IoT).
- **AC mains in India:** **230 V, 50 Hz, single phase**.
- **Coil current formula:** $I = V / R$ (steady state).
- **Coil time constant:** $\tau = L / R$ — controls actuation delay.
- **Armature force:** $F \propto I^2 / g^2$ (quadratic in current, inverse quadratic in gap).
- **Flyback diode:** **1N4007** (or UF4007 for fast switching) across coil, **reverse-biased** to clamp $V_{spike} = -L \cdot di/dt$.
- **Driver transistor:** **BC547** (NPN) for low-current; **IRFZ44N** (N-MOSFET) for high-current.
- **Optocoupler:** **PC817** used in medical/biomedical applications for **>5 kV isolation**.
- **NO contact** = open when coil OFF; **NC contact** = closed when coil OFF.
- **SPDT relay** has 3 terminals: **COM, NO, NC**.
- **Solid State Relay (SSR)** preferred for: silent operation, high cycle count, fast switching, MRI compatibility.
- **Electromagnetic Relay (EMR)** preferred for: low on-state loss, zero leakage, cost-effective AC mains switching.
- **Contact rating equation:** $P_{load,max} = V_{AC} \cdot I_{contact,max} \cdot \cos\phi$ for AC; $P_{load,max} = V_{DC} \cdot I_{contact,max}$ for DC.
- **Arduino/ESP32 GPIO** can source only **~20 mA** → driver stage is **mandatory**.
- **Back-EMF spike magnitude** without diode can reach **hundreds to thousands of volts** — always include the flyback diode.
- **Biomedical relevance:** Galvanic isolation, patient safety, AC mains switching in sterilizers, ventilators, infusion pumps, MRI-safe variants.
- **Key KTU keywords to use in answers:** *galvanic isolation, flyback diode, pull-in voltage, drop-out voltage, contact rating, SPDT, DPDT, SSR, EMR, optocoupler, zero-crossing, arcing, back-EMF.*

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember the 4 terminals of an SPDT relay:** **"C-N-N"** → **C**ommon, **N**ormally **O**pen, **N**ormally **C**losed. The **common** is the *moving* terminal, and the *other two* are *stationary*. In the **OFF** state, COM touches NC. In the **ON** state, COM moves to NO.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Controlling Servo Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Controlling Servo Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Servo Motor** is a closed-loop, rotary (or linear) actuator that uses position feedback to precisely control its angular (or linear) position, velocity, and acceleration. In the context of **IoT and embedded systems**, servo motors are controlled by generating a **Pulse Width Modulated (PWM)** signal from a microcontroller (Arduino, ESP32, Raspberry Pi, etc.) on its signal line. A standard hobby servo (e.g., **SG90, MG996R**) operates on a **50 Hz PWM** (i.e., a period of **20 ms**), where the width of the active-high pulse determines the target shaft angle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A servo is **NOT** a continuous-rotation DC motor. It is a *positioning* device. The PWM duty cycle alone does NOT control speed — it commands a *target position*. Internal potentiometer feedback closes the loop.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a servo motor as the **steering wheel of a power-steered car**:
- You do not push the wheels directly. You merely *command* a steering angle ("turn 30° left").
- The car's hydraulic/electronic system does the heavy work and *holds* that position even if a gust of wind tries to disturb it.
- The driver (microcontroller) sends short bursts ("pulse widths") telling the wheel where to be; an internal sensor (potentiometer) reports the current position back.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary for First-Time Learners:**
> - Pulse width **1.0 ms** → Servo points to **0°**
> - Pulse width **1.5 ms** → Servo points to **90°** (center)
> - Pulse width **2.0 ms** → Servo points to **180°**
> - Pulse width outside **1 ms–2 ms** → Servo may stall at mechanical limits.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| PWM Frequency | **50 Hz** |
| PWM Period ($T$) | **20 ms** |
| Minimum pulse width | **1 ms** (0°) |
| Neutral pulse width | **1.5 ms** (90°) |
| Maximum pulse width | **2 ms** (180°) |
| Operating Voltage | **4.8 V – 6.0 V** (typical **5 V**) |
| Signal Voltage | **3.3 V – 5 V TTL** |
| Stall Torque (SG90) | **1.8 kg·cm** at 4.8 V |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Command Generation:** The microcontroller (MCU) generates a **PWM** waveform on a GPIO pin.
2. **Pulse Width Encoding:** The *active-high* pulse width $t_p$ encodes the desired angular position $\theta$.
3. **Internal Reference Comparison:** An internal control circuit compares $t_p$ with the voltage from a **feedback potentiometer** coupled to the output shaft.
4. **Error Signal Generation:** A difference ($\theta_{desired} - \theta_{actual}$) drives an internal H-bridge MOSFET stage.
5. **Motor Actuation:** The DC motor rotates until the potentiometer voltage matches the command voltage, eliminating the error.
6. **Mechanical Holding:** Once aligned, the motor stops drawing current (stator de-energized); gear-train friction holds the position.

### 2.2 Why PWM and Not a DC Voltage?

- A servo motor's internal comparator needs a **voltage equivalent** of the angle, not a varying DC level.
- The MCU cannot output a true analog voltage easily without DAC hardware.
- A **fixed-period PWM with variable duty** can be **low-pass filtered** to a clean DC voltage, or — in cheaper servos — directly fed to a comparator that integrates the pulse to its average value.
- Average voltage $V_{avg} = V_{CC} \times D$, where $D$ is the duty cycle.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Memorize for KTU University Exam.**

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | PWM Period from Frequency | seconds (s) |
| $D = \dfrac{t_p}{T} \times 100\%$ | Duty Cycle | percent (%) |
| $\theta = \dfrac{(t_p - 1.0\text{ ms})}{1.0\text{ ms}} \times 180°$ | Angle from Pulse Width | degrees (°) |
| $t_p = 1.0\text{ ms} + \dfrac{\theta}{180°} \times 1.0\text{ ms}$ | Pulse Width from Angle | milliseconds (ms) |
| $\omega = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | Angular Velocity | rad/s |
| $\tau = F \times r$ | Torque (Force × radius) | N·m |
| $P_{out} = \tau \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical Output Power | Watts (W) |
| $V_{avg} = V_{CC} \cdot D$ | Filtered PWM Voltage | Volts (V) |

> **Engineering Utility:** In **biomedical IoT**, servo-driven mechanisms are used in robotic prosthetics (precise finger joint positioning), surgical tool guidance, automated insulin pumps (plunger actuation), and rehabilitation exoskeletons (controlled limb assistance).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mapping Pulse Width to Angular Position

We model the relationship between pulse width $t_p$ and servo angle $\theta$ as a **linear mapping** over the standard operating range $[1\text{ ms}, 2\text{ ms}] \rightarrow [0°, 180°]$.

**Step 1: Define boundary conditions**
- At $t_p = 1.0$ ms, the angle is $\theta = 0°$.
- At $t_p = 2.0$ ms, the angle is $\theta = 180°$.

**Step 2: Apply the two-point line equation (point-slope form)**

Let us define a normalized variable $\tau = t_p - 1.0$ ms, which represents the *excess* pulse width above the 0° reference. The linear mapping gives:

$$\theta(\tau) = m \cdot \tau + c$$

**Step 3: Substitute the first boundary condition ($\theta = 0°$ at $\tau = 0$)**

$$0 = m \cdot 0 + c \implies c = 0$$

**Step 4: Substitute the second boundary condition ($\theta = 180°$ at $\tau = 1.0$ ms)**

$$180° = m \cdot (1.0 \text{ ms}) + 0 \implies m = \frac{180°}{1.0 \text{ ms}}$$

**Step 5: Write the final linear model**

$$\boxed{\theta(t_p) = 180° \cdot \frac{(t_p - 1.0 \text{ ms})}{1.0 \text{ ms}}}$$

**Step 6: Inverse mapping — Pulse Width from Desired Angle**

Solving the above for $t_p$ yields the *command* equation that the MCU must generate:

$$\boxed{t_p(\theta) = 1.0 \text{ ms} + \frac{\theta}{180°} \cdot 1.0 \text{ ms}}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** An IoT-controlled rehabilitation exoskeleton needs a servo to position a knee joint at **75°**. The microcontroller operates at a PWM frequency of **50 Hz**. Compute (a) the required pulse width, and (b) the duty cycle.

**Solution:**

**(a) Pulse Width Calculation**

Using the inverse mapping derived above:

$$t_p = 1.0 \text{ ms} + \frac{75°}{180°} \cdot 1.0 \text{ ms}$$

$$t_p = 1.0 \text{ ms} + 0.4167 \cdot 1.0 \text{ ms}$$

$$t_p = 1.4167 \text{ ms} \approx 1.42 \text{ ms}$$

**(b) Duty Cycle Calculation**

First, compute the period from the frequency:

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{50 \text{ Hz}} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}$$

Then, the duty cycle:

$$D = \frac{t_p}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{1.4167 \text{ ms}}{20 \text{ ms}} \times 100\%$$

$$D = 0.07083 \times 100\% \approx 7.08\%$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Mechanical Output Power of a Loaded Servo

For a servo lifting a small medical instrument of mass $m$ at radius $r$ from the shaft under gravity ($g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$):

**Step 1: Compute the gravitational force**

$$F = m \cdot g$$

**Step 2: Compute the torque required**

$$\tau = F \cdot r = m \cdot g \cdot r$$

**Step 3: If the servo rotates at angular velocity $\omega$, the output power is**

$$P_{out} = \tau \cdot \omega$$

**Step 4: Substitute Step 2 into Step 3**

$$\boxed{P_{out} = m \cdot g \cdot r \cdot \omega}$$

**Step 5: Add the gearbox factor**

Real servos use an internal gear train with ratio $N:1$ (e.g., $N = 120$ for SG90). The motor shaft spins $N$ times faster than the output, so the *output* torque is amplified by $N$ and output speed is divided by $N$:

$$\tau_{out} = N \cdot \tau_{motor}, \quad \omega_{out} = \frac{\omega_{motor}}{N}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conservation Law Check:** Power is conserved (minus friction losses): $P_{out} \approx P_{motor} \cdot \eta$, where $\eta$ is gear-train efficiency (typically **0.6 – 0.85**).

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Servo

| Application | Required Torque | Required Speed | Recommended Servo |
|---|---|---|---|
| IoT door lock latch | Low (<1 kg·cm) | Low | SG90 (Plastic Gear) |
| Robotic prosthetic finger | Medium (5–10 kg·cm) | Medium | MG996R (Metal Gear) |
| Surgical robot arm | High (15–40 kg·cm) | High precision | Digital HV Servo + Encoder |
| Infusion pump plunger | Very Low, High Precision | Very Slow | Micro Servo + Lead Screw |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: IoT Servo Control Loop

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **block-level functional architecture** of an IoT-driven servo control system, suitable for a biomedical context (e.g., remotely operated rehab device).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cloudA[Cloud IoT Platform] -->|MQTT Command| nodeESP[ESP32 WiFi MCU]
    nodeESP -->|PWM 50 Hz Signal| nodeDriver[Level Shifter 3.3V to 5V]
    nodeDriver -->|Pulse Train| nodeServo[Servo Motor SG90]
    nodeServo -->|Mechanical Output| nodeJoint[Robotic Joint]
    nodeJoint -->|Visual Feedback| nodeCam[Camera Sensor]
    nodeCam -->|Image Data| nodeESP
    nodeServo -->|Internal Feedback Pot| nodeServo
```

**Architecture Walkthrough:**

1. **Cloud IoT Platform** sends a high-level intent (e.g., "rotate to 45°").
2. **ESP32 MCU** receives the MQTT message over Wi-Fi and computes the corresponding pulse width using $t_p = 1.0\text{ ms} + \frac{\theta}{180°} \cdot 1.0\text{ ms}$.
3. **Level Shifter** converts the 3.3 V GPIO logic to 5 V TTL that the servo expects.
4. **Servo Motor SG90** decodes the PWM and rotates to the commanded angle, holding it via internal feedback.
5. **Robotic Joint** physically moves (e.g., an exoskeleton knee).
6. **Camera Sensor** confirms the action (closed-loop visual verification for safety-critical medical devices).

### 4.2 Timing Diagram: PWM Signal Encoding Three Angles

```mermaid
gantt
    title PWM Pulse Width Encoding for 0 deg, 90 deg, 180 deg
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %S
    section 0 degree pulse
    High    :a1, 0, 1ms
    Low     :a2, after a1, 19ms
    section 90 degree pulse
    High    :b1, 0, 1.5ms
    Low     :b2, after b1, 18.5ms
    section 180 degree pulse
    High    :c1, 0, 2ms
    Low     :c2, after c1, 18ms
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** All three pulses share the same **20 ms period**. Only the **active-high duration** changes — that is what encodes the angle. Frequency stays **constant at 50 Hz**.

### 4.3 Hardware Pinout Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MCU[Microcontroller e.g. Arduino Uno]
        pin5V[5V Pin]
        pinGND[GND Pin]
        pinD9[Digital Pin 9 PWM]
    end

    subgraph SERVO[Servo Motor SG90]
        wireRed[Red Wire VCC 5V]
        wireBrown[Brown Wire GND]
        wireOrange[Orange Wire Signal]
    end

    pin5V -->|Power| wireRed
    pinGND -->|Ground| wireBrown
    pinD9 -->|PWM Signal| wireOrange
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Driving a high-torque servo (e.g., MG996R) directly from an Arduino **5V pin** can crash the MCU regulator. Always use an **external 5–6 V supply** with shared GND for servos drawing > 250 mA.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

*Define a servo motor. Explain how the PWM signal controls its angular position.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A servo motor is a rotary actuator with **closed-loop position feedback** that uses an internal DC motor, gear train, and potentiometer to hold a commanded angular position. **(1 mark)**

The PWM signal fed to the signal wire has a fixed period of **20 ms (50 Hz)**. The width of the active-high pulse encodes the angle: **1 ms → 0°**, **1.5 ms → 90°**, **2 ms → 180°**. The internal circuit compares this command with the potentiometer voltage and drives the motor until both match. **(2 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

*List the three wires of a standard hobby servo and state their functions. What is the standard PWM frequency?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Wire Color | Function |
|---|---|
| Red (or Orange-red) | **VCC / Power** (+5 V DC) |
| Brown (or Black) | **GND** (Common Ground) |
| Orange (or Yellow/White) | **Signal** (PWM Input, 3.3–5 V TTL) |

**(2 marks)** The standard PWM frequency is **50 Hz**, i.e., a 20 ms period. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the linear relationship between PWM pulse width $t_p$ and servo angle $\theta$ for a standard hobby servo operating in the range 0° to 180°. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biomedical IoT device needs a servo to set a prosthetic finger joint at **120°**. The PWM frequency is 50 Hz. Calculate (i) the required pulse width, and (ii) the duty cycle. Also compute (iii) the average DC voltage seen by the internal comparator if the signal line is low-pass filtered, assuming $V_{CC} = 5$ V. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks)**

Define the boundary conditions:
- Condition 1: $t_p = 1.0$ ms $\Rightarrow \theta = 0°$
- Condition 2: $t_p = 2.0$ ms $\Rightarrow \theta = 180°$

Apply linear interpolation (point-slope form):

$$\theta - 0° = \frac{180° - 0°}{2.0\text{ ms} - 1.0\text{ ms}} \cdot (t_p - 1.0\text{ ms})$$

$$\theta = 180° \cdot \frac{t_p - 1.0 \text{ ms}}{1.0 \text{ ms}}$$

Inverse mapping:

$$t_p = 1.0 \text{ ms} + \frac{\theta}{180°} \cdot 1.0 \text{ ms}$$

**(Full marks for clearly stating assumptions, showing slope calculation, and writing both forward and inverse mappings.)**

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**(i) Pulse width for $\theta = 120°$:**

$$t_p = 1.0 \text{ ms} + \frac{120°}{180°} \cdot 1.0 \text{ ms} = 1.0 \text{ ms} + 0.6667 \cdot 1.0 \text{ ms}$$

$$\boxed{t_p = 1.667 \text{ ms}}$$

**(ii) Duty cycle:**

$$T = \frac{1}{50 \text{ Hz}} = 20 \text{ ms}$$

$$D = \frac{1.667 \text{ ms}}{20 \text{ ms}} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 8.33\%}$$

**(iii) Average filtered DC voltage:**

$$V_{avg} = V_{CC} \cdot D = 5\text{ V} \cdot 0.0833$$

$$\boxed{V_{avg} \approx 0.417 \text{ V}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students often confuse **frequency with period** — losing 1 mark for writing $T = 50$ ms.
> - Forgetting to **convert units consistently** (ms ↔ s) is the #1 reason students lose 2 marks in Part (b).
> - The mapping formula is **linear only in the 1–2 ms range**. Writing it as $\theta = t_p \cdot 90°$ (a common error) yields 90° at 1 ms instead of 0° and gets **0 marks** for the derivation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the closed-loop control architecture of an IoT-based servo motor system used in a biomedical application (e.g., remote-controlled drug infusion pump or rehab exoskeleton). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A servo motor with **stall torque $\tau_s = 10$ kg·cm** is used to lift a surgical tool of mass **0.5 kg** attached at a radial distance of **8 cm** from the shaft. Compute (i) the required torque in N·m, (ii) whether the servo can lift the load, and (iii) the output power if the joint rotates at **30° per second**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Explanation (7 marks)**

The closed-loop system comprises:

1. **IoT Command Source** (Cloud / Mobile App) sends target angle via MQTT. (1 mark)
2. **WiFi MCU (ESP32)** computes pulse width from $\theta$ using the linear mapping derived earlier. (1 mark)
3. **PWM Generator** produces a 50 Hz signal with the calculated duty cycle. (1 mark)
4. **Level Shifter / Driver** delivers the signal to the servo's signal wire. (1 mark)
5. **Servo Motor** actuates the mechanical joint. (1 mark)
6. **Internal Potentiometer Feedback** provides closed-loop position correction. (1 mark)
7. **External Verification (Camera / Encoder)** ensures safety in biomedical use. (1 mark)

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $\tau_s = 10$ kg·cm (stall torque)
- $m = 0.5$ kg
- $r = 8$ cm = $0.08$ m
- $\omega = 30°/\text{s} = \frac{\pi}{6} \text{ rad/s} \approx 0.5236 \text{ rad/s}$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(i) Required torque:**

$$\tau_{req} = m \cdot g \cdot r = 0.5 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.08$$

$$\tau_{req} = 0.3924 \text{ N·m}$$

Convert stall torque to N·m:

$$\tau_s = 10 \text{ kg·cm} = 10 \cdot 0.09807 \text{ N·m} = 0.9807 \text{ N·m}$$

**(ii) Feasibility check:**

$$\tau_{req} = 0.3924 \text{ N·m} < \tau_s = 0.9807 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Yes, the servo CAN lift the load with a safety margin of } \approx 2.5\times}$$

**(iii) Output power:**

$$P_{out} = \tau_{req} \cdot \omega = 0.3924 \text{ N·m} \cdot 0.5236 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\boxed{P_{out} \approx 0.2055 \text{ W} \approx 205.5 \text{ mW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> - **Unit conversion errors** between kg·cm and N·m: $1 \text{ kg·cm} = 0.09807$ N·m (NOT 0.1 N·m exactly — losing 1 mark here is common).
> - Angular velocity must be in **rad/s** for the power formula $P = \tau \cdot \omega$. Writing 30°/s into the formula directly is a **2-mark deduction**.
> - In the block diagram, students often omit the **feedback path**, which is essential to the "closed-loop" definition — losing 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Servo = Closed-loop positioning device.** Not a continuous-rotation motor.
- **Three wires:** Red (VCC, +5V), Brown/Black (GND), Orange/Yellow (Signal/PWM).
- **Standard PWM: 50 Hz, 20 ms period.** Active-high pulse width encodes the angle.
- **Mapping Formula (must memorize):**
  - $\theta = 180° \cdot \dfrac{t_p - 1.0\text{ ms}}{1.0\text{ ms}}$
  - $t_p = 1.0\text{ ms} + \dfrac{\theta}{180°} \cdot 1.0\text{ ms}$
- **Angle-to-Pulse cheat table:** 0° → 1.0 ms | 90° → 1.5 ms | 180° → 2.0 ms.
- **Duty cycle** for center position = $\dfrac{1.5}{20} \times 100\% = 7.5\%$.
- **Power safety rule:** Always use an **external 5–6 V supply** for high-torque servos; never draw servo current through the MCU's onboard regulator.
- **Biomedical applications:** Prosthetic joints, surgical tool holders, rehab exoskeletons, infusion pump plungers, ventilator valve control.
- **SG90 specs to remember:** Torque ≈ 1.8 kg·cm, Operating Voltage = 4.8 V, Weight = 9 g.
- **MG996R specs to remember:** Torque ≈ 10–11 kg·cm, Operating Voltage = 4.8–6.0 V, Stall current ≈ 2.5 A.
- **Stall current is the design-limiting factor** for power supply sizing, NOT average running current.
- **Feedback element:** Hobby servos use a **potentiometer**; industrial servos use **optical/quadrature encoders**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DC Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚙️ DC Motor — Core Foundations for Biomedical Actuation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **DC Motor** is a singly-excited electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms **Direct Current (DC) electrical energy** into **mechanical rotational energy** based on the Lorentz force principle. In the context of **IoT & Biomedical Applications (OEEBT831, Module 3)**, the DC motor is classified as a **primary actuator transducer** — the terminal element in a biomedical embedded control loop that delivers controlled mechanical motion to a physiological, prosthetic, or surgical load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):** "A DC motor is a rotating electrical machine designed to convert DC electrical input into mechanical output, where the armature and field windings are energized by a direct current source, producing a unidirectional torque governed by the equation $T = k_t \Phi I_a$."

### Key Operating Principle — Fleming's Left-Hand Rule
When a current-carrying conductor is placed in an external magnetic field, it experiences a force. In a DC motor, the **armature conductors** (placed on a rotating drum called the **armature**) carry current $I_a$ in a magnetic field $\Phi$ produced by the **field winding** (or permanent magnets). The collective force on all conductors produces a **torque**, rotating the armature.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Cheat Sheet:**
> - **Armature (A):** Rotating winding that carries the load current.
> - **Field (F):** Stationary winding producing the working flux $\Phi$.
> - **Commutator & Brushes:** Mechanical rectifier that ensures unidirectional torque by reversing armature current every half-cycle.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Wheel in a Magnetic River" 💧➡️⚙️

Imagine a **water wheel** placed in a flowing river:

| Water Wheel Concept | DC Motor Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Flow rate of water | **Current ($I_a$)** |
| Height / Pressure of river | **Supply Voltage ($V$)** |
| Slope / Strength of river current | **Magnetic Flux ($\Phi$)** |
| Wheel rotation | **Mechanical Shaft Rotation** |
| Paddle resistance | **Load Torque ($T_L$)** |
| Speed of rotation | **RPM ($N$)** |

If you **increase the water pressure** (raise voltage), the wheel spins faster. If you **narrow the river channel** (increase flux by stronger field), each drop of water pushes harder, producing greater torque but slower speed. If a **load** (brake) is applied to the wheel, the wheel momentarily slows, reducing the "back-pressure" in the river — analogous to **back EMF** dropping when load is applied.

> [!TIP]
> **Biomedical Hook:** Think of an **insulin pump** in a diabetic patient. The DC motor is the water wheel, the **insulin flow rate** is controlled by precisely regulating the motor's speed, the **patient's blood glucose** is the load, and the **closed-loop glucose sensor** acts as the observer telling the controller how to adjust the "water pressure."

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$
- **Permeability of vacuum armature core:** $\mu_r \approx 2000 \text{ to } 6000$ (silicon-steel laminations)
- **Standard supply voltages (biomedical):** **3.3 V, 5 V, 12 V, 24 V, 48 V DC**
- **Torque unit (SI):** **N·m** (Newton-meter)
- **Speed unit (SI):** **rad/s** (industrial) or **RPM** (revolutions per minute)
- **Back EMF constant ($k_E$) ≡ Torque constant ($k_t$):** measured in **V·s/rad** ≡ **N·m/A**

> [!NOTE]
> In **SI units**, $k_E = k_t$ numerically when consistent units are used. This is a consequence of energy conservation and is a **favourite 2-mark KTU question**.

---

## 1.4 Classification Overview (Foundation for Later Sections)

DC motors used in biomedical systems are predominantly of two families:

1. **Brushed DC Motors** — Simple, cheap, used in **infusion pumps, ventilators, surgical drills**.
2. **Brushless DC Motors (BLDC)** — High reliability, used in **prosthetic limbs, robotic surgery (Da Vinci), MRI-compatible pumps**.

We focus on **brushed DC motors** in this module, with reference to BLDC where IoT-control differs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — What Happens Inside a DC Motor

The motor has **two independent electrical circuits**:

1. **Field Circuit (Stator side):** Carries field current $I_f$ producing flux $\Phi$.
2. **Armature Circuit (Rotor side):** Carries armature current $I_a$ interacting with $\Phi$.

The **sequence of energy conversion** is:

1. DC supply $V$ is applied across the armature.
2. Current $I_a$ flows through armature conductors lying in flux $\Phi$.
3. **Lorentz force** $F = B I L$ acts on each conductor (where $B$ is flux density, $L$ is conductor length).
4. Force × radius = **Torque $T$** on the armature.
5. As the armature rotates, conductors **cut the field flux**, generating an **induced EMF** — this is the **back EMF $E_b$**.
6. By **Lenz's law**, $E_b$ **opposes** the applied voltage $V$.
7. The effective voltage driving current is $V - E_b$, giving the **armature circuit equation**: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU's "Why is back EMF important?" — The Big Idea:**
> Back EMF is **negative feedback built into the motor's physics**. When load increases, speed drops, $E_b$ drops, more current flows, more torque is generated — **self-regulating!**

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the formulas below — these appear in **every KTU end-semester paper** on DC motors.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Armature voltage equation | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | $V$ in Volts, $I_a R_a$ in Volts |
| 2 | Back EMF | $E_b = k_E \Phi \omega$ | $E_b$ in V, $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 3 | Back EMF (RPM form) | $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$ | $P$=poles, $N$=RPM, $Z$=conductors, $A$=parallel paths |
| 4 | Developed Torque | $T = k_t \Phi I_a$ | $T$ in N·m |
| 5 | Torque (constructional) | $T = 0.159 \, \Phi Z I_a \, \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Numerical constant = $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}$ |
| 6 | Speed equation | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k_E \Phi}$ × 60 | For RPM |
| 7 | Power developed (mech.) | $P_m = E_b I_a$ | Watts |
| 8 | Input electrical power | $P_{in} = V I_a$ | Watts |
| 9 | Armature copper loss | $P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Watts |
| 10 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{E_b I_a}{V I_a}$ | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL:** $k_E \Phi = \dfrac{P Z}{60 A}$ is a **motor constant** that depends purely on construction. KTU examiners love to ask: *"Why does speed decrease with armature current?"* — answer: $N \propto (V - I_a R_a)$ and the $I_a R_a$ drop grows with load.

---

## 2.3 Types of DC Motors (IoT/Biomedical Context)

| Type | Field Connection | Speed Regulation | Biomedical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DC Shunt Motor** | Field in **parallel** with armature | Good, nearly constant | Ventilator blowers, centrifuge drives |
| **DC Series Motor** | Field in **series** with armature | Poor, high starting torque | Surgical drills, bone-saw handpieces |
| **DC Compound Motor** | Both shunt + series field | Intermediate | Prosthetic knee actuators, hospital bed drives |
| **PMDC (Permanent Magnet)** | Permanent magnets replace field | Excellent, simple | Insulin pumps, syringe drivers, robotic finger joints |
| **BLDC (Brushless)** | Electronic commutation | Excellent, high efficiency | MRI-room pumps, surgical robots, exoskeletons |

---

## 2.4 Why DC Motors are Critical in Biomedical Engineering 🏥

1. **Precise speed control** — Required for infusion pumps (µL/min accuracy).
2. **High torque at low speed** — Required for prosthetic grippers, surgical staplers.
3. **Compact form factor** — Miniature PMDC motors fit inside catheter tip drives.
4. **Battery compatibility** — Direct operation from Li-ion packs in portable devices.
5. **IoT-integration ease** — Easy to drive with PWM from microcontrollers (Arduino, STM32, ESP32).

> [!TIP]
> In **rehabilitation robotics**, a DC motor's torque-speed curve must be matched to the **human joint's biomechanical envelope** (e.g., elbow flexion: 0–4 N·m, 0–150 RPM). This is why **PMDC and BLDC motors dominate biomedical actuator design**.

---

## 2.5 Speed–Torque Characteristic — The Graph Every KTU Examiner Asks

For a **shunt motor** (constant $\Phi$):

- **No-load:** $I_a$ is small, $I_a R_a$ drop is small, $E_b \approx V$, so $N$ is maximum.
- **Loaded:** $I_a$ rises, $I_a R_a$ rises, $E_b$ falls, $N$ drops **slightly** (good regulation).
- The $N$ vs $T$ curve is a **nearly straight line with mild negative slope**.

For a **series motor** ($\Phi \propto I_a$):

- $T \propto I_a^2$ (huge starting torque) and $N \propto 1/I_a$ (dangerously high no-load speed — must never be run unloaded).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Standard:** Show every step. Examiners award partial marks for **method** even if the final number is wrong. No step-skipping!

---

## 3.1 Derivation of Back EMF ($E_b$) — The EMF Equation

**Step 1 — Flux cut per revolution per conductor:**

Let $P$ = number of poles, $\Phi$ = flux per pole in Webers, $N$ = speed in RPM, $Z$ = total armature conductors, $A$ = number of parallel paths.

Flux cut by **one conductor in one revolution** = $P \Phi$ Webers.

> *Why?* In one revolution, a conductor passes under **all $P$ poles** — each pole contributes $\Phi$ Webers. Total flux swept = $P \Phi$.

**Step 2 — Flux cut per second per conductor (rate of flux change):**

$$\text{Flux cut per conductor per second} = \frac{P \Phi \times N}{60}$$

> *Why?* $N$ revolutions per minute = $N/60$ revolutions per second. Multiplying gives flux cut per second.

**Step 3 — EMF induced per conductor (Faraday's Law):**

By Faraday's law, $\text{EMF} = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$. Magnitude per conductor:

$$e_{\text{conductor}} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60} \text{ Volts}$$

**Step 4 — EMF per parallel path (conductors in series per path):**

Each parallel path contains $Z/A$ conductors in series. EMF adds in series:

$$E_b = \frac{Z}{A} \times \frac{P \Phi N}{60}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A} \text{ Volts}}$$

> *Why the $A$ in the denominator?* $A$ depends on winding type — for **lap winding** $A = P$, for **wave winding** $A = 2$. Wave-wound motors have higher $E_b$ per RPM — important in **tachogenerators** for biomedical servo loops.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Torque Equation ($T$)

**Step 1 — Force on a single conductor:**

A conductor of effective length $L$ meters, carrying current $I_c$ amperes, placed in flux density $B$ Tesla, experiences:

$$F = B \, I_c \, L \text{ Newtons}$$

**Step 2 — Express $B$ in terms of $\Phi$:**

Total flux $\Phi$ from one pole is spread over the armature surface area. Using mean flux density:

$$B = \frac{\Phi}{\text{area per pole}}$$

For simplicity, we use the **constructive approach** preferred in KTU textbooks:

**Step 3 — Power balance approach (most elegant):**

Mechanical power developed = Electrical power converted = $E_b \times I_a$.

Also, mechanical power = Torque × Angular speed:

$$P_m = T \times \omega = T \times \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$$

Equating:

$$E_b I_a = T \times \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$$

$$T = \frac{60 \, E_b I_a}{2 \pi N}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $E_b$ from §3.1:**

$$T = \frac{60}{2 \pi N} \times \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A} \times I_a$$

$$T = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$$

$$\boxed{T = 0.159 \, \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{A} \text{ N·m}}$$

> *Why the constant 0.159?* $\dfrac{1}{2\pi} \approx 0.15915$. Examiners **will deduct marks** if you leave $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}$ unsimplified.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Speed Equation

From the armature circuit: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$, so $E_b = V - I_a R_a$.

Also, $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$.

Equating:

$$V - I_a R_a = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$$

Solving for $N$:

$$\boxed{N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi} \times \frac{60 \, A}{P \, Z} \text{ RPM}}$$

> *Real-world engineering use:* In **IoT-based infusion pump firmware**, the speed $N$ is regulated by a **PID controller** that adjusts the PWM duty cycle (effectively changing $V$) such that the calculated $I_a$ produces the desired flow rate.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Solved KTU-Style Numerical Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example** (model answer style — note the step labelling expected in valuation):

**Question (7 marks):** A 220 V DC shunt motor has armature resistance $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$, shunt field resistance $R_{sh} = 110 \, \Omega$, and draws a total line current of $25 \, \text{A}$ at rated load. Calculate:
(a) The shunt field current and armature current. (2 marks)
(b) The back EMF at rated load. (2 marks)
(c) The mechanical power developed and the armature copper loss. (3 marks)

**Solution:**

**(a) Shunt field current and armature current:**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2 \, \text{A}$$

Applying KCL at the supply node: $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$.

$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 25 - 2 = 23 \, \text{A}$$

**(b) Back EMF at rated load:**

From armature circuit equation: $E_b = V - I_a R_a$.

$$E_b = 220 - (23)(0.5) = 220 - 11.5 = 208.5 \, \text{V}$$

**(c) Mechanical power developed and copper loss:**

Mechanical power developed (gross):

$$P_m = E_b \times I_a = 208.5 \times 23 = 4795.5 \, \text{W}$$

Armature copper loss (heat dissipated in $R_a$):

$$P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a = (23)^2 \times 0.5 = 529 \times 0.5 = 264.5 \, \text{W}$$

Field copper loss:

$$P_{cu,sh} = I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} = (2)^2 \times 110 = 440 \, \text{W}$$

Total input power:

$$P_{in} = V \times I_L = 220 \times 25 = 5500 \, \text{W}$$

**Verification:** $P_{in} = P_m + P_{cu,a} + P_{cu,sh} = 4795.5 + 264.5 + 440 = 5500$ W ✓

$$\boxed{\text{Efficiency at rated load: } \eta = \frac{P_m}{P_{in}} = \frac{4795.5}{5500} \times 100\% \approx 87.19\%}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Working Principle Flow — Energy Conversion Inside DC Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Supply Voltage V]:::input --> B[Current Ia flows in Armature Conductors]
    C[Field Winding Energised]:::input --> D[Constant Magnetic Flux Phi established]
    B --> E[Lorentz Force F on Each Conductor]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Collective Mechanical Torque T on Armature]
    F --> G[Armature Rotates at Speed N]
    G --> H[Conductors cut Magnetic Flux]
    H --> I[Back EMF Eb Induced in Armature]
    I --> J[Eb opposes Applied Voltage Lenzs Law]
    J --> K[Net Driving Voltage V minus Eb]
    K --> B
    G --> L[Mechanical Shaft Output to Biomedical Load]:::output
    classDef input fill:#FFF4E1,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#E0F4FF,stroke:#0066CC,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The closed loop on the right (B → I → J → K → B) represents the **self-regulating electromagnetic feedback** intrinsic to every DC motor. This is *why* DC motors are preferred in biomedical closed-loop control.

---

## 4.2 Biomedical Speed Control Architecture — IoT-Enabled Closed Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF[Reference Speed from Clinician via IoT App]:::ref --> CMP[Comparator Summing Junction]
    FB[Feedback Signal from Tachogenerator or Encoder]:::fb --> CMP
    CMP --> ERR[Error Signal e t = Nref minus Nact]
    ERR --> CTRL[PID Controller Microcontroller]:::ctrl
    CTRL --> PWM[PWM Signal Generation]
    PWM --> DRV[Motor Driver H-Bridge L298N or DRV8871]:::driver
    DRV --> M[DC Motor Actuator]
    M --> SHAFT[Mechanical Shaft to Biomedical Load]
    SHAFT --> L[Load e.g. Infusion Syringe Prosthetic Joint]
    M --> ENC[Optical Encoder]:::sensor
    ENC --> FB
    L --> PAT[Patient Physiological Response]:::patient
    PAT --> SENS[Glucose Pressure EMG Sensor]:::sensor
    SENS --> IOT[IoT Gateway ESP32]:::iot
    IOT --> REF
    classDef ref fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00
    classDef fb fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082
    classDef ctrl fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400
    classDef driver fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    classDef sensor fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B
    classDef iot fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B
    classDef patient fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#228B22
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-relevant interpretation:** This block diagram represents a **complete cyber-physical system** — the DC motor is the actuator, the encoder/IoT gateway forms the **sensing layer**, and the patient's body is the **plant**. This is exactly the architecture expected in **OEEBT831 Module 3** design questions.

---

## 4.3 Power Flow Sankey-Style Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PE[Electrical Input Power V times Ia]:::pwr --> PEC[Armature Copper Loss I squared R]
    PE --> PM[Magnetic Power Conversion E times Ia]
    PM --> PCORE[Core Loss Hysteresis plus Eddy]
    PM --> PMECH[Mechanical Power Developed T times omega]
    PMECH --> PROT[Rotor Friction and Windage Loss]
    PMECH --> POUT[Useful Shaft Output Power]:::out
    classDef pwr fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#FF8C00
    classDef out fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
```

> [!TIP]
> **Biomedical relevance:** In a **continuous-flow blood pump** (LVAD), the "rotor friction" loss becomes critical because it translates into **heat generation in the patient's body**. This is why BLDC motors (no brushes, low friction) dominate ventricular assist device design.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are mapped to **KTU Course Outcomes** and follow the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**. Mark distribution follows KTU's typical **ESE (End Semester Exam)** pattern: **Part A = 2×3 = 6 marks**, **Part B = 1×14 (with choice) = 14 marks**.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define back EMF in a DC motor. Why is it called "back"?** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`
**Cognitive Level:** Remember (Bloom L1) | **CO Mapping:** CO3

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The EMF induced in the armature conductors of a DC motor, due to the rotation of the armature in the magnetic field, is called the **back EMF** (denoted $E_b$ or $E$).

$$\boxed{E_b = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A} \text{ Volts}}$$

**Why "back"?** By **Lenz's law**, the induced EMF always opposes the cause producing it. Since the cause is the applied armature voltage $V$, the induced EMF acts in the **opposite direction** (i.e., "back" against $V$) in the armature circuit. Hence the name.

**Significance:** Back EMF provides **automatic regulation of armature current** — as load increases, $N$ drops, $E_b$ drops, $I_a$ rises, torque rises, balancing the load.

---

### **Q2. State the working principle of a DC motor and name the rule used to determine the direction of rotation.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]`
**Cognitive Level:** Remember/Understand (Bloom L1–L2) | **CO Mapping:** CO3

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** A DC motor works on the principle that **when a current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field, it experiences a mechanical force**, given by $F = B I L$. In a DC motor, the armature conductors carrying current $I_a$ in the magnetic field $\Phi$ experience forces whose collective effect produces a **unidirectional torque**, causing rotation.

**Rule:** The direction of force (and hence rotation) is given by **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**:

- **Thumb** → Direction of **Force / Motion**
- **Forefinger** → Direction of **Magnetic Field (N → S)**
- **Middle finger** → Direction of **Current**

The **commutator** ensures that the current direction in each conductor reverses every half-cycle, so the torque always acts in the same rotational direction.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU provides **internal choice** in Part B — solve **either (a) OR (b)** of **either Question A or Question B**. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Theoretical + Numerical*

#### **Q.A(a) Derive the EMF and torque equations of a DC motor. State the relation between the EMF and torque constants.** `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]`
**Cognitive Level:** Apply / Analyze (Bloom L3–L4) | **CO Mapping:** CO3, CO4

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — EMF Equation Derivation:**

Let $P$ = number of poles, $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb), $Z$ = total armature conductors, $N$ = speed (RPM), $A$ = number of parallel paths.

Flux cut per conductor per revolution = $P \Phi$ Wb.

Flux cut per conductor per second = $\dfrac{P \Phi N}{60}$.

EMF per conductor (Faraday's Law) = $\dfrac{P \Phi N}{60}$ V.

Conductors per parallel path in series = $\dfrac{Z}{A}$.

Total EMF per path (= $E_b$):

$$E_b = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A}$$

**Step 2 — Torque Equation Derivation (Power Balance Method):**

Mechanical power developed = Electrical power converted:

$$T \times \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = E_b \times I_a$$

Substituting $E_b$:

$$T \times \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A} \times I_a$$

Solving for $T$:

$$T = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2 \pi A} = 0.159 \, \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{A} \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Relation between constants:**

Comparing with the form $E_b = k_E \Phi \omega$ and $T = k_t \Phi I_a$:

$$k_E = \frac{P Z}{2 \pi A}, \quad k_t = \frac{P Z}{2 \pi A}$$

In **SI units**, $\boxed{k_E = k_t}$ (numerically and dimensionally equivalent).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert RPM → rev/sec (the **÷60** is critical).
> 2. Mixing up lap ($A = P$) vs wave ($A = 2$) winding — wrong $A$ = full 7 marks lost.
> 3. Not stating the **Lenz's law** polarity in the EMF equation.
> 4. Writing $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}$ as the constant instead of **0.159** — examiners accept both but 0.159 is faster.

---

#### **Q.A(b) A 220 V DC series motor has armature resistance $R_a = 0.3 \, \Omega$ and series field resistance $R_{se} = 0.2 \, \Omega$. When running at 1500 RPM, it draws 50 A. Calculate the back EMF, the mechanical power developed, and the gross torque.** `[7 Marks]`
**Cognitive Level:** Apply (Bloom L3) | **CO Mapping:** CO3, CO4

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$V = 220$ V, $R_a = 0.3 \, \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.2 \, \Omega$, $N = 1500$ RPM, $I_a = 50$ A.

**Step 1 — Back EMF:**

In a series motor, $I_a$ flows through both armature and series field:

$$E_b = V - I_a (R_a + R_{se}) = 220 - 50 \times (0.3 + 0.2)$$

$$E_b = 220 - 50 \times 0.5 = 220 - 25 = 195 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Mechanical Power Developed:**

$$P_m = E_b \times I_a = 195 \times 50 = 9750 \, \text{W} = 9.75 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Gross Torque:**

Using $P_m = T \times \omega$ with $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$:

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 50 \pi = 157.08 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$T = \frac{P_m}{\omega} = \frac{9750}{157.08} \approx 62.07 \, \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = 195 \, \text{V}, \quad P_m = 9.75 \, \text{kW}, \quad T \approx 62.07 \, \text{N·m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting series field resistance** in a series motor — only adding $R_a$ instead of $(R_a + R_{se})$ → 1–2 marks lost.
> 2. Using $N$ in rad/s instead of converting via $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ → wrong torque.
> 3. Forgetting units in the final answer.
> 4. Not verifying: Total input $= 220 \times 50 = 11000$ W, copper loss $= 50^2 \times 0.5 = 1250$ W, $11000 - 1250 = 9750$ W ✓.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Biomedical Application-Oriented*

#### **Q.B(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the closed-loop speed control of a DC motor used in a biomedical infusion pump. Discuss the role of the PID controller and the encoder feedback.** `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]`
**Cognitive Level:** Analyze / Design (Bloom L4–L5) | **CO Mapping:** CO3, CO5

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (Refer to §4.2 above for the Mermaid schematic):**

A **closed-loop speed control** system for a DC motor in an infusion pump consists of:

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Reference Input** | Flow rate set by clinician (e.g., 2 mL/h), converted to desired RPM |
| **Comparator** | Computes error $e(t) = N_{ref} - N_{actual}$ |
| **PID Controller** | Generates control signal $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ |
| **PWM Generator** | Converts analog control signal to duty-cycle-modulated pulse train |
| **H-Bridge Driver** | Provides bidirectional current to motor (L298N / DRV8871 IC) |
| **DC Motor + Gearhead** | Drives the syringe plunger |
| **Optical Encoder** | Mounted on motor shaft, generates N pulses per revolution |
| **Feedback Path** | Pulses decoded by microcontroller (Arduino/STM32) to compute $N_{actual}$ |

**Role of PID Controller:**
- **Proportional ($K_p$):** Provides immediate response to error; reduces rise time.
- **Integral ($K_i$):** Eliminates steady-state error (critical for accurate drug delivery).
- **Derivative ($K_d$):** Anticipates future error; dampens overshoot (prevents plunger overshoot).

**Role of Encoder Feedback:**
The encoder provides **real-time digital speed measurement** with high resolution (e.g., 500 counts/rev). The microcontroller compares the actual pulses/sec with the target, computes error, and adjusts PWM duty cycle. This forms a **digital servomechanism** with typical accuracy of ±1% — sufficient for **FDA-compliant infusion devices**.

**Biomedical Significance:** In an **insulin pump** or **morphine PCA pump**, the closed loop ensures that **flow rate is independent of supply voltage variation, syringe friction, or patient back-pressure** — directly improving patient safety.

---

#### **Q.B(b) Compare Brushed DC, PMDC, and BLDC motors. Explain why BLDC motors are preferred in MRI-room equipment and robotic surgery.** `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]`
**Cognitive Level:** Analyze / Evaluate (Bloom L4–L5) | **CO Mapping:** CO3, CO5

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Brushed DC | PMDC | BLDC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Commutation** | Mechanical (brushes) | Mechanical (brushes) | Electronic (ESC) |
| **Field source** | Electromagnetic winding | Permanent magnet | Permanent magnet |
| **Efficiency** | 60–75% | 75–85% | 85–95% |
| **Maintenance** | High (brush wear) | Moderate | Very low (no brushes) |
| **EMI generated** | High (brush arcing) | Moderate | Low |
| **Lifespan** | 1000–3000 hrs | 2000–5000 hrs | 10,000+ hrs |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Control complexity** | Simple (DC voltage) | Simple | Complex (3-phase + ESC) |

**Why BLDC is preferred in MRI-room equipment:**

1. **No brush arcing** — Brushed motors generate **electromagnetic interference (EMI)** that distorts the MRI's $B_0$ field, causing **image artifacts**. BLDC motors, with electronic commutation, are EMI-quiet.
2. **Non-magnetic compatibility** — BLDC motors can be designed with **non-ferrous materials** (e.g., titanium, ceramic bearings), making them **non-ferromagnetic** — critical in the strong 1.5T–3T field of an MRI.
3. **No spark generation** — Eliminates fire hazard in oxygen-enriched MRI rooms.

**Why BLDC is preferred in robotic surgery (e.g., Da Vinci, Hugo RAS):**

1. **High torque-to-inertia ratio** — Enables precise force feedback for delicate suturing.
2. **Smooth low-speed operation** — Critical for tremor-free instrument movement.
3. **Sterilization-friendly** — Sealed construction (no brush dust) survives autoclaving.
4. **Predictable thermal behavior** — Low heat generation prevents tissue damage near the end-effector.
5. **Long operational life** — 10+ hours of continuous surgery demands reliable actuation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Marks lost in this question:**
> 1. Confusing **PMDC** (still has brushes) with **BLDC** (no brushes) — examiners mark zero.
> 2. Stating "BLDC is preferred in MRI because it has no magnets" — **wrong**; BLDC *does* have magnets, but the issue is **brush arcing EMI**, not the magnet.
> 3. Failing to mention **electronic commutation** as the key BLDC feature.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam:**

- ✅ **DC Motor** = DC electrical energy → mechanical rotational energy via Lorentz force.
- ✅ **Armature equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (always, for any DC motor type).
- ✅ **Back EMF formula:** $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$ — memorize both forms ($E_b = k_E \Phi \omega$ and the constructional form).
- ✅ **Torque formula:** $T = 0.159 \, \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{A}$ (the 0.159 = $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}$).
- ✅ **Speed equation:** $N = \dfrac{60 A (V - I_a R_a)}{P \Phi Z}$ — speed **decreases** with load.
- ✅ In SI units, $k_E = k_t$ (numerically equal).
- ✅ **Lap winding:** $A = P$; **Wave winding:** $A = 2$.
- ✅ **Shunt motor:** $\Phi$ ≈ constant → good speed regulation, moderate starting torque.
- ✅ **Series motor:** $\Phi \propto I_a$ → high starting torque, **never run unloaded** (will damage itself).
- ✅ **PMDC** = permanent magnet replaces field winding, but **still has brushes** (don't confuse with BLDC).
- ✅ **BLDC** = electronically commutated, no brushes, preferred in **MRI rooms, robotic surgery, prosthetics**.
- ✅ **Biomedical applications:** Infusion pumps, ventilators, surgical drills, prosthetic limbs, LVAD blood pumps, exoskeletons.
- ✅ **Closed-loop control** = Reference → Comparator → PID → PWM → Driver → Motor → Encoder → Feedback.
- ✅ **Units check:** $E_b$ in V, $\Phi$ in Wb, $N$ in RPM, $T$ in N·m, $P$ in W, $I$ in A.
- ✅ **Power flow:** $P_{in} = V I_a = E_b I_a + I_a^2 R_a = P_m + P_{cu,a}$.
- ✅ **Always verify** numerical answers by checking power balance: Input = Developed + Losses.
- ✅ In **lap winding** problems, $A = P$ (substitute carefully — most common mistake!).

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Final Warning:**
> In every KTU paper on DC motors, the **single most common reason for losing 2–3 marks** is **incorrect handling of the $A$ (parallel paths) parameter**. Practice both lap and wave windings, and always state the value of $A$ explicitly in your derivation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors: Light Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sensors: Light Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🌞 Module 3: Sensors — Light Sensor

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Light Sensor** (also called a **photosensor** or **optical sensor**) is an electro-optical transducer that detects the presence, intensity, wavelength, or spatial distribution of incident electromagnetic radiation within the visible (≈ 380 nm to 750 nm), infrared, and ultraviolet spectra, and converts this optical input into a measurable electrical output quantity (voltage, current, or resistance change).

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEEBT831)**, light sensors are classified as **active/passive analog transducers** belonging to the family of *physical quantity sensors* that interface directly with microcontrollers (Arduino UNO, ESP32, NodeMCU) in IoT biomedical nodes such as pulse oximeters, wearable SpO₂ rings, ambient light monitors for ICU neonatal incubators, and UV-exposure skin trackers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The focus of this module is on the *principle of operation, characteristic curves, signal conditioning circuits, and biomedical/IoT applications* of light sensors — not on the quantum-mechanical derivation of the photoelectric effect.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a light sensor as a **"smart window" that changes its electrical behavior when sunlight falls on it**:

- On a **bright sunny day**, the window "opens" more, allowing more electrical current to flow.
- On a **cloudy evening**, the window partially closes, reducing current.
- In **total darkness**, the window is almost shut, with negligible current.

Just as your eyes automatically adjust their pupil size based on the ambient brightness (a biological light sensor), an electronic light sensor adjusts its electrical output in proportion to the light falling on its active surface.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|----------------|------|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | **$2.998 \times 10^{8}$** | m/s |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | **$6.626 \times 10^{-34}$** | J·s |
| Visible spectrum lower bound | $\lambda_{min}$ | **380** | nm |
| Visible spectrum upper bound | $\lambda_{max}$ | **750** | nm |
| Solar constant (AM1.5) | $E_{sun}$ | **1000** | W/m² |
| Typical human eye peak sensitivity | $\lambda_{peak}$ | **555** | nm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners frequently test the *responsivity curve*, the *spectral matching* between sensor and light source, and the *photocurrent equation*. Memorizing the visible spectrum range (**380–750 nm**) and the **Planck–Einstein relation** ($E = h\nu$) is essential for full marks.

### 1.4 Classification of Light Sensors

Light sensors are broadly classified into **three generations** based on their underlying physical mechanism:

1. **Photo-emissive sensors** (e.g., photomultiplier tubes, vacuum photodiodes) — based on the external photoelectric effect.
2. **Photoconductive sensors** (e.g., LDR, photodiodes in reverse bias) — based on change of conductivity under illumination.
3. **Photovoltaic sensors** (e.g., solar cells, photodiodes in zero-bias) — generate a voltage/EMF on illumination.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — The "Why" and "How"

A light sensor fundamentally relies on the **photogeneration of charge carriers** when photons with energy $E = h\nu$ strike a semiconductor junction. The behavior depends on the *generation*, *separation*, and *collection* of these charge carriers.

### 2.1.1 Photoresistor (LDR — Light Dependent Resistor)

- **Construction:** A cadmium sulfide (CdS) or cadmium selenide (CdSe) semiconductor track deposited on a ceramic substrate with interdigitated electrodes.
- **Working Principle:** Photons with energy $\geq E_g$ (bandgap) create electron-hole pairs, increasing the conductivity ($\sigma = nq\mu_e + pq\mu_h$).
- **Resistance Behavior:** Resistance **decreases** exponentially as light intensity **increases** (inverse log-linear relationship).
- **Key Trade-off:** Slow response time (≈ 50–200 ms) and poor linearity, but extremely low cost and simple voltage-divider interfacing.

### 2.1.2 Photodiode (PN-junction photodetector)

- **Construction:** A PN-junction with an optical window; operated typically in **reverse bias**.
- **Working Principle:** Photons absorbed in the depletion region generate electron-hole pairs, swept apart by the electric field, producing a **photocurrent** proportional to the incident light intensity.
- **Modes of Operation:**
  1. **Photoconductive mode** (reverse-biased): Linear, fast response, but with dark current.
  2. **Photovoltaic mode** (zero-bias): Used in solar cells and precision light meters; logarithmic response.
- **Key Advantage:** Very fast (ns–µs), linear, low noise, and spectrally tunable by choice of semiconductor (Si, Ge, InGaAs).

### 2.1.3 Phototransistor

- A photodiode integrated with a BJT transistor; the base photocurrent is **amplified by $\beta$ (current gain)**, giving $I_{CE} = (\beta + 1) \cdot I_{ph}$.
- Higher sensitivity than a bare photodiode, but at the cost of speed and linearity.

### 2.1.4 Photovoltaic Cell (Solar Cell)

- A large-area PN-junction operating in **photovoltaic mode**; converts light directly into electrical power (and is also used as a sensor for light intensity).

### 2.1.5 Specialized IoT Light Sensors (ICs)

- **TEMT6000** — analog ambient light sensor (visible range, peak ≈ 570 nm).
- **BH1750** — 16-bit digital ambient light sensor (I²C, lux output).
- **TSL2561** — broadband + IR light-to-digital converter.
- **MAX44009** — low-power ambient light sensor (I²C).

These are the **most important devices for KTU practical exams and project work** in the 2024 scheme.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The formulas below are *guaranteed high-yield*. At least one of these appears in nearly every KTU ESE for this module.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy (Planck–Einstein relation) | $E$ in joules, $\lambda$ in metres |
| 2 | $I_{ph} = R_\lambda \cdot P_{opt}$ | Photocurrent = responsivity × optical power | $R_\lambda$ in A/W, $P_{opt}$ in W |
| 3 | $R_\lambda = \dfrac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$ | Quantum-efficiency based responsivity | $\eta$ = quantum efficiency (0 to 1) |
| 4 | $R_L = R_0 \left(\dfrac{L_0}{L}\right)^{\gamma}$ | LDR resistance vs. illuminance (log–linear) | $R_0$ at $L_0$, $\gamma \approx 0.7$–$0.9$ for CdS |
| 5 | $V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_L + R_{fixed}}$ | Voltage-divider output (LDR interface) | Standard analog interface |
| 6 | $E_v = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{\Omega}$ | Illuminance = luminous flux / solid angle | $E_v$ in lux (lm/m²) |
| 7 | $\text{Lux} = \dfrac{\text{lumens}}{\text{m}^2}$ | Photometric illuminance definition | $1 \text{ lux} = 1 \text{ lm/m}^2$ |
| 8 | $I_{CE} = (\beta + 1) \cdot I_{ph}$ | Phototransistor collector current | $\beta$ = transistor DC gain |
| 9 | $\tau_{rise} = R_L \cdot C_j$ | Photodiode RC time constant | $C_j$ = junction capacitance |
| 10 | $V_{OC} = \dfrac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Solar cell open-circuit voltage | $n$ = ideality factor (1–2) |

> **Note on Lux:** Lux is a *photometric* (eye-weighted) unit, NOT a radiometric unit. 1 lux ≈ $1.46 \times 10^{-3}$ W/m² at 555 nm.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart agriculture:** Light intensity monitoring for greenhouse auto-shading and photosynthesis optimization.
- **Wearable healthcare:** Pulse-oximetry (SpO₂) uses red (≈ 660 nm) and infrared (≈ 940 nm) LEDs + photodetectors to compute oxygen saturation.
- **Smart city street-lighting:** Twilight sensors (LDR-based) automatically switch ON/OFF sodium-vapour lamps at dusk/dawn.
- **Biomedical incubators (NICU):** Ambient light sensors maintain phototherapy irradiance for neonatal jaundice treatment.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Object detection on conveyor belts using photodiode + LED pairs (through-beam sensors).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Photon Energy and the Cut-off Wavelength

The energy of a single photon is given by the Planck–Einstein relation. For a semiconductor with bandgap energy $E_g$, only photons with energy $\geq E_g$ can generate electron-hole pairs.

$$
E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}
$$

For the **cut-off (longest) wavelength** that can be detected, set $E_{photon} = E_g$:

$$
E_g = \frac{hc}{\lambda_c}
$$

Solving for the cut-off wavelength $\lambda_c$:

$$
\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g}
$$

Substituting the standard values $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, and converting $E_g$ from eV to joules using $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J, we obtain a practical engineering formula:

$$
\lambda_c (\mu m) = \frac{1.24}{E_g (\text{eV})}
$$

### Step-by-Step Worked Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A silicon photodiode has a bandgap $E_g = 1.12$ eV. Calculate the maximum wavelength it can detect.

**Step 1 — Identify known quantities:**
- Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- Speed of light: $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
- Bandgap: $E_g = 1.12$ eV $= 1.12 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J $= 1.794 \times 10^{-19}$ J

**Step 2 — Apply the cut-off wavelength relation:**

$$
\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and evaluate:**

$$
\lambda_c = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \cdot (2.998 \times 10^{8})}{1.794 \times 10^{-19}}
$$

$$
\lambda_c = \frac{1.986 \times 10^{-25}}{1.794 \times 10^{-19}}
$$

$$
\lambda_c = 1.107 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 1107 \text{ nm}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** Silicon is sensitive to wavelengths up to ≈ **1100 nm**, covering the entire visible spectrum (380–750 nm) plus a portion of the near-infrared. This is why Si photodiodes are the workhorse of pulse-oximetry (660 nm and 940 nm) and ambient light sensing.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Photocurrent in a Photodiode

The **photocurrent** generated in a photodiode is directly proportional to the incident optical power:

$$
I_{ph} = R_\lambda \cdot P_{opt}
$$

The **responsivity** $R_\lambda$ (in A/W) can be derived from first principles. If $\eta$ is the quantum efficiency (probability that an incident photon generates a collected electron), then the number of electrons collected per second is $\eta \cdot \dfrac{P_{opt}}{E_{photon}} = \eta \cdot \dfrac{P_{opt} \lambda}{hc}$. Multiplying by the electron charge $q$:

$$
I_{ph} = q \cdot \eta \cdot \frac{P_{opt} \lambda}{hc}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow R_\lambda = \frac{q \eta \lambda}{hc}
$$

### Step-by-Step Worked Example

**Problem:** A photodiode with quantum efficiency $\eta = 0.85$ is illuminated by a laser of wavelength $\lambda = 900$ nm with optical power $P_{opt} = 100$ µW. Compute (a) the responsivity, and (b) the photocurrent.

**Step 1 — Convert wavelength to metres:** $\lambda = 900 \text{ nm} = 9.0 \times 10^{-7}$ m

**Step 2 — Compute responsivity using the standard equation:**

$$
R_\lambda = \frac{q \eta \lambda}{hc}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute known values:**

$$
R_\lambda = \frac{(1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \cdot (0.85) \cdot (9.0 \times 10^{-7})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \cdot (2.998 \times 10^{8})}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerator evaluation:**

$$
\text{Num} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.85 \times 9.0 \times 10^{-7} = 1.2255 \times 10^{-25}
$$

**Step 5 — Denominator evaluation:**

$$
\text{Den} = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 2.998 \times 10^{8} = 1.9865 \times 10^{-25}
$$

**Step 6 — Division:**

$$
R_\lambda = \frac{1.2255 \times 10^{-25}}{1.9865 \times 10^{-25}} = 0.617 \text{ A/W}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute photocurrent:**

$$
I_{ph} = R_\lambda \cdot P_{opt} = 0.617 \text{ A/W} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = 6.17 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A} = 61.7 \text{ µA}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: LDR Voltage Divider Output

The LDR is interfaced to a microcontroller ADC via a simple voltage divider. The output voltage is:

$$
V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_L + R_{ref}}
$$

The ADC reads a **10-bit value** (0–1023 on Arduino UNO) proportional to $V_{out}$:

$$
\text{ADC} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{out}}{V_{CC}} \cdot 1023 \right\rfloor
$$

### Step-by-Step Worked Example

**Problem:** An LDR with $R_0 = 1 \text{ M}\Omega$ at $L_0 = 10$ lux, and $\gamma = 0.8$, is connected in a divider with $R_{ref} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ across $V_{CC} = 5$ V. Find the ADC value when $L = 1000$ lux. (Arduino UNO, 10-bit ADC.)

**Step 1 — Use the LDR power-law relation:**

$$
R_L = R_0 \cdot \left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right)^{\gamma}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
R_L = 1 \times 10^{6} \cdot \left(\frac{10}{1000}\right)^{0.8} = 10^{6} \cdot (0.01)^{0.8}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $(0.01)^{0.8}$:**

$$
(0.01)^{0.8} = 10^{-1.6} = 0.0251
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $R_L$:**

$$
R_L = 10^{6} \times 0.0251 \approx 25120 \text{ }\Omega \approx 25.1 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 5 — Voltage divider output:**

$$
V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{25120}{25120 + 10000} = 5 \cdot \frac{25120}{35120} = 5 \cdot 0.7152 = 3.576 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 6 — 10-bit ADC conversion:**

$$
\text{ADC} = \left\lfloor \frac{3.576}{5} \cdot 1023 \right\rfloor = \lfloor 731.7 \rfloor = 731
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A higher ADC value corresponds to **brighter light** when the LDR is placed in the *upper* position (i.e., $R_L$ in the top arm). If you place the LDR in the lower arm, the logic inverts. Examiners often ask students to compute ADC values for *both* configurations — **don't lose marks on polarity!**

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: LDR vs. Photodiode vs. Phototransistor

| Parameter | LDR (CdS) | Photodiode (Si) | Phototransistor (Si) |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| Output quantity | Resistance change | Photocurrent (nA–mA) | Photocurrent × $\beta$ |
| Response time | 50–200 ms (slow) | ns to µs (fast) | µs |
| Spectral peak | ≈ 540–600 nm | ≈ 800–900 nm | ≈ 800 nm |
| Linearity | Poor (log–linear) | Excellent (linear) | Moderate |
| Sensitivity | High (MΩ changes) | Moderate | High (built-in gain) |
| Power consumption | Zero (passive) | Requires bias | Requires bias |
| Cost | Very low | Low–moderate | Low |
| Interface complexity | Voltage divider + ADC | Transimpedance amplifier | Single resistor + ADC |
| Typical KTU application | Streetlight auto-switch | Pulse oximeter | Line-follower robot |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Light-Sensor IoT Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LightSrc["AMBIENT LIGHT SOURCE"] --> Opt1["LENS OR OPTICAL FILTER"]
    Opt1 --> PhotoDev["PHOTOSENSITIVE ELEMENT: LDR OR PHOTODIODE"]
    PhotoDev --> SigCond["SIGNAL CONDITIONING STAGE: TRANSIMPEDANCE AMP OR VOLTAGE DIVIDER"]
    SigCond --> ADC["ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER: 10-BIT OR 12-BIT"]
    ADC --> MCu["MICROCONTROLLER UNIT: ARDUINO OR ESP32"]
    MCu --> Proc["PROCESSING: LUX CALCULATION AND THRESHOLD LOGIC"]
    Proc --> Act1["WIRELESS MODULE: WI-FI OR BLUETOOTH"]
    Proc --> Act2["DISPLAY: LCD OR OLED"]
    Proc --> Act3["ACTUATOR: RELAY OR LED DIMMER"]
    Act1 --> Cloud["CLOUD DASHBOARD: THINGSpeak OR BLYNK"]
```

## 4.2 Photodiode Photoconductive Mode Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCC["VCC +5V"] --> RL["R LOAD 10 KILO OHM"]
    RL --> PD["REVERSE BIASED PHOTODIODE"]
    PD --> GND["GROUND"]
    PD --> TCA["TRANSIMPEDANCE AMPLIFIER OP-AMP LM358"]
    TCA --> ADCPin["ADC PIN A0 ARDUINO"]
    ADCPin --> UNO["ARDUINO UNO MCU"]
    UNO --> Serial["SERIAL MONITOR OR LCD"]
```

## 4.3 Pulse Oximeter Block Architecture (Biomedical Application)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REDLed["RED LED 660 NM"] --> Finger["FINGER PROBE"]
    IRLed["IR LED 940 NM"] --> Finger
    Finger --> PD["PHOTODIODE DETECTOR"]
    PD --> AFE["ANALOG FRONT END: TIA PLUS FILTER"]
    AFE --> Mux["TIME DIVISION MULTIPLEXER"]
    Mux --> ADC12["12 BIT ADC"]
    ADC12 --> DSP["DSP ALGORITHM: AC DC RATIO"]
    DSP --> SpO2["SPO2 PERCENT DISPLAY"]
    DSP --> HR["HEART RATE BPM DISPLAY"]
```

## 4.4 Light Sensor Decision Tree for IoT Designers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["CHOOSE LIGHT SENSOR"] --> Q1{"NEED DIGITAL OUTPUT DIRECTLY?"}
    Q1 -- YES --> Dig["USE BH1750 OR TSL2561 I2C"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{"NEED HIGH SPEED?"}
    Q2 -- YES --> PD["USE PHOTODIODE WITH TIA"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{"VERY LOW COST?"}
    Q3 -- YES --> LDR["USE LDR WITH VOLTAGE DIVIDER"]
    Q3 -- NO --> PT["USE PHOTOTRANSISTOR WITH SINGLE RESISTOR"]
    Dig --> Bio1["IDEAL FOR WEARABLE LUX METERS"]
    PD --> Bio2["IDEAL FOR PULSE OXIMETRY"]
    LDR --> Bio3["IDEAL FOR NIGHT LAMP OR STREETLIGHT"]
```
