```mermaid
    PT --> Bio4["IDEAL FOR LINE FOLLOWER ROBOT"]
```

## 4.5 Spectral Response Curves (Schematic Description)

A typical KTU question asks students to *sketch and label* the spectral response of an Si photodiode vs. the human eye photopic response.

| Wavelength (nm) | Si Photodiode Responsivity (A/W) | Human Eye Photopic Response (Relative) |
|-----------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| 400 | ≈ 0.10 | 0.0004 |
| 500 | ≈ 0.25 | 0.323 |
| 555 | ≈ 0.32 | 1.000 (peak) |
| 600 | ≈ 0.40 | 0.631 |
| 700 | ≈ 0.48 | 0.0041 |
| 800 | ≈ 0.55 | — (outside visible) |
| 900 | ≈ 0.62 | — |
| 1000 | ≈ 0.40 | — |
| 1100 | ≈ 0.05 (cut-off) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU Answer Sheets:** The Si photodiode has its peak responsivity around **800–900 nm** (near-IR), while the human eye peaks at **555 nm** (green). This mismatch is why raw photodiode output must be corrected with a *photopic filter* (e.g., the **LX1972** visible-light sensor IC) when measuring "illuminance in lux" rather than just "radiant power."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a light sensor. List any four types of light sensors with one biomedical application each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **light sensor** is an electro-optical transducer that converts incident optical radiation (visible, IR, or UV) into a measurable electrical signal such as voltage, current, or resistance change.

**Four Types with Biomedical Applications:**

| # | Type | Biomedical Application |
|---|------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Photodiode** | Pulse oximeter SpO₂ measurement |
| 2 | **Phototransistor** | Heart-rate monitor fingertip clip |
| 3 | **LDR (Photoresistor)** | Ambient light control in NICU incubator |
| 4 | **Photovoltaic cell (Solar cell)** | Powering implantable/wearable biosensors |

*(3 marks — full marks for definition + 4 types + correct biomedical example.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the working principle of an LDR. Write the resistance–illuminance equation.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Light Dependent Resistor (LDR)** is a passive photoconductive sensor made of cadmium sulfide (CdS). When photons of energy $\geq E_g$ strike the CdS surface, electron-hole pairs are generated, increasing the conductivity and **decreasing the resistance**.

**Resistance–Illuminance Equation:**

$$
R_L = R_0 \left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right)^{\gamma}
$$

where $R_0$ is the resistance at reference illuminance $L_0$, $L$ is the actual illuminance in lux, and $\gamma$ is the sensitivity exponent (typically **0.7 to 0.9** for CdS).

*(3 marks — principle = 2 marks, equation with meaning of terms = 1 mark.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**A(i). [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a pulse oximeter. Discuss the role of red and infrared photodiodes in determining blood oxygen saturation (SpO₂).

**A(ii). [7 Marks]** A photodiode has a quantum efficiency of 0.75 and is illuminated by monochromatic light of wavelength 800 nm. The incident optical power is 200 µW. Calculate (a) the responsivity of the photodiode, and (b) the resulting photocurrent. Take $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

---

### Model Solution — A(i) — Pulse Oximeter

**Introduction (2 marks):** A pulse oximeter is a non-invasive biomedical device used to measure **arterial oxygen saturation (SpO₂)** and **pulse rate** by analyzing the differential absorption of red and infrared light by oxygenated (HbO₂) and deoxygenated (Hb) hemoglobin.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** (Sketch as per the Pulse-Oximeter Block Architecture in SECTION 4.3.)

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
1. Two LEDs — a **red LED (≈ 660 nm)** and an **infrared LED (≈ 940 nm)** — alternately illuminate a finger, earlobe, or toe.
2. A **photodiode** on the opposite side detects the transmitted light.
3. The pulsatile arterial blood absorbs variable amounts of light, while venous blood, skin, and bone produce a constant (DC) absorption baseline.
4. The **AC/DC ratio** is computed for both wavelengths:

$$
R = \frac{(AC_{red}/DC_{red})}{(AC_{IR}/DC_{IR})}
$$

5. An empirical calibration curve maps $R$ to SpO₂ percentage. Normal SpO₂ is **95–100%**.

**Why two wavelengths (1 mark):** Oxyhemoglobin (HbO₂) and deoxyhemoglobin (Hb) have **different absorption spectra** — HbO₂ absorbs more IR, Hb absorbs more red. The ratio cancels out common-mode errors and yields a robust SpO₂ estimate.

---

### Model Solution — A(ii) — Numerical on Responsivity

**Given:**
- Quantum efficiency $\eta = 0.75$
- Wavelength $\lambda = 800$ nm $= 8.0 \times 10^{-7}$ m
- Optical power $P_{opt} = 200$ µW $= 2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ W
- $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s

**Part (a) — Responsivity (3 marks):**

$$
R_\lambda = \frac{q \eta \lambda}{hc}
$$

$$
R_\lambda = \frac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times 0.75 \times (8.0 \times 10^{-7})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \times (3 \times 10^{8})}
$$

**Numerator:**

$$
1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.75 = 1.2 \times 10^{-19}
$$

$$
1.2 \times 10^{-19} \times 8.0 \times 10^{-7} = 9.6 \times 10^{-26}
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8} = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25}
$$

**Division:**

$$
R_\lambda = \frac{9.6 \times 10^{-26}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} = 0.4830 \text{ A/W}
$$

**Part (b) — Photocurrent (2 marks):**

$$
I_{ph} = R_\lambda \cdot P_{opt} = 0.4830 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-4} = 9.66 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A} = 96.6 \text{ µA}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (a) $R_\lambda = 0.483$ A/W
- (b) $I_{ph} = 96.6$ µA

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]`

**B(i). [7 Marks]** Compare the working principles of LDR, photodiode, and phototransistor. Mention two IoT-based applications of light sensors in healthcare.

**B(ii). [7 Marks]** An LDR has $R_0 = 500$ kΩ at $L_0 = 100$ lux, with $\gamma = 0.85$. It is connected in a voltage divider with $R_{ref} = 5$ kΩ across a 5 V supply. Find (a) the resistance of the LDR at 5000 lux, and (b) the output voltage at this illuminance.

---

### Model Solution — B(i) — Comparison

**LDR (2 marks):** Passive photoconductive device; resistance decreases as light increases; slow response (50–200 ms); simple voltage-divider interface; used where speed is not critical.

**Photodiode (2 marks):** Active PN-junction device operated in reverse bias; produces a small linear photocurrent (nA–mA); extremely fast (ns–µs); requires a transimpedance amplifier; used in high-speed biomedical applications.

**Phototransistor (2 marks):** Photodiode + BJT in one package; base photocurrent is amplified by $\beta$ (~100–1000); higher output current; moderate speed; useful in line-following and object-detection.

**IoT-based Healthcare Applications (1 mark, any two):**
1. **Wearable SpO₂ ring** using red + IR photodiodes.
2. **Smart neonatal incubator** that adjusts phototherapy lamp brightness based on ambient lux.
3. **UV-exposure skin tracker** to prevent sunburn (using a UV photodiode).
4. **Smart pillbox** with ambient-light sensing to detect day/night for dose timing.

---

### Model Solution — B(ii) — Numerical on LDR

**Given:** $R_0 = 500$ kΩ, $L_0 = 100$ lux, $\gamma = 0.85$, $R_{ref} = 5$ kΩ, $V_{CC} = 5$ V, $L = 5000$ lux.

**Part (a) — LDR resistance (3 marks):**

$$
R_L = R_0 \left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right)^{\gamma} = 5 \times 10^{5} \times \left(\frac{100}{5000}\right)^{0.85}
$$

$$
\frac{L_0}{L} = \frac{100}{5000} = 0.02
$$

$$
(0.02)^{0.85} = 10^{\log_{10}(0.02) \times 0.85} = 10^{(-1.699) \times 0.85} = 10^{-1.4441} = 0.0360
$$

$$
R_L = 5 \times 10^{5} \times 0.0360 = 18008 \text{ }\Omega \approx 18.0 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Part (b) — Output voltage (3 marks):**

$$
V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_L + R_{ref}} = 5 \cdot \frac{18008}{18008 + 5000}
$$

$$
V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{18008}{23008} = 5 \times 0.7826 = 3.913 \text{ V}
$$

**Bonus (1 mark — ADC value for Arduino UNO):**

$$
\text{ADC} = \left\lfloor \frac{3.913}{5} \times 1023 \right\rfloor = \lfloor 800.6 \rfloor = 800
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (a) $R_L \approx 18.0$ kΩ
- (b) $V_{out} \approx 3.91$ V
- (Bonus) ADC ≈ 800

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in the cut-off wavelength formula** — always convert $E_g$ from **eV to joules** before substitution, OR use the shortcut $\lambda_c (\mu m) = 1.24 / E_g (\text{eV})$.
> 2. **Forgetting $\eta$ (quantum efficiency)** in the responsivity derivation. Full marks only if you include $\eta$ in the formula.
> 3. **LDR position in the voltage divider** — students often swap $R_L$ and $R_{ref}$, inverting the ADC behaviour. Always *state* which resistor is connected to VCC.
> 4. **Numerical rounding** — keep **at least 3 significant figures** during intermediate steps; round only at the final answer.
> 5. **Don't confuse "luminous flux (lumens)" with "illuminance (lux)"** — KTU questions frequently test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ A **light sensor** converts optical radiation into an electrical quantity. Key devices: **LDR, photodiode, phototransistor, photovoltaic cell**.
- ✅ The **planck–einstein relation** $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$ governs photon energy; the **cut-off wavelength** is $\lambda_c = hc/E_g$.
- ✅ **Photocurrent equation:** $I_{ph} = R_\lambda \cdot P_{opt}$, with $R_\lambda = q\eta\lambda / hc$.
- ✅ **LDR resistance–illuminance law:** $R_L = R_0 (L_0/L)^{\gamma}$, $\gamma \approx 0.7$–$0.9$ for CdS.
- ✅ **LDR interfacing:** voltage divider $V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot R_L / (R_L + R_{ref})$; ADC value $= V_{out}/V_{CC} \times 1023$ (Arduino UNO, 10-bit).
- ✅ **Visible spectrum range:** **380 nm to 750 nm**; **lux** is a photometric (eye-weighted) unit.
- ✅ **Photodiode modes:** *photoconductive* (reverse-biased, linear, fast) vs. *photovoltaic* (zero-bias, logarithmic, used in solar cells).
- ✅ **Phototransistor gain:** $I_{CE} = (\beta + 1) I_{ph}$.
- ✅ **Pulse oximetry** uses **660 nm (red) + 940 nm (IR)** LEDs and a single photodiode; SpO₂ is derived from the **R-ratio** of AC/DC at both wavelengths.
- ✅ **Standard IoT light-sensor ICs:** **BH1750 (digital I²C)**, **TEMT6000 (analog)**, **TSL2561 (broadband + IR digital)**, **MAX44009 (low-power I²C)**.
- ✅ **Spectral mismatch** between Si photodiode (peak ≈ 850 nm) and human eye (peak = **555 nm**) must be corrected using photopic filters when measuring *illuminance in lux*.
- ✅ **Si bandgap** = 1.12 eV → **cut-off wavelength ≈ 1100 nm**.
- ✅ **Pulse oximeter normal SpO₂ range:** **95%–100%**.
- ✅ **Always quote units** (lux, W/m², A/W, nm, V) for full marks in KTU 2024 scheme.
- ✅ **Two terminal-device diagram LDR, photodiode & symbol** must be drawable from memory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Detection Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Motion Detection Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Motion Detection Sensor** is a transducer-class electronic device that senses physical movement of an object, organism, or person within a defined spatial zone and converts the kinematic change into a processable electrical signal. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context of *IoT and Biomedical Applications*, motion detection sensors form the foundational sensing layer of cyber-physical systems used in patient monitoring, ambient assisted living (AAL), fall detection, gait analysis, and smart hospital automation.

The sensor continuously monitors a pre-defined **Field of View (FoV)** and generates a discrete trigger (binary HIGH/LOW) or a continuous analogue signal whenever an object violates the steady-state condition of the monitored zone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** *"A motion detection sensor is a non-contact transducer that detects the change in position, velocity, or thermal/optical/acoustic signature of an object within its sensing range and produces a quantifiable electrical output proportional to that change, enabling event-driven actuation in IoT pipelines."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a dark room. Suddenly someone walks in. **You do not need to see them clearly** — even a faint change in air pressure on your skin, a tiny sound of footsteps, or a slight shift in heat near the doorway alerts you. You have "detected motion."

A motion detection sensor does the same thing, but with *engineered senses*:
- **PIR (Passive Infrared)** = your skin feeling a heat change
- **Ultrasonic** = your ears hearing a Doppler-shifted echo
- **Microwave / RADAR (Doppler)** = the way you sense a large object by the way it disturbs the air around you
- **Tomographic** = the way ripples in a pond tell you something fell in, even if you cannot see it

## 1.3 Classification of Motion Detection Sensors

| Class | Physical Principle | Active / Passive | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| PIR (Pyroelectric Infrared) | Change in IR radiation (heat) | Passive | Security lights, intrusion alarms |
| Ultrasonic | Time-of-flight of sound waves | Active | Robotics, parking sensors |
| Microwave / RADAR | Doppler shift of RF waves | Active | Automatic doors, vehicle speed guns |
| Tomographic | Radio-wave disturbance | Active | Privacy-preserving fall detection |
| Vibration / Triboelectric | Mechanical strain | Passive | Bed-exit monitoring, wearables |
| Image-based (Camera + AI) | Frame differencing | Passive (optical) | Patient activity recognition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Active sensors** emit energy and look for changes in the returned signal. **Passive sensors** simply listen for naturally emitted energy (heat, vibration, light). This distinction is a *favourite KTU 2-mark question*.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

- **Detection Range (R):** Maximum distance, typically **3 m – 12 m** for indoor IoT.
- **Field of View (FoV):** Angular coverage, typically **90° – 180°**.
- **Response Time (τ):** Latency from motion onset to output trigger, typically **< 200 ms**.
- **Sensitivity (S):** Minimum velocity detectable, often **0.1 m/s** for fall detection.
- **Power Consumption (P):** Quiescent current in µA range for IoT nodes.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** Triggered events per hour with no real motion.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PIR (Passive Infrared) Sensor — Working Principle

A **PIR sensor** uses a **pyroelectric crystal** (commonly Lithium Tantalate — LiTaO₃) that generates a surface charge when exposed to a change in incident infrared radiation.

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. The human body emits IR radiation centred around the **blackbody peak wavelength of ~9.4 µm** (calculated via Wien's Displacement Law for skin at 305 K).
2. A **Fresnel lens** segments the FoV into multiple alternating zones (lobes).
3. When a warm body (ΔT ≈ 5–10 K above background) crosses from one zone to the next, the differential IR flux on the dual-element detector changes polarity.
4. The internal **FET amplifier (BISS0001 / AS312)** amplifies this differential pulse.
5. The digital output pin flips from LOW to HIGH for a configurable hold time (typically **2 s – 200 s**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Fresnel lens?** Without segmentation, a person walking straight toward the sensor would not cross a "boundary" and would not be detected. The alternating hot/cold zones guarantee *transitions*, which is what the differential amplifier needs.

## 2.2 Microwave / Doppler RADAR Sensor

Uses the **Doppler Effect**. A continuous wave (CW) at frequency $f_0$ is transmitted. Any moving target of radial velocity $v$ reflects a wave whose frequency is shifted by $f_d$.

### Operational Logic

1. The Gunn diode / oscillator emits a CW microwave signal (typically **10.525 GHz** for the HB100 module, an industry-standard IoT Doppler module).
2. The signal hits a moving target and reflects.
3. A mixer combines the transmitted and received signals, producing a beat frequency equal to the Doppler shift.
4. A comparator turns this into a digital motion event.

## 2.3 Ultrasonic Sensor (HC-SR04 / IoT Class)

Uses **time-of-flight (ToF)** of an acoustic pulse above the human hearing range (typically **40 kHz**).

### Operational Logic

1. A piezoelectric transducer emits a 40 kHz burst (8 cycles).
2. The pulse travels through air, reflects off the target, and returns.
3. The Echo pin goes HIGH for a duration $t$ proportional to the round-trip distance.
4. Distance is recovered via $d = \dfrac{v_{sound} \cdot t}{2}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Equation | Symbols & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Doppler Frequency Shift** | $f_d = \dfrac{2 \cdot v \cdot f_0 \cdot \cos\theta}{c}$ | $f_d$ in Hz, $v$ in m/s, $f_0$ in Hz, $\theta$ is angle between velocity and line-of-sight, $c$ in m/s | Microwave / RADAR motion detection |
| 2 | **Speed of Sound in Air** | $v_s = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | $T$ in °C, $v_s$ in m/s | Ultrasonic ToF |
| 3 | **Ultrasonic Distance** | $d = \dfrac{v_s \cdot t}{2}$ | $d$ in m, $t$ in s | HC-SR04 ToF ranging |
| 4 | **Wien's Peak Wavelength** | $\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{T}$ | $\lambda_{max}$ in m, $T$ in K | PIR target IR signature |
| 5 | **Stefan-Boltzmann Radiant Exitance** | $M = \sigma T^{4}$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴, $T$ in K | PIR radiated power basis |
| 6 | **Radar Range (Monostatic, simple)** | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot t_r}{2}$ | $t_r$ in s | FMCW / pulsed radar |
| 7 | **Field of View Half-Angle (cone)** | $\theta_{FoV} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{r}{D}\right)$ | $r$ is lateral radius, $D$ is axial distance | Sensor placement geometry |
| 8 | **Angular Resolution (Tomographic)** | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{\lambda}{L}$ | $\lambda$ wavelength, $L$ aperture | Imaging radar |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Trick:** Whenever you see motion + wave, write down the Doppler equation first. Then re-read the question — the examiner usually wants the magnitude ($\theta = 0$ so $\cos\theta = 1$).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Hospitals:** PIR sensors trigger non-contact vital-sign monitoring in ICU.
- **Ambient Assisted Living (AEL):** Microwave Doppler sensors detect falls in bathrooms (works through steam, where PIR fails).
- **Wearable Patches:** Triboelectric motion sensors track Parkinsonian tremor.
- **Robotic Surgery:** Multimodal sensor fusion of optical + IMU + motion sensors enables sub-millimetre tracking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Doppler Frequency Shift for a Monostatic Radar

**Starting Premise:** A continuous microwave of frequency $f_0$ is transmitted from a stationary source. A target moves with radial velocity $v$ directly toward the source.

**Step 1 — Frequency observed by the moving target:**
The moving target acts as a moving observer. By the classical Doppler formula for a stationary source and moving observer:

$$
f' = f_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{v}{c}\right)
$$

where $f'$ is the frequency "seen" by the moving target, $v$ is the radial velocity (positive if approaching), and $c$ is the speed of the wave in the medium (speed of light for microwave, ≈ $3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s).

**Reasoning:** The target's surface oscillates in response to the incident EM wave. Since the target is moving toward the source, the wavefronts strike the target slightly more frequently than $f_0$ would suggest, hence the factor $\left(1 + \dfrac{v}{c}\right)$.

**Step 2 — Frequency re-radiated back to the source:**
The target now acts as a *moving source* re-radiating at $f'$. The stationary receiver at the radar observes a further Doppler shift:

$$
f_r = f' \left(1 + \dfrac{v}{c}\right)
$$

**Reasoning:** The target is "chasing" the wavefronts it re-emits, so they compress again as observed by the stationary receiver.

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2:**

$$
f_r = f_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{v}{c}\right) \left(1 + \dfrac{v}{c}\right) = f_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{v}{c}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Binomial Expansion:**
Since $v \ll c$ (target speed is metres/second, $c$ is $10^{8}$ m/s), we can safely apply $(1+x)^{2} \approx 1 + 2x$ for small $x$. Setting $x = v/c$:

$$
f_r = f_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{2v}{c}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Extract the Doppler Shift:**
The Doppler shift is defined as $\Delta f = f_r - f_0$. Subtracting:

$$
f_d = f_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{2v}{c}\right) - f_0 = \dfrac{2 v f_0}{c}
$$

**Step 6 — Generalise for arbitrary aspect angle:**
If the target's velocity vector is at angle $\theta$ to the radar's line-of-sight, only the radial component $v \cos\theta$ contributes to the Doppler shift:

$$
\boxed{\,f_d = \dfrac{2 v f_0 \cos\theta}{c}\,}
$$

**This is the master equation the KTU board expects you to derive from first principles.**

## 3.2 Derivation: Ultrasonic Distance from Time-of-Flight

**Starting Premise:** A 40 kHz pulse is emitted, reflects from a target at distance $d$, and returns.

**Step 1 — Round-trip travel time:** The wave travels a total distance of $2d$ at the speed of sound $v_s$. By the basic kinematic relation $d = v \cdot t$:

$$
2d = v_s \cdot t
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $d$:**

$$
d = \dfrac{v_s \cdot t}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Temperature correction (KTU-favourite twist):** The speed of sound in air varies with temperature. Linearising the exact thermodynamic relation around $0\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$:

$$
v_s = 331 \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{T}{273}} \approx 331 + 0.6\,T
$$

where $T$ is the temperature in degrees Celsius.

**Step 4 — Numerical example:** At $T = 27\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, the speed of sound is:

$$
v_s = 331 + 0.6 \times 27 = 331 + 16.2 = 347.2\ \mathrm{m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked problem:** If the echo pin stays HIGH for $t = 5.8$ ms at $T = 27\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, the distance is:

$$
d = \dfrac{347.2 \times 5.8 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \dfrac{2.01376}{2} \approx 1.007\ \mathrm{m}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Detection Field of View from Sensor Geometry

**Starting Premise:** A PIR sensor is mounted at height $h$ above the floor, pointing straight down. It has a half-angle cone of $\phi$ degrees.

**Step 1 — Ground-projected radius:** The footprint of the detection cone on the floor is a circle of radius $r$.

$$
\tan\phi = \dfrac{r}{h}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $r$:**

$$
r = h \tan\phi
$$

**Step 3 — Ground-projected area of coverage:**

$$
A = \pi r^{2} = \pi h^{2} \tan^{2}\phi
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU-style):** For $h = 2.4$ m and $\phi = 45^{\circ}$:

$$
r = 2.4 \times \tan 45^{\circ} = 2.4 \times 1 = 2.4\ \mathrm{m}
$$

$$
A = \pi \times (2.4)^{2} = \pi \times 5.76 \approx 18.1\ \mathrm{m}^{2}
$$

**Engineering implication:** For a 12 m² hospital room, a single ceiling-mounted PIR at $h = 2.4$ m with $\phi = 45^{\circ}$ provides more than full coverage — meaning the Fresnel lens is *over-engineered* for the room.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Selection for Biomedical IoT

| Use Case | Best Sensor | Why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fall detection in bathroom | Microwave Doppler | Penetrates steam and walls | Slightly higher cost |
| Bed-exit alarm | Triboelectric / Pressure | Privacy-preserving, direct contact | Requires patient compliance |
| Gait lab analysis | IMU + Camera fusion | High precision, multi-axis | Power hungry, data-heavy |
| Restroom occupancy (hospital) | PIR (ceiling mount) | Cheap, low power, robust | Cannot see through partitions |
| Operating theatre staff tracking | UWB + Camera | Centimetre accuracy | Complex installation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: IoT Motion Detection Node

This diagram captures the full signal chain from physical phenomenon to cloud dashboard, exactly as you would draw it in a KTU 14-mark answer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PHYSICAL MOTION EVENT] --> B[SENSOR TRANSDUCER LAYER]
    B --> B1[PIR Pyroelectric Element]
    B --> B2[Ultrasonic Transducer Pair]
    B --> B3[Microwave Doppler Mixer]
    B1 --> C[ANALOG SIGNAL CONDITIONING]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Bandpass Filter]
    C1 --> C2[Comparator and Threshold Detect]
    C2 --> D[DIGITAL PROCESSING UNIT]
    D --> D1[Microcontroller ESP32]
    D1 --> D2[Event Classifier FSM]
    D2 --> E[COMMUNICATION MODULE]
    E --> E1[Wi Fi MQTT Client]
    E --> E2[BLE GATT Server]
    E1 --> F[CLOUD EDGE LAYER]
    E2 --> F
    F --> F1[AWS IoT Core Topic]
    F1 --> F2[Time Series Database]
    F2 --> G[APPLICATION LAYER]
    G --> G1[Nurse Dashboard Alert]
    G --> G2[Fall Detection Engine]
    G --> G3[Actuator Trigger LED Buzzer]
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU-style):** A physical motion event is captured by a transducer, filtered, thresholded, and pushed by an MCU to the cloud over Wi-Fi/BLE, where a dashboard or downstream actuator (LED, buzzer, hospital pager) is triggered.

## 4.2 Working Sequence of a PIR Sensor with Fresnel Lens

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H1[Human Body IR Emitter 9.4 micrometre peak] --> H2[Fresnel Lens Array Multi Zone Segmentation]
    H2 --> H3[Dual Pyroelectric Element Differential IR Flux]
    H3 --> H4[Internal FET Amplifier BISS0001]
    H4 --> H5[Window Comparator and Pulse Shaper]
    H5 --> H6{Digital Output Pin HIGH or LOW}
    H6 --> H7[HIGH Motion Detected Hold Timer Tx]
    H6 --> H8[LOW Quiescent State No Motion]
    H7 --> I[ESP32 GPIO Interrupt Routine]
    H8 --> I
    I --> J[MQTT Publish Event Payload Timestamp Room ID]
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU-style):** The Fresnel lens focuses a moving human IR signature onto a *pair* of pyroelectric elements. The *differential* signal (not absolute) cancels ambient drift and triggers the digital output. This is why a PIR is immune to sunlight variation but very sensitive to *crossing motion* — a perfect exam point.

## 4.3 Comparison Flowchart: Choosing the Right Motion Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Need to detect motion] --> Q1{Privacy constraint strict}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2[Use non camera sensor]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q3[Camera plus AI acceptable]
    Q2 --> Q4{Need through wall sensing}
    Q4 -- YES --> Q5[Microwave Doppler 10.525 GHz]
    Q4 -- NO --> Q6{Operating environment humid or steamy}
    Q6 -- YES --> Q5
    Q6 -- NO --> Q7{Open space large hall}
    Q7 -- YES --> Q8[Tomographic Radio Wave Mesh]
    Q7 -- NO --> Q9[PIR HC SR501 Standard Indoor]
    Q3 --> Q10[Image based with frame differencing]
    Q5 --> DONE[Selected]
    Q8 --> DONE
    Q9 --> DONE
    Q10 --> DONE
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU-style):** This is a *decision tree* you can reproduce in a 7-mark sub-question. The examiner loves decision-flow answers because they show engineering judgement, not just rote memory.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
Differentiate between **active** and **passive** motion detection sensors. Give one biomedical example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Active Sensor | Passive Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Emits its own energy (sound, RF, IR) | Listens for naturally emitted energy (heat, vibration) |
| Power consumption | Higher (transmitter always on or pulsed) | Very low (µA range) |
| Example (biomedical) | **Ultrasonic Doppler foetal heart monitor** | **PIR sensor for ICU room occupancy detection** |

**One-line take:** *Active = emits, Passive = listens. PIR is passive because it does not emit IR — it only senses body-emitted IR.*

*(Marks: 1.5 for distinction table + 1 for biomedical example + 0.5 for the concluding line.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Retest Pattern]**
A PIR sensor uses a **dual-element pyroelectric detector** and a **Fresnel lens**. Explain why *both* components are essential for reliable motion detection.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Dual-element detector (1.5 marks):** Provides *differential* IR measurement, cancelling ambient temperature drift and sunlight variation. If only a single element were used, a slow rise in room temperature would generate false triggers.
2. **Fresnel lens (1.5 marks):** Segments the field of view into alternating zones. A moving body crossing zones produces the *transitions* the differential amplifier needs. Without it, motion directly *toward* the sensor would not be detected (a classic KTU trap answer).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper, Module 3]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **microwave Doppler motion sensor**. Derive the expression for the Doppler shift experienced by the reflected signal.

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
Refer to the *Working Sequence of a PIR Sensor* diagram as a template. The microwave equivalent is:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Gunn Oscillator 10.525 GHz CW] --> P2[Transmit Antenna]
    P2 --> P3[Free Space Propagation]
    P3 --> P4[Moving Target Reflector]
    P4 --> P5[Receive Antenna]
    P5 --> P6[Mixer Beat Detector]
    P1 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Low Pass Filter]
    P7 --> P8[Comparator and Pulse Shaper]
    P8 --> P9[Digital Motion Event Output]
```

**Working Explanation (2 marks):**
The Gunn diode emits a continuous microwave at 10.525 GHz. A moving target reflects this wave with a frequency shift proportional to its radial velocity. The mixer multiplies the transmitted and received signals, producing a beat note at the Doppler frequency $f_d$. A low-pass filter extracts $f_d$, and a comparator turns it into a digital pulse.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
The full derivation is in Section 3.1. Final result:

$$
\boxed{\,f_d = \dfrac{2 v f_0 \cos\theta}{c}\,}
$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
A microwave Doppler sensor operates at 10.525 GHz. A person walks toward the sensor at 1.2 m/s along the line of sight. Calculate (i) the Doppler shift, and (ii) the output beat frequency the mixer produces. Take $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

**Model Answer:**

Given: $f_0 = 10.525$ GHz $= 1.0525 \times 10^{10}$ Hz, $v = 1.2$ m/s, $\theta = 0^{\circ}$ (line of sight), $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Apply the Doppler equation (3 marks):**

$$
f_d = \dfrac{2 v f_0 \cos\theta}{c} = \dfrac{2 \times 1.2 \times 1.0525 \times 10^{10} \times \cos 0^{\circ}}{3 \times 10^{8}}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate (2 marks):**

$$
f_d = \dfrac{2.4 \times 1.0525 \times 10^{10}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = \dfrac{2.526 \times 10^{10}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 84.2\ \mathrm{Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Mixer output (2 marks):**
The mixer produces a beat note at exactly the Doppler frequency itself, so the output is **84.2 Hz**. This falls in the audio band, which is why microwave motion sensors produce an audible *thump* in the lab.

**Final Answer:** $f_d \approx 84.2$ Hz, output beat frequency = 84.2 Hz.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 3]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
Explain the working of an **ultrasonic motion / ranging sensor (HC-SR04)** with a functional block diagram. Derive the distance-measuring equation and show how the speed of sound is corrected for temperature.

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Microcontroller Trigger Pulse 10 us] --> Q2[Transmitter 40 kHz Piezo Burst]
    Q2 --> Q3[Air Medium Propagation]
    Q3 --> Q4[Target Object Reflection]
    Q4 --> Q5[Receiver Piezo Element]
    Q5 --> Q6[Amplifier and Bandpass Filter]
    Q6 --> Q7[Echo Comparator and Timer]
    Q7 --> Q8[Echo Pin HIGH Pulse Width t]
```

**Working (2 marks):**
A 10 µs trigger pulse initiates an 8-cycle burst at 40 kHz. The transducer converts electrical energy to mechanical pressure waves. The waves reflect from the target and return to the receiver. The Echo pin goes HIGH for a duration equal to the round-trip time $t$.

**Distance Equation + Temperature Correction (2 marks):**

$$
d = \dfrac{v_s \cdot t}{2}, \quad v_s = 331 + 0.6\,T
$$

Substituting:

$$
\boxed{\,d = \dfrac{(331 + 0.6\,T) \cdot t}{2}\,}
$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
An ultrasonic sensor is used in a hospital room at $T = 30\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. The echo pulse width measured by the timer is 4.6 ms. Calculate (i) the speed of sound, and (ii) the distance to the obstacle.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Speed of sound (3 marks):**

$$
v_s = 331 + 0.6 \times 30 = 331 + 18 = 349\ \mathrm{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Distance (3 marks):**

$$
d = \dfrac{v_s \cdot t}{2} = \dfrac{349 \times 4.6 \times 10^{-3}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate (1 mark):**

$$
d = \dfrac{1.6054}{2} \approx 0.803\ \mathrm{m}
$$

**Final Answer:** $v_s = 349$ m/s, $d \approx 0.803$ m (i.e., the obstacle is approximately 80 cm from the sensor).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Motion Detection questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\cos\theta$ in Doppler** — Many students write $f_d = \frac{2vf_0}{c}$ and lose 2 marks when $\theta$ is non-zero.
> 2. **Mixing up $\lambda_{max}$ and $\lambda$** — Wien's law uses peak wavelength; do NOT use the wrong constant ($2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K, not mm·K).
> 3. **Confusing active and passive** — A PIR is *passive* (it only receives IR emitted by the body). A common mistake: calling it "active" because it uses a lens.
> 4. **Not mentioning Fresnel lens** — In any PIR question worth 7 marks, the Fresnel lens MUST appear in the diagram and explanation. Skipping it = –2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ultrasonic** — The pulse travels *to* the target *and back*. One of the most common transcription errors.
> 6. **Unit mismatch** — Convert GHz to Hz, ms to s, and °C to K *before* plugging in. Examiners actively deduct for this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Motion detection sensors are the **first sensing layer** of every IoT pipeline in healthcare.
- ✅ **PIR** = passive, dual-element pyroelectric, Fresnel lens segmented, 9.4 µm peak.
- ✅ **Ultrasonic** = active, 40 kHz, ToF ranging, temperature-corrected speed of sound.
- ✅ **Microwave / Doppler** = active, 10.525 GHz typical, $f_d = \frac{2vf_0\cos\theta}{c}$.
- ✅ **Tomographic** = active, radio mesh, privacy-preserving, used in fall detection.
- ✅ The **Doppler shift** is a *frequency* (Hz), not a *wavelength* change.
- ✅ Always state **assumptions**: $v \ll c$, far-field radiation, plane-wave approximation.
- ✅ The **Wien displacement constant** $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K is the most-tested constant in PIR derivations.
- ✅ Fresnel lens = motion *transition* enabler; dual element = ambient-drift canceller.
- ✅ Active sensors (ultrasonic, microwave) have higher power budget; passive (PIR, triboelectric) are IoT-friendly.
- ✅ Mixer output in a Doppler radar = *the* Doppler frequency itself (not twice).
- ✅ Distance = $\frac{v_s \cdot t}{2}$ — the factor of 2 is for *round trip*.
- ✅ Field of view on the ground: $r = h\tan\phi$, area $= \pi h^2 \tan^2\phi$.
- ✅ For *fall detection* in steamy bathrooms, **microwave Doppler is preferred over PIR** (steam blocks IR).
- ✅ Always tag your answer with the **specific biomedical application** for full marks (e.g., "PIR for ICU room occupancy" beats a generic "PIR for security").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless Bluetooth Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wireless Bluetooth Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bluetooth (IEEE 802.15.1)** is a short-range, low-power, low-cost **wireless personal area network (WPAN)** technology operating in the unlicensed **2.4 GHz Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) radio band**. It employs **frequency-hopping spread spectrum (FHSS)** with 79 channels (Classic Bluetooth) or 40 channels (Bluetooth Low Energy), enabling cable-free interconnection between portable and fixed electronic devices within a personal operating space of approximately **10 meters**.

> [!NOTE]
> In the context of **biomedical IoT**, a *Wireless Bluetooth Sensor* is defined as a battery-operated transducer device that acquires a physiological signal (ECG, EMG, SpO₂, body temperature, blood pressure, glucose, etc.), conditions and digitises it, and transmits it wirelessly to a smartphone, gateway, or cloud server using Bluetooth (Classic or BLE) protocols for **continuous, non-invasive, ambulatory patient monitoring**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Personal Body Bubble" Intuition

Imagine you are in a busy airport lounge. Everyone has a small invisible bubble around them (about 10 m radius). Inside this bubble, your **smartwatch** quietly talks to your **phone**, your **phone** talks to your **wireless earbuds**, and a **glucose patch** stuck on your arm talks to your **insulin pump** — all without disturbing the businessman sitting next to you.

This invisible bubble is exactly what **Bluetooth** creates. The genius of the system is that thousands of these bubbles can co-exist in the same airport because every device rapidly *jumps* between 79 different frequencies 1600 times per second. Even if two bubbles momentarily collide on the same frequency, the *jumping* is so fast that no data is lost. This is the essence of **Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics for KTU:**
> - Operating Frequency Band: **2.400 – 2.4835 GHz** (ISM Band)
> - Classic Bluetooth Range: **10 m (Class 2)**, 100 m (Class 1)
> - BLE Range: **Up to 200 m (BLE 5.x coded PHY)**
> - Number of Hopping Channels (Classic): **79 channels**, 1 MHz spacing
> - Number of Channels (BLE): **40 channels**, 2 MHz spacing
> - Hop Rate (Classic): **1600 hops/second**
> - Maximum Application Throughput: **2.1 Mbps (EDR)**, up to **2 Mbps (BLE 5.0)**
> - Transmit Power: **1 mW (Class 2)**, 100 mW (Class 1)
> - Bluetooth Special Interest Group (SIG): the governing standards body

### 1.3 Why Bluetooth for Biomedical Sensors?

Biomedical applications have very specific constraints:

| Constraint | Bluetooth (BLE) Solution |
|---|---|
| Small size, must be worn/attached | Chip is < 5 × 5 mm (e.g., nRF52832) |
| Battery must last days/months | BLE peak current ~7.5 mA; sleep current ~0.5 µA |
| Patient mobility | Cable-free, range 10–200 m |
| Co-existence with Wi-Fi/Microwave | Adaptive Frequency Hopping (AFH) avoids interference |
| Security of health data | AES-128 CCM encryption built into BLE link layer |
| Smartphone ubiquity | Native support in Android & iOS (no extra dongle) |

> [!TIP]
> KTU students must remember that **BLE** (not Classic Bluetooth) is the dominant variant used in modern biomedical IoT devices, owing to its drastically lower power budget.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bluetooth Protocol Stack — The Layered Architecture

The Bluetooth protocol stack follows a **layered OSI-inspired model** but is structured for low-power, short-range operation:

**Layer 1 — Radio / Physical Layer (PHY)**
- Operates in 2.4 GHz ISM band
- Uses Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (GFSK) for Classic Bluetooth (BT 1.x), and GFSK / π/4-DQPSK / 8DPSK for EDR
- BLE uses GFSK with 2 MHz channel spacing
- Sensitivity typically: **−82 dBm (Classic)**, **−97 dBm (BLE 1 Mbps)**

**Layer 2 — Baseband Layer**
- Manages physical channels, hopping sequence, packet framing
- Defines **piconet** timing (625 µs slot)
- Handles error correction (FEC — Forward Error Correction)
- Supports ACL (Asynchronous Connection-Less) and SCO (Synchronous Connection-Oriented) links

**Layer 3 — Link Manager Protocol (LMP)**
- Responsible for link setup, authentication, encryption negotiation
- Power control, quality of service
- Manages device roles (Master/Slave)

**Layer 4 — Host Controller Interface (HCI)**
- Standardised interface between host (software) and controller (radio hardware)
- Carries commands, events, and data packets (UART/USB/SDIO transport)

**Layer 5 — Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol (L2CAP)**
- Multiplexes higher-layer protocols over baseband links
- Performs segmentation and reassembly (SAR)
- Provides **QoS** guarantees

**Layer 6 — Service Discovery Protocol (SDP)**
- Allows devices to discover available services and their characteristics

**Layer 7 — RFCOMM / OBEX / BNEP / TCS**
- RFCOMM: serial-port emulation (used for SPP — Serial Port Profile)
- OBEX: object exchange
- BNEP: network encapsulation
- TCS: telephony control

**Application Layer Profiles (e.g., HFP, A2DP, SPP, HDP)**

### 2.2 Piconet and Scatternet — The Network Topologies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piconet**: A piconet is formed when **one Master** device controls up to **seven active Slave** devices. All devices share the same hopping sequence synchronised to the Master's clock. The Master is identified by its Bluetooth Device Address (BD_ADDR) and clock.

- Maximum **7 active slaves** per piconet (255 parked slaves in standby)
- Master transmits in **even slots** (0, 2, 4, ...), slaves in **odd slots** (1, 3, 5, ...)
- A slave can only transmit when addressed in the previous master slot

**Scatternet**: When a device participates in two or more piconets, it is called a **scatternet** node. The bridging node switches its hopping between the master's sequences (Time Division Multiplexing) — losing one slot each switch.

### 2.3 Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) — Deep Analysis

BLE was introduced in **Bluetooth 4.0 (2010)** specifically for ultra-low-power IoT. The architecture is intentionally simpler and fundamentally different from Classic Bluetooth:

**BLE Protocol Stack (Simplified)**
1. **Physical Layer (PHY)** — 40 channels, 2 MHz spacing; 3 advertising channels (37, 38, 39) used for device discovery
2. **Link Layer (LL)** — Defines Advertising, Scanning, Initiating, Connection states
3. **Host Controller Interface (HCI)**
4. **Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol (L2CAP)**
5. **Attribute Protocol (ATT)** — Client/Server data exchange using attributes
6. **Generic Attribute Profile (GATT)** — Defines Services and Characteristics
7. **Generic Access Profile (GAP)** — Defines roles: Broadcaster, Observer, Peripheral, Central
8. **Security Manager Protocol (SMP)** — Pairing, bonding, AES-128 encryption
9. **Application Layer**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight**: BLE uses **37 data channels + 3 advertising channels** = 40 total channels. The 3 advertising channels (37, 38, 39) are deliberately placed at 2402, 2426, 2480 MHz — the **edges of the ISM band** — to avoid Wi-Fi interference, since Wi-Fi channels 1–13 occupy the middle of 2.4 GHz.

### 2.4 GATT — Services, Characteristics, and Descriptors

GATT is the **heart of BLE data exchange**. It uses a strict hierarchy:

```
Profile (e.g., Heart Rate Profile)
  └── Service (UUID 0x180D — Heart Rate Service)
        ├── Characteristic (UUID 0x2A37 — Heart Rate Measurement)
        │     ├── Properties (Read, Notify, Indicate, Write, etc.)
        │     ├── Value (e.g., 72 bpm)
        │     └── Descriptor (e.g., Client Characteristic Configuration Descriptor — CCCD)
        └── Characteristic (UUID 0x2A38 — Body Sensor Location)
```

- **Services** group related data items, identified by 16-bit or 128-bit UUIDs
- **Characteristics** are data points with type, value, and properties
- **Notifications** (server-push, no ack) and **Indications** (server-push, ack required) are key low-power mechanisms
- CCCD must be written by the client to enable notifications

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Formula Reference for Wireless Bluetooth Sensors (K-12 / KTU ESE High-Yield)**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Friis Transmission Equation (free-space path loss) | $P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | Maximum link-budget range estimation |
| 2 | Wavelength at 2.45 GHz | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2.45 \times 10^{9}}$ | Antenna design, FSPL computation |
| 3 | Path Loss (dB form) | $PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$ | Link budget in dBm |
| 4 | Link Budget Equation | $P_{rx,min} = P_{tx} + G_t + G_r - PL_{dB} - M_{fade}$ | Determines maximum operating range |
| 5 | Receiver Sensitivity (BLE 1 Msps) | $S = -97 \text{ dBm}$ | Floor for minimum detectable signal |
| 6 | Hop Dwell Time (Classic) | $T_{hop} = \dfrac{1}{1600} = 625 \, \mu s$ | Time spent on each of 79 channels |
| 7 | BLE Connection Interval | $7.5 \text{ ms} \leq CI \leq 4 \text{ s}$ | Trade-off: latency vs. current draw |
| 8 | Average Current (BLE duty-cycled) | $I_{avg} = I_{sleep} \cdot (1-D) + I_{tx} \cdot D$ | Battery-life prediction |
| 9 | Duty Cycle for a Sensor | $D = \dfrac{T_{tx} + T_{proc}}{T_{interval}}$ | Optimising transmission scheduling |
| 10 | Battery Life (Peukert's Approx.) | $L_{hr} = \dfrac{C_{mAh}}{I_{avg}}$ | Estimate of operational life in hours |
| 11 | Shannon-Hartley Capacity | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Theoretical max bit-rate in channel |
| 12 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (dB) | $SNR_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Link quality indicator |
| 13 | AES-128 Block Cipher Key Length | $K = 128 \text{ bits}$ | Security standard in BLE |

> **Note:** All logarithms are base-10 unless otherwise specified. Distances in km, frequency in MHz for the 32.45 constant.

### 2.6 Why This System Matters in Real Biomedical Engineering

Bluetooth-enabled biomedical sensors are revolutionising healthcare by enabling:
- **Continuous ambulatory monitoring** (Holter-style ECG, sleep studies)
- **Remote patient monitoring (RPM)** post-discharge, reducing hospital readmissions
- **mHealth** (mobile health) apps feeding AI-driven diagnostic algorithms
- **Aging-in-place** applications for elderly, fall detection, vitals telemetry
- **Closed-loop therapeutic devices** (smart insulin pumps, neurostimulators)

KTU graduates will frequently design or specify such devices; understanding the **trade-off between throughput, range, and battery life** is essential for clinical product success.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Range of a BLE Sensor (Link-Budget Analysis)

The range of a Bluetooth link is fundamentally limited by the **Friis Transmission Equation**. We will derive the maximum reliable range of a Class 2 BLE sensor in an open-field (free-space) biomedical telemetry scenario.

**Given Data (Typical KTU-style Problem):**
- Transmit power of sensor: $P_t = 0 \text{ dBm} = 1 \text{ mW}$
- Transmit antenna gain (chip antenna): $G_t = 2 \text{ dBi}$
- Receive antenna gain (smartphone): $G_r = 1 \text{ dBi}$
- Receiver sensitivity (BLE 1 Msps): $S = -97 \text{ dBm}$
- Fade margin (body absorption + multipath): $M_{fade} = 10 \text{ dB}$
- Frequency: $f = 2.45 \text{ GHz}$

**Step 1 — Formulate the Link Budget Equation**

The received power at the smartphone must be greater than or equal to the receiver sensitivity, less the fade margin:

$$P_{rx} \geq S + M_{fade}$$

$$P_{rx} \geq -97 \text{ dBm} + 10 \text{ dB} = -87 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 2 — Express Received Power in dBm (Friis Equation)**

$$P_{rx,dBm} = P_{t,dBm} + G_{t,dBi} + G_{r,dBi} - PL_{dB}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) Formula**

$$P_{rx,dBm} = P_{t,dBm} + G_{t,dBi} + G_{r,dBi} - \left[20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45\right]$$

Where:
- $d$ is in **kilometres (km)**
- $f$ is in **megahertz (MHz)**

**Step 4 — Plug in Numerical Values**

$$P_{rx,dBm} = 0 + 2 + 1 - \left[20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(2450) + 32.45\right]$$

$$P_{rx,dBm} = 3 - \left[20\log_{10}(d) + 67.78 + 32.45\right]$$

$$P_{rx,dBm} = 3 - 100.23 - 20\log_{10}(d)$$

$$P_{rx,dBm} = -97.23 - 20\log_{10}(d)$$

**Step 5 — Equate to Minimum Required Received Power and Solve for $d$**

$$-87 = -97.23 - 20\log_{10}(d)$$

$$20\log_{10}(d) = -97.23 + 87 = -10.23$$

$$\log_{10}(d) = \dfrac{-10.23}{20} = -0.5115$$

$$d = 10^{-0.5115} \text{ km}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Metres**

$$d = 10^{-0.5115} \times 1000 \text{ m} = 0.308 \times 1000 = 308 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Engineering Reality Check**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 308 m figure is the **free-space theoretical maximum**. In **biomedical on-body applications**, the body acts as an RF absorber (3–10 dB additional loss) and indoor multipath further reduces this. A practical on-body range is **10–50 m**, which is the value students should quote in KTU viva/answers.

### 3.2 Derivation: Battery Life of a Bluetooth Pulse-Oximeter Sensor

A common KTU-style analytical problem.

**Given:**
- Sensor transmission interval: $T_{interval} = 1 \text{ s}$
- Active current during transmit (BLE adv+connect): $I_{tx} = 7.5 \text{ mA}$
- Active duration per interval: $T_{active} = 3 \text{ ms}$
- Sleep current: $I_{sleep} = 5 \, \mu A = 0.005 \text{ mA}$
- Battery capacity: $C = 230 \text{ mAh}$ (typical CR2032 coin cell)

**Step 1 — Compute the Duty Cycle**

$$D = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{interval}} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{1} = 0.003$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Average Current**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{avg} &= I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D) + I_{tx} \cdot D \\
I_{avg} &= 0.005 \cdot (1 - 0.003) + 7.5 \cdot 0.003 \\
I_{avg} &= 0.004985 + 0.0225 \\
I_{avg} &= 0.027485 \text{ mA} \approx 27.5 \, \mu A
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Battery Life in Hours**

$$L_{hr} = \dfrac{C_{mAh}}{I_{avg,mA}} = \dfrac{230}{0.027485} \approx 8368 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Days**

$$L_{days} = \dfrac{8368}{24} \approx 348.6 \text{ days}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

> [!TIP]
> A single CR2032 coin cell can power a Bluetooth pulse-oximeter for **nearly a full year** of continuous operation — this is why BLE has become the de-facto standard for wearable biomedical telemetry. KTU answer should highlight this as a key advantage.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Wireless Protocols for Biomedical IoT

| Parameter | Bluetooth Classic | Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) | Wi-Fi (802.11n) | ZigBee (802.15.4) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency Band | 2.4 GHz ISM | 2.4 GHz ISM | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 2.4 GHz ISM |
| Range | 10–100 m | 10–200 m | 50–200 m | 10–100 m |
| Peak Current (Tx) | ~30 mA | ~7.5 mA | ~200 mA | ~30 mA |
| Sleep Current | ~0.5 mA | ~0.5 µA | ~10 µA | ~1 µA |
| Data Rate | 1–3 Mbps | 1–2 Mbps | 54–600 Mbps | 250 kbps |
| Battery Life (CR2032) | Days | Months–Years | Hours | Months |
| Nodes per Network | 7 active (piconet) | Unlimited* | ~32 AP | 65,000 (mesh) |
| Phone Native Support | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| Best Fit | Audio streaming, SPP | **Wearable sensors** | High-data vitals | Mesh hospital networks |

\*BLE: theoretically unlimited peripherals per central, limited by address space.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Piconet and Scatternet Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    M1[Master Device M1]:::master
    S1[Slave Device S1]:::slave
    S2[Slave Device S2]:::slave
    S3[Slave Device S3]:::slave
    S4[Slave Device S4]:::slave
    S5[Slave Device S5]:::slave
    S6[Slave Device S6]:::slave
    S7[Slave Device S7]:::slave
    M2[Master Device M2]:::master
    S8[Slave Device S8]:::slave
    S9[Slave Device S9]:::slave

    M1 --- S1
    M1 --- S2
    M1 --- S3
    M1 --- S4
    M1 --- S5
    M1 --- S6
    M1 --- S7
    M2 --- S8
    M2 --- S9
    S3 -.Scatternet Bridge.-> M2

    classDef master fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef slave fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Master M1 forms a piconet with slaves S1–S7. Slave S3 simultaneously acts as a Master of a second piconet (M2's piconet). The dotted link from S3 to M2 illustrates the **scatternet bridge**, which time-shares its radio between the two piconets.

### 4.2 Diagram B — BLE Protocol Stack vs Classic Bluetooth Protocol Stack

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph BLE_STACK[BLE Protocol Stack]
        A1[Application - GATT Services] --> A2[GAP - Roles and Modes]
        A2 --> A3[ATT - Attribute Protocol]
        A3 --> A4[L2CAP]
        A4 --> A5[Link Layer HCI]
        A5 --> A6[Physical Layer 2.4 GHz]
    end

    subgraph CLASSIC_STACK[Classic Bluetooth Protocol Stack]
        B1[Application Profiles HFP A2DP SPP] --> B2[RFCOMM OBEX BNEP]
        B2 --> B3[SDP - Service Discovery]
        B3 --> B4[L2CAP]
        B4 --> B5[Link Manager LMP]
        B5 --> B6[Baseband HCI]
        B6 --> B7[Radio PHY 2.4 GHz]
    end

    classDef stackBox fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    class BLE_STACK,CLASSIC_STACK stackBox
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The BLE stack has **only 6 functional layers** versus **7+ in Classic Bluetooth**. This simplicity is the key to its lower power consumption, smaller memory footprint, and faster boot time — all of which are critical for coin-cell biomedical sensors.

### 4.3 Diagram C — Block-Level Architecture of a Bluetooth Biomedical Sensor Node

```mermaid
graph LR
    SENSE[Physiological Signal Source ECG EMG EEG SpO2]:::sensor
    COND[Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter]:::analog
    ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]:::analog
    MCU[Microcontroller Sensor Data Processing]:::digital
    BTCHIP[BLE Transceiver SoC e.g. nRF52832]:::digital
    ANT[Chip Antenna 2.4 GHz]:::rf
    GW[Gateway Smartphone Hub]:::remote
    CLOUD[Cloud Server AI Analytics]:::remote

    SENSE --> COND --> ADC --> MCU --> BTCHIP --> ANT
    ANT -. BLE 2.4 GHz .-> GW
    GW --> CLOUD

    PWR[Coin Cell Battery 3V CR2032]:::power
    PWR -. Power Rail .-> COND
    PWR -. Power Rail .-> ADC
    PWR -. Power Rail .-> MCU
    PWR -. Power Rail .-> BTCHIP

    classDef sensor fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef analog fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef digital fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef rf fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef remote fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef power fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Diagram D — GATT Service/Characteristic/Descriptor Hierarchy for Heart Rate Profile

```mermaid
graph TB
    HRP[Heart Rate Profile 0x180D]:::profile
    HRS[Heart Rate Service 0x180D]:::service
    BR[Body Sensor Location Characteristic 0x2A38]:::characteristic
    HRM[Heart Rate Measurement Characteristic 0x2A37]:::characteristic
    CCCD[CCCD Descriptor 0x2902]:::descriptor
    VAL[Value 72 bpm]:::value
    PROP[Properties Notify Read]:::properties

    HRP --> HRS
    HRS --> BR
    HRS --> HRM
    HRM --> CCCD
    HRM --> VAL
    HRM --> PROP

    classDef profile fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef service fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000

    classDef value fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef properties fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU VIVA Tip:** Be able to recite the standard SIG-adopted UUIDs for common biomedical services — Heart Rate (0x180D), Health Thermometer (0x1809), Glucose (0x1808), Blood Pressure (0x1810), Pulse Oximeter (0x1822).

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
*Define Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE). List any four differences between Classic Bluetooth and BLE.*

**Model Answer:**

> Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) is a wireless personal area network technology introduced in the Bluetooth 4.0 specification (2010) by the Bluetooth SIG, optimised for ultra-low-power short-range communication in the 2.4 GHz ISM band. It is designed for devices that must operate for months or years on a coin-cell battery, such as biomedical wearables, beacons, and IoT sensors.

| # | Classic Bluetooth | Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 79 channels, 1 MHz spacing | 40 channels, 2 MHz spacing |
| 2 | Hop rate 1600 hops/s | No hopping in connection (channel selection algo) |
| 3 | Active current ~30 mA | Active current ~7.5 mA; sleep ~0.5 µA |
| 4 | Connection setup ~100 ms | Connection setup ~3 ms (advertising) |
| 5 | Audio (SCO/eSCO) supported | Audio via LC3/LE Audio (BT 5.2+) |
| 6 | Best for streaming, file transfer | Best for periodic sensor data |

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark for tabulated differences + 1 mark for concluding best-fit application)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*What is a piconet? Why is the maximum number of active slaves restricted to seven?*

**Model Answer:**

A **piconet** is an ad-hoc wireless network consisting of one **Master** device and up to seven **active Slave** devices sharing the same frequency-hopping sequence and timing, synchronised to the Master's Bluetooth clock (BD_ADDR).

The 3-bit active-member address field in the Bluetooth packet header limits the number of simultaneously active slaves to **2³ − 1 = 7** (the address 000 is reserved for the Master's broadcast). Although up to 255 devices can be *parked* (in standby), only 7 can be in active state exchanging data at any instant.

*(3 marks: 1 mark piconet definition + 1 mark 7-slave explanation + 1 mark parked device clarification)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **wireless Bluetooth biomedical sensor node** used for ECG monitoring. Describe the role of each block.

**(b)** Compute the **maximum free-space operating range** of a Class 2 BLE sensor given: $P_t = 4 \text{ dBm}$, $G_t = G_r = 2 \text{ dBi}$, receiver sensitivity $S = -92 \text{ dBm}$, fade margin $M = 8 \text{ dB}$, and carrier frequency $f = 2.45 \text{ GHz}$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture (7 marks):**

The system comprises six functional blocks, each addressed below:

1. **Sensor / Electrode Interface (1 mark):** Ag/AgCl wet or dry electrodes pick up the differential bioelectric potential (~1 mV) on the skin surface. Right-leg drive (RLD) circuit improves Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR > 100 dB).

2. **Signal Conditioning (1 mark):** Instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD8232, INA333) amplifies the 1 mV signal by 100–1000×. A 0.5–40 Hz band-pass filter removes DC offset, baseline wander, and 50/60 Hz mains interference. Notch filter at 50/60 Hz.

3. **ADC (1 mark):** 12- to 16-bit sigma-delta ADC samples at 250–500 Hz (per AAMI/ANSI EC13 standard for diagnostic ECG). Resolution must be ≤ 5 µV.

4. **Microcontroller / DSP (1 mark):** Performs R-peak detection (Pan-Tompkins algorithm), heart-rate computation, packet framing, BLE stack execution (SoftDevice or Zephyr).

5. **BLE Transceiver SoC (1 mark):** Integrated 2.4 GHz radio + baseband + link layer (e.g., Nordic nRF52832, TI CC2640). Handles GATT services for ECG data, advertising on 3 channels, connection management.

6. **Power Supply (1 mark):** Single 3 V CR2032 coin cell; LDO regulator; optional energy-harvesting interface.

7. **Antenna (1 mark):** Chip antenna or PCB trace antenna (meandered inverted-F) sized to $\lambda/4 \approx 3.06 \text{ cm}$ at 2.45 GHz.

**Data flow:** Electrodes → Amplifier → Filter → ADC → MCU → BLE SoC → Antenna → Smartphone → Cloud.

**(b) Range Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Link-budget minimum received power:
$$P_{rx,min} = S + M = -92 + 8 = -84 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 2:** Free-space path loss using Friis in dB form:
$$PL_{dB} = P_{t,dBm} + G_t + G_r - P_{rx,min} = 4 + 2 + 2 - (-84) = 92 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $d$ in km (with $f = 2450$ MHz):
$$PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$$

$$92 = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(2450) + 32.45$$

$$20\log_{10}(d) = 92 - 67.78 - 32.45 = -8.23$$

$$\log_{10}(d) = -0.4115 \implies d = 10^{-0.4115} \text{ km} = 0.388 \text{ km} \approx 388 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to metres:
$$d \approx 388 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark — engineering comment):** This is the *free-space theoretical* maximum. On-body, additional loss of 8–15 dB occurs due to tissue absorption, so the practical range is **~50–80 m**, which is well within the Class 2 BLE specification.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **GATT data model** used in BLE. With a clear hierarchy diagram, describe the relationship between Profiles, Services, Characteristics, and Descriptors. Use the **Pulse Oximeter Service (UUID 0x1822)** as an example.

**(b)** Discuss the **security mechanisms** employed in Bluetooth Low Energy for protecting biomedical patient data. Mention pairing, bonding, and the AES-128-CCM cipher.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) GATT Data Model (7 marks):**

GATT (Generic Attribute Profile) defines the **way two BLE devices exchange data** using a strict client-server model.

- The **GATT Server** holds the Attribute Table (data).
- The **GATT Client** reads, writes, or subscribes to data on the server.
- Data is organised as **Attributes**, each with: Handle (16-bit), Type (UUID), Permissions, and Value.

**Hierarchy (1 mark each):**

1. **Profile** — A use-case definition, e.g., *Pulse Oximeter Profile (PLX)*. It is a *predefined collection of services*.

2. **Service** — A logical grouping of related data. The Pulse Oximeter Service has **UUID 0x1822** and contains the **PLX Spot-Check Measurement** characteristic (0x2A5E) and **PLX Continuous Measurement** characteristic (0x2A5F).

3. **Characteristic** — A typed data container with a known UUID. Example: the **PLX Continuous Measurement (0x2A5F)** carries the SpO₂ percentage and pulse rate as a structured byte array (Flags byte, SpO₂ SFLOAT, PR SFLOAT, timestamp).

4. **Descriptor** — Metadata about a characteristic. The most important is the **CCCD (UUID 0x2902)**, which the client writes to enable **Notifications** or **Indications**.

**Pulse Oximeter GATT example (2 marks):**
```
PLX Profile
  └── PLX Service (0x1822)
        ├── PLX Features Characteristic (0x2A5E, Read)
        ├── PLX Measurement (0x2A5F, Notify)
        │     └── CCCD (0x2902)
        └── Record Access Control Point (0x2A52, Write/Indicate)
```

**(b) Security Mechanisms (7 marks):**

BLE security operates at multiple layers:

1. **Pairing (1.5 marks):** Initial authentication between two previously unknown devices. Three association methods based on device I/O capability:
   - **Just Works** (no user input, vulnerable to MITM)
   - **Passkey Entry** (6-digit code)
   - **Out of Band (OOB)** (via NFC for example)

2. **Bonding (1.5 marks):** Once paired, devices store long-term keys (IRK — Identity Resolving Key, LTK — Long-Term Key) in non-volatile memory. On reconnection, encryption resumes instantly using *re-connection encryption*.

3. **LE Secure Connections (1 mark):** Bluetooth 4.2+ uses **ECDH P-256** (Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman) for key exchange, providing FIPS-compliant protection against passive eavesdropping.

4. **AES-128-CCM Encryption (2 marks):** All on-air BLE data packets are encrypted using the **AES-128-CCM** (Counter with CBC-MAC) authenticated cipher. This provides both **confidentiality** (CTR mode) and **integrity / authenticity** (CBC-MAC MIC). The 4-byte Message Integrity Check (MIC) appended to each packet prevents tampering.

5. **Privacy via Address Resolution (1 mark):** Devices use **resolvable private addresses** that change every 15 minutes, preventing passive tracking of the patient (e.g., the location of an Alzheimer's tag).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conclusion:** Modern BLE (4.2 onwards) meets the requirements of healthcare data regulations such as **HIPAA** and **GDPR** for the in-flight protection of patient telemetry, provided the application is designed with proper user authentication and encrypted storage on the gateway.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU ESE Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing piconet limits:** Several students wrongly write "a piconet can have 7 *total* devices." Correct: **7 active + 255 parked**.
> 2. **Forgetting BLE's 3 advertising channels** at 2402, 2426, 2480 MHz — Examiners love to ask "Why are these specific channels chosen?" (Answer: avoid Wi-Fi interference).
> 3. **Misquoting frequencies:** BLE channels are **2 MHz** wide, Classic are **1 MHz** wide. Do not interchange.
> 4. **Range derivation errors:** Forgetting to add $G_t + G_r$ or using the wrong constant (32.45 is for km and MHz). **Use 32.44 for dB form with d in km and f in MHz** — be consistent.
> 5. **AES-CCM misunderstood:** Students often say "AES-256" — it is **AES-128-CCM** in BLE.
> 6. **GATT vs GAP confusion:** GAP = roles (Peripheral/Central); GATT = data model. Examiners check this distinction.
> 7. **Battery life ignoring sleep current:** Always include $I_{sleep}$ in average current — neglecting it is a 1-mark loss.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Know Before Entering KTU Exam Hall):**

- [x] Bluetooth operates in the **2.4 GHz ISM band** with **79 (Classic)** or **40 (BLE)** channels.
- [x] Classic Bluetooth hops at **1600 hops/second** with **625 µs** dwell time.
- [x] A piconet has **1 Master + up to 7 active slaves**; **scatternet** is an inter-connected piconet set.
- [x] **BLE** = low-power, not low-energy technically — peak current lower, deep sleep very low.
- [x] BLE channels: **40 channels**, advertising on **37, 38, 39** (at 2402, 2426, 2480 MHz).
- [x] GATT hierarchy: **Profile → Service → Characteristic → Descriptor (CCCD)**.
- [x] Security: Pairing → Bonding → **AES-128-CCM** encryption with **ECDH P-256** key exchange (LE Secure Connections).
- [x] **Class 2 BLE sensor:** TX power ≈ 0 dBm (1 mW), range 10 m nominal, up to 200 m line-of-sight (BLE 5.x Coded PHY).
- [x] Range derived via **Friis transmission equation**; in dB form uses the constant **32.45** for $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz.
- [x] Receiver sensitivity: **−82 dBm (Classic)**, **−97 dBm (BLE 1 Msps)**, **−103 dBm (BLE 125 kbps coded)**.
- [x] **BLE 5.0 features:** 2× data rate (2 Mbps), 4× range (coded PHY S=8), 8× broadcast capacity (advertising extensions).
- [x] **BLE 5.2 features:** LE Audio, LC3 codec, **LE Isochronous Channels**.
- [x] **BLE 5.4 / 5.3 / 5.4 / 6.0:** Periodic Advertising with Responses (PAwR), Encrypted Advertising Data, Channel Sounding for ranging.
- [x] Biomedical SIG-adopted services to remember: **Heart Rate 0x180D, Health Thermometer 0x1809, Glucose 0x1808, Blood Pressure 0x1810, Pulse Oximeter 0x1822, ECG 0x180D-derived, Continuous Glucose Monitoring 0x181F**.
- [x] Battery life is dominated by **sleep current × (1 − D) + active current × D**.
- [x] Coin cell CR2032 (3 V, 230 mAh) can power a BLE biomedical sensor for **months to a year** depending on duty cycle.
- [x] The **on-body path loss** for 2.45 GHz is **8–15 dB** more than free-space, reducing practical range.
- [x] Bluetooth SIG is the **standards body**; conformance certification is mandatory for any product claiming Bluetooth compliance.
- [x] Wi-Fi and Bluetooth **coexist** through **Adaptive Frequency Hopping (AFH)** which avoids busy channels.
- [x] **Mesh profile** (BT 5.0+) allows many-to-many communication, useful in hospital asset tracking.
- [x] Biomedical safety: **IEC 60601-1** governs electrical safety; **ISO/IEEE 11073** governs data interoperability of personal health devices (often layered on top of BLE GATT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *BLE is the default wireless protocol of modern biomedical IoT. Master the GATT model, the link-budget equation, and the security triad (Pairing → Bonding → AES-128-CCM) — these three topics account for > 60% of ESE marks in this module.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Handling and Analytics** in the context of **IoT and Biomedical Applications** refers to the systematic, end-to-end process of acquiring, transmitting, storing, processing, securing, and interpreting the massive volumes of heterogeneous data generated by connected medical devices, wearable sensors, and biomedical instruments, in order to extract clinically meaningful insights, enable real-time decision support, and drive intelligent automation in healthcare delivery.

The term can be decomposed into two tightly coupled sub-domains:

- **Data Handling** — Encompasses the *engineering pipeline* of data: collection at the sensor/edge node, ingestion through gateways, transmission over IoT networks, secure storage on cloud/fog servers, preprocessing (filtering, normalization, fusion), and lifecycle management (archival/purging) as dictated by medical regulations.
- **Data Analytics** — Encompasses the *intelligence layer* of data: applying statistical, computational, and machine-learning techniques to the curated data to discover patterns, identify anomalies, forecast future events, and recommend actions.

In KTU terminology, this topic bridges **Module 3 (IoT Communication & Cloud)** with **Module 4 (Data Analytics)**, forming the analytical backbone of the entire course.

## 1.2 The 5 V's of IoT Data — A Foundational Concept

> [!NOTE]
> Every KTU 2024 Scheme question on this topic will almost certainly test the **5 V's of Big Data in the IoT context**. Memorize them in this exact order.

| Dimension | Meaning | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Scale of data (Terabytes–Petabytes) | A single ICU monitor generates ~**1000 data points/second**; 1 hospital = petabytes/year |
| **Velocity** | Speed of data generation and movement | ECG streaming at **360 Hz**; real-time alarm propagation in \<**1 second** |
| **Variety** | Heterogeneity of formats and sources | ECG, EEG, EMG, MRI, EHR, genomic, imaging — all merged |
| **Veracity** | Trustworthiness / data quality | Motion artifacts, sensor noise, missing samples in wearable ECG |
| **Value** | Ultimate worth extracted | Early sepsis prediction saving lives; reducing readmission rates |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital as a Data Factory

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a modern tertiary-care hospital exactly like a large-scale manufacturing factory, except its raw material is *patient physiological data* and its finished product is a *clinical decision*.**

- The **wards and ambulances** are the *raw material collection points* (analogous to IoT sensors).
- The **nursing station and HIS (Hospital Information System)** act as the *local warehouse* (edge/fog storage).
- The **central hospital server and cloud** are the *distribution centre* (cloud storage).
- The **data analysts and AI diagnostic systems** are the *quality control and R&D department* (analytics engine).
- The **doctor at the bedside receiving an alert on a tablet** is the *end consumer* receiving the finished, value-added product.

Just as a factory cannot function without an efficient supply chain, **no IoT-enabled healthcare system can deliver value without a robust data handling and analytics pipeline**.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Biomedical IoT

Biomedical IoT is unique because the data involved is **mission-critical, life-affecting, and regulated**. Unlike consumer IoT (where a misread temperature sensor is merely inconvenient), a misread arterial blood pressure can mean the difference between life and death. Hence, the KTU 2024 scheme places heavy emphasis on the **rigour, latency, and accuracy** of the data pipeline.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Stages of the IoT Data Handling Pipeline

The complete lifecycle of biomedical IoT data consists of six sequential, iterative stages. Each stage is independently examinable in KTU ESE.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 1 — Data Acquisition (Sensing)**
> - *Why*: The physical phenomenon (heartbeat, SpO₂, body temperature) must first be transduced into an electrical signal.
> - *How*: Biosensors (e.g., PPG for pulse, electrodes for ECG) sample the signal at a rate dictated by the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** to avoid aliasing.
> - *Engineering challenge*: Trade-off between sampling rate (power consumption) and information fidelity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 2 — Data Preprocessing (Edge Processing)**
> - *Why*: Raw biomedical signals are inherently noisy (50/60 Hz powerline interference, baseline wander, motion artifact).
> - *How*: Apply digital filters (Butterworth, Chebyshev, Wavelet denoising), perform normalization, detect and impute missing values, and segment continuous streams into clinically meaningful windows (e.g., 10-second ECG strips).
> - *Engineering challenge*: Latency budget — preprocessing must complete within milliseconds for real-time alarms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 3 — Data Transmission (Networking)**
> - *Why*: Preprocessed data must reach the storage/analytics layer reliably and securely.
> - *How*: Use IoT protocols (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP/HTTPS over Wi-Fi/5G/LoRaWAN) with payload compression and end-to-end encryption (TLS 1.3, AES-256).
> - *Engineering challenge*: Bandwidth vs. battery life trade-off; QoS for life-critical packets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 4 — Data Storage (Persistence)**
> - *Why*: Historical data fuels analytics, regulatory audits, and longitudinal patient studies.
> - *How*: Hybrid storage — time-series databases (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) for vitals; relational (PostgreSQL) for EHR; object stores (AWS S3) for imaging; HL7 FHIR standards for interoperability.
> - *Engineering challenge*: Compliance with **HIPAA / DISHA (India) / GDPR** retention rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 5 — Data Analytics (Intelligence)**
> - *Why*: This is where raw data is transformed into clinical insight.
> - *How*: Apply descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive analytics using statistical, ML, and deep-learning techniques.
> - *Engineering challenge*: Explainability of AI decisions to clinicians (XAI).

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 6 — Data Visualization & Action**
> - *Why*: Insights are useless unless they reach the right person in a comprehensible form.
> - *How*: Dashboards (Grafana, Kibana), mobile alerts, HL7 messages to EMR, automated ventilator/insulin-pump control.
> - *Engineering challenge*: Avoiding alert fatigue in clinicians.

## 2.2 The Four Paradigms of Data Analytics

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically demands a clear taxonomy of analytics. The four paradigms, arranged by increasing complexity and value, are:

| Level | Paradigm | Core Question | Biomedical Example | Typical Techniques |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Descriptive** | *"What happened?"* | "Patient X had 14 tachycardia events last week" | Mean, median, dashboards, histograms |
| 2 | **Diagnostic** | *"Why did it happen?"* | "Tachycardia correlated with skipped beta-blocker dose" | Correlation, drill-down, root-cause analysis |
| 3 | **Predictive** | *"What will happen?"* | "70% probability of sepsis onset in next 6 hours" | Regression, Random Forest, LSTM, ARIMA |
| 4 | **Prescriptive** | *"What should we do?"* | "Recommend IV fluid bolus of 500 mL now" | Optimization, Reinforcement Learning, CDS engines |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU ESE answers, **always present these four in a clean numbered list or a pyramid diagram** (see SECTION 4). Marks are awarded for clarity of distinction.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> The following equations are the **mathematical core** most frequently tested under this topic. Commit them to memory with units.

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Variables / Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | $f_s$ = sampling freq (**Hz**), $f_{max}$ = highest signal freq (**Hz**) | ECG: $f_{max} \approx 150$ Hz $\Rightarrow f_s \geq 300$ Hz (clinical standard: 360–500 Hz) |
| 2 | Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | $C$ = capacity (**bps**), $B$ = bandwidth (**Hz**), SNR dimensionless | Determines max wireless data rate for medical telemetry |
| 3 | Min-Max Normalization | $x' = \dfrac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$ | $x' \in [0, 1]$ | Sensitive to outliers; use for bounded biosignals (e.g., SpO₂ 0–100) |
| 4 | Z-Score Standardization | $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $\mu$ = mean, $\sigma$ = std dev | Used for unbounded signals (e.g., heart rate, BP) |
| 5 | Arithmetic Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Units same as $x$ | Sensitive to outliers; use trimmed mean for vitals |
| 6 | Variance | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | Units = $x^2$ | Sample variance uses $n-1$ in denominator |
| 7 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Units same as $x$ | Used in anomaly detection (e.g., $3\sigma$ rule) |
| 8 | Shannon Entropy (Information Content) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \cdot \log_2 p(x_i)$ | $H$ in **bits** | $H = 0$ for certain event; max for uniform distribution |
| 9 | Data Reduction Ratio | $R = 1 - \dfrac{D_{compressed}}{D_{original}}$ | $R \in [0, 1]$ | $R = 0.9$ means 90% data size reduction |
| 10 | Compression Ratio | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{D_{original}}{D_{compressed}}$ | $\text{CR} \geq 1$ | Inverse of $(1-R)$; CR = 10 means 10× compression |
| 11 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | **dB** | ECG: SNR $\geq$ **20 dB** for diagnostic quality |
| 12 | Bit-Rate (Streaming) | $R_b = f_s \cdot n \cdot b$ | $R_b$ in **bps** | ECG at 500 Hz, 12-bit, 1 channel = **6000 bps** |
| 13 | Bayes' Theorem (Diagnostic Analytics) | $P(D \mid S) = \dfrac{P(S \mid D) \cdot P(D)}{P(S)}$ | Probabilities $\in [0,1]$ | Foundation of clinical decision support systems |
| 14 | Moving Average Filter | $y[n] = \dfrac{1}{M}\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} x[n-k]$ | $M$ = window size | Simple denoising for slowly varying vitals |
| 15 | Peak-to-Peak Amplitude (ECG R-wave) | $V_{pp} = V_{max} - V_{min}$ | **mV** | Normal QRS: $V_{pp} \approx$ **1.0–2.0 mV** |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are not academic ornaments — they are used daily in:

- **FDA-cleared ECG monitors** (Philips, GE Healthcare) — applying Eq. 1 and Eq. 11.
- **Apple Watch ECG app** — running real-time ML (Eq. 13) on every heartbeat.
- **Hospital cloud platforms** (AWS HealthLake, Google Cloud Healthcare API) — using Eq. 9 and Eq. 10 to manage petabyte-scale archives.
- **ICU sepsis-alert systems** (e.g., Epic Deterioration Index) — applying Eqs. 7, 8, 13 to predict clinical decline hours in advance.
