topics.title: Data Handling Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Handling Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Data Handling Technologies** in the context of IoT refer to the integrated set of methodologies, protocols, computational frameworks, and storage architectures used to **acquire, ingest, process, transform, transmit, persist, and analyze** the massive volumes of heterogeneous data generated by distributed IoT endpoints, sensors, actuators, and edge devices throughout their lifecycle — from the moment of sensing at the node to the final visualization, decision-making, or archival in cloud/enterprise systems.

According to the KTU 2024 OEEBT831 syllabus, this topic encompasses the layered stack of technologies that bridge the physical sensing world with the cyber information world, ensuring **data integrity, low-latency delivery, scalable storage, and actionable intelligence extraction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):**
> Data Handling Technologies cover the entire spectrum of operations performed on IoT-generated data, including data acquisition, aggregation, filtering, transformation, storage (local/edge/cloud), transmission, and analytics using contemporary big-data paradigms (Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL) and modern storage paradigms (time-series databases, data lakes, data warehouses).

---

## 1.2 The Four "V"s of IoT Data — The Defining Characteristics

IoT data is fundamentally different from traditional enterprise data. KTU examiners frequently test these four properties:

| Dimension | Property | IoT Manifestation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Volume** | Massive scale | **Terabytes to Petabytes per day** from millions of sensors |
| **Velocity** | Real-time / streaming | Data produced continuously at millisecond resolution |
| **Variety** | Multi-modal, heterogeneous | Structured (numeric), semi-structured (JSON, XML), unstructured (image, audio, video) |
| **Veracity** | Uncertainty & noise | Sensor drift, calibration errors, missing values, outliers |

> [!NOTE]
> A 5th "V" — **Value** — is often added: the ultimate goal of IoT data handling is to extract actionable business/clinical/industrial value from the raw streams.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

**🏥 The Smart Hospital Analogy:**

Imagine a 500-bed hospital with 10,000 sensors monitoring every patient — heart rate, SpO₂, ECG, temperature, infusion pump rate, location badges, equipment status.

- The **raw data** from these sensors is like a **flood of water** arriving every millisecond from 10,000 taps simultaneously.
- **Data Handling Technologies** are the **plumbing, treatment plant, storage tanks, and distribution network** that:
  1. **Catch** the water (Acquisition)
  2. **Filter out debris** (Cleaning & Filtering)
  3. **Pump it at controlled pressure** (Stream Processing)
  4. **Store it in reservoirs** (Database / Data Lake)
  5. **Analyze for contaminants** (Analytics)
  6. **Deliver clean water on demand** to doctors' dashboards (Visualization / Action)

Without this **plumbing infrastructure**, the hospital would drown in unusable raw numbers. That infrastructure is precisely **Data Handling Technologies**.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic is a KTU Hot-Spot

- It is the **bridge** between Module 3 (sensing/actuation hardware) and Module 5 (analytics/AI/visualization).
- Examiners love asking **comparison questions** (e.g., SQL vs NoSQL, Edge vs Cloud, Batch vs Stream).
- Direct 14-mark Part B questions on **architectures, ETL pipelines, and storage selection** are common.

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the 4Vs and at least **3 real-world examples** of each storage/analytics technology for guaranteed marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IoT Data Lifecycle — 6 Logical Phases

The complete data handling pipeline in any IoT system can be decomposed into **six sequential phases**. KTU examiners often test the student's ability to map a real scenario to these phases.

### Phase 1: Data Acquisition (Sensing Layer)
- Sensors + microcontrollers (e.g., ESP32, Arduino, Raspberry Pi) sample the physical world.
- Sampling is governed by the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**.
- A pre-processor (often on the MCU) performs initial **signal conditioning** (amplification, noise filtering, ADC conversion).

### Phase 2: Data Pre-processing (Edge/Local Layer)
- **Cleaning**: Remove duplicates, handle missing values (imputation), correct outliers.
- **Transformation**: Normalization, unit conversion, timestamp synchronization.
- **Compression**: Reduce payload size using algorithms like **Huffman coding, LZ77, or delta-encoding**.
- **Feature Extraction**: Derive compact features (e.g., HRV from raw ECG).

### Phase 3: Data Ingestion & Transport (Network Layer)
- Protocols: **MQTT, CoAP, AMQP, HTTP/HTTPS, DDS**.
- Gateways aggregate and forward data using publish-subscribe (pub/sub) or request-response patterns.
- **QoS levels** in MQTT (0, 1, 2) define delivery guarantees.

### Phase 4: Data Storage (Persistence Layer)
- **Three-tier storage strategy**:
  1. **Edge/Local**: SQLite, InfluxDB, LevelDB (millisecond access)
  2. **Fog/Intermediate**: Redis, Cassandra (regional, fast access)
  3. **Cloud/Long-term**: S3 (HDFS), BigQuery, data lakes

### Phase 5: Data Processing & Analytics (Computation Layer)
- **Batch processing**: Hadoop MapReduce, Apache Spark (for historical, non-time-critical workloads).
- **Stream processing**: Apache Kafka + Spark Streaming, Apache Flink, AWS Kinesis (for real-time).
- **In-database analytics**: SQL aggregations on time-series DBs.

### Phase 6: Data Visualization & Action (Application Layer)
- Dashboards (Grafana, Kibana, Tableau), alerts, automated actuator control.
- Feedback loops close the IoT cycle.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Taxonomy of Data Storage Technologies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison** — examiners ask this in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks).

### 2.2.1 Relational Databases (RDBMS / SQL)
- **Examples**: MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server.
- **Structure**: Fixed-schema, tabular, ACID-compliant.
- **Best for**: Structured data, complex joins, transactional integrity (e.g., patient records, billing).
- **Drawback**: Poor horizontal scalability for IoT-scale data.

### 2.2.2 NoSQL Databases
- **Types**:
  - **Document**: MongoDB, CouchDB (JSON/BSON).
  - **Key-Value**: Redis, Amazon DynamoDB.
  - **Column-Family**: Apache Cassandra, HBase.
  - **Graph**: Neo4j.
- **Properties**: Schema-less, horizontally scalable, BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent).
- **Best for**: Semi-structured IoT data, high write throughput.

### 2.2.3 Time-Series Databases (TSDB) — *The IoT Specialist*
- **Examples**: InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, Prometheus, OpenTSDB, QuestDB.
- **Optimized for**: Timestamp-indexed data, high ingestion rates, downsampling, retention policies.
- **Why it matters for IoT**: Sensors emit timestamped data continuously — TSDBs are purpose-built.

### 2.2.4 Data Lakes
- **Concept**: Store raw data in native format (structured, semi, unstructured) until needed.
- **Examples**: AWS S3, Azure Data Lake, Hadoop HDFS.
- **Schema-on-read** philosophy: schema applied at query time, not ingest time.
- **Best for**: ML training, archival, exploratory analytics.

### 2.2.5 Data Warehouses
- **Concept**: Highly structured, schema-on-write, optimized for OLAP queries.
- **Examples**: Snowflake, Google BigQuery, Amazon Redshift.
- **Best for**: BI reporting, historical aggregations.

### 2.2.6 In-Memory Data Stores
- **Examples**: Redis, Memcached.
- **Latency**: Sub-millisecond.
- **Use**: Caching, session, real-time leaderboards, recent-sensor-buffer.

---

## 2.3 Edge, Fog, and Cloud Computing — The Computational Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most frequently asked 14-mark topic** in Module 4.

| Parameter | Edge Computing | Fog Computing | Cloud Computing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Location** | At/near the sensor/device | Intermediate LAN gateway | Remote data center |
| **Latency** | **< 1 ms** (real-time) | **1–10 ms** | **50–500 ms** |
| **Compute Power** | Limited (MCU-class) | Moderate (gateway/SBC) | Virtually unlimited |
| **Storage** | KB–MB (flash) | GB–TB (SSD) | PB–EB (object stores) |
| **Connectivity** | Often intermittent | Stable LAN | Always-on WAN |
| **Use Case** | Local control loops, alarms | Regional aggregation | Big-data analytics, ML |
| **Example** | ESP32 PID controller | Raspberry Pi gateway | AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub |

**The Push for Edge**: A Boeing 787 generates **~40 TB per flight**. Sending all of it to the cloud is infeasible. Edge filtering is mandatory.

---

## 2.4 ETL vs ELT — The Data Transformation Paradigms

### 2.4.1 ETL (Extract → Transform → Load)
- **Traditional** approach used in data warehousing.
- Data is **transformed before loading** into the target system.
- **Pros**: Clean data in warehouse, storage-efficient.
- **Cons**: Transformation bottleneck, slow for raw/exploratory workloads.

### 2.4.2 ELT (Extract → Load → Transform)
- **Modern** approach used with data lakes and cloud warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery).
- Raw data is loaded first, transformation happens at query time.
- **Pros**: Faster ingestion, retains raw fidelity, leverages cloud's compute power.
- **Cons**: Higher storage cost, requires strong governance.

---

## 2.5 Big Data Technologies in IoT

### 2.5.1 Apache Hadoop (Batch)
- **HDFS** (storage) + **MapReduce** (compute) + **YARN** (resource manager).
- Designed for **batch processing** of petabyte-scale data.
- High latency, not suitable for real-time.

### 2.5.2 Apache Spark (In-Memory Batch/Stream)
- **100× faster than MapReduce** for many workloads (in-memory computation).
- Components: **Spark SQL, Spark Streaming, MLlib, GraphX**.
- Supports both batch and micro-batch streaming.

### 2.5.3 Apache Kafka (Streaming)
- **Distributed event streaming platform** — the **nervous system** of modern IoT pipelines.
- **Topics, partitions, producers, consumers, brokers, ZooKeeper/KRaft**.
- **Throughput**: Millions of messages/sec.
- Acts as a **durable buffer** between IoT gateways and downstream consumers.

### 2.5.4 Apache Flink (True Stream Processing)
- True event-time streaming (vs Spark's micro-batch).
- Lower latency, exactly-once semantics.
- Used in financial IoT, industrial control.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and aligned with the KTU 2024 syllabus.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Nyquist Sampling Rate** | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | $f_s$ = sampling freq. (Hz), $f_{max}$ = highest signal freq. (Hz) |
| 2 | **Data Rate per Sensor** | $R = S \cdot B \cdot C$ | $R$ = rate (bps), $S$ = samples/s, $B$ = bits/sample, $C$ = channels |
| 3 | **Total Network Data Volume** | $V = N \cdot R \cdot T$ | $V$ = volume (bits), $N$ = number of sensors, $R$ = rate (bps), $T$ = time (s) |
| 4 | **Compression Ratio** | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 5 | **Compressed Data Volume** | $V_c = \dfrac{V}{CR}$ | $V_c$ = compressed size (bits) |
| 6 | **Storage Requirement per Day** | $S_d = N \cdot R \cdot 86400$ | $S_d$ = bytes/day (when $R$ in B/s) |
| 7 | **End-to-End Latency** | $L_{total} = L_{sense} + L_{proc} + L_{trans} + L_{queue}$ | Each $L$ in milliseconds |
| 8 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $BDP = BW \cdot RTT$ | $BW$ = bandwidth (bps), $RTT$ = round-trip time (s) — bits in flight |
| 9 | **Throughput (Little's Law)** | $X = \dfrac{\lambda}{1 - \rho}$ | $X$ = throughput, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $\rho$ = utilization |
| 10 | **MQTT QoS Delivery Guarantee** | QoS 0 ≤ QoS 1 ≤ QoS 2 (at most once → at least once → exactly once) | Reliability ordering |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE** — In KTU answer sheets, always **define your variables** before substituting into a formula. Examiners award 1 mark per formula *with variable definition*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Smart Ward Data Rate Calculation

### Problem Statement
A hospital smart ward has **200 patient beds**. Each bed has 5 sensors (ECG, SpO₂, temperature, respiration, BP), each sampling at **500 Hz** with **16-bit resolution**. Calculate:
- (a) Total data rate generated per second (in Mbps)
- (b) Daily data volume (in GB) before compression
- (c) Daily data volume (in GB) after 5:1 compression

### Given Data
- $N = 200$ beds
- $k = 5$ sensors per bed
- $S = 500$ samples/s
- $B = 16$ bits/sample

### (a) Total Data Rate

**Step 1: Per-sensor bit rate**

$$R_{sensor} = S \cdot B = 500 \cdot 16 = 8000 \text{ bits/s}$$

**Step 2: Per-bed bit rate** (5 sensors)

$$R_{bed} = k \cdot R_{sensor} = 5 \cdot 8000 = 40{,}000 \text{ bits/s} = 40 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3: Ward-wide bit rate** (200 beds)

$$R_{total} = N \cdot R_{bed} = 200 \cdot 40{,}000 = 8{,}000{,}000 \text{ bits/s}$$

**Step 4: Convert to Mbps**

$$R_{total} = \dfrac{8{,}000{,}000}{10^6} = 8 \text{ Mbps}$$

> **Answer (a):** $\boxed{R_{total} = 8 \text{ Mbps}}$

---

### (b) Daily Volume (Uncompressed)

**Step 1: Total seconds in a day**

$$T = 24 \cdot 3600 = 86{,}400 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Bits per day**

$$V = R_{total} \cdot T = 8 \times 10^6 \cdot 86{,}400 = 6.912 \times 10^{11} \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3: Convert to GB** (1 GB = $8 \times 10^9$ bits)

$$V_{GB} = \dfrac{6.912 \times 10^{11}}{8 \times 10^9} = 86.4 \text{ GB/day}$$

> **Answer (b):** $\boxed{V \approx 86.4 \text{ GB/day per ward}}$

---

### (c) Daily Volume (Compressed, CR = 5)

**Step 1: Apply compression ratio**

$$V_c = \dfrac{V_{GB}}{CR} = \dfrac{86.4}{5} = 17.28 \text{ GB/day}$$

> **Answer (c):** $\boxed{V_c \approx 17.28 \text{ GB/day}}$

---

### Engineering Interpretation
A single 200-bed ward generates **~86 GB/day raw** — equivalent to streaming 30 HD movies simultaneously. This justifies:
1. **Edge preprocessing** to reduce load.
2. **Lossy compression** (e.g., wavelet-based ECG compression) achieving CR of 10–20.
3. **Cloud archival** with cold-storage tiering (S3 Glacier).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Industrial IoT Latency Budgeting

### Problem Statement
A robotic assembly line has a closed-loop control. Sensor → Edge processor → Actuator. Compute the **end-to-end latency budget** and determine if real-time control (< 10 ms) is feasible.

### Given
- $L_{sense} = 1.2$ ms (ADC + sensor response)
- $L_{proc} = 0.8$ ms (edge inference)
- $L_{trans} = 0.5$ ms (local bus, e.g., CAN/EtherCAT)
- $L_{queue} = 2.0$ ms (scheduler jitter)
- $L_{act} = 1.5$ ms (actuator response)

### Calculation

$$L_{total} = L_{sense} + L_{proc} + L_{trans} + L_{queue} + L_{act}$$

$$L_{total} = 1.2 + 0.8 + 0.5 + 2.0 + 1.5 = 6.0 \text{ ms}$$

### Conclusion
Since $L_{total} = 6.0 \text{ ms} < 10 \text{ ms}$, the system **meets the real-time control requirement** with a **4 ms safety margin**.

> [!NOTE]
> If cloud processing were added (typical 50–200 ms), real-time control becomes **impossible**. This is the **canonical proof** of why edge computing is mandatory for control loops.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Throughput Analysis using Little's Law

### Problem Statement
A Kafka cluster receives IoT messages at an arrival rate $\lambda = 10{,}000$ messages/s. The system utilization is $\rho = 0.75$. Calculate the throughput.

### Calculation

$$X = \dfrac{\lambda}{1 - \rho} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{1 - 0.75} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{0.25} = 40{,}000 \text{ messages/s}$$

> **Interpretation:** While the **arrival** rate is 10,000 msg/s, the **throughput** under 75% utilization is 40,000 msg/s (because the system can handle more, but is throttled to the input rate in steady state).

---

## 3.4 Storage Selection Decision Matrix (Analytical Tool)

| Workload Property | Recommended Storage | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Timestamped sensor stream | **InfluxDB / TimescaleDB** | Time-series optimization, downsampling |
| Patient demographics + history | **PostgreSQL** | ACID, joins, structured |
| High-write IoT telemetry | **Cassandra / MongoDB** | Horizontal write scalability |
| ML training corpus (images/video) | **S3 / HDFS (Data Lake)** | Cheap object storage, schema-on-read |
| Sub-ms caching of recent values | **Redis** | In-memory, sub-ms latency |
| BI / reporting aggregations | **Snowflake / BigQuery** | Columnar OLAP, SQL-compatible |
| Knowledge graph of device relations | **Neo4j** | Graph traversal efficiency |

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Comparison — Batch vs Stream Processing

| Criterion | Batch (Hadoop/Spark Batch) | Stream (Flink/Kafka Streams) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Data Scope** | Historical, bounded | Real-time, unbounded |
| **Latency** | Minutes to hours | Milliseconds to seconds |
| **Throughput** | Very high (petabytes) | High (millions events/s) |
| **Complexity** | Lower (well-understood) | Higher (event-time, watermarks) |
| **Use Case** | Daily reports, model retraining | Anomaly detection, live alerts |
| **Example IoT Use** | Monthly energy billing | Real-time ECG arrhythmia alarm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IoT Data Handling End-to-End Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Sensor1] --> G1
    A2[Sensor2] --> G1
    A3[Sensor3] --> G1
    A4[Actuator1] --> G1
    G1[Edge Gateway] -->|MQTT/HTTPS| B1
    B1[Broker Kafka] --> C1
    B1 --> C2
    C1[Stream Processor Flink] --> D1
    C2[Batch Processor Spark] --> D2
    D1[(Time Series DB InfluxDB)] --> E1
    D2[(Data Lake S3 HDFS)] --> E2
    D1 --> F1
    E1[Analytics Engine ML] --> F1
    E2 --> F1
    F1[Dashboard Grafana] --> U1[End User Doctor Operator]
    F1 --> A4
```

**Reading the Flow:**

- Left column → Physical world (sensors/actuators)
- Middle columns → Edge (Gateway), Transport (Broker), Compute (Stream/Batch)
- Right column → Storage (TSDB + Data Lake) → Analytics → Visualization → User
- The arrow returning to `Actuator1` represents the **closed-loop feedback** characteristic of IoT.

---

## 4.2 Edge–Fog–Cloud Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EdgeLayer [Edge Layer on Device]
        E1[Sensor Microcontroller]
        E2[Local Storage Flash]
        E3[Real Time Control Loop]
    end
    subgraph FogLayer [Fog Layer Local Network]
        F1[Gateway Raspberry Pi]
        F2[Regional Aggregator]
        F3[Local Analytics]
    end
    subgraph CloudLayer [Cloud Layer Remote Data Center]
        C1[Ingestion Service Kafka]
        C2[Big Data Store S3]
        C3[ML Training Cluster]
        C4[Dashboard API]
    end
    E1 -->|LAN| F1
    F1 -->|WAN| C1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    E3 -.feedback.-> E1
```

**Key Observations for the Examiner:**

- **Vertical** arrows represent data flow upward (telemetry) and downward (control).
- **Horizontal** arrows represent lateral data movement within a layer.
- The **dotted feedback arrow** from `Real Time Control Loop` back to `Sensor Microcontroller` is the hallmark of edge intelligence.

---

## 4.3 ETL vs ELT — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ETLpipe [ETL Pipeline]
        S1[Source IoT Devices] --> E1[Extract]
        E1 --> T1[Transform Clean Filter Aggregate]
        T1 --> L1[Load into Warehouse]
        L1 --> W1[Data Warehouse Snowflake]
    end
    subgraph ELTpipe [ELT Pipeline]
        S2[Source IoT Devices] --> E2[Extract]
        E2 --> L2[Load Raw into Lake]
        L2 --> W2[Data Lake S3]
        W2 --> T2[Transform at Query Time]
        T2 --> A1[Analytics BI ML]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Data Storage Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Data Timestamped?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Sub Second Latency Needed?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{ACID Transactions Required?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| P1[InfluxDB Redis TSDB]
    Q2 -->|No| P2[TimescaleDB Cassandra]
    Q3 -->|Yes| P3[PostgreSQL MySQL]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Unstructured Data?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| P4[MongoDB Document Store]
    Q4 -->|No| P5[Columnar BigQuery Snowflake]
```

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU exam, when asked *"Which database would you use for X?"*, walk the examiner through this decision tree. It demonstrates **structured analytical thinking** — a high-value skill on the answer sheet.

---

## 4.5 MQTT Pub/Sub Broker Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Publisher Sensor1] -->|Publish topic ward1/ecg| BR[MQTT Broker Mosquitto]
    P2[Publisher Sensor2] -->|Publish topic ward1/spo2| BR
    BR -->|Subscribe| S1[Subscriber Analytics Engine]
    BR -->|Subscribe| S2[Subscriber Dashboard]
    BR -->|Subscribe| S3[Subscriber Storage Service]
```

**Key Takeaway:** The **broker decouples** publishers from subscribers — a critical property that allows IoT systems to scale and evolve independently.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

> *List and briefly explain the four "V"s of Big Data in the context of IoT.*

**Model Answer:**

The four V's of Big Data characterizing IoT data are:

1. **Volume** — IoT systems with millions of sensors generate **terabytes to petabytes** of data per day (e.g., a smart factory: 2 PB/day).
2. **Velocity** — Data is produced at **millisecond to second** rates, often requiring real-time processing (e.g., ECG at 500 Hz).
3. **Variety** — Data exists in multiple formats: structured (numeric readings), semi-structured (JSON, XML), and unstructured (image, video, audio).
4. **Veracity** — Data may be noisy, incomplete, or contain sensor drift; quality and trustworthiness must be validated.

*(Many KTU answers also accept the 5th V — **Value** — the actionable intelligence extracted from raw data.)*

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

> *Differentiate between SQL and NoSQL databases. Give one example of each suited for IoT data.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | SQL (RDBMS) | NoSQL |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Schema | Fixed, predefined | Schema-less, dynamic |
| Data model | Tabular (rows/columns) | Document, key-value, column, graph |
| Scalability | Vertical (scale-up) | Horizontal (scale-out) |
| Consistency | Strong ACID | BASE (eventual consistency) |
| Best for IoT | Patient records, billing | Telemetry, logs, sensor streams |
| Example | PostgreSQL | MongoDB / Cassandra |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Module-end questions offer **internal choice**. You must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Model Solution**

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

> *(a)* Explain the **three-tier IoT data storage architecture** with examples. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* Discuss **ETL and ELT** paradigms with a comparative diagram. Explain why ELT is preferred in modern cloud-based IoT systems. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Part A(a): Three-Tier Storage Architecture**

The three-tier storage architecture addresses the **trade-off between latency, capacity, and cost** at different points in the IoT pipeline:

**Tier 1 — Edge/Local Storage**
- *Location:* On the sensor node or local gateway.
- *Technology:* Flash memory, microSD, SQLite, InfluxDB-edge.
- *Capacity:* KB to few GB.
- *Latency:* Microseconds to milliseconds.
- *Purpose:* Buffering during network outages, local alarms, last-known-state retention.
- *Example:* An ESP32 sensor storing 24 hours of temperature data on SPIFFS/LittleFS.

**Tier 2 — Fog/Regional Storage**
- *Location:* Gateway or local server within the LAN.
- *Technology:* Redis, Cassandra, TimescaleDB, edge NAS.
- *Capacity:* GB to TB.
- *Latency:* 1–10 ms.
- *Purpose:* Regional aggregation, deduplication, protocol translation, secondary analytics.
- *Example:* A Raspberry Pi gateway running InfluxDB aggregating 200 ward sensors before cloud upload.

**Tier 3 — Cloud/Enterprise Storage**
- *Location:* Remote data center (AWS, Azure, GCP).
- *Technology:* S3/HDFS (data lake), Snowflake/BigQuery (warehouse), ElasticSearch (search).
- *Capacity:* PB to EB.
- *Latency:* 50–500 ms.
- *Purpose:* Long-term archival, big-data analytics, ML training, regulatory compliance.
- *Example:* AWS S3 storing 5 years of patient telemetry for HIPAA-compliant archival.

**Justification of the Three-Tier Design:**
- **Cost optimization:** Hot data on fast/expensive storage; cold data on cheap/archival storage.
- **Latency matching:** Real-time needs (Tier 1) vs analytical needs (Tier 3).
- **Resilience:** Local survival during WAN outages.

---

### **Model Solution — Part A(b): ETL vs ELT**

**ETL (Extract → Transform → Load):**
- Traditional data warehousing paradigm.
- Data is **transformed before** being loaded into the target warehouse.
- Transformations (cleaning, joining, aggregating) happen on a separate processing engine.
- *Example:* Apache NiFi ingesting CSV, Spark transforming, Snowflake receiving clean data.

**ELT (Extract → Load → Transform):**
- Modern cloud-native paradigm.
- Raw data is **loaded first** into a data lake or cloud warehouse.
- Transformations are performed **inside the warehouse** using its compute engine (e.g., Snowflake's virtual warehouses, BigQuery's slots).
- *Example:* AWS Kinesis Firehose → S3 (raw) → BigQuery (transform via SQL at query time).

**Why ELT is preferred in modern IoT systems:**

1. **Speed of ingestion** — No transformation bottleneck; raw data lands in seconds.
2. **Fidelity preservation** — Original sensor values retained for retrospective analysis.
3. **Cloud elasticity** — Cloud warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery) have decoupled compute, allowing massive parallel transformation.
4. **Schema flexibility** — Schema-on-read accommodates new sensor types without re-engineering ETL pipelines.
5. **Cost efficiency** — Storage (S3) is cheap; compute is billed per query.
6. **ML readiness** — Raw data is immediately usable for training pipelines (no re-extraction).

**Comparative Summary Table:**

| Aspect | ETL | ELT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Transform location | Separate engine | Inside warehouse |
| Ingestion speed | Slow (gated by transform) | Fast (raw landing) |
| Data fidelity | Transformed only | Raw + transformed |
| Best for | On-premise, stable schemas | Cloud, evolving IoT schemas |
| Example | Informatica + Oracle | Firehose + S3 + BigQuery |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Full Model Solution (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

> *(a)* Compare **Edge, Fog, and Cloud computing** paradigms in IoT with a suitable architecture diagram. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* With a neat diagram, explain the **Apache Kafka-based streaming pipeline** for IoT data. List any **four core components** of Kafka. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Part B(a): Edge vs Fog vs Cloud**

*Refer to the hierarchical diagram in SECTION 4.2 for the architecture.*

**Definition of Each Layer:**

- **Edge Computing:** Computation performed *at or near the data source* (on the sensor/device itself). Eliminates network round-trip. Latency in **sub-millisecond** range. Limited by device compute/power.

- **Fog Computing:** Intermediate layer between edge and cloud. Computation performed on **gateway devices** in the local network (LAN). Provides aggregation, filtering, and protocol translation. Latency in **1–10 ms** range.

- **Cloud Computing:** Remote, centralized data center with virtually unlimited compute and storage. Latency in **50–500 ms** range. Best for batch analytics, ML, and archival.

**Comparative Table:**

| Parameter | Edge | Fog | Cloud |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Latency | < 1 ms | 1–10 ms | 50–500 ms |
| Location | On device | LAN gateway | Remote DC |
| Compute | Limited (MCU) | Moderate (SBC) | Unlimited |
| Storage | KB–MB | GB–TB | PB–EB |
| Network dependence | None | Local only | WAN required |
| Use case | Closed-loop control | Regional analytics | Big-data ML |
| Example | ESP32 PID | Raspberry Pi gateway | AWS IoT Core |

**Why all three are needed:**
- Pure edge: too limited for analytics.
- Pure cloud: too slow for control loops.
- Hybrid: optimal balance — real-time at edge, regional insights at fog, global intelligence at cloud.

---

### **Model Solution — Part B(b): Apache Kafka Streaming Pipeline**

*Refer to the MQTT/Kafka flow diagram in SECTION 4.5 for visual support.*

**Apache Kafka** is a **distributed event-streaming platform** designed for high-throughput, fault-tolerant, real-time data pipelines. It acts as a **durable buffer** between IoT producers and consumers.

**Architecture Components:**

1. **Producer** — Publishes (writes) messages to Kafka topics. *Example:* An IoT gateway publishing temperature readings.
2. **Consumer** — Subscribes to (reads) messages from topics. *Example:* A Spark Streaming job consuming for analytics.
3. **Broker** — A Kafka server that stores and serves messages. A cluster has multiple brokers for fault tolerance.
4. **Topic** — A logical channel/feed to which messages are published. Topics are partitioned for parallelism.
5. **Partition** — A subdivision of a topic; messages within a partition are ordered.
6. **ZooKeeper / KRaft** — Coordination service for cluster management (modern Kafka uses KRaft, removing ZooKeeper).
7. **Consumer Group** — A set of consumers that jointly consume a topic, with each partition read by exactly one consumer.

**Pipeline Flow (IoT context):**

1. IoT sensors → **Producer API** → publish to `topic_iot_telemetry`.
2. **Broker** stores messages durably across partitions.
3. **Stream processor** (Flink/Spark Streaming) subscribes, processes in real-time.
4. Processed data lands in **TSDB** (InfluxDB) and **data lake** (S3).
5. **Dashboard** and **ML models** consume from the same topic for visualization and prediction.

**Why Kafka for IoT:**

- **Throughput:** Millions of messages/second per cluster.
- **Durability:** Messages persisted to disk with replication.
- **Decoupling:** Producers and consumers evolve independently.
- **Replay:** Consumers can re-read historical messages — critical for ML retraining.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 — Data Handling Technologies:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ETL with ELT** — Many students write them as identical. Examiners **deduct 2 marks** for failing to emphasize the *location* of transformation.
> 2. **Writing "Cloud is best" universally** — Cloud has high latency and is unsuitable for control loops. Always state **"depends on use case"** and justify.
> 3. **Forgetting to define variables in formulas** — A formula without variable definitions loses **1 full mark** in numerical problems.
> 4. **Naming a database without justifying selection** — Saying "use MongoDB" is incomplete; you must say *"because the data is semi-structured JSON with high write throughput"*.
> 5. **Missing the 4Vs in Big Data questions** — Examiners award **0.75 mark per V**; missing even one costs you nearly a full mark.
> 6. **No diagram in 14-mark answers** — KTU valuation keys assign **1–2 marks** for a labeled diagram. Always include one.
> 7. **Ignoring the closed-loop nature of IoT** — Data handling is not a one-way pipe; the **actuation feedback** is part of the system.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Data Handling Technologies**

- ✅ IoT data is defined by the **4Vs**: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity (+ Value).
- ✅ **Nyquist Theorem**: Sample at ≥ 2× the highest signal frequency to avoid aliasing.
- ✅ **Data Rate** = Samples/s × Bits/sample × Channels.
- ✅ **Three-Tier Storage**: Edge (real-time, small) → Fog (regional, medium) → Cloud (archival, large).
- ✅ **Time-Series Databases** (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) are purpose-built for IoT telemetry.
- ✅ **NoSQL** types: Document, Key-Value, Column-Family, Graph — choose by data shape.
- ✅ **SQL** is for ACID transactions and complex joins (e.g., patient records).
- ✅ **Data Lake** = raw storage, schema-on-read; **Data Warehouse** = transformed, schema-on-write.
- ✅ **Edge** = sub-ms latency, no network; **Cloud** = high latency, unlimited compute.
- ✅ **ETL** transforms before load; **ELT** loads raw, transforms at query (cloud-native).
- ✅ **Apache Kafka** = distributed log; components include Producer, Consumer, Broker, Topic, Partition, Consumer Group, ZooKeeper/KRaft.
- ✅ **Apache Spark** = in-memory batch/micro-batch; **Flink** = true stream processing.
- ✅ **MQTT** is the de-facto IoT pub/sub protocol with QoS 0/1/2.
- ✅ **Closed-loop IoT** = data → analytics → action → sensor (feedback).
- ✅ **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)** = BW × RTT = bits in flight.
- ✅ **Little's Law** governs queueing: Throughput = λ / (1 − ρ).
- ✅ Always **include a labeled diagram** in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ Always **justify technology selection** with workload characteristics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Acquisition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Acquisition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Data Acquisition (DAQ)** is the process of sampling, conditioning, converting, and digitising real-world **physiological or environmental signals** so that they can be processed, stored, analysed, and transmitted by digital systems such as microcontrollers, edge processors, and cloud servers in an **Internet of Things (IoT)** framework.

In the context of **biomedical IoT**, Data Acquisition refers specifically to the chain that captures biopotential signals (ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG, body temperature, SpO₂, blood pressure, respiration, glucose, etc.) from the human body and converts them into a digital stream suitable for **wireless transmission, edge-AI inference, and remote clinical monitoring**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** A biomedical Data Acquisition System (DAS) is an *integrated electronic assembly* comprising **sensors → signal conditioning → multiplexer → Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) → digital interface → IoT communication module**, designed to faithfully translate the patient's physiological state into structured, time-stamped digital data.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Five Human Senses Made Electronic"

Imagine a patient lying on a hospital bed. The nurse is the **Data Acquisition System**.

| Human Body Function | Biomedical DAQ Equivalent | KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| 👁️ Eyes (senses light/colour) | Photodetector / Pulse-oximeter (SpO₂) sensor | Optical Sensor |
| 👂 Ears (senses sound/vibration) | Stethoscope microphone / Phonocardiogram | Acoustic Sensor |
| ✋ Skin (senses pressure/temp) | Temperature IC (LM35), Pressure transducer | Resistive / Thermal Sensor |
| 💓 Heart (electrical rhythm) | ECG electrodes + biopotential amplifier | Biopotential Electrode |
| 🧠 Brain (electrical activity) | EEG scalp electrodes | Surface Electrode |

> **Intuition:** Just as the human nervous system turns a *physical sensation* (heat, pain, sound) into a *nerve impulse* the brain can understand, a DAQ system turns a *physical biomedical signal* into a *binary number* a microcontroller can understand.

---

## 1.3 The Five Building Blocks of Any DAQ System

> [!NOTE]
> Every KTU 2024 exam question on this topic is essentially a re-arrangement of these **five canonical blocks**:

1. **Sensor / Transducer** – converts the physiological variable (e.g., heart's electrical potential) into an electrical signal.
2. **Signal Conditioning** – amplifies, filters, isolates, and linearises the raw sensor output.
3. **Multiplexer (MUX)** – sequentially routes multiple channels to a single ADC (cost-effective design).
4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** – the *core* of the system; converts continuous-time continuous-amplitude signals into discrete codes.
5. **Digital Interface / IoT Link** – SPI / I²C / UART / BLE / Wi-Fi for transmission to gateway, smartphone, or cloud.

---

## 1.4 Standard DAQ Performance Metrics (Bold = Board-Exam Favourite)

- **Sampling Rate (fs)** — samples/second
- **Resolution (n bits)** — discrete voltage step
- **Input Range (VFS)** — full-scale voltage window
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — **6.02·N + 1.76 dB**
- **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** — typically **> 80 dB** for medical amplifiers
- **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** — must be **< 1 %** in ECG/EEG
- **Input Impedance** — must be **> 10 MΩ** for biopotential electrodes

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline — "How a Heartbeat Becomes a Cloud Reading"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners love asking the question: *"Explain the block diagram of a biomedical data acquisition system."* Memorise this chain:

**Step 1 — Sensing:**  
Surface Ag/AgCl electrodes placed on the body pick up ionic currents (millivolts, high source impedance).

**Step 2 — Signal Conditioning (the 4 sub-blocks):**  
- *Pre-amplification* → **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** with high CMRR (>100 dB) and high input impedance (>10 GΩ).  
- *Filtering* → Bandpass filter (e.g., 0.5 Hz – 40 Hz for ECG; 0.5 Hz – 100 Hz for EEG).  
- *Isolation* → Optocoupler / transformer isolation (galvanic isolation for patient safety — IEC 60601).  
- *Level Shifting* → Centres the bipolar biopotential signal around the ADC's mid-scale reference.

**Step 3 — Multiplexing:**  
An 8:1 or 16:1 analog MUX (e.g., CD4051, ADG726) sequentially connects multiple patient channels to a single ADC, reducing **hardware cost** and **power budget** — critical for wearable IoT.

**Step 4 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion:**  
A Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC samples the conditioned waveform. For ECG: typical fs = **500 Hz**, resolution = **12-bit or 16-bit**. For EEG: fs = **256 Hz – 1 kHz**, resolution = **24-bit** (Σ-Δ).

**Step 5 — Digital Processing & Transmission:**  
The MCU (e.g., ESP32, STM32) packetises the data with a timestamp, optionally compresses it, and transmits it over BLE / Wi-Fi to a gateway, smartphone, or AWS IoT Core.

---

## 2.2 Sampling Theorem — The Heartbeat of Digital DAQ

> [!NOTE]
> **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem:** A bandlimited signal of maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples **if and only if** the sampling frequency is greater than twice the highest frequency component.

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

The minimum acceptable rate $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**. Sampling below this causes **aliasing** — the high frequencies "fold" onto lower ones, producing a false reading (e.g., an aliased ECG that looks like a slow tremor instead of a fast tachycardia).

**Real-world rule for biomedical DAQ (KTU favourite):**  
Engineers sample at **5× to 10×** the Nyquist rate to leave a *guard band* for practical anti-aliasing filters. Hence:
- ECG: $f_s \approx 500 \text{ Hz}$ (since $f_{max} \approx 40$ Hz)
- EEG: $f_s \approx 256$–$500$ Hz
- EMG: $f_s \approx 1$–$5$ kHz
- Heart-sound (PCG): $f_s \approx 4$–$8$ kHz

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM INSTRUCTION:** All formulas below are routinely tested in KTU Part-A and Part-B questions. Memorise the symbol, the unit, and the typical biomedical value.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Biomedical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | ECG: 500 Hz, EEG: 256–500 Hz |
| 2 | Alias Frequency | $f_{alias} = \vert f_s - f_{in} \vert$ | $f_{in} > f_s/2$ → alias |
| 3 | Quantisation Step (LSB) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | Volts / LSB |
| 4 | Quantisation Error | $e_q = \pm \dfrac{\Delta V}{2}$ | $e_q = \pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB |
| 5 | Theoretical SNR of N-bit ADC | $SNR = 6.02\,N + 1.76 \text{ dB}$ | For $N = 12$, SNR ≈ 74 dB |
| 6 | Dynamic Range | $DR = 20 \log_{10}(2^n) = 6.02\,n \text{ dB}$ | dB |
| 7 | Total Data Rate | $R = n \times f_s \times N_{ch}$ | bits / second |
| 8 | Throughput Time | $T_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | seconds / sample |
| 9 | CMRR (dB) | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | $> 80$ dB medical |
| 10 | Anti-alias Filter Cutoff | $f_c \leq \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | Hz |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Hospitals:** ICU multi-parameter monitors acquire ECG, SpO₂, BP, respiration *simultaneously* through MUX + ADC.
> - **Wearables (Apple Watch, Fitbit):** Use **Σ-Δ ADCs** for high-resolution photoplethysmography (PPG).
> - **Implantable devices (Pacemaker):** Ultra-low-power SAR ADC with $f_s = 256$ Hz to preserve battery life.
> - **Remote patient monitoring (RPM):** ESP32 + ADS1115 16-bit Δ-Σ ADC is the most common KTU lab kit.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are **four complete, hand-derivation-ready problems** modelled on KTU 2024 ESE Part-B questions. Each line of the algebra is shown; do not skip steps in your answer script.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantisation Step (LSB) of an ADC

**Given:** A 12-bit ADC with full-scale input range $V_{FS} = 3.3$ V (typical of an ESP32 / STM32 reference).

**Find:** (a) The voltage represented by 1 LSB. (b) The maximum quantisation error. (c) The theoretical SNR.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Total number of quantisation levels for an $N$-bit ADC:
$$L = 2^N$$

For $N = 12$:
$$L = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ levels}$$

Step 2 — Apply the quantisation-step formula (the LSB weight):
$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N - 1}$$

Step 3 — Substitute $V_{FS} = 3.3$ V and $2^N - 1 = 4095$:
$$\Delta V = \frac{3.3}{4095} \text{ V/LSB}$$

Step 4 — Numerical evaluation:
$$\Delta V = 8.061 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V/LSB} = 806.1 \, \mu\text{V/LSB}$$

Step 5 — Maximum quantisation error is half of one LSB:
$$e_q = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm 403.05 \, \mu\text{V}$$

Step 6 — Theoretical SNR of an ideal $N$-bit ADC (ramp-input approximation):
$$SNR = 6.02\,N + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

Step 7 — Substitute $N = 12$:
$$SNR = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}$$

**Result:**
- $\Delta V = 806.1 \, \mu\text{V/LSB}$  
- $e_q = \pm 403 \, \mu\text{V}$  
- $SNR = 74.0$ dB

> **Engineering Insight:** An ECG signal of amplitude 1 mV (R-wave) occupies only $\dfrac{1 \text{ mV}}{806 \, \mu\text{V}} \approx 1.24$ LSB on this 12-bit ADC. That is why **clinical-grade ECG systems use 16-bit or 24-bit ADCs** to give a larger dynamic range.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Sampling Rate for an ECG Signal (Nyquist)

**Given:** ECG useful bandwidth = 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz. The signal is to be sampled for digital storage.

**Find:** (a) Nyquist rate. (b) Recommended practical sampling rate. (c) Data rate per channel for a 12-bit ADC.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Identify the maximum frequency component:
$$f_{max} = 150 \text{ Hz}$$

Step 2 — Apply the Nyquist criterion:
$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 150 = 300 \text{ Hz}$$

Step 3 — Choose a practical factor of 5 (engineering safety margin):
$$f_{s,\text{practical}} = 5 \times f_{max} = 5 \times 150 = 750 \text{ Hz}$$

Step 4 — Compute per-sample bit count (12 bits):
$$R_{\text{sample}} = 12 \text{ bits/sample}$$

Step 5 — Compute per-channel data rate:
$$R = n \cdot f_s = 12 \times 750 = 9000 \text{ bits/s} = 9 \text{ kbps}$$

Step 6 — Convert to bytes per second for storage planning:
$$R = \frac{9000}{8} = 1125 \text{ bytes/s} \approx 1.1 \text{ kB/s}$$

Step 7 — For a 24-hour Holter recording of a single channel:
$$D_{24h} = 1.1 \text{ kB/s} \times 86400 \text{ s} = 95{,}040 \text{ kB} \approx 92.8 \text{ MB}$$

**Result:** The recommended $f_s = 750$ Hz gives a single-channel data rate of **9 kbps** and a 24-h Holter file of **~93 MB**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Aliasing: At What Frequency Does the Alias Appear?

**Given:** A 50 Hz mains interference (hum) is inadvertently sampled at $f_s = 200$ Hz. The ECG amplifier's bandpass filter fails to remove it.

**Find:** (a) Does aliasing occur? (b) At what apparent frequency will the 50 Hz component appear after sampling?

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Compute the Nyquist frequency:
$$f_N = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{200}{2} = 100 \text{ Hz}$$

Step 2 — Compare input frequency to Nyquist:
$$f_{in} = 50 \text{ Hz} \leq f_N = 100 \text{ Hz} \; \Rightarrow \; \text{No aliasing on this component}$$

Step 3 — Now consider the *third* harmonic of mains interference, $f_{in} = 150$ Hz (this appears in many clinical environments):

$$f_{in} = 150 \text{ Hz} > f_N = 100 \text{ Hz} \; \Rightarrow \; \text{ALIASING occurs}$$

Step 4 — Compute the aliased frequency:
$$f_{alias} = \vert f_{in} - f_s \vert = \vert 150 - 200 \vert = 50 \text{ Hz}$$

Step 5 — Note: A 150 Hz component masquerades as 50 Hz — it becomes *indistinguishable* from the fundamental mains hum, distorting the ECG.

**Result:** The 150 Hz harmonic folds back to **50 Hz**, polluting the ECG baseline. *This is why notch filters at 50 Hz / 60 Hz are mandatory in any KTU DAQ design.*

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bits Required to Resolve a 10 µV EEG Micro-Signal

**Given:** The ADC has $V_{FS} = 4.096$ V. The smallest EEG micro-voltage of interest is $V_{min} = 10 \, \mu\text{V}$.

**Find:** Number of bits $N$ required to resolve this signal.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Set the LSB equal to (or smaller than) the smallest resolvable voltage:
$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N - 1} \leq V_{min}$$

Step 2 — Rearrange for $2^N$:
$$2^N - 1 \geq \frac{V_{FS}}{V_{min}}$$

Step 3 — Substitute $V_{FS} = 4.096$ V and $V_{min} = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ V:
$$2^N \geq \frac{4.096}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = 409{,}600$$

Step 4 — Take $\log_2$ of both sides:
$$N \geq \log_2(409600) = \frac{\log_{10}(409600)}{\log_{10}(2)}$$

Step 5 — Numerator:
$$\log_{10}(409600) = 5.6124$$

Step 6 — Denominator:
$$\log_{10}(2) = 0.30103$$

Step 7 — Final division:
$$N \geq \frac{5.6124}{0.30103} \approx 18.64$$

Step 8 — Round up to the next whole bit:
$$N = 19 \text{ bits (minimum)}$$

**Result:** A minimum of **19-bit resolution** is required, which is precisely why EEG systems use **24-bit Σ-Δ ADCs** (e.g., ADS1299 used in OpenBCI).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — ADC Architecture Trade-offs

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is *very* frequently asked in KTU as a comparison question.

| Feature | Flash ADC | SAR ADC | Σ-Δ (Sigma-Delta) ADC | Dual-Slope ADC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Speed | **Fastest** (ns) | Medium (µs) | Slow (ms) | Very slow |
| Resolution | 4 – 8 bit | 8 – 18 bit | **16 – 24 bit** | 16 – 24 bit |
| Power | High | Medium | Low | Medium |
| Cost | High ($2^N$ comparators) | Medium | Low (digital-heavy) | Low |
| Best For | Oscilloscopes, video | **Microcontrollers, ECG, IoT** | **EEG, precision sensors** | Digital voltmeters |
| Biomedical Use | Rare | **Wearable, Holter, bedside monitor** | **Implantable, EEG, hearing aids** | Diagnostic labs |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Block Diagram of a Biomedical Data Acquisition System

> [!NOTE]
> This single diagram is sufficient to earn **7 marks** in a KTU Part-B question. Trace every arrow; label every block.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Patient Body]
        A1[ECG Electrode] --> A2[EEG Electrode]
        A2 --> A3[SpO2 Probe]
        A3 --> A4[Temp Sensor]
    end

    A --> B[Pre Amplifier INA]

    B --> C1[Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 40 Hz]
    C1 --> C2[Notch Filter 50 Hz]
    C2 --> C3[Isolation Optocoupler]
    C3 --> C4[Level Shifter]

    C4 --> D[Analog Multiplexer MUX]

    D --> E[Sample and Hold Circuit]

    E --> F[SAR ADC 12 bit]

    F --> G[Microcontroller STM32]

    G --> H1[SPI Display]
    G --> H2[BLE Module]
    G --> H3[WiFi Module]

    H2 --> I[Smartphone Gateway]
    H3 --> J[Cloud Server AWS IoT]
    I --> J
```

---

## 4.2 ADC Internal Architecture (SAR — Most Common in IoT)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Vin[Analog Input] --> SHS[Sample and Hold]
    SHS --> COMP[Comparator]
    COMP --> SAR[SAR Logic Register]
    SAR --> DAC[Internal DAC]
    DAC --> COMP
    SAR --> OUT[Parallel Digital Output N bit]

    CLK[Clock Source] --> SAR

    EOC[End of Conversion Flag] --> SAR
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to narrate this in an exam:** "The input voltage is held by the S/H. The SAR register performs a binary search: starting with the MSB, it sets each bit, the internal DAC converts it to an analog voltage, the comparator decides whether the input is above or below, and the SAR bit is committed. After N cycles, the digital code equals the analog input."

---

## 4.3 Sampling and Quantisation — Discrete-Time to Discrete-Value

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CT[Continuous Time Signal] --> SS[Sample at fs]
    SS --> SH[Hold for Tconv]
    SH --> Q[Quantise to N levels]
    Q --> DC[Digital Code 0 to 2N minus 1]
```

---

## 4.4 Data Acquisition Workflow in IoT — End-to-End

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Patient Physiological Signal] --> S[Sensor Front End]
    S --> SC[Signal Conditioning Chain]
    SC --> MUX[Multiplexer]
    MUX --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> MCU[Edge Microcontroller ESP32]
    MCU --> DB[Local Database / SD Card]
    MCU --> TX[Wireless Transceiver BLE / WiFi]
    TX --> GW[Gateway Smartphone]
    GW --> CL[Cloud Platform]
    CL --> DL[Doctor Dashboard Alert]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, Set A]**

*Define a Data Acquisition System. List its main functional blocks.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** is an electronic system that samples, conditions, converts, and digitises real-world analog signals (such as biomedical, temperature, or pressure signals) so that they can be processed and stored by digital systems.

Main functional blocks:
1. **Sensor / Transducer** — converts the physical quantity into an electrical signal.
2. **Signal Conditioning** — amplification, filtering, isolation, level-shifting.
3. **Multiplexer** — routes multiple channels to a single ADC.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — converts analog to digital code.
5. **Digital Interface / Display / Communication** — transfers data to controller, storage, or IoT link.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP, Set B]**

*State the Nyquist sampling theorem. What is aliasing?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem** states that a bandlimited analog signal containing frequency components up to a maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be completely reconstructed from its discrete samples, provided the sampling frequency satisfies:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

The minimum sampling rate, $f_s = 2 f_{max}$, is called the **Nyquist rate**.

**Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which frequency components above $f_s/2$ get "folded back" into the lower baseband, producing a false lower-frequency signal that is indistinguishable from the original. It is prevented by using a low-pass **anti-aliasing filter** before the ADC with cutoff frequency $f_c \leq f_s/2$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE Model QP – Module 4, Attempt A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a biomedical data acquisition system used for ECG monitoring. Describe the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12-bit ADC has a unipolar full-scale input range of 0 to 5 V. Determine: (i) the LSB weight (in mV), (ii) the maximum quantisation error, (iii) the theoretical SNR in dB. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

> [!NOTE]
> 7-mark descriptive answer — examiners award marks for: (i) labelled block diagram [3 marks], (ii) role of each block [3 marks], (iii) conclusion [1 mark].

**Block Diagram Description:**

1. **ECG Electrodes (Ag/AgCl)** — three limb electrodes + six precordial electrodes capture the cardiac potential difference (≈ 1 mV).
2. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA-118 / AD620)** — high CMRR (>100 dB), high input impedance, gain ≈ 1000.
3. **Band-Pass Filter (0.05 Hz – 150 Hz)** — removes baseline wander and high-frequency muscle artefacts.
4. **Notch Filter (50 Hz / 60 Hz)** — eliminates mains hum.
5. **Isolation Amplifier / Optocoupler** — patient electrical safety per IEC 60601.
6. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** — holds the analog voltage stable during ADC conversion.
7. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (SAR, 12-bit, 500 Hz)** — converts to digital codes.
8. **Microcontroller (STM32 / ESP32)** — packetises, timestamps, and forwards the data.
9. **Wireless Link (BLE / Wi-Fi)** — transmits to smartphone or cloud.
10. **Display / Cloud Dashboard** — visualises waveform and triggers alerts.

**Conclusion:** The above chain transforms a 1 mV cardiac potential into a robust, time-stamped digital record suitable for **IoT-based remote cardiac monitoring**.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Given:** $N = 12$ bits, $V_{FS} = 5$ V, unipolar (i.e., 0 to 5 V).

**(i) LSB weight:**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N - 1} = \frac{5}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{5}{4095}$$

$$\Delta V = 1.221 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 1.221 \text{ mV/LSB}$$

**(ii) Maximum quantisation error:**

$$e_q = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \frac{1.221}{2} = \pm 0.6105 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) Theoretical SNR:**

$$SNR = 6.02\,N + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}$$

**Final Result:** $\Delta V = 1.221$ mV, $e_q = \pm 0.6105$ mV, $SNR = 74.0$ dB.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE Model QP – Module 4, Attempt B]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** with a neat block diagram. State two advantages and two disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EEG signal has a useful frequency range of 0.5 Hz to 100 Hz. The system uses a 16-bit ADC with $V_{FS} = 2.048$ V. Calculate: (i) the Nyquist rate, (ii) a practically safe sampling rate, (iii) the LSB size in µV, and (iv) whether this ADC can resolve a 1 µV EEG micro-signal. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners expect: (i) block diagram + labels [2 marks], (ii) step-by-step working [3 marks], (iii) advantages/disadvantages [2 marks].

**Block Diagram:** (Refer SECTION 4.2 above for the SAR-ADC Mermaid diagram.)

**Working Principle:**

1. On a *Start of Conversion* pulse, the SAR register is initialised with the MSB = 1, all other bits = 0 (a trial code of 1000…0).
2. The internal **DAC** converts this trial code into an analog voltage $V_{DAC}$.
3. The **comparator** checks whether $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$.
4. If yes, the MSB remains 1; if no, the MSB is reset to 0. The decision is **latched**.
5. The SAR then proceeds to the next bit, repeating the binary-search decision.
6. After $N$ clock cycles, all $N$ bits are decided and the **End of Conversion** (EOC) flag is raised.
7. The output latch releases the parallel digital code.

**Advantages:** (i) High speed at moderate resolution (1 MSPS typical). (ii) Low power, ideal for battery-powered IoT and wearable biomedical devices.

**Disadvantages:** (i) Requires a precise internal DAC. (ii) Performance drops if the input changes during conversion (hence the need for a sample-and-hold front end).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Given:** $f_{max} = 100$ Hz, $N = 16$ bits, $V_{FS} = 2.048$ V, $V_{min,\text{target}} = 1 \, \mu\text{V}$.

**(i) Nyquist rate:**

$$f_s = 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 100 = 200 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Practically safe sampling rate (5× margin):**

$$f_{s,\text{safe}} = 5 \times f_{max} = 500 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iii) LSB size:**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N - 1} = \frac{2.048}{65535}$$

$$\Delta V = 3.125 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V} = 31.25 \, \mu\text{V/LSB}$$

**(iv) Can the ADC resolve 1 µV?**

Since the LSB weight is $31.25 \, \mu\text{V}$, any signal below $31.25 \, \mu\text{V}$ falls *within one quantisation step* and cannot be distinguished from zero.

$$\frac{V_{min,\text{target}}}{\Delta V} = \frac{1 \, \mu\text{V}}{31.25 \, \mu\text{V}} = 0.032 \text{ LSB} \ll 1 \text{ LSB}$$

> **Conclusion:** A 16-bit ADC with 2.048 V range **cannot** resolve a 1 µV EEG micro-signal. To resolve 1 µV, we would need a higher-resolution ADC (≥ 21 bits) or a smaller full-scale range with programmable gain (PGA).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 (Data Acquisition):**
> 1. **Confusing $2^N$ with $2^N - 1$ in the LSB formula.** For *unipolar* ranges, the standard textbook form is $\Delta V = V_{FS}/(2^N - 1)$. Using $2^N$ gives an incorrect (smaller) LSB. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the $+1.76$ dB term in SNR.** The full form is $6.02N + 1.76$ dB. Writing only $6.02N$ costs 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Not stating the anti-aliasing filter.** When describing a DAQ block diagram, you *must* include an anti-alias low-pass filter *before* the S/H and ADC. Skipping it loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up sampling rate with conversion rate.** $f_s$ is the *input sampling frequency*; the ADC *conversion time* is $1/f_{CLK}$ per bit (for SAR). Many students interchange them.
> 5. **Drawing the SAR-ADC block diagram without showing the DAC feedback loop.** The defining feature of a SAR ADC is the *internal DAC feeding back into the comparator*. Without it, the diagram is incomplete and earns only 1 of 2 diagram marks.
> 6. **In biomedical DAQ, never omit patient isolation (IEC 60601).** Examiners award a bonus mark for explicitly mentioning galvanic isolation / optocoupler.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ DAQ = **Sensors → Signal Conditioning → MUX → ADC → Digital Interface / IoT**.
- ✅ Nyquist Theorem: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — anything less causes **aliasing**.
- ✅ Alias frequency formula: $f_{alias} = \vert f_{in} - f_s \vert$ (when $f_{in} > f_s/2$).
- ✅ Quantisation step (LSB): $\Delta V = V_{FS}/(2^N - 1)$.
- ✅ Quantisation error: $e_q = \pm \Delta V/2 = \pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB.
- ✅ SNR of N-bit ADC: $SNR_{dB} = 6.02\,N + 1.76$.
- ✅ ECG typical $f_s$: **500 Hz**, resolution **12–16 bit**, SNR **> 70 dB**.
- ✅ EEG typical $f_s$: **256–500 Hz**, resolution **24-bit Σ-Δ** (e.g., ADS1299).
- ✅ EMG typical $f_s$: **1–5 kHz** (due to wider bandwidth 20 Hz – 500 Hz).
- ✅ Instrumentation Amplifier is **mandatory** for biopotential front-end (high CMRR, high Zin).
- ✅ Anti-aliasing filter is **mandatory before** the ADC (cutoff $\leq f_s/2$).
- ✅ 50 Hz / 60 Hz notch filter is **mandatory** for ECG/EEG.
- ✅ SAR ADC = **most common in IoT wearables**; Σ-Δ ADC = **best for EEG/precision**; Flash = fastest but rare in medical.
- ✅ Patient **galvanic isolation** (IEC 60601) is non-negotiable in clinical DAQ.
- ✅ Multiplexing (CD4051, ADG726) reduces ADC count and **power** — critical for battery-operated IoT devices.
- ✅ Wearable IoT DAQ = ESP32 / STM32 + ADS1115 (16-bit Δ-Σ) + BLE/Wi-Fi link.
- ✅ Cloud connectivity: AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, ThingSpeak — used for remote patient monitoring.
- ✅ Data rate formula: $R = N \times f_s \times N_{ch}$ (bits/s) — used for storage and bandwidth planning.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Storage in IoT** refers to the systematic capture, organization, retention, retrieval, and management of heterogeneous data generated by networked sensors, actuators, biomedical instruments, and edge devices across the IoT ecosystem. It encompasses the selection of appropriate storage media, database architectures (SQL/NoSQL/Time-Series), data formats, and cloud/edge/fog deployment models to ensure **data integrity, availability, scalability, and security** for downstream analytics, machine learning, and clinical decision support.

In the biomedical IoT context, data storage additionally involves compliance with healthcare data standards (**HL7, DICOM, FHIR**), regulatory frameworks (**HIPAA, GDPR**), and the management of high-velocity physiological signals (ECG, EEG, EMG, SpO2) alongside low-velocity metadata.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hospital Filing Cabinet Analogy** 🏥
> Imagine a massive hospital receiving **thousands of patient records every second** from wearable heart monitors, ICU ventilators, glucose sensors, and imaging machines. Some records are tiny heartbeats (**small, frequent, time-stamped**), some are large MRI scans (**bulky, infrequent, complex**), and some need to be accessed **instantly** (e.g., during surgery) while others can wait (e.g., monthly health reports). **Data Storage** is essentially the hospital's *smart digital filing system* — deciding *where* each record goes, *how* it's filed, *who* can access it, and *how long* it's kept.

The "filing system" is the **Database**, the "filing room" is the **Storage Infrastructure** (local SSD, edge gateway, fog server, or cloud data center), and the "filing rules" are the **Data Models** (relational tables vs. flexible JSON documents).

## 1.3 Core Classification of Data Storage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4-Layer Storage Hierarchy in IoT-Biomedical Systems**

| Layer | Location | Example | Latency | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Device Storage** | On the sensor/actuator | Flash memory, EEPROM | < 1 ms | Temporary buffering |
| **Edge Storage** | Gateway / Local server | Raspberry Pi, local NAS | 1–10 ms | Real-time alerts |
| **Fog Storage** | LAN-level intermediate node | Hospital server, fog node | 10–50 ms | Local analytics |
| **Cloud Storage** | Remote data center | AWS S3, Azure Blob, GCP | 50–500 ms | Long-term archival |

## 1.4 Data Types in Biomedical IoT

> [!NOTE]
> Biomedical IoT generates **three primary data categories**:
> 1. **Structured Data** — Tabular, schema-defined (e.g., patient demographics, vital signs in CSV/tables)
> 2. **Semi-Structured Data** — Loosely tagged (e.g., HL7 messages, JSON-formatted sensor packets, XML DICOM headers)
> 3. **Unstructured Data** — Binary blobs (e.g., MRI/CT images, raw ECG waveforms, audio from stethoscopes)

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of IoT Data Storage

### Step 1: Data Ingestion
Sensors capture biomedical signals (e.g., ECG at **250 Hz**, SpO2 at **1 Hz**). The gateway aggregates these into **packets** containing a timestamp, device ID, and payload.

### Step 2: Pre-processing & Filtering
Edge devices perform **noise reduction, compression, and feature extraction** to reduce storage burden. For example, an ECG stream at 250 samples/sec × 12 bits = **3 kbps per channel**.

### Step 3: Routing Decision
A **storage policy engine** decides whether data goes to:
- **Hot storage** (frequently accessed, low-latency SSD)
- **Warm storage** (moderately accessed, HDD)
- **Cold storage** (archival, tape or glacier-class)

### Step 4: Persistence
Data is written to the chosen storage tier using appropriate **write strategies**:
- **Write-Through** — synchronously written to all tiers
- **Write-Back** — asynchronously flushed
- **Write-Around** — bypasses cache, goes directly to disk

### Step 5: Indexing & Cataloging
Metadata (patient ID, timestamp, modality) is indexed in a **catalog database** (often a NoSQL key-value store like **Amazon DynamoDB** or **MongoDB**) for rapid retrieval.

### Step 6: Retrieval & Analytics
Authorized applications (clinical dashboards, ML models) query the storage using standardized protocols (**REST APIs, GraphQL, SQL, DICOMweb**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in biomedical IoT:** A single ICU generates **~1000 GB/day** of multimodal data. Without hierarchical storage and intelligent routing, real-time clinical decision-making would be crippled by network and storage bottlenecks.

## 2.2 Database Selection Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Choosing the Right Database — KTU High-Yield Decision Matrix**

| Criteria | SQL (Relational) | NoSQL (Document) | Time-Series | Object Store |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Schema** | Fixed, rigid | Flexible, schemaless | Time-indexed | Flat, blob |
| **Best For** | Patient records, billing | Device metadata, logs | Vital signs, ECG | Images, video |
| **Example** | PostgreSQL, MySQL | MongoDB, CouchDB | InfluxDB, TimescaleDB | AWS S3, MinIO |
| **Biomedical Use** | EHR systems | IoT device registry | Continuous monitoring | PACS imaging archive |
| **ACID Compliance** | Strong | Eventual consistency | Configurable | Eventual consistency |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key formulas for Data Storage calculations (KTU Board Exam Ready)**

| Symbol | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S$ | $S = N \times f_s \times b \times T$ | Total storage size (N channels, sampling freq, bits, time) | **bits** |
| $D$ | $D = \dfrac{S}{8 \times 10^9}$ | Convert bits to **Gigabytes (GB)** | GB |
| $R$ | $R = N \times f_s \times b$ | Incoming data rate (per channel × bits) | **bits/sec (bps)** |
| $L$ | $L = L_{prop} + L_{proc} + L_{queue} + L_{trans}$ | Total latency (sum of all components) | **ms** |
| $T_{ret}$ | $T_{ret} = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ | M/M/1 queue mean retention time | seconds |
| $C_{comp}$ | $C_{comp} = \dfrac{S_{orig}}{S_{comp}}$ | Compression ratio (higher = better compression) | dimensionless |
| $C_{red}$ | $C_{red} = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{C_{comp}}\right) \times 100\%$ | Percentage storage reduction | **%** |
| $N_{dev}$ | $N_{dev} = \dfrac{S_{total}}{S_{per\_dev}}$ | Number of devices supported by storage pool | dimensionless |
| $BW$ | $BW = \dfrac{R}{C}$ | Bandwidth required (data rate / channel capacity) | dimensionless |
| $MTBF$ | $MTBF = \dfrac{t_{total}}{N_{failures}}$ | Mean Time Between Failures (reliability) | **hours** |
| $A$ | $A = \dfrac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime}$ | Storage system availability | **0 to 1** |
| $P_{loss}$ | $P_{loss} = 1 - (1-p)^n$ | Probability of data loss (n replicas, failure prob p) | dimensionless |
| $Q$ | $Q = \rho + \dfrac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$ | M/M/1 queue length (ρ = λ/μ utilization) | packets |
| $T_{arch}$ | $T_{arch} = S \times c_{storage} \times t_{retention}$ | Total archival cost | **currency units** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to remember the latency components:** **"Pigs Queue Transparently"** = **P**ropagation + **P**rocessing + **Q**ueuing + **T**ransmission.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **smart hospitals**, data storage systems are the silent backbone enabling:
- **Predictive analytics** — ML models trained on years of stored ECG to predict arrhythmias
- **Telemedicine** — retrieving patient history across continents from cloud archives
- **Drug discovery** — mining structured clinical trial data for pattern recognition
- **PACS systems** — storing millions of DICOM images accessible within seconds
- **Population health** — aggregating anonymized data from thousands of wearables for epidemiological research

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Storage Requirement for Continuous Biomedical Monitoring

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Context:** A wearable ECG device monitors a patient with **3 leads**, sampling at **$f_s = 500$ Hz** with **16-bit resolution**, continuously for **24 hours**. Compute the total storage required in **GB**.

### Given Data
- Number of channels (leads): $N = 3$
- Sampling frequency: $f_s = 500$ Hz (samples per second per channel)
- Bit depth: $b = 16$ bits/sample
- Recording duration: $T = 24$ hours

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the master storage equation from the KTU Formula Sheet.

$$
S = N \times f_s \times b \times T
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values with consistent units. Convert $T$ to seconds.

$$
T = 24 \text{ hours} \times 3600 \text{ sec/hour} = 86400 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 3:** Plug all values into the formula.

$$
S = 3 \times 500 \times 16 \times 86400
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the intermediate products progressively.

$$
S = 3 \times 500 \times 16 \times 86400
$$

$$
S = 3 \times 500 \times 1{,}382{,}400
$$

$$
S = 1500 \times 1{,}382{,}400
$$

$$
S = 2{,}073{,}600{,}000 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert bits to Gigabytes using $1 \text{ GB} = 8 \times 10^9 \text{ bits}$.

$$
S_{GB} = \dfrac{2{,}073{,}600{,}000}{8 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
S_{GB} = 0.2592 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 6:** Apply compression. ECG signals compress well (typical $C_{comp} = 4:1$).

$$
S_{compressed} = \dfrac{0.2592}{4} = 0.0648 \text{ GB} \approx 64.8 \text{ MB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A single 24-hour 3-lead ECG recording requires only ~65 MB after compression — easily storable on a wearable flash chip. However, **10,000 patients** would generate **~648 GB/day**, motivating the need for **hierarchical cloud storage**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Storage Capacity Planning for a Hospital IoT Network

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Context:** A smart hospital has **500 ICU beds**, each with **10 biomedical sensors** streaming at an average rate of **$R = 2$ kbps** per sensor. Data must be retained for **$t_{retention} = 7$ years** for legal compliance. Calculate the total archival storage needed (in TB), accounting for **3× replication** for redundancy.

### Given Data
- Number of beds: $B = 500$
- Sensors per bed: $k = 10$
- Rate per sensor: $R = 2$ kbps $= 2 \times 10^3$ bps
- Retention period: $t = 7$ years
- Replication factor: $r = 3$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute aggregate data rate per bed.

$$
R_{bed} = k \times R = 10 \times 2 \times 10^3 = 20{,}000 \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute aggregate data rate for the entire hospital.

$$
R_{total} = B \times R_{bed} = 500 \times 20{,}000 = 10{,}000{,}000 \text{ bps} = 10^7 \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert retention period to seconds.

$$
t = 7 \times 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 = 2.209 \times 10^8 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute raw storage requirement.

$$
S_{raw} = R_{total} \times t = 10^7 \times 2.209 \times 10^8
$$

$$
S_{raw} = 2.209 \times 10^{15} \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to Terabytes ($1 \text{ TB} = 8 \times 10^{12} \text{ bits}$).

$$
S_{raw\_TB} = \dfrac{2.209 \times 10^{15}}{8 \times 10^{12}} = 276.13 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 6:** Apply replication factor.

$$
S_{final} = r \times S_{raw\_TB} = 3 \times 276.13 = 828.4 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 7:** Apply 20% overhead for metadata and indexes.

$$
S_{total} = 1.2 \times 828.4 \approx 994 \text{ TB} \approx 1 \text{ PB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A mid-sized smart hospital generates approximately **1 Petabyte** of replicated biomedical data every 7 years. This is why **distributed object storage (e.g., AWS S3, Ceph)** and **tiered archival (hot/warm/cold)** are essential, not optional.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: M/M/1 Queue Model for Storage I/O Latency

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Context:** A storage server receives biomedical data packets at $\lambda = 800$ packets/sec and can process them at $\mu = 1000$ packets/sec. Find the utilization, mean queue length, and mean waiting time.

### Given Data
- Arrival rate: $\lambda = 800$ packets/sec
- Service rate: $\mu = 1000$ packets/sec

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute system utilization $\rho$.

$$
\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu} = \dfrac{800}{1000} = 0.8
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the M/M/1 mean queue length formula.

$$
Q = \dfrac{\rho^2}{1 - \rho} = \dfrac{(0.8)^2}{1 - 0.8} = \dfrac{0.64}{0.2} = 3.2 \text{ packets}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute mean total time in system (Little's Law variant).

$$
T = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \dfrac{1}{1000 - 800} = \dfrac{1}{200} = 0.005 \text{ sec} = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpret for biomedical context.

$$
T_{wait} = T - \dfrac{1}{\mu} = 0.005 - 0.001 = 0.004 \text{ sec} = 4 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A utilization of $\rho = 0.8$ means the storage I/O subsystem is 80% busy. For **real-time biomedical alerts**, utilization should be kept **below 0.5** to ensure latency stays under **2 ms**. Above $\rho = 0.9$, latency explodes non-linearly, risking missed critical events (e.g., cardiac arrest detection).

---

| Parameter | Local SSD | Edge NAS | Fog Server | Cloud Object Store |

| **Capacity** | 256 GB–4 TB | 1–100 TB | 10–500 TB | **Unlimited (PB+)** |

| **Scalability** | Low | Medium | Medium | **Very High** |
| **Reliability** | Medium | Medium | High | **Very High (11 9's)** |

| **Failure Mode** | Physical loss | Network partition | Power outage | Internet outage |

## 3.5 Data Compression Derivation (Lossless ECG Compression)

**Problem:** A 12-lead ECG at 1000 Hz, 16-bit, recorded for 1 hour. Using **Huffman coding**, average codeword length is 8.5 bits. Find compression ratio and storage reduction.

**Step 1:** Raw size.

$$
S_{raw} = 12 \times 1000 \times 16 \times 3600 = 6.912 \times 10^8 \text{ bits} = 86.4 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 2:** Compressed size.

$$
S_{comp} = 12 \times 1000 \times 8.5 \times 3600 = 3.672 \times 10^8 \text{ bits} = 45.9 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 3:** Compression ratio.

$$
C_{comp} = \dfrac{S_{raw}}{S_{comp}} = \dfrac{86.4}{45.9} \approx 1.88
$$

**Step 4:** Storage reduction percentage.

$$
C_{red} = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{1.88}\right) \times 100\% = 46.8\%
$$

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)
