### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the hierarchical storage architecture for IoT biomedical systems with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of hot, warm, and cold storage tiers with suitable biomedical examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Hierarchical storage architecture in IoT biomedical systems organizes data across multiple tiers based on **access frequency, latency requirements, and cost**, optimizing both performance and expenditure.

**Hot Storage Tier (2 marks):**
- **Media:** SSD, NVMe flash on edge gateway or hospital server
- **Latency:** < 10 ms
- **Use Case:** Real-time patient monitoring alerts, ICU ventilator data, ECG arrhythmia detection
- **Retention:** Hours to days
- **Example:** A cardiac monitor in the CCU storing the last 24 hours of waveforms for instant recall during an emergency.

**Warm Storage Tier (2 marks):**
- **Media:** HDD-based NAS, on-premise hospital servers
- **Latency:** 10–100 ms
- **Use Case:** Recent patient records, post-operative analytics, weekly trend reports
- **Retention:** Days to months
- **Example:** Last 90 days of patient vital signs stored on the hospital's local NAS for physician review.

**Cold Storage Tier (2 marks):**
- **Media:** Cloud object storage (AWS S3 Glacier, Azure Archive)
- **Latency:** Seconds to minutes
- **Use Case:** Long-term archival for legal compliance, ML training datasets
- **Retention:** Years (often 7+ years for medical records)
- **Example:** Historical MRI images from 2018 archived in cloud Glacier for legal and research purposes.

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.3 of these notes for visual representation) — embedded in exam answer.**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A remote patient monitoring system has 1000 patients. Each patient wears 5 sensors, each generating 4 kbps. Data is retained for 5 years with 3× replication. Calculate the total storage required in TB. Assume 10% metadata overhead.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N_{patients} = 1000$, sensors per patient $k = 5$, rate $R = 4$ kbps
- Retention $t = 5$ years, replication $r = 3$, metadata overhead $= 10\%$

**Step 1:** Aggregate data rate.

$$
R_{total} = 1000 \times 5 \times 4 \times 10^3 = 2 \times 10^7 \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert retention to seconds.

$$
t = 5 \times 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 = 1.578 \times 10^8 \text{ sec}
$$

**Step 3:** Raw storage.

$$
S_{raw} = 2 \times 10^7 \times 1.578 \times 10^8 = 3.156 \times 10^{15} \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to TB.

$$
S_{raw} = \dfrac{3.156 \times 10^{15}}{8 \times 10^{12}} = 394.5 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply replication.

$$
S_{replicated} = 3 \times 394.5 = 1183.5 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 6:** Apply metadata overhead.

$$
S_{final} = 1.10 \times 1183.5 = \boxed{1301.85 \text{ TB} \approx 1.3 \text{ PB}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Option 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the role of time-series databases in IoT-based healthcare monitoring. Compare InfluxDB and TimescaleDB with at least four parameters.**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Time-Series Databases in Healthcare (3 marks):**
1. **Optimized for time-stamped data** — vital signs (HR, BP, SpO2) are inherently time-series
2. **High write throughput** — millions of samples per second across hospital wards
3. **Built-in downsampling** — automatic aggregation (e.g., 1-min averages of raw 1-second data)
4. **Retention policies** — automatic data expiry and tier migration
5. **Native time-based queries** — SQL-like syntax with time functions (moving averages, windowing)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | InfluxDB | TimescaleDB |
|---|---|---|
| **Underlying Engine** | Custom TSM engine | PostgreSQL extension |
| **Query Language** | InfluxQL / Flux | Standard SQL + time functions |
| **Compression** | Excellent (8-10×) | Good (4-6×) |
| **Ecosystem** | Grafana native, Telegraf agents | Mature PostgreSQL tools, BI compatible |
| **Horizontal Scaling** | Native (InfluxDB Enterprise) | Limited (relies on PostgreSQL) |
| **Best For** | Pure IoT telemetry | Hybrid SQL + time-series workloads |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the data lifecycle management (DLM) process in IoT biomedical storage with a suitable flow diagram. Explain the significance of data retention policies under HIPAA.**

**Model Solution:**

**Data Lifecycle Stages (4 marks):**

1. **Ingestion** — raw sensor data enters via MQTT/HTTP gateway
2. **Validation** — schema check, missing-value handling, outlier detection
3. **Processing & Compression** — noise filtering, feature extraction, encoding
4. **Active Storage** — recent data in hot tier, fast retrieval
5. **Aging & Migration** — automated policy moves data to warm tier (30 days), then cold (1 year)
6. **Archival & Disposal** — long-term cold storage; secure deletion after retention period

**HIPAA Retention Significance (3 marks):**
- HIPAA mandates a **minimum 6-year** retention of medical records (some states require longer)
- **Privacy Rule** requires protection of PHI (Protected Health Information) throughout the lifecycle
- **Security Rule** demands encryption at rest and in transit
- **Breach Notification Rule** — loss of unencrypted data triggers mandatory reporting
- DLM ensures compliance, **reduces liability**, and supports **audit readiness**

**Flow Diagram (refer to Section 4.4 Mermaid diagram in these notes).**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (2–3 marks lost):** Always convert bits → bytes → KB → MB → GB → TB explicitly. A frequent mistake is forgetting the $8$ factor when converting bits to bytes.
> 2. **Missing Replication Factor:** Storage calculations in KTU exams often require a replication factor (typically 2× or 3×). Forgetting this costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing Cloud Object Stores with Block Storage:** AWS S3 is **object storage** (for archives/images), while AWS EBS is **block storage** (for VM disks). Mixing these definitions loses marks.
> 4. **No Diagram in 14-Mark Answers:** A neat labeled block diagram is **mandatory** for full marks in hierarchical storage questions. Draw even if not asked.
> 5. **Ignoring HIPAA/GDPR:** For biomedical IoT questions, regulatory compliance is a **high-weightage differentiator**. Always mention at least one healthcare data regulation.
> 6. **Forgetting Compression:** Raw vs compressed size distinction is frequently tested. State compression ratio assumptions explicitly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Data Storage**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- **Data Storage** = capture + persist + organize + retrieve of IoT data
- **4-Layer Hierarchy:** Device → Edge → Fog → Cloud
- **3 Data Types:** Structured (SQL), Semi-Structured (NoSQL/JSON), Unstructured (Blob)

### 📊 Database Selection
- **SQL (PostgreSQL, MySQL):** Fixed schema, ACID, EHR systems
- **NoSQL (MongoDB, Cassandra):** Flexible schema, IoT metadata, device logs
- **Time-Series (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB):** Vital signs, continuous monitoring
- **Object Store (S3, MinIO):** Images, video, archival (DICOM, MRI)

### 🧮 Must-Know Formulas
- $S = N \times f_s \times b \times T$ — storage size
- $R = N \times f_s \times b$ — data rate
- $L = L_{prop} + L_{proc} + L_{queue} + L_{trans}$ — total latency
- $C_{comp} = S_{orig} / S_{comp}$ — compression ratio
- $S_{final} = r \times S_{raw} \times (1 + \text{overhead})$ — with replication

### 🏥 Biomedical-Specific Standards
- **HL7** — health data exchange
- **DICOM** — medical imaging
- **FHIR** — modern REST-based health API
- **HIPAA** — US healthcare data privacy
- **GDPR** — EU data protection

### ⚙️ Engineering Trade-offs
- **Hot tier:** Fast, expensive, small capacity → real-time alerts
- **Warm tier:** Medium speed/cost → recent records
- **Cold tier:** Slow, cheap, large → archival/ML training
- **Compression:** Lossless for medical accuracy; lossy for non-clinical

### 🎯 Exam Tips
- Always draw a **block diagram** for 14-mark storage architecture questions
- Show **step-by-step unit conversions** for numerical problems
- Mention **replication and metadata overhead** in capacity calculations
- Reference at least one **healthcare standard or regulation** in biomedical context
- For latency problems, identify which **tier** (edge/fog/cloud) is involved
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Data Analytics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Data Analytics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Data Analytics** is the systematic computational process of inspecting, cleansing, transforming, and modeling raw data with the objective of discovering useful information, drawing informed conclusions, and supporting evidence-based decision-making. In the context of **IoT and Biomedical Applications**, data analytics refers to the end-to-end pipeline of collecting heterogeneous sensor data (ECG, EEG, temperature, pressure, motion, environmental), processing it through statistical and machine-learning algorithms, and extracting clinically or operationally meaningful insights. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Students must clearly distinguish between **Data Analytics**, **Data Science**, and **Big Data** — these three terms are frequently interchanged in viva but carry distinct scopes. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) Think of a **smart ICU monitor in a hospital**. The monitor continuously captures a patient's heart rate, blood oxygen (SpO₂), blood pressure, and respiratory waveform — thousands of data points per second. - **Raw data** = thousands of unconnected numbers flooding the screen every second. - **Data Analytics** = the "intelligence" that sorts through this noise, identifies the normal sinus rhythm, detects an arrhythmia 30 minutes before it happens, and alerts the doctor. In simple words: > **Data Analytics is the science of turning a "data ocean" into a "decision pool."** ### 1.3 The 5 Vs of Big Data (KTU High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners love the **5 Vs** of Big Data. Memorize the acronym **VVV-VV**: **Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value**. | Characteristic | Meaning | Biomedical/IoT Example | |---|---|---| | **Volume** | Scale of data (Terabytes → Petabytes) | A single MRI scan = ~**100 MB**; one hospital/year = ~**50 TB** | | **Velocity** | Speed of data generation/processing | ECG sensor generating **250–1000 samples/second** | | **Variety** | Different data formats (structured, semi, unstructured) | ECG waveforms, X-ray images, doctor's typed notes, voice dictations | | **Veracity** | Trustworthiness/quality of data | Motion artifacts corrupting an ECG signal | | **Value** | Business/clinical worth extracted | Early cancer detection from 1 million screening images | ### 1.4 Why Data Analytics is Critical in IoT-Biomedical Systems > [!IMPORTANT] > An IoT device without analytics is just a **"data coffin"** — it stores numbers that no one ever interprets. The value of IoT lies in the **"A" — Analytics**. - **Clinical Decision Support:** Real-time alerts for sepsis, cardiac arrest, falls in elderly. - **Predictive Maintenance:** Hospital ventilator failure prediction 7 days in advance. - **Population Health:** Identifying disease outbreaks from aggregated wearable data. - **Personalized Medicine:** Tailoring drug dosage using individual patient data patterns. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Data Analytics Data analytics is broadly classified into **four progressive levels** of insight: > [!NOTE] > KTU frequently asks: *"Differentiate between Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, and Prescriptive Analytics."* This is a **guaranteed 7-mark question**. #### 2.1.1 Descriptive Analytics (What happened?) - **Purpose:** Summarizes historical data into understandable reports, dashboards, and KPIs. - **Techniques:** Mean, Median, Mode, Standard Deviation, Histograms, Pie charts. - **Biomedical Example:** *"Last month, 42% of ICU admissions were due to respiratory failure."* - **Complexity Level:** ★☆☆ (Lowest) #### 2.1.2 Diagnostic Analytics (Why did it happen?) - **Purpose:** Drills down into data to find root causes of past events. - **Techniques:** Correlation, Drill-down analysis, Regression, Fishbone diagrams. - **Biomedical Example:** *"The spike in infections was caused by a contaminated water supply in Ward 3."* - **Complexity Level:** ★★☆ #### 2.1.3 Predictive Analytics (What will happen?) - **Purpose:** Uses historical patterns to forecast future outcomes. - **Techniques:** Machine Learning (Random Forest, SVM, Neural Networks), Time-series forecasting. - **Biomedical Example:** *"This patient has an 87% probability of a cardiac event in the next 48 hours."* - **Complexity Level:** ★★★ #### 2.1.4 Prescriptive Analytics (What should we do?) - **Purpose:** Recommends optimal actions to achieve desired outcomes. - **Techniques:** Optimization, Simulation, Reinforcement Learning. - **Biomedical Example:** *"Reduce drug dosage by 15% and increase monitoring frequency to 5-minute intervals."* - **Complexity Level:** ★★★★ (Highest) ### 2.2 The Data Analytics Pipeline (Step-by-Step) The **CRISP-DM (Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining)** framework defines the standard lifecycle: 1. **Data Collection:** Sensors, EHR systems, wearables, IoT gateways. 2. **Data Storage:** Cloud databases (AWS S3, Google BigQuery), data lakes, data warehouses. 3. **Data Preprocessing:** Cleaning, normalization, handling missing values, outlier removal. 4. **Data Analysis:** Applying statistical/ML algorithms. 5. **Data Visualization:** Dashboards (Tableau, Power BI, Grafana). 6. **Decision Making:** Translating insights into clinical/operational actions. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Use `\vert` (NOT pipe symbol `\vert` in raw form) inside table cells for absolute values to prevent markdown parsing errors. | Concept | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Arithmetic Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Average of dataset | Same as $x$ | | Median | Middle value of sorted $x$ | Robust to outliers | Same as $x$ | | Variance | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | Spread of data | $(x)^2$ | | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Average deviation | Same as $x$ | | Z-Score (Outlier Detection) | $z = \dfrac{x_i - \bar{x}}{\sigma}$ | $\vert z \vert > 3 \Rightarrow$ outlier | Dimensionless | | Pearson Correlation | $r = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum(y_i-\bar{y})^2}}$ | Linear relationship strength | $[-1, +1]$ | | Root Mean Square (Signal) | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i^2}$ | Signal energy measure | Volts | | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal quality | Decibels (dB) | | Information Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Information content | Bits | | Data Reduction Ratio | $R = \dfrac{D_{original} - D_{compressed}}{D_{original}} \times 100\%$ | Compression efficiency | Percentage (%) | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **IoT Edge Analytics:** Running analytics on-device (e.g., in a wearable) to save bandwidth. - **Cloud Analytics:** Aggregating data from millions of devices for population-level insights. - **Streaming Analytics:** Apache Kafka + Apache Flink for real-time ECG monitoring. - **Federated Analytics:** Analyzing data across hospitals **without moving patient data** (critical for GDPR/HIPAA compliance). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Z-Score for Outlier Detection in ECG Signals > **Problem:** An ECG sensor records 5 heart rate readings: $\{72, 75, 71, 74, 180\}$ bpm. The last reading appears suspicious. Apply the **Z-Score method** to verify if it is an outlier. **Step 1 — Calculate the Arithmetic Mean:** $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{x} &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \\[6pt] \bar{x} &= \frac{72 + 75 + 71 + 74 + 180}{5} \\[6pt] \bar{x} &= \frac{472}{5} \\[6pt] \bar{x} &= 94.4 \text{ bpm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate the Variance (σ²):** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma^2 &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \\[6pt] \sigma^2 &= \frac{1}{5}\left[(72-94.4)^2 + (75-94.4)^2 + (71-94.4)^2 + (74-94.4)^2 + (180-94.4)^2\right] \\[6pt] \sigma^2 &= \frac{1}{5}\left[501.76 + 376.36 + 547.56 + 416.16 + 7332.16\right] \\[6pt] \sigma^2 &= \frac{1}{5}(9174.00) \\[6pt] \sigma^2 &= 1834.80 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Calculate the Standard Deviation (σ):** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma &= \sqrt{\sigma^2} \\[6pt] \sigma &= \sqrt{1834.80} \\[6pt] \sigma &\approx 42.83 \text{ bpm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Calculate the Z-Score for the suspicious reading (180 bpm):** $$ \begin{aligned} z &= \frac{x_i - \bar{x}}{\sigma} \\[6pt] z &= \frac{180 - 94.4}{42.83} \\[6pt] z &= \frac{85.6}{42.83} \\[6pt] z &\approx 2.00 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** > [!NOTE] > **Decision Rule:** If $\vert z \vert > 2$, the data point is **suspect**; if $\vert z \vert > 3$, it is a **statistical outlier**. Since $z \approx 2.00$ is borderline, we mark 180 bpm as **suspect**. In a real clinical scenario, an additional check (e.g., sensor contact quality) is performed before rejecting the data point. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Analytics Types | Parameter | Descriptive | Diagnostic | Predictive | Prescriptive | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Question Answered** | What happened? | Why did it happen? | What will happen? | What should we do? | | **Time Orientation** | Past | Past | Future | Future | | **Data Type** | Aggregated historical | Aggregated + granular | Historical + real-time | All of the above + constraints | | **Typical Techniques** | Reports, KPIs, OLAP | Correlation, drill-down | ML, regression, time-series | Optimization, simulation | | **Output** | Dashboards | Root-cause report | Forecasts | Action recommendations | | **Biomedical Example** | Monthly patient count | Cause of infection spike | Sepsis risk score | Recommended drug dosage | | **Computational Cost** | Low | Medium | High | Very High | | **Maturity in Hospitals** | Widely deployed | Common | Emerging | Experimental | ### 3.3 Worked Example: Pearson Correlation Between Two Vital Signs > **Problem:** A study records **Heart Rate (HR)** and **Blood Pressure (BP)** for 4 patients. Compute the Pearson correlation coefficient. | Patient | HR (x) | BP (y) | |---|---|---| | 1 | 70 | 120 | | 2 | 80 | 130 | | 3 | 90 | 140 | | 4 | 100 | 150 | **Step 1 — Calculate Means:** $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{x} &= \frac{70+80+90+100}{4} = 85 \\[6pt] \bar{y} &= \frac{120+130+140+150}{4} = 135 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate Deviations and Products:** | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})$ | $(y_i - \bar{y})$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | $(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 70 | 120 | -15 | -15 | 225 | 225 | 225 | | 80 | 130 | -5 | -5 | 25 | 25 | 25 | | 90 | 140 | 5 | 5 | 25 | 25 | 25 | | 100 | 150 | 15 | 15 | 225 | 225 | 225 | | **Σ** | | | | **500** | **500** | **500** | **Step 3 — Apply the Formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} r &= \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i-\bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i-\bar{y})^2}} \\[6pt] r &= \frac{500}{\sqrt{500 \times 500}} \\[6pt] r &= \frac{500}{500} = 1.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** $r = 1.0$ indicates a **perfect positive linear correlation** — as heart rate increases, blood pressure increases proportionally. This is physiologically expected. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 IoT Data Analytics Pipeline (End-to-End Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[IoT Sensors
ECG, SpO2, Temp] --> B[Edge Gateway
Data Acquisition] B --> C[Data Preprocessing
Cleaning, Filtering] C --> D[Cloud Storage
Data Lake / Warehouse] D --> E[Analytics Engine
ML, Statistics] E --> F[Visualization
Dashboards, Alerts] F --> G[Clinical Decision
Doctor / Patient] G -.Feedback Loop.-> C ``` ### 4.2 The Four Types of Analytics (Hierarchy) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Data Analytics] --> B[Descriptive
What Happened?] A --> C[Diagnostic
Why Did it Happen?] A --> D[Predictive
What Will Happen?] A --> E[Prescriptive
What Should We Do?] B --> F[Reports, KPIs] C --> G[Root Cause Analysis] D --> H[ML Forecasts] E --> I[Optimization Engine] ``` ### 4.3 The 5 Vs of Big Data (Concept Map) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Big Data Characteristics] --> B[Volume
Scale of Data] A --> C[Velocity
Speed of Generation] A --> D[Variety
Data Formats] A --> E[Veracity
Data Quality] A --> F[Value
Insights Extracted] B --> B1[Example: 50 TB per Hospital per Year] C --> C1[Example: 1000 ECG samples per second] D --> D1[Example: Images, Signals, Text] E --> E1[Example: Sensor Noise, Artifacts] F --> F1[Example: Early Disease Detection] ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Healthcare Analytics Platform ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph L1["Data Ingestion Layer"] A1[Hospital EHR] --> A2[Wearables] A2 --> A3[IoT Medical Devices] A3 --> A4[Imaging Systems] end subgraph L2["Storage Layer"] B1[Data Lake
Raw Storage] --> B2[Data Warehouse
Structured] B2 --> B3[Time-Series DB
Sensor Streams] end subgraph L3["Processing Layer"] C1[Batch Processing
Hadoop, Spark] --> C2[Stream Processing
Kafka, Flink] C2 --> C3[ML Training
Python, TensorFlow] end subgraph L4["Analytics Layer"] D1[Descriptive Dashboards] --> D2[Diagnostic Reports] D2 --> D3[Predictive Models] D3 --> D4[Prescriptive Engine] end subgraph L5["Presentation Layer"] E1[Doctor Dashboard] --> E2[Patient Mobile App] E2 --> E3[Hospital Admin Console] end L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]** *Define Data Analytics. List the four types of analytics with a one-line example each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Data Analytics is the science of examining raw data to draw conclusions and support decision-making. The four types are: 1. **Descriptive** — *e.g., monthly patient admission report.* 2. **Diagnostic** — *e.g., root cause of infection outbreak.* 3. **Predictive** — *e.g., 30-day readmission risk score.* 4. **Prescriptive** — *e.g., recommended personalized drug dosage.* (1 + 2 marks) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** *Explain the 5 Vs of Big Data with biomedical examples.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Volume:** Scale of data — *e.g., 50 TB of patient data/year.* (0.5) - **Velocity:** Speed of generation — *e.g., 1000 ECG samples/sec.* (0.5) - **Variety:** Multiple formats — *e.g., images, signals, text.* (0.5) - **Veracity:** Data quality/trust — *e.g., motion artifacts in wearables.* (0.5) - **Value:** Useful insights — *e.g., early cancer detection from 1M images.* (1.0) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]** *(a)* Explain the four types of data analytics in detail with biomedical examples. Compare their complexity and outputs. (7 marks) *(b)* With a neat block diagram, describe the IoT data analytics pipeline. Discuss how each stage contributes to clinical decision-making. (7 marks) --- ### Model Solution for Question A: **Part (a) — Four Types of Analytics (7 marks):** The four types of analytics represent increasing levels of business/clinical value: 1. **Descriptive Analytics (2 marks):** Answers *"What happened?"* It uses historical data summarized via KPIs, dashboards, and reports. *Example:* "In 2023, 35% of ICU patients in Hospital X had sepsis." Techniques include mean, median, histograms. **Limitation:** It does not explain *why* something happened. 2. **Diagnostic Analytics (1.5 marks):** Answers *"Why did it happen?"* It uses drill-down, correlation, and root-cause analysis. *Example:* "The sepsis spike was caused by a contaminated central line in Ward 5." Output is a diagnostic report identifying causal factors. 3. **Predictive Analytics (1.5 marks):** Answers *"What will happen?"* It uses ML models (Random Forest, Neural Networks) trained on historical data. *Example:* "Patient Y has an 87% probability of readmission within 30 days." Enables proactive intervention. 4. **Prescriptive Analytics (2 marks):** Answers *"What should we do?"* It combines prediction with optimization. *Example:* "Reduce drug dosage by 15% and schedule follow-up in 3 days." Most complex; uses simulation and constraint-based optimization. **Highest clinical value.** > [!NOTE] > Examiner expects a **comparison table** or **pyramid diagram** for full marks. (Mention ascending complexity: Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive.) --- **Part (b) — IoT Data Analytics Pipeline (7 marks):** The IoT data analytics pipeline consists of **six sequential stages**: 1. **Data Collection (1 mark):** Biomedical sensors (ECG, EEG, SpO₂, accelerometers) and IoT gateways capture raw signals. *Example:* A wearable patch records 250 Hz ECG. 2. **Data Transmission (1 mark):** Data is sent via Wi-Fi/5G/Bluetooth to edge or cloud servers using protocols like MQTT or HTTP. 3. **Data Preprocessing (1.5 marks):** Noise filtering, missing value imputation, normalization. *Example:* A 50 Hz notch filter removes powerline interference from ECG. 4. **Data Storage (1 mark):** Cloud data lakes (AWS S3) for raw data, warehouses for structured data, time-series DBs (InfluxDB) for sensor streams. 5. **Data Analysis (1.5 marks):** Statistical/ML algorithms extract insights. *Example:* An LSTM model predicts arrhythmia 5 minutes before onset. 6. **Visualization & Action (1 mark):** Dashboards (Grafana), mobile alerts, EMR integration. The doctor receives an alert: "Patient A — Bradycardia detected — verify vitals." The pipeline enables **real-time clinical decision support**, transforming raw sensor data into actionable medical intelligence. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]** *(a)* Define Big Data. Explain any five characteristics of Big Data with IoT/Biomedical examples. (7 marks) *(b)* The following 5 heart rate readings (in bpm) were recorded: 68, 72, 70, 74, 140. Using the Z-score method, determine whether the value 140 is a statistical outlier. (7 marks) --- ### Model Solution for Question B: **Part (a) — Big Data Definition and 5 Characteristics (7 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Big Data refers to datasets whose size, complexity, and growth rate exceed the capability of traditional data processing tools to capture, store, manage, and analyze them within acceptable time limits. **Five Characteristics (6 marks — 1.2 marks each):** 1. **Volume:** Massive scale of data. *IoT example:* A single smart factory generates **5 TB of sensor data per day.** A modern hospital generates **50–100 TB/year** from imaging and EHR systems. 2. **Velocity:** Speed at which data is generated and must be processed. *Biomedical example:* A continuous glucose monitor produces **1 reading every 5 minutes = 288 readings/day**; an ICU ventilator produces **100 readings/second.** 3. **Variety:** Heterogeneous data formats — structured (EHR tables), semi-structured (HL7 messages, JSON), unstructured (MRI images, doctor's notes). IoT systems must handle **all three** simultaneously. 4. **Veracity:** Trustworthiness and quality of data. *Example:* A wearable fitness tracker may show **±10% heart rate error** during high-intensity exercise due to motion artifacts. Poor veracity leads to wrong clinical decisions. 5. **Value:** The ultimate worth extracted from data. *Example:* Mining 10 million screening mammograms using deep learning can **detect early-stage tumors in 2,000 additional women per year** — life-saving value. *(Optional 6th V — Variability: meaning of data changes with context. Example: "Fever" definition varies between pediatric and adult populations.)* --- **Part (b) — Z-Score Outlier Detection (7 marks):** **Given Data:** $X = \{68, 72, 70, 74, 140\}$, $n = 5$ **Step 1: Mean Calculation (1.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{x} &= \frac{68 + 72 + 70 + 74 + 140}{5} \\[6pt] \bar{x} &= \frac{424}{5} = 84.8 \text{ bpm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Variance Calculation (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma^2 &= \frac{1}{5}\left[(68-84.8)^2 + (72-84.8)^2 + (70-84.8)^2 + (74-84.8)^2 + (140-84.8)^2\right] \\[6pt] \sigma^2 &= \frac{1}{5}\left[282.24 + 163.84 + 219.04 + 116.64 + 3047.04\right] \\[6pt] \sigma^2 &= \frac{1}{5}(3828.80) = 765.76 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Standard Deviation (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma &= \sqrt{765.76} \approx 27.67 \text{ bpm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Z-Score for x = 140 (1.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} z &= \frac{140 - 84.8}{27.67} = \frac{55.2}{27.67} \approx 1.995 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Conclusion (1 mark):** Since $z \approx 1.995 < 3$ but $z > 2$, the value **140 bpm is suspicious but not a definitive statistical outlier** under the strict $\vert z \vert > 3$ rule. However, since it is well above the normal adult resting range (60–100 bpm), it should be **flagged for clinical verification** — possibly a sensor error, motion artifact, or genuine tachycardia requiring intervention. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Confusing "Variance" with "Standard Deviation":** Many students write $\sigma$ when they computed $\sigma^2$. ($-1$ mark) > 2. **Forgetting units:** Always mention bpm, mmHg, °C, etc. ($-0.5$ mark) > 3. **Not interpreting the Z-score:** A numerical answer without a clinical/statistical conclusion gets partial credit only. ($-1$ mark) > 4. **Mixing up Descriptive vs Diagnostic vs Predictive vs Prescriptive** in the comparison table. Memorize one example for each. ($-2$ marks) > 5. **Skipping the diagram** in pipeline questions — KTU awards **1–2 marks specifically for the block diagram.** --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize These 12 Bullets:** - ☐ **Data Analytics** = extracting insights from raw data to support decisions. - ☐ **Big Data** = characterized by **5 Vs**: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value. - ☐ **4 Types of Analytics:** Descriptive (What?) → Diagnostic (Why?) → Predictive (Future?) → Prescriptive (Action?). - ☐ **Complexity Order:** Descriptive < Diagnostic < Predictive < Prescriptive. - ☐ **Pipeline Stages:** Collection → Transmission → Preprocessing → Storage → Analysis → Visualization → Action. - ☐ **CRISP-DM** is the standard analytics lifecycle framework. - ☐ **Mean:** $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ — sensitive to outliers. - ☐ **Median:** middle value — robust to outliers. - ☐ **Z-score rule:** $\vert z \vert > 3$ ⇒ statistical outlier. - ☐ **Pearson $r$:** ranges from $-1$ to $+1$; $r = 0$ means no linear correlation. - ☐ **SNR** is critical in biomedical signal quality — use $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(V_s / V_n)$. - ☐ **IoT without analytics is useless** — the value lies in interpretation, not collection. --- > **END OF NOTES — Module 4: Introduction to Data Analytics** > **Course:** OEEBT831 — IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS | **KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Types of Data Analytics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Data Analytics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Data Analytics** is the systematic computational process of inspecting, cleansing, transforming, and modeling data to discover useful information, support decision-making, and derive conclusions within Internet of Things (IoT) ecosystems. In the context of biomedical IoT systems, data analytics involves processing the massive volumes of physiological, sensor, and clinical data generated by wearable devices, implantable sensors, and remote monitoring equipment to extract clinically actionable insights.

The **four canonical types of data analytics** form a hierarchy of analytical complexity and business/clinical value:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Descriptive Analytics** – Answers *"What happened?"* by summarizing historical data.
> 2. **Diagnostic Analytics** – Answers *"Why did it happen?"* by identifying root causes.
> 3. **Predictive Analytics** – Answers *"What will happen?"* by forecasting future events.
> 4. **Prescriptive Analytics** – Answers *"What should we do?"* by recommending optimal actions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Smart Hospital Scenario**
> 
> Imagine a doctor treating a cardiac patient wearing an IoT-enabled ECG patch:
> - **Descriptive** = The doctor's screen showing the patient's average heart rate over the last 24 hours was **82 bpm**.
> - **Diagnostic** = The doctor discovers the heart rate spiked because the patient skipped their evening medication.
> - **Predictive** = An AI model warns that the patient has a **78% probability** of an arrhythmia episode in the next 6 hours.
> - **Prescriptive** = The system automatically recommends reducing beta-blocker dosage by **25%** and scheduling a tele-consultation.
> 
> Each level builds upon the previous one, transforming raw numbers into life-saving actions.

## 1.3 Classification of Data Analytics Types

| Type | Core Question | Time Orientation | Complexity |
|------|--------------|------------------|------------|
| Descriptive | What happened? | Past | Low |
| Diagnostic | Why did it happen? | Past | Medium |
| Predictive | What will happen? | Future | High |
| Prescriptive | What should we do? | Future | Very High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric (KTU High-Yield):** The value-to-complexity ratio increases from Descriptive → Prescriptive. Biomedical applications typically begin with Descriptive analytics before progressing to Prescriptive.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Analytics Type

### 2.1.1 Descriptive Analytics
- **Definition:** The foundational layer that condenses raw IoT sensor streams (heart rate, SpO2, body temperature, glucose levels) into interpretable summaries.
- **Core Techniques:** Mean, Median, Mode, Standard Deviation, Frequency Distribution, Data Aggregation, OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) cubes, Dashboards.
- **Operational Steps:**
  1. **Data Collection** – Ingest streams from biomedical sensors via MQTT/HTTP.
  2. **Data Aggregation** – Group readings over time windows (e.g., hourly/daily averages).
  3. **Summarization** – Compute KPIs such as mean arterial pressure, mean HR, oxygen saturation trends.
  4. **Visualization** – Render plots, heatmaps, and dashboards for clinicians.
- **Why & How:** It provides a *retrospective snapshot*. It does NOT explain causation — it merely reports the state of affairs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Example:** A weekly PDF report showing an elderly patient's average blood glucose levels, step counts, and sleep duration compiled from their smartwatch.

### 2.1.2 Diagnostic Analytics
- **Definition:** Goes beyond "what" to investigate "why" by drilling down into data to identify causes, correlations, and anomalies.
- **Core Techniques:** Correlation analysis, Regression, Drill-down analysis, Root-cause analysis, Fishbone diagrams, Anomaly detection.
- **Operational Steps:**
  1. **Trigger Identification** – Detect deviations from baseline (e.g., sudden HR spike).
  2. **Drill-Down** – Segment data by time, patient, or sensor type.
  3. **Correlation Discovery** – Find relationships (e.g., HR rises correlate with caffeine intake).
  4. **Causal Inference** – Apply statistical tests (Chi-square, ANOVA) to validate hypotheses.
- **Why & How:** It answers causation by examining the relationships between multiple variables, often using historical baseline comparisons.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Example:** A hospital discovers that ICU readmission rates spiked last month because a specific batch of infusion pumps was miscalibrated — identified through drill-down analytics.

### 2.1.3 Predictive Analytics
- **Definition:** Uses historical data, statistical algorithms, and Machine Learning (ML) models to forecast future outcomes.
- **Core Techniques:** Linear/Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, Random Forests, Neural Networks (LSTM, RNN), Time-Series Forecasting (ARIMA, Prophet).
- **Operational Steps:**
  1. **Feature Engineering** – Extract meaningful variables from raw sensor streams.
  2. **Model Training** – Fit ML models on labeled historical biomedical datasets.
  3. **Validation** – Evaluate using metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, AUC-ROC.
  4. **Forecasting** – Generate probability scores for future clinical events.
- **Why & How:** It provides probabilistic foresight, enabling preemptive clinical interventions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Example:** A LSTM neural network trained on 5 years of ICU vital signs predicts that Patient X has a **92% probability** of septic shock within the next 12 hours.

### 2.1.4 Prescriptive Analytics
- **Definition:** The most advanced analytics tier; it recommends specific actions to achieve desired outcomes, often using optimization and simulation.
- **Core Techniques:** Linear/Non-Linear Programming, Reinforcement Learning, Monte Carlo Simulation, Bayesian Networks, Recommendation Engines.
- **Operational Steps:**
  1. **Objective Definition** – Define clinical goal (e.g., minimize hospital readmission).
  2. **Constraint Modeling** – Encode medical constraints, drug interactions, patient allergies.
  3. **Optimization** – Run algorithms to find the best action sequence.
  4. **Action Execution** – Send recommendations to clinicians or automated systems.
- **Why & How:** It bridges prediction with action, transforming insights into optimal clinical decisions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Example:** An AI system recommends a personalized insulin dosage schedule for a diabetic patient, accounting for meals, exercise, and circadian glucose patterns.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Application Domain | Units |
|-----------------|------------|--------------------|-------|
| Arithmetic Mean | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Descriptive (Average HR) | bpm |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}$ | Descriptive (Variability) | Same as variable |
| Pearson Correlation | $r = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2} \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^{2}}}$ | Diagnostic (Association) | Dimensionless |
| Linear Regression | $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \epsilon$ | Predictive (Forecasting) | Variable |
| Logistic Regression (Sigmoid) | $P(y=1 \mid x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x)}}$ | Predictive (Disease Probability) | Probability [0,1] |
| Mean Squared Error | $MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}$ | Predictive (Model Error) | Squared units |
| Z-Score (Anomaly) | $Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Diagnostic (Outlier detection) | Standard deviations |
| Optimization Objective | $\min f(x)$ subject to $g_i(x) \leq 0$ | Prescriptive (Action selection) | Context-dependent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In IoT-driven telemedicine platforms, these formulas enable remote patient monitoring, early warning systems, personalized treatment plans, and resource optimization in hospitals.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Wearables (Fitbit, Apple Watch):** Use descriptive + diagnostic for daily summaries; predictive for atrial fibrillation alerts.
- **ICU Monitoring Systems:** Combine all 4 types for real-time critical care decision support.
- **Epidemic Tracking (COVID-19 dashboards):** Descriptive for case counts, predictive for outbreak forecasts, prescriptive for lockdown policies.
- **Pharmaceutical IoT:** Predictive models forecast drug demand; prescriptive models optimize supply chain logistics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Logistic Regression for Predictive Analytics in Biomedical IoT

Logistic regression is the workhorse for binary clinical predictions (e.g., disease/no-disease, arrhythmia/normal).

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Linear Combination
We begin with a linear combination of input features (e.g., age, heart rate, SpO2, BMI):

$$z = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \ldots + \beta_n x_n$$

where $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ are the biomedical features and $\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_n$ are the model coefficients (weights) to be learned.

### 3.1.2 Transformation via Sigmoid
To map $z$ (which ranges from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$) to a probability $P \in [0, 1]$, we apply the **sigmoid (logistic) function**:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

Therefore the predicted probability is:

$$P(y = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \ldots + \beta_n x_n)}}$$

### 3.1.3 Log-Odds (Logit) Form
Taking the natural log of the odds ratio yields the logit transformation:

$$\ln\left(\frac{P}{1 - P}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \ldots + \beta_n x_n$$

This linear form allows interpretation: $\beta_i$ represents the change in log-odds per unit change in $x_i$.

### 3.1.4 Cost Function — Log Loss
To train the model, we minimize the **Binary Cross-Entropy (Log Loss)**:

$$J(\beta) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1 - y_i)\log(1 - \hat{y}_i)\right]$$

where $m$ is the number of training samples, $y_i$ is the true label, and $\hat{y}_i$ is the predicted probability.

### 3.1.5 Gradient Descent Update
The coefficients are updated iteratively:

$$\beta_j := \beta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j}$$

where $\alpha$ is the learning rate. After convergence, the model is ready for clinical predictions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example:** Suppose a logistic model for diabetes risk outputs $P = 0.83$ for a patient with inputs $[Age=58, BMI=32, FastingGlucose=145]$. The model predicts an **83% probability** of diabetes onset — triggering a prescriptive recommendation for immediate endocrinology referral.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Z-Score for Diagnostic Anomaly Detection in Vital Signs

The Z-score quantifies how many standard deviations a sensor reading deviates from a patient's baseline.

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Establish Baseline
Compute the patient's historical mean heart rate $\mu_{HR}$ and standard deviation $\sigma_{HR}$ from the past 30 days.

$$\mu_{HR} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} HR_i$$

$$\sigma_{HR} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(HR_i - \mu_{HR})^{2}}$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Compute Z-Score for a New Reading
For a new instantaneous reading $HR_{new}$:

$$Z = \frac{HR_{new} - \mu_{HR}}{\sigma_{HR}}$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Thresholding for Anomaly
Typically, $\vert Z \vert > 3$ indicates a statistical anomaly (99.7% confidence under normality).

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** Patient baseline $\mu_{HR} = 72$ bpm, $\sigma_{HR} = 4$ bpm. New reading: $HR_{new} = 95$ bpm.
> 
> $$Z = \frac{95 - 72}{4} = \frac{23}{4} = 5.75$$
> 
> Since $\vert Z \vert = 5.75 > 3$, the system flags this as a critical anomaly → triggers predictive alert → activates prescriptive protocol (alert physician, recommend ECG).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Analytics Type

| Scenario | Recommended Analytics | Justification |
|----------|----------------------|---------------|
| Generate monthly patient health reports | Descriptive | Summarizes past data |
| Investigate why ICU mortality spiked last quarter | Diagnostic | Identifies root causes |
| Forecast which patients will be readmitted within 30 days | Predictive | Forecasts future events |
| Optimize ICU bed allocation during a pandemic | Prescriptive | Recommends optimal actions |
| Real-time SpO2 monitoring dashboard | Descriptive | Live data summarization |
| Investigate drug side-effect correlations | Diagnostic | Causal discovery |
| Sepsis early warning system | Predictive | Forecast onset 6-12 hours ahead |
| Personalized chemotherapy dosage adjustment | Prescriptive | Actionable treatment plan |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between descriptive and predictive analytics with a biomedical example.**

**Model Answer:**
Descriptive analytics summarizes *what has already happened* in the past, using statistical measures like mean, median, and standard deviation. Example: A weekly report showing the average heart rate of a cardiac patient over the past 7 days. Predictive analytics, in contrast, uses historical data and ML models to forecast *what is likely to happen* in the future. Example: A model predicting the probability of a heart attack in the next 30 days for a high-risk patient. The key distinction is the **time orientation** — past vs. future.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`
**List any four techniques used in prescriptive analytics.**

**Model Answer:**
The four key techniques used in prescriptive analytics are:
1. **Linear Programming** – for resource optimization problems.
2. **Reinforcement Learning** – for sequential decision-making.
3. **Monte Carlo Simulation** – for risk analysis under uncertainty.
4. **Bayesian Networks** – for probabilistic reasoning and decision support.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Explain the four types of data analytics with suitable examples in IoT-based healthcare systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the architecture of an IoT-enabled predictive analytics system for remote patient monitoring. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The four types of data analytics are:
- **Descriptive Analytics:** Answers *"What happened?"* by summarizing historical IoT sensor data. Example: A dashboard displaying the average SpO2 levels of all ICU patients over the past 24 hours.
- **Diagnostic Analytics:** Answers *"Why did it happen?"* by performing root-cause analysis. Example: Investigating why the readmission rate for cardiac patients increased last month.
- **Predictive Analytics:** Answers *"What will happen?"* by forecasting future events using ML models. Example: Predicting the onset of sepsis 12 hours before clinical symptoms appear.
- **Prescriptive Analytics:** Answers *"What should we do?"* by recommending optimal actions. Example: Suggesting a personalized insulin dosage regimen for a diabetic patient.

**(b)** The architecture of an IoT-enabled predictive analytics system for remote patient monitoring consists of the following layers:
1. **Sensor Layer:** Wearable devices (ECG patch, smart watch, SpO2 sensor) continuously collect patient vitals.
2. **Network Layer:** IoT gateway transmits data via MQTT/Wi-Fi/4G to the cloud.
3. **Data Storage Layer:** Cloud databases (e.g., AWS, Azure) store time-series physiological data.
4. **Analytics Layer:** ML models (LSTM, Random Forest) process the data and generate risk scores.
5. **Application Layer:** Clinicians receive alerts and predictions via mobile/web dashboards.
6. **Feedback Layer:** Patient outcomes are fed back to retrain and improve the models.

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`
**(a)** Discuss the role of diagnostic analytics in identifying anomalies in biomedical IoT data streams. Explain Z-score based anomaly detection with an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how prescriptive analytics can be applied to optimize ICU bed allocation during a pandemic. List the steps involved. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Diagnostic analytics plays a critical role in biomedical IoT by identifying the *root causes* of anomalies in continuous data streams. It employs drill-down, correlation, and statistical tests to investigate why sensor readings deviate from expected patterns.

**Z-score based anomaly detection example:**
A patient's historical heart rate baseline has mean $\mu = 72$ bpm and standard deviation $\sigma = 4$ bpm. A new reading of $HR = 95$ bpm is observed. The Z-score is computed as:

$$Z = \frac{HR - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{95 - 72}{4} = 5.75$$

Since $\vert Z \vert = 5.75$ exceeds the threshold of **3**, the reading is flagged as a critical anomaly, triggering a clinical alert.

**(b)** Prescriptive analytics optimizes ICU bed allocation through the following steps:
1. **Define Objective:** Minimize ICU waiting time and mortality rate.
2. **Collect Data:** Live patient severity scores, bed availability, staff schedules, equipment status.
3. **Model Constraints:** Encode medical rules (e.g., isolation requirements, ventilator availability).
4. **Run Optimization:** Apply linear/integer programming to find the best bed assignment.
5. **Recommend Actions:** Notify hospital administrators of optimal patient transfers and admissions.
6. **Continuous Feedback:** Update model with outcomes to improve future recommendations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse the time orientation of analytics (Descriptive = past, Predictive = future). Examiners **deduct 1-2 marks** for incorrect examples.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In Part B questions, students fail to provide *biomedical/IoT-specific* examples, resulting in **2-mark penalty**. Always use healthcare-related scenarios.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In Z-score derivations, students forget to mention the threshold $\vert Z \vert > 3$. Examiners expect this explicitly stated.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing *prescriptive* with *predictive* — prescriptions must include **action recommendations**, not just predictions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU Exams):**

- **Descriptive** = **"What happened?"** → Past-oriented, uses mean/median/dashboards.
- **Diagnostic** = **"Why did it happen?"** → Uses drill-down, correlation, Z-score.
- **Predictive** = **"What will happen?"** → Uses ML/regression/time-series models.
- **Prescriptive** = **"What should we do?"** → Uses optimization/simulation/recommendation engines.
- Analytics complexity increases from **Descriptive → Prescriptive** (low to very high).
- **Z-score threshold** for anomaly detection: $\vert Z \vert > 3$ (99.7% confidence).
- **Sigmoid function** maps predictions to probability: $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$.
- **Logistic regression** is the most common predictive model for binary biomedical outcomes.
- **IoT biomedical sources** include ECG, SpO2, glucose, BP, body temperature, and accelerometer data.
- In **KTU answers**, always tie examples to **healthcare/medical IoT** context for full marks.
- The four analytics types form a **progressive maturity pyramid** — each builds on the previous.
- **Prescriptive analytics** is the most clinically valuable but requires the most data and compute.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local Analytics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Local Analytics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧠 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Local Analytics** in the context of the Internet of Things (IoT) refers to the paradigm of performing **data processing, statistical analysis, pattern recognition, and decision-making operations directly at or near the data source** — on the edge device, gateway, or fog node — rather than transmitting the raw, unprocessed data stream to a centralized cloud server for analysis.

In a biomedical IoT ecosystem, *Local Analytics* specifically denotes the real-time execution of physiological signal processing, anomaly detection, and clinical-decision support algorithms on **wearable sensors, bedside monitors, implanted devices, or local gateways**, thereby closing the analytics loop within milliseconds — a critical requirement for life-critical medical applications where cloud round-trip latency is unacceptable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonyms used in KTU/industry literature:** *Edge Analytics*, *On-Device Analytics*, *In-Situ Analytics*, *Fog Analytics* (when the gateway layer hosts the analytics engine), and *Near-Source Analytics*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Security Guard vs. Postal Mail" Intuition

Imagine a hospital ICU with **1000 patients**, each generating **250 ECG samples per second**. This produces 250,000 data points every single second.

**Without Local Analytics (Cloud-Only Model):**
Think of this as a security guard who **photocopies every visitor's ID, then mails the photocopy to a distant headquarters** for verification. The guard cannot allow entry until headquarters faxes back approval. This takes **hours**, costs enormous postage, and if the mail truck breaks down, **everyone is stuck outside** — even in emergencies.

**With Local Analytics (Edge/Fog Model):**
Now imagine a *smart* security guard who carries a **handheld ID-verification device with a local database of authorized personnel**. The guard verifies IDs **instantly at the gate**, only escalating to headquarters for genuinely ambiguous cases. Routine decisions take **milliseconds**, mail costs drop to almost zero, and the headquarters is no longer a bottleneck.

**In this analogy:**
- 🧑‍⚕️ **Security Guard** = Local edge device (microcontroller / gateway)
- 🪪 **ID Card** = Sensor data (ECG, SpO₂, temperature)
- 🏢 **Distant Headquarters** = Cloud data center
- 📮 **Mail Truck** = Internet / network bandwidth
- ⚡ **Handheld Device** = Embedded ML inference engine (e.g., TensorFlow Lite Micro)

> [!TIP]
> **The single-line takeaway:** *Local Analytics brings the "brain" to the data, rather than dragging the data to a distant brain.*

---

## 1.3 Physical & Performance Constants Relevant to Local Analytics

| Constant / Metric | Typical Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Cloud round-trip latency** | **50 – 500 ms** | Too slow for closed-loop ICU control |
| **Edge inference latency** | **1 – 20 ms** | Suitable for real-time biosignal control |
| **Bandwidth (raw 1 kHz ECG, 1 patient)** | **~3.6 MB/hour** | Becomes infeasible at 1000 patients |
| **Bandwidth (locally analyzed, features only)** | **~50 KB/hour** | **~72× reduction** |
| **MCU RAM (typical edge device)** | **64 KB – 512 KB** | Constrains model size drastically |
| **MCU Flash (typical edge device)** | **256 KB – 4 MB** | Limits on-device model storage |
| **5G URLLC target latency** | **< 1 ms** | Standard for tactile medical robotics |

---

## 1.4 Why Local Analytics is a Syllabus Highlight in OEEBT831

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 2024 KTU Scheme places **Local Analytics** in Module 4 because it represents the **convergence point** of three engineering disciplines the course bridges:
> 1. **IoT Architecture** (where data lives and moves),
> 2. **Embedded Systems** (constrained compute on microcontrollers),
> 3. **Biomedical Signal Processing** (ECG, EEG, EMG analytics).
>
> A student who masters Local Analytics can **articulate why a cloud-only architecture is clinically unsafe** — a frequently tested concept.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier IoT Analytics Hierarchy

Local Analytics does not exist in isolation. KTU 2024 expects students to position it within the standard **three-tier IoT analytics model**:

### Tier 1 — Device/Edge Tier (Local Analytics Zone)
- **Where:** Sensor node, wearable, implant, smart sensor hub
- **What runs:** Filtering, thresholding, simple ML inference (TinyML), feature extraction
- **Compute:** Microcontroller (ARM Cortex-M, ESP32, nRF52)
- **Latency budget:** Sub-10 ms
- **Power budget:** mW range

### Tier 2 — Fog/Gateway Tier (Distributed Local Analytics)
- **Where:** Local gateway, smartphone, hospital bedside server, edge server
- **What runs:** Aggregation, correlation across multiple sensors, mid-complexity ML (Random Forest, small CNN)
- **Compute:** Raspberry Pi, NVIDIA Jetson Nano, gateway-class SoC
- **Latency budget:** 10 – 100 ms
- **Power budget:** Watts

### Tier 3 — Cloud Tier (Global Analytics)
- **Where:** AWS, Azure, GCP, on-premise hospital data center
- **What runs:** Deep learning, long-term trend analysis, population-level analytics
- **Compute:** GPU/TPU clusters
- **Latency budget:** 100 ms – seconds
- **Power budget:** kW–MW

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 specific framing:** "Local Analytics" in the OEEBT831 syllabus **operates across Tiers 1 and 2**. The Module 4 expectation is that students can map a given biomedical use-case to the correct tier and justify the placement with latency, power, and privacy arguments.

---

## 2.2 Core Operational Functions Performed by Local Analytics

Local Analytics is not a single algorithm — it is a **pipeline of operations** applied to the raw sensor stream. KTU examiners expect students to enumerate and explain these:

1. **Signal Preprocessing (Tier 1)**
   - Digital filtering (low-pass, high-pass, notch for 50/60 Hz mains hum)
   - Baseline wander removal (for ECG)
   - Noise reduction (moving average, median filter, Savitzky–Golay)

2. **Feature Extraction (Tier 1 → Tier 2 boundary)**
   - Time-domain: mean, RMS, peak-to-peak, zero-crossing rate
   - Frequency-domain: spectral energy, dominant frequency, band power
   - Statistical: variance, skewness, kurtosis, entropy
   - Biomedical-specific: R-R interval (HRV), QRS duration, SpO₂ perfusion index

3. **Thresholding & Rule-Based Detection (Tier 1)**
   - Hard-coded clinical thresholds (e.g., HR < 50 bpm → bradycardia alert)
   - Rule engines (if-else decision trees)

4. **Anomaly / Event Detection (Tier 1–2)**
   - Z-score outlier detection
   - Moving average deviation
   - ML-based: Isolation Forest, One-Class SVM, autoencoders

5. **On-Device Inference (Tier 1–2)**
   - TinyML models: decision trees, small neural networks
   - Frameworks: **TensorFlow Lite Micro, Edge Impulse, ONNX Runtime, Apache TVM**

6. **Data Reduction & Selective Transmission**
   - **Transmit only on event** (e.g., transmit ECG only when arrhythmia detected)
   - **Transmit only features** (not raw waveform)
   - **Compress before transmit** (wavelet, delta encoding, LZ)

7. **Local Storage & Buffering**
   - Ring buffers for post-event reconstruction
   - Store-and-forward during network outages

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table is the **definitive cheat sheet** for any numerical or derivation question on Local Analytics. **Memorize the units and conditions columns** — KTU Part A (3-mark) questions frequently test unit awareness.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{local}} + T_{\text{transmit}} + T_{\text{cloud}} + T_{\text{back}}$ | End-to-end latency decomposition | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $T_{\text{transmit}} = \dfrac{N \cdot b}{R} + t_{\text{prop}}$ | Transmission time = (packets × bits/packet) / rate + propagation | s; $R$ in bits/s |
| 3 | $N = f_s \cdot n_c \cdot t_{\text{record}}$ | Total samples generated = sample rate × channels × duration | samples |
| 4 | $D_{\text{raw}} = N \cdot w \cdot n_c$ | Raw data size (samples × bits-per-sample × channels) | bits |
| 5 | $D_{\text{local}} = f_{\text{feat}} \cdot n_f \cdot w_f$ | Locally-transmitted feature data size | bits; $n_f$ = # features |
| 6 | $C_R = \dfrac{D_{\text{raw}}}{D_{\text{local}}}$ | **Compression Ratio** (data reduction factor) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $E_{\text{local}} = P_{\text{MCU}} \cdot t_{\text{active}} + P_{\text{tx}} \cdot t_{\text{tx}}$ | Energy for local-only processing + transmit | Joules (J) |
| 8 | $E_{\text{cloud}} = P_{\text{tx}} \cdot t_{\text{tx,raw}} + P_{\text{idle}} \cdot t_{\text{wait}}$ | Energy for cloud path (raw transmit + idle wait) | Joules (J) |
| 9 | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (for preprocessing justification) | decibels (dB) |
| 10 | $f_{\text{Nyquist}} \geq 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}}$ | Sampling theorem bound (justifies local downsampling) | Hz |
| 11 | $HRV_{RMSSD} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{M-1}\sum_{i=1}^{M-1}(RR_{i+1}-RR_i)^2}$ | Local HRV feature (biomedical local analytics) | milliseconds (ms) |
| 12 | $\text{Energy}_{\text{feature}} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$ | Time-domain energy feature extracted locally | sample-units² |
| 13 | $\text{Power}_{\text{band}} = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} \vert X(f) \vert^{2}\, df$ | Band power feature (e.g., EEG alpha/beta) | (signal-units)² · Hz |
| 14 | $L_{\text{model}} = \dfrac{S_{\text{model}}}{S_{\text{Flash}}}$ | Model fit ratio (must be $\leq 1$ for MCU deployment) | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Table-Breaking Trap:** Never write absolute value as `|x|` inside a markdown table cell — it terminates the column. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead, exactly as shown above.

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Local-Analytics Operation

| Operation | Why is it done locally? (Engineering reason) |
|---|---|
| **Filtering** | Remove noise *before* it consumes bandwidth and storage; also stabilizes downstream features. |
| **Feature extraction** | A 1-second ECG segment of 250 samples becomes just 4–6 features (mean, std, RMSSD, peak freq, energy) → **~50× data reduction** with preserved clinical meaning. |
| **Thresholding** | 95% of events are normal; only the 5% anomalies need cloud attention → **massive bandwidth + cost savings**. |
| **On-device inference** | A seizure detector running on a wearable can alert the caregiver in **<100 ms** — cloud cannot. |
| **Local buffering** | Network outages in ambulances or rural clinics must not lose patient data. |
| **Selective transmission** | Power consumption scales with transmit time; an MCU transmitting only on-event can extend battery from days → **months**. |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Local Analytics Application | Why Local? |
|---|---|---|
| **Wearable cardiac monitor (e.g., Apple Watch, KardiaMobile)** | Atrial fibrillation detection on-wrist | Cannot wait 200 ms for cloud — user experience and clinical urgency |
| **Continuous glucose monitor (CGM)** | Hypoglycemia prediction on patch | 15-min cloud round-trip is clinically dangerous |
| **ICU ventilator** | Breath-pattern anomaly detection | Patient safety regulation; offline operation required |
| **Smart inhaler** | Inhaler technique scoring on-device | Privacy + immediate feedback loop |
| **Closed-loop insulin pump (artificial pancreas)** | Glucose → insulin dose every 5 min | **Life-critical closed loop;** cloud failure must not stop dosing |
| **EEG headband for seizure logging** | Seizure onset detection | Patient may be alone; latency = minutes of unsupervised seizure |
| **Smart pill bottle** | Adherence analytics on-bottle | No network in many home settings |

# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Latency Justification for Local Analytics (The Core KTU 14-Mark Proof)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark framing):**
> *"A wearable ECG patch samples at $f_s = 500$ Hz with 16-bit resolution over $n_c = 1$ channel. The cloud server is $d = 2000$ km away. The network uplink rate is $R = 250$ kbps. Compute the raw-transmission latency and show that it is unsuitable for real-time arrhythmia alerting. Then propose a local-analytics reduction and recompute."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the number of samples generated per analysis window.**

Choose a clinically relevant 1-second analysis window (a common ECG window for QRS detection):

$$N = f_s \cdot n_c \cdot t_{\text{window}}$$

**Substitution:**

$$N = 500 \, \text{Hz} \cdot 1 \cdot 1 \, \text{s}$$

**Result:**

$$N = 500 \text{ samples per second}$$

> *Textual interpretation:* The patch generates 500 voltage readings every second from one ECG lead.

---

**Step 2 — Compute the raw data size in bits per second.**

$$D_{\text{raw}} = N \cdot w \cdot n_c$$

**Substitution:**

$$D_{\text{raw}} = 500 \cdot 16 \cdot 1 \;\text{bits/second}$$

**Result:**

$$D_{\text{raw}} = 8000 \text{ bits/s} = 8 \text{ kbps}$$

> *Interpretation:* The raw ECG alone consumes 8 kbps of uplink bandwidth continuously.

---

**Step 3 — Compute the raw transmission time per second of data.**

$$t_{\text{tx,raw}} = \dfrac{D_{\text{raw}}}{R}$$

**Substitution:**

$$t_{\text{tx,raw}} = \dfrac{8000 \;\text{bits/s}}{250\,000 \;\text{bits/s}}$$

**Result:**

$$t_{\text{tx,raw}} = 0.032 \text{ s} = 32 \text{ ms}$$

---

**Step 4 — Compute the propagation delay across the 2000 km link.**

Signal travels through fiber at approximately $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (two-thirds the speed of light, accounting for refractive index):

$$t_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{d}{v} = \dfrac{2\,000\,000 \;\text{m}}{2 \times 10^8 \;\text{m/s}}$$

**Result:**

$$t_{\text{prop}} = 0.010 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms (one-way)}$$

**Round-trip (request-response):**

$$t_{\text{RTT}} = 2 \cdot t_{\text{prop}} = 20 \text{ ms}$$

---

**Step 5 — Compute the cloud processing + return latency.**

A typical cloud arrhythmia classifier (e.g., a deep CNN) takes:

$$t_{\text{cloud,inference}} \approx 50 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(measured on a GPU)}$$

$$t_{\text{cloud,return}} = t_{\text{tx,raw}} = 32 \text{ ms}$$

---

**Step 6 — Total end-to-end latency for cloud-based arrhythmia detection.**

$$T_{\text{cloud,total}} = t_{\text{tx,raw}} + t_{\text{prop}} + t_{\text{cloud,inference}} + t_{\text{prop}} + t_{\text{tx,raw}}$$

$$T_{\text{cloud,total}} = 32 + 10 + 50 + 10 + 32$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{cloud,total}} \approx 134 \text{ ms}}$$

> *Textual interpretation:* The clinician or actuator receives an arrhythmia alert **at least 134 ms** after the abnormal beat occurred. This is **acceptable for logging**, but is **borderline for closed-loop therapy** (e.g., pacing) where target is < 50 ms.

---

**Step 7 — Now design the local-analytics pipeline.**

At the edge, we replace 500 raw samples with **5 features** computed locally:
- `mean_RR`, `RMSSD`, `QRS_width`, `spectral_centroid`, `peak_amplitude`

Each feature is a 32-bit float = 32 bits.

$$D_{\text{local}} = 5 \cdot 32 = 160 \text{ bits per 1-second window}$$

$$t_{\text{tx,local}} = \dfrac{160}{250\,000} = 6.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.64 \text{ ms}$$

**Local MCU inference** (a quantized TinyML decision tree):

$$t_{\text{MCU,inference}} \approx 5 \text{ ms}$$

---

**Step 8 — Total end-to-end latency for local analytics.**

$$T_{\text{local,total}} = t_{\text{MCU,inf}} + t_{\text{tx,local}} + t_{\text{prop}} + t_{\text{tx,local}} + t_{\text{prop}}$$

$$T_{\text{local,total}} = 5 + 0.64 + 10 + 0.64 + 10$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{local,total}} \approx 26.3 \text{ ms}}$$

> *Textual interpretation:* Local analytics reduces end-to-end alerting latency from **134 ms → 26 ms** — a **~5× improvement** — and the **bandwidth cost is reduced by a factor of 50**.

---

**Step 9 — Compute the compression ratio $C_R$.**

$$C_R = \dfrac{D_{\text{raw}}}{D_{\text{local}}} = \dfrac{8000 \;\text{bits/s}}{160 \;\text{bits/s}} = 50$$

$$\boxed{C_R = 50 \times}$$

---

### 3.1.1 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Metric | Cloud-Only Path | Local Analytics Path | Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data transmitted per second | 8 000 bits | 160 bits | **50× reduction** |
| Transmission time | 32 ms | 0.64 ms | **50× faster** |
| Inference time | 50 ms (GPU) | 5 ms (MCU) | **10× faster** |
| Total latency | 134 ms | 26.3 ms | **~5× faster** |
| Cloud dependence | Hard | Soft (only for logging) | Decoupled |
| Privacy | Low (raw waveform exposed) | High (only features exposed) | Improved |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Savings Justification for Battery-Powered Wearables

**Problem Statement (typical KTU Part A or 14-mark sub-part):**
> *"A wearable transmits continuously to the cloud. Show the energy saving if it instead runs local analytics and transmits only when an event is detected."*

Given:
- MCU active power: $P_{\text{MCU}} = 30$ mW
- Radio transmit power: $P_{\text{tx}} = 100$ mW
- Event rate (arrhythmia): $\lambda = 0.5$ events/hour
- Event window to transmit: $t_{\text{event}} = 5$ s
- 1-hour observation period

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Cloud-path energy (continuous raw transmission).**

$$E_{\text{cloud}} = P_{\text{tx}} \cdot t_{\text{1h,continuous}}$$

$$E_{\text{cloud}} = 100 \text{ mW} \cdot 3600 \text{ s} = 360\,000 \text{ mJ} = 360 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Local-path energy (always-on MCU + event-triggered transmit).**

$$E_{\text{local,MCU}} = P_{\text{MCU}} \cdot 3600 = 30 \cdot 3600 = 108\,000 \text{ mJ} = 108 \text{ J}$$

$$E_{\text{local,tx}} = P_{\text{tx}} \cdot (\lambda \cdot t_{\text{event}}) \cdot 3600 = 100 \cdot (0.5 \cdot 5) = 250 \text{ mJ} \approx 0.25 \text{ J}$$

$$E_{\text{local,total}} = 108 + 0.25 = 108.25 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Energy savings factor.**

$$\eta = \dfrac{E_{\text{local,total}}}{E_{\text{cloud}}} = \dfrac{108.25}{360} \approx 0.30$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 30\% \text{ of original energy} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{savings} \approx 70\%}$$

> *Interpretation:* The local-analytics wearable uses **only ~30%** of the energy of a continuous-transmission device. With a 300 mAh Li-Po battery, this extends operational life from ~12 hours to ~40 hours.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Local Feature: Root-Mean-Square of a Real-Time ECG Window

This is a derivation KTU frequently sets as a sub-part to test whether the student understands **what** the local processor is computing.

**Definition (RMS of an $N$-sample window):**

$$x_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]^2}$$

**For $N = 250$ samples (0.5 s window at 500 Hz), the discrete computation expands as:**

$$x_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{x[0]^2 + x[1]^2 + \cdots + x[249]^2}{250}}$$

**Algorithm-equivalent pseudo-operation (no code, just expansion):**

1. Initialize `sum_sq = 0`
2. For $n = 0$ to $249$: `sum_sq = sum_sq + x[n] × x[n]`
3. Compute `mean_sq = sum_sq / 250`
4. Compute `x_RMS = sqrt(mean_sq)`

**Memory requirement** (why it fits on an MCU):

$$S_{\text{RAM}} = N \cdot w = 250 \cdot 16 = 4000 \text{ bits} = 500 \text{ bytes}$$

> *Interpretation:* Computing the RMS of a 0.5 s ECG window requires only **500 bytes of RAM** — well within the **64–512 KB** available on typical Cortex-M microcontrollers, justifying on-device computation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Justifying the Selection of Local Analytics Operations

A common KTU sub-part (7 marks) asks the student to **map clinical requirements to local-analytics operations**. The analytical matrix below provides the model mapping.

| Clinical Requirement | Selected Local-Analytics Operation | Justification Equation / Logic |
|---|---|---|
| Detect heart rate < 50 bpm | Thresholding on `mean_RR > 1.2 s` | Single comparison, $\mathcal{O}(1)$ |
| Detect QRS complex | Pan-Tompkins algorithm (derivative + squaring + moving average) | Bandpass 5–15 Hz, then squaring emphasizes R-peak |
| Detect atrial fibrillation | RMSSD + Shannon entropy on RR intervals | AF has high RMSSD variability and irregular entropy |
| Detect EEG seizure | Line length + spectral band power (8–30 Hz) | Seizures have increased high-frequency line length |
| Detect patient fall | Acceleration magnitude threshold + SVM | $a_{\text{mag}} = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2} > 2g$ |
| Detect apnea | Zero-crossing rate of airflow + 10-s absence | $ZCR = \dfrac{\# \text{zero crossings}}{N}$ |

## 4.2 Local-Analytics Processing Pipeline (Inside the Edge Device)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Raw Biosignal\n(ECG / EEG / EMG)"]:::raw --> B["1. Analog Front-End\n(Amplify + Anti-Alias Filter)"]:::stage
    B --> C["2. ADC Sampling\n(fs = 250-500 Hz)"]:::stage
    C --> D["3. Digital Preprocessing\n(Bandpass + Notch + Baseline)"]:::stage
    D --> E{"4. Event Detector\n(Threshold / Rule)"}:::decision
    E -->|"No Event"| F["Discard Window\n(Do not transmit)"]:::discard
    E -->|"Event Detected"| G["5. Feature Extraction\n(Mean, RMS, RMSSD, etc.)"]:::stage
    G --> H["6. Local ML Inference\n(TinyML classifier)"]:::stage
    H --> I["7. Decision Output\n(Normal / Arrhythmia)"]:::stage
    I --> J["8. Transmit Alert\n+ Compressed Features"]:::transmit
    J --> K["Gateway / Cloud"]:::cloud

    classDef raw fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef stage fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000
    classDef discard fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef transmit fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef cloud fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
```

> **Reading guide:** The **diamond (decision)** node is the key local-analytics choke point. The vast majority of windows (e.g., 99.5% for a healthy patient) are discarded, so the network is contacted only for clinically meaningful events.

## 4.3 Local-Analytics vs Cloud-Analytics — Data-Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    subgraph LOCAL_PATH["LOCAL ANALYTICS ARCHITECTURE"]

        B1["Sensor"] --> B2["Edge\n(Filter+Feature+ML)"] --> B3{"Event?"}
```

## 5.1 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define Local Analytics in the context of IoT. List any two advantages.**

**Model Answer (concise, KTU-valuation-ready):**

> **Local Analytics** is the paradigm of performing data analysis — including filtering, feature extraction, and machine-learning inference — on the **edge device, gateway, or fog node itself**, rather than transmitting raw data to a remote cloud server for processing.
>
> **Advantages (any two):**
> 1. **Reduced latency** — eliminates network round-trip, enabling sub-10 ms response for real-time biomedical control.
> 2. **Bandwidth conservation** — only features or events are transmitted, achieving up to **50× data reduction** in typical ECG applications.
> 3. **Improved privacy** — raw patient waveforms never leave the wearable, satisfying HIPAA/GDPR-by-design principles.
> 4. **Offline operability** — clinical decisions continue during network outages (critical in ambulances and rural clinics).
> 5. **Lower energy** — selective transmission extends battery life of wearables by ~70%.

*(Expected answer length: 4–6 lines, 3 marks = definition 1M + 2 advantages × 1M each)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]`
**Differentiate between edge analytics and fog analytics with a biomedical example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Edge Analytics | Fog Analytics |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | On the sensor/wearable MCU itself | On a local gateway (smartphone, bedside server) |
| **Compute class** | Microcontroller (Cortex-M, ESP32) | Edge server (RPi, Jetson Nano) |
| **Latency** | 1 – 20 ms | 10 – 100 ms |
| **Example** | QRS detection on a KardiaMobile patch | Multi-lead ECG correlation on a hospital bedside gateway |

> **Biomedical examples:**
> - **Edge:** A continuous glucose monitor (CGM) running a hypoglycemia-prediction TinyML model on the patch itself.
> - **Fog:** A hospital ward gateway aggregating 20 patients' heart rates to detect a ward-wide fever outbreak.

*(Expected length: tabular form + 1-line example each = 3 marks)*
