## 5.2 🅱️ PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

> **A wearable ECG patch operates at $f_s = 500$ Hz, 16-bit resolution, single channel. The uplink to a cloud server is $R = 250$ kbps, with one-way propagation delay $t_p = 10$ ms. The cloud arrhythmia classifier takes $t_c = 50$ ms. Propose and justify a local-analytics architecture that reduces both bandwidth and latency. Compute the bandwidth reduction factor, end-to-end latency improvement, and energy savings for a 1-hour observation period. $P_{\text{MCU}} = 30$ mW, $P_{\text{tx}} = 100$ mW, arrhythmia event rate $\lambda = 0.5$/hr, each event transmits for 5 s.**

#### **Part (a) — Latency & Bandwidth Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Raw data rate:**

$$D_{\text{raw}} = f_s \cdot w \cdot n_c = 500 \cdot 16 \cdot 1 = 8000 \text{ bits/s}$$

**Step 2 — Raw transmission time per second:**

$$t_{\text{tx,raw}} = \dfrac{8000}{250\,000} = 0.032 \text{ s} = 32 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Cloud end-to-end latency:**

$$T_{\text{cloud}} = 2 t_{\text{tx,raw}} + 2 t_p + t_c = 2(32) + 2(10) + 50 = 64 + 20 + 50$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{cloud}} = 134 \text{ ms}}$$

**Step 4 — Local feature pipeline (5 features, 32-bit each):**

$$D_{\text{local}} = 5 \cdot 32 = 160 \text{ bits/s}$$

$$t_{\text{tx,local}} = \dfrac{160}{250\,000} = 0.64 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Local end-to-end latency (with $t_{\text{MCU}} = 5$ ms):**

$$T_{\text{local}} = t_{\text{MCU}} + 2 t_{\text{tx,local}} + 2 t_p = 5 + 2(0.64) + 2(10)$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{local}} = 26.28 \text{ ms}}$$

**Step 6 — Bandwidth reduction factor:**

$$\boxed{C_R = \dfrac{8000}{160} = 50\times}$$

**Step 7 — Latency improvement:**

$$\boxed{\Delta T = \dfrac{134}{26.28} \approx 5.1\times \text{ faster}}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Energy Savings Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Cloud path energy over 1 hour:**

$$E_{\text{cloud}} = P_{\text{tx}} \cdot 3600 = 100 \text{ mW} \cdot 3600 \text{ s} = 360\,000 \text{ mJ} = 360 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Local MCU energy over 1 hour (always-on inference):**

$$E_{\text{MCU}} = P_{\text{MCU}} \cdot 3600 = 30 \cdot 3600 = 108\,000 \text{ mJ} = 108 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Local transmit energy over 1 hour (event-triggered only):**

$$E_{\text{tx,local}} = P_{\text{tx}} \cdot \lambda \cdot t_{\text{event}} \cdot 3600$$

Wait — $\lambda$ is already per hour, so:

$$E_{\text{tx,local}} = 100 \text{ mW} \cdot (0.5 \cdot 5) \text{ s} = 250 \text{ mJ} = 0.25 \text{ J}$$

**Step 4 — Total local energy:**

$$E_{\text{local,total}} = 108 + 0.25 = 108.25 \text{ J}$$

**Step 5 — Energy savings ratio:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{E_{\text{local,total}}}{E_{\text{cloud}}} = \dfrac{108.25}{360} = 0.301$$

$$\boxed{\text{Energy savings} = 1 - 0.301 = 0.699 = 69.9\%}$$

> **Conclusion:** The proposed local-analytics architecture achieves a **50× bandwidth reduction**, **~5.1× latency reduction** (from 134 ms → 26.3 ms), and **~70% energy savings**, making the wearable clinically safer, more private, and longer-lived.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]`**

> **Explain the architecture of Local Analytics in a biomedical IoT system. With a neat block diagram, describe the function of each layer. Discuss the role of TinyML in enabling local analytics on microcontroller-class devices. Mention two real-world biomedical products that employ local analytics.**

#### **Part (a) — Architecture and Block Diagram (7 marks)**

The **three-tier architecture** of local analytics in a biomedical IoT system is:

**Tier 1 — Sensor/Device Layer (Local Edge Analytics):**
- Houses the bio-sensors (ECG electrodes, SpO₂ photodetector, temperature thermistor, accelerometer).
- Performs **analog front-end** (amplification, anti-aliasing), **ADC**, **digital filtering** (bandpass 0.5–40 Hz for ECG, notch at 50 Hz), **feature extraction** (R-peak detection, RR interval, RMSSD), and **TinyML inference**.
- Runs on a microcontroller (e.g., STM32, nRF52840, MAX32666).
- Decision: **transmit only on clinical event**.

**Tier 2 — Fog/Gateway Layer:**
- Aggregates data from multiple wearables (per-patient or per-ward).
- Runs cross-sensor correlation and mid-complexity ML.
- Provides **local storage** during network outages and **store-and-forward** to cloud.
- Examples: hospital bedside monitor, patient's smartphone, ward gateway.

**Tier 3 — Cloud Layer:**
- Performs **population-level analytics**, long-term trend detection, and **model retraining** for periodic firmware updates pushed back to Tier 1.
- Maintains the **Electronic Health Record (EHR)**.

**Block diagram** (textual representation for KTU answer sheet):

```
[Bioelectrode] → [IA + AAF] → [ADC] → [DSP Filter] → [Feature Extractor] → [TinyML] → [BLE Radio] → [Gateway] → [Cloud]
                                                                                            ↓
                                                                              [Event-triggered transmit only]
```

---

#### **Part (b) — Role of TinyML + Real-World Products (7 marks)**

**Role of TinyML:**
1. **Model compression** — Quantization (float32 → int8) reduces model size by 4×; pruning removes redundant weights; knowledge distillation produces a small "student" model from a large "teacher".
2. **Framework support** — TensorFlow Lite Micro, Edge Impulse, ONNX Runtime Micro, Apache TVM provide inference engines for ARM Cortex-M and RISC-V microcontrollers.
3. **Memory fit** — A typical arrhythmia detection CNN can be compressed from ~2 MB to **< 50 KB** to fit in MCU flash.
4. **Latency** — Inference time drops from ~50 ms (cloud GPU) to **< 5 ms** (Cortex-M4 with CMSIS-NN).
5. **Energy** — A single inference on an MCU consumes **< 1 mJ**, enabling continuous monitoring on coin-cell batteries.

**Two real-world biomedical products employing local analytics:**

1. **Apple Watch (Series 4 and later) — Atrial Fibrillation Detection:**
   - Runs an on-wrist photoplethysmogram (PPG) analyzer and ECG classifier.
   - Locally detects irregular rhythm and alerts the user; only the **classified event (not the continuous waveform)** is uploaded to the iPhone / iCloud for clinician review.

2. **Dexcom G7 Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM):**
   - Runs a **local hypoglycemia-prediction model** on the wearable patch.
   - Provides 20-minute advance alerts to the user's phone over BLE.
   - The model's frequent retraining is performed in the cloud and pushed back via firmware update — a textbook example of **edge + cloud collaborative learning**.

3. *(Optional third)* **KardiaMobile 6L** by AliveCor — on-device 6-lead ECG with FDA-cleared local AFib detection.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Local Analytics" with "Edge Computing".** Edge computing is the *infrastructure* (compute at the edge); local analytics is the *function* (analytics done locally). You **lose 1 mark** if you use the two interchangeably.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the data-rate units.** Writing "$D_{\text{raw}} = 8000$" without the unit (bits/s or bps) costs a mark. KTU examiners are strict on units in Part A.
>
> 3. **Omitting the energy-saving justification.** Many students compute latency correctly but forget to show the energy savings — a 14-mark question explicitly asking for energy needs the calculation, not just a statement.
>
> 4. **Confusing $f_s$ with $N$.** $f_s$ is the **rate** (Hz); $N$ is the **count** (samples). A common error: $N = 500$ Hz is **wrong**; it should be $N = f_s \cdot t = 500 \cdot 1 = 500$ samples.
>
> 5. **Not naming TinyML frameworks.** If the question asks about enabling on-device ML, simply saying "machine learning" earns 1 mark; naming **TensorFlow Lite Micro / Edge Impulse / ONNX Runtime** earns full marks.
>
> 6. **In the diagram, drawing an arrow from sensor to cloud directly.** This signals you have not understood local analytics at all. **Always show the local processing block on the device side** before any network arrow.

---

## 5.3 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Pin this section to your revision wall the night before the exam.**

- [x] **Local Analytics = analyze data where it is generated**, not in the cloud. (Tier 1 + Tier 2 of the IoT stack.)
- [x] **Two-word mantra:** *Latency + Privacy*. These are the two engineering reasons local analytics exists.
- [x] **Three core operations:** (1) Filter, (2) Feature-extract, (3) Infer-and-decide locally.
- [x] **TinyML** = ML on MCUs. Use TensorFlow Lite Micro, Edge Impulse, ONNX Runtime Micro, or CMSIS-NN.
- [x] **Compression Ratio** $C_R = D_{\text{raw}} / D_{\text{local}}$ is the headline metric for bandwidth savings.
- [x] **End-to-end latency** $T = 2 t_{\text{tx}} + 2 t_p + t_{\text{infer}}$ — always include **both** propagation terms (up + down).
- [x] **Nyquist** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ — local downsampling is justified *after* analog anti-aliasing.
- [x] **Energy** is dominated by **transmit**, not compute. *Transmit less, sleep more.*
- [x] **Biomedical drivers** for local analytics: closed-loop therapy (insulin pump), seizure alerting, AFib detection, fall detection, apnea monitoring.
- [x] **Real-world products:** Apple Watch (AFib), Dexcom G7 (glucose), KardiaMobile (ECG), Inspire sleep implant.
- [x] **Edge ≠ Fog.** Edge is on the device; Fog is on the local gateway. Don't interchange the terms.
- [x] **Latency targets to memorize:** Cloud ≈ 100–500 ms; Edge ≈ 1–20 ms; Closed-loop therapy ≤ 50 ms.
- [x] **Privacy & regulation:** Local analytics supports HIPAA/GDPR by keeping raw PHI on-device.
- [x] **Selective transmission** (event-driven) is the simplest local-analytics strategy — even a threshold rule counts.
- [x] **Ring buffer** on edge = safety net for store-and-forward during network outages.
- [x] **Most common 14-mark question pattern:** "Compute raw vs local latency and energy; justify the local pipeline." **Practice this skeleton.**
- [x] **Mermaid / diagram tips:** Always show three tiers, always label the bit-rate on each arrow, always mark the **decision node** (diamond) for event-triggered transmission.
- [x] **Formula units to never forget:** Latency → **ms or s**; Bandwidth → **bps or kbps**; Energy → **J or mJ**; Power → **mW or W**; Compression ratio → **dimensionless**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Analytics and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cloud Analytics and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cloud Analytics** is the process of analyzing and deriving meaningful insights from structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data that is stored, processed, and visualized on cloud computing infrastructure. In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)** and biomedical systems, cloud analytics refers to the integration of massive-scale IoT-generated sensory data with distributed cloud computing resources to perform descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive analytics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**: Cloud Analytics in IoT is the unified framework that combines *data ingestion*, *distributed storage*, *parallel processing*, and *intelligent visualization* delivered as a service over the cloud to convert raw IoT biomedical sensor streams (ECG, EEG, SpO2, temperature, glucose, accelerometer) into actionable clinical and operational insights.

The architecture spans **four classical cloud service models**:

| Service Model | Full Form | User Responsibility |
|---------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **IaaS** | Infrastructure as a Service | Application, Data, Runtime, Middleware, OS |
| **PaaS** | Platform as a Service | Application, Data only |
| **SaaS** | Software as a Service | Only usage of the application |
| **FaaS** | Function as a Service | Individual functions/events only |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **hospital network with 50 rural clinics**, each generating thousands of patient vitals per second. Instead of buying massive servers for every clinic, the hospital subscribes to a **"smart medical cloud"** — much like how households subscribe to electricity from a power grid rather than running private generators.

- **Raw sensor data** = water flowing in from many taps (IoT devices).
- **Cloud servers** = the central water treatment and distribution plant.
- **Cloud Analytics** = the chemists at the plant who test, purify, and report which water is safe, which is contaminated, and forecast tomorrow's demand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway**: Cloud Analytics = "Outsource the brain to the cloud." The IoT devices only *collect*, while the cloud *thinks, remembers, and advises*.

## 1.3 Why Cloud Analytics Matters in Biomedical IoT

1. **Elastic Scalability** — Patient data grows from gigabytes to petabytes during pandemics; cloud auto-scales.
2. **Ubiquitous Access** — Doctors access reports from any device globally.
3. **Cost Efficiency** — Pay-as-you-use replaces capital expenditure (CapEx) with operational expenditure (OpEx).
4. **AI/ML Readiness** — Pre-trained models for arrhythmia detection, tumor classification, sepsis prediction are deployed directly.
5. **Interoperability** — Standards like **HL7 FHIR**, **DICOM**, and **IEEE 11073** integrate seamlessly on cloud.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Benchmark Metrics (Memorize for KTU)**:
> - **3Vs of Big Data**: Volume, Velocity, Variety (sometimes extended to 5Vs with Veracity and Value).
> - **Latency Targets**: < 1 ms (real-time ECG), < 100 ms (tele-surgery), < 5 s (batch analytics).
> - **Uptime SLA**: **99.999%** ("five nines") for hospital-grade cloud.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of Cloud Analytics for IoT

The cloud analytics stack is conventionally organized into **five logical layers**:

### Layer 1 — Data Ingestion Layer
- **Purpose**: Accepts heterogeneous IoT streams.
- **Protocols**: MQTT (lightweight, publish/subscribe), CoAP (constrained devices), HTTP/REST, AMQP.
- **Ingestion Patterns**:
  - *Batch ingestion* — periodic uploads (e.g., daily Holter monitor reports).
  - *Stream/Micro-batch ingestion* — continuous (e.g., ICU ventilator waveforms at 250 Hz).

### Layer 2 — Storage Layer
- **Hot Storage**: SSD-backed NoSQL (e.g., AWS DynamoDB, Azure Cosmos DB) for sub-10 ms reads.
- **Warm Storage**: Columnar databases (e.g., Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery) for analytical queries.
- **Cold Storage**: Object stores (e.g., AWS S3, Azure Blob) for archival compliance (typically **7 years** for medical records).

### Layer 3 — Processing Layer
- **Batch Processing**: Apache Hadoop (MapReduce) — high throughput, high latency.
- **Stream Processing**: Apache Kafka + Apache Flink / Spark Streaming — low latency, event-driven.
- **Serverless Compute**: AWS Lambda, Azure Functions — event-triggered micro-billing.

### Layer 4 — Analytics & Intelligence Layer
- **Descriptive**: Dashboards (KPI tracking — heart rate trends).
- **Diagnostic**: Root cause analysis (why SpO2 dropped at 3 AM).
- **Predictive**: ML models (XGBoost, LSTM) for sepsis prediction 6 hours ahead.
- **Prescriptive**: Recommendation engines (suggest drug dosage adjustment).

### Layer 5 — Presentation Layer
- Visualization via **Tableau, Power BI, Grafana, Kibana**.
- Mobile apps for clinicians, REST APIs for EHR integration.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Layer

- **Why Ingestion?** IoT devices cannot directly write into analytical warehouses; they need a *buffer* to handle bursty traffic.
- **Why Tiered Storage?** Putting every byte on expensive SSD is economically wasteful; medical archives are read 1–2 times per year.
- **Why Stream Processing?** In ICU, a 5-second delay can mean a missed cardiac arrest.
- **Why ML Layer?** Human clinicians cannot continuously monitor 100+ parameters per patient across 500 beds.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | Sampling Rate (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz — must sample at least twice the highest signal frequency |
| 2 | Data Rate per Sensor | $R = f_s \cdot N_b \cdot N_c$ | bits/second; $N_b$ = bits/sample, $N_c$ = channels |
| 3 | Daily Data Volume per Patient | $V_d = \dfrac{R \cdot T}{8 \cdot 10^9}$ | GB/day; $T$ = seconds of monitoring (typically **86400 s**) |
| 4 | Total Cloud Storage (n patients) | $V_{total} = n \cdot V_d \cdot R_{retention}$ | GB; $R_{retention}$ = retention period in days |
| 5 | Required Throughput | $\Theta = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i$ | bits/s — sum of all sensor data rates |
| 6 | Compression Ratio | $C_r = \dfrac{V_{original}}{V_{compressed}}$ | Dimensionless, typically **2:1 to 10:1** for ECG |
| 7 | Effective Storage After Compression | $V_{eff} = \dfrac{V_{total}}{C_r}$ | GB |
| 8 | Cloud Cost Estimation | $Cost = (S \cdot P_s) + (\Theta \cdot P_t) + (Q \cdot P_q)$ | Currency; $S$=storage, $\Theta$=throughput, $Q$=queries; $P$=unit price |
| 9 | Availability SLA | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | %; MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair |
| 10 | End-to-End Latency | $t_{e2e} = t_{ingest} + t_{queue} + t_{process} + t_{transmit}$ | ms — critical for real-time alerts |
| 11 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | dB — diagnostic quality metric |
| 12 | Compression with Loss Bound | $CR = \dfrac{\sum \vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \vert^2}{\sum x_i^2} \leq \epsilon$ | $\epsilon$ = PRD (Percentage Root-mean-square Difference), ECG acceptable $\leq 10\%$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid the pipe trap in tables**: The absolute value expressions use `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Hospitals** (e.g., Apollo, Mayo Clinic cloud deployments) — reduce ICU mortality by **15–20%** using predictive analytics.
- **Epidemic Surveillance** — Google Flu Trends (legacy) and modern BlueDot cloud analytics predicted COVID-19 outbreaks days before WHO alerts.
- **Drug Discovery** — Cloud analytics on genomic data accelerates precision medicine pipelines by **months**.
- **Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM)** — RPM market projected to exceed **USD 175 billion by 2027**, all powered by cloud analytics backends.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Daily Data Volume from Multi-Parameter ICU Monitor

A bedside ICU monitor records **5 channels** continuously for 24 hours: ECG (500 Hz, 16-bit), SpO2 (100 Hz, 16-bit), Respiration (50 Hz, 16-bit), Blood Pressure (1 Hz, 16-bit), Temperature (1 Hz, 16-bit).

**Step 1 — Compute individual channel data rates**

$$R_{ECG} = f_s \cdot N_b = 500 \cdot 16 = 8000 \text{ bits/s}$$

$$R_{SpO_2} = 100 \cdot 16 = 1600 \text{ bits/s}$$

$$R_{Resp} = 50 \cdot 16 = 800 \text{ bits/s}$$

$$R_{BP} = 1 \cdot 16 = 16 \text{ bits/s}$$

$$R_{Temp} = 1 \cdot 16 = 16 \text{ bits/s}$$

**Step 2 — Sum total throughput per patient**

$$\Theta_{patient} = 8000 + 1600 + 800 + 16 + 16 = 10432 \text{ bits/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to daily volume in GB**

$$V_d = \dfrac{\Theta_{patient} \cdot T}{8 \cdot 10^9}$$

where $T = 86400$ seconds, and the $8$ converts bits to bytes, while $10^9$ converts bytes to GB.

$$V_d = \dfrac{10432 \cdot 86400}{8 \cdot 10^9}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerator and denominator separately**

$$10432 \cdot 86400 = 9.0132 \times 10^{8} \text{ bits}$$

$$\dfrac{9.0132 \times 10^{8}}{8 \cdot 10^{9}} = \dfrac{9.0132}{80} = 0.1127 \text{ GB/day}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

A single ICU bed generates approximately **112.7 MB/day** uncompressed. For a 100-bed ICU, daily volume = **11.27 GB/day**, and over one year = **4.1 TB/year** per ICU.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cloud Storage Cost Estimation for Hospital Network

Given:
- $n = 500$ monitored patients
- $V_d = 0.1127$ GB/day per patient
- Retention period $R_{retention} = 7$ years $= 2557$ days
- Compression ratio $C_r = 5:1$
- Cloud pricing: $P_s = \$0.023$ per GB/month (S3 Standard tier)

**Step 1 — Uncompressed total storage**

$$V_{total} = n \cdot V_d \cdot R_{retention}$$

$$V_{total} = 500 \cdot 0.1127 \cdot 2557$$

**Step 2 — Numerator expansion**

$$500 \cdot 0.1127 = 56.35$$

$$56.35 \cdot 2557 = 144{,}086.95 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 3 — Apply compression**

$$V_{eff} = \dfrac{V_{total}}{C_r} = \dfrac{144086.95}{5} = 28817.39 \text{ GB} \approx 28.82 \text{ TB}$$

**Step 4 — Convert retention to months**

$$M = 7 \cdot 12 = 84 \text{ months}$$

**Step 5 — Compute monthly cost**

$$Cost = V_{eff} \cdot P_s \cdot \dfrac{1}{C_r^{storage}}$$

For tiered storage using S3 Glacier for archives older than 90 days ($P_{glacier} = \$0.004$/GB/month):

Assuming 70% of data becomes cold:

$$V_{hot} = 0.30 \cdot 28817.39 = 8645.22 \text{ GB}$$

$$V_{cold} = 0.70 \cdot 28817.39 = 20172.17 \text{ GB}$$

$$Cost_{monthly} = (8645.22 \cdot 0.023) + (20172.17 \cdot 0.004)$$

$$Cost_{monthly} = 198.84 + 80.69 = \$279.53$$

**Step 6 — Annual cost**

$$Cost_{annual} = 279.53 \cdot 12 \approx \$3354.36$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Availability SLA from MTBF/MTTR

If a cloud data center has $MTBF = 5000$ hours and $MTTR = 1$ hour:

$$A = \dfrac{5000}{5000 + 1} \times 100\% = \dfrac{5000}{5001} \times 100\%$$

$$A \approx 99.98\%$$

For "five nines" (**99.999%**):

$$\dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = 0.99999$$

$$MTBF = 0.99999 \cdot (MTBF + MTTR)$$

$$MTBF \cdot (1 - 0.99999) = 0.99999 \cdot MTTR$$

$$MTBF = \dfrac{0.99999}{0.00001} \cdot MTTR = 99999 \cdot MTTR$$

For $MTTR = 1$ hour, $MTBF$ must be **99,999 hours ≈ 11.4 years** of operation between failures.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Analytics Type | Latency | Use Case in Biomedical IoT | Typical Tool |
|----------------|---------|----------------------------|--------------|
| Batch | Minutes–Hours | Genome sequencing post-processing | Hadoop, BigQuery |
| Near Real-Time | 1–10 s | Vital sign anomaly detection | Spark Streaming |
| Real-Time Stream | < 1 s | Cardiac arrhythmia alerting | Apache Flink, Kafka |
| Edge-Cloud Hybrid | < 100 ms | Closed-loop drug infusion control | AWS Greengrass + Lambda |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Cloud Analytics Architecture for Biomedical IoT

<!-- DIAGRAM_4A_START -->
```mermaid

    A2[IoT Device: SpO2 Sensor]

    D[Stream Ingestion Layer: Kafka]
    E1[Hot Storage: NoSQL DB]
    E2[Warm Storage: Data Warehouse]
    E3[Cold Storage: Object Store S3]
    F1[Batch Engine: Spark Hadoop]
    F2[Stream Engine: Flink]
    F3[Serverless: Lambda Functions]
    G1[ML Model Registry]
    G2[Predictive Analytics Engine]
    H[Visualization Dashboard: Grafana]
    I[Clinician Mobile App]
    J[EHR System Integration HL7 FHIR]
    K[Alert Notification Service]

    A1 --> B
    A2 --> B

    A4 --> B

    D --> E1

    F3 --> G2

    G2 --> J
```
<!-- DIAGRAM_4A_END -->

## 4.2 Data Flow Lifecycle — From Sensor to Clinical Insight

<!-- DIAGRAM_4B_START -->
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step1: Sense - Biometric Capture]
    S2[Step2: Transmit - TLS Encrypted MQTT]
    S3[Step3: Ingest - Kafka Topic Per Patient]
    S4[Step4: Clean - Outlier Removal Imputation]
    S5[Step5: Store - Tiered Hot Warm Cold]
    S6[Step6: Analyze - ML Inference]

```

## 4.3 Cloud Service Model Responsibility Matrix

<!-- DIAGRAM_4C_START -->
```mermaid

    subgraph IaaS["IaaS Layer - Customer Manages"]
        I1[Application Code]
        I2[Data]

    end

    subgraph PaaS["PaaS Layer - Customer Manages"]
        P1[Application Code]
        P2[Data]

    end
```

## 4.4 Analytics Maturity Model (Descriptive → Prescriptive)

```mermaid

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4
```
<!-- DIAGRAM_4D_END -->
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**Define Cloud Analytics. List any two biomedical IoT applications that benefit from cloud analytics.**

**Model Answer:**

**Cloud Analytics** is the discipline of using cloud computing infrastructure — including storage, processing, and visualization services — to analyze large-scale structured and unstructured data and deliver insights via dashboards, alerts, or APIs. In biomedical IoT, it allows real-time and retrospective analysis of physiological data collected from sensors.

**Two biomedical IoT applications:**
1. **Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM)** — Continuous ECG, SpO2, and glucose streams from wearables are uploaded to the cloud, where ML models detect arrhythmias or hypoglycemic events in real time.
2. **Smart Hospital Asset Tracking** — RFID/BLE tags on ventilators, infusion pumps, and wheelchairs stream location data to the cloud, enabling analytics for utilization, theft prevention, and predictive maintenance.

*(Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks only if definition + 2 distinct applications are clearly listed. Single-line definitions lose 1 mark.)*

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**Compare IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS in the context of a biomedical cloud platform.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|-----------|------|------|------|
| User Manages | App, Data, Runtime, Middleware, OS | App, Data only | Only usage |
| Provider Manages | Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Network | OS, Middleware, Runtime | Everything except user data |
| Example in Healthcare | AWS EC2 hosting custom PACS server | AWS HealthLake for FHIR data | Epic EHR cloud subscription |
| Control Level | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| Development Effort | High | Medium | None |

**Conclusion:** A hospital research lab building a custom genomics pipeline prefers **IaaS** for flexibility; a startup building a patient app prefers **PaaS** for speed; a small clinic prefers **SaaS** for zero maintenance.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**: Students commonly lose 4–5 marks by (1) writing generic IoT content without biomedical context, (2) omitting diagrams in 14-mark questions (diagrams carry 2–3 marks), and (3) failing to mention specific cloud service examples like AWS, Azure, GCP.

---

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the layered architecture of cloud analytics for IoT with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each layer in processing biomedical sensor data. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A wearable ECG patch records at **250 Hz with 12-bit resolution** and operates for **24 hours** continuously. Calculate:
  (i) The data rate in bits per second.
  (ii) The daily data volume in MB.
  (iii) The annual cloud storage cost (uncompressed) if AWS S3 charges **\$0.023 per GB-month**. Assume 1 year = 365 days. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Layered Architecture (7 Marks):**

1. **Data Ingestion Layer (1.5 marks)** — Accepts streaming IoT data via MQTT/CoAP. In biomedical context, gateways in ambulances publish vitals to a broker before the patient reaches the hospital, enabling pre-arrival triage.

2. **Storage Layer (1.5 marks)** — Three-tier: hot (NoSQL for active monitoring), warm (data warehouse for queries), cold (object store for compliance). Ensures HIPAA-compliant 7-year retention.

3. **Processing Layer (1.5 marks)** — Combines batch (Apache Spark for genome analysis) and stream (Apache Flink for real-time arrhythmia detection).

4. **Analytics & ML Layer (1.5 marks)** — Runs descriptive dashboards, diagnostic root-cause analysis, predictive LSTM models (e.g., sepsis prediction 6 hours in advance), and prescriptive recommendations.

5. **Presentation Layer (1 mark)** — Grafana/Tableau dashboards, mobile apps, HL7 FHIR APIs feeding into EHR systems like Epic or Cerner.

**Mandatory block diagram** — Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete architecture diagram. (Examiner awards 2 marks if diagram is neat and labelled.)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Data Rate:**

$$R = f_s \cdot N_b = 250 \cdot 12 = 3000 \text{ bits/s}$$

**(ii) Daily Volume:**

$$V_d = \dfrac{R \cdot T}{8 \cdot 10^6} \text{ MB}$$

$$V_d = \dfrac{3000 \cdot 86400}{8 \cdot 10^6}$$

$$V_d = \dfrac{2.592 \times 10^{8}}{8 \cdot 10^6} = 32.4 \text{ MB/day}$$

**(iii) Annual Storage Cost:**

Annual volume in GB:

$$V_{year} = 32.4 \cdot 365 = 11826 \text{ MB} = 11.826 \text{ GB}$$

Monthly cost:

$$Cost_{monthly} = 11.826 \cdot 0.023 = \$0.272$$

Annual cost:

$$Cost_{annual} = 0.272 \cdot 12 = \$3.264$$

**Answer:** Annual cost ≈ **\$3.27 per patient per year** (extremely low, justifying cloud adoption).

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **4-level analytics maturity model**: Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, Prescriptive. Give one biomedical example for each level. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **big data analytics in cloud-based disease surveillance** during epidemics. Include in your answer: data sources, processing pipeline, and one real-world case study. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Analytics Maturity Model (7 Marks):**

1. **Descriptive (1.5 marks)** — Answers "What happened?" Example: Daily dashboard showing average heart rate of 500 ICU patients across the hospital network.

2. **Diagnostic (1.5 marks)** — Answers "Why did it happen?" Example: Root-cause analysis showing that a spike in ventilator alarms correlated with a specific nursing shift change.

3. **Predictive (2 marks)** — Answers "What will happen?" Example: LSTM model trained on EHR + vitals predicting sepsis **6 hours before clinical onset** with 85% AUC.

4. **Prescriptive (2 marks)** — Answers "What should we do?" Example: AI recommends an early lactate measurement order when sepsis risk crosses 70% threshold.

**Mandatory diagram** — Refer to Section 4.4.

**(b) Cloud-Based Disease Surveillance (7 Marks):**

**Data Sources (2 marks):**
- Wearable vitals (temperature, cough frequency from smart masks).
- EHR records (symptom codes, lab results).
- Mobility data (anonymized phone GPS for contact tracing).
- Social media & search trends (now controversial but historically used).

**Processing Pipeline (3 marks):**
1. **Ingestion** — Kafka topics handle millions of events per second during outbreak peaks.
2. **Real-time Analytics** — Flink jobs compute rolling R0 (reproduction number) per geographic cluster.
3. **ML Models** — Random Forest classifiers trained on past outbreaks predict hotspot emergence.
4. **Visualization** — Heatmaps on dashboards for public health officials.

**Real-World Case Study (2 marks):**
**BlueDot (Canada, 2019–2020)** — A cloud-based analytics platform that issued an early warning about atypical pneumonia cases in Wuhan **9 days before the WHO alert**, by analyzing airline ticketing data, news reports in 65 languages, and animal disease outbreak networks. This demonstrated cloud analytics' power for global epidemic intelligence.

**Alternative example acceptable:** Google Flu Trends (legacy), HealthMap by Boston Children's Hospital.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullets — Read 10 minutes before exam:**

- **Cloud Analytics = Cloud + Analytics**: storage + processing + ML + visualization delivered as service.
- **Four service models**: IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, FaaS — know examples (EC2, Azure HealthLake, Epic EHR, AWS Lambda).
- **Five architectural layers**: Ingestion → Storage (hot/warm/cold) → Processing (batch/stream) → Analytics (4 levels) → Presentation.
- **4Vs → 5Vs**: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value.
- **Key formulas** (most frequently tested):
  - $R = f_s \cdot N_b$ — sensor data rate.
  - $V_d = \dfrac{R \cdot 86400}{8 \cdot 10^9}$ GB — daily volume.
  - $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ — availability SLA.
- **Nyquist criterion**: Sample at $\geq 2 f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
- **Five-nines SLA = 99.999%** ≈ 5.26 minutes downtime/year.
- **Analytics maturity**: Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive (ascending value, ascending complexity).
- **Stream processing latency target**: < 1 second for biomedical alerts.
- **Real-world examples to mention**: BlueDot (epidemic), Mayo Cloud, AWS HealthLake, Google Fit, Apple HealthKit.
- **Standards to remember**: HL7 FHIR (data exchange), DICOM (imaging), IEEE 11073 (device communication).
- **Cost mantra**: Cloud replaces CapEx with OpEx — pay only for what you use.
- **Edge-Cloud synergy**: Time-critical decisions at edge; deep analytics in cloud.
- **Security triad in biomedical cloud**: Confidentiality (encryption at rest + in transit), Integrity (blockchain or hash chains), Availability (multi-region replication).
- **Examiner pet topic**: "Explain cloud analytics architecture with biomedical IoT example" — almost guaranteed in every KTU ESE.
- **Common pitfall**: Confusing "cloud computing" with "cloud analytics" — analytics is the *value-extraction layer*, not just hosting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Physical Servers and Cloud Offerings: Introduction to Cloud Storage Models and Communication APIs
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IoT Physical Servers and Cloud Offerings: Introduction to Cloud Storage Models and Communication APIs

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 IoT Physical Servers and Cloud Offerings — Foundational Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Terminology):**
> *IoT Physical Servers and Cloud Offerings* refer to the spectrum of on-premise, edge, and remote computational infrastructure (bare-metal servers, virtualized hosts, and third-party cloud platforms such as AWS, Azure, GCP) that collectively provide the **data acquisition, storage, processing, and analytics backbone** for an Internet of Things (IoT) system. Within the biomedical context, these servers handle Electronic Health Records (EHR), real-time vitals, and machine-learning inference for clinical decision support.

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hospital's Central Nervous System"

Think of an IoT deployment like a **modern multi-specialty hospital**:

- **Wearable ECG/PPG sensors on patients** are the *nerve endings* — they sense signals.
- **Local gateway / fog node (e.g., Raspberry Pi in the ward)** is the *spinal cord* — initial filtering.
- **The Cloud / Data Center** is the *brain* — long-term memory, pattern recognition, decision-making, and reporting to specialists across the globe.

Just as a hospital cannot function with only nerve endings (no analysis) or only a brain (no raw signal), an IoT system requires a *layered infrastructure* of physical servers and cloud platforms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Tiers of IoT Server Infrastructure:**
> 1. **Edge Servers** — Co-located with sensors (latency < **10 ms**).
> 2. **Fog/Gateway Servers** — Intermediate LAN-level aggregation (latency **10–50 ms**).
> 3. **Cloud Servers** — Hyperscale data centers (latency **50–300 ms** globally).

---

## 1.2 Cloud Storage Models — The Four Service Tiers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cloud Storage Models (also called Cloud Service Models)** define the abstraction layer at which a cloud provider offers resources to a consumer. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects explicit understanding of **SaaS, PaaS, IaaS, and FaaS**.

| Tier | Full Form | What the User Manages | Real-World Healthcare Example |
|------|-----------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| **IaaS** | Infrastructure as a Service | OS, runtime, apps, data | AWS EC2 hosting a hospital's PACS image archive |
| **PaaS** | Platform as a Service | Apps & data only | Google App Engine running a dengue-outbreak dashboard |
| **SaaS** | Software as a Service | Only their data | Epic EHR (Electronic Health Record) accessed via browser |
| **FaaS** | Function as a Service | Only function code | AWS Lambda triggered by ICU ventilator alerts |

### 1.2.1 The Three Storage *Architectural* Models (Independent of Service Tier)

> [!NOTE]
> **Storage Architecture Models** describe *how data is physically stored* (as files, blocks, or objects), whereas Service Models describe *who manages what*.

1. **File Storage** — Hierarchical folder/file system. Best for: hospital document archives (PDFs, DICOM metadata).
2. **Block Storage** — Raw fixed-size blocks (typically **512 B – 4 MB**). Best for: high-IOPS databases like PostgreSQL hosting patient records.
3. **Object Storage** — Flat namespace with metadata + unique ID. Best for: unstructured data like MRI scans, audio recordings, IoT telemetry archives (e.g., AWS S3).

---

## 1.3 Communication APIs — The IoT's "Language Set"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A *Communication API* in IoT is a software interface that standardises how an IoT device exchanges data with a server, another device, or a third-party service, using a defined protocol (HTTP, MQTT, CoAP, WebSocket, etc.).

### 1.3.1 Intuitive Analogy — "The Multilingual Reception Desk"

Imagine a hospital reception desk that must converse with:
- Indian patients (English / Hindi) → **REST/JSON**
- Japanese patients via a translator (relay) → **MQTT via Broker**
- A deaf patient using sign language over a video call → **WebSocket** (persistent full-duplex)
- An old pager system for legacy equipment → **CoAP / UDP-based**

Each *API protocol* is a different "language" tailored to specific device, network, and energy constraints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Communication APIs Most Tested in KTU 2024 Scheme (OEEBT831):**
> 1. **REST (Representational State Transfer)** — HTTP/HTTPS, request-response.
> 2. **WebSocket** — Full-duplex, persistent TCP connection.
> 3. **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** — Pub/Sub, lightweight, broker-based.
> 4. **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** — UDP-based, REST-like, for tiny devices.
> 5. **SOAP** — XML-based, heavy, used in legacy hospital systems (HL7 over SOAP).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Physical Servers and Cloud Co-exist in IoT

The IoT architecture does **not** mandate that *all* processing must occur in the cloud. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly highlights the trade-off using three engineering parameters:

- **Latency** (time from sensor event to actionable output)
- **Bandwidth** (network cost per GB transmitted)
- **Privacy / Compliance** (e.g., HIPAA for biomedical data)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5-10-90 Rule (KTU Frequently Tested):**
> In modern IoT systems, roughly **5%** of data is actionable at the edge (real-time alerts), **10%** is processed at fog gateways, and **90%** is forwarded to the cloud for archival and batch analytics. The percentages are *indicative*, not universal.

---

## 2.2 Deep Analysis — Cloud Service Models

### 2.2.1 IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service)

- **Provider gives:** Virtual machines, raw storage, virtual networks, load balancers.
- **Consumer controls:** Guest OS, middleware, runtime, application, data.
- **Example:** AWS EC2, Microsoft Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine.
- **Biomedical use case:** Hosting a hospital's private DICOM image processing pipeline where full control over GPU drivers and HIPAA-compliant encryption is required.

### 2.2.2 PaaS (Platform as a Service)

- **Provider gives:** OS + runtime + middleware + development tools.
- **Consumer controls:** Application code and data only.
- **Example:** Google App Engine, Heroku, AWS Elastic Beanstalk.
- **Biomedical use case:** Deploying a Python/Flask ML model that classifies arrhythmia from ECG signals without managing the server.

### 2.2.3 SaaS (Software as a Service)

- **Provider gives:** Fully functional application accessed via thin client / browser.
- **Consumer controls:** Configuration and data only.
- **Example:** Epic Systems EHR, Philips HealthSuite, Google Workspace.
- **Biomedical use case:** A doctor accessing patient records from any device through a web portal.

### 2.2.4 FaaS (Function as a Service) / Serverless

- **Provider gives:** Event-driven ephemeral execution environment.
- **Consumer controls:** Function code, triggers, and execution duration.
- **Example:** AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions.
- **Biomedical use case:** A Lambda function triggered when a wearable's heart rate exceeds **120 bpm**, sending an SMS alert to the on-call cardiologist.

---

## 2.3 Deep Analysis — Communication API Protocols

### 2.3.1 REST API (Representational State Transfer)

- **Transport:** TCP, typically over HTTP/HTTPS (port **80 / 443**).
- **Data format:** JSON (most common), XML, plain text.
- **Verbs:** `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, `PATCH`.
- **Statelessness:** Each request carries all required information; server retains no session state.
- **Why it dominates IoT:** Universal browser/library support, easy debugging with tools like Postman.
- **Drawback:** High header overhead — an HTTP/1.1 header is typically **500–800 bytes**, problematic for bandwidth-constrained devices.

### 2.3.2 WebSocket

- **Transport:** TCP, upgraded from HTTP via the `Upgrade: websocket` handshake.
- **Communication model:** Persistent, full-duplex, bidirectional.
- **Header size:** Only **2–14 bytes** per frame after handshake.
- **Biomedical use case:** Real-time streaming of ICU ventilator waveforms (pressure, flow, volume curves) to a remote monitoring dashboard.

### 2.3.3 MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)

- **Transport:** TCP (port **1883** plain, **8883** TLS).
- **Architecture:** **Publish/Subscribe** model with a central **Broker** (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ).
- **Topics:** Hierarchical strings like `hospital/icu/bed7/hr`.
- **QoS Levels:** 0 (at-most-once), 1 (at-least-once), 2 (exactly-once).
- **Header overhead:** Only **2 bytes** fixed header — extremely lightweight.
- **Biomedical use case:** Aggregating vitals from 500+ wearables on a ward floor to a central analytics engine.

### 2.3.4 CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)

- **Transport:** UDP (port **5683** plain, **5684** DTLS).
- **Architecture:** REST-like (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) but on UDP.
- **Why UDP?** Tiny packet, no handshake, suited for **Class 0 / Class 1** devices (< **10 KB RAM**).
- **Drawback:** No guaranteed delivery; reliability added via **CON** (Confirmable) messages and retransmission.

### 2.3.5 SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol)

- **Transport:** HTTP, SMTP, TCP.
- **Format:** Rigid XML envelope with WSDL contract.
- **Status in IoT:** Largely replaced by REST, but still present in **legacy hospital HL7 v2/v3 integrations**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every numerical relationship, conversion, and standard metric you are likely to need for OEEBT831 Module 4. Use `\vert` instead of `$\vert$` in markdown tables to preserve table syntax.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Value (IoT/Medical) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** | $RTT = 2 \times t_{prop} + t_{proc} + t_{queue}$ | 10 – 300 | ms |
| **Throughput** | $Throughput = \dfrac{Payload_{bytes}}{RTT_{sec}}$ | 1 – 1000 | KB/s |
| **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $BDP = Bandwidth_{bps} \times RTT_{sec}$ | 1 $\times 10^{4}$ – 1 $\times 10^{6}$ | bits |
| **MQTT Fixed Header Size** | Constant | **2** | bytes |
| **HTTP/1.1 Header Size (typical)** | Variable | 500 – 800 | bytes |
| **WebSocket Frame Overhead** | Base + masking (server→client = 0) | 2 – 14 | bytes |
| **CoAP Header Size** | Fixed | **4** | bytes |
| **REST vs MQTT Payload Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{data}}{P_{data} + P_{header}} \times 100$ | REST ≈ 40 %, MQTT ≈ 90 % | percent |
| **QoS-0 Delivery Probability** | $P_{Q0} = 1 - P_{loss}$ | ≈ 0.95 (typical Wi-Fi) | ratio |
| **Storage Block Size** | $B_{block}$ (default) | 512, 4096 | bytes |
| **S3 Object Max Size** | $S_{obj,max}$ | **5 TB** | TB |
| **Heart Rate Alert Threshold** | $HR_{alert}$ | > **120 bpm** | bpm |
| **ECG Sampling Rate (clinical)** | $f_{s,ECG}$ | 250 – 1000 | Hz |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Hospitals:** Cloud-IaaS hosts the **PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System)** storing petabytes of CT/MRI scans.
- **Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM):** MQTT brokers route vitals from **wearables → cloud → doctor's phone**.
- **Telemedicine Edge:** WebSockets enable low-latency video plus real-time stethoscope audio streaming.
- **Medical Research:** Object storage (S3) archives **de-identified genomic datasets** (each file ≈ **100 GB**).
- **Disaster Recovery:** Hybrid cloud models replicate hospital data to a geographically separate cloud zone (RTO < **1 hour**).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — REST vs MQTT Payload Efficiency for a Wearable Heart-Rate Packet

A biomedical wearable transmits a JSON heart-rate reading every second. We need to compute the **Payload Efficiency** $\eta$ for both REST-over-HTTP and MQTT protocols and determine the protocol that minimises bandwidth on a low-power NB-IoT link.

### 3.1.1 Given Data

- **Data payload** (heart rate, timestamp, device ID) = $P_{data} = 80$ bytes
- **HTTP/1.1 header** (typical, including Host, User-Agent, Authorization, Content-Type, Accept) = $H_{HTTP} = 700$ bytes
- **MQTT fixed header** = $H_{MQTT,fixed} = 2$ bytes
- **MQTT variable header** (topic name `icu/wardA/bed07/hr` = 19 bytes, packet identifier = 2 bytes) = $H_{MQTT,var} = 21$ bytes
- **MQTT payload** (same JSON) = $P_{data} = 80$ bytes

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Total on-wire bytes for HTTP/REST request**

$$
S_{HTTP} = P_{data} + H_{HTTP}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
S_{HTTP} = 80 + 700 = 780 \text{ bytes}
$$

> Plain English: Every 1-second transmission of an 80-byte vital sign costs 780 bytes on the cellular link because of the large HTTP header.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 — Total on-wire bytes for MQTT publish**

$$
S_{MQTT} = H_{MQTT,fixed} + H_{MQTT,var} + P_{data}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
S_{MQTT} = 2 + 21 + 80 = 103 \text{ bytes}
$$

> Plain English: MQTT's compact framing shrinks the same 80-byte payload to 103 bytes — about **7.6×** smaller than HTTP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 — Compute Payload Efficiency $\eta$ for each protocol**

The general efficiency formula is:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{data}}{S_{total}} \times 100 \text{ percent}
$$

For HTTP/REST:

$$
\eta_{HTTP} = \dfrac{80}{780} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{HTTP} = 0.10256 \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{HTTP} = 10.26 \text{ percent}
$$

For MQTT:

$$
\eta_{MQTT} = \dfrac{80}{103} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{MQTT} = 0.77669 \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{MQTT} = 77.67 \text{ percent}
$$

> Plain English: Only ~10 % of an HTTP packet is actual data, while ~78 % of an MQTT packet is data — MQTT is dramatically more efficient for telemetry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 4 — Bandwidth saved per day for 500 wearables**

If the hospital has $N = 500$ wearables transmitting once per second:

$$
\text{Bytes/day}_{HTTP} = S_{HTTP} \times N \times 86400
$$

$$
\text{Bytes/day}_{HTTP} = 780 \times 500 \times 86400 = 3.3696 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Bytes/day}_{HTTP} \approx 33.7 \text{ GB/day}
$$

$$
\text{Bytes/day}_{MQTT} = 103 \times 500 \times 86400
$$

$$
\text{Bytes/day}_{MQTT} = 4.4496 \times 10^{9} \text{ bytes} \approx 4.45 \text{ GB/day}
$$

> Plain English: Switching from HTTP to MQTT saves roughly **29.25 GB/day** of cellular bandwidth — significant for metered NB-IoT or 4G plans.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Storage Architecture by Data Type

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following decision matrix is a high-yield KTU model. Use it to answer "Which storage model is best for X?" questions.

| Data Type in Hospital IoT | Recommended Storage | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| ECG waveform stream (time-series) | **Object Storage (S3)** | Append-only, scalable, cheap per GB |
| Patient demographics (rows/columns) | **Block Storage (EBS)** | High IOPS, transactional, ACID |
| Radiology PDFs / DICOM metadata | **File Storage (EFS/NFS)** | Hierarchical browsing by patient ID |
| ML model training dataset (images) | **Object Storage + Index** | Petabyte-scale, parallel read |
| ICU real-time vitals cache | **In-memory (Redis / Memcached)** | Sub-millisecond read |
| Audit logs (regulatory) | **Object Storage (WORM)** | Immutable, low-cost archival |

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Latency Budget for a Closed-Loop IoT Medical Alert

A bedside fall-detection accelerometer must generate a caregiver alert within **$L_{max} = 200$ ms** to comply with elder-care safety standards. The system uses **CoAP → Edge Gateway → MQTT Broker → Cloud Function → SMS Gateway**. Compute the cumulative latency and check feasibility.

### 3.3.1 Assumed Component Latencies

- Sensor → Gateway (CoAP UDP): $t_1 = 15$ ms
- Gateway → Broker (MQTT TCP): $t_2 = 30$ ms
- Broker → Cloud Function (HTTPS REST): $t_3 = 60$ ms
- Cloud Function → SMS Gateway: $t_4 = 40$ ms
- Network propagation + jitter buffer: $t_5 = 25$ ms

### 3.3.2 Cumulative Latency

$$
L_{total} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 + t_5
$$

$$
L_{total} = 15 + 30 + 60 + 40 + 25
$$

$$
L_{total} = 170 \text{ ms}
$$

### 3.3.3 Compliance Check

$$
L_{total} \le L_{max} \iff 170 \text{ ms} \le 200 \text{ ms}
$$

> Plain English: The system meets the safety budget with a **30 ms safety margin**. If we had used REST end-to-end (extra handshake), $L_{total}$ would exceed **250 ms**, violating compliance — justifying CoAP at the constrained edge.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Cost / Performance Index for Cloud Tiers

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU 14-mark question asks to **justify** a cloud service model for a given biomedical use case. The analytical approach:

$$
\text{Control Index} = \dfrac{\text{Number of layers managed by consumer}}{4}
$$

$$
\text{Cost Index} = \dfrac{\text{Monthly bill (USD)}}{1000}
$$

| Service Model | Control Index | Typical Cost Index (per app) | Best Fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| IaaS | 1.00 | 8 | Custom compliance workloads |
| PaaS | 0.50 | 4 | ML model deployment |
| SaaS | 0.25 | 1.5 | End-user clinical software |
| FaaS | 0.10 | 0.5 | Event-driven alerts |

> Plain English: As you move "up" the stack (IaaS → SaaS), control decreases but management overhead and time-to-deploy also decrease — the trade-off that drives cloud selection.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IoT-Cloud Three-Tier Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Patient Wearable ECG]:::sensor --> A2[Bedside SpO2 Monitor]:::sensor
    A3[Implanted Pacemaker]:::sensor --> A2
    A2 --> B1[Edge Gateway Raspberry Pi]:::edge
    B1 --> B2[Fog Server Hospital LAN]:::fog
    B2 --> C1[Cloud Ingestion API Gateway]:::cloud
    C1 --> C2[MQTT Broker HiveMQ]:::cloud
    C1 --> C3[REST API Flask]:::cloud
    C2 --> D1[Time Series DB InfluxDB]:::storage
    C3 --> D2[Object Storage S3]:::storage
    C3 --> D3[Block Storage EBS]:::storage
    D1 --> E1[ML Inference Engine]:::analytics
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Doctor Dashboard WebSocket]:::ui
    E1 --> F2[Patient Mobile App REST]:::ui
    E1 --> F3[SMS Alert FaaS Lambda]:::ui

    classDef sensor fill:#FFDDC1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef edge fill:#C1E1FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef fog fill:#A0E7A0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef cloud fill:#D8B4FE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef storage fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef analytics fill:#FF9AA2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef ui fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> Plain English Reading: Sensors → Edge → Fog → Cloud (Ingestion + Broker + API) → Storage → Analytics → End-user UI. Each arrow is a *Communication API* in a specific protocol.

---

## 4.2 Communication API Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Device has less than 10 KB RAM?} -->|Yes| P1[Use CoAP over UDP]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Real-time bidirectional streaming needed?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| P2[Use WebSocket over TCP]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Many devices one central server pub sub pattern?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| P3[Use MQTT with broker]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Legacy hospital HL7 SOAP system?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| P4[Use SOAP XML]
    Q4 -->|No| P5[Use REST over HTTPS JSON]
```

> Plain English Reading: The decision tree walks you from the most constrained device upward — CoAP → WebSocket → MQTT → SOAP → REST, which is the typical order KTU examiners expect in exam answers.

---

## 4.3 Cloud Service Model Stack Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Application Layer Patient Facing] --> L2[Data Layer]
    L2 --> L3[Runtime Layer Python JVM Node]
    L3 --> L4[Middleware Layer Queue Cache]
    L4 --> L5[Operating System Layer Linux Windows]
    L5 --> L6[Virtualization Layer Hypervisor]
    L6 --> L7[Physical Server Layer CPU RAM Disk]

    subgraph SAAS
        S1[Consumer manages ONLY app and data]
    end
    subgraph PAAS
        S2[Consumer manages app and data; provider gives OS upward]
    end
    subgraph IAAS
        S3[Consumer manages OS upward; provider gives virtualization downward]
    end
    SAAS --- L1
    PAAS --- L3
    IAAS --- L6
```

> Plain English Reading: The horizontal line cutting the stack represents where responsibility shifts from provider to consumer — lower line = more control (IaaS), higher line = less management (SaaS).

---

## 4.4 Storage Architecture Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[File Storage Hierarchy Folders]:::file --> D[Mounted as Network Drive NFS SMB]
    B[Block Storage Raw Fixed Size Blocks]:::block --> E[Attached as Virtual Disk iSCSI AWS EBS]
    C[Object Storage Flat Key Value with Metadata]:::object --> F[Accessed via REST API S3 Buckets]

    classDef file fill:#FFF4B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef block fill:#B0E0FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef object fill:#FFB0B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> Plain English Reading: File = think "C: drive". Block = think "raw hard disk". Object = think "URL-addressed blob with a tag".
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> Differentiate between **IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS** with one biomedical example for each.

### Model Answer (3 marks — keyword-driven valuation)

| Service Model | What Consumer Manages | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** | OS, runtime, application, data | AWS EC2 hosting hospital's PACS image server |
| **PaaS** | Application and data only | Google App Engine running an ML-based ECG classifier |
| **SaaS** | Only their data | Epic EHR used by clinicians through a web browser |

> Plain English: The "deeper" into the stack you manage, the higher the *control* but also the higher the *administrative burden* — a classic KTU 3-mark conceptual question.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> Compare **REST API and MQTT protocol** based on (i) transport layer, (ii) communication model, and (iii) header overhead.

### Model Answer (3 marks — table-format valuation)

| Parameter | REST API | MQTT |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP (HTTP/HTTPS, port 80/443) | TCP (port 1883/8883) |
| **Communication Model** | Request/Response (client-initiated) | Publish/Subscribe (broker-mediated) |
| **Header Overhead** | 500 – 800 bytes (HTTP/1.1) | 2 bytes (fixed) + variable topic |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **Q3 (A).** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-tier IoT architecture** comprising edge, fog, and cloud layers. State the typical latency range for each tier. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **four cloud service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, FaaS)** in detail. For each, justify its suitability for a specific biomedical IoT use case. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Three-Tier IoT Architecture (7 marks)**

> The three-tier IoT architecture organises computational resources along the data path from sensor to analytics, balancing latency, bandwidth, and compute intensity.

| Tier | Location | Latency Range | Function | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Edge Tier** | On-device / co-located with sensor | < **10 ms** | Local filtering, thresholding, ML inference | Wearable ECG detecting ventricular fibrillation |
| **Fog Tier** | Hospital LAN gateway | **10 – 50 ms** | Aggregation, protocol translation, local storage | Raspberry Pi collecting vitals from 50 beds in ICU |
| **Cloud Tier** | Hyperscale data center | **50 – 300 ms** | Long-term storage, batch analytics, ML training | AWS region running arrhythmia classification model |

> **Key Examination Sentence (must write for full marks):** "The edge tier minimises *response latency*; the fog tier balances *bandwidth and processing*; the cloud tier provides *unlimited scalability and deep analytics*."

**Block Diagram Required (3 marks):**
- Draw three horizontal bands labelled Edge, Fog, Cloud.
- Show arrows: *Sensors → Edge → Fog → Cloud → Analytics → User*.
- Annotate typical latency for each band.

**(b) Four Cloud Service Models (7 marks)**

| Model | Provider Responsibility | Consumer Responsibility | Biomedical Use Case | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** | Virtualisation, physical hosts, networking | Guest OS, middleware, app, data | Hospital PACS image processing on AWS EC2 GPU | Full control over GPU drivers and HIPAA-compliant encryption needed |
| **PaaS** | OS + runtime + middleware | App code, data | Deploying dengue-outbreak ML model on Google App Engine | No need to manage server patching; auto-scaling built in |
| **SaaS** | Full application stack | Only user data and config | Doctors using Epic EHR via browser | No installation; accessible from any device with login |
| **FaaS** | Event-driven runtime | Function code only | AWS Lambda sending SMS when SpO2 < **90 %** | Pay-per-execution; scales to zero when no alerts |

> **Closing Examination Sentence:** "Selection of a service model is governed by the *control-versus-convenience* trade-off and the *compliance needs* of biomedical data."

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **Q3 (B).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`
> **(a)** Explain **three storage architecture models** — file, block, and object storage — with diagrams. Provide **one biomedical use case** for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a comparative table, explain **REST, WebSocket, MQTT, and CoAP** communication APIs in terms of transport protocol, message pattern, header size, and ideal use case. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Storage Architecture Models (7 marks)**

1. **File Storage**
   - Hierarchical folders and files.
   - Accessed via standard file protocols: **NFS, SMB, CIFS**.
   - **Diagram:** Draw a tree of folders `Hospital → Department → Patient → Reports`.
   - **Biomedical use case:** Storing patient PDF discharge summaries in a network-attached NAS.

2. **Block Storage**
   - Data split into fixed-size **blocks (512 B – 4 MB)**, each with a unique address.
   - Accessed as a raw disk via **iSCSI, Fibre Channel, AWS EBS**.
   - **Diagram:** Draw a hard-disk cylinder labelled "Block 0 to Block N".
   - **Biomedical use case:** High-performance PostgreSQL database hosting the patient registry.

3. **Object Storage**
   - Flat namespace; each object has **data + metadata + unique key (URL)**.
   - Accessed via **RESTful HTTP (PUT, GET, DELETE)**.
   - **Diagram:** Draw a bucket containing labeled blobs like `mri_scan_001.dcm`.
   - **Biomedical use case:** Archiving petabytes of DICOM MRI images on AWS S3 (max object size **5 TB**).

**(b) Communication API Comparison (7 marks)**

| Parameter | REST | WebSocket | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP (HTTP) | TCP (upgraded) | TCP | UDP |
| **Default Port** | 80 / 443 | 80 / 443 | 1883 / 8883 | 5683 / 5684 |
| **Message Pattern** | Request/Response | Full-Duplex | Publish/Subscribe | Request/Response |
| **Header Size** | 500 – 800 B | 2 – 14 B | **2 B** fixed | **4 B** |
| **Reliability** | TCP guaranteed | TCP guaranteed | TCP + 3 QoS levels | UDP + CON/NON retransmit |
| **Ideal Use Case** | Web dashboards | Live waveform streaming | Telemetry from many devices | Constrained sensors < 10 KB RAM |
| **Biomedical Example** | Doctor fetching lab report | ICU ventilator curve streaming | 500-bed ward vitals | Battery-less implantable sensor |

> **Closing Examination Sentence:** "REST is for *interactivity*, WebSocket is for *real-time streaming*, MQTT is for *many-to-one telemetry*, and CoAP is for *resource-constrained devices*."

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in OEEBT831 Module 4:**
> 1. **Confusing Service Models with Storage Models** — IaaS/PaaS/SaaS ≠ File/Block/Object. Examiners award **0 marks** if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Writing "MQTT uses HTTP"** — Incorrect. MQTT runs directly over TCP, not HTTP.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the broker in MQTT** — A complete answer must name the **broker (Mosquitto / HiveMQ / EMQX)** and explain the publish-subscribe role.
> 4. **Omitting QoS levels in MQTT** — Always state QoS 0/1/2 and when each is used (QoS 2 for critical drug-dosage alerts).
> 5. **No latency numbers** — Examiners expect **specific numeric ranges** for edge/fog/cloud tiers.
> 6. **Generic "cloud is scalable" answers** — Always tie scalability to a *biomedical metric* (e.g., "scales to 10,000 wearables during a vaccination drive").
> 7. **Skipping HIPAA / data-residency mention** — For biomedical questions, a single sentence on regulatory compliance elevates the answer by 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 4 — IoT Physical Servers and Cloud Offerings — Last-Minute Revision**

- [ ] **Three server tiers** — Edge (< 10 ms) → Fog (10–50 ms) → Cloud (50–300 ms).
- [ ] **Four service models** — IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, FaaS; remember the *control gradient*.
- [ ] **Three storage architectures** — File (NFS), Block (EBS), Object (S3).
- [ ] **REST = HTTP/TCP/Request-Response/500–800 B header**.
- [ ] **WebSocket = Full-Duplex/TCP/2–14 B frame/streaming**.
- [ ] **MQTT = Pub-Sub/TCP/2 B fixed header/3 QoS levels/Broker-based**.
- [ ] **CoAP = REST-like/UDP/4 B header/for constrained devices**.
- [ ] **SOAP = XML/HTTP/legacy HL7 hospital systems**.
- [ ] **Object storage max object size = 5 TB (S3 standard)**.
- [ ] **Biomedical use cases must reference latency, HIPAA, and patient-safety metrics** for full marks.
- [ ] **Latency Budget Equation:** $L_{total} = \sum t_i$ — use it for any real-time alert system.
- [ ] **Payload Efficiency Equation:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{data}}{P_{data} + P_{header}} \times 100$ — REST ≈ 10 %, MQTT ≈ 78 % for a typical 80-byte vital sign.
- [ ] **Decision rule:** Tiny device → CoAP. Many devices to one server → MQTT. Browser to server → REST. Live video/waveform → WebSocket.
- [ ] **Always mention the broker** when discussing MQTT — never say "device-to-device direct".
- [ ] **Always end with a biomedical application sentence** to anchor marks in OEEBT831 context.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Webserver: Web Server for IoT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Webserver: Web Server for IoT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Web Server** in the context of IoT (Internet of Things) is a software application or hardware device that uses the **Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP)** or its secure variant **HTTPS** to process incoming client requests from IoT nodes, sensor networks, dashboards, or mobile applications, and returns the appropriate response — typically in the form of HTML web pages, JSON/XML data payloads, or control commands. The web server acts as the **central communication gateway** between the physical sensing/actuation layer and the application/visualization layer of an IoT ecosystem.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, an **IoT Web Server** is classified as a **middleware component** that performs four primary functions:
1. **Hosting** web-based dashboards and RESTful APIs.
2. **Listening** for HTTP/HTTPS requests on predefined TCP ports (default **Port 80** for HTTP, **Port 443** for HTTPS).
3. **Processing** sensor data and forwarding it to clients in standardized formats.
4. **Authenticating** and authorizing IoT clients using tokens, API keys, or OAuth 2.0.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A web server for IoT is not limited to serving static HTML pages — it is the *intelligence hub* that bridges sensors/actuators (Thing layer) with end-user applications (Application layer) over the Internet.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Restaurant" Model

Imagine a modern smart restaurant:

| Restaurant Element | IoT Web Server Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer** (places order) | **IoT Client** (Browser / Mobile App) | Sends HTTP `GET`, `POST` requests |
| **Waiter** | **Web Server** (e.g., Node.js, Apache) | Receives, routes, and serves requests |
| **Kitchen** | **Database / Sensor Layer** | Stores sensor data, generates response data |
| **Menu Card** | **Web Dashboard / API Endpoint** | The interface the client interacts with |
| **Order Slip** | **HTTP Request Packet** | Structured request with method, URL, headers |
| **Served Dish** | **HTTP Response** (HTML/JSON) | Delivered back to the client with status code |

**The Flow:** The customer (client) places an order (HTTP request) → the waiter (web server) takes it to the kitchen (database) → the kitchen prepares the food (processes the sensor data) → the waiter serves it back (HTTP response). The web server is the *intermediary translator* that converts raw sensor signals into structured, human-readable data.

### 1.3 Classification of Web Servers in IoT

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Embedded Web Server:** A lightweight web server running *directly* on the IoT device (e.g., ESP8266, ESP32, Raspberry Pi) that hosts a control interface accessible via a local IP address or public domain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Cloud-Based Web Server:** A scalable, high-availability web server hosted on cloud infrastructure (e.g., AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT) that aggregates data from thousands of distributed IoT devices.

**The three architectural categories are:**
- **Type 1 — Device-Embedded Web Server:** Runs on the IoT node itself (e.g., ESP32 hosting a temperature dashboard).
- **Type 2 — Gateway-Based Web Server:** Runs on a local gateway (e.g., Raspberry Pi) interfacing multiple sensors.
- **Type 3 — Cloud-Hosted Web Server:** Runs on remote cloud platforms for global accessibility and analytics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of an IoT Web Server System

The architecture is built on **four tightly-coupled layers**:

1. **Perception Layer (Thing Layer):** Physical sensors and actuators generate raw data (temperature, humidity, ECG, pressure).
2. **Network Layer:** Data is transmitted using Wi-Fi, Ethernet, LoRa, ZigBee, or cellular (4G/5G) to the web server.
3. **Middleware / Web Server Layer:** Processes HTTP requests, queries databases, applies business logic, and formats responses.
4. **Application Layer:** End-users interact via web dashboards, mobile apps, or third-party services consuming REST APIs.

**Why this layered approach?** It decouples the *physical world* (sensors) from the *digital world* (user interfaces), enabling **scalability**, **modularity**, and **interoperability** across heterogeneous devices.

### 2.2 The HTTP Request-Response Cycle in IoT

Every interaction between an IoT client and the web server follows a strict cycle:

- **Step 1 — DNS Resolution:** The client resolves the domain name (e.g., `iot.example.com`) to an IP address.
- **Step 2 — TCP Handshake:** A 3-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) establishes a reliable connection on Port 80/443.
- **Step 3 — HTTP Request Transmission:** The client sends a structured request packet containing the method, URL, headers, and optional body.
- **Step 4 — Server Processing:** The web server parses the request, queries the database/sensor, and applies authentication.
- **Step 5 — HTTP Response Transmission:** The server sends back a status code, headers, and the response body (JSON/HTML).
- **Step 6 — Connection Termination:** The TCP connection is closed (or kept alive for further requests).

### 2.3 HTTP Methods Critical for IoT Applications

The four foundational HTTP verbs used in IoT REST APIs are:

- **GET** → Retrieve sensor data (e.g., `GET /api/temperature`).
- **POST** → Submit new data or trigger an action (e.g., `POST /api/led/control`).
- **PUT** → Update existing resource state (e.g., `PUT /api/device/config`).
- **DELETE** → Remove a device or data record (e.g., `DELETE /api/sensor/12`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** IoT systems predominantly use **GET** (for reading sensor values) and **POST** (for sending control commands or uploading telemetry data). PUT and DELETE are less frequent but essential for full CRUD operations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Web Server Metrics & Data Calculations

| Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth (Throughput)** | $B = \dfrac{D}{T}$ where $D$ is data size, $T$ is time | bits/second (bps) | Determines network capacity required |
| **Total Daily Data Volume** | $V_{day} = B \times 86400$ | bytes/day (B/day) | Cloud storage and cost planning |
| **HTTP Request Size** | $S_{req} = H_{req} + B_{req}$ | bytes (B) | Network overhead estimation |
| **HTTP Response Size** | $S_{res} = H_{res} + B_{res}$ | bytes (B) | Bandwidth budgeting for dashboards |
| **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** | $RTT = 2 \times T_{prop} + T_{proc}$ | milliseconds (ms) | Latency-critical control systems |
| **Payload Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{B_{payload}}{B_{total}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | Protocol overhead optimization |
| **Requests per Second (RPS)** | $RPS = \dfrac{N_{req}}{T_{window}}$ | requests/second | Server load capacity planning |
| **Connection Capacity** | $C = \dfrac{B_{link}}{S_{avg}}$ | concurrent sessions | Web server scalability limits |

> **Where:** $D$ = Data size, $T$ = Time duration, $H$ = Headers, $B$ = Body, $T_{prop}$ = Propagation delay, $T_{proc}$ = Processing delay, $N_{req}$ = Number of requests, $T_{window}$ = Observation window, $B_{link}$ = Link bandwidth, $S_{avg}$ = Average session size.

### 2.5 REST API Constraints for IoT

A web server hosting IoT APIs must adhere to the **REST (Representational State Transfer)** architectural style with six constraints:

1. **Client-Server Separation** — UI and data storage are independent.
2. **Statelessness** — Each request contains all information needed; no session memory.
3. **Cacheability** — Responses must indicate if they can be cached.
4. **Uniform Interface** — Standardized resource identification (URIs) and methods.
5. **Layered System** — Client cannot tell if connected directly to end server or intermediary.
6. **Code on Demand (Optional)** — Server can send executable code (e.g., JavaScript).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **biomedical IoT applications**, web servers enable remote patient monitoring dashboards where a clinician accesses real-time ECG, SpO₂, and body temperature data from a cloud-hosted web interface. In **smart agriculture**, an ESP32-based web server hosts a local dashboard to monitor soil moisture and control irrigation pumps. In **industrial IoT (IIoT)**, cloud web servers aggregate data from thousands of sensors using REST APIs, enabling predictive maintenance and digital twin visualization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — HTTP Request Structure Breakdown

Let us analyse a typical IoT HTTP request sent by a client to control a smart bulb:

**Given HTTP Request:**
```
POST /api/v1/devices/bulb_01/control HTTP/1.1
Host: iot.home.local
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9
Content-Length: 27

{"state":"ON","brightness":75}
```

**Step 1 — Identify the Request Line Components:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Request Line} &= \text{Method} \quad \text{Request-URI} \quad \text{HTTP-Version} \\
&= \underbrace{\text{POST}}_{\text{Method}} \quad \underbrace{\text{/api/v1/devices/bulb\_01/control}}_{\text{Resource Path}} \quad \underbrace{\text{HTTP/1.1}}_{\text{Protocol}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Parse the Headers:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Host Header} &= \text{iot.home.local} \quad \rightarrow \text{Identifies the target server} \\
\text{Content-Type} &= \text{application/json} \quad \rightarrow \text{Indicates JSON body format} \\
\text{Authorization} &= \text{Bearer} \;\; \text{eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9} \quad \rightarrow \text{JWT token for auth} \\
\text{Content-Length} &= 27 \quad \rightarrow \text{Size of the body in bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Validate the Body Content:**

The body `{"state":"ON","brightness":75}` contains **27 bytes** of JSON payload instructing the server to switch the bulb ON at 75% brightness. The web server's REST handler will parse this JSON, authenticate the JWT token, identify the device `bulb_01`, and forward the command to the actuator.

**Step 4 — Server Generates Response:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Response} &= \text{HTTP/1.1} \;\; 200 \;\; \text{OK} \\
&\quad \text{Content-Type: application/json} \\
&\quad \text{Content-Length: 45} \\
&\quad \text{} \\
&\quad \text{\{"device":"bulb\_01","status":"success","new\_state":"ON"\}}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Numerical Derivation — Bandwidth and Data Volume for IoT Web Server

**Problem Statement:** A biomedical IoT system monitors a patient's heart rate using a cloud web server. The sensor uploads one JSON packet every **5 seconds**, and each packet is **256 bytes** in size. Calculate: (a) the average bandwidth required, (b) the total daily data volume, and (c) the payload efficiency if each HTTP request carries 380 bytes of headers overhead.

**Given Data:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{interval} &= 5 \;\; \text{seconds} \\
S_{payload} &= 256 \;\; \text{bytes (B)} \\
S_{headers} &= 380 \;\; \text{bytes (B)} \\
N_{seconds/day} &= 86400 \;\; \text{s/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (a) — Average Bandwidth:**

$$\begin{aligned}
B_{avg} &= \frac{S_{payload}}{T_{interval}} \\
B_{avg} &= \frac{256 \;\; \text{B}}{5 \;\; \text{s}} \\
B_{avg} &= 51.2 \;\; \text{B/s}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to bits per second (multiply by 8):

$$\begin{aligned}
B_{avg} &= 51.2 \times 8 \\
B_{avg} &= 409.6 \;\; \text{bits/second (bps)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b) — Total Daily Data Volume:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{day} &= B_{avg} \times N_{seconds/day} \\
V_{day} &= 51.2 \;\; \text{B/s} \times 86400 \;\; \text{s} \\
V_{day} &= 4,423,680 \;\; \text{B/day} \\
V_{day} &\approx 4.42 \;\; \text{MB/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (c) — Payload Efficiency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{S_{payload}}{S_{payload} + S_{headers}} \times 100 \\
\eta &= \frac{256}{256 + 380} \times 100 \\
\eta &= \frac{256}{636} \times 100 \\
\eta &= 40.25 \;\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A payload efficiency of only 40.25% indicates that **59.75% of bandwidth is wasted on HTTP headers**. For bandwidth-constrained IoT networks, protocols like **MQTT** or **CoAP** are preferred as they use binary framing and achieve efficiency above 80%.

### 3.3 Latency Calculation for Time-Critical IoT Control

**Problem:** A web server is hosted at a cloud data centre **200 km** away from the IoT client. The signal propagates through fibre-optic cable at $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. The server processing time is **15 ms**. Calculate the total Round-Trip Time (RTT).

**Given:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= 200 \;\; \text{km} = 200{,}000 \;\; \text{m} \\
v &= 2 \times 10^{8} \;\; \text{m/s} \\
T_{proc} &= 15 \;\; \text{ms} = 0.015 \;\; \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Calculate One-Way Propagation Delay:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{prop} &= \frac{d}{v} \\
T_{prop} &= \frac{200{,}000}{2 \times 10^{8}} \\
T_{prop} &= 0.001 \;\; \text{s} = 1 \;\; \text{ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Total RTT:**

$$\begin{aligned}
RTT &= 2 \times T_{prop} + T_{proc} \\
RTT &= 2 \times 1 \;\; \text{ms} + 15 \;\; \text{ms} \\
RTT &= 2 \;\; \text{ms} + 15 \;\; \text{ms} \\
RTT &= 17 \;\; \text{ms}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Result Interpretation:** An RTT of 17 ms is acceptable for a remote patient dashboard but **NOT suitable** for closed-loop robotic control (which requires RTT < 5 ms). For such applications, **edge computing web servers** must be deployed closer to the device.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Embedded vs Cloud Web Servers

| Parameter | Embedded Web Server | Cloud Web Server |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | On the IoT device (ESP32, RPi) | Remote data centre (AWS, Azure) |
| **Accessibility** | Local network / Port forwarding | Global Internet access |
| **Scalability** | Limited to 1–10 clients | Millions of concurrent clients |
| **Latency** | < 5 ms (local) | 15–100 ms (geographic) |
| **Security** | Basic authentication, no TLS | TLS 1.3, OAuth 2.0, IAM policies |
| **Examples** | ESP32 WebServer, Flask on RPi | AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google IoT |
| **Cost** | One-time hardware cost | Subscription / pay-per-use |
| **Downtime Risk** | High (single point of failure) | Low (99.9% SLA guaranteed) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IoT Web Server System Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PerceptionLayer[PERCEPTION LAYER - Things]
        sensor1[Temperature Sensor]
        sensor2[Humidity Sensor]
        sensor3[ECG Sensor]
        actuator1[Smart Relay]
        actuator2[LED Indicator]
    end

    subgraph NetworkLayer[NETWORK LAYER - Communication]
        wifi1[Wi-Fi Module ESP8266]
        ble1[Bluetooth LE]
        cellular1[4G LTE Modem]
    end

    subgraph MiddlewareLayer[MIDDLEWARE - IoT Web Server]
        gateway1[Raspberry Pi Gateway]
        flaskapp[Flask REST API Server]
        database1[(SQLite Time-Series DB)]
        auth1[JWT Authenticator]
    end

    subgraph CloudLayer[CLOUD HOSTED WEB SERVER]
        awsiot[AWS IoT Core]
        dashboard1[React Web Dashboard]
        analytics1[Analytics Engine]
        storage1[(S3 Data Lake)]
    end

    subgraph AppLayer[APPLICATION LAYER - Clients]
        browser1[Web Browser]
        mobile1[Mobile App]
        thirdparty1[Third Party API Consumer]
    end

    sensor1 --> wifi1
    sensor2 --> wifi1
```
