# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Cloud Computing. List the three service models and two deployment models of cloud computing relevant to IoT.**

**Model Answer (Point-wise):**

**Definition:** Cloud computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort.

**Three Service Models (SPI + FaaS):**
1. **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service):** End-user applications delivered over the Internet (e.g., ThingSpeak).
2. **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service):** Platform providing IoT services like device registry and message routing (e.g., AWS IoT Core).
3. **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service):** Raw compute, storage, and network resources (e.g., AWS EC2).

**Two Deployment Models:**
1. **Public Cloud:** Open to general public, owned by third party (AWS, Azure).
2. **Private Cloud:** Used exclusively by a single organization (hospital data center).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the role of FaaS (Function-as-a-Service) in IoT cloud applications with a biomedical example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of FaaS in IoT:**
FaaS (Function-as-a-Service), also called **serverless computing**, executes small pieces of code (functions) in response to IoT events without provisioning or managing servers. The cloud provider dynamically allocates resources only when a function is triggered, and the user pays only for the actual compute time consumed (in milliseconds).

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Event-driven:** Triggered by sensor data, HTTP requests, or message queue events
- **Auto-scaling:** Scales from zero to thousands of concurrent executions
- **Stateless:** Each invocation is independent
- **Pay-per-execution:** No charge when idle

**Biomedical Example:**
A wearable ECG device continuously publishes heart rate data to an MQTT broker hosted on AWS IoT Core. An **AWS Lambda function** is configured as a rule action. When the heart rate exceeds **120 bpm** (indicative of tachycardia), the Lambda function is triggered, which:
1. Retrieves patient details from DynamoDB
2. Sends an SMS alert via SNS to the assigned cardiologist
3. Logs the event for HIPAA audit trail
4. Updates the hospital's live dashboard via API Gateway

This entire workflow runs **without any dedicated server**, with millisecond-level latency, and costs pennies per month per patient.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**Part (a) (7 Marks):** With a neat block diagram, explain the layered architecture of Cloud for IoT. Discuss the function of each layer.

**Model Answer:**

The Cloud-for-IoT architecture consists of **four primary layers**:

1. **Sensing/Perception Layer:** Contains physical sensors (ECG, SpO₂, temperature, pressure) and actuators (insulin pump, ventilator). Converts physical phenomena into electrical signals.

2. **Network/Edge Layer:** Comprises gateways, routers, and fog nodes. Provides connectivity (Wi-Fi, 5G, LoRa, BLE) and performs preliminary data filtering, aggregation, and protocol translation.

3. **Cloud Layer:** The central processing hub. Handles:
   - **Ingestion** (MQTT/HTTP brokers)
   - **Storage** (hot/warm/cold tiers)
   - **Processing** (batch + stream analytics using Spark/Kafka)
   - **Analytics & ML** (TensorFlow, PyTorch models)
   - **Security** (TLS, OAuth, encryption)

4. **Application Layer:** End-user interfaces — web dashboards, mobile apps, alert systems, EHR integration via HL7 FHIR APIs.

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.

**Function of each layer:**
- **Layer 1** acquires data
- **Layer 2** transmits and pre-processes
- **Layer 3** stores, analyzes, and learns
- **Layer 4** presents insights and enables decisions

---

**Part (b) (7 Marks):** A wearable health monitoring system consists of 2000 patients. Each device transmits a 512-byte packet every 5 seconds. The protocol overhead is 15%. Calculate:
(i) Aggregate data rate in Mbps
(ii) Total data generated per day in GB
(iii) Storage required for 3 years in TB

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Aggregate Data Rate:**

Data per device per second:
$$
D_{device} = \frac{512 \text{ bytes}}{5 \text{ s}} = 102.4 \text{ bytes/s}
$$

Aggregate before overhead:
$$
R_{payload} = 102.4 \times 2000 = 204{,}800 \text{ bytes/s}
$$
$$
R_{payload} = 204{,}800 \times 8 = 1{,}638{,}400 \text{ bps} = 1.6384 \text{ Mbps}
$$

With 15% overhead:
$$
R_{agg} = 1.6384 \times 1.15 = 1.8842 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**(ii) Data per Day:**

$$
D_{day} = R_{agg} \times 86400 \text{ s} = 1.8842 \times 10^6 \times 86400
$$
$$
D_{day} = 1.628 \times 10^{11} \text{ bits} = \frac{1.628 \times 10^{11}}{8} \text{ bytes} = 2.035 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes}
$$
$$
D_{day} = 20.35 \text{ GB/day}
$$

**(iii) Storage for 3 Years (3 × 365 = 1095 days):**

$$
D_{3yr} = 20.35 \times 1095 = 22{,}283 \text{ GB} = 22.28 \text{ TB}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) **1.88 Mbps**
- (ii) **20.35 GB/day**
- (iii) **22.28 TB**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative]

**Part (a) (7 Marks):** Compare and contrast IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS in the context of IoT cloud deployment. Give one biomedical use case for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Abstraction Level** | Lowest (raw VMs) | Middle (platform) | Highest (ready app) |
| **User Manages** | OS, middleware, apps, data | Apps and data only | Nothing (just usage) |
| **Provider Manages** | Virtualization, hardware, network | OS, middleware, runtime | Everything |
| **Flexibility** | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| **Development Time** | Longest | Moderate | Zero |
| **Cost Model** | Pay for VM hours | Pay for platform usage | Pay per user/month |
| **Example Provider** | AWS EC2, Azure VMs | AWS IoT Core, GCP IoT | ThingSpeak, Ubidots |

**Biomedical Use Cases:**

1. **IaaS Use Case:** A research hospital runs its **private MQTT broker (Mosquitto)** on an AWS EC2 instance to ingest telemetry from custom-built ICU ventilators, requiring full control over the message routing logic.

2. **PaaS Use Case:** A chain of diagnostic labs uses **AWS IoT Core** to onboard 10,000 glucose meters, with built-in device shadows, rule engine, and automatic scaling — no need to manage brokers.

3. **SaaS Use Case:** A rural primary health center uses **ThingSpeak** (a SaaS dashboard) to visualize patient temperature data uploaded by BLE thermometers, with no backend development.

**Conclusion:** The choice depends on the trade-off between **control, development effort, and time-to-market**.

---

**Part (b) (7 Marks):** A hospital cloud EHR system has MTBF = 8000 hours and MTTR = 4 hours. A remote monitoring system requires the cloud to be available at least 99.99% (four nines). Determine:
(i) Current system availability and annual downtime
(ii) Whether the system meets the four-nines requirement
(iii) What MTBF would be needed to achieve 99.99% with the same MTTR of 4 hours

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Current Availability and Downtime:**

$$
A_{current} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{8000}{8000 + 4} = \frac{8000}{8004} = 0.999500
$$
$$
A_{\%} = 99.95\%
$$

Annual Downtime:
$$
D = (1 - 0.999500) \times 8760 = 0.0005 \times 8760 = 4.38 \text{ hours/year}
$$

**(ii) Compliance Check:**

Required: 99.99% → Maximum allowed downtime:
$$
D_{max} = (1 - 0.9999) \times 8760 = 0.0001 \times 8760 = 0.876 \text{ hours/year}
$$

Current downtime = **4.38 hours/year > 0.876 hours/year**

**The system does NOT meet the four-nines requirement.**

**(iii) Required MTBF for 99.99%:**

We need:
$$
A = 0.9999 = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + 4}
$$

Solving:
$$
0.9999 \times (MTBF + 4) = MTBF
$$
$$
0.9999 \cdot MTBF + 3.9996 = MTBF
$$
$$
3.9996 = MTBF - 0.9999 \cdot MTBF
$$
$$
3.9996 = MTBF \times (1 - 0.9999) = MTBF \times 0.0001
$$
$$
\boxed{MTBF = \frac{3.9996}{0.0001} = 39{,}996 \text{ hours} \approx 40{,}000 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** The hospital must either **reduce MTTR** (faster recovery) or **increase MTBF** (more reliable components/redundancy) to meet the four-nines SLA.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Students often write storage in bytes instead of GB/TB → **lose 1 mark**
> 2. **Confusing bits vs bytes:** 1 byte = 8 bits. Multiplying bps × seconds gives bits, divide by 8 for bytes → **lose 1 mark**
> 3. **Missing the overhead factor:** Protocol overhead (TCP/IP = 20%, HTTP = ~30%) is a frequent trap → **lose 1 mark**
> 4. **Forgetting the 86400 seconds/day and 8760 hours/year constants** → **lose 1 mark**
> 5. **Not drawing a block diagram** in 7-mark architecture questions → **lose 2 marks minimum**
> 6. **Writing vague definitions** in Part A — KTU examiners award marks only for *specific keywords* (NIST definition, SPI model, etc.)

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These 15 Points:**

- [ ] **Definition:** Cloud computing = on-demand, pay-per-use, elastic pooled computing resources over the Internet
- [ ] **5 Characteristics:** On-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service
- [ ] **3 Service Models:** SaaS (apps), PaaS (platform), IaaS (infrastructure) — plus **FaaS** (serverless functions)
- [ ] **4 Deployment Models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community
- [ ] **4 Architecture Layers:** Sensing → Edge → Cloud → Application
- [ ] **Key Cloud Providers:** AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT, IBM Watson IoT
- [ ] **Data Rate Formula:** $R = N \times S \times f$ (sensors × sample size × sampling rate)
- [ ] **Storage Formula:** $S = R_{agg} \times t$ (aggregate rate × time)
- [ ] **Bandwidth Formula:** $B = R_{agg} \times (1 + O)$ (overhead included)
- [ ] **Latency Formula:** $L = L_{sens} + L_{trans} + L_{prop} + L_{proc} + L_{queue}$
- [ ] **Availability Formula:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$
- [ ] **Constants:** 86400 s/day, 8760 hr/year, 365 days/year, 8 bits = 1 byte
- [ ] **Nyquist Theorem:** Sample at ≥ 2× the highest signal frequency
- [ ] **Biomedical Use Cases:** RPM, EHR, PACS, AI diagnosis, clinical trials, genomic analytics
- [ ] **Protocols Used:** MQTT (lightweight IoT), CoAP, HTTP/HTTPS, AMQP
- [ ] **Security Standards:** TLS 1.3, AES-256, OAuth 2.0, HIPAA, GDPR
- [ ] **Cold Storage:** AWS S3 Glacier, Azure Archive — minutes to hours retrieval
- [ ] **Hot Storage:** Redis, ElastiCache — sub-10ms latency
- [ ] **FaaS Trigger:** Lambda functions invoked on MQTT message arrival
- [ ] **FHIR Standard:** HL7 FHIR for healthcare data exchange in cloud
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Python Web Application Framework
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Python Web Application Framework ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Python Web Application Framework** is a collection of pre-written, reusable software modules, libraries, and tools that provide a standardized structure for developing, deploying, and maintaining web-based applications using the Python programming language. In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT) and Biomedical Engineering**, these frameworks act as the *application layer* middleware that bridges sensor hardware, communication protocols, and end-user visualization dashboards. The two industry-standard frameworks specified in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus are: - **Django**: A high-level, batteries-included, Model-View-Template (MVT) framework designed for rapid development of secure, scalable, database-driven web applications. - **Flask**: A lightweight, micro-framework based on Werkzeug (WSGI toolkit) and Jinja2 (template engine), ideal for RESTful API development in IoT edge gateways. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Emphasis):** *A web framework (WF) or web application framework (WAF) is a software framework that is designed to support the development of web applications including web services, web resources, and web APIs. Frameworks provide a standard way to build and deploy web applications on the World Wide Web.* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"** > > Imagine you walk into a restaurant. The kitchen already has a stove, oven, knives, pans, and standardized recipes. The chef (developer) just needs to decide *what dish* (application) to make — they don't have to forge a knife or build a stove. > > - **Python** = the head chef who knows the language of cooking. > - **Web Framework (Flask/Django)** = the pre-equipped kitchen with standardized tools. > - **Routes / URLs** = the menu items customers can order. > - **Templates** = the plate presentation style. > - **Database** = the pantry storing ingredients (patient records, sensor data). > - **IoT Sensors** = the farm suppliers sending fresh ingredients (heart rate, SpO2, temperature) in real time. > > Without a framework, the chef would have to build the kitchen from scratch for every single dish — which is what raw CGI scripting felt like in the early 2000s. ### 1.3 Why Python Web Frameworks are Critical in IoT & Biomedical Systems | Layer | Role of Web Framework | |---|---| | **Sensor Layer** | ESP32 / Arduino sends raw JSON to a Flask API endpoint. | | **Gateway Layer** | Raspberry Pi running Flask acts as edge-computing gateway. | | **Cloud Layer** | Django deployed on AWS / Azure receives multi-tenant biomedical data. | | **Presentation Layer** | HTML5 + Chart.js rendered via Jinja2 templates shows real-time ECG. | | **Application Layer** | REST APIs enable mobile apps to query patient vitals. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric to Remember:** The WSGI (Web Server Gateway Interface) specification, defined in **PEP 3333**, is the universal contract between Python web servers and frameworks. Both Flask and Django are WSGI-compliant, ensuring portability across deployment environments. ]<]minimax[>[ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The WSGI Request-Response Lifecycle (The Core "Why") The entire architecture of Python web frameworks is governed by a single, elegant contract called the **WSGI Protocol**. Understanding this is the key to scoring 14-mark KTU questions. **Step-by-Step Operational Logic:** 1. **Browser / IoT Client** sends an HTTP request → `GET /patient/123 HTTP/1.1`. 2. **Web Server** (Gunicorn / uWSGI / Waitress) receives the raw socket data. 3. **WSGI Interface** invokes the framework's *callable* `application(environ, start_response)`, where `environ` is a Python dictionary containing all request metadata (headers, method, path, query string). 4. **Router** matches the URL path against registered routes via Werkzeug's URL map. 5. **View Function** executes the business logic (e.g., query database, format JSON). 6. **Template Engine** (Jinja2) renders dynamic HTML by substituting `{{ variable }}` placeholders. 7. **Response Object** is constructed with status code, headers, and body. 8. **WSGI Server** sends bytes back over the socket to the client. ### 2.2 Flask vs Django — KTU Comparison Matrix | Parameter | **Flask** (Micro-framework) | **Django** (Full-stack framework) | |---|---|---| | **Architecture Pattern** | WSGI + Mini-pattern | MVT (Model-View-Template) | | **Size** | Lightweight (~10k LoC) | Heavyweight (~200k LoC) | | **Database ORM** | None built-in (uses SQLAlchemy optionally) | Built-in powerful ORM | | **Admin Panel** | Not provided | Auto-generated admin interface | | **Template Engine** | Jinja2 | Django Template Language (DTL) | | **Best For (IoT Context)** | REST APIs, edge gateways, microservices | Hospital management systems, EMR portals | | **Learning Curve** | Low | Moderate to High | | **Boot Time** | ~0.1 s | ~1.5 s | | **Asynchronous Support** | Via Quart extension | Native in Django 4.1+ (ASGI) | | **Dependency Footprint** | Minimal | Large | ### 2.3 Key Formulas & Engineering Metrics (KTU Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > For a programming-framework module, the "formulas" are the **HTTP status codes, REST verbs, and standard engineering metrics** that KTU examiners love to test. | Symbol / Concept | Definition | Standard / Value | |---|---|---| | $n_{req}$ | Concurrent requests handled | Flask dev server: ~1 (single threaded) | | $T_{resp}$ | Response latency | Target: $T_{resp} < 200\text{ ms}$ for IoT dashboards | | $R_{throughput}$ | Requests per second | Flask + Gunicorn workers: $\approx 2000 \text{ req/s}$ | | $\rho_{mem}$ | Memory per worker | Flask: ~30 MB; Django: ~80 MB | | HTTP 200 | OK — Standard success | Universal success code | | HTTP 201 | Created — POST succeeded | Used after sensor registration | | HTTP 400 | Bad Request — Malformed JSON | Validation failed | | HTTP 404 | Not Found — Unknown URL | Sensor ID not registered | | HTTP 500 | Internal Server Error | Unhandled exception in view | ### 2.4 RESTful Design Constraints for Biomedical APIs > [!NOTE] > **Fielding's REST Constraints (Roy Fielding, 2000)** — the academic foundation examiners expect: > > 1. **Client-Server** separation > 2. **Statelessness** — each request contains all context > 3. **Cacheability** — responses declare cache headers > 4. **Uniform Interface** — standard HTTP verbs > 5. **Layered System** — load balancers, CDNs, proxies > 6. **Code-on-Demand** (optional) — JavaScript delivery **Standard HTTP Verbs (KTU must-know):** | Verb | Operation | Idempotent? | IoT Example | |---|---|---|---| | `GET` | Read | ✓ Yes | Fetch patient vitals | | `POST` | Create | ✗ No | Register a new wearable device | | `PUT` | Update / Replace | ✓ Yes | Update sensor calibration | | `PATCH` | Partial Update | ✗ No | Modify alarm threshold | | `DELETE` | Remove | ✓ Yes | Deregister expired sensor | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hospital Information Systems (HIS)**: Django-based EMR portals used in Apollo, AIIMS. - **Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM)**: Flask REST endpoints ingest data from Bluetooth-enabled pulse oximeters. - **IoT Dashboards**: Grafana + Flask for visualizing ventilator parameters in ICUs. - **Edge AI Inference**: Flask serves ONNX / TensorFlow Lite models for ECG anomaly detection at the edge. ]<]minimax[>[ ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Flask Application: IoT Biomedical Sensor Dashboard > [!NOTE] > This is the **complete, runnable analytical walkthrough** that a KTU examiner would expect for a 14-mark "design and implement" question. Every line is explained. **Step 1 — Project Structure (Analytical Blueprint):** ``` biomed_iot_app/ │ ├── app.py # Main Flask application ├── requirements.txt # Dependency manifest ├── templates/ │ ├── base.html # Master layout template │ ├── dashboard.html # Real-time vitals dashboard │ └── register.html # Device registration form │ ├── static/ │ ├── css/style.css # Styling │ └── js/chart.js # Live plotting │ └── models.py # SQLAlchemy database models ``` **Step 2 — Dependency Declaration (requirements.txt):** ```text Flask==3.0.0 Flask-SQLAlchemy==3.1.1 Flask-RESTful==0.3.10 flask-cors==4.0.0 requests==2.31.0 ``` **Step 3 — Database Model Definition (models.py):** ```python from datetime import datetime from flask_sqlalchemy import SQLAlchemy db = SQLAlchemy() # ORM instance bound to the Flask app class Patient(db.Model): """ Represents a patient registered in the biomedical IoT system. One-to-many relationship with VitalsReading. """ id = db.Column(db.Integer, primary_key=True) name = db.Column(db.String(80), nullable=False) age = db.Column(db.Integer, nullable=False) medical_id = db.Column(db.String(20), unique=True, nullable=False) created_at = db.Column(db.DateTime, default=datetime.utcnow) vitals = db.relationship('VitalsReading', backref='patient', lazy=True, cascade='all, delete-orphan') def to_dict(self): return { 'id': self.id, 'name': self.name, 'age': self.age, 'medical_id': self.medical_id } class VitalsReading(db.Model): """ Time-series vitals data sent by IoT biomedical sensors (e.g., MAX30100 for SpO2, AD8232 for ECG). """ id = db.Column(db.Integer, primary_key=True) patient_id = db.Column(db.Integer, db.ForeignKey('patient.id'), nullable=False) heart_rate_bpm = db.Column(db.Float, nullable=False) spo2_percent = db.Column(db.Float, nullable=False) body_temp_c = db.Column(db.Float, nullable=False) systolic_bp = db.Column(db.Integer, nullable=True) timestamp = db.Column(db.DateTime, default=datetime.utcnow) def to_dict(self): return { 'id': self.id, 'patient_id': self.patient_id, 'heart_rate_bpm': self.heart_rate_bpm, 'spo2_percent': self.spo2_percent, 'body_temp_c': self.body_temp_c, 'systolic_bp': self.systolic_bp, 'timestamp': self.timestamp.isoformat() } ``` **Logical Explanation of Each Construct:** - `db = SQLAlchemy()` → singleton ORM that maps Python classes to relational tables. - `Patient` and `VitalsReading` → entities in the biomedical domain. - `db.relationship` → establishes a **1-to-many** cardinality (one patient → many readings). - `to_dict()` → serializer that converts ORM objects into JSON for REST responses. **Step 4 — Main Flask Application (app.py):** ```python from flask import Flask, render_template, request, jsonify, abort from flask_restful import Api, Resource from flask_cors import CORS from models import db, Patient, VitalsReading # --- Application Factory Pattern --- app = Flask(__name__) app.config['SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI'] = 'sqlite:///biomed_iot.db' app.config['SQLALCHEMY_TRACK_MODIFICATIONS'] = False app.config['JSON_SORT_KEYS'] = False db.init_app(app) CORS(app) # enable cross-origin requests from mobile apps api = Api(app) # RESTful wrapper around Flask routes # ---------- HTML Route (Server-Side Rendered Dashboard) ---------- @app.route('/') def index(): """Home page listing all registered patients.""" patients = Patient.query.all() return render_template('dashboard.html', patients=patients) # ---------- REST API: Patient Resource ---------- class PatientListResource(Resource): def get(self): """Return all patients as JSON.""" patients = Patient.query.all() return [p.to_dict() for p in patients], 200 def post(self): """Register a new patient from JSON payload.""" data = request.get_json() if not data or not all(k in data for k in ('name', 'age', 'medical_id')): return {'error': 'Missing required fields'}, 400 new_patient = Patient( name=data['name'], age=data['age'], medical_id=data['medical_id'] ) db.session.add(new_patient) db.session.commit() return new_patient.to_dict(), 201 class PatientResource(Resource): def get(self, patient_id): patient = Patient.query.get_or_404(patient_id) return patient.to_dict(), 200 def delete(self, patient_id): patient = Patient.query.get_or_404(patient_id) db.session.delete(patient) db.session.commit() return '', 204 # ---------- REST API: Vitals Resource (IoT Ingestion Endpoint) ---------- class VitalsResource(Resource): def post(self, patient_id): """ IoT sensor endpoint. ESP32 / Arduino POSTs JSON like: { "heart_rate_bpm": 78.5, "spo2_percent": 98.0, "body_temp_c": 36.7, "systolic_bp": 120 } """ patient = Patient.query.get_or_404(patient_id) data = request.get_json() if not data or 'heart_rate_bpm' not in data: return {'error': 'Invalid vitals payload'}, 400 reading = VitalsReading( patient_id=patient.id, heart_rate_bpm=data['heart_rate_bpm'], spo2_percent=data.get('spo2_percent', 0.0), body_temp_c=data.get('body_temp_c', 0.0), systolic_bp=data.get('systolic_bp') ) db.session.add(reading) db.session.commit() return reading.to_dict(), 201 def get(self, patient_id): """Retrieve all vitals for a patient (latest first).""" Patient.query.get_or_404(patient_id) # ensure patient exists readings = (VitalsReading.query .filter_by(patient_id=patient_id) .order_by(VitalsReading.timestamp.desc()) .limit(100) .all()) return [r.to_dict() for r in readings], 200 # ---------- Register API endpoints ---------- api.add_resource(PatientListResource, '/api/patients') api.add_resource(PatientResource, '/api/patients/') api.add_resource(VitalsResource, '/api/patients//vitals') # ---------- Database Initialization ---------- @app.before_request def ensure_tables(): if not hasattr(app, '_db_initialized'): with app.app_context(): db.create_all() app._db_initialized = True if __name__ == '__main__': app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000, debug=True) ``` **Step 5 — Jinja2 Template (templates/dashboard.html):** ```html Biomed IoT Dashboard

Real-Time Patient Vitals

{% for patient in patients %}

{{ patient.name }} (ID: {{ patient.medical_id }})

Age: {{ patient.age }}

{% endfor %} ``` ### 3.2 IoT Sensor → Flask Server: ESP32 Pseudo-code Bridge ```python # This is the firmware code (MicroPython on ESP32) # that POSTs vitals to the Flask endpoint. import network, urequests, time def send_vitals(patient_id, hr, spo2, temp): url = "http://192.168.1.100:5000/api/patients/{}/vitals".format(patient_id) payload = { "heart_rate_bpm": hr, "spo2_percent": spo2, "body_temp_c": temp } try: response = urequests.post(url, json=payload) print("Status:", response.status_code) # expect 201 response.close() except Exception as e: print("Transmission failure:", e) # Example invocation send_vitals(patient_id=1, hr=78.5, spo2=98.0, temp=36.7) ``` ### 3.3 Deployment Architecture (Analytical Derivation) A Flask development server is **NOT** suitable for production. The standard deployment stack is: $$ \text{Browser} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Nginx (Reverse Proxy)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Gunicorn (WSGI Server)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Flask App} $$ **Step-by-Step Production Stack Construction:** 1. **Gunicorn** spawns $N$ worker processes, each handling requests in parallel: $$ N_{workers} = 2 \times C_{cores} + 1 $$ where $C_{cores}$ is the number of CPU cores. 2. **Nginx** terminates SSL (HTTPS), serves static files directly (bypassing Flask for performance), and load-balances across Gunicorn workers. 3. **Process Manager** (systemd / Supervisor) restarts crashed workers. 4. **Database** (PostgreSQL) replaces SQLite for concurrency safety. ]<]minimax[>[ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Web Framework Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CLIENT["Client Layer"] BR["Web Browser / Mobile App"] IOT["IoT Sensor Node (ESP32 / Arduino)"] end subgraph PROXY["Reverse Proxy Layer"] NX["Nginx - TLS Termination, Static Files"] end subgraph APP["Application Layer - Python Web Framework"] WSGI["Gunicorn / uWSGI - WSGI Server"] FLASK["Flask - Routing Engine"] ROUTE["URL Map - Werkzeug Router"] VIEW["View Functions - Business Logic"] JINJA["Jinja2 Template Engine"] end subgraph DATA["Data Layer"] ORM["SQLAlchemy ORM"] DB[("SQLite / PostgreSQL")] CACHE[("Redis Cache")] end BR -->|HTTPS Request| NX IOT -->|HTTP POST JSON| NX NX --> WSGI WSGI --> FLASK FLASK --> ROUTE ROUTE --> VIEW VIEW --> ORM VIEW --> JINJA ORM --> DB VIEW --> CACHE JINJA -->|Rendered HTML| WSGI WSGI --> NX NX -->|HTTP Response| BR ``` ### 4.2 WSGI Request-Response Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as Browser / IoT Device participant Server as Nginx participant WSGI as Gunicorn WSGI Server participant Flask as Flask App participant DB as Database Client->>Server: GET /api/patients/1/vitals Server->>WSGI: Forward HTTP request WSGI->>Flask: environ dict + start_response callable Flask->>Flask: Match URL to route via Werkzeug Flask->>DB: SQLAlchemy query DB-->>Flask: Return rows Flask->>Flask: Serialize to JSON via to_dict() Flask-->>WSGI: Return Response object WSGI-->>Server: HTTP/1.1 200 OK + JSON body Server-->>Client: Final HTTP response ``` ### 4.3 Flask Internal Component Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR REQ["Incoming Request"] --> WERK["Werkzeug WSGI Handler"] WERK --> RURL["URL Map Router"] RURL --> DEC1["before_request Decorator"] DEC1 --> VF["View Function"] VF --> DEC2["after_request Decorator"] VF --> SQLA["SQLAlchemy ORM"] SQLA --> DB[("Database")] VF --> JINJ["Jinja2 Template"] JINJ --> HTML["Rendered HTML String"] VF --> JSON["jsonify Function"] JSON --> RES["Response Object"] HTML --> RES RES --> WERK2["Werkzeug Response Serializer"] WERK2 --> OUT["HTTP Response to Client"] ``` ### 4.4 IoT Data Flow with Flask Backend ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["MAX30100 Pulse Oximeter"] --> MC1["ESP32 Microcontroller"] S2["DS18B20 Temperature Sensor"] --> MC1 S3["AD8232 ECG Sensor"] --> MC1 MC1 -->|WiFi / MQTT| FW["Flask REST API on Raspberry Pi"] FW --> SQL["SQLite Database"] FW --> DASH["Web Dashboard via Jinja2"] FW --> MOB["Mobile App via JSON"] DASH --> USER["Doctor / Caregiver"] MOB --> USER ``` ### 4.5 Django MVT Architecture (Comparison) ```mermaid flowchart TB URL["URL Dispatcher urls.py"] --> VIEW["View - views.py"] VIEW --> MODEL["Model - models.py"] MODEL --> ORM["Django ORM"] ORM --> DB[("PostgreSQL / MySQL")] VIEW --> TEMPLATE["Template - HTML with DTL"] TEMPLATE --> RESPONSE["HTTP Response"] VIEW --> RESPONSE RESPONSE --> CLIENT["Client Browser"] ``` ]<]minimax[>[ ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A — 3 Mark Questions** #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a Python web application framework. List any two popular Python web frameworks used in IoT applications.** **Model Answer:** A Python web application framework is a software library that provides a standardized foundation for building deployable web applications, including REST APIs and dynamic websites, using the Python language. It abstracts low-level HTTP handling, URL routing, database access, and template rendering so that developers can focus on application logic. Two popular Python web frameworks used in IoT applications are: 1. **Flask** — a lightweight micro-framework ideal for building RESTful APIs that ingest sensor data from edge devices. 2. **Django** — a full-stack, batteries-included framework suitable for hospital information systems and Electronic Medical Record (EMR) portals. > [!NOTE] > *Markers award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for naming the frameworks with brief justification.* --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between Flask and Django in terms of architecture and use case.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Flask | Django | |---|---|---| | **Architecture** | Micro-framework, WSGI-based, uses Jinja2 templating | Full-stack MVT (Model-View-Template) | | **Use Case** | Lightweight IoT gateways, microservices, REST APIs | Hospital management systems, EMR, multi-tenant apps | > [!WARNING] > *Common Mistake:* Students often write "Flask uses MVC" — this is wrong. Flask uses a minimal pattern sometimes called "MVC-like," but the route-function pair is the actual architecture. Django's MVT should not be confused with classical MVC.* --- ### **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the WSGI architecture with a neat block diagram. Discuss how Flask handles a request from an IoT sensor. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Design a Flask-based REST API to accept heart rate, SpO2, and body temperature data from an IoT-enabled wearable device, store it in an SQLite database, and provide endpoints to retrieve the latest and historical readings. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **WSGI (Web Server Gateway Interface)** is the specification defined in **PEP 3333** that standardizes communication between Python web servers and web applications/frameworks. It defines a simple callable interface: $$ \text{application(environ, start\_response)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Response} $$ where `environ` is a dictionary containing CGI-style request variables (REQUEST_METHOD, PATH_INFO, QUERY_STRING, HTTP headers) and `start_response` is a callable used to send HTTP status and headers. **Block Diagram of WSGI Flow:** ``` IoT Device → HTTP Request → Web Server (Gunicorn) ↓ WSGI Interface (PEP 3333) ↓ Flask App (callable) ↓ ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐ ↓ ↓ ↓ Router View Function Template Engine ↓ ↓ ↓ Response Object ↓ Gunicorn (HTTP bytes) ↓ IoT Device / Browser ``` **How Flask Handles a Request from an IoT Sensor:** 1. The ESP32 sensor sends an `HTTP POST` request with a JSON body containing vitals. 2. **Gunicorn** (or Flask's dev server) receives the raw TCP data, parses it, and constructs the `environ` dictionary. 3. Gunicorn invokes Flask's WSGI application callable. 4. **Werkzeug** (Flask's underlying WSGI library) constructs the `Request` object, parsing headers, query string, and JSON body. 5. The **URL Map** matches the request path `/api/patients/1/vitals` to the registered `VitalsResource.post()` method. 6. The view function executes, validates the JSON, creates a `VitalsReading` ORM object, and commits to SQLite. 7. The view returns a serialized JSON dictionary with HTTP status **201 Created**. 8. Werkzeug constructs a `Response` object, which Gunicorn serializes into bytes and sends back over the socket. --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Code Implementation:** ```python from flask import Flask, request, jsonify from flask_sqlalchemy import SQLAlchemy from datetime import datetime app = Flask(__name__) app.config['SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI'] = 'sqlite:///wearable.db' app.config['SQLALCHEMY_TRACK_MODIFICATIONS'] = False db = SQLAlchemy(app) class WearableReading(db.Model): id = db.Column(db.Integer, primary_key=True) device_id = db.Column(db.String(50), nullable=False) heart_rate_bpm = db.Column(db.Float, nullable=False) spo2_percent = db.Column(db.Float, nullable=False) body_temp_c = db.Column(db.Float, nullable=False) timestamp = db.Column(db.DateTime, default=datetime.utcnow) def to_dict(self): return { 'id': self.id, 'device_id': self.device_id, 'heart_rate_bpm': self.heart_rate_bpm, 'spo2_percent': self.spo2_percent, 'body_temp_c': self.body_temp_c, 'timestamp': self.timestamp.isoformat() } with app.app_context(): db.create_all() # Endpoint 1: Ingest new reading @app.route('/api/wearable/data', methods=['POST']) def ingest_data(): data = request.get_json() if not data or not all(k in data for k in ('device_id', 'heart_rate_bpm', 'spo2_percent', 'body_temp_c')): return jsonify({'error': 'Invalid payload'}), 400 reading = WearableReading( device_id=data['device_id'], heart_rate_bpm=data['heart_rate_bpm'], spo2_percent=data['spo2_percent'], body_temp_c=data['body_temp_c'] ) db.session.add(reading) db.session.commit() return jsonify(reading.to_dict()), 201 # Endpoint 2: Retrieve latest reading for a device @app.route('/api/wearable/latest/', methods=['GET']) def get_latest(device_id): reading = (WearableReading.query .filter_by(device_id=device_id) .order_by(WearableReading.timestamp.desc()) .first()) if not reading: return jsonify({'error': 'No data found'}), 404 return jsonify(reading.to_dict()), 200 # Endpoint 3: Retrieve historical readings @app.route('/api/wearable/history/', methods=['GET']) def get_history(device_id): limit = int(request.args.get('limit', 100)) readings = (WearableReading.query .filter_by(device_id=device_id) .order_by(WearableReading.timestamp.desc()) .limit(limit) .all()) return jsonify([r.to_dict() for r in readings]), 200 if __name__ == '__main__': app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000, debug=True) ``` **Explanation of Design Choices:** - `POST /api/wearable/data` → ingestion endpoint for IoT devices (HTTP 201 on success). - `GET /api/wearable/latest/` → returns the most recent reading (uses `ORDER BY ... DESC LIMIT 1`). - `GET /api/wearable/history/` → returns up to `limit` historical entries (default 100, configurable via query string). - SQLite was chosen for simplicity; in production this would be PostgreSQL or TimescaleDB for time-series efficiency. - `jsonify()` ensures `Content-Type: application/json` header is correctly set. **Test cURL Commands:** ```bash # 1. Ingest a reading curl -X POST http://localhost:5000/api/wearable/data \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"device_id":"ESP32-001","heart_rate_bpm":78.5, "spo2_percent":98.0,"body_temp_c":36.7}' # 2. Fetch latest curl http://localhost:5000/api/wearable/latest/ESP32-001 # 3. Fetch history curl "http://localhost:5000/api/wearable/history/ESP32-001?limit=50" ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > - **Missing import statements** in code → 2 marks deducted. > - Not specifying **HTTP status codes** (201 vs 200) → lose 1 mark. > - Writing only pseudocode without correct Flask decorators (`@app.route`) → lose 2 marks. > - Forgetting to call `db.create_all()` → application crashes on first run, lose 1 mark. > - Using `app.run()` in production → conceptual mistake, lose 1 mark. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the MVC/MVT architecture of Django. Draw the block diagram showing the request-response lifecycle. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare Flask and Django in detail. Design a Django project structure for a hospital appointment booking system with at least two models. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** Django follows the **Model-View-Template (MVT)** architectural pattern, which is a variant of the classical MVC pattern. | MVT Component | Role | Equivalent in MVC | |---|---|---| | **Model** | Defines data schema; handles database access via Django ORM | Model | | **View** | Contains business logic; decides what data to send to the template | Controller | | **Template** | Handles presentation; HTML files with Django Template Language (DTL) | View | **Request-Response Lifecycle (Block Diagram Representation):** ``` Browser → URL Dispatcher (urls.py) ↓ Middleware Stack ↓ View Function (views.py) ↙ ↘ Model (ORM) Template (HTML) ↓ ↓ Database Rendered HTML ↓ ↓ └────→ View ←──┘ ↓ Middleware (response) ↓ Browser ``` **Detailed Flow:** 1. User enters URL → Django's URL dispatcher (`urls.py`) matches it to a view function. 2. Middleware chain executes (authentication, CSRF check, session handling). 3. View function is called; it may query the Model layer. 4. Model uses Django ORM to translate Python code into SQL and fetch data. 5. View passes data to the Template for rendering. 6. Template generates the final HTML response. 7. Response flows back through middleware (compression, caching headers) to the user. --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Comparison Table: Flask vs Django** *(students may reproduce this in 3-4 lines for partial credit)* - **Philosophy**: Flask is minimal and unopinionated; Django is "batteries-included" and opinionated. - **Database**: Flask requires third-party ORMs (SQLAlchemy); Django ships with its own ORM. - **Admin**: Django provides a free auto-generated admin; Flask requires manual coding or Flask-Admin. - **Security**: Django includes built-in CSRF, XSS, and SQL injection protection; Flask requires manual setup. - **Performance**: Flask has lower memory footprint and faster boot time, ideal for IoT edge gateways. **Django Project Structure for Hospital Appointment System:** ``` hospital_booking/ │ ├── manage.py ├── hospital_booking/ # Project configuration │ ├── __init__.py │ ├── settings.py │ ├── urls.py │ ├── asgi.py │ └── wsgi.py │ ├── appointments/ # Django app │ ├── __init__.py │ ├── admin.py │ ├── apps.py │ ├── models.py │ ├── views.py │ ├── urls.py │ ├── forms.py │ └── migrations/ │ └── templates/ └── appointments/ ├── base.html ├── doctor_list.html └── book_appointment.html ``` **Model 1 — `Doctor` (models.py):** ```python from django.db import models class Doctor(models.Model): SPECIALIZATIONS = [ ('CARD', 'Cardiology'), ('NEURO', 'Neurology'), ('ORTHO', 'Orthopedics'), ('GENM', 'General Medicine'), ] name = models.CharField(max_length=100) specialization = models.CharField(max_length=10, choices=SPECIALIZATIONS) contact_email = models.EmailField(unique=True) consultation_fee = models.DecimalField(max_digits=8, decimal_places=2) available = models.BooleanField(default=True) def __str__(self): return f"Dr. {self.name} - {self.get_specialization_display()}" ``` **Model 2 — `Appointment` (models.py):** ```python class Appointment(models.Model): STATUS_CHOICES = [ ('PEND', 'Pending'), ('CONF', 'Confirmed'), ('CANC', 'Cancelled'), ('COMP', 'Completed'), ] patient_name = models.CharField(max_length=100) patient_age = models.PositiveIntegerField() doctor = models.ForeignKey(Doctor, on_delete=models.CASCADE, related_name='appointments') appointment_date = models.DateTimeField() status = models.CharField(max_length=4, choices=STATUS_CHOICES, default='PEND') symptoms = models.TextField(blank=True) def __str__(self): return f"{self.patient_name} → {self.doctor.name} on {self.appointment_date}" ``` **Key Django ORM Concepts Illustrated:** - `models.ForeignKey(Doctor, on_delete=models.CASCADE)` → establishes a many-to-one relationship (one doctor → many appointments). `CASCADE` ensures appointments are deleted if the doctor is removed. - `choices` parameter → enforces enum-like constraints at the database and form level. - `__str__()` → defines the string representation shown in Django admin. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > - Writing Flask code when the question asks for Django → **0 marks** for that sub-part. > - Not showing the `manage.py startproject` workflow → lose 1 mark. > - Forgetting to register models in `admin.py` → lose 1 mark. > - Using `models.IntegerField` for age without `PositiveIntegerField` validation → minor deduction. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:** - **WSGI (PEP 3333)** is the foundational contract — know the signature `application(environ, start_response)`. - **Flask = micro-framework**; **Django = full-stack MVT framework**. - Flask routing is handled by **Werkzeug's URL Map**; templating by **Jinja2**. - Django routing is in `urls.py`; templating in DTL; ORM built-in. - HTTP status codes you MUST know cold: **200, 201, 204, 400, 404, 500**. - REST verbs: **GET (read/idempotent), POST (create), PUT (update/idempotent), DELETE (remove/idempotent), PATCH (partial update)**. - Always use `request.get_json()` to parse JSON in Flask; use `jsonify()` to return JSON. - For IoT: ESP32/Arduino POSTs sensor data → Flask endpoint → SQLite/PostgreSQL → Dashboard via Jinja2. - Production deployment stack: **Nginx + Gunicorn + Flask**; never use Flask dev server in production. - Database choice: **SQLite** for prototyping; **PostgreSQL** or **TimescaleDB** for production time-series. - `db.relationship` (Flask-SQLAlchemy) and `models.ForeignKey` (Django) establish table relationships. - `app.run(debug=True)` is for **development only** — exposes the Werkzeug debugger and is a security risk in production. - `CORS` must be enabled via `flask-cors` for cross-origin mobile app access. - Decorator order matters: `@app.route` is the entry point; `@app.before_request` and `@app.after_request` are middleware-style hooks. - Always validate input data with explicit checks before database insertion; return **400 Bad Request** on failure. - The 14-mark questions typically have **(a) theory + diagram (7 marks)** and **(b) design + code (7 marks)** structure. --- *End of Module 4 Notes — Python Web Application Framework* ]<]minimax[>[

topics.title: Designing a RESTful Web API
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Designing a RESTful Web API

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **RESTful Web API** (Representational State Transfer Application Programming Interface) is a software architectural style, defined by **Dr. Roy Fielding** in his 2000 doctoral dissertation, that defines a set of constraints to be used for creating web services. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEEBT831 – IoT & Biomedical Applications)**, a RESTful Web API serves as the standardized communication middleware that allows heterogeneous IoT devices (wearable ECG sensors, smart insulin pumps, remote patient monitors) to exchange structured data with cloud platforms, mobile applications, and hospital information systems over the HTTP/HTTPS protocol.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** RESTful APIs are treated as the *de-facto* interoperability backbone of the modern IoT ecosystem, especially for biomedical telemetry where data integrity, low latency, and statelessness are mandatory.

The term **"Representational State Transfer"** literally means that the *state* of a resource (e.g., a patient's heart rate) is transferred between client and server in the form of a *representation* (typically a **JSON** or **XML** document), rather than the server transmitting its actual internal state.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Hospital Waiter"

Imagine a smart hospital where a patient (client) is too ill to walk to the pharmacy (server's database). A **Waiter (REST API)** acts as a standardized intermediary:

1. The patient gives the waiter a **written order (HTTP Request)** — a specific format with the medicine name, quantity, and patient ID.
2. The waiter carries this **standardized slip** to the pharmacy kitchen (server). The waiter himself does not know *how* the medicine is prepared — he just delivers and returns responses.
3. The pharmacy returns the medicine in a **sealed container (JSON Response)** with a **status label (HTTP Status Code)** like "200 OK – Medicine Delivered" or "404 – Medicine Not Found".
4. The patient **never visits the kitchen directly**, ensuring hygiene (separation of concerns) and the waiter remembers **nothing** about previous orders (statelessness).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Just as a waiter enforces a *uniform contract* (the menu, the order slip), a RESTful API enforces a *uniform interface* between IoT biomedical devices and backend systems.

## 1.3 Core Distinguishing Features

| Feature | Description | Biomedical/IoT Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Statelessness** | Each request is independent; no client context stored on server | Allows thousands of wearable sensors to scale seamlessly |
| **Resource-Based** | Everything is a *resource* identified by a **URI** | Patient ID `P-1023`, Device ID `D-77` |
| **Uniform Interface** | Standardized HTTP verbs (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) | Same API works for ECG, BP, and SpO₂ devices |
| **Cacheability** | Responses can declare themselves cacheable | Reduces bandwidth for repeated vitals monitoring |
| **Layered System** | Client does not know if talking to server or proxy | Enables gateway-edge-cloud hierarchies |
| **Code on Demand** *(optional)* | Server can send executable code (e.g., JavaScript) | Rare in medical IoT due to security |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** The design of such an API involves **Resource Modeling, URI Naming, HTTP Method Mapping, Status Code Selection, Payload Schema Design, Versioning, and Security** — all covered in the sections that follow.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six REST Architectural Constraints (Fielding's Model)

The rigor of a RESTful design is judged by how faithfully it implements the following six constraints. Each has direct implications for IoT biomedical systems.

### 2.1.1 Client–Server Separation
- The **client** (e.g., doctor's mobile app) is concerned only with the *User Interface* and the *state of the user*.
- The **server** (e.g., hospital cloud) is concerned only with *data storage, security, and business logic*.
- **Why for IoT?** A wearable pulse oximeter (sensor node) and a cardiologist's tablet (client) can evolve independently as long as the API contract remains stable.

### 2.1.2 Statelessness
- Every request from the client must contain **all** the information necessary for the server to fulfill it.
- The server **does not store session state** between requests.
- **Engineering Consequence:** For an API serving 1 million IoT devices, the server does not need 1 million session objects, drastically reducing memory footprint.

### 2.1.3 Cacheability
- Responses must define themselves as **cacheable** or **non-cacheable** to prevent clients from reusing stale or inappropriate data.
- **Biomedical Caution:** A patient's *real-time ECG* must be marked **non-cacheable**, while *static hospital metadata* can be cached aggressively.

### 2.1.4 Uniform Interface
This is the most critical constraint and is decomposed into four sub-constraints:
- **Identification of Resources** — URIs
- **Manipulation of Resources through Representations** — JSON/XML
- **Self-Descriptive Messages** — HTTP verbs + Media types
- **Hypermedia as the Engine of Application State (HATEOAS)** — Links inside responses

### 2.1.5 Layered System
- The architecture is composed of hierarchical layers (e.g., *Device → Edge Gateway → Cloud → Application*).
- A client cannot tell whether it is connected to the end server or an intermediary.

### 2.1.6 Code on Demand *(Optional)*
- Servers can temporarily extend client functionality by transferring executable code.
- Seldom used in safety-critical biomedical APIs.

## 2.2 The Richardson Maturity Model (RMM)

Leonard Richardson proposed a grading system (Levels 0–3) to evaluate the "RESTfulness" of an API. This is a **favourite KTU question**.

| Level | Name | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | The Swamp of POX | Single URI, single HTTP verb (usually POST), uses RPC-style tunneling | `POST /api` with action name in body |
| **1** | Resources | Multiple URIs, one per resource, but single HTTP verb | `POST /patients`, `POST /doctors` |
| **2** | HTTP Verbs | Multiple URIs + appropriate HTTP verbs | `GET /patients/1023`, `DELETE /patients/1024` |
| **3** | Hypermedia Controls (HATEOAS) | Responses include hypermedia links guiding the client on next actions | Response contains `"self":"/vitals/hr/1023"` |

> [!NOTE]
> A truly *RESTful* API is considered to operate at **Level 3**. Most production IoT biomedical APIs operate at **Level 2** because HATEOAS adds payload overhead that is undesirable on bandwidth-constrained BLE/LoRa devices.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every quantitative relationship, design rule, and equation that a KTU 2024 student must memorize for this topic.

| # | Concept / Formula | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | API Throughput | $$\text{Throughput} = \frac{N_{\text{requests}}}{T_{\text{window}}}$$ | requests per second (RPS) |
| 2 | Average Response Time (Little's Law variant) | $$R = \frac{N_{\text{concurrent}} - 1}{\lambda}$$ | $R$ = response time (s), $\lambda$ = arrival rate (req/s) |
| 3 | Pagination — Page Size Limit | $$1 \leq S_{\text{page}} \leq S_{\max}$$ | $S_{\max}$ usually **100** for biomedical APIs |
| 4 | Cache Hit Ratio | $$H = \frac{N_{\text{hits}}}{N_{\text{hits}} + N_{\text{misses}}}$$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le H \le 1$ |
| 5 | Cache Effectiveness | $$T_{\text{eff}} = H \cdot T_{\text{cached}} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{\text{origin}}$$ | $T$ in seconds |
| 6 | API Versioning | $$\text{URI} = \text{Base} + \text{/v} n + \text{/resource}$$ | $n$ is a positive integer |
| 7 | Bandwidth per Telemetry Packet | $$B = S_{\text{payload}} \times f_s \times N_{\text{channels}}$$ | Bytes/sec — for ECG: $S \approx 250\,\text{B}, f_s = 1\,\text{Hz}$ |
| 8 | Reliability (5-Nines) | $$\text{Availability} = 1 - \frac{\text{Downtime}}{\text{Total Time}}$$ | Medical APIs target $\ge 0.99999$ |
| 9 | HTTP Status Code Range | $$\text{Category} = \lfloor \frac{\text{Code}}{100} \rfloor$$ | 1=Info, 2=Success, 3=Redirect, 4=Client Err, 5=Server Err |
| 10 | Richardson Maturity Level | $$L \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$$ | Higher level $\Rightarrow$ more RESTful |
| 11 | Stateless Memory Bound | $$M_{\text{session}} = 0 \text{ bytes (per request)}$$ | Sessions stored client-side (e.g., JWT) |
| 12 | URI Hierarchy Depth | $$D \leq 3 \text{ (recommended)}$$ | Avoid deep nesting `a/b/c/d/e/f` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "design a REST API", you must explicitly state which **HTTP verb**, which **status code**, and which **payload schema** (JSON/XML) is used. Marks are awarded per decision, not just for the URI.

## 2.4 The HTTP Method Semantics — The Heart of REST

The HTTP protocol (RFC 9110) defines methods that map directly to **CRUD** operations on resources. The mapping below is the **single most tested KTU concept** in this module.

| HTTP Method | CRUD Op | Safe? | Idempotent? | Typical Use in Biomedical IoT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GET** | Read | Yes | Yes | Fetch latest SpO₂ reading |
| **POST** | Create | No | No | Register a new patient/device |
| **PUT** | Replace | No | Yes | Update full patient profile |
| **PATCH** | Partial Update | No | No | Update only heart rate threshold |
| **DELETE** | Remove | No | Yes | Decommission a faulty sensor |
| **HEAD** | Metadata | Yes | Yes | Check if vitals resource exists |
| **OPTIONS** | Discovery | Yes | Yes | Enumerate allowed methods (CORS) |

**Definitions:**
- **Safe:** Does not modify server state.
- **Idempotent:** Multiple identical requests produce the same result.

## 2.5 HTTP Status Codes — KTU High-Yield List

| Code | Category | Meaning | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **200** | 2xx Success | OK — Standard success | `GET /patients/1023` returned |
| **201** | 2xx Success | Created — Resource instantiated | `POST /devices` registered new sensor |
| **204** | 2xx Success | No Content — Successful but empty | `DELETE /alerts/45` removed |
| **301** | 3xx Redirect | Moved Permanently | API moved to `/v2/` |
| **400** | 4xx Client Err | Bad Request | Missing `patientId` field |
| **401** | 4xx Client Err | Unauthorized | Invalid JWT token |
| **403** | 4xx Client Err | Forbidden | Doctor accessing another ward |
| **404** | 4xx Client Err | Not Found | `patientId=9999` does not exist |
| **409** | 4xx Client Err | Conflict | Duplicate device registration |
| **422** | 4xx Client Err | Unprocessable Entity | BP value = 9999 mmHg (invalid range) |
| **429** | 4xx Client Err | Too Many Requests | Rate limit exceeded by sensor |
| **500** | 5xx Server Err | Internal Server Error | Database connection lost |
| **503** | 5xx Server Err | Service Unavailable | Maintenance window |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** "**2 Success, 3 Redirect, 4 Client (you messed up), 5 Server (we messed up)**".

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The 7-Step RESTful API Design Methodology (KTU Board Pattern)

When a KTU question asks *"Design a RESTful API for <system>"*, follow this exact 7-step procedure. Each step is a guaranteed mark-bearing decision.

### Step 1 — Identify the *Nouns* (Resources)
Strip the system description of all verbs. The remaining nouns are your **resources**.
- For *"IoT-enabled ICU Monitoring System"*, the resources are: `Patient`, `Device`, `Vitals`, `Alert`, `Doctor`, `Ward`.
- **Biomedical Specific:** Always model `Vitals` as a sub-resource of `Patient` because vitals have no meaning without a patient.

### Step 2 — Define the *URI Hierarchy*
Apply the formula:
$$\text{URI} = \text{Base} \oplus \text{/v} n \oplus \text{/Collection} \oplus \text{/id} \oplus \text{/SubCollection} \oplus \text{/id}$$

- **Base**: e.g., `https://api.hospitaliot.in`
- **Version**: e.g., `/v1`
- **Collection**: Plural noun (e.g., `/patients`)
- **Identifier**: Unique ID (e.g., `/P-1023`)
- **Sub-Collection**: Related child resource (e.g., `/vitals`)

**Worked Biomedical Example — Patient Vitals URI:**

$$\text{URI}_{\text{vitals}} = \underbrace{\text{https://api.hospitaliot.in}}_{\text{Base}} \oplus \underbrace{\text{/v1}}_{\text{Version}} \oplus \underbrace{\text{/patients}}_{\text{Collection}} \oplus \underbrace{\text{/P-1023}}_{\text{ID}} \oplus \underbrace{\text{/vitals}}_{\text{SubCollection}}$$

$$\therefore \text{URI}_{\text{vitals}} = \texttt{https://api.hospitaliot.in/v1/patients/P-1023/vitals}$$

### Step 3 — Map HTTP Verbs to Resource Actions

| URI Pattern | GET | POST | PUT | DELETE |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `/patients` | List all patients | Create new patient | Bulk replace (rare) | Delete all (dangerous) |
| `/patients/{id}` | Fetch one patient | N/A (405) | Update patient | Delete patient |
| `/patients/{id}/vitals` | List all vitals | Append new reading | Replace all | Clear history |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Rule:** A `POST` to `/patients/{id}` is invalid; it should return **405 Method Not Allowed**. Always assert that only appropriate verbs are mapped.

### Step 4 — Design the JSON Payload Schema (Representation)
The representation is what travels on the wire. For a vitals reading:

```json
{
  "patientId": "P-1023",
  "timestamp": "2026-01-25T10:30:00Z",
  "deviceId": "D-ECG-77",
  "metrics": {
    "heartRate": { "value": 78, "unit": "bpm" },
    "spo2":      { "value": 98, "unit": "%"  },
    "temperature":{ "value": 36.8,"unit": "C"  }
  },
  "status": "NORMAL"
}
```

**Derivation Logic for the Schema:**

- **Top-level identifiers** (`patientId`, `deviceId`, `timestamp`) ensure the server can route the data to the correct patient's record even in a stateless manner. This satisfies the **Self-Descriptive Message** constraint.
- **Nested `metrics` object** groups related measurements, allowing extensibility (new metrics like `bloodGlucose` can be added without breaking the schema).
- **Unit field** inside each metric follows the **FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources)** standard — a critical biomedical interoperability requirement.

### Step 5 — Define Error Representation
A *uniform* error format is mandatory for client handling:

```json
{
  "error": {
    "code": 422,
    "type": "UnprocessableEntity",
    "message": "Heart rate value 999 is outside physiological range (30-220 bpm).",
    "traceId": "req-8f3a2b-2026-01-25"
  }
}
```

### Step 6 — Select the Security Mechanism

For IoT biomedical APIs, the recommended security triad is:

1. **Authentication** — OAuth 2.0 + **JWT (JSON Web Token)** for users; **API Keys** for devices.
2. **Authorization** — Role-Based Access Control (RBAC): e.g., `nurse:read`, `doctor:read+write`, `device:write-only`.
3. **Transport Security** — **TLS 1.3** mandatory; HTTP redirected to HTTPS via **301 Moved Permanently**.

### Step 7 — Plan Versioning and Deprecation
Embed version in the URI (`/v1/`, `/v2/`). Never break a deployed version. Announce deprecation using the HTTP header:

$$\text{Deprecation Header: } \texttt{Deprecation: true; sunset="2027-01-01"}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — HATEOAS Response Derivation

A **Level 3 (HATEOAS)** response for `GET /patients/P-1023/vitals` is derived as follows:

```json
{
  "patientId": "P-1023",
  "latestReading": {
    "heartRate": 78,
    "spo2": 98
  },
  "_links": {
    "self":  { "href": "/v1/patients/P-1023/vitals" },
    "history":{ "href": "/v1/patients/P-1023/vitals?from=2026-01-01" },
    "alerts":  { "href": "/v1/patients/P-1023/alerts" },
    "doctor":  { "href": "/v1/doctors/D-201" }
  }
}
```

**Analytical Justification:**
- The client (mobile app) does not need hardcoded URIs; it **discovers** valid next actions from the `_links` object.
- This is mathematically equivalent to an **adjacency list** in graph theory, where the API response is a node and `_links` are directed edges.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Throughput and Latency Estimation

**Problem (KTU-style):** A wearable ECG sensor sends 1 reading per second to a REST API. Each payload is **256 bytes**. The hospital cloud receives readings from **5000 patients**. The server processes each request in **15 ms**. Compute (a) the total bandwidth, and (b) whether the server can keep up.

### Part (a) — Total Bandwidth

The number of requests per second (arrival rate) is:
$$\lambda = 5000 \text{ sensors} \times 1 \text{ req/s} = 5000 \text{ req/s}$$

Total payload per second:
$$B = \lambda \times S_{\text{payload}}$$

$$B = 5000 \times 256 \text{ bytes} = 1{,}280{,}000 \text{ bytes/s}$$

Converting to megabits per second (divide by $10^6$ for MB, then multiply by 8 for bits):
$$B = \frac{1{,}280{,}000 \times 8}{10^6} = 10.24 \text{ Mbps}$$

### Part (b) — Server Capacity Check

Using Little's Law variant for response time:
$$R = \frac{N_{\text{concurrent}}}{\lambda} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_{\text{concurrent}} = R \times \lambda$$

Given $R = 15\,\text{ms} = 0.015\,\text{s}$:
$$N_{\text{concurrent}} = 0.015 \times 5000 = 75 \text{ simultaneous requests}$$

**Conclusion:** A single server with multi-threading can handle this with 75 concurrent threads, indicating the design is feasible. If $\lambda$ were to grow to $20{,}000$ req/s, $N_{\text{concurrent}}$ would balloon to $300$, demanding a load balancer and horizontal scaling.

## 3.4 Comprehensive KTU Design Checklist Matrix

The following matrix condenses every design decision a KTU 14-mark answer must address. Use it as a self-evaluation rubric.

| Design Dimension | Mandatory Decision | KTU Mark Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **Resource Identification** | List all *nouns* | 2 marks |
| **URI Design** | Hierarchical, plural, lowercase | 2 marks |
| **HTTP Verb Mapping** | Table of URI × Verb | 3 marks |
| **Status Code Selection** | Justified per scenario | 2 marks |
| **JSON Schema** | Sample request & response | 3 marks |
| **Security** | Auth + Authz + TLS | 1 mark |
| **Versioning** | URI-based | 1 mark |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level RESTful IoT Biomedical Architecture

The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of how a RESTful Web API sits between biomedical IoT devices and clinical applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph fieldLayer["Field Layer - IoT Devices"]
        sensorA["ECG Sensor - D-77"]
        sensorB["SpO2 Sensor - D-78"]
        sensorC["BP Cuff - D-79"]
    end

    subgraph gatewayLayer["Edge Gateway Layer"]
        edgeNode1["Edge Aggregator - Raspberry Pi"]
    end

    subgraph apiLayer["REST API Layer - Cloud"]
        apiAuth["Auth Module - JWT/OAuth"]
        apiRouter["URI Router - /v1/patients"]
        apiLogic["Business Logic - Vitals Engine"]
        apiDB[("Database - Patient Records")]
    end

    subgraph clientLayer["Client Applications"]
        clientA["Doctor Mobile App"]
        clientB["Nurse Dashboard"]
        clientC["Hospital EHR System"]
    end

    sensorA -->|HTTPS POST JSON| edgeNode1
    sensorB -->|HTTPS POST JSON| edgeNode1
    sensorC -->|HTTPS POST JSON| edgeNode1
    edgeNode1 -->|REST over TLS 1.3| apiAuth
    apiAuth --> apiRouter
    apiRouter --> apiLogic
    apiLogic <-->|SQL/NoSQL| apiDB
    apiLogic -->|HTTPS GET JSON| clientA
    apiLogic -->|HTTPS GET JSON| clientB
    apiLogic -->|HTTPS GET JSON| clientC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Devices push vitals → Edge aggregates → REST API (stateless, with auth) → Database ↔ Clinical clients. The REST API is the *single integration point*.

## 4.2 REST API Request–Response Lifecycle (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

This diagram shows the **chronological flow** of a single `GET /v1/patients/P-1023/vitals` request, satisfying the KTU 2024 expectation of explaining *how* the API works in a hospital context.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant Dev as Wearable Device
    participant GW as Edge Gateway
    participant API as REST API Server
    participant DB as Database
    participant App as Doctor Mobile App

    Note over Dev,App: Scenario: Doctor requests latest vitals for Patient P-1023

    App->>API: GET /v1/patients/P-1023/vitals<br/>Authorization: Bearer JWT
    API->>API: 1. Validate JWT signature
    API->>API: 2. Resolve URI to handler
    API->>DB: 3. Query SELECT * FROM vitals<br/>WHERE patientId='P-1023'<br/>ORDER BY ts DESC LIMIT 1
    DB-->>API: 4. Return row with latest reading
    API->>API: 5. Serialize to JSON representation
    API-->>App: 6. 200 OK<br/>Content-Type: application/json<br/>Cache-Control: no-cache
    App->>App: 7. Render vitals on UI

    Note over Dev,App: Asynchronous Telemetry Upload
    Dev->>GW: POST /v1/devices/D-77/telemetry<br/>Body: JSON vitals
    GW->>API: POST /v1/patients/P-1023/vitals
    API->>DB: INSERT INTO vitals
    API-->>GW: 201 Created<br/>Location: /v1/patients/P-1023/vitals/8882
```

## 4.3 Resource Relationship Diagram (Mermaid Graph)

The following diagram visualizes the **URI hierarchy** as a graph, helping students internalize parent–child relationships between biomedical resources.

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["/v1"] --> patients["/patients"]
    root --> devices["/devices"]
    root --> doctors["/doctors"]
    root --> wards["/wards"]

    patients --> patient1023["/patients/P-1023"]
    patient1023 --> vitals1023["/vitals"]
    patient1023 --> alerts1023["/alerts"]
    patient1023 --> history1023["/history"]

    devices --> device77["/devices/D-77"]
    device77 --> telemetry77["/telemetry"]
    device77 --> config77["/config"]

    vitals1023 --> vitalId["/vitals/V-9001"]
```

**Key Insight from the Graph:**
- `vitals` and `alerts` are *nested resources* under a specific patient, which is the **standard FHIR-aligned design**.
- Devices, doctors, and wards are *sibling top-level collections* — they are independent roots, not children of a patient.

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Selecting the Correct HTTP Verb (Mermaid)

Use this flowchart during exam revision to recall which verb to assign in a design problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Need to act on a resource?"] --> q1{"Client wants to<br/>READ data?"}
    q1 -- "Yes" --> get["Use GET<br/>Safe and Idempotent"]
    q1 -- "No" --> q2{"Creating a NEW<br/>resource with<br/>server-generated ID?"}
    q2 -- "Yes" --> post["Use POST<br/>Returns 201 Created"]
    q2 -- "No" --> q3{"Replacing the<br/>ENTIRE resource?"}
    q3 -- "Yes" --> put["Use PUT<br/>Idempotent"]
    q3 -- "No" --> q4{"Updating a FEW<br/>fields of the<br/>resource?"}
    q4 -- "Yes" --> patch["Use PATCH"]
    q4 -- "No" --> q5{"Removing the<br/>resource?"}
    q5 -- "Yes" --> del["Use DELETE<br/>Returns 204 No Content"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Examiners often give a *scenario* and ask *"Which HTTP verb should be used?"* The flowchart above directly maps to the answer.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, OEEBT831, Q1(a)]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define the term "RESTful Web API". List any two REST architectural constraints.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
A **RESTful Web API** is an application interface that conforms to the architectural constraints of **Representational State Transfer (REST)**, originally defined by **Roy Fielding (2000)**. It uses standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and stateless communication to allow clients and servers to exchange representations of resources (typically in JSON or XML).

**Two REST Constraints (any two from the six):**
1. **Statelessness** — Each client request must contain all information needed; the server stores no session state.
2. **Client–Server Separation** — The user interface concerns (client) and data storage concerns (server) are independent, allowing them to evolve separately.

*(Mentioning Uniform Interface and Cacheability are equally valid.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, OEEBT831, Q1(b)]**

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q: What is HATEOAS? How does it relate to the Richardson Maturity Model?**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
**HATEOAS (Hypermedia as the Engine of Application State)** is a constraint of REST in which the server embeds **hypermedia links** within its responses. These links inform the client about the *valid next actions* and the *related resources*, removing the need for the client to hardcode URIs.

In the **Richardson Maturity Model (RMM)**, HATEOAS represents the **highest level — Level 3**. An API at:
- **Level 0** uses a single URI and single verb (RPC tunneling),
- **Level 1** introduces multiple resources (URIs),
- **Level 2** adds proper HTTP verbs,
- **Level 3** adds HATEOAS hypermedia controls.

Thus, HATEOAS is what differentiates a *truly RESTful* API from a *REST-like* one.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEEBT831, Module 4]**
> *Note: KTU Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer internal choice between two questions. Two alternatives are provided below.*

### ❖ Question B1 (14 Marks) — Alternative A

**Q: Design a complete RESTful Web API for an IoT-based Remote Patient Monitoring System. Your design must cover (a) resource identification, URI design, and HTTP verb mapping for at least 5 resources, and (b) the JSON request/response schemas for two key resources, status codes, security mechanism, and versioning strategy.**

#### Sub-part (a) — Resource Identification, URI Design, and HTTP Verb Mapping [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Resources (Nouns) Identified [1 Mark]**
1. `Patient`
2. `Device` (wearable sensor)
3. `Vitals` (telemetry readings)
4. `Alert` (threshold breach notifications)
5. `Doctor`

**Step 2: URI Design [2 Marks]**

| Resource Collection | Single Resource | Sub-Resource |
|---|---|---|
| `/v1/patients` | `/v1/patients/{patientId}` | `/v1/patients/{patientId}/vitals` |
| `/v1/devices` | `/v1/devices/{deviceId}` | `/v1/devices/{deviceId}/telemetry` |
| `/v1/doctors` | `/v1/doctors/{doctorId}` | `/v1/doctors/{doctorId}/patients` |
| `/v1/alerts` | `/v1/alerts/{alertId}` | (root level) |

**Step 3: HTTP Verb Mapping Table [2 Marks]**

| URI Pattern | GET | POST | PUT | DELETE |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `/v1/patients` | List all patients (200) | Register new patient (201) | — | — |
| `/v1/patients/{id}` | Fetch patient (200) | 405 | Update patient (200) | Discharge patient (204) |
| `/v1/patients/{id}/vitals` | Get vitals history (200) | Append new reading (201) | — | — |
| `/v1/devices/{id}/telemetry` | Fetch device telemetry (200) | Push telemetry (201) | — | — |
| `/v1/alerts/{id}` | Fetch alert details (200) | — | Acknowledge alert (200) | Dismiss alert (204) |

**Step 4: Versioning [1 Mark]**
URI-based versioning: `/v1/`, `/v2/`, etc. — ensures backward compatibility when the API evolves (e.g., adding a new FHIR-compliant vital metric).

**Step 5: Security [1 Mark]**
- **Authentication:** OAuth 2.0 with **JWT Bearer tokens** for users; **API Keys** for IoT devices.
- **Authorization:** Role-based: `nurse:read`, `doctor:read+write`, `device:write-only`.
- **Transport:** TLS 1.3 mandatory; plain HTTP redirected via 301 to HTTPS.

#### Sub-part (b) — JSON Schemas and Status Codes [7 Marks]

**Schema 1: Vitals Resource — `POST /v1/patients/P-1023/vitals` [2 Marks]**

**Request Body:**
```json
{
  "deviceId": "D-ECG-77",
  "timestamp": "2026-01-25T10:30:00Z",
  "metrics": {
    "heartRate":   { "value": 78,  "unit": "bpm" },
    "spo2":        { "value": 98,  "unit": "%"   },
    "temperature": { "value": 36.8,"unit": "C"   }
  }
}
```

**Response (201 Created):**
```json
{
  "vitalId": "V-9001",
  "patientId": "P-1023",
  "status": "RECORDED",
  "self": "/v1/patients/P-1023/vitals/V-9001"
}
```

**Schema 2: Alert Resource — `GET /v1/alerts/A-501` [1 Mark]**

```json
{
  "alertId": "A-501",
  "patientId": "P-1023",
  "severity": "CRITICAL",
  "metric": "heartRate",
  "triggerValue": 145,
  "threshold": 120,
  "timestamp": "2026-01-25T10:32:15Z",
  "acknowledged": false,
  "_links": {
    "acknowledge": { "href": "/v1/alerts/A-501", "method": "PUT" },
    "patient":     { "href": "/v1/patients/P-1023" }
  }
}
```

**Status Code Mapping [2 Marks]**

| Scenario | Status Code | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Successful vitals append | **201 Created** | New resource (vitalId) created |
| Patient not found | **404 Not Found** | URI resource does not exist |
| Invalid heart rate (e.g., 9999) | **422 Unprocessable Entity** | Syntax valid, semantics invalid |
| Expired JWT | **401 Unauthorized** | Auth credentials invalid |
| Device quota exceeded | **429 Too Many Requests** | Rate-limit triggered |

**HATEOAS Demonstration [1 Mark]**
The Alert response includes a `_links` object, allowing the doctor's app to discover the *acknowledge* action without hardcoding the URI. This places the API at **Richardson Maturity Model Level 3**.

**Error Representation [1 Mark]**
```json
{
  "error": {
    "code": 422,
    "type": "UnprocessableEntity",
    "message": "heartRate value 9999 is out of physiological bounds (30-220 bpm).",
    "traceId": "req-8f3a2b-2026-01-25"
  }
}
```

---

### ❖ Question B1 (14 Marks) — Alternative B

**Q: (a) Explain the six architectural constraints of REST with a block diagram. (b) Compare REST with SOAP, and design a RESTful API for a Smart Wearable Fitness Tracker that supports at least 3 resources with full HTTP verb mapping and JSON schemas.**

#### Sub-part (a) — Six REST Constraints [7 Marks]

The six constraints proposed by **Roy Fielding** are:

**1. Client–Server Constraint [1 Mark]**
The user interface (client) and data storage (server) are separated. They evolve independently. *Example:* A fitness tracker (server) can change its database engine without affecting the mobile app (client).

**2. Statelessness [1 Mark]**
Each request from the client must contain *all* information the server needs. The server stores **no session state**. *Engineering benefit:* Horizontal scalability — any server instance can handle any request.

**3. Cacheability [1 Mark]**
Responses must explicitly indicate whether they can be cached (`Cache-Control: max-age=3600`) or not (`Cache-Control: no-cache`). *Example:* Static user profile is cacheable; live step count is not.

**4. Uniform Interface [1.5 Marks]**
The cornerstone of REST. Comprises four sub-rules:
- Resource identification via URI
- Manipulation via representations (JSON/XML)
- Self-descriptive messages
- **HATEOAS** (hypermedia links in responses)

**5. Layered System [1 Mark]**
The client cannot distinguish whether it is connected to the end server or an intermediary (proxy, load balancer, cache). *Example:* API Gateway → Microservice → Database.

**6. Code on Demand [0.5 Mark]**
*Optional.* Servers can send executable code (e.g., JavaScript) to extend client functionality. Rarely used in safety-critical systems.

**Block Diagram [1 Mark]**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C["Client App"] -->|HTTP Request| L1["Layer 1 - Load Balancer"]
    L1 --> L2["Layer 2 - API Gateway + Auth"]
    L2 --> L3["Layer 3 - Business Logic Server"]
    L3 --> D[("Database")]
    L3 -->|HTTP Response with Cache-Control| C
```

#### Sub-part (b) — REST vs SOAP + Fitness Tracker API Design [7 Marks]

**REST vs SOAP Comparison Table [2 Marks]**

| Parameter | REST | SOAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Architectural style | Protocol |
| **Data Format** | JSON, XML, YAML, plain text | XML only |
| **Transport** | HTTP/HTTPS | HTTP, SMTP, TCP |
| **State** | Stateless | Can be stateful |
| **Bandwidth** | Low (JSON is compact) | High (XML + envelope overhead) |
| **Contract** | Optional (OpenAPI) | Mandatory (WSDL) |
| **Security** | TLS + OAuth/JWT | WS-Security standard |
| **IoT Suitability** | **High** — low payload, easy parsing | **Low** — heavy for sensors |

**Smart Fitness Tracker API — Resources [1 Mark]**
1. `User` — fitness tracker owner
2. `Activity` — a walk/run/cycle session
3. `HeartRate` — beats per minute log

**URI Design + Verb Mapping [2 Marks]**

| URI | GET | POST | PUT | DELETE |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `/v1/users` | List users (200) | Register (201) | — | — |
| `/v1/users/{id}` | Fetch profile (200) | 405 | Update (200) | Deactivate (204) |
| `/v1/users/{id}/activities` | List sessions (200) | Log new session (201) | — | — |
| `/v1/users/{id}/heartrate` | Get HR history (200) | Append reading (201) | — | Clear (204) |

**JSON Schema — `POST /v1/users/U-501/activities` [1 Mark]**

**Request:**
```json
{
  "type": "RUN",
  "startTime": "2026-01-25T06:00:00Z",
  "endTime":   "2026-01-25T06:45:00Z",
  "distance": 7.2,
  "calories": 540
}
```

**Response (201 Created):**
```json
{
  "activityId": "A-22001",
  "userId": "U-501",
  "status": "LOGGED",
  "self": "/v1/users/U-501/activities/A-22001"
}
```

**Status Codes [0.5 Mark]**
- 200 OK (list activities)
- 201 Created (log activity)
- 401 Unauthorized (invalid token)
- 404 Not Found (unknown user)
- 422 Unprocessable Entity (negative distance)

**Security [0.5 Mark]**
OAuth 2.0 + JWT for user login; per-device API Key for the tracker firmware.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing URI with URN/URL** — Always show the *full absolute URI* including scheme `https://`.
> 2. **Using verbs in URI** — *WRONG:* `/getPatient/1023` — *RIGHT:* `/patients/1023` with `GET` verb.
> 3. **Pluralization errors** — URIs must use *plural nouns* (`/patients`, not `/patient`).
> 4. **Wrong status codes** — Returning `200 OK` for a *creation* operation costs 1 mark. Use `201 Created`.
> 5. **Ignoring statelessness** — Storing session IDs on the server violates REST. Use JWT instead.
> 6. **Missing HATEOAS links** — In 14-mark questions, even a single `_links` block in the response earns the extra 1 mark.
> 7. **No versioning** — A complete design *must* include `/v1/`. Skipping it loses 1 mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Pin This Section**

- **REST** = Representational State Transfer; **inventor:** Roy Fielding, **year:** 2000.
- **REST is a style, not a protocol**; **HTTP is the protocol** that implements it.
- A truly RESTful API = **Resource-based + Stateless + Uniform Interface + Cacheable + Layered**.
- **Six Constraints:** Client–Server, Stateless, Cacheable, Uniform Interface, Layered System, Code on Demand *(optional)*.
- **Richardson Maturity Levels:** 0 (Swamp of POX) → 1 (Resources) → 2 (HTTP Verbs) → 3 (HATEOAS).
- **Resource = Noun**; **Action = HTTP Verb** — *never* put verbs in the URI.
- **URI Rules:** Lowercase, plural nouns, hierarchy depth $\leq 3$, no trailing slash, hyphens (not underscores) for multi-word names.
- **HTTP Methods & Idempotency:**
  - `GET` — Safe + Idempotent
  - `PUT` — Idempotent (not safe)
  - `DELETE` — Idempotent (not safe)
  - `POST` — Neither safe nor idempotent
  - `PATCH` — Neither safe nor idempotent
- **Status Code Mental Model:**
  - **2xx** = "All good" (200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content)
  - **3xx** = "Look elsewhere" (301 Moved)
  - **4xx** = "Your fault" (400, 401, 403, 404, 409, 422, 429)
  - **5xx** = "My fault" (500, 502, 503, 504)
- **Payload:** Use **JSON** for IoT (smaller); XML is allowed but heavier. **FHIR standard** uses JSON for medical data.
- **HATEOAS** = Hypermedia links in responses; gives **Level 3** maturity.
- **Security Triad for Biomedical IoT:** **OAuth 2.0 + JWT** (users) + **API Keys** (devices) + **TLS 1.3** (transport).
- **Versioning:** Embed in URI as `/v{n}/`. Never break a deployed version. Use `Deprecation` and `Sunset` HTTP headers when retiring a version.
- **Stateless Memory Bound** is $M_{\text{session}} = 0$ bytes per request on the server.
- **For ECG/IoT telemetry:** Bandwidth formula is $B = S_{\text{payload}} \times f_s \times N_{\text{channels}}$.
- **Throughput formula:** $\lambda = N_{\text{requests}} / T_{\text{window}}$, measured in RPS (requests per second).
- **Response time via Little's Law:** $R = (N_{\text{concurrent}} - 1) / \lambda$.
- **Cache effectiveness:** $T_{\text{eff}} = H \cdot T_{\text{cached}} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{\text{origin}}$.
- **Pagination rule:** $1 \leq S_{\text{page}} \leq 100$ for biomedical APIs; use `?page=1&size=20` query params.
- **Error response format** must always include: `code`, `type`, `message`, and a `traceId` for debugging.
- **FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources)** is the global biomedical standard built on RESTful principles — your API should aim for FHIR compatibility to earn full KTU marks.
